topics.title: Evolution of Mobile Communications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Evolution of Mobile Communications # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mobile Communication Evolution** is the chronological progression of wireless cellular telecommunication standards, categorized into distinct **Generations (G)**. Each generation represents a paradigm shift in radio access technology, switching techniques, modulation schemes, multiple-access methods, and supported data services — moving from purely analog voice-only systems (1G) to fully digital, packet-switched, IP-based broadband and machine-type networks (5G and beyond). The evolution is formally tracked by the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU)** through its **IMT (International Mobile Telecommunications)** family of standards, and by the **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)** which governs the commercial rollout of GSM, UMTS, LTE, and 5G NR. ## 1.2 The Six Generations — At a Glance > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a comparative study of generations. Memorize the **Year**, **Multiple Access**, **Switching**, and **Peak Data Rate** of each generation. | Generation | Era | Multiple Access | Switching | Peak Data Rate | Key Service | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **1G** | 1980s | **FDMA** | Circuit | 2.4 kbps | Analog Voice | | **2G** | 1990s | **TDMA / CDMA** | Circuit | 64 kbps | Voice + SMS | | **2.5G** | 2000 | TDMA + GPRS | Packet (partial) | 171 kbps | WAP, MMS | | **3G** | 2000s | **W-CDMA** | Packet | 2 Mbps | Mobile Internet | | **3.5G** | 2008 | HSPA | Packet | 14.4 Mbps | Video Call | | **4G** | 2010s | **OFDMA + MIMO** | All-IP | 1 Gbps | HD Streaming | | **4.5G (LTE-A)** | 2014 | OFDMA + CA | All-IP | 3 Gbps | IoT, VoLTE | | **5G NR** | 2020 | **OFDMA / NOMA** | All-IP | 20 Gbps | eMBB, URLLC, mMTC | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Think of mobile generations as "language proficiency levels" of a person:** > - **1G** = A baby crying (analog, only one tone — voice). > - **2G** = A child forming short sentences (digital SMS, limited vocabulary). > - **3G** = A teenager holding conversations and reading books (mobile internet, browsing). > - **4G** = An adult fluent in multimedia, video calling, and HD streaming. > - **5G** = A multilingual expert who can simultaneously handle billions of devices, autonomous cars, and remote surgery. ## 1.4 Core Metrics You Must Know > [!IMPORTANT] > Three metrics quantify a generation's capability: > 1. **Spectral Efficiency (bps/Hz)** — how much data per unit bandwidth. > 2. **Latency (ms)** — round-trip delay (1G: ~1000 ms, 5G: **1 ms**). > 3. **Connection Density (devices/km²)** — 5G targets **1,000,000 devices/km²**. These are the **standard benchmarks** the KTU examiner will use to test your comparative understanding. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Generations Evolve — The Driving Forces Every generational leap is driven by three pressures: 1. **Capacity Demand** — More users, more data per user (Cisco's visual networking index forecasts ~77 EB/month mobile traffic). 2. **Service Innovation** — Voice → SMS → Internet → Video → IoT/AR/VR. 3. **Spectrum Scarcity** — Each generation refines how efficiently the limited radio spectrum is reused. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Trade-off:** Higher data rate requires wider bandwidth, higher-order modulation, or better spectral efficiency. Since bandwidth is a finite natural resource, each generation pushes **spectral efficiency (η)** upward. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shannon-Hartley Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | Fundamental data-rate limit per channel | | 2 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C}{B}$ | bps/Hz | Compares generations quantitatively | | 3 | Shannon Limit (SNR) | $\dfrac{S}{N} = 2^{C/B} - 1$ | ratio | Minimum SNR to support bit-rate C over B | | 4 | Cell Reuse Cluster Size | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | cells | Frequency reuse pattern (1G/2G) | | 5 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless | 1G = 4.6, 2G = 3, 4G = 1 (universal freq reuse) | | 6 | Number of users per cell (FDMA) | $N_{u} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{B_{ch}}$ | users | 1G capacity | | 7 | 5G Peak Data Rate (3GPP) | $D_{5G} \approx 20\text{ Gbps (DL)}$ | bps | eMBB benchmark | | 8 | 5G Latency Target | $L_{5G} \leq 1\text{ ms (URLLC)}$ | ms | URLLC benchmark | | 9 | 5G Connection Density | $\rho = 10^{6}\text{ devices/km}^{2}$ | devices/km² | mMTC benchmark | | 10 | MIMO Capacity (n\_t × n\_r) | $C_{MIMO} = \min(n_t, n_r) \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps/Hz | Why 4G/5G use massive MIMO | > [!WARNING] > In KTU valuation, if you write the Shannon formula without the logarithmic base 2, the examiner deducts **2 marks**. Always state **C = B log₂(1 + S/N)**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why does a KTU engineer care about this evolution?** > - **Network Planning:** Operators (Jio, Airtel, Vi) deploy cells with reuse patterns dictated by 4G/5G. > - **IoT/Embedded Design:** Choosing NB-IoT (4.5G) vs. 5G mMTC for low-power sensor nodes. > - **VLSI/RF Chip Design:** Each generation demands new RF front-ends (e.g., mmWave transceivers in 5G). > - **Cybersecurity:** Older 2G/3G protocols have known vulnerabilities (e.g., SS7 attacks); modern systems use 5G-AKA. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Generation-by-Generation) ### **Generation 1G — Analog Voice (1980s)** - **Standard Examples:** AMPS (Advanced Mobile Phone System, USA), NMT (Nordic Mobile Telephone), TACS (UK). - **Multiple Access:** **FDMA** (Frequency Division Multiple Access). - **Modulation:** Analog FM (Frequency Modulation). - **Switching:** Circuit-switched. - **Channels:** 30 kHz per user. - **Drawbacks:** Poor voice quality, no security, no roaming, no data, eavesdropping trivial. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** FDMA assigns a unique frequency band to each user. Inefficient because of large guard bands. Also susceptible to **co-channel interference (CCI)**. ### **Generation 2G — Digital Voice + SMS (1990s)** - **Standard Examples:** GSM (Global System for Mobile), IS-95 (CDMAOne), D-AMPS (IS-136). - **Multiple Access:** **TDMA** (GSM: 8 users per 200 kHz) and **CDMA** (IS-95: spread spectrum). - **Modulation:** **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) for GSM; BPSK/QPSK for CDMA. - **Switching:** Circuit-switched (voice); SMS uses **signalling channel (SDCCH)**. - **Data Rate:** 9.6 kbps (GSM), 14.4 kbps (IS-95). - **Innovation:** First digital encryption (A5/1), SIM card, international roaming. **Derivation of GSM TDMA Frame Structure:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Frame Duration} &= 4.615 \text{ ms} \\ \text{Time Slots per Frame} &= 8 \\ \text{Slot Duration} &= \frac{4.615 \text{ ms}}{8} = 0.577 \text{ ms} \\ \text{Bit Rate per Slot} &= 148 \text{ bits} \\ \text{Gross Bit Rate} &= \frac{148}{0.577 \times 10^{-3}} = 270.833 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic (Row-by-Row):** > - Line 1: GSM uses a hierarchical TDMA frame of 4.615 ms. > - Line 2: Each frame is divided into 8 time slots (so 8 users share one 200 kHz carrier). > - Line 3: Slot duration = total frame time ÷ 8 slots. > - Line 4: One burst contains 148 bits of useful payload. > - Line 5: Gross bit rate = payload bits ÷ slot duration → ~270.833 kbps, of which the user gets 9.6 kbps after channel coding. ### **Generation 2.5G — GPRS / EDGE (2000-2003)** - **GPRS (General Packet Radio Service):** Adds **packet switching** to GSM. Uses the same TDMA slots but dynamically allocates 1–8 slots per user. - **EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution):** Upgrades GMSK to **8-PSK**, tripling data rate. - **Data Rate:** GPRS = 171.2 kbps (theoretical), EDGE = 384 kbps. **Derivation of GPRS Data Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Slot bit rate} &= 270.833 \text{ kbps} \\ \text{Slots per user (max)} &= 8 \\ \text{Modulation (GPRS)} &= \text{GMSK} = 1 \text{ bit/symbol} \\ \text{GPRS peak rate} &= 8 \times 270.833 \times 1 = 2166.67 \text{ kbps (raw)} \\ \text{Coding scheme CS-2 efficiency} &= \approx 65\% \\ \text{Realistic peak} &= 171.2 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** > - Line 1: Same as GSM bit rate per slot. > - Line 2: GPRS allows a single user to monopolize all 8 slots in a frame. > - Line 3: GMSK = 1 bit per symbol (lowest spectral efficiency modulation). > - Line 4: Multiply 8 slots × 270.833 kbps × 1 bit/symbol → raw throughput. > - Line 5: After Reed-Muller block coding (CS-2 = 2/3 efficiency) → 171.2 kbps practical. > - **EDGE upgrade:** 8-PSK gives 3 bits/symbol → 3× boost = 384 kbps. ### **Generation 3G — Mobile Internet (2000s)** - **Standard:** IMT-2000 family. Major variants: **UMTS (W-CDMA)** and **CDMA2000**. - **Multiple Access:** **W-CDMA** (Wideband CDMA) — 5 MHz carrier, chip rate 3.84 Mcps. - **Switching:** Fully packet-switched. - **Modulation:** QPSK (DL), BPSK (UL). - **Data Rate:** 384 kbps (R99) → 14.4 Mbps (HSPA+). > [!IMPORTANT] > **3G's Key Innovation — Spread Spectrum:** Each user's data is multiplied by a unique **pseudo-random spreading code** of length **SF (Spreading Factor)**. This allows many users to share the same 5 MHz band simultaneously. **Derivation of CDMA Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Processing Gain} &= G_p = \frac{W}{R} \\ \text{where } W &= 3.84 \text{ MHz (chip rate)}, \quad R = \text{user data rate} \\ \text{Effective SNR} &= \frac{S}{N} \cdot G_p \\ \text{Capacity (approx.)} &= \frac{W}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{E_b/N_0} \text{ users} \\ \text{For } W = 3.84 \text{ MHz}, R = 9.6 \text{ kbps:} \quad G_p &= \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 400 \\ \text{Number of users (ideal, } E_b/N_0 = 7 \text{ dB)} &= \frac{400}{5.01} \approx 80 \text{ users/cell} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** > - Line 1: Processing gain G\_p spreads the narrowband signal over a wide bandwidth, providing immunity against narrowband interference. > - Line 2: W is the chip rate (3.84 Mcps in UMTS), R is the user bit rate. > - Line 3: The effective SNR is improved by the processing gain. > - Line 4: Pole capacity formula — total users = W/R × 1/(E\_b/N\_0). > - Line 5: With W = 3.84 MHz, R = 9.6 kbps voice, G\_p = 400. > - Line 6: 7 dB = 10^(7/10) ≈ 5.01 in linear scale → ~80 voice users per cell. ### **Generation 3.5G — HSPA / HSPA+ (2008-2010)** - **HSDPA (High Speed Downlink Packet Access):** Adaptive Modulation (QPSK → **16-QAM**), fast scheduling, shorter TTI (2 ms). - **HSPA+:** Introduces **64-QAM** and **MIMO 2×2**. - **Peak Rate:** 14.4 Mbps (DL) → 42 Mbps (with MIMO). ### **Generation 4G — LTE / WiMAX (2010s)** - **Standard:** 3GPP Release 8 (LTE), IEEE 802.16e (WiMAX). - **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** (Downlink) and **SC-FDMA** (Uplink). - **Modulation:** QPSK, 16-QAM, **64-QAM**. - **Switching:** **All-IP** — no circuit switching at all. - **MIMO:** 2×2, 4×4 configurations. - **Peak Data Rate:** 100 Mbps (mobile) → 1 Gbps (fixed/LTE-A). **Derivation of OFDM Subcarrier Orthogonality:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Subcarrier spacing} &= \Delta f = \frac{1}{T_{sym}} \\ \text{LTE standard} &= \Delta f = 15 \text{ kHz} \\ \text{Total subcarriers in 20 MHz} &= \frac{20 \times 10^6}{15 \times 10^3} = 1333 \\ \text{Used subcarriers} &= 1200 \text{ (others are DC, guard)} \\ \text{Cyclic prefix (normal)} &= 4.69 \text{ µs} \\ \text{Useful symbol duration} &= 66.67 \text{ µs} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** > - Line 1: OFDM subcarriers are orthogonal when their spacing = 1/symbol duration. > - Line 2: LTE normalizes this to 15 kHz spacing. > - Line 3: 20 MHz bandwidth ÷ 15 kHz = 1333 subcarriers. > - Line 4: Of these, ~1200 carry data; the rest are guard bands and the DC null. > - Line 5: Cyclic prefix absorbs multipath delay spread to prevent ISI. > - Line 6: Symbol duration = 1/15 kHz = 66.67 µs. **Derivation of 4G Peak Data Rate (MIMO boost):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Single antenna peak (SISO)} &= 150 \text{ Mbps (LTE cat 4, 64-QAM, 20 MHz)} \\ \text{MIMO 2×2 multiplicative gain} &= 2\times \\ \text{MIMO 4×4 peak} &= 4 \times 150 = 600 \text{ Mbps} \\ \text{LTE-A carrier aggregation (3 carriers)} &= 3 \times 600 = 1.8 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** > - Line 1: SISO LTE with 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol) in 20 MHz yields ~150 Mbps. > - Line 2: MIMO multiplies the rate by min(n\_t, n\_r) linearly in ideal conditions. > - Line 3: 4×4 MIMO = 4× the throughput. > - Line 4: Carrier Aggregation across 3 × 20 MHz = 60 MHz total bandwidth → 1.8 Gbps theoretical peak. ### **Generation 4.5G — LTE-Advanced Pro (2014-2019)** - Adds **LAA (Licensed Assisted Access)** — LTE in unlicensed 5 GHz. - Introduces **NB-IoT** and **Cat-M1** for massive IoT. - **256-QAM** modulation. - Peak: ~3 Gbps (theoretical). ### **Generation 5G NR — New Radio (2020+)** - **Standard:** 3GPP Release 15 onwards. - **Frequency Bands:** Sub-6 GHz (FR1) and **mmWave 24-52 GHz (FR2)**. - **Numerology:** Scalable OFDM subcarrier spacing (15/30/60/120/240 kHz). - **Modulation:** Up to **256-QAM (data)**. - **Three Use Cases (ITU IMT-2020):** 1. **eMBB** — enhanced Mobile Broadband (20 Gbps). 2. **URLLC** — Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency (1 ms). 3. **mMTC** — massive Machine-Type Communications (10⁶ devices/km²). **Derivation of 5G mmWave Capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bandwidth at 28 GHz, 800 MHz allocation} &= B = 800 \text{ MHz} \\ \text{SNR (line-of-sight, 100 m)} &= 20 \text{ dB} = 100 \\ \text{Shannon Capacity} &= C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 800 \times 10^6 \times 6.66 \\ C &= 5.33 \text{ Gbps per stream} \\ \text{With 4×4 MIMO} &= 4 \times 5.33 = 21.3 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** > - Line 1: 5G FR2 (mmWave) gives access to vast contiguous bandwidth (e.g., 800 MHz at 28 GHz), unlike the fragmented sub-6 GHz LTE bands. > - Line 2: At short range with directional beamforming, SNR is high (20 dB = 100 linear). > - Line 3: Apply Shannon's formula. > - Line 4: log₂(101) ≈ 6.66 bits/Hz. > - Line 5: Multiplied by 4 MIMO streams, 5G hits the 20 Gbps eMBB target. > [!WARNING] > **Common Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse **5G NR frequency bands**. Memorize: **FR1 = 410 MHz – 7.125 GHz**, **FR2 = 24.25 GHz – 52.6 GHz**. ### **Generation 6G (Vision, 2030+)** - **Terahertz (THz) communications** (0.1–10 THz). - **AI-native network** (self-organizing, self-healing). - **Holographic telepresence**, **digital twin**, **sub-ms latency**. - **Target data rate:** 1 Tbps. - **Standardization:** ITU IMT-2030 (in progress). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mobile Communication Evolution — Timeline Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR G1G["1G 1980s
Analog Voice
FDMA FM"]:::gen1 G2G["2G 1990s
Digital Voice SMS

AMF SMF UPF"] end subgraph NR["5G New Radio Access Network"] GNB["gNodeB gNB
Base Station"] end subgraph UseCases["Three 5G Service Categories"] EMBB["eMBB
Enhanced Mobile Broadband
20 Gbps 4K AR VR"] URLLC["URLLC
Ultra Reliable Low Latency
1 ms Autonomous Cars"] MMTC["mMTC
Massive Machine Type Comm
1M devices per sq km IoT"] end GNB --> EMBB GNB --> URLLC GNB --> MMTC GNB --> SBA classDef core fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef nr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef use fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 class SBA core class GNB nr class EMBB,URLLC,MMTC use ``` ## 4.4 Circuit-Switched vs Packet-Switched — Service Evolution ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CS["Circuit Switched 1G 2G 3G voice"] CS1["Dedicated Channel 9.6 kbps voice"] CS2["Constant Bandwidth Allocated"] CS3["Channel Held Entire Call"] end subgraph PS["Packet Switched 2.5G 3G 4G 5G data"] PS1["Shared Channel Bursty"] PS2["Bandwidth on Demand"] PS3["Statistical Multiplexing"] end CS --> PS classDef cs fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef ps fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 class CS1,CS2,CS3 cs class PS1,PS2,PS3 ps ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List the differences between 1G and 2G mobile communication systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | 1G | 2G | |---|---|---| | **Era** | 1980s | 1990s | | **Signal Type** | Analog | Digital | | **Multiple Access** | FDMA | TDMA / CDMA | | **Switching** | Circuit only | Circuit (voice) + Packet (data) | | **Data Services** | Voice only | Voice + SMS | | **Security** | None (eavesdropping easy) | A5/1 encryption | | **Examples** | AMPS, NMT, TACS | GSM, IS-95 | | **Peak Rate** | 2.4 kbps | 9.6–14.4 kbps | > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip:** A simple 4-row comparison table = full 3 marks. Use **bold** keywords. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the role of OFDMA in 4G/5G systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) is the **multiple access technique** used in **4G LTE and 5G NR** systems. 1. It divides the available bandwidth into **multiple orthogonal subcarriers** (15 kHz spacing in LTE). 2. Subcarriers are grouped into **Resource Blocks (RBs)** of 12 subcarriers × 7 OFDM symbols. 3. Each user is dynamically allocated a subset of RBs in time and frequency → **flexible bandwidth allocation**. 4. Advantages: **high spectral efficiency**, **robustness to multipath fading** (via cyclic prefix), **flexible subcarrier assignment**, and **easy MIMO integration**. ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the evolution of mobile communication from 1G to 5G, highlighting the multiple access techniques, switching methods, modulation schemes, and key services in each generation.** #### **Part (a) — 1G to 3G (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1G (1980s):** - **Multiple Access:** FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access) — each user gets a unique 30 kHz channel. - **Switching:** Circuit-switched only. - **Modulation:** Analog FM (Frequency Modulation). - **Standards:** AMPS, NMT, TACS. - **Service:** Analog voice only. - **Limitations:** Poor voice quality, no security, low capacity, no roaming. **2G (1990s):** - **Multiple Access:** TDMA (GSM, 8 slots per 200 kHz) and CDMA (IS-95). - **Switching:** Circuit-switched; data on signalling channels. - **Modulation:** GMSK (Gaussian MSK) for GSM; BPSK for IS-95. - **Standards:** GSM, IS-95, D-AMPS. - **Services:** Digital voice, **SMS (160 characters)**, low-rate data (9.6 kbps), caller ID. - **Innovations:** SIM card, international roaming, A5/1 encryption. **2.5G (2000):** - **Multiple Access:** TDMA + GPRS dynamic slot allocation. - **Switching:** **Hybrid** — circuit for voice, **packet for data**. - **Modulation:** GMSK (GPRS) → 8-PSK (EDGE). - **Services:** WAP browsing, MMS, email (171 kbps GPRS, 384 kbps EDGE). **3G (2001-2008):** - **Multiple Access:** **W-CDMA** (Wideband CDMA) — 5 MHz carrier, 3.84 Mcps chip rate. - **Switching:** Fully packet-switched (PS domain + CS domain). - **Modulation:** QPSK (DL), BPSK (UL). - **Standards:** UMTS, CDMA2000, IMT-2000. - **Services:** Mobile internet, video calling, GPS, mobile TV (384 kbps → 2 Mbps). #### **Part (b) — 3.5G to 5G (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **3.5G (2008):** - **Multiple Access:** W-CDMA + HSPA scheduling. - **Modulation:** 16-QAM (HSDPA), 64-QAM (HSPA+). - **Data Rate:** 14.4 Mbps (HSPA) → 42 Mbps (HSPA+ with MIMO 2×2). **4G / LTE (2010):** - **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** (DL) and **SC-FDMA** (UL). - **Switching:** **All-IP** — no circuit switching. - **Modulation:** QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM. - **MIMO:** 2×2, 4×4. - **Data Rate:** 100 Mbps (mobile) → 1 Gbps (LTE-A with carrier aggregation). - **Services:** HD video streaming, VoLTE, mobile cloud, online gaming. **4.5G (LTE-Advanced Pro, 2014):** - Adds **256-QAM**, **LAA** (unlicensed 5 GHz), **NB-IoT**, **Cat-M1**. - Peak rate ~3 Gbps; supports massive IoT. **5G NR (2020+):** - **Multiple Access:** OFDMA (with scalable numerology: 15/30/60/120/240 kHz subcarrier spacing). - **Frequency Bands:** **FR1 (sub-6 GHz)** and **FR2 (mmWave 24-52 GHz)**. - **Modulation:** Up to 256-QAM. - **Three Use Cases:** 1. **eMBB** — 20 Gbps peak (4K/8K video, AR/VR). 2. **URLLC** — 1 ms latency (autonomous vehicles, remote surgery). 3. **mMTC** — 10⁶ devices/km² (smart city, IoT). - **Other innovations:** Network slicing, beamforming, edge computing. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students write "**4G uses OFDM**" — partially correct. The accurate answer is "**4G uses OFDMA in DL and SC-FDMA in UL**". Writing only "OFDM" = lose 1 mark. > - Confusing **3G W-CDMA** with **3.5G HSPA** is a common 2-mark deduction. Remember: 3G = UMTS FDD/TDD; 3.5G = HSDPA/HSUPA/HSPA+ on top of UMTS. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **With the help of a comparison table, describe the evolution of mobile communication generations. Also explain Shannon's capacity formula and its role in determining the data rate of each generation.** #### **Part (a) — Comparative Table of Generations (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Feature | 1G | 2G | 3G | 4G | 5G | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Year** | 1980 | 1990 | 2000 | 2010 | 2020 | | **Standard** | AMPS | GSM | UMTS | LTE | 5G NR | | **Multiple Access** | FDMA | TDMA/CDMA | W-CDMA | OFDMA | OFDMA/NOMA | | **Modulation** | FM | GMSK | QPSK | 64-QAM | 256-QAM | | **Switching** | Circuit | Circuit | Packet | All-IP | All-IP | | **Peak Rate** | 2.4 kbps | 64 kbps | 2 Mbps | 1 Gbps | 20 Gbps | | **Service** | Voice | SMS | Internet | HD Video | IoT/AR/VR | | **Latency** | ~1000 ms | 300 ms | 100 ms | 10 ms | 1 ms | **Explanation (2-3 lines per generation):** - **1G:** First analog cellular system, used FDMA; limited to voice; no security. - **2G:** First digital system, introduced SMS, SIM cards, and roaming (GSM standard). - **3G:** Mobile internet via W-CDMA; video calling; introduced by ITU IMT-2000 vision. - **4G:** All-IP packet core; OFDMA + MIMO enabled broadband speeds; VoLTE. - **5G:** Three service categories (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC); mmWave; network slicing. #### **Part (b) — Shannon Capacity & Generational Data Rates (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Shannon-Hartley Theorem:** $$ C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ Where: - $C$ = Channel capacity (bps). - $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz). - $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear, not dB). **Role in Generations:** Each generation **maximizes C** by improving one or more variables: 1. **Wider Bandwidth (B):** - 1G: 30 kHz per channel → C ≈ 2.4 kbps. - 5G mmWave (FR2): 800 MHz → C ≈ 21 Gbps (1000× more bandwidth). 2. **Higher SNR (S/N):** - Better antenna design, beamforming (5G), lower noise amplifiers. - 5G mmWave: SNR ≈ 20 dB → C = B × 6.66 bps/Hz. 3. **Better Spectral Efficiency (η = C/B):** - 1G (FM): ≈ 0.1 bps/Hz. - 2G (GMSK): ≈ 1.35 bps/Hz. - 3G (W-CDMA): ≈ 1.6 bps/Hz. - 4G (OFDMA + 64-QAM + MIMO): ≈ 30 bps/Hz. - 5G (256-QAM + Massive MIMO): ≈ 60 bps/Hz. **Numerical Demonstration (3G to 5G transition):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{3G UMTS (W-CDMA)} &: B = 5 \text{ MHz}, \quad S/N = 10 \text{ dB} = 10 \\ C_{3G} &= 5 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(11) = 5 \times 10^6 \times 3.46 = 17.3 \text{ Mbps} \\[6pt] \text{4G LTE (20 MHz, 64-QAM)} &: B = 20 \text{ MHz}, \quad S/N = 20 \text{ dB} = 100 \\ C_{4G} &= 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(101) = 20 \times 10^6 \times 6.66 = 133.2 \text{ Mbps} \\[6pt] \text{4G with 4×4 MIMO} &: C_{4G-MIMO} = 4 \times 133.2 = 532.8 \text{ Mbps} \\[6pt] \text{5G NR (mmWave 800 MHz, 4×4 MIMO)} &: C_{5G} = 4 \times 800 \times 10^6 \times 6.66 = 21.3 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic (Row-by-Row):** > - Line 1: 3G W-CDMA: 5 MHz bandwidth, moderate SNR (10 dB). Applying Shannon gives ~17.3 Mbps. > - Line 2: 4G LTE: 4× bandwidth and 10× better SNR due to digital processing → 133 Mbps. > - Line 3: 4×4 MIMO multiplies capacity by 4. > - Line 4: 5G mmWave (40× more bandwidth + MIMO) crosses 20 Gbps — confirming the IMT-2020 eMBB target. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse Shannon Capacity with actual achievable throughput.** Shannon's C is an **upper bound**; real systems achieve 50-70% of C due to coding, overhead, and implementation losses. KTU answer sheets that claim "LTE delivers 1 Gbps = Shannon capacity" lose 1 mark. > - Always write $S/N$ as a **linear ratio** (e.g., 100), not in dB (e.g., 20 dB). Convert dB to linear using $10^{dB/10}$ before substituting. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Evolution of Mobile Communications** - ✅ **1G (1980s):** Analog, **FDMA**, FM modulation, circuit-switched, voice only (AMPS/NMT/TACS). - ✅ **2G (1990s):** Digital, **TDMA/CDMA**, GMSK, GSM introduced **SIM + SMS + A5/1 encryption**. - ✅ **2.5G (2000):** **GPRS** (171 kbps) and **EDGE** (384 kbps) — first packet data. - ✅ **3G (2000s):** **W-CDMA**, 5 MHz, IMT-2000 vision, UMTS, mobile internet (2 Mbps). - ✅ **3.5G (2008):** **HSDPA/HSPA+** with 16-QAM/64-QAM, 14–42 Mbps. - ✅ **4G (2010):** **OFDMA (DL) + SC-FDMA (UL)**, 64-QAM, **MIMO**, **All-IP core**, LTE (1 Gbps). - ✅ **4.5G (2014):** **LTE-Advanced Pro**, **256-QAM**, **NB-IoT**, **LAA** (3 Gbps). - ✅ **5G NR (2020):** **Scalable OFDM numerology (15-240 kHz)**, **mmWave (FR2: 24-52 GHz)**, **3 use cases (eMBB/URLLC/mMTC)**, **20 Gbps peak, 1 ms latency, 10⁶ devices/km²**. - ✅ **6G (2030+):** Terahertz (THz), AI-native, holographic, 1 Tbps. - ✅ **Shannon Capacity Formula:** $C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ — defines the upper bound; real throughput ≈ 50-70% of C. - ✅ **Spectral Efficiency Progression:** 1G (0.1) → 2G (1.35) → 3G (1.6) → 4G (30) → 5G (60) bps/Hz. - ✅ **Switching Evolution:** Circuit (1G) → Circuit + Packet (2.5G/3G) → **All-IP (4G/5G)**. - ✅ **Modulation Evolution:** Analog FM → GMSK → BPSK/QPSK → 16-QAM → 64-QAM → **256-QAM (5G)**. - ✅ **Multiple Access Evolution:** **FDMA → TDMA/CDMA → W-CDMA → OFDMA → OFDMA + NOMA + Massive MIMO**. - ✅ **5G FR1 (sub-6 GHz, 410 MHz – 7.125 GHz)**, **FR2 (mmWave, 24.25 – 52.6 GHz)**. - ✅ **3G organizations:** **ITU (IMT-2000)**, **3GPP (UMTS)**, **3GPP2 (CDMA2000)**. - ✅ **4G organizations:** **3GPP Release 8+ (LTE)**, **IEEE 802.16 (WiMAX)**. - ✅ **5G organization:** **3GPP Release 15+ (5G NR)**, **ITU IMT-2020**. - ✅ **Latency Targets:** 1G ~1000 ms → 2G ~300 ms → 3G ~100 ms → 4G ~10 ms → **5G 1 ms (URLLC)**. - ✅ **Why each generation matters to you:** 4G/5G enables IoT, autonomous vehicles, telemedicine, smart cities. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Generations:** > **"A-D-D-D-D-A-T"** = **A**nalog(1G) → **D**igital SMS(2G) → **D**ata packets(2.5G) → **D**ynamic internet(3G) → **D**enser HSPA(3.5G) → **A**ll-IP LTE(4G) → **T**erahertz 5G(5G).

topics.title: Mobile Radio System Around the World
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mobile Radio System Around the World

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Radio System Around the World** refers to the chronological evolution, deployment, and comparative study of cellular and mobile communication systems that have been developed and standardized across different geographical regions (North America, Europe, Japan, and the rest of the world) from the **1970s to the present day**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCPT502), this topic is a **foundational survey module** that traces the **generational evolution** of mobile radio systems — from **1G (Analog)** to **5G (and beyond)** — and explains *why* different regions adopted different standards despite a common underlying goal: **ubiquitous, reliable, high-capacity mobile telephony**.

The topic typically covers:
- **First Generation (1G)** – Analog FDMA systems (AMPS, NMT, TACS)
- **Second Generation (2G)** – Digital TDMA/CDMA systems (GSM, IS-95/DAMPS)
- **2.5G / 2.75G** – GPRS, EDGE (packet-switched overlay)
- **Third Generation (3G)** – UMTS, CDMA2000 (IMT-2000 family)
- **Fourth Generation (4G)** – LTE, WiMAX (all-IP, OFDMA)
- **Fifth Generation (5G)** – eMBB, URLLC, mMTC (New Radio / NR)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Mobile Radio Evolution as the "Transportation Analogy":**
> Imagine the world of personal travel. In the 1970s, you had a **single, basic bicycle** (1G) — it worked, but only one person could use it, it was analog (pushing pedals), and only on certain "paths" (frequencies). By the 1990s, we had **digital scooters** (2G) — faster, multiple users, text messaging became possible. In the 2000s, we built **shared highways with controlled lanes** (3G) — broadband internet, video calls. 4G gave us **modern electric cars on smart highways** (LTE) — high-speed, low-latency, all-digital. 5G is the **autonomous drone highway** — connecting not just humans but *billions of devices* with ultra-low latency.

### 🌍 Why "Around the World"?

Different continents developed **independent standards** because:
| Region | Lead Driver | Dominant Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **North America (USA)** | FCC / AT&T / Verizon | AMPS → IS-95 → CDMA2000 → LTE |
| **Europe** | CEPT / ETSI | GSM (DECT, TETRA, HiperLAN) |
| **Japan** | NTT DoCoMo / ARIB | PDC → WCDMA → LTE (early adopter) |
| **Korea** | ETRI / SK Telecom | CDMA → WiMAX pioneer → 5G |
| **Rest of World** | ITU-T coordination | Adopted GSM as global baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The "Around the World" perspective is critical because KTU frequently asks comparison questions between standards (e.g., AMPS vs GSM, GSM vs IS-95) — these are **high-frequency 7-mark questions**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Carrier Frequencies (1G AMPS):** **824–894 MHz** (UHF band)
- **Channel Bandwidth (AMPS):** **30 kHz** (FDMA)
- **Channel Bandwidth (GSM):** **200 kHz** (TDMA/FDMA hybrid)
- **GSM Frame Duration:** **4.615 ms**
- **GSM Timeslot:** **577 µs**
- **3G Minimum Data Rate (IMT-2000):** **144 kbps (vehicular)** / **384 kbps (pedestrian)** / **2 Mbps (indoor)**
- **4G Peak Data Rate (LTE):** **100 Mbps (high mobility)** / **1 Gbps (low mobility)**
- **5G Peak Data Rate:** **20 Gbps** (eMBB) / **1 ms latency** (URLLC)
- **ITU Frequency Allocation (IMT-2000):** **1885–2025 MHz** & **2110–2200 MHz**
- **Mobile Network Operator Density (Global, 2024):** ~ **800+ operators worldwide**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Generation — Structured Logic

### 🔹 Generation 1 (1G) — The Analog Foundation (1980s)

- **Operational Principle:** **Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA)** — each user gets a dedicated frequency channel.
- **Modulation:** Analog **FM (Frequency Modulation)**.
- **Driving Problem Solved:** Replacing the *IMTS (Improved Mobile Telephone System)* which could only support ~20 users per city.
- **Key Limitation:** Voice-only, no encryption, no roaming, susceptible to eavesdropping, low capacity.
- **Standards Around the World:**
  - 🇺🇸 **AMPS** (Advanced Mobile Phone System) — USA, 824–894 MHz
  - 🇪🇺 **NMT** (Nordic Mobile Telephone) — Scandinavia, 450/900 MHz
  - 🇬🇧 **TACS** (Total Access Communication System) — UK
  - 🇯🇵 **NTT MCS-L1/L2** — Japan
  - 🇩🇪 **C-Netz** — Germany

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Analog Faded:** The cellular concept (proposed by AT&T Bell Labs, 1947–1977) could not scale with analog because of **co-channel interference limits** and **bandwidth inefficiency**. Spectrum is finite, demand is infinite.

---

### 🔹 Generation 2 (2G) — The Digital Revolution (1990s)

- **Operational Principle:** Digital transmission with **TDMA** (Time Division Multiple Access) or **CDMA** (Code Division Multiple Access).
- **Modulation:** **GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)** for GSM; **QPSK / BPSK** for IS-95.
- **Driving Problems Solved:** Capacity, voice quality, encryption, SMS, roaming.
- **Two Dominant Standards (A "World War of Standards"):**

| Feature | 🇪🇺 **GSM** | 🇺🇸 **IS-95 (cdmaOne)** |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple Access | TDMA / FDMA hybrid | CDMA / FDMA hybrid |
| Channel BW | 200 kHz | 1.25 MHz |
| Users per channel | 8 (full-rate) | ~ 64 (variable) |
| Frequency Band | 900 / 1800 MHz | 800 / 1900 MHz |
| Country Origin | Europe (ETSI) | USA (Qualcomm) |
| Global Adoption | **~ 90% of 2G users** | Limited (USA, Korea) |
| Handoff | Hard handoff | Soft handoff |

- **Japan (2G):** **PDC (Personal Digital Cellular)** — TDMA, 800/1500 MHz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** GSM won the global war because of its **SIM card modularity** (a user could switch phones easily) and **early international roaming agreements**. IS-95 was technically superior (CDMA capacity gain) but lost commercially.

---

### 🔹 Generation 2.5G & 2.75G — The Packet Overlay (Late 1990s–2000s)

- **GPRS (General Packet Radio Service):** Adds **packet-switched** data to GSM. Theoretical max **171.2 kbps**.
- **EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution):** Uses **8-PSK modulation** to push data to **384 kbps**.
- **IS-95B:** Packet data on CDMA, up to **115.2 kbps**.

---

### 🔹 Generation 3 (3G) — The Mobile Internet (2000s)

- **Operational Principle:** **Wideband CDMA (W-CDMA)** or **CDMA2000** — based on ITU's **IMT-2000** family.
- **Driving Problem Solved:** Broadband mobile data, video calling, mobile internet.
- **Standards Around the World:**
  - **UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System)** — Europe, uses W-CDMA, 5 MHz channel.
  - **CDMA2000 1xEV-DO** — USA, Korea, 1.25 MHz channel.
  - **TD-SCDMA** — China (unique to Chinese market).
  - **WCDMA** — Japan (NTT DoCoMo — pioneer of 3G with FOMA in 2001).

---

### 🔹 Generation 4 (4G) — All-IP, OFDMA (2010s)

- **Operational Principle:** **OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)** + **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)** + **all-IP packet core**.
- **Standards:**
  - **LTE (Long Term Evolution)** — 3GPP, dominant globally. **5/10/15/20 MHz** channels.
  - **WiMAX (IEEE 802.16e/m)** — Intel-led, deployed in Korea, India (BSNL), later faded.
  - **HSPA+ (3.5G bridge):** Evolution of UMTS, up to **42 Mbps** theoretical.
- **Modulation:** **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** (and 256-QAM in LTE-Advanced).
- **Switched-off 2G/3G networks** globally beginning 2022–2025.

---

### 🔹 Generation 5 (5G) — The IoT & Tactile Internet (2020s)

- **Operational Principle:** **5G NR (New Radio)** with flexible numerology, **mmWave (24–52 GHz)** + sub-6 GHz.
- **Three Use-Case Pillars (defined by ITU-R IMT-2020):**
  - **eMBB** (Enhanced Mobile Broadband) — up to **20 Gbps**
  - **URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency) — **< 1 ms** latency
  - **mMTC** (Massive Machine-Type Communications) — **10⁶ devices/km²**
- **Standards:** 3GPP Release 15 (NSA), Release 16 (SA), 17, 18.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Mobile Radio System Calculations**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reuse Ratio (Cluster Size N) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | i, j are shift parameters (integers, non-negative) |
| 2 | Total Channels per Cell | $C = \dfrac{M}{N}$ | M = total channels, N = cluster size |
| 3 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | D = distance between co-channel cells, R = cell radius |
| 4 | Co-channel Interference Ratio (S/I) | $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(Q)^n_0}{6}$ | n₀ = path loss exponent (typically 3–4) |
| 5 | Capacity (Erlangs) per Cell | $A = C \cdot a$ | a = offered traffic per user (Erlangs) |
| 6 | Trunking Efficiency (GOS formula) | $P(blocking) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$ | Erlang B formula |
| 7 | Spectral Efficiency (bps/Hz) | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Data Rate}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | LTE: up to 30 bps/Hz with 4×4 MIMO |
| 8 | 3G Minimum Bit Rate | $R_{3G} = 144 \text{ kbps (vehicular)}$ | IMT-2000 requirement |
| 9 | 4G Peak Data Rate (LTE) | $R_{4G} = 100 \text{ Mbps (high mobility)}$ | ITU IMT-Advanced |
| 10 | 5G Peak Data Rate | $R_{5G} \leq 20 \text{ Gbps}$ | eMBB target |
| 11 | GSM Frame Period | $T_{frame} = 4.615 \text{ ms}$ | 8 timeslots per frame |
| 12 | GSM Bit Period | $T_b = 3.69 \text{ µs}$ | Bit rate ≈ 270.833 kbps |
| 13 | Cell Radius (Okumura-Hata, urban) | $R = f(f_c, h_b, h_m)$ | f_c = carrier freq, h_b/h_m = base/mobile antenna heights |
| 14 | Number of Users per GSM Channel | $U_{GSM} = 8$ | Full-rate TDMA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pipe Rule Compliance:** The `\vert` or `\mid` symbol is intentionally used (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of the literal pipe `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Mobile Radio Standards |
|---|---|
| **Telecom Operators** | Spectrum auction, network planning, inter-standard roaming |
| **IoT & M2M** | Selecting 2G/3G/4G/5G/NB-IoT/LTE-M for device longevity |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | URLLC sub-1ms latency for V2X communication |
| **Smart Cities** | mMTC for sensor density (1M devices/km²) |
| **Disaster Management** | TETRA / satellite-mobile hybrid for first responders |
| **Defense (India)** | Make-in-India 4G/5G stack (BSNL, TCS, Tejas) — DRDO secure LTE |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Co-channel Reuse Ratio (Q) — From Cluster Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **mandatory for KTU 14-mark problems** involving cellular capacity / interference calculation. Marks are awarded for showing the geometric origin of $Q = \sqrt{3N}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry

Consider a hexagonal cellular layout. Let:
- $R$ = radius of a hexagonal cell
- $D$ = distance between the centers of two co-channel cells (cells that use the *same* set of frequency channels)

For a **regular hexagonal cluster of size N**, the co-channel cell distance is given by the geometry of the hexagonal lattice. Pick two adjacent co-channel cells separated by $i$ cells along one axis and $j$ cells along the rotated axis (60° apart).

### Step 2 — Express D in Terms of R, i, j

Using the geometry of a regular hexagon with side $R$:

$$
D^2 = (i \cdot \sqrt{3} R)^2 + (j \cdot \sqrt{3} R)^2 + 2 \cdot (i\sqrt{3}R)(j\sqrt{3}R)\cos(60°)
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- The "i-step" displacement is $i \cdot \sqrt{3} R$ (one hexagon side = R, full diagonal = $\sqrt{3} R$).
- The "j-step" displacement is $j \cdot \sqrt{3} R$.
- The angle between the two displacement vectors is **60°** (hexagonal interior angle of lattice axes).

### Step 3 — Simplify Using $\cos(60°) = 1/2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= 3R^2 i^2 + 3R^2 j^2 + 2 \cdot 3R^2 \cdot ij \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \\
D^2 &= 3R^2 i^2 + 3R^2 j^2 + 3R^2 ij \\
D^2 &= 3R^2 \left( i^2 + ij + j^2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Cluster Size Identity

For a hexagonal system, the **cluster size N is given by**:

$$
N = i^2 + ij + j^2
$$

This is a **fundamental KTU identity** — memorize it.

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 &= 3 R^2 N \\
\dfrac{D}{R} &= \sqrt{3N} \\
\boxed{Q} &= \sqrt{3N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $Q$ is the **co-channel reuse ratio** — a dimensionless number that quantifies how "isolated" co-channel cells are in space. A **larger Q means less interference** but **lower capacity** per area.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]:**
> A cellular system uses a cluster size of N = 7. The path loss exponent is n₀ = 4. Compute the Signal-to-Interference ratio (S/I) for the system. State the result in dB.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Calculate Q

$$
Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the S/I Formula (First-Tier Interference, 6 Co-channel Cells)

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{Q^{n_0}}{6} = \dfrac{(4.583)^4}{6}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4.583)^2 &= 21.00 \\
(4.583)^4 &= (21.00)^2 = 441 \\
\dfrac{S}{I} &= \dfrac{441}{6} = 73.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Decibels

$$
\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $S/I = 73.5$ (linear) $\approx 18.66$ dB. This is **acceptable for AMPS** (which requires ≥ 18 dB) but **marginal for GSM** (which requires ≥ 9 dB with frequency hopping).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Generation-Wise Comparative Study

| Parameter | 1G | 2G | 3G | 4G | 5G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | 1980s | 1990s | 2000s | 2010s | 2020s |
| **Signal Type** | Analog | Digital | Digital | Digital (all-IP) | Digital (cloud-native) |
| **Multiple Access** | FDMA | TDMA / CDMA | W-CDMA / CDMA2000 | OFDMA / SC-FDMA | OFDMA / NOMA / Grant-free |
| **Modulation** | FM | GMSK / QPSK | QPSK / 8-PSK | QPSK–256-QAM | Up to 1024-QAM |
| **Channel BW** | 30 kHz | 200 kHz (GSM) | 5 MHz | Up to 20 MHz | Up to 400 MHz (FR2) |
| **Peak Data Rate** | 2.4 kbps | 64 kbps | 2 Mbps | 1 Gbps | 20 Gbps |
| **Latency** | N/A | ~ 500 ms | ~ 100 ms | ~ 10 ms | < 1 ms |
| **Switching** | Circuit | Circuit + Packet (2.5G) | Packet + Circuit | All packet | All packet + slicing |
| **Handoff Type** | Hard | Hard (GSM) / Soft (IS-95) | Soft | Hard (X2/S1) | Conditional & beam-based |
| **Standards** | AMPS, NMT, TACS | GSM, IS-95, PDC | UMTS, CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA | LTE, WiMAX | 5G NR (NSA/SA) |
| **Dominant Region** | USA (AMPS) | Europe (GSM) | Japan / Korea | Global (LTE) | Korea / China / USA |
| **Spectrum** | < 1 GHz | 900/1800 MHz | 2 GHz | 700 MHz–2.6 GHz | sub-6 GHz + mmWave |
| **Core Innovation** | Cellular concept | Digital + SIM | Mobile broadband | IP + MIMO | Network slicing, NFV |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Generational Evolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[1G Analog 1980s AMPS NMT TACS] --> B[2G Digital 1990s GSM IS95 PDC]
    B --> C[2.5G GPRS EDGE Packet Data]
    C --> D[3G IMT2000 2000s UMTS CDMA2000]
    D --> E[3.5G HSPA HSPA+ 42 Mbps]
    E --> F[4G LTE 2010s OFDMA MIMO]
    F --> G[4.5G LTE Advanced Pro]
    G --> H[5G NR 2020s eMBB URLLC mMTC]
    H --> I[6G Research 2030 AI Native THz]
    A --> J[Voice Only]
    B --> K[SMS First Time]
    C --> L[Mobile Internet]
    D --> M[Video Calling]
    E --> N[App Economy]
    F --> O[Streaming HD]
    G --> P[IoT Foundation]
    H --> Q[Autonomous Systems]
    I --> R[Holographic Comm]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Regional Standard Adoption Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NA[North America]
        AMPS1[AMPS 1G]
        IS95[IS-95 2G]
        CDMA2K[CDMA2000 3G]
        LTE1[LTE 4G]
        NR1[5G NR]
        AMPS1 --> IS95 --> CDMA2K --> LTE1 --> NR1
    end
    subgraph EU[Europe]
        NMT1[NMT 1G]
        GSM1[GSM 2G]
        UMTS1[UMTS 3G]
        LTE2[LTE 4G]
        NR2[5G NR]
        NMT1 --> GSM1 --> UMTS1 --> LTE2 --> NR2
    end
    subgraph JP[Japan]
        MCSL1[NTT MCS 1G]
        PDC1[PDC 2G]
        FOMA1[FOMA WCDMA 3G]
        LTE3[LTE 4G]
        NR3[5G NR]
        MCSL1 --> PDC1 --> FOMA1 --> LTE3 --> NR3
    end
    subgraph KO[Korea]
        AMPS2[AMPS 1G]
        CDMA1[IS-95 CDMA 2G]
        CDMA3[CDMA2000 3G]
        WIMAX1[WiMAX 4G]
        NR4[5G NR]
        AMPS2 --> CDMA1 --> CDMA3 --> WIMAX1 --> NR4
    end
    NA --- EU
    EU --- JP
    JP --- KO
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: GSM TDMA Frame Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[GSM Hyperframe 2715648 frames 3h 28m 53s 760ms] --> F2[GSM Superframe 1326 frames 6.12s]
    F2 --> F3[GSM Multiframe Type 1 26 frames 120ms]
    F2 --> F4[GSM Multiframe Type 2 51 frames 235.4ms]
    F3 --> F5[GSM Frame 8 timeslots 4.615ms]
    F5 --> TS0[Timeslot 0]
    F5 --> TS1[Timeslot 1]
    F5 --> TS2[Timeslot 2]
    F5 --> TS3[Timeslot 3]
    F5 --> TS4[Timeslot 4]
    F5 --> TS5[Timeslot 5]
    F5 --> TS6[Timeslot 6]
    F5 --> TS7[Timeslot 7]
    F3 --> TCH[TCH Traffic Channels]
    F3 --> CCCH[CCCH Control Channels]
    F4 --> FACCH[FACCH Fast Associated]
    F4 --> SACCH[SACCH Slow Associated]
    F4 --> IDLE[IDLE Frames]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 4G LTE Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UE[User Equipment UE]
        UE1[Mobile Phone Modem]
        UE2[USIM Card]
        UE3[RF Transceiver]
    end
    subgraph RAN[Radio Access Network E-UTRAN]
        ENB1[eNodeB Cell A]
        ENB2[eNodeB Cell B]
        ENB3[eNodeB Cell C]
    end
    subgraph EPC[Evolved Packet Core]
        MME1[MME Mobility Management]
        SGW1[SGW Serving Gateway]
        PGW1[PGW Packet Gateway]
        HSS1[HSS Home Subscriber Server]
        PCRF1[PCRF Policy Charging]
    end
    subgraph EXT[External Networks]
        INT1[Internet]
        IMS1[IMS VoLTE]
        APP1[App Servers]
    end
    UE1 --> ENB1
    ENB1 <--> ENB2
    ENB2 <--> ENB3
    ENB1 --> MME1
    ENB1 --> SGW1
    MME1 --> HSS1
    SGW1 --> PGW1
    PGW1 --> INT1
    PGW1 --> IMS1
    PCRF1 --> PGW1
    HSS1 --> MME1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *system-level architecture*. The all-IP EPC architecture of LTE is a **favourite 7-mark descriptive question** topic, especially the role of MME, SGW, PGW, and the *absence of a circuit-switched domain* in 4G.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### ❓ Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: List any three key differences between 1G and 2G mobile communication systems.**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| # | Parameter | 1G | 2G |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Signal Type** | Analog (FM) | Digital (GMSK) |
| 2 | **Multiple Access** | FDMA only | TDMA / CDMA |
| 3 | **Services** | Voice only | Voice + SMS + low-rate data |
| 4 | **Capacity** | Low (small clusters) | High (frequency hopping, sectoring) |
| 5 | **Security** | None (eavesdropping easy) | Encrypted (A5/1, A5/2) |
| 6 | **Example** | AMPS, NMT | GSM, IS-95 |

> **Awarding:** 1 mark per valid difference, max 3.

---

### ❓ Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: What is the role of ITU in standardizing mobile radio systems? Name the IMT-2000 and IMT-Advanced visions.**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

- **Role of ITU:** The **International Telecommunication Union (ITU-R)** is the global coordinator that defines performance requirements, spectrum allocation, and roadmap for mobile generations. It does *not* design systems — it sets targets and accepts standards from regional bodies (3GPP, 3GPP2).
- **IMT-2000 (3G):** Specified minimum **144 kbps (vehicular)**, **384 kbps (pedestrian)**, **2 Mbps (indoor)**.
- **IMT-Advanced (4G):** Specified peak **1 Gbps (low mobility)**, **100 Mbps (high mobility)**, all-IP, OFDMA-based.
- **IMT-2020 (5G):** Three pillars — **eMBB, URLLC, mMTC**, targeting **20 Gbps peak, 1 ms latency, 10⁶ devices/km²**.

> **Awarding:** 1 mark for ITU role + 1 mark for IMT-2000 + 1 mark for IMT-Advanced.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 Question B-A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the evolution of mobile radio systems from 1G to 5G with emphasis on the multiple access technique, modulation scheme, and representative standard of each generation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare GSM and IS-95 (cdmaOne) in terms of multiple access, channel bandwidth, capacity, handoff mechanism, and country of origin. Why did GSM become the global 2G standard despite the technical superiority of CDMA? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Evolution 1G → 5G (7 Marks)

**1G (1 Mark):**
- **Era:** 1980s. **Multiple Access:** FDMA. **Modulation:** Analog FM. **Standard:** AMPS (USA), NMT (Europe), TACS (UK), NTT MCS-L1 (Japan). Voice-only, no data, no encryption. Limited by capacity and interference.

**2G (1 Mark):**
- **Era:** Early 1990s. **Multiple Access:** TDMA (GSM), CDMA (IS-95). **Modulation:** GMSK (GSM), QPSK (IS-95). **Standard:** GSM (Europe), IS-95 (USA), PDC (Japan). Digital, encrypted, SMS enabled, SIM card portability.

**2.5G / 2.75G (1 Mark):**
- **GPRS** (packet-switched overlay on GSM, 171 kbps), **EDGE** (8-PSK, 384 kbps), **IS-95B** (CDMA packet data). This was the **bridge to mobile internet** before 3G.

**3G (1 Mark):**
- **Era:** 2000s. **Multiple Access:** W-CDMA (UMTS), CDMA2000 1xEV-DO. **Modulation:** QPSK. **Standard:** UMTS, CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA. Driven by **IMT-2000** target. Mobile broadband, video calling.

**4G (1 Mark):**
- **Era:** 2010s. **Multiple Access:** OFDMA (downlink), SC-FDMA (uplink). **Modulation:** Up to 64-QAM (256-QAM in LTE-A). **Standard:** LTE (3GPP), WiMAX (IEEE 802.16e). **All-IP core (EPC)**. Up to 1 Gbps.

**5G (2 Marks):**
- **Era:** 2020s. **Multiple Access:** OFDMA + grant-free + NOMA. **Modulation:** Up to 1024-QAM. **Standard:** 5G NR (3GPP Release 15+). Three use cases: **eMBB (20 Gbps), URLLC (< 1 ms), mMTC (10⁶ devices/km²)**. Sub-6 GHz + mmWave (24–52 GHz). Network slicing, edge computing, cloud-native core (5GC).

### Part (b) — GSM vs IS-95 (7 Marks)

| Feature | GSM | IS-95 (cdmaOne) |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple Access | TDMA / FDMA hybrid | CDMA / FDMA hybrid |
| Channel Bandwidth | 200 kHz | 1.25 MHz |
| Users per Channel | 8 (full-rate) | ~ 64 (capacity depends on voice activity) |
| Modulation | GMSK | BPSK/QPSK |
| Handoff Type | Hard handoff (MAHO – Mobile-Assisted) | Soft handoff (make-before-break) |
| Country of Origin | Europe (ETSI) | USA (Qualcomm) |
| Voice Codec | RPE-LTP, later AMR | QCELP, EVRC |
| SIM Card | Yes (removable) | No (MIN-based, later R-UIM) |
| Global Adoption | ~ 90% of 2G | Limited (USA, Korea, parts of Latin America) |

**Why GSM won globally (3 Marks):**

1. **SIM Card Modularity** — users could change phones effortlessly; IS-95 phones were SIM-locked to the carrier.
2. **Roaming Agreements** — ETSI mandated open international roaming from day 1; CDMA operators were regional.
3. **Spectrum Efficiency in Practice** — although CDMA had a theoretical capacity advantage, GSM with **frequency hopping and sectoring** matched it in dense deployments.
4. **Operator Lobbying & Politics** — Europe deployed GSM as a *single common standard* across 18+ countries simultaneously, creating a massive device ecosystem economy of scale.
5. **Cost of Handsets** — GSM handset volumes were 10× higher, driving down per-unit cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often claim "CDMA has higher capacity than TDMA" without qualifying that this is true *only under perfect power control* with all users active simultaneously. GSM with **DTX (Discontinuous Transmission)** and **frequency hopping** closed the gap.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing "GSM = 3G" or confusing GSM with 3G. **GSM is 2G.** UMTS is 3G. KTU strictly deducts ½ mark for such misclassifications.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention **MAHO (Mobile-Assisted Handoff)** in GSM — this is a KTU-favoured keyword.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In evolution questions, missing **2.5G/2.75G (GPRS/EDGE)** — students jump from 2G to 3G, losing 1 mark.

---

### 📝 Question B-B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Draw the block diagram of a 4G LTE network showing UE, E-UTRAN, and EPC. Explain the role of MME, SGW, PGW, and HSS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hexagonal cellular system has cluster size N = 7. The total system bandwidth is 12.5 MHz, and the channel bandwidth is 30 kHz (FDMA, no sectoring). Compute:
   1. The total number of channels in the system.
   2. The number of channels per cell.
   3. The co-channel reuse ratio Q.
   4. The S/I ratio assuming n₀ = 4.
   **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

### Part (a) — 4G LTE Architecture (7 Marks)

**Diagram (3 Marks):** See Section 4.4 above (E-UTRAN + EPC + External Networks).

**Role of Key Components (4 Marks):**

- **UE (User Equipment):** Mobile phone + USIM. Contains the RF transceiver, baseband processor, and user subscription identity.
- **eNodeB (Evolved Node B):** The **only node in E-UTRAN**. Handles radio resource management, scheduling, handover, encryption, and IP header compression. Unlike 3G, there is no separate RNC (Radio Network Controller) — control is distributed to eNodeBs for lower latency.
- **MME (Mobility Management Entity):** The *control-plane brain*. Manages **attach/detach, tracking area updates, bearer establishment, authentication**, and selects the SGW/PGW for a user.
- **SGW (Serving Gateway):** The *local mobility anchor*. Routes user-plane packets, buffers downlink data during idle-to-active transitions, and acts as the local handover anchor between eNodeBs.
- **PGW (Packet Data Network Gateway):** The **border gateway** to the Internet / IMS. Assigns IP addresses, enforces QoS, performs charging, and provides packet filtering.
- **HSS (Home Subscriber Server):** The **master database** of subscribers. Stores user identity (IMSI), authentication keys, subscription profiles, and service authorizations.
- **PCRF (Policy and Charging Rules Function):** Decides **QoS policy and charging rules** for each bearer.

**Key Innovation of 4G:** The EPC is **all-IP, all-packet** — there is **no circuit-switched (CS) domain** in LTE. Even voice is carried as **VoLTE (Voice over LTE)** over IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem).

### Part (b) — Cellular Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Cluster size $N = 7$
- Total bandwidth $B = 12.5 \text{ MHz}$
- Channel bandwidth $B_c = 30 \text{ kHz}$
- Path loss exponent $n_0 = 4$

**Step 1 — Total channels in the system (1 Mark):**

$$
M = \dfrac{B}{B_c} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{30 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}} \approx 416.67
$$

We take $M = 416$ channels (integer; some channels are used as control channels in practice).

**Step 2 — Channels per cell (1 Mark):**

$$
C = \dfrac{M}{N} = \dfrac{416}{7} \approx 59.43
$$

So approximately **59 channels per cell** (1 Mark).

**Step 3 — Co-channel reuse ratio (2 Marks):**

$$
Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$

**Step 4 — S/I ratio (3 Marks):**

$$
\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{Q^{n_0}}{6} = \dfrac{(4.583)^4}{6}
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4.583)^2 &= 21.00 \\
(4.583)^4 &= (21.00)^2 = 441 \\
\dfrac{S}{I} &= \dfrac{441}{6} = 73.5
\end{aligned}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 10 \times 1.866 = 18.66 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B-B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to apply the **co-channel interference factor of 6** in the S/I formula. Many students write $S/I = Q^{n_0}$ which is wrong — it should be **divided by 6** (first-tier interference from 6 surrounding co-channel cells).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $N$ with $C$ in the question. Read carefully — "channels per **cell**" means $C = M/N$, not $M \times N$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $\sqrt{3N}$ as $\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{N}$ without showing the derivation — KTU awards full marks only if the hexagonal cluster geometry is referenced.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In architecture questions, students often forget that **eNodeB integrates both RNC and Node-B functions** of 3G. This integration is a **defining feature of 4G** and is worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this list — KTU 2024 Exam Day Quick Recall**

- ⭐ **1G** = **Analog + FDMA + FM** → Standards: AMPS, NMT, TACS, NTT MCS.
- ⭐ **2G** = **Digital + TDMA/CDMA + GMSK/QPSK** → GSM (200 kHz, 8 slots), IS-95 (1.25 MHz, soft handoff), PDC.
- ⭐ **2.5G** = GPRS (171 kbps, packet-switched); **2.75G** = EDGE (384 kbps, 8-PSK).
- ⭐ **3G** = **W-CDMA / CDMA2000** + UMTS (5 MHz); IMT-2000 target → 144 kbps / 384 kbps / 2 Mbps.
- ⭐ **4G** = **LTE / WiMAX** + **OFDMA + MIMO + all-IP EPC**; up to 1 Gbps, ~ 10 ms latency.
- ⭐ **5G** = **5G NR** + mmWave; eMBB (20 Gbps) + URLLC (1 ms) + mMTC (10⁶/km²).
- ⭐ **Cluster formula:** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ — pick valid (i, j) pairs: (1,0)→3, (1,1)→3, (2,0)→4, (2,1)→7, (3,0)→9, (2,2)→12, (3,1)→13, (3,2)→19, (4,0)→16, (4,1)→21.
- ⭐ **Reuse ratio:** $Q = \sqrt{3N}$.
- ⭐ **S/I formula:** $S/I = Q^{n_0} / 6$ (linear) → $10 \log_{10}$ for dB.
- ⭐ **AMPS minimum S/I:** ≥ **18 dB**; **GSM minimum S/I:** ≥ **9 dB** (with frequency hopping).
- ⭐ **GSM frame:** 4.615 ms, 8 timeslots, 26-frame multiframe; bit period 3.69 µs; gross bit rate 270.833 kbps.
- ⭐ **Why GSM won:** SIM portability + international roaming + European unified deployment + scale economics.
- ⭐ **Soft handoff** (IS-95/3G) = make-before-break; **Hard handoff** (GSM/4G) = break-before-make.
- ⭐ **ITU-R** = global roadmap; **3GPP** = GSM/UMTS/LTE/5G NR; **3GPP2** = CDMA2000 (defunct).
- ⭐ **4G EPC components:** MME (control plane) + SGW (local anchor) + PGW (Internet gateway) + HSS (subscriber DB) + PCRF (policy).
- ⭐ **5G NR numerology:** subcarrier spacing scales 15 kHz × 2ⁿ; max 240 kHz in FR1.
- ⭐ **India's mobile journey:** 1G (1995, Modi's Telstra) → 2G (1996, Airtel) → 3G (2008) → 4G (2012, Reliance Jio revolution) → 5G (2022, Airtel + Jio).
- ⭐ **Japan 3G pioneer:** NTT DoCoMo FOMA (May 2001) — world's first W-CDMA commercial deployment.
- ⭐ **Korea's 5G leadership:** First nationwide 5G launch (April 2019, SK Telecom/KT/LG U+).

> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** KTU loves asking **comparison tables** (GSM vs IS-95, 3G vs 4G, EPC vs 5GC). Practice drawing these in **under 5 minutes** with the right column headers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Wireless Communication System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Wireless Communication System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Wireless Communication Systems**, the term "Types" refers to the **fundamental classification of communication links based on the directionality of signal flow** between the transmitting and receiving entities. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCPT502, Module 1), wireless communication systems are broadly classified into three primary categories based on the duplexing/operational mode of data exchange:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Wireless Communication System** is a system that enables the transfer of information between two or more points (transmitter and receiver) without the use of a physical wired medium, relying instead on electromagnetic waves propagating through free space. The **type** of the system is determined by whether communication is unidirectional or bidirectional, and whether the bidirectional flow can be simultaneous.

The three canonical types are:

1. **Simplex (SX) Wireless Communication**
2. **Half-Duplex (HDX) Wireless Communication**
3. **Full-Duplex (FDX) Wireless Communication**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Let us use a simple **walkie-talkie / road analogy** to make this crystal clear:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Simplex: A One-Way Road**
> Imagine a **FM Radio broadcast** from All India Radio (AIR) to your car. The radio station *transmits*, and your car *receives*. **You cannot transmit back to AIR using your car radio.** The road has traffic flowing in **only one direction**. This is *Simplex*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Half-Duplex: A Narrow Bridge with One-Lane Traffic**
> Imagine a **walkie-talkie (Handheld Radio)**. When person A presses the "Push-to-Talk" button, person B can hear. But while A is talking, B **cannot** talk back. They must take turns. This is *Half-Duplex* — communication is **two-way**, but **only one way at a time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Full-Duplex: A Two-Lane Highway**
> Imagine a **mobile phone call**. You speak into the microphone while simultaneously hearing the other person speak. The signal flows in **both directions at the same time**. This is *Full-Duplex*.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Simplex:** A single channel of $S$ bandwidth used for one-way transmission. Channel utilization = **50%**.
- **Half-Duplex:** A single shared channel used bidirectionally. Maximum channel utilization = **50%** (at any instant).
- **Full-Duplex:** Either two separate physical channels or a single channel with echo cancellation. Channel utilization = **100%**.

## 1.4 Operational Spectrum Summary

| Type | Direction | Simultaneous? | Channel Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One-way | No (only TX or RX) | **Unidirectional** |
| **Half-Duplex** | Two-way | **No** (alternating) | Shared |
| **Full-Duplex** | Two-way | **Yes** | Dual or echo-cancelled |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Quick-Fact:** In modern cellular systems like **4G LTE** and **5G NR**, the standard operating mode is **Full-Duplex** (specifically **Frequency Division Duplex — FDD** or **Time Division Duplex — TDD**), as it doubles the spectral efficiency compared to Simplex.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Type

### 2.1.1 Simplex Wireless Communication System

- **Operational Logic:** The transmitter and receiver operate on **pre-assigned, distinct roles**. The transmitter sends an RF modulated signal via an antenna; the receiver is locked to that frequency and demodulates it.
- **Why this design?** Simplex is used when **feedback from the receiver is not required** or is impractical (e.g., broadcasting to millions of users).
- **Examples:**
  - **AM/FM Radio Broadcasting** (88 MHz – 108 MHz for FM)
  - **Television Broadcasting** (VHF/UHF bands, 54–890 MHz)
  - **Paging Systems** (paging towers → pagers)
  - **GPS Satellites** (downlink only to GPS receivers)
  - **Beacon Transmitters** in aviation

**Key Limitation:** The receiver has **no return path** to acknowledge or request data. There is no **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** possible.

### 2.1.2 Half-Duplex Wireless Communication System

- **Operational Logic:** Both devices have a transceiver, but they **share a single frequency channel**. A control mechanism (often a "Push-to-Talk" PTT switch or a TDD scheduler) ensures that only one device transmits at any given instant.
- **Why this design?** Cost-effective when **wireless spectrum is expensive** or when traffic is bursty (e.g., short voice exchanges).
- **Examples:**
  - **Walkie-Talkies / Handheld Radios** (e.g., police, military)
  - **Citizen Band (CB) Radio** (around **27 MHz**)
  - **Ham Radio (Amateur Radio)**
  - **Two-way Radio Trunking Systems** used in taxi dispatch
  - **Early packet radio networks** (ALOHA — half-duplex in initial versions)

**Key Limitation:** The "turn-around time" between TX and RX (often 100–300 ms in commercial radios) causes perceptible **latency**, and overall **throughput is halved** compared to full-duplex.

### 2.1.3 Full-Duplex Wireless Communication System

- **Operational Logic:** Two devices can transmit and receive **simultaneously**. This is achieved using one of two engineering strategies:

**Strategy A — Frequency Division Duplex (FDD):**
- Two **separate frequency bands** are allocated — one for uplink (UL), one for downlink (DL).
- A device called a **duplexer** (a combination of bandpass filters) allows simultaneous TX and RX on the same antenna.
- A **guard band** separates the two frequencies to prevent interference.
- Used in **2G GSM, 3G WCDMA, 4G LTE (paired spectrum)**.

**Strategy B — Time Division Duplex (TDD):**
- A **single frequency band** is shared, with TX and RX occurring in **alternating time slots** so rapidly that to the user, it appears simultaneous.
- Used in **4G LTE (unpaired spectrum), 5G NR, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth**.

**Strategy C — Full-Duplex Radio (In-Band Full Duplex — IBFD):**
- Theoretically, both TX and RX on the **same frequency at the same time** using **self-interference cancellation (SIC)** circuits. This is an active research area in 5G/6G.

- **Examples:**
  - **Mobile Phone Calls (4G/5G)**
  - **Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)**
  - **Bluetooth (Classic & LE)**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation for the topic "Types of Wireless Communication Systems." **Memorize the spectral efficiency relationship.**

| Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spectral Efficiency (Simplex)** | $\eta_{SX} = \dfrac{R_b}{B}$ | $R_b$ = bit rate, $B$ = bandwidth | $\text{bits/s/Hz}$ |
| **Spectral Efficiency (Half-Duplex)** | $\eta_{HDX} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{R_b}{B}$ | One direction at a time → half throughput | $\text{bits/s/Hz}$ |
| **Spectral Efficiency (Full-Duplex FDD)** | $\eta_{FDX} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{R_b}{B_{total}}$ | Two channels → double of simplex | $\text{bits/s/Hz}$ |
| **Shannon Capacity (any link)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S$ = signal power, $N$ = noise power | $\text{bits/second}$ |
| **FDD Guard Band Requirement** | $B_{guard} \geq 2 \cdot \Delta f_{Doppler}$ | $\Delta f_{Doppler} = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Hz |
| **Total FDD Bandwidth** | $B_{total} = B_{UL} + B_{guard} + B_{DL}$ | UL = Uplink, DL = Downlink | Hz |
| **TDD Frame Utilization** | $\eta_{TDD} = \dfrac{T_{TX} + T_{RX}}{T_{frame}}$ | $T_{frame}$ = total frame duration | dimensionless |
| **PTT Turnaround Latency** | $T_{turn} \approx \dfrac{1}{B}$ | B = channel bandwidth | seconds |
| **Noise Power** | $N = kTB$ | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (Boltzmann) | Watts |
| **Free-Space Path Loss** | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength | dimensionless (ratio) |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoided:** Note the use of `\vert` is not needed here, but I have used `\dfrac` and `\left( \right)` for proper sizing. In your answer sheets, always use `\cdot` for multiplication to avoid confusion with variable $x$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Simplex systems** form the backbone of mass media distribution (TV, radio, satellite TV like **DTH — Direct-to-Home**).
- **Half-duplex systems** are still the standard in **public safety** (police, fire, ambulance), **aviation communications**, and **maritime** services due to their **simplicity, low cost, and long battery life**.
- **Full-duplex systems** are mandatory for high-throughput **mobile broadband (4G/5G)** and **VoIP** (WhatsApp calls, Zoom), where bidirectional conversational latency must be below **150 ms** (ITU-T G.114 recommendation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Comparison of Spectral Efficiency Across the Three Types

We will derive the **maximum achievable data rate per unit bandwidth** for each communication type, assuming a system with total available bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$.

**Starting Point — Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem:**

For a single channel of bandwidth $B$ over an AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) link, the maximum data rate is given by:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

where:
- $C$ is the **channel capacity** in bits per second
- $B$ is the **bandwidth** in Hertz
- $S$ is the **average signal power** at the receiver
- $N = kTB$ is the **noise power** in Watts
- $k$ is **Boltzmann's constant** $= 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- $T$ is the **absolute temperature** in Kelvin

### 3.1.1 Case 1: Simplex System

In a Simplex link, the entire bandwidth $B$ is dedicated to **one-way** transmission from the source (Alice) to the destination (Bob).

$$
\boxed{\;C_{SX} = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\;}
$$

The spectral efficiency becomes:

$$
\eta_{SX} = \frac{C_{SX}}{B} = \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bits/s/Hz)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The full bandwidth contributes to the unidirectional data rate.

### 3.1.2 Case 2: Half-Duplex System

In Half-Duplex, the same channel bandwidth $B$ is **time-shared** between Alice and Bob. Over a long observation window, each user effectively gets only **half** the time, hence half the average capacity.

Let the duty cycle for each direction be $50\%$. The average data rate per direction is:

$$
C_{HDX, \, UL} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{HDX, \, UL} = \frac{B}{2} \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\;}
$$

The bidirectional **sum-rate** equals:

$$
C_{HDX, \, total} = C_{HDX, \, UL} + C_{HDX, \, DL} = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** The total bidirectional rate matches Simplex, but **per-user** throughput is **halved**.

### 3.1.3 Case 3: Full-Duplex FDD System

In FDD, **two independent channels** of bandwidth $B_{UL}$ and $B_{DL}$ (typically $B_{UL} = B_{DL} = B/2$ if the total is $B$, excluding a small guard band) operate concurrently. Each direction achieves:

$$
C_{FDX, \, UL} = B_{UL} \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S_{UL}}{N_{UL}}\right)
$$

$$
C_{FDX, \, DL} = B_{DL} \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S_{DL}}{N_{DL}}\right)
$$

Assuming $B_{UL} = B_{DL} = B/2$ and symmetric SNR:

$$
C_{FDX, \, total} = 2 \cdot \frac{B}{2} \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{FDX, \, total} = B \cdot \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\;}
$$

**Critical Insight:** The total capacity of an FDD full-duplex system with total bandwidth $B$ is **twice** that of a half-duplex system using the same total bandwidth.

### 3.1.4 Summary Comparison Table

| Type | Bidirectional Capacity | Per-User Capacity (Symmetric) |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | $C_{SX} = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | $C_{SX}$ (only one user) |
| **Half-Duplex** | $B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | $\frac{B}{2} \log_2(1+S/N)$ |
| **Full-Duplex (FDD)** | $2B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | $B \log_2(1+S/N)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway:** Full-duplex **doubles** the bidirectional capacity of a half-duplex system, and provides **four times** the per-user capacity when compared head-to-head.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Question:** A wireless system has a total available spectrum of $B = 200$ kHz. The received SNR is $30$ dB. Compute the capacity for: (a) Simplex, (b) Half-Duplex, (c) Full-Duplex (FDD, symmetric). Assume $B_{guard} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
30 = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \implies \frac{S}{N} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the unit Shannon capacity:**

$$
C_{unit} = \log_{2}(1 + 1000) = \log_{2}(1001)
$$

Using $\log_{2}(1001) \approx \log_{2}(1024) - \text{correction} = 10 - 0.034 = 9.966$ bits/s/Hz

(We use the approximation $\log_{2}(1001) \approx 9.967$)

**Step 3 — Apply to each case:**

For (a) **Simplex**, total rate is:

$$
C_{SX} = 200{,}000 \times 9.967 = 1{,}993{,}400 \;\text{bits/s} \approx 1.993 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

For (b) **Half-Duplex** (per direction):

$$
C_{HDX, UL} = \frac{200{,}000}{2} \times 9.967 = 996{,}700 \;\text{bits/s} \approx 0.997 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

For (c) **Full-Duplex (FDD)**, total bidirectional:

$$
C_{FDX, total} = 2 \times 100{,}000 \times 9.967 = 1{,}993{,}400 \;\text{bits/s} \approx 1.993 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** Full-duplex achieves the **same total throughput as simplex** but supports **bidirectional** flow, while half-duplex halves the per-direction rate.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Wireless Communication Systems

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the three types of wireless systems, including their key components and signal flow directions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Wireless Communication Systems]:::root
    A --> B[Simplex SX]:::leaf
    A --> C[Half Duplex HDX]:::leaf
    A --> D[Full Duplex FDX]:::leaf

    B --> B1[Transmitter Only]:::comp
    B --> B2[Receiver Only]:::comp
    B --> B3[FM Radio, TV, GPS]:::example

    C --> C1[Transceiver with PTT]:::comp
    C --> C2[Shared Single Channel]:::comp
    C --> C3[Walkie Talkie, CB Radio]:::example

    D --> D1[FDD Two Frequency Bands]:::comp
    D --> D2[TDD Time Slot Alternation]:::comp
    D --> D3[IBFD with SIC]:::comp
    D --> D4[Mobile Phone, Wi Fi, 5G]:::example

    B1 -- RF Signal Unidirectional --> B2
    C1 -- Alternate TX or RX --> C2
    D1 -- Simultaneous via Duplexer --> D4
    D2 -- Rapid Time Switch --> D4
    D3 -- Cancels Self Interference --> D4

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef comp fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef example fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: FDD vs TDD Full-Duplex Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FDD[FDD Full Duplex]
        F1[Base Station TX]:::tx -- Frequency f1 --> F2[Duplexer]:::dup
        F2 -- Antenna --> F3[Mobile User RX]:::rx
        F3 -- Frequency f2 --> F2
        F2 --> F1
    end

    subgraph TDD[TDD Full Duplex]
        T1[Base Station]:::tx -- Slot 1 TX, Slot 2 RX --> T2[Switch]:::dup
        T2 -- Single Antenna --> T3[Mobile User]:::rx
    end

    classDef tx fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef dup fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow (Block-Level)

The following block diagram illustrates the **signal path architecture** for a generic **Half-Duplex** wireless system, including the critical PTT (Push-to-Talk) control logic:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Voice Input] --> B[Microphone]
    B --> C[Speech Codec]
    C --> D[Modulator BPSK or QPSK]
    D --> E[RF Upconverter]
    E --> F[Power Amplifier]
    F --> G[Duplexer Switch in TX Mode]
    G --> H[Antenna]
    H -. Electromagnetic Wave .-> I[Remote Antenna]
    I --> J[LNA Low Noise Amplifier]
    J --> K[RF Downconverter]
    K --> L[Demodulator]
    L --> M[Speech Decoder]
    M --> N[Speaker Output]

    O[PTT Controller Microcontroller] -.-> G
    O -. TX or RX Select .-> G

    classDef audio fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class G,H,I,O ctrl
```
