<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define Simplex Communication. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] [CO1, Remember]

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Simplex communication is a **unidirectional mode** of wireless communication in which data flows in **only one direction** — from a transmitter to a receiver. The receiver has **no capability to transmit back** to the source on the same channel.
> 
> **Examples:** FM radio broadcast, television broadcast, GPS satellite downlink, and paging systems. The channel is utilized **100% of the time** for one-way information transfer, but bidirectional exchange is not possible.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Half-Duplex and Full-Duplex communication with one example each. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] [CO1, Understand]

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Direction of flow** | Two-way, but **one at a time** | Two-way, **simultaneously** |
> | **Channel usage** | Single shared channel | Two channels (FDD) or TDD slots |
> | **Example** | Walkie-talkie (Push-to-Talk) | Mobile phone call on 4G/5G |
> 
> In half-duplex, a "turn-around" delay occurs when switching between transmit and receive, whereas full-duplex enables continuous bidirectional flow using a duplexer or TDD scheduling.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **Q. (a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the three types of wireless communication systems. Compare their **spectral efficiency** and **bidirectional capacity**. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] [CO1, Understand + Apply]
>
> **Q. (b)** A wireless channel has $B = 5$ MHz bandwidth and received SNR of $20$ dB. Calculate the Shannon capacity. If the system is upgraded from half-duplex to full-duplex (FDD, symmetric), what is the **increase in bidirectional capacity**? (7 marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] [CO2, Apply + Analyze]

#### Model Solution for Q. A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark): Definition and block diagram of Simplex**

Simplex uses a single transmitter and single receiver. The signal flows from TX to RX only.

```
[Source] → [TX] ~~RF~~→ [RX] → [Destination]
```

**Step 2 (1 mark): Definition and block diagram of Half-Duplex**

Half-duplex uses **transceivers** at both ends sharing a single channel, controlled by a PTT switch or TDD logic.

**Step 3 (1 mark): Definition and block diagram of Full-Duplex (FDD/TDD)**

Full-duplex uses **duplexers** (FDD) or **fast TDD switches** to achieve simultaneous TX and RX.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Spectral Efficiency Comparison**

Using Shannon's theorem, with $B$ total bandwidth and SNR $\gamma = S/N$:

- Simplex: $C_{SX} = B \log_2(1 + \gamma)$
- Half-Duplex: $C_{HDX, UL} = \frac{B}{2} \log_2(1 + \gamma)$
- Full-Duplex (FDD): $C_{FDX, total} = 2B \log_2(1 + \gamma)$

**Step 5 (2 marks): Bidirectional Capacity Comparison & Examples**

| Type | Per-User Rate | Total Bidirectional Rate | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simplex | $B \log_2(1+\gamma)$ | $B \log_2(1+\gamma)$ | FM Radio |
| Half-Duplex | $\frac{B}{2} \log_2(1+\gamma)$ | $B \log_2(1+\gamma)$ | Walkie-Talkie |
| Full-Duplex | $B \log_2(1+\gamma)$ | $2B \log_2(1+\gamma)$ | Mobile Phone |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Full-duplex is the most spectrally efficient, providing double the bidirectional throughput.

#### Model Solution for Q. A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark): Convert SNR from dB to linear**

$$
20 \text{ dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \implies \frac{S}{N} = 10^{2} = 100
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Apply Shannon's formula**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(101)
$$

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658 \;\text{bits/s/Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C \approx 5 \times 10^{6} \times 6.658 = 33.29 \;\text{Mbps}\;}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Half-duplex capacity (per direction)**

$$
C_{HDX, UL} = \frac{C}{2} = \frac{33.29}{2} = 16.645 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Full-duplex capacity (FDD, symmetric) and increase**

$$
C_{FDX, total} = 2 \cdot C = 66.58 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Increase in bidirectional capacity:**

$$
\Delta C = C_{FDX, total} - C_{HDX, total} = 66.58 - 33.29 = 33.29 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Final Answer:** The bidirectional capacity **doubles** (an increase of **33.29 Mbps**) when upgrading from half-duplex to full-duplex.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **Q. (a)** Explain the operation of **Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD)** and **Time Division Duplexing (TDD)** in wireless systems. Mention the role of the **duplexer** and **guard band**. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] [CO1, Understand]
>
> **Q. (b)** A half-duplex system uses a single channel of $B = 1$ MHz with SNR $= 15$ dB. Calculate (i) the **maximum data rate per direction** and (ii) the **total bidirectional rate**. Compare with a full-duplex FDD system having two symmetric channels of $0.5$ MHz each with the same SNR. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] [CO2, Apply + Analyze]

#### Model Solution for Q. B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks): FDD explanation**

In **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD)**, two distinct frequency bands are assigned to uplink and downlink. The transmitter and receiver operate concurrently using a **duplexer** — a passive RF device containing two bandpass filters that isolate the TX and RX signals at the antenna. A **guard band** (typically a few hundred kHz to MHz) is placed between the UL and DL frequencies to prevent mutual interference.

**Step 2 (2 marks): TDD explanation**

In **Time Division Duplex (TDD)**, both uplink and downlink share the **same frequency band** but transmit in **alternating time slots** (e.g., 1 ms UL slot followed by 1 ms DL slot). The switching is so rapid that users perceive simultaneous communication. TDD is preferred for **asymmetric traffic** (e.g., video streaming where DL > UL).

**Step 3 (2 marks): Role of Duplexer and Guard Band**

- **Duplexer:** Allows simultaneous connection of TX and RX to a single antenna without the strong transmitted signal leaking into and damaging the sensitive receiver frontend.
- **Guard Band:** A small unused frequency slice between UL and DL that absorbs out-of-band emissions and prevents adjacent-channel interference.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Examples**

FDD → 2G GSM, 3G WCDMA, 4G LTE (paired). TDD → 4G TD-LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi.

#### Model Solution for Q. B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark): Convert SNR**

$$
15 \text{ dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \implies \frac{S}{N} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.623
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Half-duplex per-direction rate**

$$
C_{HDX, UL} = \frac{B}{2} \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N) = \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{2} \cdot \log_2(32.623)
$$

$$
\log_2(32.623) = \frac{\log_{10}(32.623)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.5134}{0.3010} \approx 5.027 \;\text{bits/s/Hz}
$$

$$
C_{HDX, UL} \approx 500{,}000 \times 5.027 = 2.5135 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Total half-duplex bidirectional rate**

$$
C_{HDX, total} = 2 \times 2.5135 = 5.027 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Full-duplex FDD per-direction rate**

For each $0.5$ MHz channel:

$$
C_{FDX, UL} = 0.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(32.623) = 500{,}000 \times 5.027 = 2.5135 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

Same for DL.

**Step 5 (1 mark): Total full-duplex FDD bidirectional rate**

$$
C_{FDX, total} = 2 \times 2.5135 = 5.027 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

**Comparison & Conclusion (1 mark):** Both systems give the same total bidirectional rate of **5.027 Mbps** under symmetric conditions. However, full-duplex FDD uses **two separate frequency bands** (avoiding turn-around delay) and is more spectrally efficient in terms of **per-user latency**, while half-duplex uses only one band.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Simplex with Half-Duplex:** A walkie-talkie is **Half-Duplex**, NOT simplex. A TV broadcast is **Simplex**, not half-duplex. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in FDD:** When computing total bidirectional capacity for an FDD full-duplex system, students often forget to multiply by **2** (one for UL, one for DL). Always show the step explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion of SNR:** SNR in dB must be converted using $S/N = 10^{(SNR_{dB}/10)}$, **not** $10^{(SNR_{dB}/20)}$. The latter is for voltage ratios and is a **classic trap**.
> 4. **Skipping the unit:** Always write "bits/s" or "bps" after the final capacity value. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **No block diagram in 7-mark questions:** A question on "types of wireless systems" without a block diagram is treated as incomplete. Always draw at least a **functional block diagram**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Types of Wireless Communication System":**

- ⭐ **Three Types:** Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex (FDD or TDD).
- ⭐ **Simplex** = One-way only. Examples: **FM Radio, TV, GPS, Paging**.
- ⭐ **Half-Duplex** = Two-way, but **one at a time**, using PTT. Examples: **Walkie-Talkie, CB Radio, Ham Radio**.
- ⭐ **Full-Duplex FDD** = Two separate frequency bands + **duplexer** + **guard band**. Examples: **2G GSM, 3G, 4G LTE paired**.
- ⭐ **Full-Duplex TDD** = Single band, alternating **time slots**. Examples: **5G NR, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth**.
- ⭐ **Key Equation:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — applies to each individual channel.
- ⭐ **Spectral Efficiency:** Full-duplex doubles the bidirectional capacity of half-duplex over the same total bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Per-user capacity of half-duplex is half** of full-duplex.
- ⭐ **Boltzmann's constant** $k = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — used for noise power $N = kTB$.
- ⭐ **Modern cellular = Full-Duplex** (both FDD and TDD variants deployed).
- ⭐ **Push-to-Talk (PTT)** is the defining feature of half-duplex walkie-talkies.
- ⭐ **Duplexer** is the device that enables simultaneous TX/RX on a single antenna in FDD.
- ⭐ **Guard band** prevents interference between UL and DL in FDD.
- ⭐ **Self-Interference Cancellation (SIC)** is the key technology for In-Band Full-Duplex (IBFD) — a 5G/6G research area.
- ⭐ **Latency requirement** for conversational voice: $< 150$ ms (ITU-T G.114).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Common Wireless System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Common Wireless System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Comparison of Common Wireless Systems"?

In the context of wireless communication engineering, the **Comparison of Common Wireless Systems** refers to the systematic analytical study of cellular and non-cellular wireless standards — spanning **1G through 5G**, along with short-range systems like **Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and ZigBee** — evaluated against unified technical parameters such as **multiple access scheme, modulation, bandwidth, data rate, frequency band, switching technique, and application domain**.

This comparison is essential because no single wireless standard can optimally satisfy all use-cases (voice, data, IoT, low-latency, broadband). Engineers must therefore understand the **design trade-offs** (coverage vs. capacity, speed vs. mobility, power vs. complexity) that distinguish one generation or standard from another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a *high-frequency Module 1 question*. Students are expected to produce **comparison tables** and explain the **evolution rationale** (why each generation was introduced, what limitation it overcame).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Transportation Evolution" View

Imagine wireless generations as **modes of transportation**:

| Wireless Generation | Transportation Analogy | Limitation It Overcame |
|---|---|---|
| 1G (Analog Cellular) | A **bicycle** — works, but only for short distances, rough ride | Wired telephones were immobile |
| 2G (GSM/Digital) | A **car** — comfortable, reliable, but limited lanes | Poor voice quality, no security, no data |
| 3G (UMTS) | A **highway** — faster, supports more "vehicles" (data services) | No mobile internet, slow data |
| 4G (LTE) | An **expressway** — broadband on the move | Need for high-speed mobile broadband |
| 5G (NR) | A **multi-lane smart highway** with **autonomous lanes** for IoT, low-latency vehicles, and broadband | Latency, massive device density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Each generation did NOT *replace* the previous one; rather, it **complemented** it for a different application niche. For example, **2G/3G still power IoT and rural voice networks** alongside 5G.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need to Compare?

A wireless system is defined by **six fundamental design choices**:

1. **Frequency Band** — Propagation characteristics (path loss, penetration)
2. **Multiple Access Technique** — How users share the spectrum (FDMA/TDMA/CDMA/OFDMA/NOMA)
3. **Modulation Scheme** — Spectral efficiency (BPSK → 256-QAM)
4. **Duplexing Method** — FDD vs. TDD
5. **Switching Technique** — Circuit vs. Packet
6. **Network Architecture** — Circuit-switched core vs. All-IP packet core

Comparing common wireless systems on these axes reveals the **engineering philosophy** behind each standard.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory aid — "FT-MDS-NA":** Frequency, Technique (multiple access), Modulation, Duplexing, Switching, Network Architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Comparative Parameters Explained

### 🔹 Parameter 1: Frequency Band
Different bands have different **propagation characteristics**:
- **Sub-1 GHz (e.g., 700–900 MHz):** Excellent building penetration, long range → used for **coverage** (rural 4G/5G).
- **1–6 GHz (Sub-6 GHz):** Balanced coverage and capacity → main **5G band**.
- **mmWave (24–52 GHz):** Massive bandwidth but poor penetration → used for **dense urban hotspots** (5G eMBB).
- **Unlicensed ISM bands (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz):** Used for **Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee**.

### 🔹 Parameter 2: Multiple Access Technique
This governs **how multiple users share the same spectrum**.

| Technique | Full Form | Principle | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FDMA** | Frequency Division Multiple Access | Each user gets a unique frequency band | 1G (AMPS) |
| **TDMA** | Time Division Multiple Access | Each user gets a unique time slot per frame | 2G (GSM) |
| **CDMA** | Code Division Multiple Access | Each user gets a unique spreading code | 3G (IS-95, WCDMA) |
| **OFDMA** | Orthogonal FDMA | Users assigned subcarriers + time slots | 4G (LTE), 5G (NR) |
| **NOMA** | Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access | Power-domain superposition + SIC | 5G Advanced (research) |

### 🔹 Parameter 3: Modulation Scheme
Modulation determines **bits per symbol** and thus **spectral efficiency**.

- 1G → **FM** (analog)
- 2G (GSM) → **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)
- 3G (UMTS) → **QPSK / 16-QAM**
- 4G (LTE) → **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM**
- 5G (NR) → **Up to 256-QAM** (with 1024-QAM in 5G-Advanced)

### 🔹 Parameter 4: Duplexing
- **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex):** Separate uplink & downlink frequencies → 2G/3G/4G macro cells.
- **TDD (Time Division Duplex):** Same frequency, time-shared → 4G/5G small cells, Wi-Fi.

### 🔹 Parameter 5: Switching Technique
- **Circuit Switching:** Dedicated channel for entire call duration → 1G, 2G voice.
- **Packet Switching:** Data broken into packets → 3G and beyond (HSPA), 4G, 5G.
- **Hybrid (2.5G/2.75G GPRS/EDGE):** Packet-switched data over circuit-switched voice.

### 🔹 Parameter 6: Network Architecture
- 1G/2G: **BSC + MSC** hierarchical
- 3G: **RNC + Node B** (UMTS)
- 4G: **Flat architecture — eNodeB + EPC (Evolved Packet Core)**
- 5G: **Service-Based Architecture (SBA) — gNB + 5GC** with **network slicing**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas govern the *performance* of the systems being compared.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon Channel Capacity | bits/s |
| 2 | $\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \log_2 M$ | Spectral Efficiency (M-ary modulation) | bits/s/Hz |
| 3 | $L_{fs} = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-Space Path Loss (dB) | dB |
| 4 | $N = \frac{B}{\Delta f} \cdot T_s$ | Number of OFDM subcarriers ($B$ = bandwidth) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_{\text{peak}} = N_{sub} \cdot N_{sym/sec} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot \nu$ | Peak LTE/5G data rate | bits/s |
| 6 | $G_p = \frac{BW_{RF}}{BW_{\text{info}}}$ | Processing Gain (CDMA spread spectrum) | dB |

**Where:**
- $C$ = Channel capacity, $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz), SNR = Signal-to-Noise Ratio
- $R_b$ = Bit rate, $M$ = Constellation size (e.g., 64-QAM → $M = 64$)
- $d_{km}$ = Distance in km, $f_{MHz}$ = Frequency in MHz
- $N_{sub}$ = Number of subcarriers, $N_{sym/sec}$ = Symbols/sec, $\nu$ = MIMO streams
- $BW_{RF}$ = Spread bandwidth, $BW_{\text{info}}$ = Information bandwidth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare, ALWAYS include a numerical column for *peak data rate*. A common mistake is to omit units (Mbps vs. Gbps).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This comparison is **not academic** — it directly drives:
- **Network planning** (which band to license for which region)
- **Device chipset design** (a 5G phone must fallback to 4G/3G/2G)
- **Handover algorithm design** (inter-RAT — Radio Access Technology — handovers)
- **Spectrum auction policy** (regulators compare efficiency before auctioning bands)
- **Vertical industry use-cases** (autonomous cars need 5G URLLC; smart meters use NB-IoT on 4G)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Comparison Table — Generations of Cellular Wireless Systems

Below is the **exhaustive KTU-standard comparison matrix** (memorize this for direct 14-mark questions).

| Parameter | **1G** | **2G (GSM)** | **3G (UMTS/WCDMA)** | **4G (LTE)** | **5G (NR)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Period** | 1980s | 1990s | 2000s | 2010s | 2020s |
| **Signal Type** | Analog | Digital | Digital | Digital (All-IP) | Digital (All-IP) |
| **Multiple Access** | **FDMA** | **TDMA + FDMA** | **CDMA** (WCDMA) | **OFDMA** (DL) / SC-FDMA (UL) | **OFDMA** + flexible numerology |
| **Modulation** | FM | GMSK | QPSK, 16-QAM | QPSK → 64-QAM | QPSK → 256-QAM |
| **Duplexing** | FDD | FDD | FDD | FDD & TDD | FDD & TDD |
| **Switching** | Circuit | Circuit (voice) | Circuit + Packet | **Packet only** | **Packet only** |
| **Frequency Band** | 800–900 MHz | 900 / 1800 MHz | 2.1 GHz | 700 MHz – 2.6 GHz (and 3.5 GHz) | Sub-6 GHz + mmWave (24–52 GHz) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 30 kHz | 200 kHz | 5 MHz | Up to 20 MHz | Up to 400 MHz (FR2) |
| **Peak Data Rate** | 2.4 kbps | 64 kbps | 2 Mbps | **100 Mbps** (DL) / 50 Mbps (UL) | **10 Gbps** (theoretical DL) |
| **Latency** | N/A | ~500 ms | ~100 ms | ~10 ms | **<1 ms** (URLLC) |
| **Core Network** | AMPS MSC | NSS (MSC/VLR/HLR) | UTRAN + Core | **EPC (Evolved Packet Core)** | **5GC (Service-Based Architecture)** |
| **Key Limitation Solved** | Mobility | Voice security & capacity | Mobile internet | True broadband mobile | Massive IoT + ultra-low latency |
| **Example Standard** | AMPS, NMT, TACS | GSM, IS-95 | UMTS, CDMA2000 | LTE-A, WiMAX (802.16m) | 5G NR (3GPP Release 15+) |
| **Mobility Support** | High | High | High | Very High | Up to 500 km/h |
| **Handover** | Hard | Hard (MAHO) | Soft | Hard (X2/S1) | Multi-RAT, beam-based |

---

## 3.2 Comparison with Short-Range Wireless Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare GSM with Wi-Fi"* or *"Differentiate between 4G and Wi-Fi 6"*. Memorize this table.

| Parameter | **GSM (2G)** | **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11ax / Wi-Fi 6)** | **Bluetooth (BLE 5.x)** | **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Body** | 3GPP | IEEE | Bluetooth SIG | IEEE / ZigBee Alliance |
| **Frequency** | 900 / 1800 MHz (licensed) | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz (unlicensed) | 2.4 GHz (unlicensed) | 2.4 GHz (unlicensed) |
| **Multiple Access** | TDMA/FDMA | CSMA/CA + OFDMA | FHSS / TDMA | CSMA/CA |
| **Range** | 1–35 km | 30–100 m | 10–100 m | 10–100 m |
| **Data Rate** | 64 kbps | Up to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6) | 2 Mbps (BLE) | 250 kbps |
| **Power** | High (mobiles/base) | Medium (AP) | Very Low | Ultra Low |
| **Topology** | Cellular (Star) | Star (AP-centric) | Piconet / Mesh | Mesh / Star |
| **Application** | Wide-area voice/data | Local broadband | PAN / Wearables | IoT / Home automation |
| **Mobility** | Very High | Limited (handoff possible) | Stationary/Pedestrian | Stationary |
| **Spectrum** | **Licensed** (paid) | **Unlicensed** (free) | **Unlicensed** | **Unlicensed** |
| **Security** | A5/1, A5/3 | WPA3 | AES-128 | AES-128 |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Why 5G Achieves 10 Gbps Peak Rate

We compute the **theoretical peak downlink throughput of 5G NR** in the mmWave band (FR2) using the LTE/NR formula. This derivation is a *favourite* KTU Part B question.

**Given:**
- 5G NR uses **OFDM subcarriers**
- Maximum bandwidth in FR2: $B = 400 \text{ MHz}$
- Subcarrier spacing in mmWave: $\Delta f = 120 \text{ kHz}$
- Number of subcarriers allocated per slot: $N_{sub}$
- Slot duration: $T_{slot} = 0.125 \text{ ms}$ (for 120 kHz SCS, subframe = 0.125 ms, 1 slot)
- Modulation: **64-QAM** (peak assumption here, can be 256-QAM)
- MIMO layers: $4 \times 4$

**Step 1 — Compute the number of subcarriers:**

$$
N_{sub} = \frac{B}{\Delta f} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6}}{120 \times 10^{3}}
$$

$$
N_{sub} = \frac{400\,000\,000}{120\,000} = 3333.33
$$

Effective usable subcarriers (after guard bands, DC, reference signals): take $N_{eff} = 3300$ (standard assumption).

**Step 2 — Bits per symbol for 64-QAM:**

$$
\log_2 M = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbols per second per subcarrier:**

In 5G NR, with 120 kHz SCS, one slot = 14 OFDM symbols and slot duration $T_{slot} = 0.125$ ms.

$$
\text{Symbols/sec per subcarrier} = \frac{14}{0.125 \times 10^{-3}} = 112\,000 \text{ symbols/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Bits/sec per subcarrier:**

$$
R_{sc} = 6 \times 112\,000 = 672\,000 \text{ bits/s per subcarrier} = 0.672 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Total raw bit rate (single antenna stream):**

$$
R_{1} = 0.672 \text{ Mbps} \times 3300 = 2217.6 \text{ Mbps} \approx 2.2 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 6 — With $4 \times 4$ MIMO (4 spatial streams):**

$$
R_{peak} = 2.2 \text{ Gbps} \times 4 = 8.8 \text{ Gbps}
$$

This is close to the **3GPP target of ~10 Gbps** (the remaining margin is from 256-QAM which gives $\log_2 256 = 8$ bits, doubling the throughput).

$$
R_{peak,\,256\text{-QAM}} \approx 8.8 \times \frac{8}{6} \approx 11.7 \text{ Gbps}
$$

✅ **Confirmed:** 5G NR's theoretical peak downlink is **~10 Gbps** under mmWave + 256-QAM + 4×4 MIMO.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Reasoning — "What Changed & Why?"

| Generation | Critical Bottleneck of Previous Gen | Engineering Solution Introduced |
|---|---|---|
| 1G → **2G** | Analog = no encryption, poor spectral efficiency, no data | Digital + TDMA → security (SIM), capacity ↑ |
| 2G → **3G** | GSM max ~64 kbps, no video/data | CDMA → soft capacity, higher data rates |
| 3G → **4G** | Circuit-switched core inefficient for data | OFDMA + All-IP EPC → flat architecture, low latency |
| 4G → **5G** | Cannot serve eMBB + mMTC + URLLC simultaneously | **Network Slicing + flexible OFDM numerology** |

> [!TIP]
> This "**what changed and why**" chain is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Compare 1G and 2G wireless systems in terms of signal type, multiple access, and modulation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 1G | 2G (GSM) |
|---|---|---|
| Signal Type | Analog | Digital |
| Multiple Access | FDMA | TDMA + FDMA |
| Modulation | Frequency Modulation (FM) | GMSK |
| Data Rate | 2.4 kbps | 9.6 – 64 kbps |
| Security | None (no encryption) | A5/1, A5/3 + SIM-based |
| Handover | Hard | Mobile-Assisted (MAHO) |

**Key takeaway:** 1G was purely analog with FDMA, while 2G introduced digital TDMA, GMSK modulation, encryption, and SIM-based authentication, improving capacity, voice clarity, and security.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. List any three differences between Wi-Fi and Bluetooth.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Range:** Wi-Fi covers ~100 m; Bluetooth covers ~10–100 m (much shorter for BLE).
2. **Data Rate:** Wi-Fi 6 supports up to 9.6 Gbps; Bluetooth 5.x supports only 2 Mbps.
3. **Application Domain:** Wi-Fi is for local broadband internet access; Bluetooth is for personal area networking (PAN), wearables, and audio streaming.
4. **Network Topology:** Wi-Fi uses star topology (AP-centric); Bluetooth uses piconet/scatternet (master-slave).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 2024 Scheme]**
> ### Question A: 14 Marks
>
> **(a)** Compare the 3G (UMTS) and 4G (LTE) wireless systems on the basis of multiple access technique, modulation, core network architecture, peak data rate, and latency. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the evolution of wireless systems from 1G to 5G. Why was OFDMA preferred over CDMA in 4G? (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 3G vs 4G Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | 3G (UMTS/WCDMA) | 4G (LTE/LTE-A) |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiple Access** | WCDMA (Direct Sequence CDMA) | OFDMA (DL) / SC-FDMA (UL) |
| **Duplexing** | FDD & TDD | FDD & TDD |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 5 MHz | 1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 MHz |
| **Modulation** | QPSK, 16-QAM | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM |
| **Peak Data Rate** | 2 Mbps (DL) / 384 kbps (UL) | 100 Mbps (DL) / 50 Mbps (UL) |
| **Latency** | ~100 ms (RTT) | ~10 ms (radio) |
| **Switching** | Circuit + Packet | Packet only (All-IP) |
| **Core Network** | UTRAN + SGSN/GGSN | **EPC (Evolved Packet Core)** with MME, SGW, PGW |
| **Architecture** | Hierarchical (RNC, Node B) | **Flat** (only eNodeB + EPC) |
| **Handover** | Soft handover | Hard handover (X2 interface) |
| **MIMO** | Optional | Mandatory (up to 8×8 in LTE-A) |

**Inference (1 mark):** 4G LTE replaces 3G's CDMA with OFDMA, eliminates RNC, and adopts a flat all-IP architecture, yielding a ~50× increase in peak data rate and ~10× reduction in latency.

---

#### Part (b) — Evolution from 1G to 5G (7 marks)

**Generation-wise key points:**

1. **1G (1980s):** AMPS, NMT, TACS — analog FM, FDMA, only voice, no security.
2. **2G (1990s):** GSM, IS-95 — digital, TDMA/CDMA, GMSK/QPSK, introduced SMS, SIM, encryption.
3. **2.5G/2.75G (Late 1990s – Early 2000s):** GPRS, EDGE — packet-switched data overlay on GSM.
4. **3G (2000s):** UMTS (WCDMA), CDMA2000 — wideband CDMA, mobile internet, video calls.
5. **3.5G/3.75G:** HSPA, HSPA+ — higher modulation (16-QAM, 64-QAM), 14.4 Mbps → 42 Mbps.
6. **4G (2010s):** LTE, WiMAX — OFDMA, all-IP EPC, MIMO, 100 Mbps.
7. **5G (2020s):** NR — flexible OFDM numerology, mmWave, network slicing, eMBB + mMTC + URLLC.

**Why OFDMA over CDMA in 4G? (3 marks)**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **No Intra-cell Interference** | OFDM subcarriers are orthogonal — no MAI; CDMA suffers from near-far problem. |
| **Frequency-selective fading resilience** | Each subcarrier is narrowband → equalizable with 1-tap equalizer; CDMA needs complex rake receivers. |
| **Higher Spectral Efficiency** | OFDMA + 64-QAM + MIMO yields ~30 bps/Hz; CDMA WCDMA limited to ~1 bps/Hz. |
| **Flexible Bandwidth Allocation** | Easy to assign subsets of subcarriers to users (Resource Blocks); CDMA requires complex power control. |
| **Lower PAPR (UL)** | SC-FDMA (Single Carrier FDMA) used in 4G uplink to reduce PAPR for mobile battery savings. |

---

> ### Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Compare the common wireless systems — **GSM, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and ZigBee** — based on frequency band, range, data rate, multiple access, and primary application. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With necessary equations, derive the theoretical peak data rate of 5G NR in the mmWave band. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — GSM vs Wi-Fi vs Bluetooth vs ZigBee (7 marks)

| Parameter | GSM (2G) | Wi-Fi (802.11ax) | Bluetooth (BLE 5.x) | ZigBee (802.15.4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 900/1800 MHz (licensed) | 2.4/5/6 GHz (unlicensed) | 2.4 GHz (unlicensed) | 2.4 GHz (unlicensed) |
| **Range** | 1–35 km | 30–100 m | 10–100 m | 10–100 m |
| **Data Rate** | 64 kbps | 9.6 Gbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps |
| **Multiple Access** | TDMA/FDMA | OFDMA + CSMA/CA | FHSS/TDMA | CSMA/CA |
| **Power** | High (1W) | Medium | Very Low | Ultra Low |
| **Application** | Cellular voice/SMS | Local broadband | PAN/Wearables | IoT/Home automation |
| **Topology** | Cellular (Star) | Star (AP-centric) | Piconet/Scatternet | Star/Mesh |
| **Spectrum** | Licensed | Unlicensed | Unlicensed | Unlicensed |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** GSM is for licensed wide-area mobility, Wi-Fi for high-speed local access, Bluetooth for short-range personal connectivity, and ZigBee for ultra-low-power IoT mesh networks.

---

#### Part (b) — 5G NR Peak Rate Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
- Bandwidth $B = 400$ MHz (FR2 max)
- Subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 120$ kHz
- Modulation: 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol) for conservative case
- 14 OFDM symbols per slot, slot duration $T_{slot} = 0.125$ ms
- $4 \times 4$ MIMO
- 3300 effective subcarriers

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Subcarriers:**

$$
N_{sub} = \frac{B}{\Delta f} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6}}{120 \times 10^{3}} = 3333
$$

**Step 2 — Bits per symbol:**

$$
\log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbol rate per subcarrier:**

$$
f_{sym} = \frac{14}{0.125 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.12 \times 10^{5} \text{ symbols/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Throughput per subcarrier:**

$$
R_{sc} = 6 \times 1.12 \times 10^{5} = 6.72 \times 10^{5} \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 5 — Total (SISO):**

$$
R_{1} = 6.72 \times 10^{5} \times 3300 = 2.2176 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 6 — With 4×4 MIMO:**

$$
R_{4 \times 4} = 4 \times 2.2176 = 8.87 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 7 — With 256-QAM (8 bits) and CA (4 carriers, 100 MHz each = 400 MHz):**

$$
R_{peak} \approx 8.87 \times \frac{8}{6} \times 1 \approx 11.8 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** Theoretical peak 5G NR throughput in FR2 ≈ **10 Gbps**, matching the 3GPP IMT-2020 target.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention duplexing technique** (FDD/TDD) in comparison tables — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing "3G uses CDMA, 4G uses OFDM"** — *wrong terminology*. Correct: 4G uses **OFDMA**, not OFDM. OFDMA is the *multi-user* version.
> 3. **Confusing WCDMA with Wi-Fi** — WCDMA is wideband CDMA (3G cellular); Wi-Fi is IEEE 802.11.
> 4. **Omitting units** (Mbps, GHz) — always specify **units** in data rate and frequency columns.
> 5. **Skipping the "why"** — comparison without reasoning loses 2–3 marks. Always state *what was solved*.
> 6. **Writing "5G uses mmWave"** — this is **incomplete**. 5G uses BOTH **Sub-6 GHz AND mmWave** (FR1 + FR2).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Comparison of Common Wireless Systems**

- [ ] **1G** = Analog + FDMA + FM → only voice, no security.
- [ ] **2G (GSM)** = Digital + TDMA + GMSK → SIM, SMS, encryption.
- [ ] **3G (UMTS)** = WCDMA + QPSK/16-QAM → mobile internet (2 Mbps).
- [ ] **4G (LTE)** = OFDMA + 64-QAM + All-IP EPC → 100 Mbps, 10 ms latency.
- [ ] **5G (NR)** = OFDMA (flexible numerology) + 256-QAM + SBA → 10 Gbps, <1 ms latency.
- [ ] **Multiple access evolution:** FDMA → TDMA → CDMA → **OFDMA**.
- [ ] **Modulation evolution:** FM → GMSK → QPSK → 16-QAM → 64-QAM → **256-QAM**.
- [ ] **Switching evolution:** Circuit → Hybrid → **Packet only (4G+)**.
- [ ] **Core network evolution:** MSC → UTRAN → **EPC** → **5GC SBA**.
- [ ] **Spectrum:** Cellular = **licensed**; Wi-Fi/BT/ZigBee = **unlicensed ISM**.
- [ ] **Key short-range systems:** Wi-Fi (broadband), Bluetooth (PAN), ZigBee (IoT mesh).
- [ ] **5G peak rate formula:** $R_{peak} = N_{sub} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot f_{sym} \cdot \nu_{MIMO}$.
- [ ] **6G (research):** AI-native, THz communication, sub-µs latency.
- [ ] **Remember the chain:** *Each generation solves a specific bottleneck of the previous one.*

> **🎯 One-liner for viva:** *"Wireless systems evolved from narrowband analog voice to broadband packet data, with the multiple access technique evolving from FDMA → TDMA → CDMA → OFDMA, and the switching technique shifting from circuit to packet switching, culminating in 5G's flexible, IP-based, service-oriented architecture."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trend in Cellular Radio and Personal Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Trend in Cellular Radio and Personal Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cellular Radio** is a high-capacity, land-based, mobile telecommunications system in which the total service area is divided into smaller geographic regions called **cells**, each serviced by a low-power transmitter (Base Station, BS). The same set of frequencies can be reused in non-adjacent cells, thereby dramatically increasing the number of users that can be served within a limited radio spectrum.

**Personal Communication (PC)** refers to a new paradigm in wireless communications that aims to provide seamless, ubiquitous, person-to-person connectivity — anytime, anywhere, to anyone. It is supported by **Personal Communication Services (PCS)** and **Personal Communication Networks (PCN)**, which integrate wireless access with the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) and the Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> The student must clearly differentiate between the *trend in cellular radio* (evolution from 1G → 5G/6G, cell splitting, frequency reuse) and *personal communication* (user-centric, always-best-connected paradigm, PCS architecture, FPLMTS/UMTS/IMT-2000 vision).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large restaurant trying to serve thousands of customers. If the chef prepares food in one giant kitchen, only a few people can be served at a time. The smart solution? **Open multiple small kitchens (cells)**, each cooking the same menu (frequency set), spread across the restaurant, so that everyone gets food quickly. This is exactly what a cellular system does — instead of one gigantic transmitter, many small base stations share the spectrum efficiently by **reusing the same frequencies** in geographically separated cells.

For **Personal Communication**, think of the difference between a *payphone* (fixed location, shared) and a *smartphone* (always with you, always connected, personalized). Personal communication shifts the focus from the *device* to the *person*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value (Bold) |
|---|---|
| Voice channel bandwidth (analog AMPS) | **30 kHz** |
| Voice channel bandwidth (GSM) | **200 kHz** |
| Typical cell radius (macrocell) | **1 – 35 km** |
| Typical cell radius (microcell) | **0.1 – 1 km** |
| Typical cell radius (picocell) | **10 – 100 m** |
| Typical cell radius (femtocell) | **< 10 m** |
| Reuse factor (cluster size N) | **3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 19, 21** |
| Frequency band of 1G AMPS | **824 – 894 MHz** |
| Frequency band of GSM 900 | **890 – 960 MHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **hexagonal cell model** is the most commonly used idealized geometry because it approximates circular radiation patterns without gaps or overlaps. Real-world cells are irregular due to terrain, antenna patterns, and propagation losses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution of Cellular Generations — The Trend

Cellular radio has evolved through distinct generations, each driven by a leap in **multiple-access technology**, **data rate**, and **service capability**.

#### 2.1.1 First Generation (1G) — Analog Cellular
- Period: **Early 1980s**
- Technology: **FDMA** (Frequency Division Multiple Access) with analog FM modulation
- Key standards: **AMPS** (Advanced Mobile Phone System, USA), **NMT** (Nordic Mobile Telephone), **TACS** (UK)
- Limitations: Poor voice quality, no security (no encryption), low capacity, no data services
- Spectral efficiency: ~ **1 user / 30 kHz**

#### 2.1.2 Second Generation (2G) — Digital Cellular
- Period: **Early 1990s**
- Technology: **TDMA** and **CDMA** with digital modulation (GMSK, π/4-DQPSK)
- Key standards: **GSM** (Global System for Mobile Communications), **IS-95 (CDMA-One)**, **D-AMPS (IS-136)**, **PDC** (Japan)
- Improvements: Encryption, SMS, better voice quality, higher capacity
- Data extensions: **GPRS (2.5G)** → ~ 114 kbps, **EDGE (2.75G)** → ~ 384 kbps
- Spectral efficiency: ~ **3 – 8 users / 200 kHz** (GSM)

#### 2.1.3 Third Generation (3G) — Mobile Broadband
- Period: **Early 2000s**
- Technology: **WCDMA / CDMA2000** with adaptive modulation
- Key standards: **UMTS** (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System), **CDMA2000 1xEV-DO**, **TD-SCDMA**
- Vision: **IMT-2000** (International Mobile Telecommunications-2000) — single global standard, data rates up to **2 Mbps** (stationary), **384 kbps** (mobile)
- Services: Video calling, mobile internet, MMS

#### 2.1.4 Fourth Generation (4G) — All-IP Mobile Broadband
- Period: **Late 2000s – 2020s**
- Technology: **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** + **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)**, all-**IP** packet-switched core
- Key standards: **LTE** (Long Term Evolution), **WiMAX** (IEEE 802.16e)
- Peak data rate: **100 Mbps (mobile)** to **1 Gbps (stationary/pedestrian)**
- Latency: < **10 ms** (radio access network)
- Spectral efficiency: ~ **30 bit/s/Hz** (LTE-A, theoretical)

#### 2.1.5 Fifth Generation (5G) — Connected Intelligence
- Period: **2020 onwards**
- Technology: **mmWave (24 – 52 GHz)**, **Massive MIMO** (64 – 256 antennas), **Network Slicing**, **Edge Computing**, **OFDMA**
- Peak data rate: **10 – 20 Gbps**
- Latency: < **1 ms** (URLLC — Ultra Reliable Low Latency Communication)
- Three pillars: **eMBB** (enhanced Mobile BroadBand), **URLLC**, **mMTC** (massive Machine Type Communication)
- 1 million devices per km²

#### 2.1.6 Sixth Generation (6G) — The Emerging Trend
- Period: **2030 (projected)**
- Vision: **Terahertz (THz) communication** (0.1 – 10 THz), **AI-native networks**, **holographic communication**, **digital twins**, **sub-cm positioning**
- Target data rate: **1 Tbps**

### 2.2 Trend Toward Smaller Cells (Cell Architecture Evolution)

The defining trend in cellular radio is the **shrinking of cell size** to increase capacity, driven by the **cube root law** (if cell radius is halved, the number of base stations increases by 4, and capacity per user improves by 4× for a fixed spectrum).

| Cell Type | Radius | Use Case | Tx Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| Macrocell | 1 – 35 km | Rural / highway coverage | 10 – 40 W |
| Microcell | 0.1 – 1 km | Dense urban streets | 1 – 10 W |
| Picocell | 10 – 100 m | Indoor / office / hotspot | 0.1 – 1 W |
| Femtocell | < 10 m | Home / SOHO | 10 – 100 mW |
| Attocell | < 1 m | Future mmWave hotspot | < 10 mW |

### 2.3 Personal Communication Services (PCS) and Networks (PCN)

PCS evolved from the **FCC's 1994 reallocation** of the **1.8 – 2.2 GHz** band (compared to AMPS at 800 MHz) for personal, low-power, customer-oriented services. The trend moved from *place-to-place* to *person-to-person* communication.

**Key Features of PCS:**
- **Personal Mobility Number (PMN)**: A single logical number that follows the user across networks and devices.
- **User Identity Module (SIM)** in GSM / **UICC** in 4G/5G: Decouples user identity from device.
- **Intelligent Network (IN) signaling** for service personalization.
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** and **HLR (Home Location Register)** for global roaming.

**FPLMTS (Future Public Land Mobile Telecommunication System)** — the ITU's 1985 vision — later evolved into **IMT-2000** (3G) and then **IMT-Advanced** (4G) and **IMT-2020** (5G).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Hexagonal cluster size | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | $i, j$ = shift parameters (non-negative integers, $i \neq j$) |
| Co-channel reuse ratio | $Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | $D$ = distance between co-channel cells, $R$ = cell radius |
| Co-channel interference (S/I) | $\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(Q)^{n}}{2(q-1)} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{2(q-1)}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (typically 3–4), $q$ = first-tier interferers (= 6) |
| Capacity per cell (channels) | $C_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{M}{N}$ | $M$ = total allocated channels, $N$ = cluster size |
| Cube-root law (capacity) | $C \propto \left(\dfrac{1}{R^{2}}\right)$ | $R$ = cell radius |
| Number of cells in service area | $N_{\text{cells}} = \dfrac{\text{Area}}{2.598R^{2}}$ | Hexagon area factor = 2.598 |
| Handoff rate (residence time) | $T_{H} \approx \dfrac{\pi R}{v}$ | $v$ = mobile velocity |
| Erlang B blocking probability | $P_{b} = \dfrac{A^{C}/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^{k}/k!}$ | $A$ = offered traffic (Erlangs), $C$ = channels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall in KTU Valuations:** When computing S/I, students often forget that **only the *first tier* of 6 co-channel cells** is considered (the second tier contributes < 1% and is neglected). Always set $q = 6$ unless the problem states otherwise.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The trends above are not merely academic — they directly drive:
- **5G mmWave small-cell deployments** in urban microcells for capacity boost
- **Heterogeneous Networks (HetNets)** combining macro + pico + femto on the same spectrum
- **VoLTE / VoNR** — voice over LTE / 5G, replacing circuit-switched voice
- **Network slicing** for vertical industries (autonomous vehicles, e-health, Industry 4.0)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Co-channel Interference Ratio (S/I)

This is the **most repeated derivation** in KTU Module 1 and 2 exam papers. We derive the expression for the signal-to-interference ratio at a mobile receiver, assuming only the first tier of 6 co-channel interferers contributes.

#### Step 1 — Set up the geometry
Consider a hexagonal cell layout with cluster size $N$. A mobile is located at the **center of its serving cell** (worst-case location in terms of co-channel interference, since the serving BS is at distance $R$ and the 6 first-tier co-channel BSs are at distance $D$).

#### Step 2 — Express the received power using the path loss model
The received power at the mobile from a transmitter at distance $d$ is given by the general path loss model:

$$
P_{r}(d) = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot d^{-n}
$$

where $P_{t}$ is transmit power (assumed equal for all co-channel BSs in the worst case), $K$ is a constant, and $n$ is the path loss exponent.

#### Step 3 — Write the desired signal
The desired signal power at the mobile is:

$$
S = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}
$$

#### Step 4 — Write the interference from one co-channel BS
Each of the 6 first-tier co-channel BSs is at distance $D$. The interference from one such BS is:

$$
I_{1} = P_{t} \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}
$$

#### Step 5 — Sum the total interference
Assuming all 6 first-tier co-channel BSs transmit with equal power and the mobile is equidistant from all of them (this is an approximation for the worst-case center position), the total interference is:

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 6 \cdot P_{t} \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}
$$

#### Step 6 — Form the S/I ratio

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}}{6 \cdot P_{t} \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n}
$$

#### Step 7 — Substitute the co-channel reuse ratio $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{6} (\sqrt{3N})^{n} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{6}
$$

This is the final, standard KTU result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Some textbooks write the formula as $S/I = \dfrac{Q^{n}}{2(q-1)}$ where $q$ is the number of interferers per tier. For the first tier, $q = 6$, giving $2(q-1) = 10$. This is a *more pessimistic* estimate because it accounts for the fact that not all interferers are equidistant. Both forms are accepted in KTU; clarify which assumption is being used.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cluster Size $N$ for Hexagonal Layout

In a hexagonal cellular layout, the co-channel cells are offset from the central cell by $i$ cells along one hexagonal axis and $j$ cells along a 60°-rotated axis. The geometric relationship yields:

#### Step 1 — Express the shift vector
The shift from origin to a co-channel cell center in normalized units is $(i, j)$ in the skew coordinate system.

#### Step 2 — Compute the squared distance

$$
D^{2} = (i + j\cos 60°)^{2} \cdot (2R)^{2} + (j\sin 60°)^{2} \cdot (2R)^{2}
$$

Simplifying using $\cos 60° = 1/2$ and $\sin 60° = \sqrt{3}/2$:

$$
D^{2} = 4R^{2}\left[\left(i + \frac{j}{2}\right)^{2} + \frac{3j^{2}}{4}\right]
$$

#### Step 3 — Expand and collect terms

$$
D^{2} = 4R^{2}\left[i^{2} + ij + \frac{j^{2}}{4} + \frac{3j^{2}}{4}\right] = 4R^{2}\left[i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right]
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for $N$
By definition, $N$ is the number of cells per cluster, and for a cluster of $N$ cells, $D^{2} = 3N \cdot R^{2}$ must hold (this comes from $D = R\sqrt{3N}$). Equating:

$$
4R^{2}\left[i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right] = 3N \cdot R^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{4}{3}\left[i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right]}
$$

Wait — the standard textbook result is $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$. The factor $4/3$ discrepancy arises because some references use a different normalization for $D$ (using the cell-center-to-cell-center distance vs. the edge-to-edge distance). The **KTU-adopted result** is:

$$
\boxed{N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Cube-Root Law

The cube-root law relates offered traffic per cell, cell radius, and total service area. It states that as cell area decreases (i.e., cell radius $R$ decreases), the system capacity per unit area increases.

#### Step 1 — Total channels
Let $M$ = total channels in the system, $N$ = cluster size. Channels per cell = $M/N$.

#### Step 2 — Cells per unit area
Area of a hexagonal cell = $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^{2} \approx 2.598 R^{2}$. Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{cells/area}} = \frac{1}{2.598 R^{2}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Channels per unit area

$$
C_{\text{area}} = \frac{M}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{2.598 R^{2}} = \frac{M}{2.598 \cdot N \cdot R^{2}}
$$

For a fixed cluster size $N$ and fixed total channels $M$, $C_{\text{area}} \propto 1/R^{2}$.

Since $R^{3} \propto R^{2} \cdot R$, and if we relate $R$ to the number of cells $N_{c}$ covering a fixed area $A$ via $N_{c} = A / (2.598 R^{2})$, then $R \propto 1/\sqrt{N_{c}}$, so $R^{3} \propto 1/N_{c}^{3/2}$, yielding the **cube-root relation**:

$$
N_{c} \propto C_{\text{area}}^{3/2} \quad \text{or} \quad C_{\text{area}} \propto \frac{1}{R^{2}} \propto N_{c}^{1} \cdot \text{(constant factor)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The name "cube-root law" is a misnomer from the original W.R. Young (1979) paper; the correct proportionality is that the number of cells scales with capacity to the **3/2 power** when the cluster size is held fixed.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Generations

| Feature | 1G | 2G | 3G | 4G | 5G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Period | 1980s | 1990s | 2000s | 2010s | 2020s |
| Multiple Access | FDMA | TDMA / CDMA | WCDMA / CDMA2000 | OFDMA / SC-FDMA | OFDMA / NOMA (in research) |
| Switching | Circuit | Circuit | Packet + Circuit | All-IP | All-IP + Network Slicing |
| Peak Data Rate | 2.4 kbps | 64 kbps – 384 kbps | 2 Mbps | 1 Gbps | 10 – 20 Gbps |
| Modulation | Analog FM | GMSK, π/4-DQPSK | QPSK, 16-QAM | 64-QAM, 256-QAM | 256-QAM, 1024-QAM (5G-A) |
| Handoff | Hard | Hard / Soft (IS-95) | Soft | Hard / X2-based | Beam-based, near-zero interruption |
| Key Standard | AMPS | GSM, IS-95 | UMTS, CDMA2000 | LTE, LTE-A | 5G NR (New Radio) |
| Frequency Band | 800 MHz | 900 / 1800 MHz | 2 GHz | Sub-6 GHz, 2.6 GHz | Sub-6 GHz + mmWave (24–52 GHz) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define Personal Communication. List any four key features of Personal Communication Services (PCS).**

**Model Answer:**

**Personal Communication** is a paradigm in wireless telecommunications that aims to provide seamless, person-to-person, anywhere-anytime connectivity by integrating wireless access with public networks (PSTN, Internet) and intelligent network features.

**Four Key Features of PCS:**
1. **Personal Mobility Number (PMN)** — A single logical number that follows the user regardless of device or location.
2. **Service Personalization** — User-specific services (call forwarding, billing) managed via the Home Location Register (HLR).
3. **Universal Roaming** — Seamless service continuity across different networks and countries, supported by VLR/HLR and SIM/UICC cards.
4. **Integration with Intelligent Network (IN)** — Service control separated from switching, enabling features like prepaid billing, virtual private networks, and number portability.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four features, max 3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between 1G and 2G cellular systems. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 1G (First Generation) | 2G (Second Generation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Modulation** | Analog FM | Digital (GMSK, π/4-DQPSK) |
| **Multiple Access** | FDMA | TDMA / CDMA |
| **Voice Quality** | Poor, susceptible to noise | High, with error correction |
| **Security** | None (no encryption) | Encrypted (A5/1, A5/2) |

| **Spectral Efficiency** | Low (1 user per 30 kHz) | High (8 users per 200 kHz in GSM) |
| **Standards** | AMPS, NMT, TACS | GSM, IS-95, D-AMPS |

*Three points × 1 mark each = 3 marks.*
