### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 1 — KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the evolution of cellular mobile communication from 1G to 5G, highlighting the technology, peak data rate, and multiple-access scheme in each generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cellular operator is allocated a total of **400 channels**. If the system uses a cluster size of **N = 7**, determine:
- (i) The number of channels per cell.
- (ii) The co-channel reuse ratio $Q$.
- (iii) The co-channel interference ratio $S/I$, assuming a path loss exponent $n = 4$.
- (iv) The number of cells required to cover a metropolitan area of **2100 km²**, if each hexagonal cell has a radius of **6 km**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Evolution from 1G to 5G:**

| Generation | Period | Key Technology | Multiple Access | Peak Data Rate | Modulation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1G | 1980s | Analog cellular | FDMA | 2.4 kbps | Analog FM |
| 2G | 1990s | Digital cellular | TDMA / CDMA | 64 kbps | GMSK, π/4-DQPSK |
| 2.5G | Late 1990s | GPRS | TDMA + packet | 114 kbps | GMSK, 8-PSK (EDGE) |
| 3G | 2000s | IMT-2000 / UMTS | WCDMA / CDMA2000 | 2 Mbps | QPSK, 16-QAM |
| 3.5G | Mid 2000s | HSPA | WCDMA enhanced | 14 Mbps | 16-QAM, 64-QAM |
| 4G | 2010s | LTE / LTE-A / WiMAX | OFDMA / SC-FDMA + MIMO | 1 Gbps (stationary) | 64-QAM, 256-QAM |
| 5G | 2020s | 5G NR | OFDMA + Massive MIMO + mmWave | 10–20 Gbps | 256-QAM, 1024-QAM |

*Drawing a clean timeline diagram earns 1 mark. Tabulating 5 generations earns 5 marks. Brief explanation of the trend (→ higher data rate, → all-IP, → lower latency) earns 1 mark. = 7 marks.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Channels per cell:**

$$
C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{400}{7} = 57.14
$$

Since channels must be an integer, in practice the cluster is **N = 7** with 57 channels in 6 cells and 58 in 1 cell. For exam purposes, write **≈ 57 channels per cell**. (1 mark)

**(ii) Co-channel reuse ratio:**

$$
Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583
$$

(1 mark)

**(iii) Co-channel interference ratio:**

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{2(q-1)} = \frac{(\sqrt{21})^{4}}{2(6-1)} = \frac{(21)^{2}}{10} = \frac{441}{10} = 44.1
$$

In decibels:

$$
\frac{S}{I} \bigg\vert_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(44.1) \approx 16.44 \text{ dB}
$$

(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer in both linear and dB)

**(iv) Number of cells to cover 2100 km²:**

Area of one hexagonal cell with radius $R = 6$ km:

$$
A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^{2} = 2.598 \times 36 = 93.53 \text{ km}^{2}
$$

Number of cells:

$$
N_{\text{cells}} = \frac{2100}{93.53} \approx 22.45
$$

Since cells cannot be fractional, **≈ 23 hexagonal cells** are required. (2 marks — 1 for area formula, 1 for division and rounding)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the functional architecture of a **Personal Communication System (PCS)**. List the functions of **HLR, VLR, MSC, and SIM/UICC**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hexagonal cellular system has a cluster size of **N = 4**. The total spectrum allocation supports **300 traffic channels**.
- (i) Determine the number of channels per cell.
- (ii) Compute the co-channel reuse ratio $Q$.
- (iii) If the path loss exponent is $n = 3$, find the S/I ratio in dB.
- (iv) If the same area is to be re-engineered with a cluster size of **N = 12**, by what factor does the S/I improve? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — PCS Architecture:**

A PCS consists of three major subsystems (as shown in the Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.2 above):

1. **User Domain** — Mobile stations, SIM/UICC card, IoT devices.
2. **Radio Access Network (RAN)** — Base stations (BTS / NodeB / eNodeB / gNodeB), base station controllers.
3. **Core Network** — MSC, HLR, VLR, SGSN, IMS.
4. **External Networks** — PSTN, Internet, satellite.

**Functions:**
- **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent database of subscribers belonging to the home network — stores IMSI, MSISDN, service profile, current VLR pointer.
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database of roamers currently in the area — stores subscriber data fetched from HLR for fast call setup.
- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Central switching entity — performs call routing, handoff control, inter-BSC coordination, and gateway function to PSTN.
- **SIM/UICC:** Subscriber Identity Module / Universal IC Card — physically stores IMSI, Ki (authentication key), operator data, and is the *decoupling point* between user identity and device.

*Block diagram = 2 marks, HLR/VLR/MSC/SIM functions = 4 marks, overall explanation = 1 mark = 7 marks.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Channels per cell:**

$$
C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{300}{4} = 75 \text{ channels/cell}
$$

(1 mark)

**(ii) Co-channel reuse ratio:**

$$
Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 4} = \sqrt{12} = 2\sqrt{3} \approx 3.464
$$

(1 mark)

**(iii) S/I ratio for $n = 3$:**

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{n}}{2(q-1)} = \frac{(\sqrt{12})^{3}}{2(6-1)} = \frac{12\sqrt{12}}{10} = \frac{12 \times 3.464}{10} = 4.157
$$

In decibels:

$$
\frac{S}{I} \bigg\vert_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(4.157) \approx 6.19 \text{ dB}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iv) Improvement factor when $N$ changes from 4 to 12:**

For $N_{1} = 4$:

$$
(S/I)_{1} = \frac{(\sqrt{12})^{3}}{10} = 4.157
$$

For $N_{2} = 12$:

$$
(S/I)_{2} = \frac{(\sqrt{36})^{3}}{10} = \frac{6^{3}}{10} = \frac{216}{10} = 21.6
$$

Improvement factor:

$$
\frac{(S/I)_{2}}{(S/I)_{1}} = \frac{21.6}{4.157} \approx 5.196
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\frac{(S/I)_{2}}{(S/I)_{1}} = \left(\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{n/2} = \left(\frac{12}{4}\right)^{3/2} = 3^{1.5} = 3\sqrt{3} \approx 5.196
$$

(3 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2(q−1):** Many students write $S/I = (\sqrt{3N})^{n}/6$, which is the *simplified worst-case at cell center* form. The KTU preferred form is $S/I = (\sqrt{3N})^{n}/[2(q-1)] = (\sqrt{3N})^{n}/10$. Either is acceptable if you **state the assumption clearly** (cell-center, equidistant interferers). Failing to state the assumption loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign errors in dB conversion:** $S/I$ in dB = $10 \log_{10}(S/I)$ (since S and I are powers). Using $20 \log_{10}$ is a common error that costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up cluster size N and frequency reuse distance D:** Cluster size $N$ is dimensionless (cells per cluster). $D$ has units of length. $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ is the *normalized* ratio.
> 4. **Not specifying the path loss exponent $n$:** If the problem says "urban area" and you assume $n = 4$, you must state this assumption.
> 5. **Personal Communication answers without SIM/UICC mention:** Examiners expect a discussion of the *user-centric* (not device-centric) nature, which is rooted in the SIM/UICC concept. Omitting this is a 1–2 mark deduction in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 6. **In evolution tables, listing only 1G, 2G, 3G:** You must cover at least up to **5G** in any 7-mark evolution question; partial coverage caps your marks at 4–5.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Cellular radio = high-capacity land mobile** using low-power cells and frequency reuse.
- [x] **Personal Communication = person-to-person**, anywhere-anytime, user-centric paradigm.
- [x] **Generational trend:** 1G (analog FDMA) → 2G (digital TDMA/CDMA) → 3G (WCDMA, IMT-2000) → 4G (OFDMA, all-IP, LTE) → 5G (mmWave, massive MIMO, network slicing) → 6G (THz, AI-native).
- [x] **Hexagonal cell model** is the standard idealization; cell area = $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^{2} \approx 2.598 R^{2}$.
- [x] **Cluster size** $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ for $i, j$ non-negative integers with $i \neq j$; common values: 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 19, 21.
- [x] **Co-channel reuse ratio** $Q = \sqrt{3N}$; higher $N$ means more isolation but fewer channels per cell.
- [x] **Co-channel S/I formula** $S/I = (\sqrt{3N})^{n} / [2(q-1)]$ with $q = 6$ for first tier.
- [x] **Cell splitting trend:** macro → micro → pico → femto → attocell; capacity grows per the cube-root law.
- [x] **PCS core entities:** HLR (permanent), VLR (visitor/roamer), MSC (switching), SIM/UICC (user identity).
- [x] **PCS frequency band:** 1.8 – 2.2 GHz (vs. 800 MHz for AMPS), enabling smaller cells and personal handsets.
- [x] **FPLMTS → IMT-2000 → IMT-Advanced → IMT-2020** is the ITU's evolutionary roadmap.
- [x] **PCS features:** PMN, UPT (Universal Personal Telecommunications), intelligent network signaling, service portability.
- [x] **5G three pillars:** eMBB, URLLC, mMTC; peak 10–20 Gbps; < 1 ms latency; 1 million devices/km².
- [x] **Trend toward all-IP** starting from 3G packet core, fully realized in 4G EPC and 5G Core (5GC).
- [x] **Trend toward network slicing, edge computing, AI-driven RAN** is the defining feature of 5G and beyond.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second Generation Cellular Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Second Generation Cellular Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Second Generation (2G) Cellular Networks** are digital cellular telecommunications systems that superseded the analog First Generation (1G) systems in the early 1990s. The two principal 2G standards are:

- **GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** — originally developed by ETSI in Europe, using a combination of **FDMA + TDMA** multiple access on **200 kHz** carriers.
- **IS-95 (CDMA One / cdmaOne)** — standardized by TIA (USA) in 1993, employing **Direct Sequence CDMA (DS-CDMA)** with a chip rate of **1.228 Mcps** spreading voice across a **1.25 MHz** bandwidth.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> 2G introduced **digital voice**, **SMS**, **SIM-based authentication**, and **data services (up to 9.6 kbps)**. The two dominant 2G standards—**GSM (TDMA-based)** and **IS-95 (CDMA-based)**—represent fundamentally different approaches to multiple access and are core exam topics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

🎯 **Think of it this way:**

Imagine a busy 8-lane highway representing a 1G analog system. Cars (voice calls) zoom along, but every driver in every lane hears every other car engine — there is massive **interference and no privacy**.

**2G is like converting that highway into a digital subway system:**

- **GSM (TDMA)** is like a subway where each train (user) gets its own **time slot** on a specific track (frequency). Trains leave at fixed intervals, so multiple trains share one track without colliding.
- **IS-95 (CDMA)** is like giving every passenger a **unique secret code (PN sequence)**. Everyone can speak at once in the same room, but your radio only "understands" your own code — all other conversations sound like meaningless noise.

> [!NOTE] **Key Analogy Summary**
> - 1G = Open megaphone (analog, noisy, no privacy)
> - 2G GSM = Subway with assigned time slots (organized, scheduled)
> - 2G IS-95 = Cocktail party with unique languages (code-division, simultaneous)

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics (At a Glance)

| Parameter | GSM | IS-95 (CDMA One) |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple Access | TDMA / FDMA | CDMA (DS) |
| Carrier Bandwidth | **200 kHz** | **1.25 MHz** |
| Modulation | **GMSK** | BPSK / QPSK |
| Speech Codec | **RPE-LTP (13 kbps)** | QCELP / EVRC (8/13 kbps) |
| User Data Rate | **9.6 kbps** | **9.6 kbps** (max 14.4 kbps) |
| Frequency Bands | **900 / 1800 / 1900 MHz** | **800 / 1900 MHz** |
| Handoff Type | Hard | Soft / Softer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GSM Architecture — The Four Subsystems

The GSM network is divided into **four major subsystems**:

### 📡 A. Mobile Station (MS)
- Comprises the **Mobile Equipment (ME)** and the **SIM (Subscriber Identity Module)**
- The SIM stores IMSI, Ki (authentication key), and personal user data
- Allows user identity portability across handsets

### 🗼 B. Base Station Subsystem (BSS)
- **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** The radio interface; handles the air interface, encryption/decryption, channel coding
- **BSC (Base Station Controller):** Manages multiple BTS; handles handover decisions, frequency hopping, power control, channel allocation

### 🌐 C. Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)
- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** The central switching node; connects mobile calls to PSTN
- **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent subscriber database — IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, services
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database of subscribers currently in the MSC's area
- **AUC (Authentication Center):** Stores Ki and generates authentication triplets (RAND, SRES, Kc)
- **EIR (Equipment Identity Register):** Blacklists stolen / unauthorized handsets via IMEI

### 💻 D. Operation Support Subsystem (OSS)
- Handles network management, billing, fault management, and performance monitoring

## 2.2 GSM Radio Interface — Physical Channel Structure

GSM uses a hierarchical frame structure on each **200 kHz** carrier:

```
Hyperframe (3 h 28 min 53.76 s) = 2,048 superframes
   └── Superframe (6.12 s) = 1,326 TDMA frames
        └── TDMA Frame (4.615 ms) = 8 time slots
             └── Time Slot (577 μs) = 156.25 bits
                  └── Bit (3.69 μs)
```

- **Multiframe (26 frames, 120 ms):** Used for traffic channels
- **Multiframe (51 frames, 235.4 ms):** Used for control channels

## 2.3 GSM Logical Channels

### 🟢 Traffic Channels (TCH)
- **TCH/F:** Full-rate traffic channel (gross 22.8 kbps, net 9.6 kbps)
- **TCH/H:** Half-rate traffic channel (4.8 kbps)

### 🔵 Control Channels (CCH) — Three Categories

**1. Broadcast Channels (BCH) — One-to-Many, Downlink only**
- **BCCH (Broadcast Control Channel):** Cell ID, frequency list, power control parameters
- **PCH (Paging Channel):** Pages incoming calls
- **AGCH (Access Grant Channel):** Allocates SDCCH after RACH request

**2. Common Control Channels (CCCH)**
- **RACH (Random Access Channel):** Uplink; used by MS to request a channel (slotted Aloha)
- **FACCH (Fast Associated Control Channel):** Steals traffic bursts for urgent signaling (e.g., handover)
- **SACCH (Slow Associated Control Channel):** Carries power control + measurement reports every 480 ms

**3. Dedicated Control Channels (DCCH)**
- **SDCCH (Stand-alone Dedicated Control Channel):** Used for call setup, location update, SMS before TCH is allocated

## 2.4 GSM Transmission Chain (Speech Path)

The signal processing chain from microphone to antenna is:

```
Speech (analog) → A/D → 20 ms segmentation → RPE-LTP codec (13 kbps)
                → Channel coding (convolutional, rate 1/2) → 22.8 kbps
                → Interleaving (8 blocks × 57 bits) → 456 bits / 20 ms
                → Burst formatting (Normal Burst, 156 bits / 4.615 ms)
                → GMSK Modulation (BT = 0.3) → RF transmission
```

> [!NOTE] **Reverse on Reception:** Demodulation → Deinterleaving → Viterbi decoding → Speech decoding → D/A → Speaker.

## 2.5 IS-95 (CDMA One) — Key Concepts

IS-95 uses **Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)** on **1.25 MHz** channels. Multiple users transmit simultaneously in the same frequency band, separated by unique **PN (Pseudo-Noise) codes**.

**Key features:**
- **Processing Gain:** ≈ 21 dB (128 chips per symbol)
- **Power Control:** Open-loop (80 dB dynamic range) + Closed-loop (± 6 dB adjustments every 1.25 ms)
- **Soft Handoff:** Mobile connects to ≥ 2 base stations simultaneously; uses **RAKE receiver** with 3 fingers to combine multipath
- **Soft Capacity:** No hard limit on users; capacity degrades gracefully as users increase
- **Sectorization:** 3 sectors per cell → 3× capacity
- **Voice Activity Factor (VAF):** ≈ 0.4 (silence periods) → 2.5× effective capacity boost

**IS-95 Channel Types:**
- **Forward (BTS → MS):** Pilot, Sync, Paging, Traffic (4 types, each Walsh-coded)
- **Reverse (MS → BTS):** Access, Traffic (each user uses a different long-code PN mask)

## 2.6 Evolution Path: 2G → 2.5G → 3G

| Generation | Technology | Data Rate | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2G** | GSM | 9.6 kbps | Digital voice + SMS |
| **2G** | IS-95 | 9.6 / 14.4 kbps | CDMA spread spectrum |
| **2.5G** | GPRS | 56–171.2 kbps | Packet-switched, "always-on" |
| **2.5G** | EDGE | up to 384 kbps | 8-PSK modulation |
| **3G** | UMTS / CDMA2000 | 384 kbps – 2 Mbps | WCDMA, multimedia |

> [!IMPORTANT] **EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution)** uses **8-PSK** instead of GMSK, packing 3 bits per symbol — boosting the data rate of GSM to 3× without changing the bandwidth.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] **KTU Examiner's Note:** These formulas appear directly in 14-mark derivations.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GSM frame duration | $T_f$ | **4.615 ms** |
| 2 | Slots per TDMA frame | $N_{slot}$ | **8** |
| 3 | Time slot duration | $T_{slot} = T_f / 8$ | **0.577 ms** |
| 4 | Bits per time slot | $N_b$ | **156.25 bits** |
| 5 | Bit duration | $T_b = T_{slot} / 156.25$ | **3.69 μs** |
| 6 | Bit rate per channel | $R_b = 1 / T_b$ | **270.833 kbps** |
| 7 | GSM speech codec rate | $R_{speech}$ | **13 kbps** (RPE-LTP) |
| 8 | Coded rate (convolutional 1/2 + protection) | $R_{coded}$ | **22.8 kbps** |
| 9 | User data rate (full rate) | $R_{user}$ | **9.6 kbps** |
| 10 | Carrier bandwidth | $B_c$ | **200 kHz** |
| 11 | Uplink frequency (GSM-900) | $f_{UL}$ | **890–915 MHz** |
| 12 | Downlink frequency (GSM-900) | $f_{DL}$ | **935–960 MHz** |
| 13 | Duplex spacing | $\Delta f_{duplex}$ | **45 MHz** |
| 14 | Total uplink carriers | $N_c = B_{total} / B_c$ | **125 carriers** |
| 15 | Total simultaneous users (per band) | $N_{users} = N_c \times 8$ | **1000** |
| 16 | IS-95 chip rate | $R_c$ | **1.2288 Mcps** |
| 17 | IS-95 user data rate | $R_b$ | **9.6 kbps** |
| 18 | Processing Gain (IS-95) | $G_p = R_c / R_b$ | **128 (≈ 21 dB)** |
| 19 | Processing Gain in dB | $G_p(dB) = 10 \log_{10}(G_p)$ | **21.07 dB** |
| 20 | SNR after despreading | $(S/N)_o = (S/N)_i \times G_p$ | — |
| 21 | Reuse factor (GSM) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | **3, 4, 7, 9, 12** |
| 22 | C/I ratio (omni-dir, 7-cell) | $C/I$ | **17.2 dB (GSM req.)** |
| 23 | Frame efficiency | $\eta_f$ = data bits / total bits | **91.7%** (148/156) |
| 24 | GPRS theoretical max (8 slots) | $8 \times 21.4$ kbps | **171.2 kbps** |
| 25 | EDGE bit rate per slot | $R_{EDGE}$ | **69.2 kbps** (8-PSK) |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These parameters are the foundation for **network planning tools** (e.g., Atoll, Mentum Planet) used by telecom operators in Kerala (BSNL, Jio, Airtel) to design cell towers, frequency reuse plans, and traffic engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔢 Derivation 1: Number of GSM Carriers and Total Channels in the 900 MHz Band

> **Problem:** The GSM-900 uplink band spans 890–915 MHz. With each carrier occupying 200 kHz and a 100 kHz guard band at each edge, determine the number of carriers and the total number of simultaneous full-rate users.

### Step 1: Compute the usable bandwidth
The total uplink band is from 890 MHz to 915 MHz.

$$B_{total} = 915 \text{ MHz} - 890 \text{ MHz} = 25 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 2: Account for the guard band
A **100 kHz guard band** is reserved at each end of the band to prevent interference with adjacent services.

$$B_{usable} = 25 \text{ MHz} - 2 \times 100 \text{ kHz} = 25 \text{ MHz} - 0.2 \text{ MHz} = 24.8 \text{ MHz}$$

> **Reasoning:** Guard bands act as buffer zones — like a safety gap between two adjacent railway tracks to prevent vibration coupling.

### Step 3: Compute the number of GSM carriers
Each GSM carrier occupies a 200 kHz channel.

$$N_{carriers} = \frac{B_{usable}}{B_{carrier}} = \frac{24.8 \text{ MHz}}{200 \text{ kHz}} = \frac{24\,800 \text{ kHz}}{200 \text{ kHz}} = 124$$

### Step 4: Compute the total number of simultaneous users
GSM uses 8 time slots per carrier, each supporting one full-rate voice user.

$$N_{users} = N_{carriers} \times 8 = 124 \times 8 = 992 \text{ users}$$

> **Conclusion:** A single GSM-900 uplink band (with standard guard bands) supports approximately **992 simultaneous full-rate voice users**.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 2: GSM Time Slot and Bit Rate Calculation

> **Problem:** Given that a GSM TDMA frame has 8 time slots and lasts 4.615 ms, find the time slot duration, bit duration, and gross bit rate per channel. Each time slot carries 156.25 bits (148 data + 8.25 training/guard).

### Step 1: Time slot duration

$$T_{slot} = \frac{T_{frame}}{8} = \frac{4.615 \text{ ms}}{8} = 0.5769 \text{ ms} \approx 577 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 2: Bit duration

$$T_b = \frac{T_{slot}}{N_b} = \frac{577 \text{ μs}}{156.25} \approx 3.692 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 3: Gross bit rate per physical channel

$$R_b = \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{3.692 \times 10^{-6}} = 270.833 \text{ kbps}$$

### Step 4: Net user data rate
Of the 156.25 bits per slot, only 148 carry data. Across 8 slots per 4.615 ms:

$$R_{user} = \frac{148 \text{ bits} \times 8 \text{ slots}}{4.615 \text{ ms}} = \frac{1184 \text{ bits}}{4.615 \text{ ms}} \approx 256.55 \text{ kbps (gross per 8 slots)}$$

But **one TCH** uses 1 slot per frame:

$$R_{TCH,gross} = \frac{148 \text{ bits}}{4.615 \text{ ms}} = 32.07 \text{ kbps}$$

After 20 ms segmentation, interleaving, and convolutional coding (rate 1/2 with 9-bit error protection), the user data rate becomes:

$$R_{user} = \frac{260 \text{ bits}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times \frac{1}{1.35} \approx 9.6 \text{ kbps}$$

> **Interpretation:** The 270.833 kbps "raw" bit rate collapses to **9.6 kbps** of user data after speech coding (13 kbps RPE-LTP) → channel coding (rate 1/2 + 9 protection bits) → interleaving.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 3: IS-95 Processing Gain

> **Problem:** IS-95 spreads a 9.6 kbps user data stream with a 1.2288 Mcps chip rate. Calculate the processing gain in linear and dB scales.

### Step 1: Linear Processing Gain
The processing gain is the ratio of spread (chip) rate to the data rate:

$$G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6 \text{ chips/s}}{9.6 \times 10^3 \text{ bits/s}}$$

$$G_p = 128$$

### Step 2: Express in decibels
$$G_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 10 \times 2.1072 \approx 21.07 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 3: Demonstrate the jamming margin
If the minimum SNR at the receiver (after despreading) is, say, 6 dB for acceptable $E_b/N_0$:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{i} = \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o} - G_p = 6 \text{ dB} - 21.07 \text{ dB} = -15.07 \text{ dB}$$

> **Interpretation:** The signal can be **15 dB below the noise floor** at the transmitter input and still be recovered! This is the magic of spread spectrum — 2G CDMA could extract conversations from a sea of interference.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 4: GSM Reuse Cluster Size and Co-Channel Interference

> **Problem:** For a hexagonal cell system with reuse pattern $N = 7$ and cluster offsets $(i=2, j=1)$, find the co-channel reduction factor $q = D/R$ and the $C/I$ ratio (assume path loss exponent $n = 4$).

### Step 1: Compute the co-channel reduction factor
For a hexagonal layout, the distance $D$ between co-channel cells is:

$$\frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$$

### Step 2: Compute C/I ratio
Using the standard hexagonal formula with $n = 4$:

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{6} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5$$

In decibels:

$$\frac{C}{I} \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66 \text{ dB}$$

> **Verification:** GSM requires $C/I \geq 9 \text{ dB}$ for real-time voice (with slow frequency hopping) and 12 dB without hopping. Our $N=7$ plan gives **18.66 dB**, providing a 6+ dB safety margin.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 5: Frequency Reuse Pattern — Cluster Size $N$

For a hexagonal cellular layout, the cluster size is given by:

$$N = i^2 + ij + j^2$$

where $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers, $i \geq j$.

| $i$ | $j$ | $N$ | Use case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 3 | High capacity, urban (tight reuse) |
| 2 | 0 | 4 | High capacity |
| 2 | 1 | 7 | **Standard GSM** |
| 3 | 0 | 9 | Suburban |
| 3 | 2 | 19 | Low-density rural |

> **Why?:** Larger $N$ = more frequency reuse distance = lower interference but fewer channels per cell = lower capacity. The trade-off is **capacity vs. quality**.

---

## 📊 Comparative Analysis Matrix: GSM vs. IS-95

| Feature | GSM | IS-95 |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiple Access** | TDMA / FDMA | CDMA |
| **Bandwidth** | 200 kHz | 1.25 MHz |
| **Soft Capacity?** | ❌ No (hard limit) | ✅ Yes |
| **Handoff** | Hard | Soft / Softer |
| **Frequency Reuse** | 1/7 (cluster) | 1/1 (universal reuse) |
| **Receiver Complexity** | Simple | RAKE receiver required |
| **Power Control** | Slow (BSC) | Fast (every 1.25 ms) |
| **Voice Activity Exploited?** | ❌ No | ✅ VAF ≈ 0.4 |
| **Sectorization Gain** | Limited | 3× with 3 sectors |
| **Interference** | Co-channel | Self-interference |
| **Synchronization** | Not required | Strict (GPS-based) |
| **Deployment Cost** | Lower | Higher (RAKE) |
| **Cell Capacity (typical)** | 8–10 users/carrier | 20–25 users/sector |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📐 Diagram 1: GSM Network Architecture (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS[Mobile Station]
    SIM[SIM Card]
    BTS1[BTS Cell A]
    BTS2[BTS Cell B]
    BSC1[BSC 1]
    BSC2[BSC 2]
    MSC[Mobile Switching Center MSC]
    HLR[Home Location Register HLR]
    VLR[Visitor Location Register VLR]
    AUC[Authentication Center AUC]
    EIR[Equipment Identity Register EIR]
    PSTN[Public Switched Telephone Network PSTN]
    OSS[Operations Support System OSS]

    SIM -.embed.-> MS
    MS -- Radio Air Interface Um --> BTS1
    MS -- Radio Air Interface Um --> BTS2
    BTS1 -- Abis Interface --> BSC1
    BTS2 -- Abis Interface --> BSC2
    BSC1 -- A Interface --> MSC
    BSC2 -- A Interface --> MSC
    MSC --- HLR
    MSC --- VLR
    MSC --- AUC
    MSC --- EIR
    MSC -- Gateway --> PSTN
    MSC -.OSS link.-> OSS
    HLR -.Signaling SS7.-> VLR
    AUC -.Authentication data.-> HLR
    EIR -.IMEI validation.-> MSC

    classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef access fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000

    class MS,SIM user
    class BTS1,BTS2,BSC1,BSC2 access
    class MSC,HLR,VLR,AUC,EIR core
    class PSTN,OSS ext
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A subscriber's call travels: **MS → BTS → BSC → MSC → PSTN**, while subscriber data is fetched from **HLR** and authentication triplets from **AUC**.

---

## 📐 Diagram 2: GSM TDMA / FDMA Frame Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HF[Hyperframe\n3h 28min 53.76s\n2,048 Superframes]
    SF[Superframe\n6.12 seconds\n1,326 TDMA Frames]
    MF26[Multiframe Type 1\n120 ms\n26 TDMA Frames\nfor TCH and SACCH]
    MF51[Multiframe Type 2\n235.4 ms\n51 TDMA Frames\nfor CCH and BCCH]
    FR[TDMA Frame\n4.615 ms\n8 Time Slots]
    TS[Time Slot\n577 microsec\n156.25 bits]
    BIT[Bit\n3.69 microsec]
    NB[Normal Burst\n156 bits\n57 bits payload x 8 blocks]

    HF --> SF
    SF --> MF26
    SF --> MF51
    MF26 --> FR
    MF51 --> FR
    FR --> TS
    TS --> BIT
    TS --> NB

    classDef lvl1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef lvl3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef lvl4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef lvl5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000

    class HF,SF lvl1
    class MF26,MF51 lvl2
    class FR lvl3
    class TS lvl4
    class BIT,NB lvl5
```

---

## 📐 Diagram 3: GSM Speech Coding & Transmission Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Speech analog\nmicrophone]
    ADC[Analog to Digital\n8 kHz sampling]
    RPE[RPE LTP Codec\n13 kbps output]
    CC[Convolutional\nChannel Coding\nrate 1 over 2]
    INT[Interleaving\n8 x 57 bit blocks]
    BF[Burst Formatting\nNormal Burst\n156 bits]
    GMSK[GMSK Modulation\nBT equals 0.3]
    RF[RF Transmission\n900 MHz band]
    CH[Wireless Channel\nmultipath fading]
    RX[Receiver Path\nreverse order]
    SPK[Speaker analog]

    SP --> ADC --> RPE --> CC --> INT --> BF --> GMSK --> RF --> CH --> RX --> SPK

    classDef tx fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef rx fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    class SP,ADC,RPE,CC,INT,BF,GMSK,RF tx
    class CH ch
    class RX,SPK rx
```

---

## 📐 Diagram 4: IS-95 Forward and Reverse Link Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER1[User 1\nunique PN code]
    USER2[User 2\nunique PN code]
    USERN[User N\nunique PN code]
    MIX[Shared 1.25 MHz\nCDMA Channel]
    RAKE[RAKE Receiver\n3 fingers\nmax ratio combining]
    BTS[Base Station\nBTS]
    MSC2[Mobile Switching\nCenter]

    USER1 --> MIX
    USER2 --> MIX
    USERN --> MIX
    MIX --> RAKE
    RAKE --> BTS
    BTS --> MSC2

    PC1[Power Control\nClosed loop\nevery 1.25 ms]
    MIX -.feedback.-> PC1
    PC1 -.adjusts.-> USER1
    PC1 -.adjusts.-> USER2
    PC1 -.adjusts.-> USERN

    classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    class USER1,USER2,USERN user
    class MIX,RAKE,BTS,MSC2 sys
    class PC1 ctrl
```

> **Interpretation:** Multiple users share the **same 1.25 MHz frequency simultaneously** but are separated by unique PN codes. The **RAKE receiver** combines 3 multipath components, and **fast power control** prevents the near-far problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the four subsystems of the GSM network architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The GSM network is divided into four major subsystems:

1. **Mobile Station (MS):** The user equipment, comprising the Mobile Equipment (handset) and the SIM card. The SIM stores the IMSI and authentication key (Ki), enabling user mobility across handsets.

2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS):** Includes the **BTS** (Base Transceiver Station), which handles the radio air-interface and channel coding, and the **BSC** (Base Station Controller), which manages multiple BTS, performs handover decisions, frequency hopping, and radio resource allocation.

3. **Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS):** Core network containing the **MSC** (call switching), **HLR** (permanent subscriber database), **VLR** (temporary subscriber database for roaming users), **AUC** (authentication triplet generation), and **EIR** (equipment identity validation against stolen handsets).

4. **Operation Support Subsystem (OSS):** Centralized management of the network including billing, fault management, performance monitoring, and configuration control.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for naming each subsystem, 0.5 marks for a brief description each. (Total 3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why does IS-95 require fast power control (every 1.25 ms), whereas GSM uses slower power control?**

**Model Answer:**

IS-95 uses **Direct Sequence CDMA** where all users transmit in the **same 1.25 MHz frequency band** simultaneously, separated only by their unique PN codes. This makes CDMA highly susceptible to the **near-far problem**: a mobile close to the base station could drown out a far user's signal (even with codes) if their powers are not perfectly equalized.

Hence, IS-95 requires **fast closed-loop power control at 800 Hz (every 1.25 ms)** with ± 6 dB adjustments to maintain equal received power at the BTS for all users. The dynamic range is **80 dB**.

In contrast, **GSM uses TDMA/FDMA** — users are isolated by **time and frequency**, not codes. Interference is dominated by **co-channel interference from neighboring cells**, which is far more stable and changes with slow log-normal shadowing. Hence, GSM uses slow power control (~ every 480 ms) at best, sufficient to combat slow fading and save battery.

**Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks for near-far problem + CDMA reasoning, 1 mark for GSM TDMA/FDMA reasoning, 0.5 mark for the specific timing difference. (Total 3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the GSM system architecture. List the functions of HLR, VLR, AUC, and EIR. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the GSM TDMA frame structure with a neat diagram. Calculate the number of carriers and the total number of users per cell for a GSM-900 network (uplink: 890–915 MHz, carrier BW = 200 kHz, 8 time slots/frame, 100 kHz guard band at each edge). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q.A (a): GSM Architecture**

GSM is divided into four subsystems:

**1. Mobile Station (MS):**
- Mobile Equipment (ME) + SIM card
- SIM stores IMSI, Ki, MSISDN, PIN, contacts

**2. Base Station Subsystem (BSS):**
- **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** Antenna, transceiver, channel coder/decoder, encryption unit
- **BSC (Base Station Controller):** Manages 30–40 BTS; handles intra-BSC handovers, frequency hopping, channel allocation, power control

**3. Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS):**
- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Routes calls, interfaces with PSTN, performs inter-BSC handovers
- **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent database. Stores IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, subscribed services, Ki
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database. Stores IMSI, MSISDN, TMSI, location area, services of roamers
- **AUC (Authentication Center):** Generates (RAND, SRES, Kc) triplets using Ki and A3/A8 algorithms
- **EIR (Equipment Identity Register):** Validates IMEI; maintains white, grey, and black lists

**4. Operation Support Subsystem (OSS):**
- Centralized O&M, billing, network statistics

(Refer to **Diagram 1** in Section 4 for the architecture flow.)

**Marking Scheme:** 4 marks for block diagram + 3 marks for function explanation (0.75 each for HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR). **Total 7 marks.**

---

### **Solution to Q.A (b): TDMA Frame + Capacity Calculation**

**GSM Frame Hierarchy (refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4):**

- 1 Hyperframe = 2,048 Superframes = 2,715,648 TDMA frames = **3 h 28 min 53.76 s**
- 1 Superframe = 1,326 TDMA frames = 6.12 s
- 1 Multiframe (traffic) = 26 frames = 120 ms
- 1 Multiframe (control) = 51 frames = 235.4 ms
- 1 TDMA frame = 8 time slots = **4.615 ms**
- 1 Time slot = 156.25 bits = **0.577 ms**
- 1 Bit = **3.69 μs**

**Capacity Calculation:**

$$B_{total} = 915 - 890 = 25 \text{ MHz}$$

$$B_{usable} = 25 \text{ MHz} - 2 \times 0.1 \text{ MHz} = 24.8 \text{ MHz}$$

$$N_{carriers} = \frac{24.8 \text{ MHz}}{200 \text{ kHz}} = 124 \text{ carriers}$$

$$N_{users/cell} = 124 \times 8 = 992 \text{ full-rate users}$$

**Time slot & bit rate:**

$$T_{slot} = 4.615/8 = 0.577 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_b = 1/(3.69 \text{ μs}) = 270.833 \text{ kbps per physical channel}$$

$$R_{user} = 9.6 \text{ kbps (after coding + interleaving)}$$

**Marking Scheme:** 3 marks for frame structure diagram, 2 marks for carrier calculation, 2 marks for user capacity derivation. **Total 7 marks.**

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the principle of spread spectrum in IS-95. Derive the processing gain and discuss its significance. A CDMA system uses a chip rate of 1.2288 Mcps to spread a 9.6 kbps data signal. Calculate the processing gain in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare GSM (TDMA/FDMA) and IS-95 (CDMA) with respect to multiple access, capacity, handoff, power control, and interference management. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q.B (a): Spread Spectrum in IS-95**

**Principle:**
In Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS), each user data bit (rate $R_b$) is multiplied by a high-rate Pseudo-Noise (PN) code (rate $R_c$). This spreads the signal bandwidth from $R_b$ to $R_c$, making it appear as low-level noise to non-decoding receivers.

**IS-95 Parameters:**
- Chip rate $R_c = 1.2288$ Mcps
- User data rate $R_b = 9.6$ kbps

**Derivation:**

$$G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 128$$

$$G_p(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(128) = 10 \times 2.1072 = 21.07 \text{ dB}$$

**Significance:**

1. **Anti-jamming:** A 21 dB processing gain means the despreader can recover the signal even if it is **21 dB below the noise floor** at the input.
2. **Multiple access:** Many users can share the same 1.25 MHz band, separated by unique orthogonal (Walsh) codes.
3. **Low probability of intercept:** The wideband signal is indistinguishable from background noise for non-authorized receivers.
4. **Multipath resolution:** A chip duration of 813.8 ns allows resolving multipath components separated by ~ 244 m — RAKE receiver combines them constructively.

**Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for principle, 2 marks for derivation, 3 marks for significance discussion. **Total 7 marks.**

---

### **Solution to Q.B (b): GSM vs. IS-95 Comparison**

| Parameter | GSM (TDMA/FDMA) | IS-95 (CDMA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiple Access** | TDMA + FDMA (8 slots × 200 kHz) | DS-CDMA (1.25 MHz shared) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 200 kHz per carrier | 1.25 MHz total |
| **Capacity** | Hard-limited; 8 users per carrier per cell | Soft capacity; degrades gracefully |
| **Frequency Reuse** | 1/7 cluster typical | Universal reuse (1/1) |
| **Handoff** | Hard (break-before-make) | Soft (make-before-break) |
| **Power Control** | Slow (optional, ~ 480 ms) | Fast (every 1.25 ms, ± 6 dB) |
| **Interference Management** | Frequency planning + reuse pattern | Power control + sectorization + VAF |
| **Receiver** | Standard equalizer (Viterbi) | RAKE receiver (3 fingers) |
| **Synchronization** | Not strictly required | Required (GPS-based) |
| **Cell Breathing?** | No | Yes (capacity shrinks with load) |
| **Sectorization Gain** | Limited | 3× with 3 sectors |
| **Voice Activity** | Not exploited | Exploited (VAF ≈ 0.4 → 2.5×) |

**Diagrammatic Representation** (can be drawn in the answer):

```
GSM:  Time → |Slot 0|Slot 1|Slot 2|...|Slot 7|
       Freq →  F1, F2, F3, ..., F124  (each 200 kHz)

IS-95: All users share same 1.25 MHz, separated by code.
       User A: c_A(t).d_A(t)
       User B: c_B(t).d_B(t)
       ...
       User N: c_N(t).d_N(t)
```

**Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks per parameter (covering 5+ parameters well) ≈ 7–8 marks. Ensure at least 5–6 parameters are compared with reasoning, not just tabular.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing HLR and VLR:** Many students write that HLR stores temporary data. **HLR is PERMANENT** (home network); **VLR is TEMPORARY** (current visiting area). Wrong = 1 mark lost.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the guard band calculation:** When asked for number of carriers, students often forget the 100 kHz guard band subtraction. **Always state:** "After accounting for 2 × 100 kHz guard bands, usable bandwidth = 24.8 MHz."
>
> 3. **Wrong processing gain unit:** Students compute linear gain (128) and forget the dB conversion (21.07 dB). **Always provide BOTH** unless specifically asked.
>
> 4. **Mixing IS-95 chip rate:** IS-95 has TWO chip rates historically — 1.2288 Mcps (IS-95A/B). Do not confuse with WCDMA's 3.84 Mcps.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Power Control significance:** In 14-mark questions on CDMA, **mention the near-far problem explicitly** before explaining power control. Examiners reward it.
>
> 6. **Conflating GSM and IS-95 channel spacing:** GSM = 200 kHz, IS-95 = 1.25 MHz. A common silly error.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning frequency hopping** in GSM as a form of interference averaging (only FFSK/GMSK).
>
> 8. **Forgetting VAF (Voice Activity Factor)** in CDMA capacity discussions — examiners expect "**~ 40% talk spurts → 2.5× capacity boost**".

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **2G = digital**; replaces 1G analog. **Two standards: GSM (TDMA/FDMA) and IS-95 (CDMA)**
- ⭐ **GSM = 200 kHz carrier, 8 slots/frame, 4.615 ms frame, 270.833 kbps gross, 9.6 kbps user**
- ⭐ **Speech codec = RPE-LTP (13 kbps)**, channel coding = rate 1/2 convolutional, modulation = **GMSK (BT=0.3)**
- ⭐ **GSM architecture = MS + BSS (BTS+BSC) + NSS (MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR) + OSS**
- ⭐ **HLR permanent, VLR temporary, AUC for authentication, EIR for stolen phones**
- ⭐ **Logical channels: TCH (traffic), BCH (broadcast), CCCH (common control: RACH/PCH/AGCH), DCCH (SDCCH)**
- ⭐ **GSM uses 1/7 cluster reuse (typical); 3 sectors common**
- ⭐ **IS-95 = DS-CDMA; 1.25 MHz; 1.2288 Mcps chip rate; 9.6 kbps user data**
- ⭐ **Processing gain = 128 (21 dB); jamming margin ~ 15 dB below noise**
- ⭐ **CDMA = soft capacity, soft handoff, RAKE receiver, fast power control (1.25 ms)**
- ⭐ **Voice Activity Factor ≈ 0.4 → 2.5× CDMA capacity**
- ⭐ **Sectorization (3 sectors) → 3× CDMA capacity**
- ⭐ **2.5G: GPRS (171.2 kbps), EDGE (384 kbps with 8-PSK)**
- ⭐ **Frame structure: Hyperframe → Superframe → Multiframe → TDMA Frame → Time slot → Bit**
- ⭐ **Authentication uses RAND, SRES, Kc triplets from AUC**
- ⭐ **Interleaving depth = 8 (in GSM) → distributes burst errors**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Third Generation (3G) Wireless Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Third Generation (3G) Wireless Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of 3G Wireless Networks

**Third Generation (3G)** wireless networks refer to the generation of mobile telecommunications standards and technology defined by the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU)** under the **IMT-2000 (International Mobile Telecommunications-2000)** specification framework. 3G systems are designed to support high-speed data transmission, multimedia services, and global roaming across multiple network standards.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> 3G is a generation of mobile communication technology that supports data rates of at least **144 kbps** (mobile users in vehicles), **384 kbps** (pedestrians), and **2 Mbps** (stationary indoor users), enabling services like video calling, mobile TV, GPS-based location services, and high-speed internet browsing.

## 1.2 Evolution Path from 2G to 3G

The evolution of cellular networks shows a clear generational progression:

| Generation | Year | Key Technology | Data Rate | Switching |
|------------|------|----------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1G | 1980s | Analog (AMPS, NMT) | 2.4 kbps | Circuit |
| 2G | 1990s | Digital (GSM) | 9.6–14.4 kbps | Circuit + SMS |
| 2.5G | Late 1990s | GPRS | 56–114 kbps | Packet |
| 2.75G | Early 2000s | EDGE | 120–384 kbps | Packet |
| **3G** | **2000s** | **WCDMA/CDMA2000** | **144 kbps – 2 Mbps** | **Packet** |
| 3.5G | Mid 2000s | HSPA | 1.8–14.4 Mbps | Packet |
| 3.75G | Late 2000s | HSPA+, EV-DO | 42–100 Mbps (theoretical) | Packet |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE] Real-World Analogy
> Imagine 2G as a narrow, single-lane village road where only one bullock cart (voice call) can pass at a time. 3G is like a modern multi-lane highway where multiple vehicles (voice, video, data) travel simultaneously at high speed, all coordinated by traffic signals (code-based multiplexing). Just as a highway system needs proper entry/exit ramps (NodeB, RNC) and a central control station (Core Network), 3G requires a sophisticated infrastructure of base stations and switching centers to manage diverse traffic types seamlessly.

## 1.4 Key Features of 3G Networks

- **Universal Roaming & Global Standardization**: Single standard usable across countries
- **High Data Rates**: Up to **2 Mbps** for stationary, **384 kbps** for pedestrian, **144 kbps** for vehicular
- **Packet-Switched Architecture**: All traffic carried as IP packets
- **CDMA-Based Air Interface**: Uses Code Division Multiple Access instead of TDMA/FDMA
- **Multimedia Support**: Video calling, streaming, mobile TV
- **QoS Differentiation**: Four traffic classes — conversational, streaming, interactive, background
- **Backward Compatibility**: Supports 2G/2.5G handover

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Frequently Tested Highlight
> The four standard 3G traffic classes defined by 3GPP are:
> 1. **Conversational** (real-time voice/video) — strictest delay requirements
> 2. **Streaming** (audio/video streaming) — delay-tolerant but bandwidth-sensitive
> 3. **Interactive** (web browsing) — low error rate required
> 4. **Background** (email, FTP) — best-effort, lowest priority
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 3G Standards Family (IMT-2000)

The IMT-2000 family consists of five radio interfaces:

```
IMT-2000 Family
├── IMT-DS (Direct Spread)      → UMTS / WCDMA (FDD)
├── IMT-MC (Multi-Carrier)      → CDMA2000 1x/EV-DO
├── IMT-TC (Time-Code)          → TD-SCDMA, TD-CDMA
├── IMT-SC (Single Carrier)     → EDGE
└── IMT-FT (Frequency Time)     → DECT
```

## 2.2 UMTS (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System)

UMTS is the European 3G standard developed by 3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project).

### 2.2.1 UMTS Architecture Components

1. **User Equipment (UE)**: Mobile device + USIM card
2. **UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)**: NodeB (base station) + RNC (Radio Network Controller)
3. **Core Network (CN)**: MSC/VLR, SGSN, GGSN, HLR, AuC

### 2.2.2 WCDMA Air Interface Parameters

WCDMA uses a **5 MHz** carrier bandwidth with chip rate of **3.84 Mcps** (Mega chips per second).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula/Value | Description | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|------|
| Chip Rate ($R_c$) | $R_c = 3.84 \times 10^6$ | WCDMA chip rate | cps |
| Symbol Rate ($R_s$) | $R_s = R_c / SF$ | Symbol rate after despreading | sps |
| Spreading Factor (SF) | $SF = 4 \text{ to } 512$ | Number of chips per symbol | chips/symbol |
| Processing Gain ($G_p$) | $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(SF)$ | Spreading gain | dB |
| Bit Energy | $E_b = R_c / R_b$ | Energy per information bit | J |
| SNR after despreading | $(E_b/N_0)$ | Bit energy to noise ratio | dB |
| Capacity (Single Cell) | $N = 1 + \frac{W/R}{(E_b/N_0)}$ | Poles capacity formula | users/cell |
| Pole's Capacity Limit | $N_{\max} = 1 + \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0}$ | Max users with $E_b/N_0$ requirement | users/cell |
| Walsh Code Count | $N_{code} = SF$ | Max orthogonal codes per SF | codes |
| HSPA Peak Downlink | $14.4 \text{ Mbps}$ | 16-QAM, SF=16, 3 codes | Mbps |
| HSPA+ Peak Downlink | $42 \text{ Mbps}$ | 64-QAM, 2x2 MIMO | Mbps |
| Frame Duration (WCDMA) | $T_f = 10 \text{ ms}$ | Radio frame length | ms |
| Slot Duration | $T_{slot} = 0.667 \text{ ms}$ | 15 slots per frame | ms |
| Uplink Band (UMTS FDD) | $1920 \text{–} 1980 \text{ MHz}$ | UE → NodeB | MHz |
| Downlink Band (UMTS FDD) | $2110 \text{–} 2170 \text{ MHz}$ | NodeB → UE | MHz |

> [!NOTE] Engineering Application
> WCDMA's processing gain allows 3G to coexist with 2G systems and operate at low SNR, making it robust against interference — a critical feature for dense urban deployments.

## 2.4 HSPA (High Speed Packet Access) Evolution

HSPA is the 3.5G enhancement to UMTS:

- **HSDPA (High Speed Downlink Packet Access)**: Peak DL rate **14.4 Mbps** using 16-QAM, 15 spreading codes
  - Introduced: Adaptive Modulation, Fast Scheduling, Hybrid ARQ
- **HSUPA (High Speed Uplink Packet Access)**: Peak UL rate **5.76 Mbps**
  - Introduced: Enhanced uplink DCH
- **HSPA+**: Peak rates **42 Mbps** (DL) / **11.5 Mbps** (UL) using 64-QAM + MIMO

## 2.5 Key Operational Concepts

### 2.5.1 Soft Handover
A UE maintains simultaneous connections with **multiple NodeBs** during handover, using macro-diversity combining — unique to CDMA systems.

### 2.5.2 Power Control
Fast power control at **1500 Hz** updates UE transmit power every 0.667 ms to combat near-far effect in CDMA.

### 2.5.3 Spreading and Scrambling
- **Spreading codes** (orthogonal, e.g., OVSF): Channelization — preserve orthogonality within a cell
- **Scrambling codes** (pseudo-random): Cell identification — distinguish cells

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Question Point
> Difference between Spreading Code and Scrambling Code:
> - **Spreading** increases bandwidth and provides channelization (orthogonal within cell)
> - **Scrambling** does NOT increase bandwidth; only identifies the cell/sector on the downlink, or the UE on the uplink
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Processing Gain in WCDMA

The processing gain quantifies the immunity of a spread spectrum system to narrowband interference.

### Step 1: Define Chip and Bit Rates
Let $R_c$ be the chip rate (chips per second) and $R_b$ be the information bit rate (bits per second).

$$
R_c = 3.84 \times 10^6 \text{ chips/second (WCDMA standard)}
$$

### Step 2: Define Spreading Factor
The spreading factor $SF$ is the number of chips used to represent one information bit:

$$
SF = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{\text{Chip Rate}}{\text{Bit Rate}}
$$

For a voice service with $R_b = 9.6 \text{ kbps}$:
$$
SF = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 400
$$

> Note: WCDMA uses $SF$ values from 4 to 512 in powers of 2 for uplink, and 4 to 512 for downlink.

### Step 3: Convert to Decibel Form
The processing gain in dB is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p \text{ (dB)} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(400) \\
&= 10 \times 2.602 \\
&\approx 26.02 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
A processing gain of **26 dB** means the despreader can recover the original bit even when the input SNR is as low as $-26$ dB (theoretically), as long as the interference is broadband.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pole's Capacity Formula (CDMA Capacity)

For a single-cell CDMA system, the maximum number of users is bounded by the pole capacity.

### Step 1: Express Received Signal Power
Assume perfect power control, so each user signal arrives at the base station with equal power $S$. Total received signal power from $N$ users is $N \cdot S$.

### Step 2: Compute the Required $E_b/N_0$
The bit energy to effective noise plus interference ratio is:

$$
\frac{E_b}{(N_0 + I_0)} = \frac{S/R_b}{(N-1)S/W}
$$

where $W$ is the spread bandwidth and $I_0$ is the interference spectral density.

### Step 3: Rearrange the Equation
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_b}{(N_0 + I_0)} &= \frac{S}{R_b} \cdot \frac{W}{(N-1)S} \\
&= \frac{W/R_b}{N-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for N (Number of Users)
$$
\begin{aligned}
N - 1 &= \frac{W/R_b}{E_b/(N_0 + I_0)} \\
N &= 1 + \frac{W/R_b}{E_b/N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Pole's Capacity Formula** for a single-cell CDMA system.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** WCDMA system has $W = 3.84$ MHz, voice bit rate $R_b = 9.6$ kbps, and required $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB (linear: 5.01). Find capacity.

**Solution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\max} &= 1 + \frac{3.84 \times 10^6 / 9.6 \times 10^3}{5.01} \\
&= 1 + \frac{400}{5.01} \\
&= 1 + 79.84 \\
&\approx 81 \text{ users}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 3G vs 2G Comparison

| Parameter | 2G (GSM) | 3G (UMTS/WCDMA) | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Multiple Access | TDMA/FDMA hybrid | CDMA (Direct Sequence) | CDMA offers soft capacity |
| Carrier Bandwidth | 200 kHz | 5 MHz | 25× more bandwidth |
| Data Rate (Peak) | 14.4 kbps (GPRS: 114) | 2 Mbps (HSPA: 14.4) | ~1000× improvement |
| Handover Type | Hard handover | Soft handover (make-before-break) | Reduced call drops |
| Power Control Rate | 2 Hz (slow) | 1500 Hz (fast) | Mitigates near-far effect |
| Cell Breathing | Not applicable | Yes (load-dependent) | Capacity-coverage tradeoff |
| Frequency Reuse | 1/7 or 1/3 | 1 (universal reuse) | Higher spectral efficiency |
| Voice Codec | 13 kbps (EFR) | Adaptive Multi-Rate (AMR) | Better voice quality |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Spreading Factor Trade-off

### Step 1: Relationship between SF and Bit Rate
For a fixed chip rate of 3.84 Mcps:

$$
R_b = \frac{R_c}{SF} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{SF}
$$

### Step 2: Compute for Various SF Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF = 4 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad R_b = 960 \text{ kbps} \\
SF = 16 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad R_b = 240 \text{ kbps} \\
SF = 64 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad R_b = 60 \text{ kbps} \\
SF = 256 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad R_b = 15 \text{ kbps} \\
SF = 512 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad R_b = 7.5 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Engineering Trade-off
- **Lower SF** → higher bit rate → less processing gain → more vulnerable to interference
- **Higher SF** → lower bit rate → more processing gain → more robust

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Problem-Solving Note
> Always convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear scale using $10^{(E_b/N_0)/10}$ before substituting into the Pole's capacity formula. Example: 7 dB → $10^{0.7} \approx 5.01$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UMTS Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UE_LAYER["User Equipment Layer"]
        UE_MOBILE["UE Mobile Station"]
        UE_SIM["USIM Card"]
    end

    subgraph UTRAN_LAYER["UTRAN Access Network"]
        NODEB1["NodeB Site A"]
        NODEB2["NodeB Site B"]
        NODEB3["NodeB Site C"]
        RNC_MAIN["Radio Network Controller RNC"]
    end

    subgraph CN_LAYER["Core Network CN"]
        SGSN_NODE["SGSN Packet Switched"]
        GGSN_NODE["GGSN Gateway GPRS"]
        MSC_NODE["MSC Circuit Switched"]
        HLR_NODE["HLR Home Location Register"]
        AUC_NODE["AuC Authentication"]
    end

    EXT_NET["External Internet / PSTN"]

    UE_MOBILE -->|"Uu Radio Interface"| NODEB1
    UE_MOBILE -->|"Uu Radio Interface"| NODEB2
    UE_SIM -.->|"Identity and Auth"| UE_MOBILE
    NODEB1 -->|"Iub Interface"| RNC_MAIN
    NODEB2 -->|"Iub Interface"| RNC_MAIN
    NODEB3 -->|"Iub Interface"| RNC_MAIN
    RNC_MAIN -->|"Iu-PS Interface"| SGSN_NODE
    RNC_MAIN -->|"Iu-CS Interface"| MSC_NODE
    SGSN_NODE -->|"Gn Interface"| GGSN_NODE
    SGSN_NODE -->|"Gr Interface"| HLR_NODE
    GGSN_NODE -->|"Gi Interface"| EXT_NET
    MSC_NODE -->|"PSTN Gateway"| EXT_NET
    HLR_NODE -->|"MAP Signaling"| AUC_NODE

    classDef ueStyle fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef utranStyle fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cnStyle fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef extStyle fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class UE_MOBILE,UE_SIM ueStyle
    class NODEB1,NODEB2,NODEB3,RNC_MAIN utranStyle
    class SGSN_NODE,GGSN_NODE,MSC_NODE,HLR_NODE,AUC_NODE cnStyle
    class EXT_NET extStyle
```

## 4.2 WCDMA Protocol Stack (User Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UPLANE["User Plane Uplink and Downlink"]
        APP_LAYER["Application Layer HTTP, RTP, VoIP"]
        PDCP_LAYER["PDCP Layer Header Compression"]
        RLC_LAYER["RLC Layer Segmentation and Reassembly"]
        MAC_LAYER["MAC Layer HARQ, Scheduling, Multiplexing"]
        PHY_LAYER["Physical Layer Spreading, Scrambling, Modulation"]
        RF_LAYER["RF Frontend Antenna, PA, LNA"]
    end

    APP_LAYER --> PDCP_LAYER
    PDCP_LAYER --> RLC_LAYER
    RLC_LAYER --> MAC_LAYER
    MAC_LAYER --> PHY_LAYER
    PHY_LAYER --> RF_LAYER

    classDef layerStyle fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class APP_LAYER,PDCP_LAYER,RLC_LAYER,MAC_LAYER,PHY_LAYER,RF_LAYER layerStyle
```

## 4.3 3G Evolution Timeline (HSDPA → HSPA+)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UMTS3G["UMTS Release 99\n2 Mbps Peak"]
    HSDPA["HSDPA Release 5\n14.4 Mbps DL"]
    HSUPA["HSUPA Release 6\n5.76 Mbps UL"]
    HSPA["HSPA Combined\n14.4 Down + 5.76 Up"]
    HSPAPLUS["HSPA Plus Release 7\n42 Mbps DL with MIMO"]

    UMTS3G --> HSDPA
    HSDPA --> HSUPA
    HSUPA --> HSPA
    HSPA --> HSPAPLUS

    classDef genStyle fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class UMTS3G,HSDPA,HSUPA,HSPA,HSPAPLUS genStyle
```

## 4.4 Spreading and Scrambling Process in WCDMA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DATA_IN["Information Bits\nRate Rb"]
    SPREAD_CODE["OVSF Channelization Code\nLength SF"]
    SCRAMBLE_CODE["Scrambling Code\nLength 38400 chips per frame"]
    CHIP_OUT["Chip Stream\nRate 3.84 Mcps"]
    MODULATOR["Modulator QPSK 16QAM"]
    RF_OUT["RF Transmitted Signal"]

    DATA_IN --> SPREAD_CODE
    SPREAD_CODE --> SCRAMBLE_CODE
    SCRAMBLE_CODE --> CHIP_OUT
    CHIP_OUT --> MODULATOR
    MODULATOR --> RF_OUT

    classDef processStyle fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
