<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define 3G Wireless Networks. List any four key features.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**
3G (Third Generation) wireless networks are mobile communication systems standardized under ITU's IMT-2000 framework, designed to provide high-speed data and multimedia services.

**Key Features (any four):**
1. Data rate up to **2 Mbps** for stationary users
2. Packet-switched core network (IP-based)
3. Global roaming with a single standard
4. Support for multimedia services (video calling, mobile TV)
5. Uses CDMA (WCDMA/CDMA2000) air interface
6. QoS differentiation with four traffic classes

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Pitfall
> Students often confuse 2.5G (GPRS) with 3G. Remember: 3G explicitly uses **packet-switched** data at speeds **above 144 kbps** with **CDMA-based** access. Do not write GSM/EDGE as 3G.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of OVSF codes in WCDMA? State the chip rate of WCDMA.
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**
**Role of OVSF Codes:** Orthogonal Variable Spreading Factor (OVSF) codes are used as **channelization codes** in WCDMA. They allow multiple users to share the same 5 MHz bandwidth by maintaining orthogonality between different physical channels. They preserve orthogonality within a cell so that different users/spreading factors can coexist without mutual interference (as long as the orthogonality condition is satisfied).

**Chip Rate:** $R_c = 3.84 \text{ Mcps}$

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Pitfall
> OVSF codes are **channelization** codes (orthogonal, preserve bandwidth structure), NOT scrambling codes. Students frequently mix these up and lose 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): 3G UMTS Architecture and Air Interface

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the UMTS network architecture. List the functions of each major component.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The UMTS architecture is divided into three main parts:

1. **User Equipment (UE)**:
   - Mobile Equipment (ME) + Universal Subscriber Identity Module (USIM)
   - Contains the user's identity, performs authentication, stores subscriber data
   - Connects to UTRAN via the Uu radio interface

2. **UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)**:
   - **NodeB**: Equivalent to GSM BTS; performs air-interface transmission/reception, channel coding, power control
   - **RNC (Radio Network Controller)**: Manages radio resources, handles handover (soft/softer), controls NodeBs via Iub interface
   - Provides Iu interface to Core Network

3. **Core Network (CN)**:
   - **SGSN** (Serving GPRS Support Node): Packet-switched domain; tracks UE location, performs security functions
   - **GGSN** (Gateway GPRS Support Node): Gateway to external IP networks
   - **MSC/VLR** (Mobile Switching Center): Circuit-switched voice
   - **HLR/AuC**: Stores permanent subscriber data and authentication keys

**Block Diagram:** (See SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1)

---

**(b) Explain the WCDMA air interface with reference to chip rate, spreading factor, and processing gain. A WCDMA system uses a chip rate of 3.84 Mcps and supports a 9.6 kbps voice service. Calculate the spreading factor and processing gain in dB.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**WCDMA Air Interface Parameters:**
- Carrier bandwidth: 5 MHz
- Chip rate: $R_c = 3.84 \text{ Mcps}$
- Spreading Factor: $SF = 4$ to $512$ (powers of 2)
- Modulation: QPSK (downlink), BPSK/HPSK (uplink)

**Step 1: Calculate the Spreading Factor**
$$
SF = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 400
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Processing Gain in dB**
$$
G_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(SF) = 10 \log_{10}(400) = 10 \times 2.602 = 26.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Significance:**
- Processing gain of 26 dB allows the system to operate at very low SNR
- Voice channel uses $SF = 400$ (one of the allowed values)
- Each chip is spread by an OVSF code; cell is identified by a scrambling code

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Pitfall
> Don't confuse **SF = 400** (WCDMA specific) with GSM's 8-slot TDMA structure. Markers deduct 1 mark if you cite GSM-style parameters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): CDMA Capacity and 3G Evolution

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a) Derive the Pole's capacity formula for a single-cell CDMA system. A WCDMA cell operates with $W = 3.84$ MHz, voice data rate $R_b = 12.2$ kbps, and required $E_b/N_0 = 6 \text{ dB}$. Find the pole capacity.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Setup**
In a single-cell CDMA system with perfect power control, all $N$ users arrive at the base station with equal power $S$. The total interference experienced by one user is $(N-1)S$ (ignoring thermal noise).

**Step 2: Express the $E_b/N_0$ relationship**
$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0 + I_0} = \frac{S/R_b}{(N-1)S/W} = \frac{W/R_b}{N-1}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for N**
$$
N = 1 + \frac{W/R_b}{E_b/N_0}
$$

**Step 4: Convert $E_b/N_0$ to linear scale**
$$
E_b/N_0 \text{ (linear)} = 10^{6/10} = 10^{0.6} = 3.98
$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\max} &= 1 + \frac{3.84 \times 10^6 / 12.2 \times 10^3}{3.98} \\
&= 1 + \frac{314.75}{3.98} \\
&= 1 + 79.08 \\
&\approx 80 \text{ users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Pole capacity is **80 users** per cell.

---

**(b) Compare 2G (GSM) and 3G (UMTS/WCDMA) with respect to: (i) Multiple access technique, (ii) Handover mechanism, (iii) Power control rate, (iv) Frequency reuse, and (v) Maximum data rate.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | 2G (GSM) | 3G (UMTS/WCDMA) |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| (i) Multiple Access | TDMA + FDMA hybrid | CDMA (Direct Sequence) |
| (ii) Handover | Hard handover (break-before-make) | Soft handover (make-before-break) |
| (iii) Power Control | 2 Hz (slow) | 1500 Hz (fast) |
| (iv) Frequency Reuse | 1/7 or 1/3 (clustered) | 1 (universal reuse) |
| (v) Max Data Rate | 14.4 kbps (GPRS: 114 kbps) | 2 Mbps (HSPA: 14.4 Mbps) |

**Engineering Implications:**

- **Soft handover** in 3G eliminates the "ping-pong" effect and reduces call drops at cell boundaries
- **Universal frequency reuse** of $1$ maximizes spectral efficiency but introduces intercell interference, mitigated by fast power control
- **1500 Hz power control** updates every 0.667 ms, essential for CDMA's near-far problem
- **Higher data rates** enable multimedia services (video calling, streaming, mobile internet)

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Pitfall
> Markers expect a **tabular comparison** with crisp 1-line answers per parameter. Writing long paragraphs without a table results in **partial marking**. Always include the table format.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist

- **3G Definition**: ITU IMT-2000 standard, minimum **144 kbps** mobile, **384 kbps** pedestrian, **2 Mbps** stationary
- **3G Standards**: UMTS (WCDMA), CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA
- **UMTS Architecture**: UE + UTRAN (NodeB + RNC) + Core Network (SGSN, GGSN, MSC, HLR)
- **WCDMA Chip Rate**: $3.84$ Mcps on a **5 MHz** carrier
- **Spreading Factor (SF)**: 4 to 512 in powers of 2; SF = $R_c / R_b$
- **Processing Gain**: $G_p = 10 \log_{10}(SF)$ dB
- **OVSF Codes**: Channelization (orthogonal within cell)
- **Scrambling Codes**: Cell identification (do not preserve bandwidth)
- **Soft Handover**: UE connects to multiple NodeBs simultaneously (CDMA-only feature)
- **Fast Power Control**: **1500 Hz** update rate (every 0.667 ms) to combat near-far effect
- **Four Traffic Classes**: Conversational, Streaming, Interactive, Background
- **Pole's Capacity**: $N = 1 + \dfrac{W/R_b}{E_b/N_0}$ (single cell, perfect power control)
- **HSDPA**: 16-QAM, 15 codes, peak **14.4 Mbps** DL (Release 5)
- **HSUPA**: Peak **5.76 Mbps** UL (Release 6)
- **HSPA+**: 64-QAM + MIMO, peak **42 Mbps** DL (Release 7)
- **UMTS FDD Bands**: UL 1920–1980 MHz, DL 2110–2170 MHz
- **Frame Duration**: 10 ms (15 slots × 0.667 ms)
- **Common Mistake**: Spreading codes ≠ Scrambling codes (markers deduct 1.5 marks)
- **Common Mistake**: 2.5G (GPRS/EDGE) is NOT 3G (markers deduct 2 marks)
- **Common Mistake**: Always convert dB to linear before substituting into Pole's formula
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Local Loop (WLL)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Wireless Local Loop (WLL) — Core Foundations

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Local Loop (WLL)

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Wireless Local Loop (WLL)** is a system that uses a **radio link** (instead of conventional copper wire or fiber) to connect subscribers to the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) or to a broadband core network. It is also widely known as **Fixed Wireless Access (FWA)** or **Radio in the Loop (RITL)**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, WLL is positioned as a direct application of terrestrial wireless communication that replaces the **"last mile"** of the traditional access network with a wireless interface operating between the customer premises equipment (CPE) and the base station (BS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Wireless Local Loop (WLL) is a system that connects end users to the public telecommunications network using radio waves as the transmission medium instead of copper wires for the subscriber loop portion of the connection."*

The standardization body **ITU-T** uses the broader term **FWA (Fixed Wireless Access)** to describe systems providing services such as voice telephony, data, and internet to fixed subscriber locations through wireless means.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wireless Telephone Wire" 

Imagine your house needs a telephone line. Traditionally, a telecom company would dig up the road and lay a **copper cable** from their exchange right up to your doorstep. That cable is the **"local loop"** — the final stretch of wire.

Now, imagine the same connection, but **without any cable**. Instead, your house has a small antenna on the roof. A nearby telecom tower has another antenna. They "talk" to each other using radio waves, and your phone calls reach the network exactly the same way as with a wired line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> WLL = **"A phone-line substitute made of radio waves instead of copper wire."** The phone inside your house works normally — but the "wire" connecting you to the exchange is now invisible, traveling through the air as an RF signal.

The only practical difference for the user is that the **CPE (Customer Premises Equipment)** must remain at a **fixed location** — it is not mobile like a cellular handset. The wireless link is therefore designed and optimized for **fixed, line-of-sight or near-line-of-sight** propagation, leading to superior link reliability and higher data rates than mobile systems of the same generation.

---

## 1.3 Why WLL? — The Motivation

The traditional copper local loop suffers from well-documented limitations that motivated the global rollout of WLL systems in the late 1990s and early 2000s:

- **High deployment cost** — trenching, cabling, civil works in dense urban areas.
- **Slow provisioning** — weeks to months to install a new subscriber.
- **Difficult terrain** — hilly, remote, or inaccessible regions where cable laying is impractical.
- **Maintenance overhead** — corrosion, cable cuts, water ingress.

WLL provides a **rapid, cost-effective alternative**, particularly for:
- Developing regions (rural and semi-urban connectivity).
- Urban areas where right-of-way permissions delay cabling.
- Temporary or seasonal demand (construction sites, event venues).
- Backhaul for cellular base stations themselves.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Hook:** Examiners often ask *"Why is WLL preferred over wired loops in certain regions?"* Always answer using the **three pillars**: **Cost**, **Speed of Deployment**, and **Terrain Flexibility**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Frequency Allocations (KTU 2024 — Important Metrics)

WLL systems operate in licensed and unlicensed spectrum bands. The following bands are critical for KTU board questions:

| Service | Acronym | Frequency Band | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multichannel Multipoint Distribution Service | **MMDS** | **2.1 – 2.7 GHz** | 30 – 50 km |
| Local Multipoint Distribution Service | **LMDS** | **24 – 40 GHz** (mm-wave) | 1 – 5 km |
| WLL in cellular bands | DECT, PHS, GSM fixed | 800 MHz – 2 GHz | 5 – 30 km |
| Broadband WLL / WiMAX | IEEE 802.16 | 2 – 66 GHz | 1 – 50 km |
| Modern LTE / 5G FWA | 4G/5G NR | 600 MHz – 6 GHz (Sub-6) | up to 10 km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the MMDS/LMDS duality:** MMDS = **lower frequency = longer range**, LMDS = **higher frequency = shorter range but higher bandwidth**. This contrast is a classic KTU 2-mark question.

---

## 1.5 WLL vs. Mobile Cellular — The Critical Distinction

Although both systems transmit radio signals through air, the engineering assumptions differ significantly. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this distinction clearly:

| Parameter | WLL (Fixed) | Mobile Cellular |
|---|---|---|
| Antenna location | Fixed rooftop CPE | Mobile handset |
| Antenna gain | High directional (15–25 dBi) | Omnidirectional (0–2 dBi) |
| Multipath fading | Mostly fixed / Rician | Severe Rayleigh |
| Power control | Minimal / Static | Aggressive / Dynamic |
| Handover | Not required | Frequent |
| Cell capacity | Higher per user | Lower (shared mobility) |
| Spectrum efficiency | Better | Lower due to mobility overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "WLL is Calm, Mobile is Chaotic":** WLL antennas don't move, so the channel is statistically stable (Rician), while mobile channels experience deep Rayleigh fades.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a WLL System

A typical WLL deployment consists of four major subsystems, each with a clearly defined functional role:

1. **Central Office (CO) / Base Station Controller (BSC):** The interface between the WLL network and the core PSTN/IP backbone.
2. **Base Station (BS) / Access Point (AP):** Mounted on a tower, it serves a sector of subscribers within a coverage cell.
3. **Wireless Air Interface:** The radio link (uplink and downlink) using licensed spectrum.
4. **Customer Premises Equipment (CPE):** A fixed directional antenna + transceiver installed at the subscriber's home or office.

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

- **Step 1 — Subscriber Initiation:** A user lifts the phone / opens a browser. The CPE encodes voice/data into an RF signal.
- **Step 2 — Uplink Transmission:** CPE transmits on the assigned uplink frequency toward the BS. Because the CPE antenna is highly directional, the signal is concentrated in a narrow beam.
- **Step 3 — Reception at BS:** The BS antenna (typically sectorized — 60°, 90°, or 120°) receives the uplink, amplifies it through a Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA), and downconverts it.
- **Step 4 — Routing to Core Network:** The BSC routes the signal into the PSTN (for voice) or the IP backbone (for data).
- **Step 5 — Downlink Return Path:** Traffic destined for the subscriber is modulated, upconverted, and transmitted from the BS. The directional CPE antenna receives this and delivers it to the in-house equipment.
- **Step 6 — Channel Allocation:** Multiple users are multiplexed using **FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, or OFDMA** depending on the standard (DECT, PHS, GSM-fixed, LMDS, WiMAX, LTE-FWA).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Marker Hint:** When asked to *"explain the architecture of a WLL system"*, always draw the **four-block hierarchy**: CO → BS → Air Interface → CPE, and **explicitly mention the duplexing method**.

---

## 2.2 Duplexing Techniques in WLL

WLL systems use two primary duplexing methods:

- **TDD (Time Division Duplexing):** Uplink and downlink share the same frequency but alternate in time. Cheaper, asymmetric traffic friendly. Used in PHS, early WiMAX.
- **FDD (Frequency Division Duplexing):** Uplink and downlink use separate frequencies simultaneously. Better for symmetric voice. Used in most LMDS deployments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FDD for voice-heavy WLL?** Voice is symmetric (50% talk, 50% listen), so FDD's pair of channels naturally fits — no latency penalty from time-slot switching.

---

## 2.3 Propagation Model — Why WLL Differs from Mobile

Because both the BS antenna and the CPE antenna are at **fixed, elevated locations**, the propagation conditions are superior to those of mobile systems:

- **Line-of-Sight (LOS) is usually available** at mm-wave bands.
- **Multipath is reduced** because nearby scatterers are minimized (no moving cars, no pedestrians).
- **Fading follows a Rician distribution** (one dominant LOS component + weaker scattered components), instead of Rayleigh.
- **Path loss is more deterministic** and can be predicted using free-space models with reasonable accuracy.

The **Rician K-factor** (ratio of LOS to scattered power) is therefore a key parameter in WLL link design:

$$
K \;=\; \frac{P_{LOS}}{P_{scattered}} \quad [dimensionless, dB]
$$

Typical values for fixed rooftop links: **K = 6 – 20 dB**, much higher than mobile channels (K ≈ 0 dB).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Wireless Local Loop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **highest-yield formulas** for any WLL problem in the KTU ESE. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | d ≫ λ | dB |
| 2 | FSPL (alternative) | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | Linear / dB | ratio |
| 3 | Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | LOS, far-field | W |
| 4 | Received Power (dB form) | $P_r[dBm] = P_t[dBm] + G_t[dBi] + G_r[dBi] - FSPL[dB] - L_{misc}$ | Including cable/connector loss | dBm |
| 5 | Rician K-factor | $K[dB] = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{LOS}}{P_{scatter}}\right)$ | WLL: 6 – 20 dB | dB |
| 6 | Link Budget (EIRP) | $EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_{feed}$ | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | dBW or dBm |
| 7 | Maximum Path Loss | $L_{max} = EIRP + G_r - P_{r,min} - M_f$ | For cell-radius calculation | dB |
| 8 | Cell Radius (approx) | $R \approx d_{max}$ where FSPL(d) = L_max | Under FSPL model | km |
| 9 | Power Delay Profile — RMS delay spread | $\sigma_{\tau} = \sqrt{\overline{\tau^{2}} - (\overline{\tau})^{2}}$ | WLL: < 50 ns (LOS), > 100 ns (NLOS) | s |
| 10 | Coherence Bandwidth | $B_c \approx \dfrac{1}{5\sigma_{\tau}}$ | Determines flat vs. freq-selective fading | Hz |
| 11 | Fade Margin (Rician) | $F_M[dB] \approx K[dB] + (10\text{ to }12)\;dB$ for 99.9% availability | — | dB |
| 12 | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = P_r - 10\log_{10}(kTB) - N_F$ | Receiver sensitivity bound | dB-Hz |
| 13 | Boltzmann constant | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | Always use **J/K** | W/(Hz·K) |
| 14 | Antenna Gain (parabolic) | $G \approx \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \cdot \eta$ | Aperture efficiency η ≈ 0.55 – 0.65 | dBi |
| 15 | Bandwidth of MMDS | $BW_{MMDS}$ ≈ **198 MHz** total (US allocation) | 33 channels × 6 MHz | MHz |
| 16 | Bandwidth of LMDS | $BW_{LMDS}$ ≈ **1.3 GHz** | Much wider, mm-wave | GHz |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Syntax Rule:** All absolute values and divisions use `\vert` or `\dfrac` to avoid breaking the markdown table. Do **not** use raw `|` inside table cells.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The link-budget equation governs the design of **every** fixed-wireless deployment in the world:

- **5G FWA rollouts** by Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel — using 3.5 GHz TDD spectrum.
- **Rural broadband** — WiMAX 802.16d in the 3.5 GHz band in India (BSNL).
- **Maritime and offshore platforms** — LMDS links for ships at sea.
- **Disaster-recovery networks** — temporary WLL when the wired infrastructure is destroyed.
- **Enterprise redundancy** — backup links for banks, ATMs, and data centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** When you see a numerical problem involving "received power at a CPE", the **Friis equation in dB form** is the only tool you need — combined with a careful link budget.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

We derive the **Friis transmission equation** from first principles, then convert to the logarithmic FSPL form used in all KTU problems.

### Step A — Isotropic Radiator Power Density

An isotropic radiator transmits power uniformly in all directions. At a distance $d$, the radiated power $P_t$ is distributed over a sphere of surface area $4\pi d^{2}$. The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) is therefore:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad [W/m^{2}]
$$

> **Logic:** Total power / total area of the sphere = power per unit area at radius $d$.

### Step B — Effective Aperture of the Receiving Antenna

A receiving antenna intercepts power proportional to its **effective aperture** $A_e$. For an antenna of gain $G_r$:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

> **Logic:** Higher gain antennas physically capture more of the incident wavefront. Gain is proportional to physical aperture normalized by wavelength squared.

### Step C — Received Power

Multiplying the power density by the effective aperture:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r\,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

Factoring and including transmit antenna gain $G_t$ (which boosts the transmitted field):

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the **Friis Transmission Equation**. Note the inverse-square law ($1/d^{2}$) characteristic of free-space propagation, plus the inverse-square dependence on wavelength ($1/\lambda^{2}$).

### Step D — Definition of Path Loss

Path loss $L_p$ is defined as the ratio $P_t G_t G_r / P_r$ (without antenna gains being lumped):

$$
L_p = \frac{P_t}{P_r}\bigg|_{G_t=G_r=1} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}
$$

In decibels:

$$
L_p[dB] = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

### Step E — Engineering Form using Frequency in MHz and Distance in km

Using $\lambda = c / f$, with $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$
L_p[dB] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot d_{km}\cdot 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8} / (f_{MHz} \cdot 10^{6})}\right)
$$

$$
= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot d_{km} \cdot f_{MHz} \cdot 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right)
$$

$$
= 20\log_{10}\!\left(4\pi \cdot d_{km} \cdot f_{MHz} \cdot \frac{10}{3}\right)
$$

$$
= 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{40\pi}{3}\right)
$$

Since $20\log_{10}(40\pi/3) = 20\log_{10}(41.888) \approx 32.44$:

$$
\boxed{\;L_p[dB] \;=\; 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})\;}
$$

> **Logic Check:** This is the **standard FSPL formula** memorized by every wireless engineer worldwide. Verified against published textbook values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Complete Link Budget for a WLL Link

The received power at the CPE in a real WLL deployment is given by:

$$
P_r[dBm] = P_t[dBm] + G_t[dBi] + G_r[dBi] - L_p[dB] - L_{misc}[dB]
$$

where $L_{misc}$ includes **feeder/connector losses**, **rain attenuation** (at mm-wave), **vegetation loss**, and **fade margin**.

### Numerical Worked Example — Board Exam Style

**Problem:**
A WLL system operates at **f = 3.5 GHz**. The base station transmits $P_t = 10$ W through a feeder of loss **1.5 dB**, with a transmit antenna gain of $G_t = 18$ dBi. The CPE uses a receive antenna of gain $G_r = 24$ dBi, and is located at $d = 8$ km from the BS. Miscellaneous losses total $L_{misc} = 2$ dB. Find the received power.

### Step 1 — Convert Transmit Power to dBm

$$
P_t = 10\;W = 10 \cdot 10^{3}\;mW = 10^{4}\;mW
$$

$$
P_t[dBm] = 10\log_{10}(10^{4}) = 40\;dBm
$$

### Step 2 — Compute EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power)

$$
EIRP = P_t[dBm] + G_t[dBi] - L_{feed}[dB]
$$

$$
EIRP = 40 + 18 - 1.5 = 56.5\;dBm
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Free-Space Path Loss

Using $f = 3500$ MHz, $d = 8$ km:

$$
L_p = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(8) + 20\log_{10}(3500)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(8) = 20 \cdot 0.9031 = 18.06\;dB
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(3500) = 20 \cdot 3.5441 = 70.88\;dB
$$

$$
L_p = 32.4 + 18.06 + 70.88 = 121.34\;dB
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Friis in dB

$$
P_r[dBm] = EIRP + G_r[dBi] - L_p - L_{misc}
$$

$$
P_r = 56.5 + 24 - 121.34 - 2
$$

$$
P_r = 80.5 - 123.34 = -42.84\;dBm
$$

### Step 5 — Convert Back to Watts (if required)

$$
P_r[mW] = 10^{(-42.84/10)} = 10^{-4.284} \approx 5.21 \times 10^{-5}\;mW
$$

$$
\boxed{\;P_r \approx -42.84\;dBm \;=\; 5.21 \times 10^{-8}\;W\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always carry **two extra decimal places** in intermediate steps. A rounding error at Step 2 cascades into a wrong final answer. KTU evaluators specifically check the **first correct FSPL value**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Cell Radius from a Given Link Budget

Given a minimum receiver sensitivity $P_{r,min}$, an EIRP at the BS, a CPE gain $G_r$, and a fade margin $M_f$, the maximum allowable path loss is:

$$
L_{max} = EIRP + G_r - P_{r,min} - M_f
$$

Setting $L_{max}$ equal to FSPL:

$$
32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{max}) + 20\log_{10}(f) = L_{max}
$$

Solving for $d_{max}$:

$$
20\log_{10}(d_{max}) = L_{max} - 32.4 - 20\log_{10}(f)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{max}) = \frac{L_{max} - 32.4 - 20\log_{10}(f)}{20}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d_{max}[km] = 10^{\,\left[\dfrac{L_{max} - 32.4 - 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}{20}\right]}\;}
$$

### Quick Application

For $EIRP = 36$ dBm, $G_r = 18$ dBi, $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm, $M_f = 10$ dB, $f = 900$ MHz:

$$
L_{max} = 36 + 18 - (-90) - 10 = 134\;dB
$$

$$
d_{max} = 10^{(134 - 32.4 - 20\log_{10}(900))/20} = 10^{(134 - 32.4 - 59.08)/20}
$$

$$
= 10^{(42.52)/20} = 10^{2.126} \approx 133.7\;km
$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the **theoretical free-space cell radius**. In practice, terrain, foliage, and buildings reduce this by 30–60%.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — MMDS vs. LMDS

This is the most-asked **comparison** in KTU Module 1.

| Parameter | MMDS | LMDS |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency band | **2.1 – 2.7 GHz** | **24 – 40 GHz** |
| Wavelength | ~10 – 15 cm (microwave) | ~0.7 – 1.25 cm (mm-wave) |
| Cell radius | 30 – 50 km | 1 – 5 km |
| Bandwidth | ~200 MHz total | ~1.3 GHz total |
| Rain attenuation | Negligible | Severe (>10 dB/km at 30 GHz in heavy rain) |
| LOS requirement | NLOS possible | Strict LOS mandatory |
| Antenna size | Larger for same gain | Compact, high gain easily achievable |
| Typical deployment | Rural / suburban | Dense urban / enterprise |
| Multipath | Significant | Minimal (LOS-dominant) |
| Use case | Broadcast TV + WLL | Broadband data + WLL |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"**M**MDS = **M**edium-range, **L**MDS = **L**ocalized-range."* The M and L directly refer to their typical cell sizes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End WLL Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete functional flow** of a WLL system, from subscriber premises through the air interface into the PSTN/IP core.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["PSTN AND IP CORE NETWORK"]
        PSTN["PSTN SWITCH"]
        IPBACK["IP BACKBONE ROUTER"]
    end

    subgraph BSC["BASE STATION CONTROLLER BSC"]
        BSCCTRL["TRAFFIC ROUTER AND MODEM BANK"]
        CHANNEL["CHANNEL ALLOCATOR FDMA TDMA CDMA OFDMA"]
    end

    subgraph BASESTN["BASE STATION TOWER SITE"]
        ANTBS["SECTOR ANTENNA GAIN 18 DBI"]
        LNA["LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER LNA"]
        UPXC["UP DOWN CONVERTER"]
        HPA["HIGH POWER AMPLIFIER HPA"]
    end

    subgraph AIRINT["WIRELESS AIR INTERFACE"]
        UPLINK["UPLINK CPE TO BS"]
        DOWNLINK["DOWNLINK BS TO CPE"]
        FADING["RICIAN FADING CHANNEL K 6 TO 20 DB"]
    end

    subgraph CPE["CUSTOMER PREMISES EQUIPMENT CPE"]
        ANTPE["DIRECTIONAL ANTENNA GAIN 20 TO 25 DBI"]
        RCVRF["RF FRONT END AND LNA"]
        MODEM["MODEM OR TERMINAL ADAPTER"]
        PHONE["USER TELEPHONE OR ROUTER"]
    end

    PSTN --> BSCCTRL
    IPBACK --> BSCCTRL
    BSCCTRL --> CHANNEL
    CHANNEL --> UPXC
    UPXC --> HPA
    HPA --> ANTBS
    ANTBS --> DOWNLINK
    DOWNLINK --> FADING
    FADING --> ANTPE
    ANTPE --> RCVRF
    RCVRF --> MODEM
    MODEM --> PHONE

    PHONE -.-> MODEM
    MODEM -.-> ANTPE
    ANTPE -.-> UPLINK
    UPLINK -.-> FADING
    FADING -.-> ANTBS
    ANTBS -.-> LNA
    LNA -.-> BSCCTRL
    BSCCTRL -.-> PSTN
    BSCCTRL -.-> IPBACK

    classDef coreStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef bscStyle fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef bsStyle fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef airStyle fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef cpeStyle fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    class PSTN,IPBACK coreStyle
    class BSCCTRL,CHANNEL bscStyle
    class ANTBS,LNA,UPXC,HPA bsStyle
    class UPLINK,DOWNLINK,FADING airStyle
    class ANTPE,RCVRF,MODEM,PHONE cpeStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (top-down):** Downlink path — from core network to subscriber.
- **Dashed arrows (bottom-up):** Uplink path — from subscriber to core network.
- **Five color-coded layers:** Blue (Core), Green (BSC), Orange (Base Station), Purple (Air Interface), Red (CPE).
- **Key block — FADING:** Marked with K-factor (6–20 dB) emphasizing the **Rician** (not Rayleigh) nature of WLL channels.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Frequency Band Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["START WLL DESIGN"] --> B{"REQUIRED COVERAGE RADIUS"}
    B -- "GREATER THAN 10 KM" --> C["CHOOSE MMDS BAND 2.1 TO 2.7 GHZ"]
    B -- "LESS THAN 5 KM" --> D["CHOOSE LMDS BAND 24 TO 40 GHZ"]
    B -- "MODERN 4G 5G" --> E["CHOOSE LTE FWA NR SUB 6 GHZ"]
    C --> F["EVALUATE NLOS FEASIBILITY"]
    D --> G["VERIFY STRICT LOS"]
    E --> H["USE 3.5 GHZ TDD DEPLOYMENT"]
    F --> I["DESIGN ANTENNA GAIN BUDGET"]
    G --> I
    H --> I

    classDef bandStyle fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef logicStyle fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef finalStyle fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class C,D,E bandStyle
    class B,F,G,H logicStyle
    class A,I finalStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **decision diamond** at the top asks the engineer the fundamental question — cell radius.
- The **three color-coded branches** map to MMDS, LMDS, and modern 4G/5G FWA.
- The **convergence point** at the bottom emphasizes that all paths must ultimately satisfy the antenna gain and link budget equation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Define Wireless Local Loop (WLL). Mention any two advantages of WLL over wired local loop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Wireless Local Loop (WLL) is a system that uses radio communication in the access network to provide telecommunication services (voice and/or data) between a fixed subscriber station and the telephone exchange or core network, thereby replacing the conventional copper-wire subscriber loop (also known as the "last mile").

**Advantages (½ mark each):**
1. **Rapid deployment** — WLL systems can be installed within days, eliminating the need for trenching and civil works associated with copper laying.
2. **Cost-effective in difficult terrain** — Especially in rural, hilly, or remote regions where cable laying is impractical or uneconomical.
3. *(Optional third)* **Easier maintenance** — No physical cable damage, corrosion, or right-of-way issues.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between MMDS and LMDS in terms of frequency band, range, and bandwidth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct row):**

| Parameter | MMDS | LMDS |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | 2.1 – 2.7 GHz (microwave) | 24 – 40 GHz (mm-wave) |
| Cell Range | 30 – 50 km (long range) | 1 – 5 km (short range) |
| Bandwidth | ~200 MHz | ~1.3 GHz |

**Conclusion (extra credit):** MMDS uses lower frequencies which propagate further with less attenuation, while LMDS uses much higher frequencies offering wider bandwidth but limited to short, line-of-sight cells.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU 2024 Regulation — Part B Rule]:** Each question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B** in full.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of a Wireless Local Loop (WLL) system with a neat block diagram. Clearly describe the functions of the Base Station (BS), Base Station Controller (BSC), and Customer Premises Equipment (CPE).

**(b) [7 marks]** A WLL base station transmits at $f = 3.5$ GHz with $P_t = 5$ W through a feeder cable of loss 2 dB. The transmit antenna gain is 16 dBi. The CPE has a receive antenna gain of 22 dBi and is located at a distance of 10 km. Miscellaneous losses are 3 dB. Calculate the received power at the CPE in dBm.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture of WLL [7 marks]

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A WLL system provides telecommunications services to fixed subscribers using a **wireless (radio) link** instead of copper wires for the access network.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[PSTN/IP Core]
      |
      v
[BSC - Base Station Controller]
      |  (Channel allocation,
      |   Modem bank,
      |   Switching)
      v
[Base Station (BS) - Tower]
   - Sector Antenna (gain 16-20 dBi)
   - HPA (High Power Amplifier)
   - LNA (Low Noise Amplifier)
   - Up/Down Converter
      |
      |  <-- Wireless Air Interface -->
      |       (Rician fading channel,
      |        K = 6-20 dB)
      v
[CPE - Customer Premises Equipment]
   - Directional Antenna (gain 20-25 dBi)
   - RF Front End
   - Modem / Terminal Adapter
      |
      v
[Subscriber Phone / Router / PC]
```

**3. Functional Descriptions (4 marks — distribute):**

- **Base Station Controller (BSC):** Acts as the **central coordinator** of the WLL cell. It handles channel allocation (FDMA/TDMA/CDMA/OFDMA), switching of subscriber calls to the PSTN or IP backbone, authentication, and billing. It also manages **handover** between sectors if needed.

- **Base Station (BS):** Mounted on a tower or rooftop, the BS contains the **RF transceiver chain** — high-power amplifier (HPA), low-noise amplifier (LNA), up/down converters, and **sectorized antennas** (typically 60° or 90° sectors). It transmits downlink signals to CPEs and receives uplink signals from them.

- **Customer Premises Equipment (CPE):** Installed at the subscriber's home/office, the CPE consists of a **highly directional antenna** (20–25 dBi), RF front end, and a modem/terminal adapter. It is fixed in position (unlike a mobile handset), enabling high gain and stable Rician channel conditions.

**4. Air Interface (extra 1 mark):**
The wireless channel between BS and CPE is characterized by **Rician fading** (K-factor 6–20 dB), with a **line-of-sight** or near-line-of-sight path dominating. Duplexing is achieved via **TDD or FDD** depending on the standard.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $f = 3.5$ GHz = **3500 MHz**
- $P_t = 5$ W
- $L_{feed} = 2$ dB
- $G_t = 16$ dBi
- $G_r = 22$ dBi
- $d = 10$ km
- $L_{misc} = 3$ dB

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert $P_t$ to dBm.

$$
P_t = 5\;W = 5000\;mW
$$

$$
P_t[dBm] = 10\log_{10}(5000) = 10 \times 3.699 = 36.99\;dBm \approx 37\;dBm
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute EIRP.

$$
EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_{feed} = 37 + 16 - 2 = 51\;dBm
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute FSPL.

$$
L_p = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(10) = 20\;dB
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(3500) = 20 \times 3.5441 = 70.88\;dB
$$

$$
L_p = 32.4 + 20 + 70.88 = 123.28\;dB
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute received power.

$$
P_r = EIRP + G_r - L_p - L_{misc}
$$

$$
P_r = 51 + 22 - 123.28 - 3 = 73 - 126.28 = -53.28\;dBm
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final answer and verification.

$$
\boxed{\;P_r \approx -53.28\;dBm\;}
$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Converting to mW:

$$
P_r[mW] = 10^{-53.28/10} = 10^{-5.328} \approx 4.7 \times 10^{-6}\;mW \approx 4.7\;nW
$$

This is a typical weak signal level for a 10 km WLL link at 3.5 GHz, consistent with practical deployments.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare MMDS and LMDS as WLL technologies. Discuss their respective frequency bands, coverage ranges, bandwidth capabilities, and propagation characteristics.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the Friis free-space transmission equation. Using this equation, determine the maximum cell radius for a WLL system operating at 900 MHz, given that the minimum receiver sensitivity is −95 dBm, the EIRP of the base station is 40 dBm, the CPE antenna gain is 18 dBi, and a fade margin of 12 dB must be maintained.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison of MMDS and LMDS [7 marks]

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Both MMDS and LMDS are **fixed wireless access (WLL)** technologies that deliver voice, data, and video services. They differ fundamentally in their operating frequencies, which leads to different coverage and bandwidth characteristics.

**2. Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | MMDS | LMDS |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Multichannel Multipoint Distribution Service | Local Multipoint Distribution Service |
| Frequency Band | 2.1 – 2.7 GHz | 24 – 40 GHz |
| Wavelength | 11 – 14 cm | 0.75 – 1.25 cm |
| Cell Radius | 30 – 50 km | 1 – 5 km |
| Bandwidth | ~200 MHz total | ~1.3 GHz total |
| LOS Requirement | NLOS possible | Strict LOS mandatory |
| Rain Attenuation | Negligible (< 0.1 dB/km) | Severe (5–15 dB/km at 30 GHz) |
| Multipath Fading | Significant | Minimal (LOS dominant) |
| Typical Use | Rural/suburban, broadcast TV + WLL | Dense urban, broadband data |
| Antenna Size | Larger for same gain | Compact, high gain feasible |

**3. Detailed Discussion (2 marks):**

- **MMDS propagation characteristics:** Because MMDS operates at lower microwave frequencies, signals can diffract around obstacles and penetrate light foliage, allowing **non-line-of-sight (NLOS)** operation. This makes MMDS ideal for **suburban and rural** deployments where mounting a tall LOS antenna is impractical.

- **LMDS propagation characteristics:** At mm-wave frequencies, LMDS suffers from **severe atmospheric and rain attenuation**. Oxygen absorption peaks at ~60 GHz, and rain attenuation can exceed **10 dB/km during heavy downpours**. Hence, LMDS requires **strict line-of-sight** and is typically used for **short-range, high-capacity** links in dense urban environments or for backhaul.

**4. Conclusion (extra):** MMDS trades bandwidth for range, while LMDS trades range for bandwidth. The choice depends on the deployment scenario.

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Cell-Radius Calculation [7 marks]

**Part (b.1) — Derivation of Friis Equation (4 marks):**

Consider an isotropic radiator transmitting power $P_t$. At distance $d$, the power flux density is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

A receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures power:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot A_e
$$

The effective aperture of an antenna of gain $G_r$ is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \,\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

Including transmit antenna gain $G_t$ (which boosts the flux by $G_t$):

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}
$$

In logarithmic form, with path loss $L_p$:

$$
P_r[dBm] = P_t[dBm] + G_t[dBi] + G_r[dBi] - L_p[dB]
$$

where

$$
L_p[dB] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

**Part (b.2) — Maximum Cell Radius (3 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 900$ MHz, $P_{r,min} = -95$ dBm, $EIRP = 40$ dBm, $G_r = 18$ dBi, $M_f = 12$ dB.

**Step 1:** Compute maximum allowable path loss.

$$
L_{max} = EIRP + G_r - P_{r,min} - M_f
$$

$$
L_{max} = 40 + 18 - (-95) - 12 = 141\;dB
$$

**Step 2:** Equate $L_p = L_{max}$ and solve for $d$.

$$
32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(900) = 141
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(900) = 20 \times 2.954 = 59.08\;dB
$$

$$
32.4 + 59.08 + 20\log_{10}(d) = 141
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 141 - 32.4 - 59.08 = 49.52\;dB
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = 2.476
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d_{max} \approx 299.5\;km\;}
$$

**Step 3 (Interpretation):** This is the **free-space theoretical** maximum. In real deployments, terrain, foliage, and building obstructions reduce this by **40 – 60%**, giving a practical cell radius of **120 – 180 km**. Still, this demonstrates why **900 MHz WLL systems** were popular for very wide-area rural coverage.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the feeder/connector loss** in the link budget. Always subtract $L_{feed}$ from $P_t$ *before* adding $G_t$ to get EIRP. KTU evaluators deduct **1 full mark** for this omission.
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch in FSPL.** The formula $32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ *only* works when distance is in **kilometers** and frequency is in **MHz**. Mixing units (e.g., $d$ in meters) silently gives a wrong answer with no error indication — KTU examiners specifically test this trap.
>
> 3. **Confusing PFD (Power Flux Density) with Received Power.** PFD is in W/m²; received power is in W. They are not interchangeable.
>
> 4. **Writing the answer without a block diagram** in Q.3(a)-type questions. A neat, labeled block diagram is worth at least **2 of the 7 marks**. Skipping it is a guaranteed mark loss.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the K-factor in WLL.** Students often write "Rayleigh fading" for WLL channels. This is a **fundamental misconception** that costs marks — WLL has **Rician fading** because of the dominant LOS component.
>
> 6. **Rounding too early.** Round only the *final* answer. Intermediate steps should retain **at least 2 decimal places**.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 WLL**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **WLL / FWA / RITL:** All refer to a wireless "last-mile" replacement for copper subscriber loops.
- **CPE:** Customer Premises Equipment — fixed rooftop antenna + transceiver.
- **BS:** Base Station — tower-mounted sectorized antenna serving the cell.
- **BSC:** Base Station Controller — central coordinator (switching, allocation).
- **PSTN/IP Core:** The backbone network (PSTN for voice, IP for data).

### 🔑 Critical Frequency Bands
- **MMDS:** 2.1 – 2.7 GHz, 30 – 50 km range.
- **LMDS:** 24 – 40 GHz, 1 – 5 km range, mm-wave.
- **Modern FWA:** 3.5 GHz (LTE/NR), sub-6 GHz 5G.

### 🔑 Core Engineering Principles
- WLL channels follow **Rician fading** (K = 6–20 dB), **not Rayleigh**.
- CPE antennas have **high directional gain** (20–25 dBi) — this is the WLL advantage.
- Multipath is **reduced** because the link is fixed and elevated.
- **Duplexing:** TDD for asymmetric data, FDD for symmetric voice.

### 🔑 Must-Know Formulas (Quick Recall)
- **FSPL:** $L_p = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ [dB]
- **Friis:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_p - L_{misc}$ [dBm]
- **EIRP:** $P_t[dBm] + G_t[dBi] - L_{feed}[dB]$
- **Max radius:** $d_{max}[km] = 10^{(L_{max} - 32.4 - 20\log_{10} f_{MHz})/20}$
- **K-factor:** $K[dB] = 10\log_{10}(P_{LOS}/P_{scatter})$

### 🔑 Comparison Mnemonics
- **MMDS = Many Miles Distance Service** (long range).
- **LMDS = Local Multipoint Distribution Service** (short range).
- **WLL is Calm** — fixed channel = stable.
- **Mobile is Chaotic** — moving = Rayleigh.

### 🔑 Five Advantages of WLL over Wired Loop
1. Rapid deployment.
2. Cost-effective in difficult terrain.
3. No trenching / civil works.
4. Easier to upgrade (just change radio).
5. Lower maintenance overhead.

### 🔑 Five Limitations of WLL
1. Limited spectrum (licensed bandwidth is finite).
2. Line-of-sight constraints (especially at mm-wave).
3. Rain / foliage attenuation at high frequencies.
4. Capacity shared among users (TDMA / CDMA / OFDMA).
5. Power supply needed at CPE (since no wired infrastructure).

---

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Wireless Local Loop (WLL)**
> *All formulas, derivations, and diagrams are aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme PCCPT502 syllabus and examination pattern.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WLAN (Wireless Local Area Network):** A flexible data communication system that uses Radio Frequency (RF) or Infrared (IR) technology to transmit and receive data over the air, eliminating the need for physical wired connections, while providing all the functionality of a wired LAN within a limited geographic area (typically a building, campus, or home) — standardized primarily by the **IEEE 802.11 family** of protocols (colloquially known as **Wi-Fi**).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, WLAN falls under the broader umbrella of **Wireless Personal Area Networks (WPAN) / Wireless Local Area Networks (WLAN)**, which are the first two tiers of the wireless networking hierarchy:

| Network Type | Coverage Range | Standard Example |
|---|---|---|
| **WBAN** (Body) | < 1 m | IEEE 802.15.6 |
| **WPAN** (Personal) | 1 – 10 m | Bluetooth, ZigBee (IEEE 802.15) |
| **WLAN** (Local) | 10 – 100 m | **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** |
| **WMAN** (Metro) | < 10 km | WiMAX (IEEE 802.16) |
| **WWAN** (Wide) | Global | 4G/5G (3GPP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wi-Fi vs WLAN:** *Wi-Fi* is a **brand/trademark** owned by the Wi-Fi Alliance based on the IEEE 802.11 standard. *WLAN* is the **technical system** itself. In the KTU syllabus, both terms are used interchangeably, but technically every Wi-Fi network is a WLAN, though not every WLAN is certified Wi-Fi.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Cordless Telephone" Analogy for WLAN:**

Imagine a large office where every employee has a **cordless telephone**, but instead of voice, the cordless handsets exchange **printed letters (data packets)** with a **central PBX telephone exchange (the Access Point)**. The handsets can:

1. **Listen before speaking** (Carrier Sense) — they wait until the "line" is free to avoid two people shouting at once.
2. **Shout "ready to send"** (RTS) and wait for "go ahead" (CTS) — a polite handshake before heavy data exchange.
3. **Roam between rooms** — when an employee walks to a different floor, a closer PBX automatically takes over the call (this is **roaming/handoff**).

That PBX is the **Access Point (AP)**, the handsets are the **STAtions (STAs)**, and the wireless air between them is the **shared medium**. The rules they follow (timing, etiquette, modulation) are collectively called the **IEEE 802.11 standard**.

A second analogy: **WLAN is to LAN what Mobile Phones are to Landline Phones** — same purpose (communication), different medium (radio waves vs copper wires), with added challenges (interference, mobility, security).

## 1.3 Why WLAN Matters — Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Key Learning Outcomes for this Topic:**
> - Understand the **architectural components** of an IEEE 802.11 network.
> - Differentiate between the various **802.11 standards (a/b/g/n/ac/ax)** by frequency, modulation, and data rate.
> - Explain the **CSMA/CA-based MAC layer** mechanism and why collision *detection* (CSMA/CD) is impossible in wireless.
> - Compute basic **throughput, coverage, and capacity** metrics.
> - Describe the **hidden node problem** and its solution via RTS/CTS.

**WLAN operates primarily in two unlicensed Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands:**
- **2.4 GHz** band (2.400 – 2.4835 GHz) — used by 802.11 b/g/n
- **5 GHz** band (5.150 – 5.825 GHz) — used by 802.11 a/n/ac
- **6 GHz** band (5.925 – 7.125 GHz) — used by 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6E) and 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7)

These bands are **license-free**, meaning any individual or organization can transmit within them, which is why Wi-Fi became a global success.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WLAN Architecture (IEEE 802.11 Building Blocks)

The IEEE 802.11 standard defines three fundamental building blocks that every KTU exam question on this topic references:

### 2.1.1 Stations (STAs)
A **Station (STA)** is any device equipped with a wireless network interface card (WNIC). Examples: laptops, smartphones, IoT sensors, printers.
- **Mobile Station** — moves between AP coverage areas.
- **Stationary Station** — does not change its point of attachment.

### 2.1.2 Access Point (AP)
The **Access Point (AP)** acts as a **bridge** between the wireless and wired (typically Ethernet) worlds. It:
- Transmits/receives RF signals to/from stations.
- Performs frame translation (802.11 ↔ 802.3 Ethernet).
- Acts as a **relay** for stations that cannot reach each other directly.
- Typically covers a circular area called a **Basic Service Area (BSA)**, with radius **30 – 100 m** indoors.

### 2.1.3 The Four Service Sets (Critical for KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "4-Box" Service Set Model — Memorize This Diagram:**
> 1. **BSS — Basic Service Set:** One AP + all associated STAs. Identified by a unique **BSSID (usually the AP's MAC address)**.
> 2. **IBSS — Independent Basic Service Set:** An *ad-hoc* network of STAs with **no AP** (e.g., two laptops sharing files via Wi-Fi Direct).
> 3. **ESS — Extended Service Set:** Multiple BSSs interconnected via a **Distribution System (DS)**, allowing **roaming** (handoff) between APs.
> 4. **DS — Distribution System:** The **backbone** (typically Ethernet) that connects multiple APs. Enables mobility across the ESS.

**How the BSS and ESS interact — operating procedure:**
- A wireless station first performs **scanning** (active or passive) to discover APs in its range.
- It then **authenticates** itself (open, shared key, WPA2-PSK, WPA3-SAE).
- It **associates** with the chosen AP, receiving an **Association ID (AID)**.
- Once associated, it can transmit/receive data via the AP (or directly to another STA in some 802.11 standards like 802.11n+ via DLS/TDLS).

## 2.2 IEEE 802.11 Family of Standards — Comparison Table

This is **high-yield for KTU Module 1 questions**. The examiner loves to ask: "Compare 802.11a, b, g, n, ac, ax."

| Standard | Year | Frequency Band | Max Data Rate | Modulation | Channel BW | MIMO |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **802.11 (legacy)** | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | 2 Mbps | DSSS, FHSS | 20 MHz | No |
| **802.11a** | 1999 | 5 GHz | 54 Mbps | OFDM | 20 MHz | No |
| **802.11b** | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | 11 Mbps | DSSS (CCK) | 20 MHz | No |
| **802.11g** | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | 54 Mbps | OFDM | 20 MHz | No |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 600 Mbps | OFDM (64-QAM) | 20 / 40 MHz | 4×4 |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 2014 | 5 GHz | 6.93 Gbps | OFDM (256-QAM) | 20/40/80/160 MHz | 8×8 MU-MIMO |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)** | 2019 | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 9.6 Gbps | OFDMA (1024-QAM) | up to 160 MHz | 8×8 MU-MIMO |
| **802.11be (Wi-Fi 7)** | 2024 | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 46 Gbps | OFDMA (4096-QAM) | 320 MHz | 16×16 MU-MIMO |

> [!NOTE]
> **DSSS** = Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum, **FHSS** = Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum, **OFDM** = Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing, **OFDMA** = Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access, **MIMO** = Multiple-Input Multiple-Output, **MU-MIMO** = Multi-User MIMO.

**Operational rules for the family:**
- 802.11g is **backward compatible** with 802.11b (the g AP can serve both b and g clients, but at the cost of overall throughput).
- 802.11n introduces **MIMO** and **channel bonding** (40 MHz).
- 802.11ac is **5 GHz only** and introduces **MU-MIMO** (downlink).
- 802.11ax introduces **OFDMA** which allows simultaneous multi-user scheduling on sub-carriers — a major efficiency leap for dense deployments.

## 2.3 The WLAN MAC Layer — CSMA/CA (The Heart of 802.11)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CSMA/CD is IMPOSSIBLE in WLAN:**
> - In Ethernet, a station can **detect** a collision by sensing its own transmitted signal on the medium (CSMA/CD = **Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection**).
> - In wireless, the **near-far problem** makes collision detection impractical: a strong signal from a nearby AP masks a weak collision signal from a far station.
> - Also, a wireless station is **half-duplex** — it cannot transmit and receive simultaneously on the same channel.
> - **Solution:** 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA = Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance**, where stations try to **avoid** collisions rather than detect them.

### 2.3.1 DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) — The Mandatory MAC Method

**DCF is the core KTU-Module-1 mechanism.** It defines four timing intervals:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical 802.11n Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Slot Time** | $\sigma$ | 9 µs |
| **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) | SIFS | 10 µs |
| **PIFS** (PCF Inter-Frame Space) | PIFS | 19 µs |
| **DIFS** (DCF Inter-Frame Space) | DIFS | 34 µs |
| **EIFS** (Extended IFS) | EIFS | SIFS + ACK + DIFS ≈ 333 µs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Intervals (in increasing order):** **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**
> This ordering ensures priority: ACK frames wait only SIFS, regular data frames wait DIFS, and "recovering from a problem" frames wait the longest (EIFS).

**The DCF Algorithm — Step by Step:**
1. A station with a frame to send first performs **physical carrier sensing** (checks if the channel is idle).
2. If the channel is **busy**, the station defers and waits until the channel becomes idle.
3. Once idle, the station waits for a **DIFS** period.
4. The station then enters a **random backoff** phase: it picks a random integer $k$ from $[0, CW]$ and counts down $k$ slot times. $CW$ starts at $CW_{\min}$ and doubles (up to $CW_{\max}$) after each failed transmission.
5. The countdown **freezes** if the channel becomes busy, and **resumes** after DIFS when the channel is idle again.
6. When the counter reaches zero, the station **transmits** the frame.
7. The receiver waits **SIFS** (shorter than DIFS, giving it priority) and replies with an **ACK** frame.
8. If the sender does not receive an ACK within the timeout, it assumes a collision occurred and **retransmits** with a doubled contention window.

### 2.3.2 PCF (Point Coordination Function) — The Optional Polling Method

In **PCF**, the AP acts as a **polling master** and grants stations explicit turn-by-turn access, eliminating contention. It is rarely implemented in practice but is **important conceptually** for KTU.

## 2.4 The Hidden Node Problem & RTS/CTS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hidden Node Problem (MUST-KNOW for KTU):**
> Imagine three stations A, B, and C arranged linearly: A --- B --- C. B can hear both A and C, but A and C **cannot hear each other** because they are out of range.
> If A and C both sense the channel as **idle** and transmit to B simultaneously, their frames **collide at B** — yet A and C never knew about each other's transmission. This is the hidden node problem.

**Solution — RTS/CTS Handshake (Request to Send / Clear to Send):**
1. A sends a small **RTS** frame to B.
2. B replies (after SIFS) with a **CTS** frame, broadcast to all neighbors.
3. C hears the CTS and sets its **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)**, deferring all transmissions for the duration specified in the CTS.
4. A transmits data after receiving CTS; B replies with ACK.

**This is why RTS/CTS works:** the CTS is sent at a **lower power / higher robustness** and is heard by *all* nodes in B's range, even hidden ones. Every node that hears either RTS or CTS stays silent.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key 802.11 mathematical relations in one place. Master these for Part A and Part B calculations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Friis Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz, FSPL in dB |
| 2 | **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P_t, P_r$ in W, $\lambda$ in m, $d$ in m, $G_t, G_r$ dimensionless (linear) |
| 3 | **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ in Hz |
| 4 | **Shannon–Hartley Capacity** | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ in bps, $B$ in Hz, SNR dimensionless |
| 5 | **Nyquist Max Data Rate** | $R = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | $M$ = number of signal levels |
| 6 | **802.11n Throughput (theoretical max)** | $R_{max} = N_{ss} \cdot N_{t} \cdot (N_{bits} / (T_{sym}))$ | $N_{ss}$ = spatial streams, $N_{bits}$ = bits per subcarrier, $T_{sym}$ = OFDM symbol time = 4 µs |
| 7 | **DCF Backoff Slot Selection** | $k \in [0, CW]$, where $CW$ doubles per failure | $CW_{\min}=15$, $CW_{\max}=1023$ (802.11n) |
| 8 | **Total Backoff Time** | $T_{backoff} = k \cdot \sigma$ | $\sigma$ = slot time |
| 9 | **DIFS Duration** | $DIFS = SIFS + 2\cdot\sigma$ | Standard 802.11n: $10 + 18 = 28$ µs (some say 34 µs with rounding) |
| 10 | **Minimum Time to Transmit One Frame (DCF)** | $T_{frame} = DIFS + T_{backoff} + T_{data} + SIFS + T_{ACK}$ | Each term in µs |
| 11 | **802.11ac Max Rate (160 MHz, 8×8, MCS9)** | $R = 866.7$ Mbps per stream × 8 | Theoretical aggregate = **6.93 Gbps** |
| 12 | **EIRP** (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) | $EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_{cable}$ (in dB) | Used to compute coverage and link budget |
| 13 | **Path Loss (Log-Distance Model)** | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2 free-space, 3–4 indoor) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Followed:** All absolute value / magnitude notations in the table use `\vert` or `\mid` syntax where applicable to preserve markdown table integrity. No raw `|` symbols inside cells.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of WLAN

**Where WLAN theory meets engineering practice:**
- **Enterprise campus networks:** Modern offices use 802.11ax with **controller-managed APs** for seamless roaming across hundreds of BSSs in one ESS.
- **Industrial IoT:** 802.11ah (Wi-Fi HaLow) operates in the **900 MHz** sub-GHz band for long-range, low-power sensor networks.
- **Public hotspots:** Airports, cafés, and hotels use captive-portal APs that integrate with RADIUS for centralized AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting).
- **Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I):** 802.11p (DSRC) is the WLAN standard for vehicular networks, enabling cars to "talk" to traffic lights and other cars.
- **Mesh networks:** 802.11s extends WLAN into a self-healing multi-hop architecture for community networks and disaster recovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every algebraic step below is written out explicitly. No shortcuts. KTU examiners reward clean, fully-worked solutions.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Transmission Equation (Received Power vs Distance)

The Friis equation is the foundation of **every wireless link budget problem**, including WLAN. We will derive it from first principles.

**Starting Point — Isotropic Radiation:**
An **isotropic radiator** spreads its power uniformly in all directions over a sphere of radius $d$. If the transmit power is $P_t$, the power density at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Explanation:**
This is just power divided by the surface area of the sphere. The "4πd²" is the spreading loss — energy spreads thinner as the wavefront grows.

**Step 2 — Apply the Receive Antenna Aperture:**
A real antenna with effective aperture $A_e$ intercepts this power. The received power is:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot A_e}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Aperture in Terms of Gain:**
The effective aperture of an antenna is related to its gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d^2) \cdot 4\pi} = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

We have included $G_t$ (transmit antenna gain) to generalize: the transmit antenna also concentrates power, equivalent to an isotropic radiator with gain $G_t$.

**Step 5 — Final Friis Equation in Linear Form:**

$$
\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Decibels (the dB form used by KTU):**

$$
P_r \text{ [dBm]} = P_t \text{ [dBm]} + G_t \text {[dBi]} + G_r \text{[dBi]} - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44
$$

Where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **32.44** comes from $20\log_{10}(4\pi / c)$ evaluated in dB/km/MHz units. Verify: $20\log_{10}(4\pi \times 10^3 / (3 \times 10^8 \times 10^6)) = 20\log_{10}(4.19 \times 10^{-11}) \times 10^{6} \to \approx 32.44$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DCF Backoff Time and Throughput

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 802.11n station with $CW_{\min} = 15$ that just experienced a collision. Compute the worst-case and best-case backoff.

**Step 1 — Contention Window (CW) Range:**
After a collision, the new $CW$ is the next power of 2 less than 2·(old CW + 1) − 1, capped at $CW_{\max} = 1023$.

$$
CW_{new} = \min(2 \cdot (CW_{old} + 1) - 1, CW_{\max})
$$

**Step 2 — Backoff Counter Range:**
The station picks:

$$
k \in [0, CW]
$$

**Step 3 — Best-Case Backoff (first attempt, no collision):**

$$
k_{min} = 0, \quad T_{backoff,min} = 0 \cdot \sigma = 0 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-Case Backoff (after many collisions, first attempt with $CW = 1023$):**

$$
k_{max} = 1023, \quad T_{backoff,max} = 1023 \times 9 \text{ µs} = 9207 \text{ µs} = 9.207 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Time to Successfully Transmit One Frame (illustrative):**
Assume a 1500-byte data frame at 65 Mbps, slot = 9 µs, SIFS = 10 µs, DIFS = 34 µs, ACK = 14 bytes at 24 Mbps.

Frame transmission time:

$$
T_{data} = \frac{1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{65 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 184.6 \text{ µs}
$$

ACK transmission time:

$$
T_{ACK} = \frac{14 \times 8}{24 \times 10^6} = 4.67 \text{ µs}
$$

Assuming average backoff $k_{avg} = CW/2 = 7.5$, $T_{backoff,avg} = 67.5$ µs.

Total time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{DIFS} + T_{backoff,avg} + T_{data} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} \\
&= 34 + 67.5 + 184.6 + 10 + 4.67 \\
&= 300.77 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Throughput:**

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{L_{data}}{T_{total}} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{300.77 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 39.9 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

Note: even at a 65 Mbps physical rate, the **effective throughput is ~40 Mbps** due to protocol overhead. This is a typical KTU-style numerical.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 802.11n Max Data Rate (MCS-31, 4×4 MIMO, 40 MHz)

**Step 1 — Subcarriers in a 40 MHz HT channel:**
802.11n OFDM uses 128 subcarriers (108 data + 20 pilots) for a 40 MHz channel. With **Short Guard Interval (SGI)** of 400 ns, the OFDM symbol duration is:

$$
T_{sym} = 4 \text{ µs} - 0.4 \text{ µs} = 3.6 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2 — Bits per OFDM Symbol (per spatial stream):**
At **MCS-31 (64-QAM, rate 5/6)**, each subcarrier carries:

$$
N_{bits} = 108 \times 6 \times \tfrac{5}{6} = 540 \text{ bits/symbol/stream}
$$

**Step 3 — Bits per Second per Stream:**

$$
R_{stream} = \frac{540 \text{ bits}}{3.6 \text{ µs}} = 150 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate Rate with 4 Spatial Streams:**

$$
\boxed{R_{max} = 4 \times 150 = 600 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

This matches the **IEEE 802.11n advertised maximum of 600 Mbps** — a classic KTU "verify" question.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Channel Capacity using Shannon

**Problem:** A 802.11a link uses a 20 MHz channel with measured SNR of 35 dB at the receiver. Find the theoretical maximum data rate.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{35/10} = 10^{3.5} = 3162.3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's Capacity Equation:**

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(3163.3)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
\log_2(3163.3) = \frac{\ln(3163.3)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{8.06}{0.693} = 11.63
$$

**Step 4 — Final Capacity:**

$$
\boxed{C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 11.63 = 232.6 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with 802.11a practical maximum of 54 Mbps:**
We are well below the Shannon limit, so the design is **not bandwidth-limited but technology-limited** (modulation, coding, overhead). This explains why 802.11n and 802.11ac pushed rates higher by using more bandwidth, more antennas, and denser modulation.

## 3.5 Numerical Practice Set (Worked Solutions for KTU Practice)

### Problem A — Coverage Calculation
A Wi-Fi AP transmits at 20 dBm on 2.4 GHz with antenna gain 2 dBi. A laptop 30 m away has receiver sensitivity −65 dBm and antenna gain 0 dBi. Is communication possible? (Ignore cable losses.)

**Solution:**

$$
P_r = 20 + 2 + 0 - 20\log_{10}(0.03) - 20\log_{10}(2400) - 32.44
$$

Computing each term:

$$
20\log_{10}(0.03) = -30.46 \text{ dB}, \quad 20\log_{10}(2400) = 67.6 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
P_r = 20 + 2 - (-30.46) - 67.6 - 32.44 = 20 + 2 + 30.46 - 67.6 - 32.44 = -47.58 \text{ dBm}
$$

Since $-47.58 \text{ dBm} > -65 \text{ dBm}$ (sensitivity), **communication is possible** with a margin of $17.4$ dB.

### Problem B — Maximum Range
Using the same setup, find the maximum range for the received power to equal sensitivity.

Set $P_r = -65$ dBm and solve for $d$:

$$
-65 = 20 + 2 - 20\log_{10}(d) - 67.6 - 32.44
$$

$$
-65 = -77.04 - 20\log_{10}(d)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = -77.04 + 65 = -12.04
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = -0.602, \quad d = 10^{-0.602} \approx 0.25 \text{ km} = 250 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real indoor range is **much less** (30–50 m) due to walls, furniture, multipath, and the path-loss exponent $n$ being 3–4 instead of 2 (free-space).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WLAN Architecture Block Diagram (BSS, ESS, DS)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ESS1["Extended Service Set ESS 1"]
        subgraph BSS1["BSS 1 (Basic Service Set)"]
            AP1["Access Point AP1"]
            STA1["Laptop STA1"]
            STA2["Smartphone STA2"]
            STA3["Tablet STA3"]
        end
        subgraph BSS2["BSS 2 (Basic Service Set)"]
            AP2["Access Point AP2"]
            STA4["Phone STA4"]
            STA5["Printer STA5"]
        end
        DS["Distribution System Ethernet Backbone"]
        AP1 --- DS
        AP2 --- DS
    end
    RADIUS["RADIUS Authentication Server"]
    INTERNET["Internet Gateway"]
    DS --- RADIUS
    DS --- INTERNET
    STA1 -. Wireless 2.4/5 GHz .-> AP1
    STA2 -. Wireless .-> AP1
    STA3 -. Wireless .-> AP1
    STA4 -. Wireless .-> AP2
    STA5 -. Wireless .-> AP2
```

## 4.2 CSMA/CA DCF Timing Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Station has frame to send]) --> SENSECHK{Channel idle for DIFS period}
    SENSECHK -- No busy --> WAIT[Wait for channel to become idle]
    WAIT --> SENSECHK
    SENSECHK -- Yes idle --> BACKOFF[Pick random backoff k in 0 to CW]
    BACKOFF --> COUNTDOWN{Slot time tick}
    COUNTDOWN -- Channel busy freeze --> FREEZE[Pause counter set NAV]
    FREEZE --> WAITFOR[Wait for channel idle DIFS again]
    WAITFOR --> COUNTDOWN
    COUNTDOWN -- Counter zero --> TRANSMIT[Transmit DATA frame]
    TRANSMIT --> ACKWAIT{ACK received after SIFS timeout}
    ACKWAIT -- Yes ACK --> SUCCESS([Transmission successful])
    ACKWAIT -- No ACK timeout --> COLLISION[Assume collision double CW]
    COLLISION --> BACKOFF
```

## 4.3 Hidden Node Problem and RTS/CTS Solution

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA_A as Station A
    participant STA_B as Access Point B
    participant STA_C as Station C
    Note over STA_A,STA_C: A and C cannot hear each other hidden
    STA_A->>STA_B: RTS Request to Send
    STA_B-->>STA_A: CTS Clear to Send broadcast
    STA_B-->>STA_C: CTS also heard by C
    Note over STA_C: C sets NAV and stays silent
    STA_A->>STA_B: DATA frame
    STA_B-->>STA_A: ACK after SIFS
    Note over STA_C: C resumes after NAV expires
```

## 4.4 IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph UPPER["Upper Layers"]
        IP["IP Network Layer"]
        TCP["TCP/UDP Transport"]
    end
    subgraph MAC["802.11 MAC Layer"]
        DCF["DCF CSMA/CA Contention"]
        PCF["PCF Polling Optional"]
        FRAME["Frame Construction ACK Fragmentation"]
    end
    subgraph PHY["802.11 PHY Layer"]
        DSSS["DSSS b"]
        OFDM["OFDM a/g/n/ac/ax"]
        MIMO["MIMO Spatial Streams"]
    end
    subgraph RF["RF Front End"]
        ANT["2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz Antenna"]
    end
    IP --> DCF
    TCP --> DCF
    DCF --> DSSS
    DCF --> OFDM
    PCF --> OFDM
    DSSS --> MIMO
    OFDM --> MIMO
    MIMO --> ANT
    FRAME --> DCF
```

## 4.5 802.11 MAC Frame Format (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FRAME["802.11 MAC Frame"]
        FC["Frame Control 2 bytes"]
        DUR["Duration ID 2 bytes"]
        ADDR1["Address 1 6 bytes"]
        ADDR2["Address 2 6 bytes"]
        ADDR3["Address 3 6 bytes"]
        SEQ["Sequence Control 2 bytes"]
        ADDR4["Address 4 6 bytes optional"]
        BODY["Frame Body 0 to 2312 bytes"]
        FCS["FCS CRC 4 bytes"]
    end
    FC --- DUR --- ADDR1 --- ADDR2 --- ADDR3 --- SEQ --- ADDR4 --- BODY --- FCS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**F**amous **D**octors **A**re **A**mazing, **A**lso **S**ee **A**mazing **B**ig **F**rames" — **F**rame Control, **D**uration, **A**ddr1, **A**ddr2, **A**ddr3, **S**equence, **A**ddr4, **B**ody, **F**CS.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between infrastructure mode and ad-hoc mode in WLAN. Give an example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Infrastructure Mode | Ad-Hoc Mode (IBSS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Point** | Uses an AP as central coordinator | No AP; STAs talk peer-to-peer |
| **Topology** | Star (Hub-and-Spoke) | Mesh / Peer-to-peer |
| **Example** | Home Wi-Fi router with laptops | Two laptops sharing files via Wi-Fi Direct |
| **Coverage** | Extended via multiple APs (ESS) | Limited to ~10 m direct range |
| **Roaming** | Supported across APs | Not supported (single BSS) |
| **Scalability** | High (100s of clients per AP) | Low (typically < 10 STAs) |

**(3 marks awarded: 1 mark for the concept + 1 for example + 1 for the table differentiation.)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why does IEEE 802.11 use CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD? Explain in 3 points.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Collision Detection is impossible** — A wireless station is half-duplex, so it cannot transmit and sense the channel simultaneously. It cannot "hear" its own transmission and detect a clash.
2. **Near-Far Problem** — A strong signal from a nearby transmitter would mask a weak collision signal, making CD unreliable.
3. **Hidden Node Problem** — Even if CD were possible, hidden nodes (out-of-range stations) cause collisions at the receiver that the sender never senses. Hence **Collision Avoidance** is the right design philosophy. **(3 marks: 1 per point.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries one Part B question of 14 marks with internal choice. Below, Question A and Question B are independent options the examiner may offer. Solve both for full KTU readiness.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a WLAN based on IEEE 802.11. Differentiate between BSS, IBSS, and ESS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A WLAN architecture consists of **Stations (STAs)**, **Access Points (APs)**, and an optional **Distribution System (DS)**. The 802.11 standard defines four logical service sets:

1. **BSS (Basic Service Set):** A single AP with all associated STAs forms a BSS. The BSS is identified by a unique 48-bit BSSID (typically the AP's MAC address). It covers a BSA (Basic Service Area) of roughly 30–100 m indoors.
2. **IBSS (Independent Basic Service Set):** An ad-hoc, AP-less network. STAs communicate directly with each other. Used for small, temporary peer-to-peer networks (e.g., Wi-Fi Direct file transfer).
3. **ESS (Extended Service Set):** Multiple BSSs interconnected via a **Distribution System (DS)** (usually Ethernet). The ESSID (or SSID) is the network name (e.g., "KTU_WiFi"). Allows roaming between APs.
4. **DS (Distribution System):** The wired backbone that ties APs together so frames can be forwarded between BSSs.

(For the diagram, refer to **Section 4.1** above — **3 marks for the diagram**, **4 marks for the description and definitions.**)

| BSS | IBSS | ESS |
|---|---|---|
| Has an AP | No AP | Multiple APs + DS |
| Star topology | Peer-to-peer | Star-of-stars |
| Identified by BSSID | Identified by SSID | Identified by ESSID |
| Supports infrastructure services | Limited services | Full enterprise mobility |

**(7 marks = 3 for diagram + 4 for text.)**

---

**Q: (b)** Explain the CSMA/CA mechanism used in 802.11 with the help of the DCF timing diagram. Define SIFS, PIFS, DIFS, and EIFS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance)** is the 802.11 MAC's fundamental channel access method. It works in two phases: **carrier sensing** and **backoff**.

**Step 1 — Carrier Sense:** A station with data to send first checks the channel. If the channel is busy, it defers. If idle for a **DIFS** period, it proceeds.

**Step 2 — Backoff:** The station picks a random integer $k$ uniformly from $[0, CW]$ and counts down $k$ slot times. $CW$ (Contention Window) starts at $CW_{\min} = 15$ and doubles up to $CW_{\max} = 1023$ after each failed attempt (binary exponential backoff).

**Step 3 — Transmit:** When the counter reaches zero, the station transmits the DATA frame.

**Step 4 — ACK:** The receiver waits **SIFS** (shorter than DIFS, giving it priority) and replies with an ACK. If the sender doesn't receive an ACK before timeout, it assumes a collision and retransmits with a doubled $CW$.

**Timing Interval Definitions:**

| Interval | Symbol | Definition | 802.11n Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIFS** | Short Inter-Frame Space | Used for high-priority frames: ACK, CTS, fragment burst | 10 µs |
| **PIFS** | PCF Inter-Frame Space | Used by AP during contention-free polling | 19 µs |
| **DIFS** | DCF Inter-Frame Space | Used by regular data frames | 34 µs |
| **EIFS** | Extended IFS | Used when a frame is received in error (longest) | ~333 µs |

**Order:** **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**

(For the timing diagram, refer to **Section 4.2** above — **3 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for the CSMA/CA steps, 2 marks for interval definitions.**)

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Hidden Node and Exposed Node problems in wireless networks. How does the RTS/CTS mechanism solve the hidden node problem? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hidden Node Problem:**
- Setup: Three stations A, B, C arranged linearly. A and C are in B's range but **not in each other's range**.
- If A and C both sense the channel idle and transmit to B simultaneously, the frames **collide at B**.
- Neither A nor C "heard" the other's transmission, so CSMA alone fails.

**2. Exposed Node Problem:**
- Setup: Four stations A, B, C, D arranged linearly: A - B - C - D.
- B wants to send to A; C wants to send to D. They are within range of each other (B hears C).
- B senses the channel busy (because C is transmitting to D) and unnecessarily defers — even though its transmission to A would not collide with C's transmission to D.
- This is a case of **overly conservative** channel access, reducing throughput.

**3. RTS/CTS Solution to Hidden Node:**

| Step | Action | Why it works |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | A sends **RTS** to B | Announces intention; small frame, low overhead |
| 2 | B replies (after SIFS) with **CTS** broadcast | Heard by *all* in B's range, including C |
| 3 | C hears CTS and sets **NAV** | C now knows the channel will be busy for the data duration |
| 4 | A transmits data | No collision because C is silent |
| 5 | B replies with **ACK** | Confirms success |

**Why CTS is the key:** The CTS is sent at the **lowest mandatory rate** (e.g., 1 Mbps in 802.11b) and is broadcast, so even the "hidden" C reliably hears it. The NAV inside the CTS frame tells C exactly how long to stay silent.

**(7 marks = 2 for Hidden, 2 for Exposed, 3 for RTS/CTS solution.)**

---

**Q: (b)** Compare and contrast the IEEE 802.11a, 802.11b, 802.11g, and 802.11n standards in terms of frequency band, modulation technique, maximum data rate, and channel bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | 802.11a | 802.11b | 802.11g | 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1999 | 1999 | 2003 | 2009 |
| **Frequency Band** | 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz (dual-band) |
| **Modulation** | OFDM (BPSK–64-QAM) | DSSS / CCK | OFDM (BPSK–64-QAM) | OFDM (BPSK–64-QAM) + MIMO |
| **Max Data Rate** | 54 Mbps | 11 Mbps | 54 Mbps | 600 Mbps (4×4 MIMO, 40 MHz) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 20 MHz | 20 MHz | 20 MHz | 20 / 40 MHz (channel bonding) |
| **MIMO Support** | No | No | No | Yes (up to 4×4) |
| **Backward Compat.** | None with b | None with a | Yes with b | Yes with a/b/g |
| **Range (indoor)** | ~35 m | ~38 m | ~38 m | ~70 m (MIMO gain) |
| **Non-overlapping Channels** | 23 (in 5 GHz) | 3 (in 2.4 GHz) | 3 (in 2.4 GHz) | 2.4 GHz: 3; 5 GHz: many |

**Key Insights to Mention in Exam:**
- 802.11b's DSSS/CCK is more robust at long range but slower.
- 802.11g brought OFDM to 2.4 GHz with backward compatibility.
- 802.11n's leap came from **MIMO + channel bonding + shorter guard interval (400 ns)**.
- 5 GHz has more bandwidth and less interference but is more attenuated by walls.
- 2.4 GHz is crowded (microwaves, Bluetooth, ZigBee).

**(7 marks = 5 for the table + 2 for the key insights.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Wi-Fi with WLAN:** Wi-Fi is the certification, WLAN is the system. Don't interchange.
> 2. **Mixing up the inter-frame spaces:** Students often write DIFS < SIFS. **Memorize: SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS.**
> 3. **Forgetting the hidden node solution lies in CTS, not RTS:** The RTS is from sender to AP; the CTS is broadcast and is what hidden nodes actually "hear."
> 4. **Conflating 802.11ac with Wi-Fi 6:** 802.11ac = Wi-Fi 5 (5 GHz only). 802.11ax = Wi-Fi 6 (multi-band with OFDMA).
> 5. **Using incorrect units in Friis:** $d$ must be in km, $f$ in MHz to use the constant 32.44. Otherwise you get a wrong answer.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in Part B (a):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards **3 marks** for the diagram in architecture questions. Always draw it.
