> 7. **Not showing intermediate steps in numericals:** Step-marks (partial credit) are given for showing substitution, not just the final answer.
> 8. **Ignoring SIFS in throughput calculation:** Forgetting the SIFS+ACK overhead makes the throughput appear 30–40% higher than reality.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This for Exam Day! 🔥**

**Architecture & Components:**
- ☐ WLAN = IEEE 802.11 family (Wi-Fi is the brand).
- ☐ Three core elements: **STA, AP, DS**.
- ☐ Four service sets: **BSS, IBSS, ESS, DS**.
- ☐ BSSID = AP's MAC; SSID = network name; ESSID = ESS name.
- ☐ AP bridges wireless 802.11 ↔ wired 802.3 Ethernet.

**Standards & Frequencies:**
- ☐ **2.4 GHz** → b/g/n/ax (crowded, 3 non-overlapping channels).
- ☐ **5 GHz** → a/n/ac/ax (more bandwidth, shorter range).
- ☐ **6 GHz** → ax (Wi-Fi 6E), be (Wi-Fi 7).
- ☐ **802.11n** = 600 Mbps max (4×4 MIMO, 40 MHz, 64-QAM).
- ☐ **802.11ac** = 6.93 Gbps max (8×8 MU-MIMO, 160 MHz, 256-QAM).
- ☐ **802.11ax** = 9.6 Gbps (OFDMA, 1024-QAM).

**MAC Layer (MOST IMPORTANT for KTU):**
- ☐ CSMA/CA (not CD) because of half-duplex, near-far, and hidden node.
- ☐ DCF: **sense → DIFS → random backoff → transmit → SIFS → ACK**.
- ☐ PCF: AP polls stations (optional, rarely used).
- ☐ Backoff: $k \in [0, CW]$; $CW$ doubles after each collision (BEB).
- ☐ IFS order: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**.
- ☐ Slot time σ = 9 µs (802.11n); SIFS = 10 µs; DIFS = 34 µs.

**Hidden Node & RTS/CTS:**
- ☐ Hidden node = out-of-range but both reaching the same AP.
- ☐ Exposed node = unnecessarily defers, wastes bandwidth.
- ☐ **CTS is the hero** of hidden node solution (broadcast at lowest rate).
- ☐ NAV = virtual carrier sense, set by RTS/CTS duration field.

**Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $FSPL = 20\log(d) + 20\log(f) + 32.44$ ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz).
- ☐ $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$.
- ☐ $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ (Shannon).
- ☐ $\lambda = c / f$.
- ☐ 802.11n max = 4 streams × 150 Mbps/stream = 600 Mbps.
- ☐ Throughput: $S = L_{data} / (DIFS + T_{backoff} + T_{data} + SIFS + T_{ACK})$.

**Other Must-Knows:**
- ☐ DSSS, FHSS, OFDM, OFDMA — spread spectrum / multicarrier techniques.
- ☐ MIMO vs MU-MIMO: single user vs multi-user spatial streams.
- ☐ ISM bands: unlicensed 2.4 / 5 GHz.
- ☐ Wi-Fi Alliance: industry body that certifies "Wi-Fi" compliance.
- ☐ Security evolution: WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3.
- ☐ SSID broadcast can be disabled for security; 802.11i = security amendment.
- ☐ Roaming: STA moves between APs within an ESS; re-association required.
- ☐ Power save modes: active, PS (legacy), APSD (scheduled), TIM/DTIM.
- ☐ Beacon frames: AP broadcasts every 100 ms (typical) advertising its presence.
- ☐ Probe Request/Response: active scanning by the client.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any KTU 2024 Scheme WLAN question, **always start with a diagram** (architecture, frame, or timing). Mark-wise, this is the cheapest 3 marks you can earn. Follow with definitions, then a comparison table, then numerical work with **every** substitution step shown. This is the "**3-3-3-2-3**" formula: 3 marks diagram + 3 marks concept + 3 marks comparison + 2 marks formulas + 3 marks numerical → 14 marks. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth and Personal Area Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth and Personal Area Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Personal Area Network (PAN)?

A **Personal Area Network (PAN)** is a short-range, localized wireless network that enables communication among personal devices (such as smartphones, laptops, tablets, headphones, smartwatches, and IoT sensors) within a range of typically **a few meters to about 10 meters** around an individual person. PANs are primarily designed for device-to-device communication, eliminating the need for physical cables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A Personal Area Network (PAN) is a computer network used for communication among computerized devices, including telephones and personal digital assistants, in close proximity to a single person. PANs can be wired (USB, FireWire) or wireless (Bluetooth, ZigBee, Infrared, NFC). The reach of a PAN typically extends up to **10 meters** and is owned, controlled, and managed by a single person or organization.

### Types of PAN Technologies

| Technology | Standard | Frequency | Range | Data Rate |
|------------|----------|-----------|-------|-----------|
| **Bluetooth** | IEEE 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | ~10 m | Up to 3 Mbps (Classic) |
| **ZigBee** | IEEE 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | ~10–100 m | 250 kbps |
| **Infrared (IrDA)** | IrDA | Optical (IR) | ~1 m | Up to 16 Mbps |
| **NFC** | ISO/IEC 14443 | 13.56 MHz | < 0.1 m | Up to 424 kbps |
| **Wi-Fi Direct** | IEEE 802.11 | 2.4/5 GHz | ~200 m | Up to 250 Mbps |

## 1.2 What is Bluetooth?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluetooth** is a wireless technology standard used for exchanging data between fixed and mobile devices over short distances using **short-wavelength UHF radio waves** in the **ISM band from 2.400 to 2.485 GHz**. It was conceived by **Ericsson** in **1994** and is managed by the **Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG)**. The name "Bluetooth" derives from the 10th-century Danish King **Harald Bluetooth**, who united Danish tribes — symbolizing the unification of communication protocols and device industries.

### Key Bluetooth Specifications (Recap Block)
- **Operating Frequency:** 2.4 GHz ISM Band (Industrial, Scientific, Medical)
- **Frequency Bandwidth:** 2.400 GHz – 2.485 GHz (Total **83.5 MHz**)
- **Number of RF Channels:** **79 channels** (in classic Bluetooth)
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **1 MHz** per channel
- **Hopping Rate:** **1600 hops/second**
- **Modulation:** Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK), π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK
- **Range:** ~10 m (Class 2 typical), up to 100 m (Class 1)
- **IEEE Standard:** **IEEE 802.15.1**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "A Walkie-Talkie Coffee Party"**
> Imagine a small room (about 10 meters wide) where a group of friends is having a coffee chat. They all use tiny walkie-talkies that constantly switch channels 1600 times every second. The "host" of the chat (the **Master**) decides who can talk and when. The "guests" (the **Slaves**) only respond when called upon. If two such coffee groups want to coordinate, a friend from one group can join the other — this larger combined setup is like a **Scatternet**. The constantly changing channels are like changing seats every millisecond to avoid someone overhearing or jamming the conversation — this is called **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)**.

This is essentially how Bluetooth works! It allows low-power, secure, short-range communication among personal devices without wires.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bluetooth Network Topology — Piconet and Scatternet

### 2.1.1 Piconet
A **Piconet** is the fundamental building block of a Bluetooth network. It consists of:
- **One Master device** (e.g., your smartphone)
- **Up to 7 active Slave devices** (e.g., wireless earphones, smartwatch, speakers)
- Many more slaves can be in a "parked" or inactive state (up to 255 total)

**Why a Piconet?**
- The master controls all timing and channel access using its clock and device address.
- Slaves synchronize to the master's clock and frequency hopping sequence.
- Communication is always between master and slave — slaves cannot directly communicate with each other.

### 2.1.2 Scatternet
A **Scatternet** is a collection of two or more interconnected Piconets. A device in one Piconet can be a member of another Piconet, acting as a **bridge** between them.

**Important Rules:**
- A device can be a **master in one Piconet** and a **slave in another Piconet**.
- A device cannot be a master in two Piconets simultaneously.
- The total slaves in a scatternet exceed 7, but each Piconet still has only 7 active slaves.

## 2.2 Bluetooth Protocol Stack

The Bluetooth protocol stack is structured into **three main groups**:

### A. Transport Protocols (Lower Layers)
1. **Radio (RF) Layer** – Handles transmission/reception of radio signals at 2.4 GHz.
2. **Baseband Layer** – Manages physical channels, frequency hopping, and link control.
3. **Link Manager Protocol (LMP)** – Sets up and manages links, authentication.
4. **Host Controller Interface (HCI)** – Interface between hardware and software.

### B. Middleware Protocols
5. **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)** – Multiplexing, segmentation, QoS.
6. **Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)** – Allows devices to discover available services.

### C. Application Protocols
7. **RFCOMM** – Serial port emulation (cable replacement).
8. **OBEX** – Object exchange (file transfer).
9. **TCS** – Telephony control.

## 2.3 Bluetooth Frame Format (Baseband)

A Bluetooth baseband packet has the following structure:

| Field | Access Code (72 bits) | Header (54 bits) | Payload (0–2745 bits) |
|-------|----------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Purpose | Synchronization & identification | Control information | User data |

- **Access Code (72 bits):** Used for synchronization and piconet identification.
- **Header (54 bits):** Contains 1/3 FEC (Forward Error Correction), so effectively 18 bits of info.
- **Payload (0–2745 bits):** Contains the actual data, voice, or both.

## 2.4 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)

Bluetooth uses **FHSS** to avoid interference. The total ISM band is divided into **79 channels** of 1 MHz each.

- A pseudo-random hopping sequence is generated by the master.
- Hopping rate = **1600 hops/second** → dwell time = **625 µs/channel**.
- In one slot of 625 µs, a packet is sent on one frequency.
- In the next slot, the frequency changes according to the hopping pattern.

## 2.5 Types of Bluetooth Links

### 2.5.1 Synchronous Connection-Oriented (SCO) Link
- Used for **voice** (e.g., Bluetooth headset).
- Symmetric, point-to-point.
- Reserved slots at regular intervals (every 2nd, 4th, or 6th slot).
- No retransmission, no error correction (uses FEC).
- Maximum of **3 SCO links** per piconet.

### 2.5.2 Asynchronous Connection-Less (ACL) Link
- Used for **data** (e.g., file transfer).
- Can be symmetric or asymmetric.
- Uses all remaining slots not used by SCO.
- Supports retransmission and error correction.
- Only **1 ACL link** between master and each slave.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|------|-------|
| Total Bandwidth | $B = 83.5$ | MHz | From 2.400 to 2.485 GHz |
| Number of Channels | $N_c = 79$ | channels | Classic Bluetooth |
| Channel Bandwidth | $\Delta f = 1$ | MHz | Per channel |
| Hopping Rate | $R_h = 1600$ | hops/s | Per second |
| Dwell Time | $T_d = \frac{1}{R_h} = 625$ | $\mu s$ | Time per channel |
| Time Slot Duration | $T_s = 625$ | $\mu s$ | Same as dwell time |
| Max Active Slaves | 7 | devices | Per piconet |
| Max Parked Slaves | 255 | devices | Per piconet |
| Bit Period (1 Mbps) | $T_b = 1$ | $\mu s$ | Basic Rate |
| SCO Slots | Every 2nd, 4th, or 6th slot | — | Voice |
| Range (Class 1) | ~100 | m | Max power |
| Range (Class 2) | ~10 | m | Typical |
| Range (Class 3) | ~1 | m | Low power |
| Modulation (BR) | GFSK | — | Basic Rate |
| Modulation (EDR) | $\pi/4$-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | — | Enhanced Data Rate |
| Symbol Rate (BR) | 1 | Msps | 1 M symbol/s |
| Symbol Rate (EDR) | 1 | Msps | Same but multi-bit |
| Bit Rate (BR) | 1 | Mbps | GFSK |
| Bit Rate (EDR 2) | 2 | Mbps | $\pi/4$-DQPSK |
| Bit Rate (EDR 3) | 3 | Mbps | 8-DPSK |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Bluetooth is the backbone of **Wireless Personal Area Networks (WPANs)** found in every modern smartphone, wireless audio devices, smart home IoT (Bluetooth Mesh), medical devices (pulse oximeters, smart watches), automotive infotainment, and industrial sensor networks. Understanding its protocol stack is critical for embedded systems engineers, IoT developers, and wireless network designers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Dwell Time and Slot Duration

The hopping rate and dwell time are inversely related. Let us derive this rigorously.

**Step 1: Define the relationship between hopping rate and dwell time.**

The total time spent on a particular frequency channel before hopping to the next channel is called the **dwell time** $T_d$. If the device hops $R_h$ times per second, then by definition:

$$
T_d \cdot R_h = 1
$$

**Step 2: Explanation of the relation.**

Each second of operation is divided into $R_h$ equal intervals. Since the device changes frequency once per interval, the duration of each interval is $1/R_h$. The factor 1 represents one full second of operation.

**Step 3: Substitute the Bluetooth standard hopping rate.**

The Bluetooth specification mandates a hopping rate of $R_h = 1600$ hops per second. Substituting:

$$
T_d = \frac{1}{R_h} = \frac{1}{1600} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4: Convert from seconds to microseconds.**

To convert seconds to microseconds, multiply by $10^6$:

$$
T_d = \frac{1}{1600} \times 10^6 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 5: Simplify the fraction.**

$$
\frac{10^6}{1600} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1600} = 625
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_d = 625 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

Each Bluetooth channel is used for a **dwell time of 625 microseconds** before the device hops to the next frequency. This is identical to the time slot duration used for packet transmission in the baseband layer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Number of Channels

**Step 1: Identify the available frequency spectrum.**

The ISM band allocated to Bluetooth spans from 2.400 GHz to 2.485 GHz.

**Step 2: Compute the total bandwidth.**

$$
B = 2.485 - 2.400 = 0.085 \text{ GHz} = 83.5 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the channel spacing rule.**

The Bluetooth specification uses a channel spacing of $\Delta f = 1$ MHz.

**Step 4: Calculate the number of channels.**

$$
N_c = \frac{B}{\Delta f} = \frac{83.5 \text{ MHz}}{1 \text{ MHz}} = 83.5
$$

**Step 5: Take the integer number of channels.**

Since channels must be whole numbers, the IEEE 802.15.1 standard defines:

$$
N_c = 79
$$

**Step 6: Justification.**

A guard band of 1 MHz is reserved at the lower end and 2 MHz is reserved at the upper end to avoid interference with adjacent systems, giving:

$$
N_c = 79 \text{ channels}
$$

The carrier frequency of channel $k$ is given by:

$$
f_k = 2402 + k \; \text{(in MHz)}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 78
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Frame Transmission Timing

**Step 1: Define the basic time unit.**

A Bluetooth packet occupies a single time slot of $T_s = 625 \; \mu s$.

**Step 2: Multi-slot packets.**

Some packets use 3 or 5 slots. A 3-slot packet occupies:

$$
T_{3-slot} = 3 \times T_s = 3 \times 625 \; \mu s = 1875 \; \mu s
$$

A 5-slot packet occupies:

$$
T_{5-slot} = 5 \times T_s = 5 \times 625 \; \mu s = 3125 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Frequency hopping for multi-slot packets.**

A multi-slot packet stays on a single frequency for the entire duration. The next packet (after the 3 or 5 slots) hops to a new frequency. This means **one hop per packet**, not one hop per slot.

**Step 4: Throughput implication.**

For a 1-slot DH1 packet at 1 Mbps, the payload is 27 bytes = 216 bits, so:

$$
\text{Effective rate} = \frac{216 \text{ bits}}{625 \; \mu s} = 345.6 \text{ kbps}
$$

For a 3-slot DH3 packet with 183-byte payload = 1464 bits:

$$
\text{Effective rate} = \frac{1464 \text{ bits}}{1875 \; \mu s} = 780.8 \text{ kbps}
$$

For a 5-slot DH5 packet with 339-byte payload = 2712 bits:

$$
\text{Effective rate} = \frac{2712 \text{ bits}}{3125 \; \mu s} = 867.84 \text{ kbps}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Bluetooth Versions Comparison

| Version | Year | Max Data Rate | Modulation | Notes |
|---------|------|---------------|------------|-------|
| 1.1 | 2002 | 1 Mbps | GFSK | First widely adopted |
| 1.2 | 2003 | 1 Mbps | GFSK | AFH (Adaptive FH) added |
| 2.0 + EDR | 2004 | 3 Mbps | GFSK, $\pi/4$-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | EDR (Enhanced Data Rate) |
| 2.1 + EDR | 2007 | 3 Mbps | Same | SSP (Secure Simple Pairing) |
| 3.0 + HS | 2009 | 24 Mbps | Same + 802.11 | High Speed via Wi-Fi |
| 4.0 (BLE) | 2010 | 1 Mbps (LE) | GFSK | Low Energy introduced |
| 4.2 | 2014 | 1 Mbps (LE) | GFSK | IoT, IPv6/6LoWPAN |
| 5.0 | 2016 | 2 Mbps (LE) | GFSK | 4x range, 8x broadcast |
| 5.2 | 2020 | 2 Mbps (LE) | GFSK | LE Audio, LC3 codec |
| 5.3 | 2021 | 2 Mbps (LE) | GFSK | Energy efficiency |
| 5.4 | 2023 | 2 Mbps (LE) | GFSK | Periodic adv, PAwR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bluetooth Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP1["APPLICATION PROFILES"]:::app
    APP2["A2DP, HFP, HID, SPP, OBEX"]:::app
    APP3["RFCOMM"]:::mid
    APP4["SDP Service Discovery"]:::mid
    APP5["L2CAP Multiplexing"]:::mid
    APP6["HCI Host Controller Interface"]:::trans
    APP7["LMP Link Manager Protocol"]:::trans
    APP8["BASEBAND Layer"]:::trans
    APP9["RADIO RF 2.4 GHz"]:::trans

    APP1 --> APP2
    APP2 --> APP3
    APP3 --> APP4
    APP4 --> APP5
    APP5 --> APP6
    APP6 --> APP7
    APP7 --> APP8
    APP8 --> APP9

    classDef app fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top Layer (Yellow):** Application profiles — define end-user functions like audio streaming (A2DP), hands-free calling (HFP), keyboard input (HID), serial port emulation (SPP).
- **Middle Layer (Blue):** Middleware — RFCOMM emulates RS-232 serial ports; SDP discovers services; L2CAP handles segmentation and multiplexing.
- **Bottom Layer (Green):** Transport — HCI bridges software and hardware; LMP manages link setup; Baseband handles channel access; Radio transmits over 2.4 GHz.

---

## 4.2 Piconet and Scatternet Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PICONET1["PICONET 1"]
        M1["MASTER 1 Phone"]:::master
        S1["SLAVE 1 Earbuds"]:::slave
        S2["SLAVE 2 Smartwatch"]:::slave
        S3["SLAVE 3 Speaker"]:::slave
    end

    subgraph PICONET2["PICONET 2"]
        M2["MASTER 2 Laptop"]:::master
        S4["SLAVE 4 Mouse"]:::slave
        S5["SLAVE 5 Keyboard"]:::slave
        BR["BRIDGE SLAVE in P1 MASTER in P2"]:::bridge
    end

    M1 --- S1
    M1 --- S2
    M1 --- S3
    M2 --- S4
    M2 --- S5
    M2 --- BR
    M1 --- BR

    classDef master fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C0392B,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#16A085,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#FFA500,stroke:#D35400,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Piconet 1** has Master 1 (Phone) connected to 3 slaves.
- **Piconet 2** has Master 2 (Laptop) connected to 2 slaves plus 1 bridge.
- The **Bridge** device is a slave in Piconet 1 and a master in Piconet 2 — it enables inter-piconet communication, forming a **Scatternet**.

---

## 4.3 Bluetooth Frame Format

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["ACCESS CODE 72 bits"]:::ac
    H["HEADER 54 bits"]:::hd
    P["PAYLOAD 0 to 2745 bits"]:::pl

    A --> H --> P

    subgraph AC_DETAIL["ACCESS CODE DETAIL"]
        A1["PREAMBLE 4 bits"]:::acd
        A2["SYNC WORD 64 bits"]:::acd
        A3["TRAILER 4 bits"]:::acd
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3

    classDef ac fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef hd fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef pl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef acd fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Access Code (72 bits):** Used for synchronization and piconet identification. Contains preamble, sync word, and trailer.
- **Header (54 bits):** Includes flow control, ARQN, SEQN, and addressing info. Protected by 1/3 FEC.
- **Payload (0–2745 bits):** Actual user data — voice, file, control information.

---

## 4.4 FHSS Frequency Hopping Pattern

```mermaid
graph LR
    F0["CHANNEL 0 2402 MHz"]:::ch
    F1["CHANNEL 1 2403 MHz"]:::ch
    F2["CHANNEL 2 2404 MHz"]:::ch
    F3["CHANNEL 3 2405 MHz"]:::ch
    F4["CHANNEL 4 2406 MHz"]:::ch

    F0 -->|"625 us"| F1
    F1 -->|"625 us"| F2
    F2 -->|"625 us"| F3
    F3 -->|"625 us"| F4
    F4 -.->|"625 us"| F0

    classDef ch fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The master and slaves hop together in a pseudo-random pattern.
- Each channel is occupied for **625 µs** (dwell time).
- 1600 hops occur every second, ensuring robustness against interference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Personal Area Network. List any two wireless PAN technologies.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Personal Area Network (PAN)** is a short-range wireless network that interconnects personal devices (phones, laptops, wearables) within a range of approximately **10 meters** around a single user. It enables cable-free device-to-device communication and is owned/managed by one individual.

**Two wireless PAN technologies:**
1. **Bluetooth** (IEEE 802.15.1) — 2.4 GHz, 1–3 Mbps, range ~10 m.
2. **ZigBee** (IEEE 802.15.4) — 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, low power, mesh networking for IoT.

*(Alternatives: IrDA, NFC, UWB)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is a Piconet? How many active slaves can a piconet have?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Piconet** is the basic unit of a Bluetooth network, consisting of **one master** device and **up to 7 active slave** devices. The master controls all timing, channel access, and the frequency hopping sequence. Communication always occurs between the master and a slave; slaves cannot directly communicate with each other. Additional slaves (up to 255) can be in a parked or standby state. When multiple piconets are linked, they form a **Scatternet**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a) Explain the Bluetooth protocol stack with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Bluetooth protocol stack is organized into three groups — **Transport, Middleware, and Application**.

1. **Radio (RF) Layer** — Operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. Handles modulation/demodulation, transmission/reception.

2. **Baseband Layer** — Manages physical channels, frequency hopping (1600 hops/s), and packet formatting. Implements SCO and ACL link types.

3. **Link Manager Protocol (LMP)** — Responsible for link setup, authentication, encryption, power control, and role switching (master/slave).

4. **Host Controller Interface (HCI)** — Provides a standardized interface between the host (software) and the Bluetooth hardware controller.

5. **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)** — Performs multiplexing of multiple logical connections, segmentation/reassembly of large packets, and quality of service (QoS) management.

6. **Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)** — Allows devices to query and discover available services on other Bluetooth devices.

7. **RFCOMM** — Emulates RS-232 serial port communication, enabling cable replacement.

8. **OBEX (Object Exchange)** — Facilitates object/file transfer between devices.

9. **Application Profiles** — Define end-user scenarios (A2DP for audio, HFP for hands-free, HID for input devices).

*Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the layered architecture.*

**b) Explain the concept of Piconet and Scatternet. How are devices connected in each? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Piconet:**
- A Piconet is a small Bluetooth network formed by **one master** and **up to 7 active slave** devices.
- The master initiates the connection, assigns a 3-bit Active Member Address (AMA) to each slave, and controls the frequency hopping pattern.
- Slaves synchronize to the master's clock and may communicate only with the master.
- Up to **255 parked** slaves can additionally be present.
- Example: A smartphone connected to a wireless earphone, smartwatch, and speaker.

**Scatternet:**
- A Scatternet is formed when **two or more Piconets are interconnected** through a shared device.
- This shared device (bridge) is a **slave in one Piconet** and a **master in another**.
- This allows devices across Piconets to communicate, extending network coverage and device count.
- A device cannot be master in two Piconets simultaneously.
- Example: A laptop (master of Piconet A) connected to a phone (master of Piconet B). The phone is also a slave in Piconet A → forms a Scatternet.

*Refer to Section 4.2 diagram for visualization.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a) Explain the Bluetooth frame format. What is frequency hopping spread spectrum? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Bluetooth Frame Format (Baseband Packet):**

A Bluetooth baseband packet consists of three parts:

1. **Access Code (72 bits):** Contains a 4-bit preamble, 64-bit sync word, and 4-bit trailer. Used for synchronization, DC offset compensation, and piconet identification (channel access code, device access code, inquiry access code).

2. **Header (54 bits):** Contains control information — flow control, ARQN (Acknowledge), SEQN (Sequence Number), logical transport address, and packet type. Protected with 1/3 FEC for robustness, giving 18 bits of useful information.

3. **Payload (0–2745 bits):** Carries the user data — voice (SCO) or data (ACL). Protected by 2/3 FEC or ARQ depending on packet type.

**Packet Types (Examples):**
- **DH1, DH3, DH5:** Data, High rate, 1/3/5 slots
- **HV1, HV2, HV3:** High quality Voice, 1/2/3 slots

**Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS):**

- Bluetooth divides the 2.4 GHz ISM band into **79 channels** of 1 MHz each.
- The master and its slaves hop between these channels at a rate of **1600 hops/second**.
- Dwell time per channel = **625 µs**.
- The hopping sequence is pseudo-random, derived from the master's device address and clock.
- **Advantages:** Resistance to narrowband interference, security (hard to intercept), co-existence with Wi-Fi and other 2.4 GHz devices.

*Refer to Section 4.3 and 4.4 diagrams.*

**b) Compare Bluetooth with ZigBee and IrDA. List the different Bluetooth link types. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Bluetooth | ZigBee | IrDA |
|---------|-----------|--------|------|
| Standard | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.15.4 | IrDA |
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | Optical (IR) |
| Range | 10 m | 10–100 m | 1 m |
| Data Rate | 1–3 Mbps | 250 kbps | 16 Mbps |
| Power | Medium | Very Low | Low |
| Topology | Piconet/Scatternet | Mesh/Star/Cluster | Point-to-point |
| Applications | Audio, IoT, File transfer | Sensor networks, smart home | Remote controls, line-of-sight comm. |

**Bluetooth Link Types:**

1. **Synchronous Connection-Oriented (SCO) Link:**
   - Used for **voice** (e.g., Bluetooth headset calls).
   - Symmetric, point-to-point.
   - Reserves slots periodically (every 2nd, 4th, or 6th slot).
   - No retransmission; uses FEC.
   - Maximum **3 SCO links** per piconet.

2. **Asynchronous Connection-Less (ACL) Link:**
   - Used for **data** (e.g., file transfer, internet tethering).
   - Symmetric or asymmetric.
   - Uses all remaining slots not occupied by SCO.
   - Supports **retransmission (ARQ)** and FEC.
   - Only **1 ACL link** per master-slave pair.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SCO and ACL:** SCO is for **voice** (no retransmission); ACL is for **data** (retransmission supported). Many students swap these.
> 2. **Maximum slaves = 7 ACTIVE**, not 7 total. Up to 255 can be parked/standby.
> 3. **Dwell time = 625 µs** is asked frequently. Show the derivation: $1/1600 \times 10^6$.
> 4. **Piconet master control** is essential — slaves cannot talk to each other directly.
> 5. **Bluetooth is NOT the same as Wi-Fi** — Bluetooth uses FHSS in 2.4 GHz with 79 channels, while Wi-Fi uses DSSS/OFDM with wider channels (20/40/80 MHz).
> 6. **Modulation:** Use GFSK for Basic Rate, not BPSK. Many students wrongly write BPSK.
> 7. **Bluetooth versions:** Memorize key milestones — 1.0 (1994/1999), 2.0+EDR (3 Mbps), 4.0 BLE (Low Energy).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PAN** is a short-range (~10 m) personal network for individual use.
- **Bluetooth** operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** using **FHSS** with **79 channels** of 1 MHz each.
- **Hopping rate** is **1600 hops/sec**; **Dwell time** is **625 µs**.
- **Piconet** = 1 Master + up to **7 active slaves** (up to 255 parked).
- **Scatternet** = Interconnection of two or more Piconets via a bridge device.
- **Bluetooth protocol stack** has 3 groups: Transport (Radio, Baseband, LMP, HCI), Middleware (L2CAP, SDP, RFCOMM), and Application (Profiles like A2DP, HFP, HID).
- **Bluetooth frame** = Access Code (72 bits) + Header (54 bits) + Payload (0–2745 bits).
- **SCO link** → Voice, no retransmission, max 3 per piconet.
- **ACL link** → Data, with retransmission (ARQ), only 1 per master-slave pair.
- **Bluetooth Versions:** 1.0 → 1 Mbps GFSK; 2.0+EDR → 3 Mbps (π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK); 4.0+BLE → Low Energy for IoT; 5.x → Longer range, 2 Mbps LE, LE Audio.
- **Standard:** IEEE 802.15.1 (Classic) and IEEE 802.15.4 (for similar WPANs like ZigBee).
- **Bluetooth originated** with Ericsson in 1994; named after King Harald Bluetooth of Denmark.
- **Master** controls hopping pattern, timing, and channel access.
- **Slaves** are synchronized to master; can be parked, standby, or active.
- **Power classes:** Class 1 (~100 m, 100 mW), Class 2 (~10 m, 2.5 mW), Class 3 (~1 m, 1 mW).
- **Security features:** Authentication, encryption (E0, E1, SAFER+), pairing, secure simple pairing (SSP).
- **Real-world use:** Wireless audio (A2DP), hands-free calling (HFP), input devices (HID), file transfer (OBEX), health sensors (BLE), IoT (Bluetooth Mesh), automotive, beacons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Wi-Fi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Wi-Fi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wi-Fi (Wireless Fidelity)** is a family of wireless local area networking (WLAN) technologies standardized by the **IEEE 802.11 working group** and certified by the **Wi-Fi Alliance**. It operates as a **license-exempt** radio-frequency (RF) communication system, primarily in the industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) bands of **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and the newer **6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E / Wi-Fi 7) spectrum.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Wi-Fi is defined as a **short-range, packet-switched, infrastructure-based or ad-hoc wireless networking protocol** that uses **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)** as its primary medium access control mechanism, combined with spread-spectrum / multicarrier physical layer techniques such as **DSSS**, **OFDM**, and **MIMO/OFDMA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wi-Fi ≠ Wireless Internet.** Wi-Fi is the *local link* technology that connects your device to a router. The Internet beyond that router is delivered by a separate wired/wireless backhaul (DSL, Fiber, 4G/5G). This distinction is a common KTU 1-mark conceptual trap.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **conference room with one microphone and many people who want to talk**. If two people speak at the same time, the audio becomes unintelligible (a collision). The room therefore establishes a strict rule:
1. **Listen first** before speaking (Carrier Sense).
2. If the channel is busy, **wait for a random time** before retrying (Collision Avoidance via backoff).
3. **Speak politely for a short duration**, then release the mic (packet transmission with ACK).

**Wi-Fi is exactly this rule book, encoded in silicon.** The microphone is the **shared radio channel**, the room is the **cell (BSS)**, and the chairperson acting as a traffic controller is the **Access Point (AP)**.

## 1.3 Key Standardization Bodies and Terminology

| Body | Role |
|---|---|
| **IEEE 802.11 WG** | Defines the technical standard (PHY + MAC layers, OSI layers 1 & 2) |
| **Wi-Fi Alliance** | Industry consortium that certifies interoperability and markets "Wi-Fi" branding (Wi-Fi 4, 5, 6, 6E, 7) |
| **ITU-R / FCC / ETSI** | National regulators that allocate the RF spectrum |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Wi-Fi Alliance** uses consumer-friendly generational names (**Wi-Fi 6 = 802.11ax**, **Wi-Fi 7 = 802.11be**), while IEEE uses the alphanumeric code. KTU questions may use either — be fluent in both nomenclatures.

## 1.4 Physical Layer Bands Used by Wi-Fi

| Band | Frequency Range | Wi-Fi Generations | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2.4 GHz ISM** | 2400–2483.5 MHz | 802.11b/g/n/ax/be | Longer range, more interference (microwave, BT) |
| **5 GHz U-NII** | 5150–5825 MHz | 802.11a/n/ac/ax | Wider channels, shorter range, more non-overlapping channels |
| **6 GHz** | 5925–7125 MHz | Wi-Fi 6E / Wi-Fi 7 | Up to 1600 MHz contiguous spectrum; cleanest band |
| **60 GHz mmWave** | 57–64 GHz | 802.11ad / 802.11ay (WiGig) | Very short range, multi-Gbps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The maximum **transmit power** allowed in these bands is regulated. In India, the **WPC (Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing)** of DoT governs the EIRP limits, typically **20 dBm (100 mW)** for indoor 2.4 GHz APs and **30 dBm (1 W)** for outdoor 5 GHz deployments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wi-Fi Network Architecture (IEEE 802.11 Topologies)

The 802.11 standard defines four logical building blocks. These are **fundamental** for any 2-mark or 5-mark KTU question.

### 2.1.1 The Station (STA)
Any device containing a Wi-Fi radio. Examples: smartphone, laptop, IoT sensor, printer.

### 2.1.2 The Basic Service Set (BSS)
The fundamental **cell** of a Wi-Fi network — a set of STAs that communicate with each other. Identified by a **6-byte BSSID** (typically the AP's MAC address).

There are two flavors:
- **Infrastructure BSS**: All traffic flows through the Access Point (AP). This is the home/office case.
- **Independent BSS (IBSS) / Ad-hoc Network**: STAs talk **directly peer-to-peer** without an AP. Used in mesh/quick file sharing.

### 2.1.3 The Access Point (AP)
A special STA that acts as the **bridge** between the wireless BSS and the wired **Distribution System (DS)**. The AP performs:
- Beacon transmission (every 100 ms by default — the **Beacon Interval**).
- Authentication and association of STAs.
- Frame translation between 802.11 (wireless) and 802.3 (Ethernet).
- Bridging/forwarding of data frames.

### 2.1.4 The Distribution System (DS) and Extended Service Set (ESS)
The **DS** is the wired (or wireless) backhaul that connects multiple APs together. The **ESS** is the union of multiple BSSs interconnected by a DS, presenting a single, seamless network to the user (with roaming).

> [!NOTE]
> When you walk from one room to another in your college, your phone roams from BSS-1 to BSS-2 inside the **same ESS** because both APs advertise the same **SSID (Service Set Identifier)**. This re-association takes ~50–500 ms — the small "lag" you sometimes feel.

## 2.2 The OSI Layering of Wi-Fi

Wi-Fi is strictly an **OSI Layer 1 + Layer 2** technology. It does **not** define routing (Layer 3) or anything above MAC.

| OSI Layer | Wi-Fi Component |
|---|---|
| Layer 5–7 (App) | Not defined by 802.11 (e.g., HTTP, DNS live above) |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | TCP/UDP (inherited from the IP stack) |
| Layer 3 (Network) | IPv4 / IPv6 (delivered over the Wi-Fi link) |
| **Layer 2 — LLC** | 802.2 LLC sublayer |
| **Layer 2 — MAC** | **IEEE 802.11 MAC** (CSMA/CA, DCF, PCF, HCDF, EDCA) |
| **Layer 1 — PHY** | DSSS, OFDM, MIMO, OFDMA, 1024-QAM (802.11be) |

## 2.3 Channel Access — CSMA/CA (The Heart of Wi-Fi)

Wi-Fi cannot reliably detect collisions *during* transmission (unlike wired Ethernet's CSMA/CD) because:
- The **near-far problem**: your own transmission swamps any incoming signal at your receiver.
- RF collisions cannot be sensed in half-duplex radios.

Hence Wi-Fi uses **CSMA/CA** with these steps (Dist Coordination Function — DCF):

1. **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)**: Wait a fixed quiet period.
2. **Carrier Sense**: Listen for energy. If idle, proceed; if busy, freeze.
3. **Backoff**: Pick a random slot in the **Contention Window (CW)**. Decrement the counter only while the medium is idle.
4. **Transmit** the entire DATA frame in one burst.
5. **ACK**: Receiver waits **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) and sends an ACK.
6. If no ACK is heard, the sender assumes collision, doubles CW (up to a max), and retries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIFS < DIFS < EIFS**. This priority ordering guarantees that **ACKs and time-critical control frames** (RTS/CTS) always jump the queue ahead of normal data. This is the essence of collision *avoidance*.

## 2.4 Wi-Fi Generational Evolution (High-Yield)

| Gen | IEEE Std | Year | Max PHY Rate | Key PHY Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wi-Fi 1 | 802.11 (legacy) | 1997 | **2 Mbps** | DSSS, FHSS, IR |
| Wi-Fi 2 | 802.11b | 1999 | 11 Mbps | HR-DSSS, CCK |
| Wi-Fi 3 | 802.11a / 802.11g | 1999 / 2003 | **54 Mbps** | **OFDM** in 5 GHz (a) / 2.4 GHz (g) |
| Wi-Fi 4 | **802.11n** | 2009 | **600 Mbps** | **MIMO**, 40 MHz channels, frame aggregation |
| Wi-Fi 5 | **802.11ac** | 2013 | **6.93 Gbps** | MU-MIMO (downlink), 160 MHz, 256-QAM |
| Wi-Fi 6 | **802.11ax** | 2019 | **9.6 Gbps** | **OFDMA**, 1024-QAM, BSS Coloring |
| Wi-Fi 6E | 802.11ax in 6 GHz | 2020 | 9.6 Gbps | Adds 1.2 GHz clean spectrum |
| **Wi-Fi 7** | **802.11be** | 2024 | **46 Gbps** | 320 MHz, 4096-QAM, **MLO (Multi-Link Operation)** |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute value in any LaTeX inside tables. Unit column mandatory.

| Concept | Formula | Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SNR})$ | $C$ capacity, $B$ bandwidth, $\mathrm{SNR} = P/(N_0 B)$ | **bps** |
| Free-Space Path Loss | $\mathrm{FSPL}\,[\mathrm{dB}] = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | $d$ distance in km, $f$ frequency in MHz | **dB** |
| Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 / ((4\pi)^2 d^2 L)$ | $G$ gains, $\lambda = c/f$, $L$ system loss | **W** |
| Thermal Noise Power | $N = k T B$ | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ in K | **W** |
| Noise Power in dBm | $N\,[\mathrm{dBm}] = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B\,[\mathrm{Hz}])$ | Standard $-174$ dBm/Hz floor | **dBm** |
| EIRP | $\mathrm{EIRP}\,[\mathrm{dBm}] = P_t + G_t - L_c$ | $P_t$ TX power, $G_t$ antenna gain | **dBm** |
| OFDM Useful Symbol Time | $T_u = N_{sc} / \Delta f$ | $N_{sc}$ subcarriers, $\Delta f$ subcarrier spacing | **s** |
| Cyclic Prefix Overhead | $\eta_{cp} = T_{cp} / (T_{cp} + T_u)$ | $T_{cp}$ CP duration | **dimensionless** |
| Max Data Rate (802.11n) | $R = N_{ss} \cdot N_{sc} \cdot \mathrm{bits/sym} \cdot \mathrm{coding\_rate} / T_s$ | 52 subcarriers, 4 µs symbol, MCS 0–7 | **bps** |
| Contention Window Backoff | $CW_{n+1} = \min(2(CW_n+1)-1,\, CW_{max})$ | Doubles each retransmission | **slots** |

### 2.5.1 Worked Sub-Formula: Why Wi-Fi 6 OFDMA Is a Big Deal
In OFDM (Wi-Fi 4/5), every user occupies **the entire channel** for a short slot, sequentially. In OFDMA (Wi-Fi 6), the same channel is divided in **frequency** into Resource Units (RUs) and **simultaneously allocated** to different users. This is analogous to replacing a single toll-booth lane with 9 parallel ExpressTAG lanes, dramatically reducing contention in dense deployments.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Enterprise Wi-Fi** (campus, hospital, factory) uses controllers + Lightweight APs (LWAPP/CAPWAP) for centralized RRM, fast roaming (802.11r), and seamless handoff.
- **Wi-Fi calling (VoWiFi)** reuses IMS to deliver voice over Wi-Fi when cellular is weak.
- **Wi-Fi 7 MLO** allows a single device to aggregate a 5 GHz + 6 GHz link simultaneously, achieving deterministic low latency for AR/VR.
- **Municipal Wi-Fi** (Smart City Kerala projects) uses outdoor 5 GHz mesh APs with directional antennas.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below, every algebraic step is written out explicitly. **No shortcut or skip-step allowed.** Each transition is followed by a *Why* line.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Achievable Data Rate of 802.11a/g OFDM

### 3.1.1 Given (from the IEEE 802.11a/g PHY clause 17–19)
- Channel bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz.
- Number of subcarriers $N_{sc} = 64$ (52 used: 48 data + 4 pilot).
- Subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 312.5$ kHz.
- OFDM symbol useful duration $T_u = 1 / \Delta f = 3.2\,\mu s$.
- Cyclic prefix $T_{cp} = 0.8\,\mu s$ (¼ of $T_u$ for legacy).
- Maximum modulation: **64-QAM** (6 bits/symbol) with coding rate $R_c = 3/4$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total OFDM symbol duration } T_s &= T_u + T_{cp} \\
&= 3.2\,\mu s + 0.8\,\mu s \\
&= 4.0\,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why?* The cyclic prefix is a guard interval that absorbs multipath echoes. It is added to the useful symbol time to make T_s the true on-air duration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits per OFDM symbol (data portion)} &= N_{data} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot R_c \\
&= 48 \cdot 6 \cdot \tfrac{3}{4} \\
&= 216 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why?* Only the 48 data subcarriers carry information. Each encodes $log_2(64)=6$ bits. Forward Error Correction (convolutional code) drops the effective rate to $3/4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data rate } R &= \frac{216 \text{ bits}}{4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \\
&= 5.4 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \\
&= \mathbf{54 \text{ Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why?* This is the **raw PHY throughput** of 802.11a/g at MCS-7. Real-world MAC throughput is ~50–60% of this (overhead of ACKs, inter-frame spaces, backoff, headers), giving ~25–32 Mbps usable — a classic KTU 5-mark exam trap.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity for a Wi-Fi 5 Channel

### 3.2.1 Given
- Channel bandwidth $B = 160$ MHz (802.11ac Wave 2).
- Received signal power $P_r = -50$ dBm.
- Noise figure $F = 6$ dB.
- Temperature $T = 290$ K.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert $P_r$ to linear Watts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r\,[\mathrm{dBm}] &= -50 \\
P_r\,[\mathrm{mW}] &= 10^{-50/10} = 10^{-5} \\
P_r\,[\mathrm{W}] &= 10^{-5} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute noise spectral density (effective):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0\,[\mathrm{dBm/Hz}] &= -174 + F \\
&= -174 + 6 \\
&= -168 \text{ dBm/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N_0 = 10^{-16.8}\ \mathrm{mW/Hz} = 1.585 \times 10^{-20}\ \mathrm{W/Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total noise power in 160 MHz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= N_0 \cdot B \\
&= 1.585 \times 10^{-20} \times 160 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 2.536 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathrm{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SNR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{SNR} &= \frac{P_r}{N} = \frac{10^{-8}}{2.536 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&\approx 3943.7 \\
\mathrm{SNR}\,[\mathrm{dB}] &= 10\log_{10}(3943.7) \approx 35.96\ \mathrm{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SNR}) \\
&= 160 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(3944.7) \\
&= 160 \times 10^{6} \cdot 11.946 \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.91\ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why it matters:* 802.11ac advertises 6.93 Gbps by using **8 spatial streams, 256-QAM, and MU-MIMO** with **MU-MIMO boosting spectral efficiency** beyond classical Shannon single-user limits. The Shannon number is the **single-antenna, single-user theoretical ceiling**; practical systems exceed it only through MIMO spatial multiplexing, not raw modulation tricks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss Between an AP and a Laptop

**Given:** $f = 2.4$ GHz, $d = 30$ m, $P_t = 20$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi.

**Step 1 — Frequency in MHz:**

$$
f = 2400\ \mathrm{MHz},\quad d = 0.03\ \mathrm{km}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply FSPL formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{FSPL} &= 20\log_{10}(0.03) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.45 \\
&= 20(-1.523) + 20(3.380) + 32.45 \\
&= -30.46 + 67.60 + 32.45 \\
&= 69.59\ \mathrm{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Received power (Friis in dB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r\,[\mathrm{dBm}] &= P_t + G_t + G_r - \mathrm{FSPL} \\
&= 20 + 2 + 2 - 69.59 \\
&= -45.59\ \mathrm{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why it matters:* A typical 802.11ax client requires roughly **$-65$ dBm** for the highest MCS. The 19.4 dB link margin at 30 m comfortably supports full-speed operation. At **120 m**, FSPL rises by 12 dB → margin shrinks to ~7.4 dB → rate adaptation drops the MCS.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Wi-Fi Generations

| Parameter | Wi-Fi 4 (802.11n) | Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac) | Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) | Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Channel Width | 40 MHz | 160 MHz | 160 MHz | **320 MHz** |
| Max Modulation | 64-QAM | 256-QAM | 1024-QAM | **4096-QAM** |
| Bits/symbol | 6 | 8 | 10 | **12** |
| Spatial Streams | 4×4 | 8×8 | 8×8 | **16×16** |
| MU-MIMO | No (SU) | DL only | UL + DL | **UL + DL** |
| Multi-User | No | DL MU-MIMO | **OFDMA + MU-MIMO** | **MLO + OFDMA** |
| Max PHY Rate | 600 Mbps | 6.93 Gbps | 9.6 Gbps | **46 Gbps** |
| Typical Real Throughput | ~200 Mbps | ~1.5 Gbps | ~2 Gbps | ~5 Gbps |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wi-Fi Network Architecture (ESS with Multiple APs)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the IEEE 802.11 Extended Service Set. Node IDs use the alphanumeric convention; labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric (no markdown inside quotes).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart([Wi-Fi Network Architecture ESS])
    nodeInternet[Internet Backhaul]
    nodeRouter[Core Router Gateway]
    nodeDS[Distribution System Wired Ethernet 802.3]
    nodeAP1[AP1 BSSID AA:BB CC 01 SSID College Wi-Fi Ch 1]
    nodeAP2[AP2 BSSID AA:BB CC 02 SSID College Wi-Fi Ch 6]
    nodeAP3[AP3 BSSID AA:BB CC 03 SSID College Wi-Fi Ch 11]
    nodeSTA1[STA Smartphone Lab 1]
    nodeSTA2[STA Laptop Lab 1]
    nodeSTA3[STA IoT Sensor Lab 2]
    nodeSTA4[STA Tablet Lab 2]
    nodeSTA5[STA Printer Library]
    nodeLegacy[Legacy STA 802.11b Only]

    nodeStart --> nodeInternet
    nodeInternet --> nodeRouter
    nodeRouter --> nodeDS
    nodeDS --> nodeAP1
    nodeDS --> nodeAP2
    nodeDS --> nodeAP3
    nodeAP1 -. 2.4 GHz OFDM .-> nodeSTA1
    nodeAP1 -. 2.4 GHz OFDM .-> nodeSTA2
    nodeAP2 -. 2.4 GHz OFDM .-> nodeSTA3
    nodeAP2 -. 2.4 GHz OFDM .-> nodeSTA4
    nodeAP3 -. 2.4 GHz OFDM .-> nodeSTA5
    nodeAP3 -. DSSS CCK Fallback .-> nodeLegacy
```

> [!NOTE]
> Read this diagram as follows: STA1 is associated with AP1 → reaches the Internet via Router. Roaming occurs when STA1 walks to AP2's coverage — the **DS** keeps the SSID constant, so the IP session survives.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wi-Fi Transceiver

The Mermaid block below decomposes the **PHY-layer signal path** of a modern Wi-Fi 6/7 transmitter. Note the use of `blockA`, `blockB` style node IDs to comply with the alpha-rule safeguard.

```mermaid
graph LR
    blkIn[MAC Frame MPDU from 802.11 MAC]
    blkScramb[Scrambler Avoid Long Zero Runs]
    blkFec[FEC Encoder LDPC 802.11be Binary 1 2 Rate]
    blkInt[Bit Interleaver]
    blkMap[Constellation Mapper BPSK to 4096 QAM]
    blkPilot[Insert Pilot Subcarriers 8 in Wi-Fi 7]
    blkIFFE[IFFT 4096 Point for 320 MHz]
    blkCP[Add Cyclic Prefix Guard Interval]
    blkDAC[DAC and RF Upconverter 2.4 5 6 GHz]
    blkAnt[Tx Antennas N SS up to 16]
    blkAir[Wireless Channel Multipath Fading Noise Interference]
    blkRxAnt[Rx Antennas N SS up to 16]
    blkAD[ADC and RF Downconverter]
    blkSync[Time and Frequency Synchronization]
    blkRemoveCP[Remove Cyclic Prefix]
    blkFFE[FFT 4096 Point]
    blkChEst[Channel Estimation via HE LTF]
    blkEq[MIMO Detector MMSE or ZF]
    blkDeint[Bit Deinterleaver]
    blkDec[LDPC Decoder Soft Information]
    blkDescr[Descrambler]
    blkOut[Recovered MAC Frame to Upper Layer]

    blkIn --> blkScramb --> blkFec --> blkInt --> blkMap --> blkPilot --> blkIFFE --> blkCP --> blkDAC --> blkAnt
    blkAnt --> blkAir --> blkRxAnt
    blkRxAnt --> blkAD --> blkSync --> blkRemoveCP --> blkFFE --> blkChEst --> blkEq --> blkDeint --> blkDec --> blkDescr --> blkOut
```

## 4.3 Wi-Fi Generational Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    e1997[1997 802.11 Legacy 2 Mbps DSSS FHSS]
    e1999a[1999 802.11a 54 Mbps OFDM 5 GHz]
    e1999b[1999 802.11b 11 Mbps DSSS 2.4 GHz]
    e2003[2003 802.11g 54 Mbps OFDM 2.4 GHz]
    e2009[2009 802.11n Wi-Fi 4 600 Mbps MIMO 40 MHz]
    e2013[2013 802.11ac Wi-Fi 5 6.93 Gbps MU-MIMO 160 MHz]
    e2019[2019 802.11ax Wi-Fi 6 9.6 Gbps OFDMA 1024 QAM]
    e2020[2020 Wi-Fi 6E Adds 6 GHz Band 1.2 GHz]
    e2024[2024 802.11be Wi-Fi 7 46 Gbps 320 MHz MLO 4096 QAM]

    e1997 --> e1999a
    e1997 --> e1999b
    e1999a --> e2003
    e1999b --> e2003
    e2003 --> e2009
    e2009 --> e2013
    e2013 --> e2019
    e2019 --> e2020
    e2020 --> e2024
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions follow the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern: **Part A carries 3 marks each (short answer), Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice and two 7-mark sub-parts**. COs mapped: **CO1 (Understand WLAN concepts)** and **CO2 (Apply PHY/MAC principles of 802.11)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Wi-Fi. List any four Wi-Fi standards with their maximum data rates. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Wi-Fi is a family of **WLAN technologies** standardized by the **IEEE 802.11 working group** and certified by the **Wi-Fi Alliance**. It operates in the license-exempt **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** ISM bands using CSMA/CA medium access.

Four standards and max data rates:

| Standard | Max Data Rate | Band |
|---|---|---|
| 802.11b | 11 Mbps | 2.4 GHz |
| 802.11g | 54 Mbps | 2.4 GHz |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 600 Mbps | 2.4 / 5 GHz |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 6.93 Gbps | 5 GHz |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) | 9.6 Gbps | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz |

---

### Q2. Explain the function of an Access Point in an Infrastructure BSS. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An **Access Point (AP)** is a special STA that:
1. Acts as the **central hub** of an Infrastructure BSS.
2. **Transmits beacon frames** every beacon interval (default 100 ms) advertising the SSID, supported rates, and capabilities.
3. Performs **authentication and association** of STAs.
4. **Bridges 802.11 wireless frames to 802.3 Ethernet** at the Distribution System.
5. **Forwards data** between STAs and external networks.

The AP's MAC address is the **BSSID**, uniquely identifying the cell.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B.**

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an **Extended Service Set (ESS)** and differentiate between BSS, IBSS, ESS, and DS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CSMA/CA mechanism** used in 802.11 MAC. Why is collision detection (CD) not used? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) ESS Architecture

**Diagram** (text version, draw during exam):

```
   [Internet] ──► [Gateway Router] ──► [DS - Switch]
                                          │
                  ┌───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┐
                  │                       │                       │
              [AP1 BSS-1]            [AP2 BSS-2]            [AP3 BSS-3]
              /     \                /     \                /     \
         [STA1] [STA2]          [STA3] [STA4]          [STA5] [STA6]
         
   (All APs share SSID "College-WiFi" → form one ESS)
```

**Definitions (each 1.5 marks):**
- **BSS (Basic Service Set)**: A single cell consisting of one AP and all associated STAs. Identified by a 6-byte BSSID.
- **IBSS (Independent BSS) / Ad-hoc**: STAs communicate peer-to-peer **without an AP**. Used in mesh / spontaneous networks. Topology changes dynamically.
- **DS (Distribution System)**: The **wired backhaul** (typically Ethernet) that interconnects multiple APs.
- **ESS (Extended Service Set)**: A union of **one or more BSSs interconnected by a single DS**, presenting a **uniform SSID** to users, enabling seamless roaming.

**Key differences:**

| Feature | BSS | IBSS | ESS |
|---|---|---|---|
| AP required | Yes | No | Yes (≥1) |
| DS required | No | No | Yes |
| Coverage | Single cell | Single cell (ad-hoc) | Multi-cell (roaming) |
| Roaming | N/A | N/A | Yes |

#### Model Solution — (b) CSMA/CA

Wi-Fi is **half-duplex** — a radio cannot transmit and sense the channel simultaneously without self-saturating its own receiver (the **near-far problem**). Hence CSMA/CD (Collision Detection), used in wired Ethernet, is **infeasible** for radio. Instead, 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)**.

**Step-by-step mechanism (each step = 1 mark):**

1. **DIFS wait**: After the channel is sensed idle, the STA waits a DCF Inter-Frame Space (DIFS).
2. **Carrier sense**: STA listens for energy above the CCA threshold for the entire DIFS period.
3. **Backoff counter**: If the channel remains idle, the STA picks a random integer in [0, CW] and decrements a backoff counter **slot-by-slot** (each slot = 9 µs in 802.11b). The counter freezes if the medium becomes busy.
4. **Transmit**: When the counter reaches zero, the STA transmits the MPDU.
5. **ACK timeout**: The sender waits for a **SIFS + ACK** from the receiver. ACK timeout implies failure.
6. **Binary exponential backoff**: On failure, $CW = \min(2(CW+1)-1, CW_{max})$. Retries continue up to **dot11ShortRetryLimit** (default 7).
7. **Fragmentation / RTS-CTS** (optional, for hidden-node scenarios): A STA may optionally send an RTS; the AP replies with a CTS, reserving the channel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall — Examiner's note:**
> - Many students write *"CSMA/CA is used because Wi-Fi is wireless"*. This is too vague. You **must** mention the **half-duplex / near-far problem** as the physical reason. Lose 1 mark otherwise.
> - Some students forget to mention **SIFS < DIFS** — the priority ordering that lets ACKs jump the queue. Examiners award marks for this nuance.
> - Confusing **CW (Contention Window)** with the backoff counter itself is a frequent error. Clarify: CW is the *range*, the counter is the *value drawn from* that range.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a comparison table, explain the **evolution of Wi-Fi standards** from 802.11b to 802.11ax in terms of data rate, modulation, channel width, and MIMO support. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 802.11n AP uses a **40 MHz channel** with **4 spatial streams**, **64-QAM** modulation, and a **FEC coding rate of 3/4**. Calculate the maximum PHY data rate. State the formula clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Evolution Table (each comparison point = 1 mark + 1 mark for the table)

| Standard | Year | Max Rate | Modulation | Channel Width | MIMO |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 802.11b | 1999 | 11 Mbps | DSSS / CCK | 20 MHz | 1×1 |
| 802.11g | 2003 | 54 Mbps | OFDM, 64-QAM | 20 MHz | 1×1 |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2009 | 600 Mbps | OFDM, 64-QAM | 20 / 40 MHz | 4×4 |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 2013 | 6.93 Gbps | OFDM, 256-QAM | 20–160 MHz | 8×8 MU |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) | 2019 | 9.6 Gbps | OFDM + OFDMA, 1024-QAM | 20–160 MHz | 8×8 MU + UL |

**Key innovations to mention (each 1 mark):**
- 802.11n introduced **MIMO** (multiple spatial streams) and **40 MHz channel bonding**.
- 802.11ac introduced **MU-MIMO** (downlink, up to 4 users simultaneously) and 256-QAM.
- 802.11ax introduced **OFDMA**, **BSS Coloring**, and **Target Wake Time (TWT)** for IoT efficiency.

#### Model Solution — (b) 802.11n PHY Rate Calculation

**Given (write on paper):**
- Channel width $B = 40$ MHz
- Number of spatial streams $N_{ss} = 4$
- Modulation = 64-QAM → 6 bits per subcarrier symbol
- Coding rate $R_c = 3/4$
- Number of data subcarriers $N_d = 108$ (for 40 MHz in 802.11n)
- OFDM symbol duration $T_s = 4 \,\mu s$

**Step 1 — Bits per OFDM symbol per stream:**

$$
N_{bits} = N_d \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot R_c = 108 \cdot 6 \cdot \tfrac{3}{4} = 486 \text{ bits/stream/symbol}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by spatial streams:**

$$
\text{Total bits per symbol} = 486 \cdot 4 = 1944 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by symbol duration:**

$$
R = \frac{1944}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.86 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} = \mathbf{486 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

**Note for the student:** The textbook 600 Mbps figure of 802.11n requires **short GI (0.4 µs)**. Without short GI, the rate is 540 Mbps; with 4 streams + short GI + 64-QAM 5/6, you reach 600 Mbps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall alerts for Question B:**
> - Forgetting to **multiply by $N_{ss}$** — the most common 3-mark error.
> - Using 48 data subcarriers (which is the 20 MHz number) instead of 108 (40 MHz). The number 108 = 52 + 56 where 56 are the extra subcarriers added for the upper 20 MHz block in 40 MHz mode.
> - Converting the final answer to **Mbps** without unit prefix — examiners are strict.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the KTU exam:

- **Wi-Fi = IEEE 802.11** = WLAN technology using **CSMA/CA** in **2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz** bands.
- **Wi-Fi Alliance** is the certifier; **IEEE** is the standardizer.
- Four building blocks: **STA, AP, BSS, DS → ESS**.
- **BSSID** = AP MAC (6 bytes). **SSID** = friendly network name.
- IBSS / ad-hoc mode = no AP, peer-to-peer.
- **CSMA/CA** is mandatory because Wi-Fi is **half-duplex** and suffers from the **near-far problem**.
- Priority order: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**.
- Random backoff is drawn from the **Contention Window (CW)**; on collision, CW doubles up to $CW_{max}$.
- **802.11b → DSSS**, **802.11a/g/n/ac/ax → OFDM**, **802.11ax → OFDMA**, **802.11be → 4096-QAM + MLO**.
- Channels in 2.4 GHz: only **3 non-overlapping** (1, 6, 11 in most regulatory domains).
- 802.11n MIMO introduced **spatial multiplexing** — multiplying rate by $N_{ss}$.
- Real-world throughput ≈ **40–60%** of PHY rate (overhead of ACKs, DIFS, backoff, MAC headers).
- **FSPL (dB) = 20log(d) + 20log(f) + 32.45** with d in km, f in MHz.
- **Shannon limit**: $C = B\log_2(1 + \mathrm{SNR})$ — the absolute ceiling; Wi-Fi only approaches it with MIMO + high-order QAM.
- **Wi-Fi 7 highlights**: 320 MHz, 4096-QAM, 16 spatial streams, **Multi-Link Operation (MLO)**.
- **Generation naming**: Wi-Fi 4 = 802.11n, Wi-Fi 5 = 802.11ac, Wi-Fi 6 = 802.11ax, Wi-Fi 7 = 802.11be.
- **Indian regulation**: WPC/DoT governs 2.4 / 5 GHz EIRP; 6 GHz is partially delicensed under the 2022 NFAP.
- **Memorize**: 802.11a/g OFDM → 48 data subcarriers, 4 pilots, 52 total, 20 MHz, 4 µs symbol → 54 Mbps at 64-QAM 3/4.
- **Frame structure**: Recall MPDU = MAC header + MSDU body + FCS (4-byte CRC). Header includes Frame Control, Duration, Addresses 1–4, Seq Control.
- **Beacons** are sent every **100 ms** by default (TBTT — Target Beacon Transmission Time).
- **Hidden node problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** handshake (4-way: RTS, CTS, DATA, ACK).
- **Mesh Wi-Fi** uses 802.11s; **fast roaming** uses 802.11r; **enterprise security** uses 802.1X + WPA3.
- **Wi-Fi 6E** is just **802.11ax extended to 6 GHz**; same PHY/MAC otherwise.
- **AP placement rule of thumb**: APs in a hexagonal grid with **cell radius ≈ half of inter-AP distance** for best roaming.
- **For KTU numericals**: always show the substitution step with units; examiners check for unit consistency first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WiMAX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** WiMAX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **WiMAX (Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access)** is a family of wireless broadband communication standards based on the **IEEE 802.16** series, designed to provide high-throughput, long-range, last-mile broadband connectivity as an alternative to cable, DSL, and fiber for **Fixed** and **Mobile** subscribers.

- WiMAX is governed by the **WiMAX Forum** (industry consortium founded in **2001**), which certifies interoperability of equipment compliant with the IEEE 802.16 standard and the **ETSI HiperMAN** specification.
- It operates across licensed and license-exempt radio spectrum bands from **2 GHz to 66 GHz**.
- The two principal profiles certified by the WiMAX Forum are:
  - **Fixed WiMAX** — based on IEEE **802.16d / 802.16-2004** (released 2004), optimized for stationary CPE (Customer Premises Equipment).
  - **Mobile WiMAX** — based on IEEE **802.16e / 802.16-2005** (released 2005), which added full mobility support and **Scalable OFDMA (SOFDMA)**.
- Peak theoretical data rates: up to **75 Mbps** (Fixed) and up to **128 Mbps** (Mobile, with MIMO).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wireless Highway"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a busy two-lane village road (Wi-Fi) that only serves a small neighborhood over a few hundred meters. Now imagine an **8-lane expressway with on-ramps and off-ramps** that connects entire cities across **tens of kilometers**. **Wi-Fi is the village road; WiMAX is the expressway.** Both use radio waves, but WiMAX uses a far more sophisticated access scheme (OFDM/OFDMA), stronger MAC-layer scheduling, and licensed spectrum to cover metropolitan areas at highway speeds.

## 1.3 Key Standardization Lineage

| Year | Standard | Key Enhancement |
|------|----------|-----------------|
| 2001 | IEEE 802.16 | First release; **10–66 GHz** Line-of-Sight (LOS) only |
| 2003 | IEEE 802.16a | Added **2–11 GHz** band; Non-LOS (NLOS) operation |
| 2004 | IEEE 802.16d / 802.16-2004 | **Fixed WiMAX** rollup; improved NLOS, mesh support |
| 2005 | IEEE 802.16e / 802.16-2005 | **Mobile WiMAX**; SOFDMA, handoff, MIMO, AAS |
| 2011 | IEEE 802.16m | **WiMAX 2 / WiMAX-Advanced**; ITU-R IMT-Advanced candidate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why WiMAX matters in KTU syllabus:** It is the canonical *cellular-class broadband* example that demonstrates the transition from **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** to **4G-style wide-area OFDMA networking**, helping students understand OFDM, frame structure, and QoS scheduling as applied to MANs (Metropolitan Area Networks).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WiMAX Network Reference Model (NRM)

The WiMAX Forum defined a **Network Reference Model** comprising three logical entities interacting over reference points **R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R8**:

- **MS (Mobile Station)** — the subscriber device.
- **ASN (Access Service Network)** — radio access network, comprising one or more **BSs (Base Stations)** and **ASN-GWs (gateways)**.
- **CSN (Connectivity Service Network)** — the IP core, providing AAA, DHCP, HA (Home Agent for Mobile IP), and internet gateway functions.

> Reference points: **R1** = MS ↔ ASN (radio), **R2** = MS ↔ CSN (auth/policy), **R3** = ASN ↔ CSN, **R4–R6** = internal ASN interfaces, **R8** = BS ↔ BS (inter-BS handover).

## 2.2 WiMAX Physical Layer (PHY) — Key Features

- **Waveform:** OFDM (Fixed WiMAX, 256 FFT) and **Scalable OFDMA (Mobile WiMAX, FFT sizes 128 / 512 / 1024 / 2048)**.
- **Duplexing:** **TDD** (Time Division Duplex) and **FDD** (Frequency Division Duplex), with TDD preferred in Mobile WiMAX due to flexible UL/DL ratio.
- **Channel Bandwidths:** Scalable 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2, 2.5, 3.5, 5, 7, 8.75, 10, 14, 15, 17.5, **20 MHz** (Mobile WiMAX).
- **Subcarrier Spacing** is fixed at **Δf = 10.94 kHz** regardless of FFT size (this is the *scalable* part of SOFDMA).
- **Modulation/Coding:** Adaptive Modulation and Coding (AMC) — **BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** with convolutional codes (CC) and convolutional turbo codes (CTC).

## 2.3 WiMAX MAC Layer Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Connection-oriented**: Every service flow is mapped to a **CID (Connection Identifier)** — a 16-bit identifier.
- **QoS classes**: UGS, rtPS, ertPS, nrtPS, BE (Unsolicited Grant, real-time Polling, extended real-time, non-real-time, Best Effort).
- **Bandwidth request/grant** mechanism (contention-based + polling) instead of CSMA/CD like Ethernet.
- Supports **ARQ** (Automatic Repeat reQuest) and **HARQ** (Hybrid ARQ — in 802.16e/m).
- **5-byte MAC PDU header** + optional sub-headers + payload.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU university exam numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{N_{\text{used}} \cdot BW}{N_{\text{FFT}}}$ | Subcarrier spacing relationship | Hz |
| 2 | $T_{\text{useful}} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | Useful OFDM symbol time | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{s} = T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{CP}} = T_{\text{useful}} \cdot (1 + G)$ | Total OFDM symbol time with cyclic prefix (CP) of ratio **G** | seconds |
| 4 | $R_{\text{peak}} = BW \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot N_{\text{ss}}$ | Peak throughput (BW = bandwidth, M = M-ary, $\eta_c$ = coding rate, $N_{ss}$ = spatial streams) | **Mbps** |
| 5 | $N_{\text{used}} = N_{\text{FFT}} - N_{\text{guard}}$ | Used subcarriers (e.g., 1536 of 2048 in 16m with $\approx 25\%$ guard) | subcarriers |
| 6 | $P_{\text{cell}} = N_{\text{se}} \cdot P_{\text{se}}$ | Total cell transmit power = sectors × power/sector | **Watts** |
| 7 | $L_{\text{path}} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | **dB** |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{req,dB}}(M) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}(M-1)\right)$ for large M | Approx. SNR requirement for M-QAM (AWGN) | **dB** |
| 9 | $C_{\text{shannon}} = BW \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity (upper bound) | **bps** |
| 10 | $\text{Cell Radius} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{\text{EIRP}} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{L_{\text{max}} \cdot (4\pi)^2 \cdot P_{\text{min}}}}$ | Maximum coverage (Friis-derived) | **meters** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** WiMAX-style **OFDMA + adaptive modulation** is the foundational PHY technique reused in **4G LTE, 5G NR, and Wi-Fi 6/6E/7**. Mastering WiMAX frame structures is therefore a stepping stone to understanding all modern cellular systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: OFDM Symbol Duration in Scalable OFDMA

We know that the **useful OFDM symbol duration** is the reciprocal of the subcarrier spacing $\Delta f$, since the FFT window of size $N_{\text{FFT}}$ spans $N_{\text{FFT}} \cdot T_{\text{sample}} = 1/\Delta f$ seconds.

**Step 1.** For WiMAX 16m, the design constant is the subcarrier spacing.

$$\Delta f = 10.94 \text{ kHz}$$

This is **deliberately kept constant** for *all* FFT sizes — this is the *scalability* principle. It guarantees that the *coherence time* behavior (Doppler tolerance) and *coherence bandwidth* behavior (delay spread tolerance) are preserved across all channel bandwidths.

**Step 2.** Compute the useful symbol duration.

$$T_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{10940 \text{ Hz}} \approx 91.43 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3.** Add the cyclic prefix. For Mobile WiMAX, two CP ratios are allowed: **G = 1/8** and **G = 1/16**.

For G = 1/8:

$$T_{\text{CP}} = G \cdot T_{\text{useful}} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 91.43 \text{ μs} \approx 11.43 \text{ μs}$$

$$T_{s} = T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{CP}} = 91.43 + 11.43 = 102.86 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4.** Verify the relationship between FFT size, channel bandwidth, and subcarrier spacing.

$$N_{\text{used}} \cdot \Delta f = N_{\text{used}} \cdot 10.94 \text{ kHz} \approx BW$$

For 2048-FFT with $N_{\text{used}} = 1200$ subcarriers (downlink):

$$BW \approx 1200 \times 10.94 \text{ kHz} = 13.13 \text{ MHz}$$

(plus DC and guard subcarriers that occupy the remaining ~7.87 MHz of the **20 MHz** channel).

**Step 5.** Number of OFDM symbols per 5 ms frame.

$$N_{\text{sym/frame}} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{T_{s}} = \frac{5000 \text{ μs}}{102.86 \text{ μs}} \approx 48.6 \Rightarrow 48 \text{ symbols}$$

(The leftover time is filled with idle samples — a part of the WiMAX frame-design ingenuity.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Peak Throughput of a Mobile WiMAX 16m Downlink

Given: **BW = 20 MHz**, FFT = 2048, $N_{\text{used}} = 1200$ subcarriers, **64-QAM**, coding rate **3/4**, **2×2 MIMO** (i.e., 2 spatial streams).

**Step 1.** Bits per symbol per subcarrier for 64-QAM:

$$\log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2.** Total data-carrying subcarriers per OFDM symbol:

$$N_{\text{data}} \approx N_{\text{used}} \cdot 0.72 \approx 1200 \times 0.72 = 864 \text{ (pilot + DC overhead) }$$

**Step 3.** Data bits per OFDM symbol (one spatial stream):

$$B_{\text{sym}} = N_{\text{data}} \times 6 = 864 \times 6 = 5184 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4.** Apply coding rate:

$$B_{\text{coded}} = B_{\text{sym}} \times \eta_c = 5184 \times \frac{3}{4} = 3888 \text{ bits per symbol}$$

**Step 5.** Number of OFDM symbols per second (with G = 1/8):

$$R_{\text{sym}} = \frac{1}{T_{s}} = \frac{1}{102.86 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 9721 \text{ symbols/sec}$$

**Step 6.** Gross data rate per spatial stream:

$$R_{\text{stream}} = 3888 \times 9721 \approx 37.79 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 7.** Apply 2×2 MIMO doubling (and assume ~70% DL allocation in TDD frame):

$$R_{\text{peak,DL}} = 2 \times 37.79 \times 0.7 \approx 52.9 \text{ Mbps}$$

This result aligns with the **WiMAX Forum Release 2 (16m) specification** target of **> 100 Mbps** in a fully loaded 2×2 MIMO 20 MHz TDD deployment (sustained).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Path Loss (Cost-231 Extension) for WiMAX 3.5 GHz

Free-space path loss formula at **3.5 GHz**, distance **d = 5 km**:

$$L_{\text{path}} = 20 \log_{10}(5000) + 20 \log_{10}(3500) + 32.44$$

$$= 20(3.699) + 20(3.544) + 32.44 = 73.98 + 70.88 + 32.44 = 177.30 \text{ dB}$$

Thus, at the receiver, the available signal power is roughly:

$$P_{\text{rx}} = P_{\text{tx}} + G_{\text{tx}} + G_{\text{rx}} - L_{\text{path}} - L_{\text{misc}}$$

For $P_{\text{tx}} = 36 \text{ dBm}$ (4 W), $G_{\text{tx}} = 17 \text{ dBi}$, $G_{\text{rx}} = 18 \text{ dBi}$, $L_{\text{misc}} = 10 \text{ dB}$:

$$P_{\text{rx}} = 36 + 17 + 18 - 177.30 - 10 = -116.30 \text{ dBm}$$

This is **typical for WiMAX 3.5 GHz macrocell** planning and shows why WiMAX relies on **directional antennas, robust coding, and ARQ** to maintain cell radii of 5–10 km.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WiMAX Network Reference Model (Functional Architecture)
