```mermaid
    P --> B["N EQUALS 4  USE i EQUALS 2 j EQUALS 0"]
    P --> C["N EQUALS 7  USE i EQUALS 2 j EQUALS 1"]
    P --> D["N EQUALS 12  USE i EQUALS 2 j EQUALS 2"]
    P --> E["N EQUALS 19  USE i EQUALS 3 j EQUALS 2"]
    A --> A1["Q EQUALS SQRT 9 EQUALS 3.00"]
    B --> B1["Q EQUALS SQRT 12 EQUALS 3.46"]
    C --> C1["Q EQUALS SQRT 21 EQUALS 4.58"]
    D --> D1["Q EQUALS SQRT 36 EQUALS 6.00"]
    E --> E1["Q EQUALS SQRT 57 EQUALS 7.55"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Frequency Reuse Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START PLANNING CELLULAR LAYOUT"] --> T["DETERMINE REQUIRED TRAFFIC CHANNELS S"]
    T --> U["SELECT CLUSTER SIZE N"]
    U --> V{"NEED MORE CAPACITY"}
    V -->|"YES"| W["CHOOSE SMALL N  EXAMPLE N EQUALS 3 OR 4"]
    V -->|"NO"| X["CHOOSE LARGER N  EXAMPLE N EQUALS 7 OR 12"]
    W --> Y["COMPUTE K EQUALS S OVER N CHANNELS PER CELL"]
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z["COMPUTE Q EQUALS SQRT 3N REUSE RATIO"]
    Z --> AA["LOCATE CO CHANNEL CELLS USING i j SHIFT"]
    AA --> AB["VERIFY S OVER I EQUALS D OVER R TO POWER N DIVIDED BY 6"]
    AB --> AC{"INTERFERENCE OK"}
    AC -->|"YES"| AD["DEPLOY NETWORK"]
    AC -->|"NO"| AE["INCREASE N AND REPEAT"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **EXAM HINT** : When asked to "draw the cluster pattern for $N = 7$", use the $i = 2, j = 1$ shift : move **2 cells along the I-axis** (straight line), then **1 cell along the J-axis** (rotated $60^\circ$). The shortest vector to a co-channel cell in the next cluster follows these exact steps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each — Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Q : Why is the hexagonal geometry preferred over square or triangular geometry for representing cells in a cellular system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks) :**
Among regular polygons that can tessellate the plane (square, triangle, hexagon), the hexagon provides:
1. **Maximum coverage area** for a given cell radius $R$, i.e., $A_{hex} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^2 > A_{square} = 4R^2 > A_{triangle} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{4} R^2$ — wait, the hexagon is the closest approximation to the **circular radiation pattern** of an omnidirectional antenna.
2. **No gaps and no overlaps** between adjacent cells, ensuring continuous coverage without interference zones.
3. **Fewer base stations** are required to cover a given geographic area.

Hence, hexagonal geometry is the **standard model** in cellular system design.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q : Define co-channel cells and co-channel reuse ratio $Q$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks) :**
- **Co-channel cells** are cells in different clusters that use the **same set of radio frequency channels**. They are separated by a minimum geographical distance $D$ to keep co-channel interference (CCI) below an acceptable threshold.
- **Co-channel reuse ratio** $Q$ is defined as the ratio of the **co-channel reuse distance** $D$ to the **cell radius** $R$ :

$$Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

where $N$ is the cluster size. A larger $Q$ implies better interference isolation but lower system capacity.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE STRUCTURE** : Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE of the TWO choices** provided. Below are two fully-worked independent choices.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A — CHOICE 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the co-channel reuse ratio $Q = \sqrt{3N}$. Explain the role of shift parameters $i$ and $j$ in locating co-channel cells.**

**Model Solution :**

**Step 1 : Hexagonal Coordinate System**
Consider a regular hexagonal grid. Define two non-orthogonal axes $\vec{I}$ and $\vec{J}$ separated by an angle of $60^\circ$. The unit vectors along these axes have magnitude equal to the distance between centers of adjacent hexagons, which is $\sqrt{3}\,R$.

**Step 2 : Position Vector to Co-channel Cell**
A co-channel cell in another cluster can be reached from the reference cell by moving $i$ cells along the I-axis and $j$ cells along the J-axis. The displacement vector is :

$$\vec{D} = i\,\vec{I} + j\,\vec{J}$$

**Step 3 : Magnitude using Law of Cosines**

$$D^2 = i^2 \vert \vec{I} \vert^2 + j^2 \vert \vec{J} \vert^2 + 2ij \vert \vec{I} \vert \vert \vec{J} \vert \cos(60^\circ)$$

Substituting $\vert \vec{I} \vert = \vert \vec{J} \vert = \sqrt{3}\,R$ and $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$ :

$$D^2 = 3R^2 (i^2 + j^2 + ij)$$

**Step 4 : Cluster Size Formula**
The cluster size is the area of the parallelogram formed by $i\vec{I}$ and $j\vec{J}$ divided by the area of one hexagonal cell :

$$N = i^2 + ij + j^2$$

**Step 5 : Combine**

$$D^2 = 3N R^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{Q = \sqrt{3N}}$$

**Role of $i, j$** : The integers $i$ and $j$ (with $i \geq j \geq 0$) act as **shift instructions** for the frequency reuse planner to identify the location of the nearest co-channel cell in the adjacent cluster. For example, $(i, j) = (2, 1)$ gives $N = 7$, the most common pattern in macro-cellular networks.

**(b) [7 Marks] A cellular system has a total of $S = 1000$ duplex channels. If the cluster size is $N = 7$ and each cell has radius $R = 4$ km, find (i) channels per cell, (ii) co-channel reuse ratio, (iii) reuse distance, and (iv) total system capacity if the service area is $4000 \text{ km}^2$.**

**Model Solution :**

**(i) Channels per cell :**

$$k = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{1000}{7} \approx 143 \text{ channels/cell}$$

**(ii) Co-channel reuse ratio :**

$$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$$

**(iii) Reuse distance :**

$$D = Q \times R = 4.58 \times 4 = 18.33 \text{ km}$$

**(iv) Total System Capacity :**
Area of one cell :

$$A_{cell} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\, R^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 16 = 24\sqrt{3} \approx 41.57\, \text{km}^2$$

Total cells :

$$\text{Total cells} = \frac{4000}{41.57} \approx 96.2 \approx 96 \text{ cells}$$

Number of clusters :

$$M = \frac{96}{7} \approx 13.71 \approx 13 \text{ clusters}$$

Total system capacity :

$$C = M \times S = 13 \times 1000 = 13{,}000 \text{ channels}$$

(Or full capacity, if no rounding applied : $C = 96 \times 143 = 13{,}728$ channels)

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — CHOICE 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks] With neat derivations, explain why a regular hexagonal cell shape is chosen over square or triangular cell shapes in cellular network design.**

**Model Solution :**

**Step 1 : Tessellation Requirement**
A cellular layout requires non-overlapping, gap-free coverage. Only three regular polygons tessellate : equilateral triangle, square, regular hexagon.

**Step 2 : Area Comparison**
For a fixed cell radius $R$ (largest inscribed circle in the polygon) :

| Shape | Area |
|---|---|
| Equilateral Triangle | $A_T = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4}\, R^2 \approx 1.30\, R^2$ |
| Square | $A_S = 4\, R^2$ |
| **Regular Hexagon** | $A_H = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\, R^2 \approx 2.60\, R^2$ |

The hexagon provides **maximum coverage area** per cell.

**Step 3 : Approximation to Circular Coverage**
Antennas radiate approximately circular patterns. Hexagon is the **closest regular polygon** that approximates a circle, minimizing the overlap or gap areas at the boundaries.

**Step 4 : Fewer Handovers**
Larger cells = fewer handovers for moving users = better Quality of Service.

**Conclusion** : Regular hexagon is the **optimal cell geometry** for cellular networks.

**(b) [7 Marks] A hexagonal cellular system uses cluster size $N = 4$. Each cell has radius $R = 2$ km. Calculate (i) the co-channel reuse distance $D$, (ii) the distance between centers of adjacent cells, (iii) the area of one cell, and (iv) verify whether $(i, j) = (2, 0)$ produces this $N$.**

**Model Solution :**

**(i) Co-channel reuse distance :**

$$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 4} = \sqrt{12} = 2\sqrt{3} \approx 3.464$$

$$D = Q \times R = 3.464 \times 2 = 6.928 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Distance between adjacent cell centers :**

$$d = \sqrt{3}\, R = \sqrt{3} \times 2 = 3.464 \text{ km}$$

**(iii) Area of one cell :**

$$A_{cell} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\, R^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 4 = 6\sqrt{3} \approx 10.39\, \text{km}^2$$

**(iv) Verification of $(i, j) = (2, 0)$ :**

$$N = i^2 + ij + j^2 = 2^2 + (2)(0) + 0^2 = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 \checkmark$$

Verified. Hence $(i, j) = (2, 0)$ produces $N = 4$.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS (Read Before Writing Exam)**
> 1. **Formula Mix-Up** : Many students write $Q = 3N$ or $Q = \sqrt{N}$ instead of $Q = \sqrt{3N}$. The cube-root-free form $\sqrt{3N}$ is the ONLY correct one. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Cluster Formula Error** : Writing $N = i^2 + j^2$ instead of $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ loses full marks on derivation. **(−3 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting $a = R$** : The side length $a$ and cell radius $R$ are equal in standard hexagonal model. Don't substitute them incorrectly in area formula. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Units Missing** : Always quote numerical answers with units (km, channels, $\text{km}^2$). **(−0.5 mark per numerical)**
> 5. **No Diagram** : For "describe the hexagonal cluster for $N = 7$" questions, **always include a sketch** of the 7-cell cluster with co-channel cells marked. **(−2 marks if omitted)**
> 6. **Cell Radius Definition Confusion** : In hexagonal cells, $R$ = corner-to-center distance (NOT center-to-side). The center-to-side distance is the apothem $r = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}R$.

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Hexagon** is preferred over square/triangle because it gives **maximum area** ($\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}R^2$) with **no overlap/gap** and **circular approximation**.
- [ ] **Cluster size** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ where $i, j$ are non-negative integers with $i \geq j$.
- [ ] **Co-channel reuse ratio** $Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ — this is THE most important formula.
- [ ] **Common cluster sizes** : $N = 3$ ($i=1,j=1$), $N = 4$ ($i=2,j=0$), $N = 7$ ($i=2,j=1$), $N = 12$ ($i=2,j=2$), $N = 9$ ($i=3,j=0$).
- [ ] **Channels per cell** $k = S/N$ — smaller $N$ = more channels per cell = higher capacity.
- [ ] **Reuse distance** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ — larger $N$ = larger $D$ = less interference.
- [ ] **Adjacent cell center distance** $d = \sqrt{3}\,R$ (NOT $2R$ as in circles).
- [ ] **Hexagon has 6 sides**, 6 vertices, 6 equilateral triangles, each interior angle $120^\circ$.
- [ ] **Apothem** $r = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}R$ — perpendicular distance from center to any side.
- [ ] **Total system capacity** $C = M \times S$ where $M$ = number of clusters = (Total Area) / ($N \times A_{cell}$).
- [ ] **Capacity vs. Quality Trade-off** : Smaller $N$ → more capacity but worse interference; Larger $N$ → less interference but fewer channels per cell.
- [ ] **Most commonly used $N$ in real-world networks** : $N = 7$ for macro-cellular, $N = 4$ for small/micro cells.
- [ ] The path loss exponent $n$ in $S/I$ formula is typically **3 to 4** for urban environments.
- [ ] Always label the **co-channel cells** by enclosing them with dashed lines and marking them as "Cluster A", "Cluster B", etc., in diagrams.
- [ ] In KTU 2024 ESE, expect at least **one 14-mark derivation question** on $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ and **one numerical** involving $N$, $R$, $D$, $k$ combined.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Channel Assignment Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Channel Assignment Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Channel Assignment Strategies** are systematic algorithms and rules used by the Mobile Switching Center (MSC) and Base Station Controller (BSC) of a cellular network to allocate the limited pool of radio channels (frequencies, time-slots, spreading codes, or any combination thereof) to cells, sectors, or individual mobile users, such that the *Frequency Reuse Constraint*, the *Co-Channel Interference (CCI) Constraint*, and the *Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI) Constraint* are simultaneously satisfied, while maximizing spectral efficiency and minimizing the *Grade of Service (GoS)* — the probability of call blocking.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the problem is referred to as the **Channel Assignment Problem (CAP)**, an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to be able to: (i) classify the three principal strategies, (ii) formulate the CAP mathematically, and (iii) compute the blocking probability using the Erlang-B formula for a given traffic load.

### 1.2 The Three Principal Strategies (Pillar Classification)

| # | Strategy | Acronym | Core Philosophy |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Fixed Channel Assignment | **FCA** | Each cell permanently owns a static subset of channels |
| 2 | Dynamic Channel Assignment | **DCA** | Channels are assigned *on-the-fly* per call from a central pool |
| 3 | Hybrid Channel Assignment | **HCA** | A *fixed* subset is reserved for baseline traffic, *dynamic* pool handles bursts |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Room Problem"

Imagine a city with **5 hotels** (cells) along a single highway, and only **40 identical rooms** (channels) exist in the entire city.

- **FCA** = Each hotel permanently owns 8 rooms. If hotel-3 is full but hotel-1 is empty, the system still **rejects** the guest. (Rigid, simple, predictable.)
- **DCA** = All 40 rooms are pooled at a *central reception*. A guest asking hotel-3 may be allocated a room physically located in hotel-1, provided taxi service exists. (Flexible, but logistics overhead is high.)
- **HCA** = Each hotel permanently owns 5 rooms (for its *expected* baseline), but the remaining **15 rooms** float in a city-pool for *peak-hour surges*. (Best of both worlds.)

> [!NOTE]
> The "rooms" represent radio channels. The "guests" represent voice/data call requests. The "taxi service" represents the signal-strength cost of using a channel that is physically distant from the requesting cell — a key reason DCA is not *always* superior to FCA.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Constants to Remember

- **Minimum Cluster Size**: $N_{\min} = 3$ (for hexagonal cells, the absolute theoretical minimum to avoid CCI).
- **Typical Cluster Size**: $N = 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21$ (used in practical 4G/5G frequency reuse patterns).
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio**: $q = D / R$, where $D$ is the *co-channel reuse distance* and $R$ is the *cell radius*. For hexagonal geometry, $q = \sqrt{3N}$.
- **SIR Threshold for Voice**: $\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\min} = 18 \text{ dB}$ (standard for acceptable narrowband voice quality).
- **Path-Loss Exponent**: $n = 2$ (free space) to $n = 4$ (typical urban macro-cell).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed Channel Assignment (FCA) — The "Static" Strategy

In pure FCA, the $C$ total system channels are divided equally into $N$ disjoint groups, where $N$ is the *cluster size* (the number of cells forming one complete frequency-reuse pattern). Cell $k$ permanently owns $C_k = C / N$ channels.

**Why it works:** Because two cells separated by the *reuse distance* $D = qR$ use disjoint channel sets, co-channel interference is, by construction, bounded.

**Why it fails:** Cellular traffic is *spatially non-uniform*. A business district at 5 PM may require 100 channels, while a residential cell at the same instant needs only 5. Pure FCA cannot re-route the surplus 95 channels — they sit idle.

#### 2.1.1 FCA Borrowing Strategies (Advanced Variants)

To overcome FCA's rigidity, *channel borrowing* schemes are employed:

1. **Simple Borrowing (SB):** A congested cell (*acceptor*) borrows a free channel from a *rich* neighboring cell (*donor*), provided the borrowed channel does not cause co-channel interference with the donor's own co-channel cells. The borrow is *locked* — only the acceptor uses it.
2. **Borrowing from the Richest (SBR):** A refinement that always selects the donor with the maximum number of free channels, balancing the network.
3. **Borrowing with Reassignment (BDI - Borrowing with Directed Inference):** A more aggressive scheme that *rearranges* the channel assignment of the donor cell to free a contiguous block for the acceptor.
4. **Channel Reuse Partitioning (CRP):** Each cell is divided into concentric sub-cells (e.g., *inner*, *outer*). Inner sub-cells use a smaller cluster size (more aggressive reuse), while outer sub-cells use a larger cluster size. This exploits the fact that inner-tier users can tolerate higher CCI.

### 2.2 Dynamic Channel Assignment (DCA) — The "Just-in-Time" Strategy

All $C$ channels are kept in a *central pool* (or a *distributed pool* replicated across MSCs). At call-setup time, the MSC picks the *best* available channel that satisfies:
- **Co-channel Constraint:** The channel is not in use in any of the requesting cell's *co-channel cells* (those at distance $D$).
- **Co-site Constraint:** If the channel is in use in the *same* cell, it cannot be reused (this is the *trunking efficiency* concept).
- **Adjacent-Channel Constraint:** The channel's frequency must be separated by at least a *guard band* from any channel already assigned in the same cell.

#### 2.2.1 Variants of DCA

- **Centralized DCA:** A single MSC controller makes all allocation decisions. Optimal but suffers from a *single-point-of-failure* and high signaling load.
- **Distributed DCA:** Each BSC decides locally. More robust, lower latency, but globally suboptimal.
- **Non-Centralized DCA:** Each cell autonomously picks based on local sensing. Cheapest, but suffers from *hidden-terminal* style conflicts.

### 2.3 Hybrid Channel Assignment (HCA)

A *fixed* portion (typically 60–80%) of the nominal cell capacity is permanently assigned. The remaining 20–40% is held in a dynamic pool, accessible to any cell on demand. This balances the *predictability* of FCA with the *flexibility* of DCA, and is the de-facto choice in modern 4G/5G systems.

### 2.4 The Erlang-B Formula — Computing the GoS

The **Grade of Service (GoS)** is defined as the probability that a newly arriving call is *blocked* because no channel is available. For an *M/M/C/C* queue (Poisson arrivals, exponential service, $C$ servers, no waiting room), the steady-state blocking probability is the **Erlang-B formula**:

$$B(C, A) = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

where:
- $C$ = number of channels per cell (the *trunk* size).
- $A$ = offered traffic in **Erlangs** = $\lambda / \mu$ (call-arrival rate divided by mean service-completion rate).
- $B(C, A)$ = blocking probability = GoS.

> [!NOTE]
> One **Erlang** equals one continuously occupied channel for 1 hour. A traffic load of $A = 84$ Erlangs with $C = 90$ channels gives a GoS of approximately **2%** (a typical engineering target).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Cluster size – shift parameters | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | $i, j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$; gives $N \in \{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, ...\}$ |
| 2 | Co-channel reuse ratio | $q = D / R = \sqrt{3N}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Worst-case SIR (6 interferers) | $\frac{S}{I} = \frac{q^n}{6}$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent |
| 4 | Worst-case SIR (in dB) | $\frac{S}{I}\vert_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{q^n}{6}\right)$ | Use $\log$ base 10, not natural |
| 5 | Channels per cell (FCA) | $C_{\text{cell}} = C_{\text{total}} / N$ | Integer division; $N$ must divide $C_{\text{total}}$ |
| 6 | Offered traffic | $A = \lambda \cdot \bar{T}$ | Erlangs; $\lambda$ in calls/hr, $\bar{T}$ in hours |
| 7 | Erlang-B blocking probability | $B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$ | Recursive form: $B(0,A)=1$, $B(C,A)=\dfrac{A \cdot B(C-1,A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1,A)}$ |
| 8 | Cell capacity (users/cell) | $U = C_{\text{cell}} \cdot \left(1 - B(C, A)\right)$ | Average carried users per cell |
| 9 | Reuse distance | $D = R \sqrt{3N}$ | Meters |
| 10 | Maximum cells in a system | $M = C_{\text{total}} / N$ | For a service area of $M$ cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The choice of $N$ is the most fundamental trade-off in cellular design. Larger $N$ → lower CCI, better voice quality, but fewer channels per cell → higher blocking. Smaller $N$ → more channels per cell → lower blocking, but higher CCI. Modern systems (LTE, 5G NR) use $N = 1$ *Universal Frequency Reuse* and instead manage CCI via OFDMA scheduling and ICIC (Inter-Cell Interference Coordination), but the *principle* taught in your KTU syllabus is still the foundation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cluster Size from Shift Parameters

**Problem:** Given two integers $i$ and $j$ representing the regular *shifts* of the frequency-reuse pattern along the two axes of the hexagonal lattice, derive the formula $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Place the reference cell at the origin of a hexagonal coordinate system. The two basis vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ of the hexagonal lattice have length $R\sqrt{3}$ (the distance between centers of adjacent hexagons) and enclose an angle of $60^\circ$.

**Step 2 — Express the shift to a co-channel cell.**
To find the position of a co-channel cell, the reuse pattern is *shifted* by $i$ cells along $\vec{a}$ and $j$ cells along $\vec{b}$. The displacement vector to the co-channel cell is:

$$\vec{D} = i \cdot \vec{a} + j \cdot \vec{b}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude squared.**
Using $|\vec{a}|^2 = |\vec{b}|^2 = 3R^2$ and the dot product $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 3R^2 \cos(60^\circ) = \frac{3R^2}{2}$:

$$D^2 = \left(i \vec{a} + j \vec{b}\right) \cdot \left(i \vec{a} + j \vec{b}\right)$$

$$D^2 = i^2 \vert \vec{a} \vert^2 + 2ij (\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}) + j^2 \vert \vec{b} \vert^2$$

$$D^2 = i^2 (3R^2) + 2ij \left(\frac{3R^2}{2}\right) + j^2 (3R^2)$$

$$D^2 = 3R^2 \left(i^2 + ij + j^2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the definition of $N$.**
By definition, $D^2 = 3N R^2$. Equating with the expression derived in Step 3:

$$3N R^2 = 3R^2 \left(i^2 + ij + j^2\right)$$

$$\boxed{N = i^2 + ij + j^2}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of valid $N$ values.**
For $i, j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$:
- $(0,1) \Rightarrow N = 1$ *(degenerate; same cell)*
- $(1,1) \Rightarrow N = 3$ *(minimum non-trivial)*
- $(2,0) \Rightarrow N = 4$
- $(2,1) \Rightarrow N = 7$ *(most commonly used in textbook examples)*
- $(3,0) \Rightarrow N = 9$
- $(2,2) \Rightarrow N = 12$
- $(3,1) \Rightarrow N = 13$
- $(4,0) \Rightarrow N = 16$

### 3.2 Derivation: Worst-Case Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)

**Problem:** A mobile is located at the *corner* of a hexagonal cell (worst-case geometry, distance $d_0 = R$ from the serving base station). There are exactly **6 co-channel base stations** in the first tier, each at the minimum co-channel distance $D_i = D = qR$. Derive the SIR for an arbitrary path-loss exponent $n$.

**Step 1 — Received power from the serving BS.**
Using the generalized path-loss model $P_r \propto d^{-n}$:

$$S = P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}$$

where $K$ is a constant collecting transmit power, antenna gains, and frequency.

**Step 2 — Total interference power from 6 co-channel BSs.**
By symmetry, all 6 first-tier interferers are at distance $D = qR$ from the mobile:

$$I = \sum_{k=1}^{6} P_t \cdot K \cdot D_k^{-n} = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot (qR)^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Form the SIR ratio.**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}}{6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot (qR)^{-n}} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot q^{-n} R^{-n}} = \frac{q^n}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $q = \sqrt{3N}$.**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels.**

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check for the KTU-default case $N=7$, $n=4$.**

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(21)^{2}}{6}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{441}{6}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(73.5)$$

$$\boxed{\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{N=7,\, n=4} \approx 18.66 \text{ dB}}$$

This is **marginally above** the **18 dB** threshold for acceptable voice — a classic exam result that demonstrates *why* $N=7$ is the textbook default for omnidirectional antennas.

### 3.3 Derivation: Blocking Probability via Recursive Erlang-B

**Problem:** For $C = 90$ channels per cell and offered traffic $A = 84$ Erlangs, compute the blocking probability.

**Step 1 — Use the recursive form** to avoid computing the factorials directly:

$$B(0, A) = 1$$
$$B(C, A) = \frac{A \cdot B(C-1, A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1, A)}$$

**Step 2 — Iterate from $C=1$ to $C=90$** (a step-by-step partial table is shown below for the first few iterations, and the final value is read from a standard Erlang-B table):

| $C$ | $B(C, 84)$ |
|----:|-----------:|
| 1 | $84 / 85 \approx 0.9882$ |
| 2 | $(84 \cdot 0.9882) / (2 + 84 \cdot 0.9882) \approx 0.9766$ |
| 3 | $\approx 0.9650$ |
| ... | ... |
| 84 | $\approx 0.0612$ |
| 85 | $\approx 0.0573$ |
| 86 | $\approx 0.0537$ |
| 87 | $\approx 0.0502$ |
| 88 | $\approx 0.0470$ |
| 89 | $\approx 0.0439$ |
| 90 | $\approx 0.0410$ |

$$\boxed{B(90, 84) \approx 0.0410 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{GoS} \approx 4.1\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Engineering interpretation: Roughly 4 out of every 100 arriving calls will be *blocked* at peak hour. To achieve the industry-standard 2% GoS, the operator must either (a) add more channels (e.g., micro-cells), (b) reduce cluster size, or (c) deploy **HCA** to dynamically load-balance.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: FCA vs DCA vs HCA

| Criterion | FCA | DCA | HCA |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Complexity | **Low** | **High** | **Medium** |
| Adaptability to traffic | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Signaling load | Low | High | Medium |
| Robustness to failure | High (no central point) | Low (centralized) | Medium |
| Blocking performance (uniform traffic) | Matches theory | Slightly better | Matches DCA |
| Blocking performance (non-uniform traffic) | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| Latency at call-setup | None | High (MSC query) | Low |
| Used in 1G/2G (AMPS, GSM) | Yes | Rare | Yes (with borrowing) |
| Used in modern 4G/5G | Conceptually adapted as "frequency reuse-1 + scheduling" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Channel Assignment Strategies

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Channel Assignment Strategies] --> subB[Fixed Channel Assignment FCA]
    rootA --> subC[Dynamic Channel Assignment DCA]
    rootA --> subD[Hybrid Channel Assignment HCA]

    subB --> leafB1[Pure FCA Static partition]
    subB --> leafB2[Borrowing SB SBR]
    subB --> leafB3[Reuse Partitioning CRP]
    subB --> leafB4[Channel Segregation]

    subC --> leafC1[Centralized DCA MSC controlled]
    subC --> leafC2[Distributed DCA BSC local]
    subC --> leafC3[Non Centralized DCA Autonomous]
    leafC1 --> leafC1a[First Available FA]
    leafC1 --> leafC1b[Locally Optimized]
    leafC1 --> leafC1c[Measurement Based]

    subD --> leafD1[Fixed portion 70 percent of C]
    subD --> leafD2[Dynamic pool 30 percent of C]
    subD --> leafD3[Triggered Borrowing]
```

### 4.2 Interference Geometry — Mobile at Hexagonal Cell Corner

```mermaid
graph LR
    bssrv[Serving Base Station at distance R] -->|Signal S equals R power n| mobms[Mobile Station at cell corner]
    bsc1[Co channel BS tier 1] -->|Interference I1 equals D power n| mobms
    bsc2[Co channel BS tier 2] -->|Interference I2 equals D power n| mobms
    bsc3[Co channel BS tier 3] -->|Interference I3 equals D power n| mobms
    bsc4[Co channel BS tier 4] -->|Interference I4 equals D power n| mobms
    bsc5[Co channel BS tier 5] -->|Interference I5 equals D power n| mobms
    bsc6[Co channel BS tier 6] -->|Interference I6 equals D power n| mobms
    mobms --> calc[SIR equals S over sum I1 to I6 equals q power n over 6]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dynamic Channel Assignment (DCA) System

```mermaid
graph TD
    msreq[Mobile Call Request] --> bsc1[BSC Channel Request Handler]
    bsc1 --> msc1[MSC Central Channel Allocator]
    msc1 --> dblookup[Central Channel Pool Database]
    dblookup --> chstatus[Check Co channel Constraint]
    chstatus --> chadj[Check Adjacent Channel Constraint]
    chadj --> chcosite[Check Co site Constraint]
    chcosite --> decision{All Constraints Satisfied}
    decision -->|Yes| assign[Allocate Best Match Channel]
    decision -->|No| reject[Return Blocking Signal GoS]
    assign --> handover[Update Pool Database]
    reject --> retry[Mobile may retry after timer]
```

### 4.4 Flow-Chart: Decision Logic for Choosing a Strategy in a Real Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    startstep[Network Planning Phase] --> q1[Is traffic spatially uniform?]
    q1 -->|Yes| pickFCA[Choose FCA with N chosen for SIR margin]
    q1 -->|No| q2[Is signaling infrastructure robust?]
    q2 -->|Yes| pickDCA[Choose Centralized DCA]
    q2 -->|No| q3[Is bursty peak to mean ratio high?]
    q3 -->|Yes| pickHCA[Choose HCA 70 percent fixed 30 percent dynamic]
    q3 -->|No| pickFBorrow[Choose FCA with Borrowing Strategy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between Fixed Channel Assignment (FCA) and Dynamic Channel Assignment (DCA).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FCA | DCA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Channel ownership | Permanent, per cell | Temporary, allocated per call from central pool |
| Adaptability | Low; cannot handle non-uniform traffic | High; tracks traffic variations in real-time |
| Complexity | Simple to implement | High; requires central controller and signaling |
| Blocking under uniform load | Acceptable | Slightly better |
| Blocking under non-uniform load | Poor (channels sit idle in low-traffic cells) | Excellent (pool re-distributes on demand) |

(1 mark for the *ownership* definition, 1 mark for adaptability contrast, 1 mark for complexity/GoS trade-off.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Erlang-B formula. What do the symbols represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Erlang-B formula computes the probability of call blocking in a trunked system with no queuing:

$$B(C, A) = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

- $C$ = total number of channels (trunk size) — **1 mark**
- $A$ = offered traffic in Erlangs ($A = \lambda \bar{T}$) — **1 mark**
- $B(C, A)$ = blocking probability = Grade of Service (GoS) — **1 mark**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Explain with neat diagrams the Fixed Channel Assignment (FCA) scheme and its borrowing variants. State the conditions under which channel borrowing is permitted.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Pure FCA (2 marks):**
In pure FCA, the $C$ available channels are partitioned into $N$ disjoint sets of $C/N$ channels each, where $N$ is the cluster size. Cell $k$ permanently owns the set $S_k$, and the constraint $S_k \cap S_\ell = \emptyset$ for all co-channel cells $k, \ell$ guarantees zero CCI by construction. The advantage is *simplicity* and *determinism*; the disadvantage is *channel wastage* when traffic is non-uniform.

**2. Simple Borrowing (2 marks):**
In the *Simple Borrowing (SB)* scheme, a *congested* cell (acceptor) may borrow one free channel from a *neighboring rich* cell (donor), subject to two conditions:
- The borrowed channel must not interfere with any of the donor's own co-channel cells.
- The borrow is *locked* — only the acceptor uses the borrowed channel until the call terminates.

**3. Borrowing from the Richest (1 mark):**
The acceptor picks the donor with the *most* free channels, balancing the network. Reduces the chance of *secondary* borrowing in subsequent calls.

**4. Channel Reuse Partitioning — CRP (1 mark):**
Each cell is conceptually split into an *inner* zone and an *outer* zone. Inner-zone users are closer to the BS and can tolerate higher interference; they are assigned channels from a smaller cluster (e.g., $N=3$). Outer-zone users get channels from a larger cluster (e.g., $N=9$). This increases the *effective* capacity without violating SIR.

**5. Conditions for Borrowing (1 mark):**
- Donor must have $\geq 1$ free channel.
- Borrowed channel's frequency must not be active in any of the donor's 6 co-channel cells.
- Adjacent-channel separation must be maintained in the acceptor cell.

**(b) [7 marks]**
*A cellular system uses a total of $C = 395$ channels, with cluster size $N = 7$. The system serves 49 cells. If the offered traffic per cell is $A = 80$ Erlangs, compute (i) the number of channels per cell, (ii) the maximum number of simultaneous users per cell, and (iii) the Grade of Service (GoS) using the Erlang-B formula. Use the Erlang-B table as needed.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Channels per cell (2 marks):**

$$C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{395}{7} \approx 56.43$$

Since the number of channels must be an integer, we take the floor: $C_{\text{cell}} = 56$ channels per cell (the remaining 3 channels are typically held in reserve or assigned to a control sub-system). For exam purposes, $\boxed{C_{\text{cell}} = 56}$ — though some textbooks round to 56.43 if they assume fractional channel sharing.

**(ii) Maximum simultaneous users (2 marks):**
If each user occupies exactly one channel, the *physical* maximum is $C_{\text{cell}} = 56$ users per cell. The *statistical* average (carried traffic) is computed in part (iii).

**(iii) Grade of Service (3 marks):**
Using the Erlang-B formula with $C = 56$ and $A = 80$ Erlangs:

$$B(56, 80) \approx 0.058 \;\; \text{(from Erlang-B table)}$$

Therefore, $\boxed{\text{GoS} \approx 5.8\%}$. This means about 6 out of every 100 calls will be blocked at peak hour — a relatively high blocking rate, indicating the operator should consider adding capacity (e.g., deploying micro-cells or migrating to HCA).

**Verification step:**
Check that the offered traffic is *above* the system capacity: $A = 80 > C = 56$, so the blocking probability must be substantial — this is consistent with the calculated 5.8%.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Derive the Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR) for a hexagonal cellular system assuming six first-tier co-channel interferers. For $N = 7$ and a path-loss exponent $n = 4$, show that the SIR exceeds the 18 dB voice-quality threshold.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the geometry (1 mark):**
Consider a mobile located at the corner of the hexagonal cell, at distance $d_0 = R$ from the serving base station. There are 6 co-channel base stations in the first tier, each at a distance $D_i = D = qR$ from the mobile.

**Step 2 — Received signal power (1 mark):**
Using the path-loss model $P_r(d) = P_t K d^{-n}$:

$$S = P_t K R^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Total interference from 6 co-channel cells (1 mark):**

$$I = \sum_{k=1}^{6} P_t K D_k^{-n} = 6 \cdot P_t K (qR)^{-n}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SIR (1 mark):**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 (qR)^{-n}} = \frac{q^n}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $q = \sqrt{3N}$ (1 mark):**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution (2 marks):**
For $N = 7$ and $n = 4$:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{4/2}}{6} = \frac{21^2}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5$$

In decibels:

$$\left.\frac{S}{I}\right|_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 7 — Threshold check (1 mark):**
Since $18.66 \text{ dB} > 18 \text{ dB}$, the SIR exceeds the voice-quality threshold by 0.66 dB (a *margin* of about 9% in linear power). This confirms that $N=7$ is a valid choice for a voice-grade omnidirectional cellular system with $n=4$ propagation.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**
*Compare the three channel assignment strategies — FCA, DCA, and HCA — in a tabular form. Under what specific real-world conditions would you recommend HCA over pure DCA?*

**Model Solution:**

**Tabular comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | FCA | DCA | HCA |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Channel-to-cell mapping | Static, permanent | Dynamic per call | Mixed: 70% static, 30% dynamic |
| Suitability for uniform traffic | High | High | High |
| Suitability for non-uniform traffic | Low | Very High | High |
| Computational complexity | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Signaling load at call-setup | None | High (MSC query) | Medium (only for the 30% pool) |
| Resilience to MSC failure | High (no central control) | Low (central point of failure) | Medium |
| Blocking probability (uniform) | $B(C/N, A)$ | Slightly better | Close to DCA |
| Real-world deployment | 1G AMPS | Research, military | 2G GSM, 3G, modern LTE/5G (adapted) |

**Conditions favoring HCA over pure DCA (3 marks):**

1. **Signaling infrastructure is moderately reliable but not pristine.** Pure DCA needs a robust, low-latency MSC-to-BSC link at every call. If the link has even a 1% failure rate, blocked-call rate spikes. HCA's 70% fixed portion can serve most calls *locally*, avoiding the fragile central path.
2. **Traffic is bimodal: a steady baseline with predictable peaks.** Pure DCA over-engineers the baseline case. HCA reserves a dynamic pool *exactly* sized for the peak, saving 20–30% of the central-controller processing load.
3. **Operator mandates carrier-grade reliability (5-nines / 99.999%).** Pure DCA's central controller is a *single point of failure*; HCA's distributed fixed portion provides a *graceful degradation* fallback. In 2G/3G, this was the deciding factor for global operators.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Channel Assignment Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the $D^2 = 3N R^2$ relation** when computing SIR in dB. Many students write $\frac{S}{I} = 10 \log_{10}(q^n / 6)$ but use $q = N$ (a common slip). Always expand $q = \sqrt{3N}$ *before* substituting.
> 2. **Confusing the units of Erlangs.** $A = \lambda \bar{T}$ requires *consistent* units. If $\lambda$ is in calls/minute, then $\bar{T}$ must also be in minutes. A frequent 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Miscounting the co-channel cells.** A first-tier hexagonal cell has **6** co-channel interferers, not 4 or 12. The number 6 is geometric, not a free parameter.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert SIR to dB** in the final answer when the question asks for a comparison against the 18 dB threshold. The threshold is in dB; the linear ratio *is not* directly comparable.
> 5. **Treating FCA and "frequency planning" as synonymous.** They are related but not identical. FCA is the *assignment*; frequency planning is the *engineering process* that determines $N$ and the cell layout.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Channel Assignment Strategies** solve the *Channel Assignment Problem (CAP)*: allocate limited radio resources to satisfy traffic demand while keeping interference below threshold.
- **Three pillars:** **FCA** (static, simple, poor under non-uniform traffic), **DCA** (dynamic, flexible, complex), **HCA** (hybrid, the engineering sweet spot).
- **Key formula — cluster size:** $\boxed{N = i^2 + ij + j^2}$, only for non-negative integers $i, j$.
- **Key formula — reuse ratio:** $\boxed{q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}}$.
- **Key formula — worst-case SIR:** $\boxed{S/I = q^n / 6}$, convertible to dB via $10 \log_{10}$.
- **Key formula — Erlang-B:** $\boxed{B(C,A) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}}$ gives the GoS.
- **Voice SIR threshold:** **18 dB** — memorize this for the KTU exam.
- **Path-loss exponent:** Typically $n = 4$ for urban macro-cells unless otherwise stated.
- **FCA variants:** Simple Borrowing, Borrowing from Richest (SBR), Borrowing with Channel Reassignment, Channel Reuse Partitioning (CRP).
- **DCA variants:** Centralized (MSC), Distributed (BSC), Non-Centralized (autonomous).
- **Erlang unit:** 1 Erlang = 1 channel continuously busy for 1 hour. Always use *consistent time units*.
- **Recursion trick for Erlang-B:** $B(0,A) = 1$; $B(C,A) = \dfrac{A \cdot B(C-1,A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1,A)}$ — exam-friendly, no factorial computation.
- **Engineering mantra:** *Larger $N$* $\Rightarrow$ *better quality, worse capacity*. *Smaller $N$* $\Rightarrow$ *better capacity, worse quality*. The trade-off is the *heart* of cellular design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance to Frequency Reuse Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Distance to Frequency Reuse Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Distance to Frequency Reuse Ratio** (also called the **Co-channel Reuse Ratio**, denoted as $q$ or $Q$) is defined as the ratio of the **Co-channel Reuse Distance ($D$)** to the **Cell Radius ($R$)**.

$$q = \frac{D}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The co-channel reuse distance $D$ is the minimum distance between the centers of two cells that use the same set of frequencies. The ratio $q = D/R$ is a critical parameter that determines the system's ability to suppress **Co-Channel Interference (CCI)** while maximizing spectral efficiency.

**Key Terminology:**
- **Co-channel Cells:** Cells that share the same set of frequency channels.
- **Co-channel Reuse Distance ($D$):** The physical separation between the centers of two co-channel cells required to maintain acceptable interference levels.
- **Cell Radius ($R$):** The distance from the center of a hexagonal cell to any of its six vertices.
- **Cluster Size ($N$):** The number of cells in a frequency reuse cluster.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a School Exam Schedule:** 🎓
>
> Imagine your university conducts the **same exam paper** in multiple classrooms simultaneously (this represents **frequency reuse**). If two classrooms conducting the *same exam* are placed side-by-side, students might overhear answers from one room to the other — this is **co-channel interference**.
>
> To prevent this, the university must place these "same-exam" classrooms sufficiently far apart — this physical separation is the **Co-channel Reuse Distance ($D$)**.
>
> Now, the size of each classroom (representing the **Cell Radius $R$**) is fixed by the building. The ratio $q = D/R$ tells the university: *"For every unit of classroom size, how much separation do we need to keep the exam fair?"*
>
> A **larger $q$** means classrooms are spread out more (less interference, but fewer classrooms fit on campus). A **smaller $q$** means classrooms are packed tightly (more efficient, but higher cheating/interference risk).

This tradeoff is the heart of cellular frequency planning — engineers must balance **capacity** (fitting more users) against **quality** (minimizing interference).

---

## Physical Significance

The value of $q$ directly impacts:
1. **Signal to Co-channel Interference Ratio (SIR / C/I)** — higher $q$ improves SIR.
2. **Spectral Efficiency** — lower $q$ permits tighter frequency reuse.
3. **Cellular System Capacity** — smaller $q$ allows more clusters per coverage area.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Geometric Foundation: Hexagonal Cell Layout

In cellular systems, **regular hexagons** are used to model cell coverage because hexagons provide the closest approximation to a circular radiation pattern with no overlapping or gaps. Each hexagonal cell has **six neighboring cells** at distance $\sqrt{3} R$ from its center.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hexagons?**
> - Hexagons tessellate perfectly (no coverage holes).
> - They have a more uniform area-to-perimeter ratio than squares or triangles.
> - The geometry allows clean mathematical derivations of the reuse ratio.

---

## Core Theoretical Relationship

For a hexagonal cellular system, the **co-channel reuse distance $D$** is related to the **cluster size $N$** and **cell radius $R$** through the fundamental equation:

$$D = R\sqrt{3N}$$

Therefore, the **Distance to Frequency Reuse Ratio** becomes:

$$q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

This is the **most important KTU formula** for this topic.

---

## Cluster Size and Shift Parameters

The cluster size $N$ is determined by two **shift parameters** $i$ and $j$ (both non-negative integers, not both zero):

$$N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The shift parameters $(i, j)$ describe how to move from one co-channel cell to another:
> 1. Move $i$ cells along any chain of hexagons.
> 2. Turn 60° counter-clockwise (or clockwise).
> 3. Move $j$ cells along the new direction.
>
> The destination cell is a **co-channel cell** of the source.

### Valid Combinations of $(i, j)$ Producing Valid Cluster Sizes:

| Shift Parameters $(i, j)$ | Cluster Size $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | Common Designation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(1, 0)$ or $(0, 1)$ | **3** | Smallest feasible |
| $(1, 1)$ | **3** | Alternate path |
| $(2, 0)$ or $(0, 2)$ | **4** | Often used |
| $(2, 1)$ or $(1, 2)$ | **7** | Most widely used (classic) |
| $(3, 0)$ or $(0, 3)$ | **9** | |
| $(2, 2)$ | **12** | |
| $(3, 1)$ or $(1, 3)$ | **13** | |
| $(3, 2)$ or $(2, 3)$ | **19** | |
| $(4, 0)$ or $(0, 4)$ | **16** | |
| $(4, 1)$ or $(1, 4)$ | **21** | |

> [!NOTE]
> The cluster size $N$ **cannot** be 1, 2, 5, 6, 8, 10, 11, 14, 15, 17, 18, 20, etc. — these values are geometrically impossible to achieve with hexagonal cells.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Engineering Meaning** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Reuse Ratio | $q$ | $q = D/R$ | Dimensionless | Core metric of the topic |
| Reuse Ratio (Hexagonal) | $q$ | $q = \sqrt{3N}$ | Dimensionless | Most used KTU formula |
| Co-channel Distance | $D$ | $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ | km or m | Physical separation between co-channel cells |
| Cluster Size | $N$ | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | Integer (cells) | Number of cells per cluster |
| Adjacent Cell Center Distance | $d_{adj}$ | $d_{adj} = \sqrt{3} \cdot R$ | km or m | Center-to-center of neighboring cells |
| SIR (approximate, 1st tier) | $\frac{C}{I}$ | $\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^{n_{0}}}{i_{0} \cdot 6}$ | Ratio (dB) | Where $i_{0}$ = number of co-channel cells in first tier (typically 6) |
| Path Loss Exponent (general) | $n$ (or $n_{0}$) | — | Dimensionless | Typically 2 to 4 in mobile environments |

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical cellular deployment (4G LTE, 5G NR), the **reuse ratio $q$** is used to:
- **Plan frequency assignments** across the service area.
- **Predict Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)** for cell-edge users.
- **Optimize cluster size** to maximize system capacity for a given acceptable SIR (minimum 18 dB for voice, ~10–15 dB for LTE data).
- **Design handover boundaries** between sectors and cells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: The Relationship $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ for Hexagonal Cells

### Setup

Consider a hexagonal cell at the **origin (O)**. The six neighboring cells are at distance $\sqrt{3} R$ from O. A co-channel cell is reached by:
1. Translating $i$ cell-centers along one of the six directions (say, along the horizontal chain).
2. Rotating 60° counter-clockwise.
3. Translating $j$ cell-centers along the new direction.

Let us call the displacement vector from O to the co-channel cell as $\vec{D}$.

### Step 1: Define the Lattice Vectors

The center-to-center displacement between **adjacent hexagonal cells** can be expressed as two linearly independent lattice vectors:

$$
\vec{u} = \sqrt{3} R \cdot (1, 0)
$$

$$
\vec{v} = \sqrt{3} R \cdot \left(\cos 60^{\circ}, \sin 60^{\circ}\right) = \sqrt{3} R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
$$

> **Reasoning:** Each step moves us by the inter-cell center distance $\sqrt{3}R$ in the respective hexagonal direction.

### Step 2: Express the Co-channel Displacement Vector

The displacement to the co-channel cell located at shift parameters $(i, j)$ is:

$$
\vec{D} = i \cdot \vec{u} + j \cdot \vec{v}
$$

$$
\vec{D} = i \cdot \sqrt{3} R \cdot (1, 0) + j \cdot \sqrt{3} R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Components of $\vec{D}$

The x-component is:

$$
D_{x} = \sqrt{3} R \cdot i + \sqrt{3} R \cdot \frac{j}{2} = \sqrt{3} R \left(i + \frac{j}{2}\right)
$$

The y-component is:

$$
D_{y} = 0 + \sqrt{3} R \cdot \frac{j \sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{3Rj}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Magnitude $D^{2} = D_{x}^{2} + D_{y}^{2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{2} &= \left[\sqrt{3} R \left(i + \frac{j}{2}\right)\right]^{2} + \left[\frac{3Rj}{2}\right]^{2} \\
&= 3R^{2}\left(i + \frac{j}{2}\right)^{2} + \frac{9R^{2}j^{2}}{4} \\
&= 3R^{2}\left(i^{2} + ij + \frac{j^{2}}{4}\right) + \frac{9R^{2}j^{2}}{4} \\
&= 3R^{2} i^{2} + 3R^{2} ij + \frac{3R^{2} j^{2}}{4} + \frac{9R^{2} j^{2}}{4} \\
&= 3R^{2} i^{2} + 3R^{2} ij + 3R^{2} j^{2} \\
&= 3R^{2} \left(i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Combine the $j^{2}$ terms: $\frac{3}{4} + \frac{9}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3$. Factor out $3R^{2}$.

### Step 5: Substitute $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$

$$
D^{2} = 3 R^{2} N
$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$
D = R \sqrt{3N}
$$

### Step 6: Derive the Reuse Ratio

$$
q = \frac{D}{R} = \frac{R\sqrt{3N}}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $q = \sqrt{3N}$ — this is the central KTU formula derived from hexagonal geometry.

---

## Derivation 2: Cluster Size from Shift Parameters $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$

### Proof Using the Magnitude Equation

From the previous derivation, we established:

$$
D^{2} = 3R^{2} \left(i^{2} + ij + j^{2}\right)
$$

By the definition of the cluster size, the number of unique cells in a frequency-reuse cluster is:

$$
N = \frac{\text{Area of cluster in frequency-reuse lattice}}{\text{Area of one cell}}
$$

For hexagonal geometry, this evaluates to $i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$. Combining with the squared distance expression:

$$
N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}
$$

### Quick Verification

For $(i, j) = (2, 1)$:
- $N = (2)^{2} + (2)(1) + (1)^{2} = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$ ✓ (matches the well-known $N = 7$ cluster)
- $q = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$
- $D = R\sqrt{21} \approx 4.58 R$

For $(i, j) = (1, 0)$:
- $N = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1$ — **Not valid** (would mean same cell uses same frequency, infinite interference)
- Wait — actually $N=1$ IS allowed mathematically, but not practical. Let me re-verify: For $(i,j) = (1,0)$ or $(0,1)$, $N = 1$. This is theoretically the smallest cluster but is impractical because it provides zero isolation. The standard accepted smallest *practical* cluster sizes are $N=3, 4, 7, 9...$

---

## Derivation 3: Number of Co-channel Cells in the First Tier

The number of co-channel interfering cells in the first surrounding tier around a reference cell is **always 6** for a hexagonal cellular system, regardless of $N$.

**Reasoning:**
- The first tier of co-channel cells forms a ring around the reference cell.
- The number of cells at shift distance $D$ from a given cell is determined by counting unique $(i, j)$ pairs whose norm equals $\sqrt{N}$.
- For any valid $N$ achievable via $(i, j)$, there are exactly **6** co-channel cells in the first tier (related to the 6-fold symmetry of the hexagonal lattice).

This constant value of **6** is crucial for SIR calculations:

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{n_{0}} = \frac{q^{n_{0}}}{6} = \frac{(3N)^{n_{0}/2}}{6}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why SIR is independent of the cell layout orientation but depends strongly on $N$ and the path loss exponent $n_{0}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
