# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions are compulsory, answer in 3–4 sentences, each carrying **3 marks**.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define handoff. Why is it necessary in cellular systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Handoff (or handover)** is the process of transferring an ongoing call or data session of a Mobile Station (MS) from one Base Station (BS) to another **without interrupting communication**, as the MS moves across cell boundaries. It is necessary because (i) received signal strength decreases with distance due to path loss, causing the serving BS link to degrade; (ii) co-channel interference from neighbouring cells may degrade CIR below the minimum required for the modulation scheme; and (iii) the MS must always be served by the BS offering the **best downlink quality** to maintain target BER and QoS. **(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for the three reasons)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between hard handoff and soft handoff.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Hard Handoff | Soft Handoff |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Link status during HO | Old link released **before** new link is established (*break-before-make*) | Both old and new links active simultaneously (*make-before-break*) |
| Channel usage | 1 channel at any instant | 2 channels during HO |
| Used in | GSM, LTE | UMTS, CDMA2000 |
| Ping-pong susceptibility | Higher | Lower (macro-diversity) |
**(3 marks — tabular comparison + 1 line for each system example.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Module 2 internal choice → Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### ⭐ CHOICE 1 — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q-A(a) [7 Marks] Explain the different types of handoff strategies used in cellular systems. Compare hard, soft and softer handoff with suitable diagrams.

**Model Solution:**

A handoff (handover) is the mechanism that maintains an active call when the MS moves from one cell to another. The major classifications are:

**(i) Hard Handoff — "Break before Make"**
The old connection with the serving BS is broken *first*, and only then is the new connection with the target BS established. Used in **time-division systems like GSM** and in **LTE X2/S1 handover**. Advantage: simple, requires only one channel at any instant. Disadvantage: brief audible gap (≈ 100 ms in GSM) and risk of call drop if the new BS is unavailable.

**(ii) Soft Handoff — "Make before Break"**
The MS establishes a connection with the new BS *before* releasing the old one. For a short duration the MS communicates with **two BSs simultaneously**, using macro-diversity combining at the RNC. Used in **CDMA-based systems (UMTS, CDMA2000)**. Advantage: seamless, no audible gap. Disadvantage: doubled channel usage, complex signalling, more battery drain.

**(iii) Softer Handoff**
A special case of soft handoff where the MS connects to **two sectors of the same Node-B** on the **same frequency**. Combining is performed inside the Node-B itself (no backhaul to RNC required). Used in UMTS when the MS lies between two sectors of a tri-sectored site.

**Comparison Diagram:**
```
HARD SOFT                SOFT
  |    |                   |
  v    v                   v
t1   t2  t1   t2    t1   t2  t3    t1   t2   t3
[==A==]   [====A===========B=======]
         gap
         [==A==][==A===B===][==B==]
                   overlap
```
**(7 marks — 2 each for hard/soft, 1 for softer, 2 for diagram and example systems.)**

#### Q-A(b) [7 Marks] With neat block diagram explain the four RSS based handoff algorithms. State one advantage and disadvantage of each.

**Model Solution:**

Let $X_{\text{old}}$ = RSS of serving BS, $X_{\text{new}}$ = RSS of neighbouring BS, $T$ = absolute threshold, $H$ = hysteresis margin.

**Block Diagram of the Decision Engine:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RxOld["RSS_old sampler"] --> MUX{"Comparator MUX"}
    RxNew["RSS_new sampler"] --> MUX
    Ttbl["Threshold T table"] --> MUX
    Htbl["Hysteresis H table"] --> MUX
    MUX --> DEC["Decision Boolean"]
    DEC --> HO["HO Trigger Signal"]
```

**The Four Algorithms:**

| # | Algorithm | Decision Condition | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | RSS only | $X_{\text{new}} > X_{\text{old}}$ | Simplest; always picks strongest BS | Excessive ping-pong near boundary |
| 2 | RSS + Threshold | $X_{\text{new}} > X_{\text{old}}$ AND $X_{\text{new}} > T$ | Avoids handoff into weak cells | If both BSs below $T$, call may drop even when handoff would help |
| 3 | RSS + Hysteresis | $X_{\text{new}} > X_{\text{old}} + H$ | Suppresses ping-pong | Delays handoff for fast-moving MS → risk of dropped call |
| 4 | RSS + H + T | $X_{\text{new}} > X_{\text{old}} + H$ AND $X_{\text{new}} > T$ | Best of both worlds; lowest ping-pong AND low failure | Most complex; needs careful tuning of both $H$ and $T$ |

**Conclusion:** Algorithm 4 is the de-facto choice in modern systems (LTE, 5G NR), where $H \approx 3\text{ dB}$ and $T$ is set 5–6 dB above the receiver sensitivity.

**(7 marks — 1.5 for block diagram + 4 for table + 1.5 for conclusion with engineering numbers.)**

---

### ⭐ CHOICE 2 — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Q-B(a) [7 Marks] Describe the handoff process in detail. Explain the role of MAHO and NCHO with examples.

**Model Solution:**

The handoff process is executed in **four phases**:

**Phase 1 — Measurement:**
The MS continuously monitors the **downlink** beacon (BCCH in GSM, RS in LTE) of the serving BS and the surrounding neighbour BSs. Measurement metrics: RSSI, RSRP, RSRQ, SINR. The serving BS also measures the **uplink** RSS of the MS.

**Phase 2 — Decision:**
Based on the algorithm (Sec 2.3), the system decides whether a handoff is needed. The decision authority determines MAHO vs NCHO:
- **MAHO (Mobile-Assisted Handoff):** The **MS makes the measurements** and reports to the network; the **network (BSC/RNC/eNodeB) takes the final decision**. Used in GSM, UMTS, LTE.
- **NCHO (Network-Controlled Handoff):** The **network alone** makes both measurements and the decision. The MS is essentially passive. Used in 1G AMPS and early 2G systems.
- **MCHO (Mobile-Controlled Handoff):** The MS takes both measurement and decision; network just executes. Used in DECT, PACS, and 5G NR (with CHO enhancements).

**Phase 3 — Execution:**
Channel allocation, signalling exchange (`HANDOVER COMMAND`, `HANDOVER ACCESS`, `PHYSICAL CHANNEL RECONFIGURATION`). In LTE, X2-based handover takes < 50 ms.

**Phase 4 — Completion:**
Old channel released; location-area update propagated to VLR (2G/3G) or MME (LTE); billing/seamless continuity confirmed.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | NCHO | MAHO |
|--------|:----:|:----:|
| Decision authority | Network (BSC/RNC) | Network (eNodeB) |
| Measurement by | BS (uplink RSS) | MS (downlink) + BS (uplink) |
| Signalling load on network | High | Low |
| Speed of decision | Slow | Fast |
| Used in | AMPS, early GSM | GSM, UMTS, LTE, 5G |

**(7 marks — 2 for phases, 2 for MAHO/NCHO definition, 3 for comparison table.)**

#### Q-B(b) [7 Marks] A mobile is moving at 60 km/h in a cellular system with cell radius 2 km. Average call duration is 3 minutes. Calculate: (i) average cell dwell time, (ii) average number of handoffs per call, (iii) probability that cell dwell time is less than 20 seconds.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $v = 60\text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67\text{ m/s}$
- $R = 2\text{ km} = 2000\text{ m}$
- $T_{\text{call}} = 3\text{ min} = 180\text{ s}$
- $T_0 = 20\text{ s}$ for part (iii)

**Part (i) — Average cell dwell time:**

Using the hexagonal-cell result derived in Section 3.1:

$$\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{2.823\,R}{v} = \dfrac{2.823 \times 2000}{16.67} = \dfrac{5646}{16.67} \approx 338.7\text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{\tau} \approx 338.7\text{ s} \;\approx\; 5\text{ min }38\text{ s}}$$

**Part (ii) — Average number of handoffs:**

$$\bar{N}_h = \dfrac{T_{\text{call}}}{\bar{\tau}} = \dfrac{180}{338.7} \approx 0.531$$

$$\boxed{\bar{N}_h \approx 0.53\text{ handoffs per call}}$$

**Interpretation:** Less than one handoff per call on average — most calls terminate before the MS crosses a cell boundary.

**Part (iii) — Probability that $\tau < T_0 = 20\text{ s}$:**

Assuming exponential dwell-time distribution (Section 3.1, Step 6):

$$P(\tau < 20) = 1 - e^{-T_0 / \bar{\tau}} = 1 - e^{-20 / 338.7}$$

$$= 1 - e^{-0.05906} = 1 - 0.9426 = 0.0574$$

$$\boxed{P(\tau < 20\text{ s}) \approx 5.74\%}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Only ≈ 5.7 % of cell dwells are shorter than 20 s — these are the *high-velocity* scenarios where handoff algorithms must be tuned aggressively.

**(7 marks — 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 2 for part (iii), 1 for engineering interpretation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing MAHO and MCHO** — MAHO = *Mobile-Assists*, MCHO = *Mobile-Controls*. Many students write them as synonyms. ❌
> 2. **Forgetting the velocity unit conversion** (km/h → m/s) in numericals — instant 1–2 mark loss.
> 3. **Not mentioning "break-before-make" vs "make-before-break"** in Hard vs Soft comparison — these exact phrases are valued at 1 mark each by examiners.
> 4. **In Algorithm 4**, students often forget to write **both** conditions joined by AND. ❌
> 5. **Cell dwell time formula** — some textbooks use $2R/v$ (circular cell) and others $2.823 R/v$ (hexagonal). KTU expects the **hexagonal** answer; write the assumption explicitly to be safe.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Handoff** = transferring an active call between BSs without interruption.
- [x] Triggered by low RSS, low CIR, high BER, distance, or load balancing.
- [x] **Hard HO** = Break-before-make → GSM, LTE.
- [x] **Soft HO** = Make-before-break → UMTS, CDMA2000.
- [x] **Softer HO** = Two sectors of the *same* Node-B.
- [x] **MAHO** = MS measures, Network decides (GSM/LTE).
- [x] **NCHO** = Network both measures and decides (AMPS).
- [x] **MCHO** = MS both measures and decides (DECT, 5G NR).
- [x] **4 RSS Algorithms** — memorize conditions, pros, cons.
- [x] **Algorithm 4 (H + T)** is industry standard.
- [x] **Hysteresis $H$** reduces ping-pong; **Threshold $T$** prevents handover into weak cells.
- [x] Average dwell time in hexagon: $\bar{\tau} = 2.823 R / v$.
- [x] Average handoffs per call: $\bar{N}_h = T_{\text{call}} / \bar{\tau}$.
- [x] Dwell-time CDF: $P(\tau < T_0) = 1 - e^{-T_0/\bar{\tau}}$.
- [x] Ping-pong probability: $Q\!\left(\dfrac{H}{\sqrt{2}\sigma}\right)$.
- [x] 5G uses **Conditional Handover (CHO)** — direct descendant of Algorithm 4.
- [x] **Intra-cell handoff** = change channel within same cell (due to interference).
- [x] **Inter-cell handoff** = change BS = the standard meaning.
- [x] Handoff delay target: ≤ 100 ms (voice) / ≤ 50 ms (LTE/5G).
- [x] KPI trio: $P_f$, $P_{nu}$, $P_h$ — must be jointly optimized.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Umbrella Cell Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Umbrella Cell Concept # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition The **Umbrella Cell Concept** is a hierarchical cellular network design strategy in which a **large-radius macrocell** (the "umbrella cell") is intentionally overlaid on top of a cluster of **smaller, lower-power cells** (microcells, picocells, or picocells). The umbrella cell's primary role is to act as a **coverage safety net** — it absorbs traffic from fast-moving Mobile Stations (MS) and from areas where the smaller underlying cells cannot provide reliable service, thereby preventing dropped calls and avoiding the "ping-pong" handover effect at high speeds. In formal KTU/Rappaport terminology, an umbrella cell is a cell whose **Base Station Antenna (BSA) is mounted at an Elevated height, transmits at Higher Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP), and uses a wider beamwidth** compared to the inner-tier cells. It co-exists in the same geographical region but operates on a **different frequency channel** (or with a different cell ID) to avoid co-channel interference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Umbrella Cell Concept is a direct application of the **Hierarchical Cell Structure (HCS)** principle and is essential to solving the **High-Speed Handover Problem** in modern 2G/3G/4G/5G networks. Examiners love questions framed around the **"highway coverage"** and **"microcell-macrocell handover"** scenarios. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large shopping mall with many small shops** (the small cells/microcells). Inside the mall, customers move slowly between shops and are served by the shop's own Wi-Fi. Now imagine a **giant umbrella** (the umbrella cell) covering the *entire* mall parking lot and the road leading into the mall. - If a customer (the mobile user) is **walking slowly inside the mall**, the small shops handle them perfectly. - If the customer is **driving fast on the highway adjacent to the mall**, the small shops cannot keep up — the customer would be passed between too many small shop-Wi-Fi zones, causing a *ping-pong* of connections. The giant umbrella takes over, providing one stable, large coverage zone for the entire highway. **Another Real-World Analogy — The Mother Hen and Chicks:** Think of the umbrella cell as a **mother hen** standing tall over a group of **chicks** (the microcells). If a chick (a small cell) gets too weak or if a fast-moving object (a car) flies past, the mother hen (umbrella cell) immediately shelters it. The chicks handle the dense, slow-moving urban traffic; the mother hen handles sparse, fast-moving traffic. ## Key Physical / Engineering Metrics (KTU Standard Values) | Parameter | Umbrella Cell | Microcell (Underlying) | |---|---|---| | **Cell Radius** | **1 km to 35 km** (typically 5–20 km) | **100 m to 1 km** | | **Antenna Height (BSA)** | **> 30 m** (often 50–100 m on towers) | **< 15 m** (lamp-posts, rooftops) | | **Transmit Power** | **> 10 W (often 20–40 W)** | **0.1 W to 1 W** | | **EIRP** | High | Low | | **Beamwidth** | Wide (omni / sector 120°) | Narrow (often sectoral) | | **Handover Rate Burden** | Low (handles high-speed MS) | High (handles pedestrian MS) | > [!NOTE] > The umbrella cell is **NOT** a replacement for the microcells — it works *in parallel* with them. Both are active simultaneously, and the network decides which one serves the MS at any given instant based on **signal strength, MS speed, and traffic load**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## The Core Engineering "Why" — Three Driving Problems The umbrella cell concept was invented to solve **three specific, well-documented problems** in cellular network engineering: ### Problem 1: The High-Speed Handover (Ping-Pong) Problem When a mobile station (e.g., a car traveling at **120 km/h ≈ 33.33 m/s**) moves through a sequence of small microcells (each ~500 m radius), it crosses cell boundaries very frequently. A typical microcellular cluster on a highway would force a handover every **~15–30 seconds**. Each handover has a non-zero failure probability (due to signaling delay, interference, or measurement errors). The cumulative probability of call drop over a long journey becomes **unacceptably high**. > **Solution Provided by Umbrella Cell:** The umbrella cell covers the entire stretch of highway with one large cell. The MS remains in the same umbrella cell for the entire journey, drastically reducing the number of handovers. ### Problem 2: The Coverage Gap / Shadowing Problem In real urban environments, small microcells suffer from **deep fades and shadowing** caused by buildings, tunnels, and overpasses. The umbrella cell's higher antenna and higher power provide a **"macro-diversity"** layer that fills these gaps. ### Problem 3: Capacity Offloading During Congestion If a microcell site goes down (equipment failure, power loss) or becomes saturated (high traffic load), the umbrella cell can **absorb the overflow traffic** using its larger bandwidth pool and broader coverage footprint. ## Operational Hierarchy — How the Network Decides The Mobile Switching Center (MSC) / Base Station Controller (BSC) executes a **layered handover decision algorithm**: 1. **Layer 1 (Lowest Tier — Picocells):** Used for indoor office / hotspot. Range ~10 m. 2. **Layer 2 (Middle Tier — Microcells):** Used for urban street-level coverage. Range ~100 m – 1 km. 3. **Layer 3 (Top Tier — Umbrella / Macrocell):** Used for highways, rural areas, and as a fallback. Range ~1 km – 35 km. When the MS measures signal strength: - If it is **slow and indoor** → picocell handles it. - If it is **pedestrian or slow vehicular** → microcell handles it. - If it is **fast-moving (v > 60 km/h)** OR signal from microcell is weak → **umbrella cell takes over**. ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Maximum Cell Radius (R)** | $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C}{N_c \cdot \rho}}$ | Maximum radius supported for given traffic density. | | 2 | **Handover Rate (per MS)** | $H = \dfrac{v \cdot L}{\pi \cdot R}$ | Handoffs per second, where $v$ = MS velocity (m/s), $L$ = perimeter crossed, $R$ = cell radius. | | 3 | **Co-channel Reuse Ratio** | $Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | $D$ = distance to co-channel cell, $N$ = cluster size. | | 4 | **Path Loss (Okumura-Hata, Urban)** | $L_{p} = 69.55 + 26.16\log f_c - 13.82\log h_b - a(h_m) + (44.9 - 6.55\log h_b)\log d$ | $f_c$ in MHz, $h_b$ base antenna height (m), $d$ distance (km). | | 5 | **Receive Power (Friis / Two-Ray)** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^n$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2–4); $\lambda$ = wavelength. | | 6 | **Handover Reduction Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{H_{micro}}{H_{umbrella}} = \dfrac{R_{umbrella}}{R_{micro}}$ | Ratio showing how umbrella cell reduces handovers. | | 7 | **Crossover Hysteresis** | $\Delta = P_{threshold}^{micro} - P_{threshold}^{umbrella}$ | Margin (in **dB**) preventing rapid flip-flop between tiers. | | 8 | **Carrier-to-Interference Ratio (C/I)** | $\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{P_t \cdot R^{-n}}{\sum_{i=1}^{i_0} P_{ti} \cdot D_i^{-n}}$ | Minimum 18 dB for AMPS, 9–12 dB for GSM. | | 9 | **Spectral Efficiency (Erlangs/cell)** | $E = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A \cdot R^2}{N_c \cdot B}$ | Where $A$ = area, $B$ = bandwidth, $\rho$ = traffic density. | | 10 | **Doppler Shift (Velocity Dependence)** | $f_d = \dfrac{v}{\lambda} \cos\theta$ | Justifies the need for umbrella cells at high $v$. | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, examiners often provide the MS velocity in **km/h**. Always convert to **m/s** by dividing by **3.6** before plugging into Doppler / handover formulas. ## Real-World Engineering Utility - **Highway Coverage (Express Corridors):** Deploying umbrella cells along the **NH-44, NH-48** and other national highways in India ensures continuous 4G/5G coverage without forced call drops every few kilometers. - **Railways:** India's **Vande Bharat and Shatabdi trains** travel at 130–160 km/h — impossible to serve reliably with microcells alone. - **Disaster Recovery:** When microcell sites are destroyed in floods/earthquakes, umbrella cell sites (mounted on tall towers with backup power) maintain coverage. - **5G Heterogeneous Networks (HetNets):** The umbrella cell is the **macrocell layer** in a 5G HetNet, while the underlying small cells handle mmWave/indoor capacity. - **Maritime / Aerial Coverage:** Coastal umbrella cells provide coverage out to sea for fishing vessels. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Handover Rate Reduction Using Umbrella Cell ### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem Setup) - Microcell radius: $R_{micro} = 0.5 \text{ km} = 500 \text{ m}$ - Umbrella cell radius: $R_{umb} = 5 \text{ km} = 5000 \text{ m}$ - Mobile Station velocity: $v = 90 \text{ km/h}$ - Number of microcells inside one umbrella cell: $N_{micro} = 7$ (typical hexagonal cluster) ### Step 1: Convert Velocity to SI Units The velocity must be in m/s for SI consistency. $$ v = 90 \ \dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times \dfrac{1000 \ \text{m}}{1 \ \text{km}} \times \dfrac{1 \ \text{h}}{3600 \ \text{s}} $$ $$ v = \dfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \ \text{m/s} $$ ### Step 2: Handover Rate Formula For a mobile moving in a **straight line across a cell**, the average number of handovers per unit time is given by the ratio of velocity to the cell diameter (in the direction of motion). $$ H = \dfrac{v}{2R} $$ This is because the mobile traverses a distance of $2R$ (the full diameter) before re-entering the same cell on the opposite side. ### Step 3: Handover Rate in Microcell $$ H_{micro} = \dfrac{v}{2R_{micro}} = \dfrac{25}{2 \times 500} $$ $$ H_{micro} = \dfrac{25}{1000} = 0.025 \ \text{handovers/s} = 1.5 \ \text{handovers/min} $$ **Interpretation:** At 90 km/h, the mobile triggers a handover **every 40 seconds** in the microcellular layer. ### Step 4: Handover Rate in Umbrella Cell $$ H_{umb} = \dfrac{v}{2R_{umb}} = \dfrac{25}{2 \times 5000} $$ $$ H_{umb} = \dfrac{25}{10000} = 0.0025 \ \text{handovers/s} = 0.15 \ \text{handovers/min} $$ **Interpretation:** In the umbrella cell, the mobile triggers a handover only **once every ~6.67 minutes**. ### Step 5: Handover Reduction Ratio $$ \eta = \dfrac{H_{micro}}{H_{umb}} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.0025} = 10 $$ **Conclusion:** The umbrella cell reduces the handover burden by a factor of **10×** in this scenario. This is the single most important quantitative result examiners test. ## Derivation 2: Effect of Doppler Spread on Tier Selection The Doppler shift quantifies how rapidly the channel is changing and directly justifies the umbrella cell. ### Given - Carrier frequency: $f_c = 900 \text{ MHz}$ (GSM-900 uplink band) - MS velocity: $v = 120 \text{ km/h}$ - Angle of arrival: $\theta = 0°$ (worst case — MS moving directly toward BTS) ### Step 1: Compute Wavelength $$ \lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_c} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}}{900 \times 10^6 \ \text{Hz}} $$ $$ \lambda = 0.333 \ \text{m} $$ ### Step 2: Convert Velocity $$ v = \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{3600} = 33.33 \ \text{m/s} $$ ### Step 3: Maximum Doppler Shift $$ f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{v}{\lambda} = \dfrac{33.33}{0.333} \approx 100 \ \text{Hz} $$ ### Step 4: Coherence Time of Channel $$ T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_{d,\max}} = \dfrac{0.423}{100} \approx 4.23 \ \text{ms} $$ **Conclusion:** A coherence time of **~4 ms** means the channel is changing extremely rapidly. The microcell's fast power-control loop (operating at 1500 Hz in GSM) struggles to track this. The umbrella cell, with its macro-diversity and slower but more stable power control, handles this scenario much more gracefully. ## Derivation 3: SINR Difference Between Microcell and Umbrella Cell A microcell and an umbrella cell overlapping in the same area must be designed so that the SINR at the boundary is acceptable for both. ### Given - Microcell transmit power: $P_{t,micro} = 1 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ dBm}$ - Umbrella cell transmit power: $P_{t,umb} = 20 \text{ W} = 43 \text{ dBm}$ - Antenna gain difference: $G_{umb} - G_{micro} = 6 \text{ dB}$ (higher antenna, higher gain) - Path loss exponent: $n = 3.5$ (urban) - Cell-edge distance (microcell): $d_{micro} = 500 \text{ m}$ - Cell-edge distance (umbrella): $d_{umb} = 5000 \text{ m}$ ### Step 1: Path Loss at Microcell Edge (Two-Ray Approx. generalised) $$ L_{p,micro} \propto d_{micro}^{\,n} = (500)^{3.5} $$ ### Step 2: Path Loss at Umbrella Cell Edge $$ L_{p,umb} \propto d_{umb}^{\,n} = (5000)^{3.5} $$ ### Step 3: Power Ratio at Edges The received power from each cell at its own edge is: $$ P_{r,micro} = \dfrac{P_{t,micro} \cdot G_{micro}}{L_{p,micro}} \quad ; \quad P_{r,umb} = \dfrac{P_{t,umb} \cdot G_{umb}}{L_{p,umb}} $$ Plugging in: $$ P_{r,micro} = \dfrac{1 \cdot G_{micro}}{(500)^{3.5}} \quad ; \quad P_{r,umb} = \dfrac{20 \cdot (4 G_{micro})}{(5000)^{3.5}} $$ ### Step 4: Compute the Numerical Ratio $$ \dfrac{P_{r,umb}}{P_{r,micro}} = \dfrac{20 \times 4}{(5000/500)^{3.5}} = \dfrac{80}{(10)^{3.5}} = \dfrac{80}{3162.3} \approx 0.0253 $$ In dB: $$ 10 \log_{10}(0.0253) \approx -15.97 \ \text{dB} $$ **Conclusion:** At the umbrella cell's far edge, the received power is about **16 dB lower** than at the microcell edge. The network must use **different SINR thresholds** for the two tiers, and a **hysteresis margin** of ~5–10 dB prevents ping-pong handovers. ## Analytical Comparison Matrix | Aspect | Without Umbrella Cell | With Umbrella Cell | |---|---|---| | Handovers per minute (90 km/h) | 1.5 | 0.15 | | Call drop probability (per hour) | High (~5–10%) | Low (< 0.5%) | | Coverage of highway gaps | Poor | Excellent | | Handover signaling load on MSC | High | Low | | Frequency reuse efficiency | Lower (forced cluster size) | Higher (different tiers reuse independently) | | Hardware cost | Many micro-BTS | Fewer, but taller macro-BTS | | Power consumption per cell | Low | Higher (but fewer cells) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: Hierarchical Layout of Umbrella Cell Over Microcells ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HCS["HIERARCHICAL CELL STRUCTURE HCS"] direction TB UMB["UMBRELLA MACROCELL
Radius 5 km to 20 km
High Power High Antenna"] end subgraph MICRO["UNDERLYING MICROCELL LAYER"] direction TB M1["MICROCELL M1
Radius 500 m"] M2["MICROCELL M2
Radius 500 m"] M3["MICROCELL M3
Radius 500 m"] M4["MICROCELL M4
Radius 500 m"] M5["MICROCELL M5
Radius 500 m"] M6["MICROCELL M6
Radius 500 m"] M7["MICROCELL M7
Radius 500 m"] end subgraph PICO["PICO AND FEMTO LAYER"] direction TB P1["PICOCELL P1
Radius 50 m"] P2["PICOCELL P2
Radius 50 m"] P3["FEMTOCELL F1
Radius 10 m"] end UMB --- MICRO MICRO --- PICO MS1(["SLOW MS PEDESTRIAN"]) --> P1 MS2(["MEDIUM MS CAR 30 km per h"]) --> M2 MS3(["FAST MS CAR 90 km per h"]) --> UMB style UMB fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style MICRO fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style PICO fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#1f3864,stroke-width:1px style MS1 fill:#fff2cc style MS2 fill:#fce5cd style MS3 fill:#f4cccc ``` ## Diagram 2: Handover Decision Flow Between Tiers ```mermaid flowchart TD A(["MS MOVING IN COVERAGE AREA"]) --> B{"MS VELOCITY V ESTIMATED FROM DOPPLER"} B -->|V less than 10 km per h| C["ASSIGN TO PICOCELL LAYER"] B -->|V 10 to 60 km per h| D["ASSIGN TO MICROCELL LAYER"] B -->|V greater than 60 km per h| E["ASSIGN TO UMBRELLA CELL LAYER"] C --> F{"MICROCELL SINR BELOW THRESHOLD"} D --> F E --> G{"UMBRELLA CELL SINR BELOW THRESHOLD"} F -->|YES| H["EXECUTE INTER TIER HANDOVER
PICOCELL TO MICROCELL"] F -->|NO| I["REMAIN IN CURRENT TIER"] G -->|YES| J["EXECUTE INTER TIER HANDOVER
MICROCELL TO UMBRELLA"] G -->|NO| I H --> I J --> I style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style B fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1f3864 style E fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000 style J fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000 style I fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d ``` ## Diagram 3: Functional Block Architecture of Umbrella-Enabled Network ```mermaid flowchart LR MS(["MOBILE STATION MS
HANDOVER DECISION ENGINE"]) --> ANT["ANTENNA AND RF FRONT END"] ANT --> SIG["SIGNAL STRENGTH ESTIMATOR
RxLEV RxQUAL"] SIG --> HCSCTL{"HCS CONTROLLER
IN BSC"] HCSCTL --> T1["TIER 1 FEMTO BTS
10 m"] HCSCTL --> T2["TIER 2 PICO BTS
50 m"] HCSCTL --> T3["TIER 3 MICRO BTS
500 m"] HCSCTL --> T4["TIER 4 UMBRELLA MACRO BTS
5 km TALL TOWER"] T1 --> BSC["BSC BASE STATION CONTROLLER"] T2 --> BSC T3 --> BSC T4 --> BSC BSC --> MSC["MSC MOBILE SWITCHING CENTER"] MSC --> PSTN(["PSTN PUBLIC SWITCHED TELEPHONE NETWORK"]) MSC --> INET(["INTERNET IP CORE NETWORK"]) style MS fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style HCSCTL fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style T4 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style BSC fill:#c9daf8 style MSC fill:#b4c7e7 ``` > [!NOTE] > The HCS Controller inside the BSC is the **brain** of the umbrella cell concept. It continuously polls the MS's measured RxLEV/RxQUAL values and decides — within milliseconds — which tier should serve the call. This is exactly the architecture used in modern 4G/5G SON (Self-Organizing Networks). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the Umbrella Cell Concept. Mention any two of its advantages. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **Umbrella Cell Concept** is a hierarchical cellular design in which a large-radius, high-power macrocell (the "umbrella cell") is overlaid on top of a group of smaller, low-power microcells/picocells. It provides a continuous coverage envelope for fast-moving mobile stations and acts as a fallback for the inner-tier cells. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Reduces the number of handovers** for high-speed MS by an order of magnitude (typically 10×), drastically lowering call-drop probability on highways. 2. **Provides coverage in shadowed or shadow-faded regions** where microcell signals are weak, by using a higher antenna and higher EIRP. *(Any two valid advantages accepted.)* **[3 marks]** --- ### Q2. Why is an umbrella cell required for highways, but not for indoor offices? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Model Answer:** In **indoor offices**, mobile stations are mostly **stationary or slow-moving pedestrians** (velocity < 5 km/h). Their handover rate is low, and picocells/femtocells can provide ample capacity and coverage. An umbrella cell is unnecessary and would cause interference. In contrast, on **highways**, MS velocities reach 80–120 km/h. Without an umbrella cell, the MS would cross microcell boundaries every 15–30 seconds, leading to a **handover failure rate of 5–10%**. The umbrella cell, with its radius of 5–20 km, ensures the MS stays in one cell for the entire highway stretch, eliminating the ping-pong handover problem. **[3 marks]** --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Umbrella Cell Concept** in a hierarchical cellular network. Discuss how the MSC decides which tier (umbrella, micro, or pico) should serve a given mobile station. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A mobile station is moving at **120 km/h** on a highway. The microcell radius is **400 m** and the umbrella cell radius is **8 km**. Calculate the number of handovers per minute in (i) the microcell layer, and (ii) the umbrella cell layer. Comment on the result. **[7 marks]** --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Diagram:** *(Refer to the HCS block diagram in Section 4 of these notes.)* **Explanation:** The Umbrella Cell Concept is a part of the **Hierarchical Cell Structure (HCS)**. It uses **three or more tiers of cells** of progressively increasing size: - **Tier 1 (Inner):** Femtocells (~10 m) and picocells (~50 m) for indoor / hotspot areas. - **Tier 2 (Middle):** Microcells (~100 m – 1 km) for street-level urban coverage. - **Tier 3 (Outer / Umbrella):** Macrocell (~1 km – 35 km) for highways, rural, and overlay coverage. **MSC Decision Algorithm:** 1. The MS continuously measures **RxLEV** (Received Level) and **RxQUAL** (Received Quality) of the serving cell and the surrounding cells. 2. The MS also implicitly (or explicitly, via GPS) reports its **velocity vector**. 3. The HCS Controller in the BSC applies the following rules: - If the MS velocity is high ($v > 60$ km/h) **OR** the microcell RxLEV is below the **outage threshold** (e.g., –100 dBm), the controller executes a **layer-up handover** to the umbrella cell. - If the MS slows down **AND** a microcell RxLEV exceeds the **entry threshold** (with a hysteresis margin of 5–10 dB), the controller executes a **layer-down handover** back to the microcell. 4. This is done in a **make-before-break** fashion to avoid call drops. **Why it works:** The umbrella cell's higher antenna and higher EIRP ensure that its signal is still strong even at the cell edge, giving the high-speed MS a stable connection. The microcells below continue serving slow MS in their zones. The two layers are **frequency-separated** to avoid co-channel interference. **[7 marks]** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - $v = 120$ km/h - $R_{micro} = 400$ m - $R_{umb} = 8$ km = 8000 m **Step 1: Convert velocity to m/s** $$ v = \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{3600} = 33.33 \ \text{m/s} $$ **Step 2: Handover rate in microcell** $$ H_{micro} = \dfrac{v}{2R_{micro}} = \dfrac{33.33}{2 \times 400} = \dfrac{33.33}{800} $$ $$ H_{micro} \approx 0.0417 \ \text{handovers/s} = 2.5 \ \text{handovers/min} $$ **Step 3: Handover rate in umbrella cell** $$ H_{umb} = \dfrac{v}{2R_{umb}} = \dfrac{33.33}{2 \times 8000} = \dfrac{33.33}{16000} $$ $$ H_{umb} \approx 0.00208 \ \text{handovers/s} = 0.125 \ \text{handovers/min} $$ **Step 4: Handover Reduction Ratio** $$ \eta = \dfrac{H_{micro}}{H_{umb}} = \dfrac{0.0417}{0.00208} = 20 $$ **Step 5: Comment** The umbrella cell reduces handovers by a factor of **20×** at 120 km/h. This means the MS triggers a handover **every 24 seconds in the microcell** versus **once every 8 minutes in the umbrella cell**. The cumulative call-drop probability over a 1-hour highway journey drops dramatically. **[7 marks]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the **Hierarchical Cell Structure (HCS)** with reference to the Umbrella Cell Concept. Compare the antenna height, transmit power, and cell radius of the umbrella cell with the underlying microcell. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A GSM operator uses a carrier frequency of **1800 MHz**. A mobile station moves at **60 km/h** directly toward the base station. Calculate (i) the wavelength, (ii) the maximum Doppler shift, and (iii) the coherence time of the channel. Why is this analysis relevant to the Umbrella Cell Concept? **[7 marks]** --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **HCS Definition:** Hierarchical Cell Structure is a multi-tier cellular architecture where cells of different sizes (femto < pico < micro < macro) coexist in the same geographical area, each optimized for a different traffic type and MS speed. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Umbrella Macrocell | Microcell | |---|---|---| | Cell Radius | **1 km – 35 km** | **100 m – 1 km** | | Antenna Height | **> 30 m (often 50–100 m)** | **< 15 m** | | Transmit Power | **> 10 W (often 20–40 W)** | **0.1 W – 1 W** | | EIRP | High | Low | | Coverage Use | Highway, rural, fallback | Urban street | | Handover Load | Low (one big cell) | High (many small cells) | **Key Insight:** The umbrella cell is *not* a replacement; it works **in parallel** with the underlying cells, providing a "safety net" of coverage. The MSC routes the MS to the appropriate tier based on its velocity and signal strength. **[7 marks]** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $f_c = 1800 \text{ MHz} = 1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, $v = 60$ km/h, $\theta = 0°$ **Step (i): Wavelength** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_c} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1.8 \times 10^9} $$ $$ \lambda = 0.1667 \ \text{m} = 16.67 \ \text{cm} $$ **Step (ii): Convert velocity** $$ v = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \ \text{m/s} $$ **Maximum Doppler Shift ($\theta = 0°$):** $$ f_{d,\max} = \dfrac{v}{\lambda} \cos 0° = \dfrac{16.67}{0.1667} \times 1 $$ $$ f_{d,\max} = 100 \ \text{Hz} $$ **Step (iii): Coherence Time** Using Jakes' approximation: $$ T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_{d,\max}} = \dfrac{0.423}{100} $$ $$ T_c \approx 4.23 \ \text{ms} $$ **Relevance to Umbrella Cell:** A coherence time of only **~4 ms** means the channel fades very rapidly. A high-speed MS cannot be served reliably by a single microcell with fast power control, because: 1. The channel changes before power control can compensate. 2. The MS crosses cell boundaries faster than the signaling can complete a handover. The **umbrella cell** solves this by: 1. Providing **macro-diversity** — a stable average signal from its tall tower. 2. Eliminating frequent handovers (one big cell covers the entire highway). 3. Operating with a **slower but more robust** power control loop suited to the macro channel. This is why **Doppler analysis is fundamental** to the umbrella cell design. **[7 marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s. This single error in a numerical problem costs **2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Confusing Handover Rate with Handover Probability:** These are different quantities. State which one you are computing. > 3. **Forgetting Hysteresis:** Students often describe tier selection as a hard threshold. Always mention the **5–10 dB hysteresis margin** to prevent ping-pong. > 4. **Treating Umbrella Cell as a "Big Microcell":** Examiners penalise answers that omit the **co-existence with microcells** and the **different frequency channel** requirement. > 5. **Skipping the Doppler Relevance:** Any high-velocity handover problem must include a brief Doppler / coherence-time discussion to score full marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Umbrella Cell = High-Power, Tall-Antenna Macrocell** overlaid on microcells/picocells. - **Primary Purpose:** Reduce handovers for **fast-moving MS** and provide a **coverage safety net**. - **Hierarchy:** Femto (~10 m) → Pico (~50 m) → Micro (~500 m) → **Umbrella Macro (5–20 km)**. - **Handover Reduction:** $\eta = R_{umb} / R_{micro}$, typically **10× to 20×** for highway speeds. - **Doppler Shift:** $f_d = (v/\lambda) \cos\theta$ — increases linearly with velocity. - **Coherence Time:** $T_c \approx 0.423 / f_{d,\max}$ — shorter for fast MS, justifying umbrella cell. - **Hysteresis:** Always include a **5–10 dB margin** between tier-up and tier-down thresholds to prevent ping-pong. - **Frequency Reuse:** Umbrella cell uses a **separate frequency channel** from the microcells to avoid co-channel interference. - **Architecture:** HCS Controller in the **BSC** makes the tier-selection decision; the **MSC** does the actual handover signaling. - **Real-World Use:** Highway corridors (NH/SH), high-speed rail (Vande Bharat, Shatabdi), maritime coastal coverage, 5G HetNet macro layer. - **KTU Hot-Key Words:** HCS, macro-diversity, ping-pong handover, RxLEV, RxQUAL, hysteresis, Doppler spread, macrocell-microcell co-existence. ---

topics.title: Trunking and Grade of Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trunking and Grade of Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trunking** is a fundamental resource-sharing technique in telecommunications and wireless cellular systems, in which a **large population of subscribers is allowed to access a small, fixed pool of radio channels on a demand basis**, rather than dedicating a channel to each user permanently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grade of Service (GoS)** is defined as the **probability that a user’s call request is denied access to a channel** (blocked) — or alternatively, in delayed-trunking systems, the probability that the call is **delayed beyond an acceptable threshold**. It is a *quality-of-service* metric of the trunked system, evaluated during the **busy hour** (the continuous 60-minute interval during which the traffic intensity is highest).

Mathematically, GoS is the **probability of call blocking** $P(\text{block})$, or the **probability of call delay** $P(\text{delay} > t_0)$, depending on the trunking discipline.

## 1.2 Key Metric — The Erlang

> [!NOTE]
> The dimensionless unit of traffic intensity is the **Erlang (E)**, named after Danish mathematician **A. K. Erlang**. **One Erlang** represents the traffic load equivalent to **one channel continuously occupied for the entire observation period**.

If a channel is busy 50% of the time during the busy hour, the offered traffic intensity on that channel is **0.5 Erlang**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Model

Imagine a small town with **10 restaurants (channels)** that can seat **4 customers each**, giving **40 total tables (channel-seats)**. However, there are **5,000 residents (users)**. During the Saturday dinner rush (busy hour), every resident wants a table simultaneously. Obviously, most will be turned away.

- **Without trunking**: We would need 5,000 tables — economically impossible.
- **With trunking**: Only 40 tables are built, and the GoS (probability of being turned away) is computed from statistical traffic models.

The **GoS is the engineered trade-off** that minimizes infrastructure cost while accepting a tolerable, small blocking probability (typically **2%** in cellular telephony).

## 1.4 Two Canonical Trunking Models (KTU Board Focus)

| Discipline | What happens to a blocked call? | GoS Definition | Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blocked Calls Cleared (BCC)** | Call is *lost* (user redials) | $B = P(\text{blocked})$ | **Erlang B** |
| **Blocked Calls Delayed (BCD)** | Call is *queued* until a channel frees | $P(\text{delay} > 0)$ | **Erlang C** |

> [!NOTE]
> Cellular systems (AMPS, GSM) are conventionally modeled as **Erlang B** (no queueing — user hears a fast busy tone and redials). Call-center and satellite systems often use **Erlang C**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Model the Call Arrival Process
Call arrivals are modeled as a **Poisson process** with mean arrival rate $\lambda$ (calls/second). Holding times are **exponentially distributed** with mean $H$ (seconds/call). This gives rise to the **M/M/C/C** birth-death Markov chain.

### Step 2: Define Traffic Intensity
The **offered traffic load** (in Erlangs) is the product of arrival rate and mean holding time:

$$A = \lambda \cdot H$$

- $\lambda$ — average call arrival rate (calls/sec)
- $H$ — average call holding/duration time (sec/call)
- $A$ — offered traffic intensity (**Erlangs**)

### Step 3: System States
The system can be in one of $C+1$ discrete states: state $k$ means exactly $k$ of the $C$ channels are busy.

### Step 4: Birth-Death Equilibrium
- **Birth rate** (call arrival): $\lambda_k = \lambda$ (constant for all $k$).
- **Death rate** (call completion): $\mu_k = k \cdot \mu$, where $\mu = 1/H$.
- **Traffic offered per service facility**: $a = \lambda / \mu = \lambda H = A$.

### Step 5: Steady-State Probability Distribution
Solving $\pi_k \lambda_k = \pi_{k+1} \mu_{k+1}$ iteratively yields:

$$\pi_k = \frac{A^k / k!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i / i!}$$

### Step 6: Blocking Probability
The system rejects a call only when **all $C$ channels are busy** (state $C$). Thus:

$$B(C, A) = \pi_C = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i / i!}$$

This is the celebrated **Erlang B formula**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — every KTU board numerical on this topic is solved using only these equations.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \lambda H$ | Traffic intensity (Erlang B/C input) | Erlangs (E) |
| 2 | $B(C,A) = \dfrac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{i=0}^{C} A^i/i!}$ | **Erlang B** — blocking probability | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P(\text{delay}) = \dfrac{A^C/C!}{(A^C/C!)+(1-A/C)\sum_{i=0}^{C-1} A^i/i!}$ | **Erlang C** — probability of waiting | dimensionless |
| 4 | $A_c = A(1-B)$ | **Carried traffic** (actually serviced) | Erlangs |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{A_c}{A_u}$ | Number of users supported | users |
| 6 | $\text{GoS} = 2\%$ | Standard cellular blocking (typical) | % |
| 7 | $\text{BHCA}$ | Busy Hour Call Attempts | calls/hour |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{M \cdot A_c}{B_W \cdot K}$ | Spectral efficiency (trunked cellular) | Erlangs/Hz/cell |

> [!NOTE]
> **In the table above**, $\vert$ is written as `\vert` to keep LaTeX safe inside markdown tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cellular capacity planning**: A cell designer chooses $C$ (channels) and computes the maximum number of subscribers $N$ that can be served at a GoS of 2% during the busy hour.
- **Switching and switching-center design**: Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) and Mobile Switching Center (MSC) sizing.
- **Satellite multiple-access design**: VSAT, DAMA, and Inmarsat systems use Erlang B to dimension transponder resources.
- **Call-center staffing**: Erlang C predicts queue lengths and agent counts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Erlang B Formula

We consider an **M/M/C/C loss system** with:
- Poisson arrivals of rate $\lambda$
- Exponential holding times with mean $1/\mu$ per channel
- $C$ identical servers (channels)
- **No waiting room** — a call arriving when all servers are busy is *lost forever* (cleared).

### Step (a): Birth and Death Rates
For a system in state $k$ ($0 \le k \le C$):
- **Birth rate** (new call arrives, $k \to k+1$): $\lambda_k = \lambda$
- **Death rate** (call ends, $k \to k-1$): $\mu_k = k\mu$

**Logic**: When $k$ channels are busy, there are $k$ independent exponential service clocks running simultaneously. The minimum of $k$ i.i.d. exponentials is itself exponential with rate $k\mu$, so the death rate scales linearly.

### Step (b): Local Balance Equation
In steady state, the flow into state $k$ equals the flow out:

$$\pi_k \lambda_k = \pi_{k+1} \mu_{k+1}$$

Substituting our rates:

$$\pi_k \lambda = \pi_{k+1} (k+1)\mu$$

Therefore:

$$\pi_{k+1} = \frac{\lambda}{(k+1)\mu}\,\pi_k$$

### Step (c): Define the Traffic-Load Parameter
Let:

$$A = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \lambda H$$

(where $H = 1/\mu$ is the mean holding time). Then:

$$\pi_{k+1} = \frac{A}{k+1}\,\pi_k$$

### Step (d): Iterative Unrolling
Applying the recursion repeatedly from $\pi_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= \frac{A}{1}\pi_0 \\
\pi_2 &= \frac{A}{2}\pi_1 = \frac{A^2}{2!}\pi_0 \\
\pi_3 &= \frac{A}{3}\pi_2 = \frac{A^3}{3!}\pi_0 \\
\vdots \\
\pi_k &= \frac{A^k}{k!}\pi_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**General form** (text-row conversion logic: at each step the denominator $k+1$ accumulates, giving $k!$):

$$\pi_k = \frac{A^k}{k!}\,\pi_0 \quad \text{for } k=0,1,2,\dots,C$$

### Step (e): Normalization (Find $\pi_0$)
All steady-state probabilities must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{C} \pi_k = 1$$

Substituting the closed form:

$$\pi_0 \sum_{k=0}^{C} \frac{A^k}{k!} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\pi_0 = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

### Step (f): Blocking Probability
A call is blocked precisely when it arrives to find **all $C$ channels busy**, i.e., the system is in state $C$. The blocking probability is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B(C,A) &= \pi_C = \frac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!} \\
&= \frac{A^C / C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{C}\frac{A^k}{k!}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Erlang B formula**, valid for any integer $C \ge 1$ and offered traffic $A \ge 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **denominator is a truncated Taylor series of $e^A$**. As $C \to \infty$, the denominator tends to $e^A$ and $B \to 0$ (no blocking). In practice, KTU board questions always use the *finite* truncated form.

## 3.2 Carried Traffic & Capacity Derivation

The **carried traffic** $A_c$ is the actual traffic that the system services (i.e., offered traffic minus blocked traffic). Since a fraction $B$ of offered traffic is lost:

$$A_c = A(1 - B)$$

In a system with $N$ users each contributing traffic $A_u$:

$$A = N \cdot A_u \quad \text{and} \quad A_c = N \cdot A_u (1 - B)$$

The number of supported users at a given GoS constraint $B^*$ is therefore:

$$N = \frac{A^*}{A_u}$$

where $A^*$ is the maximum offered traffic that the system can carry at blocking $B^*$, obtained by inverting the Erlang B table.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem**: A trunked cell has $C = 8$ channels. Each user makes on average **2 calls per hour** with an average holding time of **3 minutes**. The target GoS is **2%**. Find the number of users that can be supported.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Compute per-user traffic.

$$\lambda = \frac{2 \text{ calls}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ calls/s}$$

$$H = 3 \text{ min} = 180 \text{ s}$$

$$A_u = \lambda H = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \times 180 = 0.1 \text{ Erlang}$$

Step 2: Find maximum offered traffic for $C = 8$, GoS = 2% from Erlang B tables.

Standard tables give $A^* \approx 5.08$ Erlangs.

Step 3: Compute number of users.

$$N = \frac{A^*}{A_u} = \frac{5.08}{0.1} = 50.8 \approx 50 \text{ users}$$

The cell can support **50 users** at 2% blocking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Trunked Cellular System

```mermaid
graph TD
    UserPool[USER POOL N SUBSCRIBERS] -->|Call Request| CtlCh[CONTROL CHANNEL SETUP]
    CtlCh -->|Channel Request| Switch[MSC MTSO CENTRAL SWITCH]
    Switch -->|Dynamic Allocation| ChPool[CHANNEL POOL C RADIO CHANNELS]
    ChPool -->|Bearer Assignment| CallActive[CALL IN PROGRESS STATE K BUSY]
    CallActive -->|Handover or Release| Switch
    ChPool -->|Busy Idle Status| Mon[TRAFFIC MONITOR BUSY HOUR]
    Mon -->|Measured Load A| Switch
    Switch -->|GoS Probability B| Output[BLOCKED CALL FAST BUSY TONE]
    Output -->|Redial| UserPool
    Switch -->|Served Call| CarriedT[CARRIED TRAFFIC AC = A 1 MINUS B]
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Flow of an M/M/C/C Loss System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State0
    State0 --> State1: Arrival rate lambda
    State1 --> State0: Service rate 1 times mu
    State1 --> State2: Arrival rate lambda
    State2 --> State1: Service rate 2 times mu
    State2 --> State3: Arrival rate lambda
    State3 --> State2: Service rate 3 times mu
    State3 --> StateC: Arrival rate lambda
    StateC --> StateCminus1: Service rate C times mu
    StateC --> Blocked: Arrival rate lambda Call Lost
    Blocked --> [*]
    StateCminus1 --> StateC: Arrival rate lambda
    State0 --> [*]: System Idle
```

## 4.3 Logical Relationship Between GoS, C, and A

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[OFFERED TRAFFIC A ERLANGS] -->|Input| EB[ERLANG B ENGINE]
    C[CHANNELS C INTEGER] -->|Input| EB
    EB -->|Output| B[B BLOCKING PROBABILITY GoS]
    B -->|Compare with 2 percent| Decision{GoS LTE 2 percent}
    Decision -->|Yes| Accept[SYSTEM ACCEPTABLE]
    Decision -->|No| Reject[ADD CHANNELS OR REDUCE LOAD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **functional block schematics**, not physical RF drawings. They map the *interactions* among subscribers, the central switch, the channel pool, and the traffic monitor — which is the *intended pedagogical level* for KTU Module 2 of PCCPT502.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define Grade of Service. Why is 2% blocking considered the standard in cellular systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks)**: Grade of Service (GoS) is defined as the probability that a user’s call is **blocked** during the busy hour when all channels in a trunked system are busy. Mathematically, $\text{GoS} = B(C, A)$, the Erlang B blocking probability.

> **Why 2% (1.5 marks)**: A GoS of 2% (i.e., 2 out of every 100 calls blocked in the busy hour) represents an optimal engineering trade-off. Lower blocking (e.g., 0.1%) would require **drastically more channels** per cell (exponential cost increase) for negligible customer-perceived improvement. Higher blocking (e.g., 5–10%) produces significant customer dissatisfaction and lost revenue. International telecommunications standards (ITU, AMPS, GSM) converged on **2% as the industry standard** for cellular voice service.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define an Erlang. A user makes 3 calls per hour with an average call duration of 6 minutes. Calculate the traffic offered by this user.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark)**: An **Erlang** is the dimensionless unit of telecommunications traffic intensity, equal to **one continuously occupied channel** for the entire measurement interval.

> **Calculation (2 marks)**:
> 
> 
> $$\lambda = \frac{3 \text{ calls}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ calls/s}$$
> 
> 
> $$H = 6 \text{ min} = 360 \text{ s}$$
> 
> 
> $$A_u = \lambda H = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \times 360 = 0.3 \text{ Erlang}$$
> 

The user generates **0.3 Erlang** of offered traffic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: In Erlang B numericals, students frequently (1) confuse $\lambda$ units (per hour vs per second), (2) drop the $1 - A/C$ term in the Erlang C denominator, and (3) report $B$ as a percentage without stating the formula. Always write the **formula first**, then plug numbers. Marks are awarded for *correct formula citation* even if arithmetic is slightly off.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Erlang B + System Capacity
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the **Erlang B formula** for a trunked system with $C$ channels and offered traffic $A$ Erlangs. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cellular operator deploys a cell with **$C = 10$ channels**. Each subscriber makes on average **1.5 calls per hour** with a **mean holding time of 120 seconds**. Determine: **(i)** the per-user traffic, **(ii)** the maximum number of subscribers at a GoS of **2%**, and **(iii)** the carried traffic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)** — *See the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 above. The full birth-death Markov chain argument is required.*

> Key steps expected (each worth 1–2 marks):
> 1. M/M/C/C loss-system assumption (Poisson arrivals, exponential service).
> 2. State definition $k$ = number of busy channels.
> 3. Birth rate $\lambda_k = \lambda$ and death rate $\mu_k = k\mu$.
> 4. Local balance: $\pi_k \lambda = \pi_{k+1}(k+1)\mu$.
> 5. Iterative solution: $\pi_k = (A^k/k!)\pi_0$.
> 6. Normalization to find $\pi_0$.
> 7. Final Erlang B expression: $B(C,A) = \dfrac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Per-user traffic (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1.5}{3600} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ calls/s} \\
H &= 120 \text{ s} \\
A_u &= \lambda H = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \times 120 = 0.05 \text{ Erlang}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum subscribers (3 marks):**
From the Erlang B table for $C = 10$ and GoS = 2% (0.02), the maximum offered traffic is:

$$A^* = 5.08 \text{ Erlangs (standard table value)}$$

Therefore:

$$N_{\max} = \frac{A^*}{A_u} = \frac{5.08}{0.05} = 101.6 \approx 101 \text{ users}$$

**(iii) Carried traffic (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= A^*(1 - B) = 5.08 \times (1 - 0.02) \\
&= 5.08 \times 0.98 = 4.978 \text{ Erlangs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Erlang C + Spectral Efficiency
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Erlang B and Erlang C** trunking models. Under what realistic conditions is each model preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microcell has **$C = 5$ channels** and offered traffic of **$A = 2$ Erlangs**. Compute: **(i)** the probability that an arriving call is **delayed** (Erlang C), **(ii)** the probability that the delay exceeds **20 seconds** given a mean holding time of 100 s, and **(iii)** the carried traffic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

> **Erlang B (Blocked Calls Cleared) — 3.5 marks**:
> - Blocked calls are *lost* (user hears busy tone and must redial).
> - System model: M/M/C/C (no queueing buffer).
> - Formula: $B(C,A) = \dfrac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$.
> - **Use case**: Cellular voice (AMPS, GSM, LTE voice) — humans redial naturally; queueing in real-time is unacceptable.
>
> **Erlang C (Blocked Calls Delayed) — 3.5 marks**:
> - Blocked calls are *queued* until a channel becomes free.
> - System model: M/M/C (infinite queue).
> - Formula: $P(\text{delay}) = \dfrac{A^C/C!}{(A^C/C!) + (1 - A/C)\sum_{k=0}^{C-1} A^k/k!}$.
> - **Use case**: Call centers, satellite reservation systems, packet-data queueing (M/M/1-like delays), where delayed service is preferable to rejection.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Erlang C delay probability (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{delay}) &= \frac{A^C/C!}{(A^C/C!) + (1 - A/C) \sum_{k=0}^{C-1} A^k/k!} \\
&= \frac{2^5/5!}{(2^5/5!) + (1 - 2/5)\left[1 + 2 + 2^2/2! + 2^3/3! + 2^4/4!\right]} \\
&= \frac{32/120}{32/120 + (3/5)\left[1 + 2 + 2 + 4/3 + 16/24\right]} \\
&= \frac{0.2667}{0.2667 + 0.6 \times 6.3333} \\
&= \frac{0.2667}{0.2667 + 3.8} = \frac{0.2667}{4.0667} = 0.0656
\end{aligned}
$$

So $P(\text{delay}) \approx 6.56\%$.

**(ii) Probability delay exceeds 20 s (2 marks):**

For exponential service time, $P(\text{delay} > t) = P(\text{delay} > 0) \cdot e^{-(C\mu - \lambda)t}$. With $\mu = 1/100$ s$^{-1}$ and $C\mu - \lambda = 5 \times 0.01 - 0.02 = 0.03$ s$^{-1}$:

$$P(\text{delay} > 20) = 0.0656 \times e^{-0.03 \times 20} = 0.0656 \times e^{-0.6} = 0.0656 \times 0.5488 = 0.0360$$

So $P(\text{delay} > 20\text{ s}) \approx 3.60\%$.

**(iii) Carried traffic (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= A \cdot (1 - P(\text{delay})) \\
&= 2 \times (1 - 0.0656) = 2 \times 0.9344 = 1.869 \text{ Erlangs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Trunking** = sharing $C$ channels among $N$ users; $N \gg C$.
- [x] **GoS** = blocking probability $B$ for Erlang B, or delay probability $P(\text{delay})$ for Erlang C.
- [x] **Traffic intensity** $A = \lambda H$ measured in **Erlangs**.
- [x] **Erlang B**: $B = \dfrac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$ — for **lost calls**.
- [x] **Erlang C**: includes the corrective factor $(1 - A/C)$ in the denominator — for **queued calls**.
- [x] **Carried traffic** $A_c = A(1 - B)$ Erlang B or $A(1 - P(\text{delay}))$ Erlang C.
- [x] **Number of users** $N = A_c / A_u$ where $A_u$ is per-user traffic.
- [x] **Standard cellular GoS** is **2%** during the **busy hour**.
- [x] **Assumptions** of Erlang models: Poisson arrivals, exponential holding time, statistical equilibrium, large user population, **memoryless** property.
- [x] **Spectral efficiency** $\eta = (M \cdot A_c)/(B_W \cdot K)$ — combines trunking with frequency reuse.
- [x] **KTU trap**: Always convert $\lambda$ to **per-second** and $H$ to **seconds** before multiplying. Time-unit mismatch is the #1 cause of numerical errors.
- [x] **KTU trap**: The denominator of Erlang B is a *finite* sum from $k=0$ to $C$, **not** $e^A$. Students who write $e^A$ lose full marks.
- [x] **Inverted lookup**: For "find $N$" problems, *invert* the Erlang B table — locate the row for $C$ channels, find the $A$ value where blocking = 2%, then divide by $A_u$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improving Coverage and Capacity in Cellular System - Cell Splitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Improving Coverage and Capacity in Cellular System - Cell Splitting # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > **Cell Splitting** is a process of subdividing a congested cell in a cellular network into smaller cells (called **microcells**), each with its own base station and a corresponding reduction in antenna height and transmitter power, thereby increasing the system **capacity** and improving **coverage** in high-traffic areas without increasing the allocated frequency spectrum. In the formal KTU framework, cell splitting is classified as one of the principal **capacity enhancement techniques** in cellular system design, alongside *sectoring*, *reuse partitioning*, and *microcell zoning*. It fundamentally addresses the **trunking exhaustion** problem that arises when a cell reaches its maximum subscriber capacity. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a popular restaurant (your base station) that can seat **50 customers** (channel capacity). As the town grows, the waiting line spills onto the street. What does the manager do? - **Option A**: Get a bigger restaurant (analogous to allocating more spectrum — *not feasible*, spectrum is a scarce, regulated resource). - **Option B**: Divide the existing restaurant into **4 smaller dining zones**, each with its own counter, kitchen station, and waiter team (analogous to **Cell Splitting**). Total customers served = **200**, with each smaller zone (microcell) handling its own local crowd efficiently. The customers are now spread across 4 smaller, well-managed zones instead of crammed into 1 large chaotic one. The same physical "footprint" of resources is used, but the **serving capacity has quadrupled**. Similarly, in wireless networks: - The original **macrocell** (large coverage area) becomes congested. - Engineers subdivide it into **microcells** (smaller radius, lower transmit power). - Each microcell now reuses the available frequency channels more efficiently. ### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics The following physical and system parameters must be tracked during cell splitting: > [!NOTE] Standard Metrics > - **Cell Radius ($R$)**: Distance from base station to cell boundary, measured in **kilometers (km)**. > - **Co-channel Reuse Ratio ($Q$)**: Ratio $Q = D/R$, where $D$ is the distance between co-channel cells. > - **Frequency Reuse Factor ($1/N$)**: Where $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ is the **cluster size**. > - **System Capacity ($C$)**: $C = M \cdot N_s$ where $M$ is the number of channels per cell. > - **Transmit Power ($P_t$)**: Must be reduced in proportion to the cell radius reduction. > - **Path Loss Exponent ($n$)**: Typically ranges from **2 (free space)** to **4 (shadowed urban)**. ### 1.4 When is Cell Splitting Required? Cell splitting is triggered under the following operational conditions: 1. **Persistent blocking probability** exceeding 2% during busy hours. 2. **Grade of Service (GoS)** dropping below operator-defined thresholds. 3. **Subscriber density** exceeding the design capacity of the macrocell. 4. **Traffic hot spots** — e.g., stadiums, business districts during working hours. 5. **Handoff failure rate** rising due to channel exhaustion at cell boundaries. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — How Cell Splitting Works Cell splitting is executed in a structured, sequential manner: ### Step 1: Identification of Congested Cells The network operator identifies "saturated" macrocells through: - Call Detail Record (CDR) analysis - KPI monitoring (dropped call rate, blocked call rate, handoff success rate) ### Step 2: Subdivision of Cell Geography The original cell of radius $R_1$ is divided into $N_{split}$ smaller cells. The two most common configurations are: > [!IMPORTANT] Common Subdivision Geometries > - **4-to-1 Split (Most Common)**: One macrocell → 4 microcells of radius $R_2 = R_1/2$. The reduction factor is **2**. > - **7-to-1 Split (Aggressive)**: One macrocell → 7 microcells (miniaturized hexagonal layout). > - **3-to-1 Split**: Used in sector-aligned splitting for combined sectoring + splitting. ### Step 3: Antenna and Transmitter Reconfiguration - **Antenna Height**: Reduced proportionally (typically halved for a 2× radius reduction). - **Transmit Power**: Reduced to maintain the same received power at the new (smaller) cell boundary. - **Downtilt**: Increased to confine radiation pattern to the smaller cell footprint. ### Step 4: Channel Reassignment (The Critical Step) - The smaller cells must be assigned channels such that **co-channel interference is maintained** at the original $Q = D/R$ ratio. - The original cluster size $N$ is preserved. - A new **frequency plan** is computed for the split region. ### Step 5: Handoff Boundary Definition - New handoff thresholds are defined at the microcell boundaries. - The **Original BS** may remain operational as a transitional macro-overlay, or be decommissioned. ## 2.2 The Fundamental Engineering Trade-off Cell splitting is governed by a critical trade-off between **capacity gain** and **infrastructure cost**: | Parameter | Macrocell (Before Split) | Microcell (After Split) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Radius $R$ | Large (e.g., 10 km) | Small (e.g., 1–5 km) | | Transmit Power $P_t$ | High (e.g., 40 W) | Low (e.g., 1–5 W) | | Antenna Height | High (e.g., 50 m) | Low (e.g., 15 m) | | Capacity per cell | Lower | Higher (per unit area) | | Handoff rate | Lower | Higher (frequent handoffs) | | Infrastructure cost | Lower | Higher (more BS sites) | | Coverage per BS | Wide | Narrow | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] All formulas below are KTU High-Yield, directly tested in ESE | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | Cluster size for hexagonal cells | $i, j$ are non-negative integers | | 2 | $C = M \cdot k$ | System capacity (channels) | $M$ = channels/cell, $k$ = total cells | | 3 | $C_{split} = C_{original} \cdot N_{split}$ | Capacity after splitting | $N_{split}$ = number of sub-cells | | 4 | $R_2 = R_1 \cdot \alpha$ | New cell radius | $\alpha$ = reduction factor (e.g., 1/2) | | 5 | $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \alpha^n$ | Transmit power reduction | $n$ = path loss exponent (2 to 4) | | 6 | $Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | Co-channel reuse ratio | Must remain constant post-split | | 7 | $D_2 = D_1 \cdot \alpha$ | New co-channel distance | Proportional reduction | | 8 | $S/I = \frac{(D/R)^n}{6}$ | Signal-to-Interference ratio (worst case) | Valid for hexagonal cluster $N$ | | 9 | $\text{Capacity Density} = \frac{C}{\pi R^2}$ | Channels per unit area | Measures spectral efficiency | | 10 | $N_{split} = \frac{1}{\alpha^2}$ | Number of cells in split (radius reduced by $\alpha$) | For uniform geometric split | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Cell splitting is the **backbone of capacity scaling** in modern wireless networks. Specific real-world applications include: 1. **5G NR Deployment**: Operators use cell splitting to deploy **small cells** (picocells, femtocells) in urban hotspots. 2. **Stadium and Event Networks**: A single macrocell cannot handle 50,000 concurrent users — splitting into picocells is mandatory. 3. **Highway and Tunnel Coverage**: Linear cell splitting along road corridors to maintain signal quality. 4. **Indoor Coverage Enhancement**: Office buildings split into per-floor microcells. 5. **Disaster Recovery**: Temporary microcells (COWs — Cells on Wheels) for capacity surge. ## 2.5 Limitations and Engineering Challenges > [!WARNING] Common Mistakes to Avoid > - **Handoff Ping-Pong**: Smaller cells mean more frequent handoffs, which can overload the Mobile Switching Center (MSC). > - **Inter-Cell Interference**: Improper frequency planning during the split can cause co-channel interference. > - **Transitional Complexity**: During the split, the system must support both old and new cells simultaneously. - **Increased Backhaul**: More base stations = more backhaul links = higher operational cost. - **Site Acquisition**: Finding physical sites for new base stations in dense urban areas is increasingly difficult. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: New Cell Radius After Splitting **Problem Setup**: A hexagonal macrocell of radius $R_1$ is split into 4 equal hexagonal microcells (4-to-1 split). Derive the new cell radius $R_2$. ### Step 1: Equate Areas The area of a regular hexagon is given by: $$A_{hex} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^2$$ For the original macrocell: $$A_{macro} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R_1^2$$ For each new microcell: $$A_{micro} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R_2^2$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Area Conservation Principle Since 1 macrocell is replaced by 4 microcells covering the same geographic area: $$A_{macro} = 4 \cdot A_{micro}$$ Substituting: $$\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R_1^2 = 4 \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R_2^2$$ ### Step 3: Simplify The constants $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}$ cancel from both sides: $$R_1^2 = 4 R_2^2$$ Taking the square root of both sides (and taking the positive root, since radius is non-negative): $$R_1 = 2 R_2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{R_2 = \frac{R_1}{2}}$$ **Interpretation**: To split a cell into 4 equal microcells, the new cell radius must be exactly **half** of the original radius. This is the canonical 4-to-1 cell splitting geometry. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transmitter Power Reduction Ratio **Problem Setup**: Derive the new transmit power $P_{t2}$ required for the microcell such that the received signal strength at the (new, smaller) cell boundary equals that of the original macrocell at its boundary. ### Step 1: Recall the Received Power Equation The general received power in a wireless channel is given by: $$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\right)^2 \cdot d^{-n}$$ where: - $P_t$ = Transmit power - $G_t, G_r$ = Transmit and receive antenna gains - $\lambda$ = Wavelength - $d$ = Distance - $n$ = Path loss exponent For a fixed system, the gain terms and wavelength are constant. We can rewrite the proportionality: $$P_r \propto P_t \cdot d^{-n}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Boundary Condition At the macrocell boundary, the received power is: $$P_{r1} = K \cdot P_{t1} \cdot R_1^{-n}$$ where $K$ represents all the constant system parameters. At the microcell boundary: $$P_{r2} = K \cdot P_{t2} \cdot R_2^{-n}$$ ### Step 3: Equate Boundary Received Powers For consistent coverage, the received signal strength at the cell boundary must be the same: $$P_{r1} = P_{r2}$$ $$K \cdot P_{t1} \cdot R_1^{-n} = K \cdot P_{t2} \cdot R_2^{-n}$$ ### Step 4: Solve for the Power Ratio Cancel $K$ from both sides: $$P_{t1} \cdot R_1^{-n} = P_{t2} \cdot R_2^{-n}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{P_{t2}}{P_{t1}} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{-n} = \left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^{n}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute the 4-to-1 Split Result From Section 3.1, $R_2 = R_1/2$, so $R_1/R_2 = 2$: $$\boxed{\frac{P_{t2}}{P_{t1}} = 2^{-n}}$$ For typical path loss exponents: | Environment | $n$ | Power Ratio $P_{t2}/P_{t1}$ | Power Reduction | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Free space | 2 | $1/4$ | $4\times$ | | Urban cellular | 3 | $1/8$ | $8\times$ | | Shadowed urban | 4 | $1/16$ | $16\times$ | **Engineering Insight**: In urban environments, the transmit power must be reduced by a factor of **8 to 16** when performing a 4-to-1 cell split. This dramatic power reduction is what makes cell splitting economically viable for dense deployments. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacity Enhancement Factor **Problem Setup**: Derive the capacity enhancement factor when a macrocell is split into $N_{split}$ microcells. ### Step 1: Original Capacity Assume the original macrocell supports $M$ channels per cell. The total system capacity is: $$C_{original} = M \cdot k_{total}$$ where $k_{total}$ is the total number of cells in the system. ### Step 2: New Capacity After Splitting After splitting, each macrocell becomes $N_{split}$ microcells, but each microcell still supports $M$ channels (the cluster size $N$ is preserved). The new total system capacity is: $$C_{new} = M \cdot (N_{split} \cdot k_{total})$$ ### Step 3: Capacity Enhancement Ratio $$\frac{C_{new}}{C_{original}} = \frac{M \cdot N_{split} \cdot k_{total}}{M \cdot k_{total}} = N_{split}$$ For a 4-to-1 split ($N_{split} = 4$): $$\boxed{\frac{C_{new}}{C_{original}} = 4}$$ **Interpretation**: A 4-to-1 cell split **quadruples the system capacity** without any additional spectrum allocation. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style) > [!NOTE] Standard Problem Pattern > **Problem**: A cellular system uses a 7-cell cluster ($N=7$) with a cell radius of 10 km. The operator decides to split the cells into microcells of radius 5 km. Given a path loss exponent $n = 3$ and a transmit power of 40 W for the original macrocell, calculate: > (a) The new transmit power for the microcells. > (b) The new co-channel cell separation distance. > (c) The capacity enhancement factor. ### Solution: **(a) New Transmit Power**: The radius reduction factor is $\alpha = R_2/R_1 = 5/10 = 1/2$. $$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \alpha^n = 40 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = 40 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = 5 \text{ W}$$ **(b) New Co-channel Distance**: The co-channel reuse ratio must be preserved: $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \cdot 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$ Original: $D_1 = Q \cdot R_1 = 4.58 \cdot 10 = 45.8 \text{ km}$ New: $D_2 = Q \cdot R_2 = 4.58 \cdot 5 = 22.9 \text{ km}$ Equivalently, $D_2 = D_1 \cdot \alpha = 45.8 \cdot 0.5 = 22.9 \text{ km}$. **(c) Capacity Enhancement Factor**: $$\frac{C_{new}}{C_{original}} = N_{split} = \frac{1}{\alpha^2} = \frac{1}{(1/2)^2} = 4$$ **Capacity increases by a factor of 4**. --- ## 3.5 Co-Channel Interference Analysis After Splitting The **Signal-to-Interference ratio (S/I)** is a critical metric that must remain compliant after splitting: $$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{6} = \frac{Q^n}{6}$$ Since $Q = D/R$ must remain constant (it is a function of the cluster size $N$ only, not the absolute cell radius), the **S/I ratio is invariant under cell splitting**. This is a critical insight: cell splitting does not degrade the interference environment, provided the frequency plan is correctly recomputed. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Cell Splitting — Before and After The following diagram illustrates the geometric transformation of a single macrocell into 4 microcells, showing the reduced radius and the maintained co-channel separation ratio. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE SPLITTING - One Macrocell"] direction TB BS1["BS_MACRO
Radius R1 = 10 km
Transmit Power = 40 W
Antenna Height = 50 m"] CELL1["HEXAGONAL COVERAGE
Area = 259.8 sq km
Capacity = M channels"] end subgraph AFTER["AFTER SPLITTING - Four Microcells"] direction TB BS2A["BS_MICRO_A
Radius R2 = 5 km
Transmit Power = 5 W
Antenna Height = 25 m"] BS2B["BS_MICRO_B
Radius R2 = 5 km
Transmit Power = 5 W
Antenna Height = 25 m"] BS2C["BS_MICRO_C
Radius R2 = 5 km
Transmit Power = 5 W
Antenna Height = 25 m"] BS2D["BS_MICRO_D
Radius R2 = 5 km
Transmit Power = 5 W
Antenna Height = 25 m"] CELL2["FOUR HEXAGONAL MICROCELLS
Total Area = 259.8 sq km
Total Capacity = 4M channels"] end BS1 ==> CELL1 BS2A -.-> CELL2 BS2B -.-> CELL2 BS2C -.-> CELL2 BS2D -.-> CELL2 classDef macro fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef micro fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef container fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class BS1 macro class BS2A,BS2B,BS2C,BS2D micro class CELL1,CELL2 container ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Frequency Reuse Plan in Cell Splitting This diagram shows how the frequency channels are reassigned to microcells while preserving the cluster size $N$ and the co-channel reuse ratio $Q$. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CLUSTER["7-CELL CLUSTER - N = 7"] direction TB C1["CELL 1
Channel Group A
f1 to fM"] C2["CELL 2
Channel Group B
fM+1 to f2M"] C3["CELL 3

f4M+1 to f5M"] C6["CELL 6
Channel Group F
f5M+1 to f6M"] C7["CELL 7
Channel Group G
f6M+1 to f7M"] end C1 -- "Co-channel distance D" --- C8["NEXT CLUSTER
CELL 1
Channel Group A"] C1 -- "Reuse Ratio Q = D/R" --- C8 classDef clusterCell fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef coChannel fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7 clusterCell class C8 coChannel ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cell Splitting Process Flow This block diagram shows the end-to-end engineering workflow for executing cell splitting in a live network. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STAGE 1: TRAFFIC ANALYSIS
Identify congested macrocells
Measure blocking probability
Analyze CDR records"] --> B["STAGE 2: PLANNING
Choose split ratio - 4:1 or 7:1
Compute new cell radius R2
Compute new transmit power Pt2"] B --> C["STAGE 3: FREQUENCY PLANNING
Preserve cluster size N
Recompute channel assignments
Verify co-channel ratio Q"] C --> D["STAGE 4: INFRASTRUCTURE DEPLOYMENT
Install new microcell base stations
Reduce antenna height and downtilt
Configure lower transmit power"] D --> E["STAGE 5: TRANSITION MANAGEMENT
Define handoff boundaries
Maintain macrocell as overlay
Gradual subscriber migration"] E --> F["STAGE 6: VALIDATION AND OPTIMIZATION
Measure new capacity
Verify S/I ratio unchanged
Monitor handoff success rate"] classDef stage fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F stage ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [KTU University Exam – Model Question] > **Q1. Define cell splitting. Why is it required in a cellular system?** > > **Model Answer** (3 marks): > **Cell splitting** is the process of subdividing a congested cell (macrocell) into smaller cells (microcells), each with its own base station, reduced antenna height, and lower transmit power. > It is required to: > 1. **Increase system capacity** without allocating additional spectrum (3 marks structure: 1 mark definition + 1 mark reason-1 + 1 mark reason-2). > [KTU University Exam – Model Question] > **Q2. State the formula relating the new transmit power to the old transmit power after cell splitting, and explain each term.** > > **Model Answer** (3 marks): > $$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^n$$ > where $P_{t1}$ = original transmit power, $P_{t2}$ = new transmit power, $R_1$ = original cell radius, $R_2$ = new cell radius, and $n$ = path loss exponent (typically 2 to 4) (1 mark formula + 1 mark term explanation + 1 mark value of n). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > [KTU University Exam – Module 2 | 14 Marks] > **Question A** > (a) Explain the concept of cell splitting in cellular systems with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the new cell radius and the original cell radius for a 4-to-1 split. **(7 marks)** > (b) A cellular system with cell radius 8 km uses a 7-cell reuse pattern. The system operator performs a cell split reducing the radius to 4 km. If the path loss exponent is 3 and the original transmit power is 32 W, determine: > (i) The new transmit power. > (ii) The new co-channel cell separation. > (iii) The capacity enhancement factor. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — 7 Marks**: - **Definition (1 mark)**: Cell splitting is subdividing a macrocell into smaller cells to increase capacity. - **Explanation (2 marks)**: Discuss the 4-to-1 split, antenna height reduction, transmit power reduction, and frequency plan preservation. - **Diagram (1 mark)**: Show macrocell → 4 microcells. - **Derivation (3 marks)**: As shown in Section 3.1 above.
