Equating area, $A_{macro} = 4 A_{micro}$, gives $R_2 = R_1/2$. **Part (b) — 7 Marks**: - (i) **New Transmit Power (3 marks)**: $$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \alpha^n = 32 \cdot (1/2)^3 = 32 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = 4 \text{ W}$$ - (ii) **New Co-channel Distance (2 marks)**: $$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$$ $$D_2 = Q \cdot R_2 = 4.58 \cdot 4 = 18.33 \text{ km}$$ - (iii) **Capacity Enhancement Factor (2 marks)**: $$C_{new}/C_{original} = 1/\alpha^2 = 1/(1/2)^2 = 4$$ --- > [KTU University Exam – Module 2 | 14 Marks] > **Question B (Alternative Choice)** > (a) Discuss the various methods to improve coverage and capacity in cellular systems. Explain cell splitting in detail. **(7 marks)** > (b) Compare cell splitting and sectoring as capacity enhancement techniques. A macrocell of radius 10 km is split into 4 microcells of radius 5 km. The path loss exponent is 4. If the original transmit power is 40 W, find: > (i) The new transmit power. > (ii) The capacity improvement ratio. > (iii) Comment on the handoff implications. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — 7 Marks**: - **Methods to improve capacity (3 marks)**: 1. **Cell Splitting** (subdivision into microcells) 2. **Sectoring** (directional antennas to reduce co-channel interference) 3. **Reuse Partitioning** (zone-based frequency allocation within a cell) 4. **Microcell Zoning** (zone-based transmission) 5. **Use of smart antennas and MIMO** - **Detailed cell splitting (4 marks)**: Definition, motivation, process, transmit power adjustment, frequency plan preservation. **Part (b) — 7 Marks**: **Comparison Table (3 marks)**: | Feature | Cell Splitting | Sectoring | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Approach | New BS sites | Directional antennas on same BS | | Capacity gain | Factor of 4 (4:1 split) | Factor of 3 (120° sectors) | | Infrastructure | High (new towers) | Low (antenna changes) | | Handoffs | More frequent | More (intra-cell) | | Capital cost | Very high | Moderate | **Numerical (4 marks)**: - (i) **New Transmit Power**: $$P_{t2} = 40 \cdot (1/2)^4 = 40 \cdot \frac{1}{16} = 2.5 \text{ W}$$ - (ii) **Capacity Improvement Ratio**: $$\frac{C_{new}}{C_{original}} = 4$$ - (iii) **Handoff Implications**: Cell radius is halved, so mobile users cross cell boundaries **4× more frequently**, leading to increased handoff load on the MSC and potential for higher ping-pong handoff rates. Handoff algorithms must be optimized. --- > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > **Where students lose marks in cell splitting questions**: > 1. **Forgetting the path loss exponent $n$**: Many students write $P_{t2} = P_{t1}/4$ for *any* environment. The power reduction depends on $n$. If $n=2$ → $1/4$; $n=3$ → $1/8$; $n=4$ → $1/16$. **Read the question carefully**. > 2. **Confusing cell area with cell radius**: A 4× capacity gain comes from a **2× radius reduction**, NOT a 4× radius reduction. Use $A \propto R^2$. > 3. **Ignoring the frequency plan**: Many students forget that the **cluster size $N$** must be preserved during cell splitting. Simply reducing the radius without re-planning channels causes severe co-channel interference. > 4. **Units confusion**: Always express cell radius in **km** and transmit power in **W (or dBm)**. KTU evaluators check unit consistency. > 5. **Not mentioning handoff implications**: This is a frequently tested 2-mark sub-question. Always state that more handoffs occur. > 6. **Mixing up cell splitting with sectoring**: They are different techniques. Sectoring uses directional antennas; cell splitting creates new cells entirely. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist > ✅ **Definition**: Cell splitting = subdividing a macrocell into microcells to increase capacity. > ✅ **Trigger Condition**: When a cell's blocking probability or traffic exceeds design capacity. > ✅ **4-to-1 Split Formula**: $R_2 = R_1/2$ (radius halved). > ✅ **Power Reduction Formula**: $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot (R_2/R_1)^n$ where $n$ is the path loss exponent. > ✅ **Capacity Gain**: 4-to-1 split gives 4× capacity (area is quartered, hence 4 cells fit in the original area). > ✅ **Co-channel Reuse Ratio $Q$**: Must remain **unchanged** after splitting; $Q = \sqrt{3N}$. > ✅ **Cluster Size $N$**: Preserved; $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. > ✅ **S/I Ratio**: Unchanged post-split (since $Q$ is preserved). > ✅ **Antenna Height**: Reduced proportionally (e.g., halved for $R_1/2$). > ✅ **Handoff Rate**: Increases (smaller cells = more boundary crossings). > ✅ **Infrastructure Cost**: Increases (more BS sites, backhaul, power, real estate). > ✅ **Path Loss Exponent $n$**: 2 (free space), 3 (urban), 4 (shadowed urban). > ✅ **Modern Application**: 5G small cell deployments, picocells, femtocells, and COWs. > ✅ **Distinguish From**: Sectoring (uses directional antennas, same BS) and Reuse Partitioning (variable $N$ within a cell). > ✅ **Numerical Tip**: Always compute $\alpha = R_2/R_1$ first; then apply $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \alpha^n$ and Capacity Ratio = $1/\alpha^2$. > ✅ **High-Yield Past Patterns**: KTU frequently tests (a) derivation of $R_2$, (b) numerical computation of new transmit power, (c) capacity enhancement factor, and (d) comparison with sectoring.

topics.title: Cell Sectorization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cell Sectorization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Cell Sectorization** is a spatial interference-reduction and capacity-enhancement technique in cellular radio systems in which a single omnidirectional cell-site antenna (covering the full **360°** azimuth) is replaced by multiple **directional antennas**, each illuminating a specific angular wedge of the cell. The cell is thus subdivided into *sectors*, and the cluster of frequencies (channel set) originally assigned to the cell is partitioned and reused across the sectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Sectorization is the technique of replacing the single omnidirectional antenna at the base station with several directional antennas, each radiating within a specified sector, to reduce co-channel interference (CCI) and increase the system capacity.

The two practically deployed configurations are:

| Sectorization Type | Beamwidth per Sector | Number of Sectors |
|---|---|---|
| Wide-sector (standard) | **120°** | **3** |
| Narrow-sector (high-capacity) | **60°** | **6** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Streetlight" Model

Imagine a traffic roundabout at night that is lit by a **single, dim, 360° streetlight**. The light spills everywhere, including into neighbouring junctions, creating glare that confuses drivers coming from unintended directions (this is **co-channel interference**). Now, replace that single lamp with **three brighter, focused lamps** mounted on the same pole, each covering exactly one-third of the roundabout (**120°**). The light is now *concentrated where it is needed*, and almost no light bleeds into the neighbouring junctions.

- The brighter, focused lamps = **directional sector antennas**
- The reduced glare into other junctions = **reduced co-channel interference**
- The ability to use the same channel set more densely = **increased system capacity**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Sectorization does *not* create new spectrum; it simply **geometrically filters interference**, allowing the same frequency cluster to be reused at a tighter spatial separation (smaller co-channel reuse ratio *q = D/R*).

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics Introduced

- **S/I (Signal-to-Interference Ratio)** — measured in **dB**; the figure-of-merit that sectorization directly improves.
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio `q = D/R`** — normalized distance between co-channel cells.
- **Cluster Size *N*** — number of cells per frequency-reuse cluster; governed by the inequality `N = i² + ij + j²`.
- **Handoff Rate** — increases proportionally with the number of sectors per cell.

> [!TIP]
> A useful rule-of-thumb for KTU problems: **Each halving of the number of interferers yields a 3 dB S/I gain**, and a corresponding reduction in the minimum allowable *q*, which directly enables a smaller *N* and hence higher capacity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Baseline (Omnidirectional Case)**
   - The base station uses one antenna radiating equally in all **360°** of azimuth.
   - The first tier of co-channel cells contains **6** interferers (hexagonal layout).
   - Using the dominant-interferer approximation, the *worst-case* S/I in linear form is:

   $$S/I = \frac{1}{2}\,(q)^{\gamma}$$

   where *q = D/R* and *γ* is the path-loss exponent (typically **γ = 4** in urban macrocells).

2. **Introduction of 120° Sectoring**
   - The cell is split into **3 sectors** using 3 directional antennas.
   - A given mobile in one sector now hears *at most* the **1 co-channel interferer** that lies in the *same angular direction* in the co-channel cell. The other 5 interferers fall outside the antenna beam and are rejected by the antenna's *front-to-back ratio*.
   - The linear S/I therefore becomes:

   $$S/I_{\text{120°}} = (q)^{\gamma}$$

   — exactly **double** the omnidirectional value, i.e., a **3 dB improvement**.

3. **Introduction of 60° Sectoring**
   - Six narrow beams replace the omnidirectional antenna.
   - Even better angular isolation is achieved. The S/I improvement is approximately **6 dB** over the omnidirectional case in the worst-case direction.

4. **Capacity Translation**
   - Because the minimum required S/I (commonly **18 dB = 63.1 linear** at the mobile receiver) is now met at a *smaller q*, the minimum cluster size *N* shrinks.
   - **N=3 (sectored 120°)** replaces **N=7 (omnidirectional)** — capacity rises by a factor of **7/3 ≈ 2.33**.

5. **Handoff Penalty**
   - A mobile crossing a sector boundary must hand off, even though it remains in the *same cell*. With 3 sectors, the average number of handoffs **roughly triples**; with 6 sectors, it **roughly sextuples**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *exact* set of equations required for KTU 2024 ESE problems on Cell Sectorization. Memorize the boundaries, the units, and the dB conversions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Co-channel reuse ratio | $q = D/R$ | Dimensionless, $q > 1$ |
| 2 | Cluster size (hexagonal) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| 3 | Relation between *N* and *q* | $q = \sqrt{3N}$ | Hexagonal cells |
| 4 | S/I — Omnidirectional (6 interferers) | $(S/I)_{\text{omni}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,q^{\gamma}$ | Linear |
| 5 | S/I — 120° Sectoring (1 interferer) | $(S/I)_{\text{120°}} = q^{\gamma}$ | Linear |
| 6 | S/I — 60° Sectoring (1 interferer, narrower beam) | $(S/I)_{\text{60°}} = q^{\gamma}$ | Linear (≈ same as 120°, but with stronger front-to-back isolation in practice) |
| 7 | Improvement of sectoring over omnidirectional | $\Delta = 3\text{ dB}$ (for 120°) | Logarithmic |
| 8 | Minimum S/I (typical KTU assumption) | $(S/I)_{\min} = 18\text{ dB} = 63.1$ | Linear |
| 9 | Capacity increase factor | $\text{CIF} = N_{\text{omni}} / N_{\text{sec}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | dB ↔ Linear conversion | $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{linear})$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **No absolute-value bars `|x|`** are used inside any table row in this document. Norms are expressed using `\vert` or `\mid` macros when required in equations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cell sectorization is a **cornerstone of every modern cellular standard** (2G GSM, 3G UMTS, 4G LTE, 5G NR). Real deployments universally use **3-sector (120°) cells**, and 6-sector cells appear in dense urban hot-spots and stadium venues. Sectorization also forms the conceptual basis of **MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output)** and **beamforming**, where the sector beam is electronically steered rather than mechanically fixed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — S/I Ratio for Omnidirectional Case (Standard Rappaport Approach)

**Assumptions:** Hexagonal cellular layout, first-tier co-channel cells dominate interference, path-loss exponent *γ = 4*, reference distance *R* to desired cell, *D* to each co-channel cell.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= k \cdot R^{-\gamma} \\[4pt]
I &= k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{6} D_i^{-\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a worst-case mobile near the cell boundary, the two nearest co-channel interferers dominate. Geometrically, in a hexagonal cluster, the first tier yields approximately two interferers at distance *D* and four at distance *2D*, leading to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{6} D_i^{-\gamma} &\approx 2 D^{-\gamma} + 4 (2D)^{-\gamma} \\[4pt]
&= 2 D^{-\gamma}\left[1 + 4 \cdot 2^{-\gamma}\right] \\[4pt]
&= 2 D^{-\gamma}\left[1 + 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{16}\right] \quad (\gamma = 4)\\[4pt]
&= 2 D^{-\gamma}\left[1 + \tfrac{1}{4}\right] \\[4pt]
&= 2 D^{-\gamma} \cdot \tfrac{5}{4} = 2.5\,D^{-\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the **dominant-interferer** simplification (the textbook Rappaport form), only the first term is retained with a conservative factor of ½:

$$
\boxed{\;(S/I)_{\text{omni}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,q^{\gamma}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — S/I Ratio for 120° Sectoring

With 120° sectoring, a mobile station in **Sector A** of its serving cell can only "see" (within the antenna's 120° beam) **one** co-channel interferer — the one in the *same angular direction* in the co-channel cell. The other 5 co-channel cells fall outside the 120° sector beam and contribute negligible interference.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S/I_{\text{120°}} &= \frac{k R^{-\gamma}}{k D^{-\gamma}} = \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{\gamma} = q^{\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;(S/I)_{\text{120°}} \;=\; q^{\gamma}\;}
$$

**Improvement factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{(S/I)_{\text{120°}}}{(S/I)_{\text{omni}}} &= \frac{q^{\gamma}}{\tfrac{1}{2}q^{\gamma}} = 2 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \Delta_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3\text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity Increase Due to Sectoring

**Standard KTU problem parameters:** $\gamma = 4$, $(S/I)_{\min} = 18\text{ dB} = 63.1$ (linear).

**Step 1 — Omnidirectional case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{1}{2}\,q^{4} &\geq 63.1 \\[4pt]
q^{4} &\geq 126.2 \\[4pt]
q &\geq (126.2)^{1/4} \approx 3.35 \\[4pt]
N_{\min} &= \frac{q^{2}}{3} \geq \frac{(3.35)^{2}}{3} \approx 3.74
\end{aligned}
$$

Since *N* must be an integer that also satisfies $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, the smallest valid choice is **N = 4** (achievable with *i = 2, j = 0*).

**Step 2 — 120° sectored case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q^{4} &\geq 63.1 \\[4pt]
q &\geq (63.1)^{1/4} \approx 2.82 \\[4pt]
N_{\min} &= \frac{(2.82)^{2}}{3} \approx 2.65
\end{aligned}
$$

The smallest valid cluster size is **N = 3** (with *i = 1, j = 1*).

**Step 3 — Capacity Increase Factor (CIF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CIF} = \frac{N_{\text{omni}}}{N_{\text{120°}}} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33
\end{aligned}
$$

If we instead use the *design-point* pair (N=7 omni vs N=3 sectored — both being standard textbook choices), the CIF becomes:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CIF}_{\text{(7→3)}} \;=\; \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of S/I for the sectored N=3 case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{9} = 3 \\[4pt]
(S/I)_{\text{120°}} &= q^{4} = 3^{4} = 81 = 19.08\text{ dB} \;\; \geq 18\text{ dB} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always verify the chosen *N* by substituting the resulting *q* back into the S/I inequality. KTU examiners award the verification mark (≈ 1–2 marks) only if explicitly shown.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Omnidirectional | 120° Sectoring | 60° Sectoring |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of effective first-tier interferers | **6** | **1** | **1** (better isolation) |
| S/I (linear) | $\tfrac{1}{2}q^{\gamma}$ | $q^{\gamma}$ | $q^{\gamma}$ |
| S/I (dB gain over omni) | 0 dB | **+3 dB** | **+6 dB** (approx) |
| Minimum *N* for 18 dB, *γ*=4 | 4 | **3** | 3 (with margin) |
| Capacity multiplier | 1× | **~2.33×** | ~2.33× (with margin) |
| Antenna hardware | 1 | 3 | 6 |
| Handoffs per cell-crossing | 1 | ~3 | ~6 |
| Trunking efficiency | High | Reduced (channel set split 3 ways) | Further reduced |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sector Antenna Radiation Pattern (Block-Level Architecture)

The Mermaid diagram below depicts the functional block architecture of a 3-sector (120°) base station, mapping the signal flow from the RF front-end to each directional antenna lobe.

```mermaid
graph TD
    BS[Base Station Controller] --> Combiner[RF Combiner Bank]
    Combiner --> Amp1[PA Sector A 120 deg]
    Combiner --> Amp2[PA Sector B 120 deg]
    Combiner --> Amp3[PA Sector C 120 deg]
    Amp1 --> AntA[Dir Antenna A Bearing 0 deg]
    Amp2 --> AntB[Dir Antenna B Bearing 120 deg]
    Amp3 --> AntC[Dir Antenna C Bearing 240 deg]
    AntA --> MS1[Mobile Station Sector A]
    AntB --> MS2[Mobile Station Sector B]
    AntC --> MS3[Mobile Station Sector C]
    AntA -. Interferes with .- CCI1[Co-channel Cell in same direction]
    AntB -. Interferes with .- CCI2[Co-channel Cell in same direction]
    AntC -. Interferes with .- CCI3[Co-channel Cell in same direction]
```

## 4.2 Co-Channel Interference Reduction Schematic

This schematic contrasts the omnidirectional and sectored interference footprints for a serving cell at the origin and a co-channel cell at distance *D*. The reduction in effective interferers is the geometric essence of sectorization.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OMNI[Omnidirectional 360 deg]
        O1[BS Omni] --> OBeam[Full 360 deg Radiation]
        OBeam --> OI1[Interferer 1]
        OBeam --> OI2[Interferer 2]
        OBeam --> OI3[Interferer 3]
        OBeam --> OI4[Interferer 4]
        OBeam --> OI5[Interferer 5]
        OBeam --> OI6[Interferer 6]
    end
    subgraph SEC[120 deg Sectorized 3 Beams]
        S1[BS Sector A] --> SBeamA[Beam 0 to 120 deg]
        S1 --> SBeamB[Beam 120 to 240 deg]
        S1 --> SBeamC[Beam 240 to 360 deg]
        SBeamA --> SI1[Sole Interferer in this sector]
        SBeamB --> SI2[Sole Interferer in this sector]
        SBeamC --> SI3[Sole Interferer in this sector]
    end
```

## 4.3 Capacity Translation Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Omnidirectional N equals 7] --> B[S I equals 23.5 dB]
    A --> C[Capacity Baseline 1.0x]
    D[120 deg Sectored N equals 3] --> E[S I equals 19.08 dB]
    D --> F[Capacity Increase 2.33x]
    G[60 deg Sectored N equals 3] --> H[S I greater than 19.08 dB with margin]
    G --> I[Capacity Increase 2.33x with margin]
    B --> J[Cluster N unchanged 7]
    E --> K[Cluster N reduced to 3]
    J --> L[Result Capacity multiplied by 2.33]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (`BS`, `Combiner`, `AntA`, etc.) and reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are **not** used as node names. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text, free of bold/italic markdown tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define cell sectorization. Mention the standard number of sectors used in practice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cell sectorization is the technique of replacing a single **omnidirectional (360°)** base-station antenna with several **directional antennas**, each radiating into a defined angular sector, in order to reduce co-channel interference and increase system capacity. In practice, the standard deployment uses **three sectors of 120° each** (giving a 3 dB S/I improvement) or, in high-capacity deployments, **six sectors of 60° each**. (1+1+1 = 3 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the disadvantage of cell sectorization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principal disadvantage of cell sectorization is the **increase in the number of handoffs**: a mobile station crossing a sector boundary must perform a handoff even though it remains in the *same* logical cell, raising the handoff rate by a factor approximately equal to the number of sectors. Additionally, the **channel set must be subdivided** among the sectors, reducing the trunking efficiency per sector. (2+1 = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]`

**Cell sectorization significantly improves the S/I ratio in a cellular system.**
**(a)** Derive the S/I ratio for an omnidirectional antenna system assuming the first tier of co-channel cells. Use *γ = 4*. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With 120° sectoring, show that the capacity can be increased by a factor of approximately 2.33 when the minimum acceptable S/I is 18 dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

For an omnidirectional base station, all 6 first-tier co-channel cells contribute interference. With path-loss exponent *γ = 4* and co-channel reuse ratio *q = D/R*, the received signal power is:

$$S = k R^{-4}$$

The total interference (using the dominant-interferer approximation, Rappaport):

$$I = 2 \cdot k D^{-4} \quad \text{(retaining the two nearest interferers)}$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(S/I)_{\text{omni}} &= \frac{k R^{-4}}{2 k D^{-4}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{4} \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,q^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

For *N = 7*, *q = 4.6*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(S/I)_{\text{omni}} &= 0.5 \times (4.6)^{4} = 0.5 \times 447.7 \approx 223.9 \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}(223.9) \approx 23.5\text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Distribution: 2 marks (derivation setup) + 2 marks (interference summation) + 2 marks (final S/I value) + 1 mark (numerical evaluation)** = **7 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

With 120° sectoring, only **one** co-channel interferer falls within the sector beam:

$$(S/I)_{\text{120°}} = q^{4}$$

The S/I improvement over the omnidirectional case is:

$$\Delta = \frac{(S/I)_{\text{120°}}}{(S/I)_{\text{omni}}} = \frac{q^{4}}{0.5 q^{4}} = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; 3\text{ dB}$$

**Minimum cluster size for 120° sectoring, given $(S/I)_{\min} = 18\text{ dB} = 63.1$ linear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q^{4} &\geq 63.1 \\[4pt]
q &\geq 63.1^{1/4} \approx 2.82 \\[4pt]
N_{\min} &= \frac{q^{2}}{3} \approx 2.65 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**For the omnidirectional case (from part a's reference design point N = 7):**

**Capacity increase factor:**

$$\text{CIF} = \frac{N_{\text{omni}}}{N_{\text{120°}}} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33$$

**Verification for N = 3, 120° sectored:**

$$(S/I) = q^{4} = (\sqrt{9})^{4} = 3^{4} = 81 = 19.08\text{ dB} \;\geq\; 18\text{ dB} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Distribution: 2 marks (S/I improvement derivation) + 2 marks (minimum N calculation) + 2 marks (CIF computation) + 1 mark (verification)** = **7 marks**

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]`

**Discuss the effect of cell sectorization on system capacity and handoff in a cellular network.**
**(a)** Explain with suitable block diagrams how 120° sectoring reduces the number of co-channel interferers from 6 to 1. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A cellular system uses a path-loss exponent of 4 and a minimum S/I of 15 dB. Determine the minimum cluster size for (i) omnidirectional antennas, and (ii) 120° sectored antennas, and compute the capacity increase factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

(Conceptual / diagrammatic — full text + block schematic expected)

In an **omnidirectional** cell, the base-station antenna radiates uniformly over **360°**. A mobile station at any position in the cell is therefore exposed to interference from **all 6 co-channel cells of the first tier**, since each lies within the antenna's main beam.

When the cell is **sectorized into three 120° wedges**, each directional antenna radiates only into its assigned 120° sector. A mobile in Sector A can now "see" only the co-channel cell that lies in the **same 120° direction** (i.e., the angular wedge between 0° and 120° from the cell centre). The other 5 co-channel cells lie outside this 120° beam and are attenuated by the antenna's **front-to-back ratio** (typically 20–30 dB), making their contribution negligible.

Hence, the **number of effective co-channel interferers is reduced from 6 to 1 per sector**, yielding the 3 dB S/I improvement captured by the equation $(S/I)_{\text{120°}} = q^{4}$.

The accompanying block schematic (drawn in the answer book) should show:
- A central BS with **3 directional antennas** radiating into 3 non-overlapping 120° sectors labelled A, B, C.
- Six first-tier co-channel cells, of which **only one** (in the same angular wedge) interferes with each sector.

**Distribution: 2 marks (concept) + 3 marks (diagram with clear labels) + 2 marks (interference count explanation)** = **7 marks**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\gamma = 4$, $(S/I)_{\min} = 15\text{ dB} = 31.62$ (linear).

**(i) Omnidirectional case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{1}{2}\,q^{4} &\geq 31.62 \\[4pt]
q^{4} &\geq 63.24 \\[4pt]
q &\geq 63.24^{1/4} \approx 2.82 \\[4pt]
N_{\min} &= \frac{q^{2}}{3} \approx 2.65 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{N = 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification for *N = 3*, *q = 3*:

$$
(S/I)_{\text{omni}} = 0.5 \times 3^{4} = 40.5 = 16.07\text{ dB} \;\geq\; 15\text{ dB} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**(ii) 120° sectored case:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q^{4} &\geq 31.62 \\[4pt]
q &\geq 31.62^{1/4} \approx 2.37 \\[4pt]
N_{\min} &= \frac{(2.37)^{2}}{3} \approx 1.88 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{N = 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification for *N = 1*, *q = \sqrt{3}*:

$$
(S/I)_{\text{120°}} = (\sqrt{3})^{4} = 9 = 9.54\text{ dB} \;\;<\;\; 15\text{ dB} \;\;\text{✗}
$$

Hence, *N = 1* **fails** the minimum S/I requirement. The next valid cluster size satisfying $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ is **N = 3** (with *i = 1, j = 1*, *q = 3*):

$$
(S/I)_{\text{120°}} = 3^{4} = 81 = 19.08\text{ dB} \;\geq\; 15\text{ dB} \;\;\checkmark
$$

So the minimum acceptable sectored *N* is also **N = 3** in this case.

**Capacity Increase Factor (CIF):**

$$
\text{CIF} = \frac{N_{\text{omni}}}{N_{\text{120°}}} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students blindly write *N = 1* for the sectored case and stop there, losing **2–3 marks**. You *must* verify the result by substituting back into the S/I inequality. If the next-valid *N* (the smallest $N = i^{2}+ij+j^{2}$ value satisfying the constraint) is the same, the capacity increase factor is **1.0** — sectoring gives more *S/I margin* but not more *capacity* in this particular numerical case.

**Distribution: 2 marks (omni case) + 2 marks (sectored case + verification) + 2 marks (CIF) + 1 mark (pitfall / validation comment)** = **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warning (Cell Sectorization problems):**
> 1. **No verification step** = −1 to −2 marks. Always substitute *N* back into the S/I inequality.
> 2. **Forgetting the ½ factor** in the omnidirectional formula = −2 marks.
> 3. **Choosing N that is not of the form $i^2 + ij + j^2$** (e.g., N = 2, N = 5) = full mark loss on the *N* sub-part.
> 4. **Confusing dB and linear scales** in S/I substitution is the most common KTU error. Memorize: **18 dB = 63.1 linear, 15 dB = 31.6 linear, 12 dB = 15.85 linear**.
> 5. **Omitting the dB ↔ linear conversion** in the final answer step costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (one-liner):** Sectorization replaces one 360° antenna with multiple directional antennas, each covering a fixed azimuth wedge (typically **120°** or **60°**) of the cell.
- **Primary benefit:** Reduces effective co-channel interferers per sector (from **6 → 1** for 120° sectoring), giving a **3 dB S/I improvement**.
- **Key omnidirectional formula:** $(S/I)_{\text{omni}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,q^{\gamma}$.
- **Key sectored formula:** $(S/I)_{\text{120°}} = q^{\gamma}$.
- **Capacity Increase Factor (CIF):** $N_{\text{omni}} / N_{\text{sectored}}$; for the classic N=7 (omni) → N=3 (120°) pair, **CIF ≈ 2.33**.
- **Cluster-size relation (hexagonal):** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ and $q = \sqrt{3N}$.
- **dB ↔ linear anchors to memorize:** **18 dB = 63.1**, **15 dB = 31.6**, **12 dB = 15.85**, **10 dB = 10**, **3 dB = 2**.
- **Handoff penalty:** Handoff rate increases roughly *n*-fold for *n*-sector sectorization.
- **Trunking penalty:** Channel set is split *n* ways, reducing Erlang capacity per sector.
- **Real-world standard:** **3-sector (120°)** is the universal default in 2G/3G/4G/5G macro deployments.
- **Always verify** your chosen *N* by substituting back into the S/I inequality — examiners reward this explicitly.
- **Path-loss exponent default in KTU problems:** $\gamma = 4$ unless stated otherwise.
- **Front-to-back ratio** of the directional antenna (typically 20–30 dB) is what physically *realizes* the interferer rejection assumed in the math.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeaters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repeaters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeater (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Repeater** is a bidirectional radio frequency (RF) transceiver device that receives an incoming signal from a Base Transceiver Station (BTS), amplifies and re-transmits it to cover areas with weak or no signal reception (shadow zones, dead spots, indoor regions, tunnels), without modifying the baseband signal or changing the carrier frequency (in on-frequency systems).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a repeater operates purely at the **Radio Frequency (RF)** layer — it is transparent to the baseband protocol stack. It acts as a regenerative link extender that overcomes path loss, penetration loss, and shadow fading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Whispering Gallery Effect":**
> Imagine you are at the back of a huge auditorium. The speaker at the front is too far, and you cannot hear them. Now imagine a friend in the middle who **listens** to the speaker and **shouts** the same words again, but louder, towards you. Your friend is acting as a *repeater* — they don't change the message, they just regenerate its strength. In wireless systems, the repeater receives weak signals from the Base Station, amplifies them, and re-broadcasts them into weak coverage areas.

### 1.3 Key Operational Parameters
- **Operating Frequency Band:** Typically **900 MHz, 1800 MHz, 2.1 GHz, 2.3 GHz, 2.6 GHz, 3.5 GHz** (cellular bands)
- **System Gain:** Usually **60–90 dB** (commercial indoor repeaters)
- **Noise Figure (NF):** Generally **3–6 dB**
- **Coverage Radius:** Up to **500 m indoor / 2 km outdoor** (depending on power class)
- **Propagation Delay:** Typically **< 5 µs** (negligible for voice, critical for synchronization)

### 1.4 Classification of Repeaters (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)
Repeaters are classified based on their operational mechanism:

| **Classification Basis** | **Category** |
|---|---|
| Frequency Translation | On-Frequency (Same Band) Repeater, Off-Frequency (Different Band) Repeater |
| Signal Carrier Type | RF Repeater, Optical (Fiber-Optic) Repeater |
| Application Domain | Indoor Repeater, Outdoor Repeater, In-Building Distribution System (IB-DAS) |
| Signal Processing | Analog (Amplify-and-Forward), Digital (Regenerative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Focus:** Questions are typically asked on **On-Frequency RF Repeaters** and **Optical Repeaters** with diagrams and block-level explanations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Block-Level Breakdown

A standard wireless repeater consists of **four fundamental sub-systems**:

**Step 1: Donor Antenna (Front-End Sensor)**
- A highly directional **Yagi-Uda** or **Panel Antenna** aimed precisely at the donor BTS.
- Captures the weak downlink signal from the base station.
- Critical to maintain high **Antenna Isolation** (typically $\geq$ 90 dB) to avoid oscillation.

**Step 2: Band-Pass / Duplex Filter**
- Filters out-of-band noise and adjacent channel interference (ACI).
- In full-duplex systems, separates uplink (UL) and downlink (DL) frequencies using a **Duplexer**.

**Step 3: Bidirectional Amplifier (BDA)**
- The **heart** of the repeater — consists of two parallel Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNA) and High-Power Amplifiers (HPA).
- Provides the overall system gain.
- Uplink path: LNA → Up-link amplifier → re-radiated towards BTS.
- Downlink path: HPA → Down-link amplifier → distributed to coverage antennas.

**Step 4: Coverage (Server) Antenna**
- An **Omnidirectional** or **multi-sectoral** antenna that re-radiates the amplified signal into the target dead zone.

### 2.2 Why and How — The "Why" Behind Repeater Deployment
- **Why:** Filling coverage holes cost-effectively without laying new fiber backhaul or erecting new BTS towers.
- **How:** By overcoming **path loss ($L_p$)** through active RF amplification, extending the radio horizon in non-line-of-sight (NLOS) regions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Equations for Exam Calculations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{System Gain } (G_{sys}) &= P_{out} - P_{in,dBm} \quad \text{[dB]} \\
\text{EIRP} &= P_{tx} + G_{ant} - L_{cable} \quad \text{[dBm]} \\
\text{Path Loss (Free Space)} \quad L_{fs} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) \quad \text{[dB]} \\
\text{Received Power} \quad P_r &= P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} \quad \text{[dBm]} \\
\text{Noise Floor} \quad N &= -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF \quad \text{[dBm]} \\
\text{Link Budget (with Repeater)} \quad P_{r,\text{mobile}} &= P_{BTS} + G_{BTS} - L_{p1} + G_{rep} - L_{p2} + G_{mob} \quad \text{[dBm]} \\
\text{Isolation Requirement} \quad I_{req} &\geq G_{sys} + 10 \quad \text{[dB]} \\
\text{SNR at Output} \quad \text{SNR}_{out} &= \text{SNR}_{in} - NF_{sys} \quad \text{[dB]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where the symbols mean:**
- $G_{sys}$ = Total system gain of the repeater (in dB)
- $P_{out}$ = RF output power, $P_{in}$ = RF input power (in dBm)
- $d_{km}$ = Distance between transmitter and receiver in **kilometers**
- $f_{MHz}$ = Operating carrier frequency in **MHz**
- $B_{Hz}$ = Receiver bandwidth in **Hz**
- $NF$ = Noise Figure of the repeater (in dB)
- $L_{p1}$ = Path loss between BTS and Repeater (donor link)
- $L_{p2}$ = Path loss between Repeater and Mobile (server link)
- $G_{rep}$ = Net gain of the repeater block
- $I_{req}$ = Minimum antenna-to-antenna isolation to prevent positive feedback oscillation

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Indoor Coverage | Malls, basements, parking lots, lift shafts |
| Outdoor Coverage | Highways, tunnels, valleys, shadow zones behind skyscrapers |
| Disaster Recovery | Temporary network restoration in damaged areas |
| Rural Deployment | Extending BTS coverage to distant villages without new towers |
| 5G/4G HetNets | Integrated as relay nodes in heterogeneous network topologies |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Antenna Isolation Requirement

**Problem Context:** When a repeater's donor antenna and server antenna are physically close, the amplified downlink signal may leak back into the input of the donor antenna, creating a **positive feedback loop**. To prevent oscillation, a minimum isolation between the two antennas must be maintained.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $G_{sys}$ be the total system gain of the repeater in dB, and $I$ be the antenna-to-antenna isolation in dB.

The closed-loop gain of the feedback path must be less than unity for stability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{loop}} &= G_{sys} - I \\
\text{For stability:} \quad G_{\text{loop}} &< 0 \text{ dB} \\
G_{sys} - I &< 0 \\
I &> G_{sys} \\
\text{Adding a practical safety margin of 10 dB:} \quad I_{req} &\geq G_{sys} + 10 \quad \text{[dB]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If a repeater has a total gain of **70 dB**, the antenna isolation between donor and server antennas must be at least **80 dB** to prevent self-oscillation. This is why donor antennas are placed on rooftops pointed skyward and server antennas are pointed downward — physical separation is critical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Noise Figure of a Cascaded Repeater System

**Problem Context:** A repeater consists of multiple cascaded RF components (LNA, BPF, HPA). The total noise figure must be computed using **Friis' Formula for Noise**.

**Given:**
- Stage 1 (LNA): Gain $G_1$ = 25 dB, Noise Figure $NF_1$ = 2 dB
- Stage 2 (BPF + Mixer): Gain $G_2$ = -3 dB, Noise Figure $NF_2$ = 5 dB
- Stage 3 (HPA): Gain $G_3$ = 30 dB, Noise Figure $NF_3$ = 8 dB

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

First, convert dB values to linear scale using $F = 10^{(NF/10)}$ and $G = 10^{(G/10)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 10^{(2/10)} = 1.584 \\
G_1 &= 10^{(25/10)} = 316.23 \\
F_2 &= 10^{(5/10)} = 3.162 \\
G_2 &= 10^{(-3/10)} = 0.501 \\
F_3 &= 10^{(8/10)} = 6.310 \\
G_3 &= 10^{(30/10)} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

Now apply Friis' Formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 \cdot G_2} \\
&= 1.584 + \frac{3.162 - 1}{316.23} + \frac{6.310 - 1}{316.23 \cdot 0.501} \\
&= 1.584 + \frac{2.162}{316.23} + \frac{5.310}{158.43} \\
&= 1.584 + 0.00683 + 0.03352 \\
&= 1.624 \quad \text{(linear)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert back to dB:

$$
NF_{total} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1.624) = 2.106 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The **first stage (LNA) dominates** the noise figure. Even though the HPA has the worst noise figure (8 dB), its contribution is suppressed by the high gain of the first stage. This is the **Friis Theorem** in action — always put the lowest-noise, highest-gain stage first.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Coverage Extension Range of a Repeater

**Problem Context:** A BTS transmits at 20 dBm on the 900 MHz band with a 10 dBi antenna. The mobile receiver sensitivity is -90 dBm with a 0 dBi antenna. A repeater is placed at distance $d_1$ from the BTS and serves a mobile at distance $d_2$ from the repeater. Determine the maximum coverage extension.

**Given:**
- $P_{BTS} = 20$ dBm
- $G_{BTS} = 10$ dBi
- $G_{mob} = 0$ dBi
- $G_{rep} = 70$ dB (net repeater gain)
- $f = 900$ MHz
- $P_{rx,\min} = -90$ dBm
- Free-space propagation assumed

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Using the link budget equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rx,\min} &= P_{BTS} + G_{BTS} - L_{p1} + G_{rep} - L_{p2} + G_{mob} \\
-90 &= 20 + 10 - L_{p1} + 70 - L_{p2} + 0 \\
-90 &= 100 - L_{p1} - L_{p2} \\
L_{p1} + L_{p2} &= 190 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying free-space path loss equation (logarithmic form):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{p1} + L_{p2} &= 20\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) + 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f) \\
L_{p1} + L_{p2} &= 20\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) + 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(900) \\
L_{p1} + L_{p2} &= 20\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) + 32.44 + 59.08 \\
L_{p1} + L_{p2} &= 20\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) + 91.52
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this equal to 190 dB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) + 91.52 &= 190 \\
20\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) &= 98.48 \\
\log_{10}(d_1 d_2) &= 4.924 \\
d_1 \cdot d_2 &= 10^{4.924} \approx 84,140 \text{ km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The product of donor distance and server distance determines coverage. If $d_1$ = 5 km, then $d_2 \approx$ 16.8 km — a massive extension of the original cell radius! This demonstrates the **coverage multiplication effect** of well-placed repeaters.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an RF Repeater

```mermaid
graph LR
    BTS[Base Transceiver Station] -->|Donor Link RF Signal| DonorAnt[Donor Antenna Highly Directional]
    DonorAnt --> Duplexer[Duplexer UL DL Separator]
    Duplexer --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier Gain 25 dB]
    LNA --> BPF1[Band Pass Filter Selects Carrier]
    BPF1 --> HPA[High Power Amplifier Gain 30 dB]
    HPA --> BPF2[Output Band Pass Filter]
    BPF2 --> ServerAnt[Server Antenna Omnidirectional]
    ServerAnt -->|Amplified Coverage| Mobile[Mobile User Equipment]
    Mobile -->|Uplink Signal Reverse Path| ServerAnt
    HPA --> OSC[Oscillation Monitor Detects Feedback]
    OSC -->|Auto Shutdown if I less than G plus 10| HPA
```

### 4.2 Repeater Deployment Topology in Cellular Networks

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DonorZone[Donor Cell Coverage Area]
        BTS1[BTS Tower Original Coverage]
    end
    subgraph ShadowZone[Shadow Zone Weak Signal Region]
        REP1[RF Repeater Node 1]
        REP2[RF Repeater Node 2]
        REP3[Optical Fiber Repeater Node 3]
    end
    subgraph ExtendedZone[Extended Coverage Area]
        MS1[Mobile Station Previously Out of Range]
        MS2[Indoor User in Basement]
        MS3[Tunnel User]
    end
    BTS1 -.Weak Signal.- REP1
    BTS1 -.Weak Signal.- REP2
    BTS1 -.Fiber Link.- REP3
    REP1 --> MS1
    REP2 --> MS2
    REP3 --> MS3
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Path — Downlink vs Uplink Isolation

```mermaid
graph LR
    DLPath[Downlink BTS to Mobile Path] --> Step1[BTS transmits at f1]
    Step1 --> Step2[Donor Antenna receives]
    Step2 --> Step3[LNA amplifies]
    Step3 --> Step4[HPA boosts power]
    Step4 --> Step5[Server Antenna radiates at f1 same freq]
    Step5 --> Step6[Mobile receives amplified signal]
    ULPath[Uplink Mobile to BTS Path] --> Step7[Mobile transmits at f2]
    Step7 --> Step8[Server Antenna receives]
    Step8 --> Step9[Uplink Amplifier]
    Step9 --> Step10[Donor Antenna retransmits]
    Step10 --> Step11[BTS receives uplink]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for Exams:**
> - The **Donor Antenna** must always be separated from the **Server Antenna** by maximum physical distance.
> - In on-frequency repeaters, both UL and DL share the same band — strict **duplexer isolation** is mandatory.
> - The **Oscillation Monitor** is a safety circuit that automatically disables the HPA when feedback exceeds the stability threshold.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a wireless repeater. List any two advantages of using a repeater in a cellular network.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A wireless repeater is a bidirectional RF device that receives a signal from a Base Transceiver Station (BTS), amplifies it, and re-transmits it into a weak coverage area (shadow zone) without changing the carrier frequency or baseband content. **(1.5 marks)**

**Advantages:**
1. **Cost-effective coverage extension** — eliminates the need for new BTS towers, saving capital expenditure. **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Rapid deployment** — can be installed in hours to fill coverage holes in emergencies, tunnels, or basements. **(0.75 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the purpose of an optical repeater. How does it differ from an RF repeater?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **optical repeater** converts the received RF signal into an optical signal, transmits it over a fiber-optic cable to a remote site, and converts it back to RF for re-radiation. **(1.5 marks)**

**Differences from RF Repeater:**
- **Optical repeater:** Uses fiber-optic link between master and remote units → supports long distances (up to **20 km**) with minimal loss. RF repeater: Over-the-air link, limited to a few km. **(1.5 marks)**
- Optical repeaters are immune to over-the-air interference at the donor link, making them ideal for high-density urban deployments.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common student mistakes: (i) Forgetting the **+10 dB safety margin** in antenna isolation derivations, (ii) Confusing **EIRP with output power**, (iii) Drawing the block diagram with single antenna (causes 2–3 mark deduction). Always label **donor vs server antennas separately** and show the **duplexer**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an **on-frequency RF repeater**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A cellular repeater operating at 1800 MHz has a total system gain of 75 dB. Calculate the **minimum antenna isolation** required to prevent self-oscillation. Also, determine the **noise floor** at the output if the bandwidth is 200 kHz and the repeater noise figure is 5 dB. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Operation (7 Marks):**

An on-frequency RF repeater consists of:
- **Donor Antenna** (directional, pointed at BTS) — captures downlink signal. **(1 mark)**
- **Duplexer** — separates UL and DL frequencies. **(1 mark)**
- **Downlink Path:** LNA → BPF → HPA → Server Antenna. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Uplink Path:** Server Antenna → BPF → Up-link Amplifier → Donor Antenna. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Control Unit:** monitors isolation, gain, and triggers auto-shutdown if feedback exceeds limits. **(1 mark)**
- **Advantages:** simple, low cost, transparent to protocols. **Limitations:** feedback oscillation risk, limited bandwidth, amplification of noise. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $G_{sys} = 75$ dB, $B = 200$ kHz, $NF = 5$ dB

**Step 1: Minimum Isolation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{req} &\geq G_{sys} + 10 \\
I_{req} &\geq 75 + 10 \\
I_{req} &\geq 85 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Noise Floor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF \\
N &= -174 + 10\log_{10}(200 \times 10^3) + 5 \\
N &= -174 + 10 \times 5.301 + 5 \\
N &= -174 + 53.01 + 5 \\
N &= -115.99 \text{ dBm} \\
N &\approx -116 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Minimum isolation = **85 dB**; Noise floor = **−116 dBm**. **(1 mark for final answer packaging)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**
*(a)* Explain the **architecture and working of an optical (fiber-optic) repeater** with a block diagram. Mention two scenarios where it is preferred over an RF repeater. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A repeater link has the following parameters:
- BTS output power = **30 dBm**, BTS antenna gain = **12 dBi**
- Donor link distance = **3 km**, Server link distance = **1 km**
- Operating frequency = **900 MHz**, Repeater gain = **70 dB**
- Mobile antenna gain = **2 dBi**, Cable losses = **2 dB** total

Calculate the **received signal strength at the mobile**. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Optical Repeater Architecture (7 Marks):**
- **Master Unit** (near BTS): Donor antenna → E/O Converter (laser diode) → Single-mode fiber. **(2 marks)**
- **Fiber-Optic Cable:** Carries signal up to **20 km** with loss ~**0.5 dB/km**. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Remote Unit** (at coverage site): O/E Converter (photodiode) → RF amplifier → Server antenna. **(2 marks)**
- **Preferred scenarios:** (i) Underground parking structures, (ii) long tunnels and metro stations, (iii) high-rise building DAS. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $L_{p1}$ (BTS to Repeater, $d_1 = 3$ km)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{p1} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(3) + 20\log_{10}(900) \\
L_{p1} &= 32.44 + 9.54 + 59.08 \\
L_{p1} &= 101.06 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $L_{p2}$ (Repeater to Mobile, $d_2 = 1$ km)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{p2} &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(1) + 20\log_{10}(900) \\
L_{p2} &= 32.44 + 0 + 59.08 \\
L_{p2} &= 91.52 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Link Budget**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r,mob} &= P_{BTS} + G_{BTS} - L_{p1} + G_{rep} - L_{p2} + G_{mob} - L_{cable} \\
P_{r,mob} &= 30 + 12 - 101.06 + 70 - 91.52 + 2 - 2 \\
P_{r,mob} &= -80.58 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Received signal strength at mobile = **−80.58 dBm**, which is well above the typical mobile sensitivity of **−100 dBm** — indicating a healthy link. **(0.5 marks for conclusion)**

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Repeaters (KTU 2024)**

- **Definition:** Repeater = bidirectional RF amplifier that retransmits BTS signals into dead zones. **(Must know)**
- **Two main types:** RF Repeater (over-the-air) and Optical Repeater (fiber-fed). **(Frequent question)**
- **Key components:** Donor antenna, Duplexer, BDA (LNA + HPA), Server antenna. **(Diagram mandatory)**
- **Critical equation:** $I_{req} \geq G_{sys} + 10$ dB (antenna isolation rule). **(Numerical problem favorite)**
- **Friis' Formula:** First stage dominates total noise figure. **(Conceptual question)**
- **Free-space path loss:** $L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log(d) + 20\log(f)$ **(Plug-and-play formula)**
- **Noise floor:** $N = -174 + 10\log(B) + NF$ dBm **(Essential formula)**
- **Optical repeaters** preferred for: long distances (>2 km), immunity to donor-link interference, fiber-rich deployments.
- **Drawbacks of RF repeaters:** feedback oscillation risk, noise amplification, no protocol awareness.
- **Link budget equation** with repeater: $P_{rx} = P_{BTS} + G_{BTS} - L_1 + G_{rep} - L_2 + G_{mob}$ **(Board exam standard)**
- **Exam tip:** Always show units (dBm, dB, km, MHz) and clearly distinguish **donor vs server** antennas in diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Cell Zone Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Micro Cell Zone Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro Cell Zone Concept (MCZC)** is a capacity-enhancement and interference-mitigation technique in cellular mobile systems in which a single cell of radius $R$ is sub-divided into **three (or more) smaller zones**, each served by a low-power **zone site (zone server / zone selector)**. All zone sites belonging to a particular cell are linked to a **single Base Station (BS)** via dedicated radio links or wireline (landline) connections, and they collectively share the **same set of radio channels** allocated to that cell.

The fundamental objective of MCZC is to:
1. **Increase Carrier-to-Interference ($\frac{C}{I}$) ratio** without increasing the cluster size $N$ or changing the frequency reuse pattern.
2. **Maintain the same number of channels per cell** as in a conventional omnidirectional cell (i.e., capacity is **not** sacrificed).
3. **Reduce handoff frequency** between cells (intra-cell handover between zones is much simpler than inter-cell handover).

The strategy was popularized for the **AMPS (Advanced Mobile Phone System)** architecture and is a foundational concept in modern heterogeneous network (HetNet) design, where it generalises to "small-cell" or "distributed-antenna" systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Hospital Reception"**
>
> Imagine a very large multi-storey hospital (this is your **macro-cell** of radius $R$). Patients (mobile users) walk in from different corridors. Instead of forcing every patient to walk all the way back to a single central reception desk on the ground floor (the **conventional Base Station**), the hospital installs **three small reception kiosks** — one in each wing (the **zone sites**). Each kiosk handles only the patients physically close to it. All three kiosks are hard-wired via an internal network to the central reception computer (the **Base Station / MTSO**) which keeps a single unified patient database and channel assignment.
>
> Result: a patient shouting from a corridor is heard more clearly by the nearby kiosk (high $\frac{C}{I}$), but the hospital still uses the **same set of doctors / frequencies** as before. The hospital has not hired new doctors (no extra spectrum) but communication quality has improved dramatically.

This is precisely what MCZC achieves: **localised transmission and reception with centralised control**.

## 1.3 Key Physical / System Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|---|
| Path-loss exponent | $\gamma$ | **4** (urban macro environment, Okumura–Hata) |
| Number of zones per cell | $Z$ | **3** (standard hexagonal split) |
| Zone radius | $R_z$ | $R_z \approx \dfrac{R}{3}$ (textbook approximation) |
| Co-channel cell distance | $D$ | $D = R \sqrt{3N}$ |
| Co-channel reuse ratio | $q$ | $q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ |
| Number of co-channel interferers (first tier) | $i_0$ | **6** |

These constants will be used throughout the derivations in SECTION 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The MCZC works on a well-defined set of principles. Each is listed below with its **"Why"** and **"How"**.

### Step 1 — Cell Sub-Division
- **What:** A conventional hexagonal cell of radius $R$ is partitioned into $Z = 3$ hexagonal zones that tessellate the parent cell.
- **Why:** A smaller transmission/reception footprint reduces both the **transmit power required** and the **interference footprint** seen by co-channel cells.
- **How:** Zone boundaries are designed so that any mobile station (MS) lies unambiguously inside exactly one zone (no overlap ambiguity).

### Step 2 — Deployment of Zone Sites
- **What:** A **zone site** (a low-power radio port) is placed at the centre of each zone.
- **Why:** Brings the antenna physically closer to the MS → stronger received signal $C$ → higher $\frac{C}{I}$.
- **How:** Zone site equipment is much cheaper and smaller than a full BS, since its only jobs are RF up/down-conversion and zone selection.

### Step 3 — Backhaul to a Common Base Station
- **What:** All $Z$ zone sites are connected to a **single parent BS** by dedicated **radio links** (e.g., microwave) or **landline (T1/E1)**.
- **Why:** Channel allocation, mobility management, and switching are performed **centrally**, preserving the simplicity of the cellular system.
- **How:** The BS acts as a hub; the zone sites act as "remote RF heads" (this is the philosophical precursor to today's Cloud-RAN / C-RAN).

### Step 4 — Dynamic Zone Selection
- **What:** The MS is served by the **nearest** zone site (lowest path loss).
- **Why:** Since the same channel set is shared across all zones, the *server* — not the *channel* — switches as the user moves.
- **How:** The BS uses signal-strength reports to perform **intra-cell handoff** between zone sites, which is far less complex (and faster) than inter-cell handoff.

### Step 5 — Shared Channel Pool (Reuse inside the Cell)
- **What:** All $Z$ zones reuse the **entire** set of channels assigned to the parent cell.
- **Why:** Unlike cell-splitting (which gives each micro-cell its own reduced channel set) or sectoring (which divides channels), MCZC **keeps channel count constant**.
- **How:** Inter-zone interference is suppressed by keeping zone transmit power low and by careful zone geometry.

### Step 6 — Effect on Co-Channel Interference
- **What:** The dominant co-channel interference is now produced by the **zone sites of the first-tier co-channel cells**, not by their full-power BSs.
- **Why:** Because zone sites are smaller, an interfering zone site is geometrically **further** (in the worst case) from the victim's BS than a full cell centre would be.
- **How:** This **increases the effective co-channel reuse distance $D$** relative to the **smaller zone radius $R_z$**, raising $\frac{C}{I}$ by a factor of $\left(\dfrac{R}{R_z}\right)^{\gamma}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are exam-critical and frequently appear in KTU 2024 ESE Module-2 questions. Units are explicit; the **$\vert$** in any expression such as "$\vert x \vert$" is to be interpreted as absolute-value / magnitude.

| # | Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Co-channel reuse ratio | $q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$ | Dimensionless; $D$ in km, $R$ in km |
| 2 | $\frac{C}{I}$ — conventional cell (6 interferers) | $\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{1}{i_0}\left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^{\gamma} = \dfrac{q^{\gamma}}{6}$ | $\gamma = 4$ typical |
| 3 | $\frac{C}{I}$ in dB — conventional | $\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{q^{\gamma}}{6}\right)$ | dB |
| 4 | Zone radius (3-zone hexagonal split) | $R_z = \dfrac{R}{\sqrt{3}} \;\; \text{(approx. equal-area)}$ | km; some texts use $R_z = R/3$ for back-of-envelope |
| 5 | Improved co-channel distance ratio | $q_{z} = \dfrac{D}{R_z} = \sqrt{3} \cdot q$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\frac{C}{I}$ — with micro cell zoning | $\dfrac{C}{I}_{z} = \dfrac{1}{6}\left(\dfrac{D}{R_z}\right)^{\gamma} = \left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{C}{I}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Improvement factor** in $\frac{C}{I}$ | $\eta_{\text{MCZC}} = \left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma}$ | $\eta = 9$ for $\gamma=4$ |
| 8 | Improvement in dB | $\Delta_{\text{dB}} = \gamma \cdot 10 \log_{10}(\sqrt{3})$ | $\approx 4.77$ dB per zone step |
| 9 | Capacity (channels / cell) | $C_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{M}{N}$ | $M$ = total channels, $N$ = cluster size — **unchanged** by MCZC |
| 10 | Co-channel cells in 1st tier | $i_0 = 6$ | Constant for hexagonal layout |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **4G / 5G HetNet:** MCZC is the conceptual ancestor of **Distributed Antenna Systems (DAS)** and **C-RAN (Cloud-Radio Access Network)**, where multiple low-power Remote Radio Heads (RRHs) connect to a centralised BBU pool.
> - **Indoor Coverage:** Modern stadiums, airports, and malls use exactly this "zone site + central hub" topology for uniform coverage.
> - **Power Efficiency:** Lower zone-site transmit power → reduced battery drain on handsets and lower electromagnetic exposure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. **No step is skipped.** All numerical work is reproducible. The path-loss exponent is taken as $\gamma = 4$ (a KTU-favoured default for urban macrocells) unless stated otherwise.

## 3.1 Derivation I — Conventional $\frac{C}{I}$ Without MCZC

### Step 1: Define the received signal power model

The received signal power at the mobile from its serving BS is given by the **general path-loss model**:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{d}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

where $P_t$ = transmit power, $K$ = constant accounting for antenna gains and wavelength, $d$ = T-R separation, and $\gamma$ = path-loss exponent.

### Step 2: Carrier power $C$

The MS is at distance $R$ from its serving BS (worst-case cell edge):

$$
C = P_{t1} \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{R}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 3: Interference from one co-channel BS

Each of the $i_0 = 6$ first-tier co-channel BSs is at distance $D$ from the MS. Its interference contribution is:

$$
I_j = P_{t2} \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 4: Sum of interferences

For equal-power co-channel interferers ($P_{t1} = P_{t2}$), total interference is:

$$
I_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{6} I_j = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 5: Form the $\frac{C}{I}$ ratio

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-\gamma}}{6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-\gamma}} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 6: Express in terms of cluster size $N$

Using $q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\sqrt{3N}\right)^{\gamma} = \frac{(3N)^{\gamma/2}}{6}
$$

### Step 7: Numerical evaluation for $N=7$, $\gamma=4$

$$
q = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.5826
$$

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(4.5826)^4}{6} = \frac{441.67}{6} \approx 73.61
$$

In decibels:

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.61) \approx 18.67 \text{ dB}
$$

This is the **standard reference value** (matches Rappaport) and **fails to meet the 18 dB minimum** for AMPS voice quality, so techniques like MCZC are deployed.

## 3.2 Derivation II — $\frac{C}{I}$ With Micro Cell Zoning

### Step 1: Re-define geometry

The cell of radius $R$ is divided into $Z = 3$ hexagonal zones. For a near-equal-area split:

$$
R_z = \frac{R}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

The MS now communicates with the **nearest zone site** at distance $\leq R_z$ instead of the cell-centre BS at distance $R$.

### Step 2: New carrier power $C_z$

$$
C_z = P_t \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{R_z}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 3: Worst-case interference path

The dominant interferer is now the **zone site** in the co-channel cell that is geometrically closest. By symmetry of the 6 first-tier co-channel cells and the 3-zone tessellation, the worst-case interferer-zone distance is the original $D$ (zone site offset is small relative to $D$).

### Step 4: New interference total

$$
I_{\text{total},z} = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 5: New $\frac{C}{I}$ ratio

$$
\frac{C}{I}_z = \frac{P_t \cdot K \cdot R_z^{-\gamma}}{6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-\gamma}} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R_z}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute $R_z = R / \sqrt{3}$

$$
\frac{C}{I}_z = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D \cdot \sqrt{3}}{R}\right)^{\gamma} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

### Step 7: Factor out the original $\frac{C}{I}$

$$
\frac{C}{I}_z = \left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma} \cdot \frac{C}{I}
$$

### Step 8: Improvement factor $\eta_{\text{MCZC}}$

$$
\eta_{\text{MCZC}} = \frac{C/I_z}{C/I} = \left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

For $\gamma = 4$:

$$
\eta_{\text{MCZC}} = (\sqrt{3})^{4} = 3^{2} = 9
$$

In decibels:

$$
\Delta_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(9) \approx 9.54 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 9: New numerical $\frac{C}{I}$ in dB for $N=7$, $\gamma=4$

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{z,dB} = 18.67 + 9.54 \approx 28.21 \text{ dB}
$$

This **comfortably exceeds** the 18 dB AMPS threshold with a healthy **10 dB margin**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Conventional Cell | MCZC Cell | Remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Channels per cell | $M/N$ | $M/N$ (unchanged) | Capacity preserved |
| Cluster size $N$ | 7 (assumed) | 7 (unchanged) | No change in reuse plan |
| Effective radius used in $\frac{C}{I}$ | $R$ | $R_z = R/\sqrt{3}$ | Reduced footprint |
| $\frac{C}{I}$ (linear) | $73.6$ | $662.5$ | $9\times$ improvement |
| $\frac{C}{I}$ (dB) | $18.67$ dB | $28.21$ dB | $+9.54$ dB |
| BS transmit power | High | Lower (per zone) | Power efficiency gain |
| Handoff type | Inter-cell heavy | Mostly intra-cell | Lower signalling load |
| Hardware cost per cell | 1 high-power BS | 1 BS + 3 zone sites | Slightly higher CapEx |
| Backhaul | Trivial | 3 dedicated links required | Landline / microwave |

## 3.4 Quick Sanity Check — Edge Cases

> [!NOTE]
> - **If $\gamma = 2$ (free space):** $\eta_{\text{MCZC}} = (\sqrt{3})^2 = 3$ → improvement of $10\log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB. The benefit **shrinks** in less lossy environments.
> - **If $\gamma = 3$:** $\eta = (\sqrt{3})^3 \approx 5.20$ → $+7.16$ dB.
> - **If $\gamma = 4$ (urban):** $\eta = 9$ → $+9.54$ dB (canonical case).
> - **If $\gamma = 5$ (heavy shadowing):** $\eta = (\sqrt{3})^5 \approx 15.59$ → $+11.93$ dB. Benefit **grows** with harsher propagation.

This monotonic dependence on $\gamma$ is why MCZC is **especially attractive in dense urban deployments** — exactly the KTU textbook scenario.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS["MOBILE STATION MS"]
    ZA["ZONE SITE A North"]
    ZB["ZONE SITE B South West"]
    ZC["ZONE SITE C South East"]
    BS["CENTRAL BASE STATION BS"]
    MTSO["MTSO Mobile Switching Center"]
    COCH1["CO CHANNEL CELL 1 6 interferers"]
    COCH2["CO CHANNEL CELL 2 6 interferers"]

    MS -- radio link weakest path loss --> ZA
    MS -. weak link .-> ZB
    MS -. weak link .-> ZC
    ZA -- landline or microwave backhaul --> BS
    ZB -- landline or microwave backhaul --> BS
    ZC -- landline or microwave backhaul --> BS
    BS -- high capacity trunk --> MTSO
    MTSO -- handoff signalling --> COCH1
    MTSO -- interference coordination --> COCH2

    style MS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ZA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ZB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ZC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style BS fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MTSO fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style COCH1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style COCH2 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU Examiner's expectation):**
- The MS is **served by the closest zone site** (solid arrow to ZA), which carries the strongest signal and lowest path loss.
- All three zone sites are slaves to a **single parent BS** — channel allocation, paging, and handoff are centralised.
- Co-channel interference arrives from the **first-tier co-channel cells** (COCH1, COCH2), but their effective interference footprint is now bounded by the **smaller zone sites**, not their full-power BSs.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Signal Flow View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["USER DOMAIN"]
        U1["Voice or Data Source"]
    end

    subgraph ACCESS["RADIO ACCESS NETWORK"]
        A1["Zone Antenna Low Power"]
        A2["Zone RF Front End Up Down Conversion"]
        A3["Base Station Channel Bank Switching Fabric"]
        A4["Backhaul Interface T1 E1 Microwave"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["CORE NETWORK"]
        C1["MTSO Mobility Database"]
        C2["PSTN or Internet Gateway"]
    end

    U1 -- Uplink RF --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 <--> A4
    A4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 -. Signalling .-> C1
    C1 -. Channel Assignment .-> A3
    A3 -. Zone Selection .-> A1

    style U1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
    style A4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Take-away:** Notice the **closed control loop** between MTSO $\leftrightarrow$ BS $\leftrightarrow$ Zone site. This is what enables **dynamic zone selection** and **intra-cell handoff** — the operational heart of MCZC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question is calibrated to KTU's 2024 ESE pattern: a 3-mark direct-conceptual item requiring a tight, well-bulleted answer (≈ 60–90 words).

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Micro Cell Zone Concept. Mention its two primary objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Micro Cell Zone Concept (MCZC) is a cellular capacity-enhancement technique in which a macro-cell of radius $R$ is sub-divided into three smaller hexagonal **zones**, each served by a low-power **zone site**. All zone sites of a cell are connected to a common **Base Station (BS)** via dedicated landline or radio backhaul and share the **same set of radio channels**.

**Two primary objectives:**
1. To **improve the Carrier-to-Interference ratio $\frac{C}{I}$** without changing the cluster size $N$ or sacrificing channel capacity.
2. To **reduce the frequency of inter-cell handoffs** by replacing them with simpler **intra-cell zone-to-zone handoffs**.

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for the two objectives.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages of the Micro Cell Zone Concept over conventional cell-site deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Improved $\frac{C}{I}$:** Co-channel interference is reduced because the effective transmission/reception footprint is the smaller zone radius $R_z$ instead of the cell radius $R$. Quantitatively, the improvement factor is $(\sqrt{3})^{\gamma}$ (≈ 9 for $\gamma = 4$).
2. **Capacity preservation:** Unlike cell-splitting, MCZC **does not reduce** the number of channels per cell — the full $M/N$ channel set is reused across all zones.
3. **Simpler handoff:** Zone-to-zone handover is intra-cell, requiring **no MTSO-level signalling**, drastically reducing switching load and the "ping-pong" effect at cell boundaries.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part-B requires a single question with sub-parts of **7 marks + 7 marks**. To honour the **internal choice** rule, two fully independent 14-mark alternatives are provided below.

---

### `OPTION A — Q1 (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### (a) Explain the architecture and working of the Micro Cell Zone Concept with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Architecture (3 marks):**
A macro-cell of radius $R$ is divided into **three hexagonal zones** ($Z = 3$). Each zone is served by a low-power **zone site** (zone selector) placed at its centre. The three zone sites are connected via **landline (T1/E1) or point-to-point microwave links** to a **single Base Station (BS)**. The BS in turn interfaces with the **Mobile Telephone Switching Office (MTSO)**, which connects to the PSTN. All three zones share the **same set of $M/N$ channels** assigned to the parent cell.

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- The MS transmits to the **nearest zone site**, which forwards the signal to the central BS over the backhaul link.
- The BS performs channel assignment, switching, and signalling — exactly as in a conventional cell.
- As the MS moves from one zone to another, the BS executes an **intra-cell handoff** by simply re-routing the call to the new zone site. **No MTSO involvement is required** for zone-to-zone handover.
- Inter-zone interference is minimised by the low transmit power of each zone site and by the natural isolation of hexagonal tessellation.

**3. Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the block diagram from SECTION 4.1.)*

---

#### (b) Derive the expression for the improvement in Carrier-to-Interference ratio ($\frac{C}{I}$) achieved by the Micro Cell Zone Concept for a cluster size of $N = 7$ and a path-loss exponent $\gamma = 4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Conventional $\frac{C}{I}$ (2 marks):**

For a hexagonal cellular system with $i_0 = 6$ first-tier co-channel interferers and worst-case MS at cell edge ($d = R$):

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{\gamma}
$$

With $N = 7$, $q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$:

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(4.583)^{4}}{6} = \frac{441.67}{6} \approx 73.6
$$

In dB: $\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.6) \approx 18.67$ dB.

**Step 2 — MCZC $\frac{C}{I}$ (3 marks):**

The MS is served by a zone site at distance $R_z = R/\sqrt{3}$. Worst-case interference from 6 co-channel zone sites is at distance $D$:

$$
\frac{C}{I}_z = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D}{R_z}\right)^{\gamma} = \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{D \cdot \sqrt{3}}{R}\right)^{\gamma} = \left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma} \cdot \frac{C}{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Improvement factor (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{\text{MCZC}} = (\sqrt{3})^{4} = 9
$$

In dB: $\Delta = 10\log_{10}(9) \approx 9.54$ dB.

**Step 4 — Numerical result (1 mark):**

$$
\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{z} = 9 \times 73.6 \approx 662.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 28.21 \text{ dB}
$$

This exceeds the 18 dB AMPS threshold by a 10 dB margin.

---

### `OPTION B — Q2 (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### (a) Compare the Micro Cell Zone Concept with cell-splitting and sectoring. Mention the engineering trade-off in each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (tabular format expected):**

| Parameter | Cell-Splitting | Sectoring | Micro Cell Zoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell radius | Reduced (e.g., $R \to R/2$) | Unchanged | Unchanged (cell) but **zone radius $R_z < R$** |
| Transmit power per BS | Lower | Lower (per sector) | Lower (per zone) |
| Channels per cell | **Reduced** by factor $k^2$ (sector area ratio) | **Reduced** by sector count | **Unchanged** — $M/N$ preserved |
| Handoff complexity | High (more cells) | Moderate (sector-to-sector intra-cell) | **Low** (intra-cell, BS-controlled) |
| $\frac{C}{I}$ improvement | Yes (smaller $R$) | Yes (reduced interferer count from 6 to 2) | **Yes — by factor $(\sqrt{3})^{\gamma}$** |
| Antenna complexity | Standard | Directional antennas | Standard omnidirectional zone antennas |
| Engineering trade-off | **Capacity drops** per cell; backhaul grows | **Trunking efficiency drops**; more antennas | **CapEx rises** (zone sites + backhaul) |

**Summary (2 marks):** Cell-splitting and sectoring trade **channel count for interference reduction**, whereas MCZC trades **capital expenditure (zone sites + backhaul) for interference reduction** while preserving channel count — making it the preferred option when **spectrum is the bottleneck**.

---

#### (b) An AMPS cellular operator uses $N = 7$ cluster reuse, $M = 420$ total channels, and a path-loss exponent $\gamma = 4$. Compute (i) channels per cell, (ii) $\frac{C}{I}$ with and without MCZC, (iii) improvement in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $M = 420$, $N = 7$, $\gamma = 4$.

**(i) Channels per cell (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{420}{7} = 60 \text{ channels}
$$

**(ii) $\frac{C}{I}$ without MCZC (2 marks):**

$$
q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$

$$
\frac{C}{I} = \frac{q^{\gamma}}{6} = \frac{(4.583)^{4}}{6} = \frac{441.67}{6} \approx 73.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 18.67 \text{ dB}
$$

**$\frac{C}{I}$ with MCZC (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{C}{I}_z = (\sqrt{3})^{4} \times 73.6 = 9 \times 73.6 \approx 662.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 28.21 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) Improvement in dB (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta_{\text{dB}} = 28.21 - 18.67 = 9.54 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/6$ factor** in the $\frac{C}{I}$ formula → lose 1 mark. Always write the **6-interferer sum explicitly**.
> 2. **Confusing $R_z = R/3$ with $R_z = R/\sqrt{3}$.** The **equal-area hexagonal tessellation** gives $R/\sqrt{3}$; the $R/3$ form is only an exam-shortcut. Pick **one consistently** and state it.
> 3. **Stating capacity is improved by MCZC** — it is **NOT**. Capacity ($M/N$) is **unchanged**. Only $\frac{C}{I}$ improves. Examiners explicitly test this misconception.
> 4. **Dropping units** in the final answer (e.g., writing "dB" or "channels/cell" inconsistently) → lose half a mark.
> 5. **Using $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios** — correct. Do **NOT** use $20 \log_{10}$ (that is for voltage/field ratios).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Things You Must Remember (Module 2 — MCZC):**

1. MCZC **sub-divides a cell into zones**; the cell radius $R$ stays the same, the **zone radius $R_z < R$**.
2. Each zone has a low-power **zone site (zone selector)**, all linked to **one Base Station**.
3. All zones of a cell **share the same channel set** ($M/N$ channels) — **capacity is preserved**.
4. The MS is **always served by the nearest zone site** (lowest path loss).
5. The **improvement factor** in $\frac{C}{I}$ is $\eta = (\sqrt{3})^{\gamma}$; for $\gamma = 4$, this equals **9 (≈ 9.54 dB)**.
6. Handoff in MCZC is **mostly intra-cell (zone-to-zone)** — no MTSO signalling, lower switching load.
7. The co-channel reuse distance $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ and ratio $q = \sqrt{3N}$ are **unchanged** — only the effective radius in the $\frac{C}{I}$ formula shrinks from $R$ to $R_z$.
8. Conventional $\frac{C}{I}$ formula: $\dfrac{C}{I} = \dfrac{1}{6}\left(\dfrac{D}{R}\right)^{\gamma}$.
9. MCZC $\frac{C}{I}$ formula: $\dfrac{C}{I}_z = \dfrac{1}{6}\left(\dfrac{D}{R_z}\right)^{\gamma} = \left(\sqrt{3}\right)^{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{C}{I}$.
10. AMPS minimum acceptable $\frac{C}{I}$ threshold: **18 dB**. With MCZC + $N = 7$ you get **~28 dB** → safe margin.
11. **Trade-off:** CapEx rises (extra zone sites and backhaul) but **spectrum is conserved** — the ideal compromise when **frequencies are scarce**.
12. MCZC is the **conceptual ancestor** of modern **DAS (Distributed Antenna Systems)** and **C-RAN** in 4G/5G HetNets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Channel Antenna System Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Channel Antenna System Design Considerations # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Channel Antenna System Design** refers to the integrated engineering process of jointly optimizing antenna parameters (geometry, gain, polarization, height, beamwidth, VSWR) with the statistical and physical characteristics of the wireless propagation channel (path loss, fading, multipath, delay spread, noise) to achieve a specified Quality of Service (QoS), link budget, coverage, and spectral efficiency. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **channel-antenna system** is treated as a *single coupled entity* rather than two independent blocks. The antenna is not merely a radiator; it is a **spatial filter**, a **polarization filter**, and an **impedance transformer** whose behavior directly alters the perceived statistics of the channel. Key design parameters grouped under this topic: - **Electrical parameters**: Radiation pattern, directivity $D$, gain $G$, half-power beamwidth (HPBW), polarization, input impedance, VSWR, bandwidth. - **Mechanical/Physical parameters**: Height above ground, form factor (wire, microstrip, aperture), mounting geometry, mast/structure effects. - **Channel-coupled parameters**: Mean path loss, shadow fading margin, Ricean $K$-factor, delay spread, coherence bandwidth, antenna correlation coefficient. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Lighthouse and the Foggy Harbour** > Imagine a lighthouse (the antenna) trying to send light (the radio wave) across a foggy, wavy harbour (the wireless channel). The lighthouse keeper can rotate the lamp (change beamwidth), focus the beam using lenses (change gain/directivity), tint the light with a colour filter (change polarization), and raise the lamp higher on the tower (change height). Meanwhile, the fog density and wave height (channel characteristics) are constantly changing. > A good **channel-antenna system design** is the act of tuning the lighthouse *and* knowing the typical weather, so the ships reliably see the beam.
