You cannot design the lamp in isolation — the fog decides how strong the lamp must be. ## 1.3 Fundamental Constants and Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > Highlight these standard values — they appear frequently in KTU numerical problems. > - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s > - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_{0} = 120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega$ > - Boltzmann constant: $k_{B} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K > - Reference distance for free-space models: $d_{0} = 1$ m (outdoor macrocell) or $d_{0} = 0.1$ m (indoor) > - Standard thermal noise floor at 290 K: $-174$ dBm/Hz ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme The course outcome **CO2** of PCCPT502 specifically maps "antenna system parameters and their influence on wireless link design" to the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** in Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Expect either a 7-mark derivation sub-question or a 7-mark numerical problem based on Friis / path-loss integration with antenna gains. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Antenna as the Channel Boundary Element In a wireless link, the antenna acts at **two boundaries simultaneously**: 1. **Circuit boundary** — between the guided RF signal on a transmission line (TEM mode in coax, quasi-TEM in microstrip) and the radiated spherical wave in free space. 2. **Spatial boundary** — between the *near-field reactive region* and the *far-field radiative region*. The far-field boundary (Fraunhofer distance) is given by: $$ d_{F} = \frac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda} $$ where $D$ is the largest physical dimension of the antenna and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength. Beyond $d_{F}$, the radiated field pattern is independent of distance — *this is the region where channel models (Friis, Okumura-Hata, COST-231) are valid.* ## 2.2 Design Considerations — Structured Logic > [!NOTE] > **The Six Pillars of Channel-Antenna Co-Design** **Pillar 1 — Frequency and Bandwidth Selection** - Determines $\lambda$ and hence element size, beamwidth, and material losses. - Path loss scales as $f^{n}$ where $n$ is the path loss exponent (typically $2$ in free space, $3$–$5$ in urban cellular, $1.6$–$1.8$ in indoor LOS). - Wider bandwidth supports higher data rates but constrains VSWR tolerance. **Pillar 2 — Radiation Pattern and Directivity** - Directivity $D$ is the ratio of radiation intensity in a given direction to the average radiation intensity. - The KTU exam often relates $D$ to HPBW approximately as: $$ D \approx \frac{41253}{\theta_{1} \cdot \theta_{2}} $$ where $\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}$ are HPBW in the two orthogonal planes (E-plane and H-plane) in degrees. **Pillar 3 — Gain, EIRP and Link Budget** - Realized gain $G = \eta_{r} \cdot D$, where $\eta_{r}$ is radiation efficiency ($0 \le \eta_{r} \le 1$). - EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) combines transmit power and antenna gain: $$ \text{EIRP} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \quad \text{(linear)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{EIRP}_{\text{dBm}} = P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_{t,\text{dBi}} $$ **Pillar 4 — Polarization Match and Polarization Loss** - Co-polarized antennas transfer maximum power; cross-polarized antennas are isolated by ~20–30 dB. - Polarization Loss Factor (PLF) for linear vs. elliptical polarization mismatch: $$ \text{PLF} = \vert \hat{\rho}_{t} \cdot \hat{\rho}_{r}^{*} \vert^{2} = \cos^{2}(\psi) $$ where $\psi$ is the angle between the two polarization unit vectors. **Pillar 5 — Antenna Height and Ground Clearance** - Base-station antenna height $h_{b}$ appears as a *logarithmic* factor in the Okumura-Hata model. - Increasing $h_{b}$ by a factor of 10 roughly improves median path loss by 10–15 dB in suburban areas. - Fresnel zone clearance must exceed ~60% of the first Fresnel radius for LOS validity. **Pillar 6 — Diversity and Correlation** - Space diversity requires antenna separation greater than the coherence distance $d_{c} \approx \lambda / (2 \sin(\theta_{\text{spread}}))$. - Polarization diversity achieves decorrelation at $\lambda / 10$ spacing — a major advantage for handset design. - Pattern diversity exploits directional elements with low envelope correlation. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions on antenna-channel design in the KTU 2024 ESE. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Linear / dB | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L_{FS}(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss | dB | d | | 2 | $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Friis transmission equation | W | linear | | 3 | $G = \frac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Gain in terms of effective aperture | numeric | linear | | 4 | $A_{e} = \eta_{r} A_{p}$ | Effective aperture = efficiency × physical aperture | m² | linear | | 5 | $\text{EIRP} = P_{t} G_{t}$ | Effective isotropic radiated power | W | linear | | 6 | $\text{PLF} = \cos^{2}(\psi)$ | Polarization loss factor | numeric ($0$–$1$) | linear | | 7 | $d_{F} = 2 D^{2} / \lambda$ | Fraunhofer (far-field) distance | m | linear | | 8 | $D \approx 41253 / (\theta_{1} \theta_{2})$ | Approx. directivity from beamwidths | numeric | degrees | | 9 | $\Gamma = (Z_{L} - Z_{0})/(Z_{L} + Z_{0})$ | Reflection coefficient (VSWR basis) | numeric | complex | | 10 | $\text{VSWR} = (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$ | Voltage standing wave ratio | numeric $\ge 1$ | linear | | 11 | $L_{p}(d) = L_{p}(d_{0}) + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_{0}) + X_{\sigma}$ | Log-distance path loss with shadow fading | dB | d | | 12 | $F_{n} = \lambda / (2 \sin \theta_{s})$ | Coherence distance for diversity | m | linear | | 13 | $r_{1} = \sqrt{\lambda d_{1} d_{2} / (d_{1} + d_{2})}$ | First Fresnel zone radius | m | linear | | 14 | $T_{\text{ant}} = T_{0}(10^{\text{NF}/10} - 1)$ | Antenna / receiver noise contribution | K | linear | | 15 | $S/N = P_{r} / (k_{B} T B)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (thermal) | numeric | linear / dB | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz for formula 1 (Friis in dB form). When $d$ is in metres and $f$ in Hz, replace the constant 32.44 with $-147.55$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This design discipline directly drives: - **Cellular network planning**: Cell radius, sectorization (3-sector at 120°, 6-sector at 60°), tilt optimization. - **Satellite link design**: EIRP budgets, G/T figure of merit, rain margin calculation. - **IoT / LPWAN**: Low-gain omni antennas for indoor devices; high-gain Yagi/panel for rural gateways. - **5G/6G massive MIMO**: Beamforming weight vectors depend on channel-antenna joint response. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Friis Transmission Equation (Full Version with Antenna Gains) **Statement:** In free space, the received power at the output of the receive antenna terminals is related to the transmit power by the product of transmitter gain, free-space spreading factor, and receiver gain. **Starting premise — Effective aperture of an isotropic antenna:** By definition, an isotropic radiator distributes power uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$. $$ S_{\text{iso}}(d) = \frac{P_{t}}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{(power flux density, W/m²)} $$ The effective aperture of an isotropic antenna is defined as the ratio of received power to incident power flux density. Since an isotropic antenna by definition absorbs equally from all directions, this is conventionally chosen as: $$ A_{e,\text{iso}} = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi} $$ This is the **isotropic effective aperture**, derived from thermodynamic / antenna theory reciprocity. **Step 1:** With a transmit antenna of gain $G_{t}$ (linear, relative to isotropic), the actual power flux density at distance $d$ becomes: $$ S(d) = \frac{P_{t} G_{t}}{4\pi d^{2}} $$ The multiplication by $G_{t}$ captures the fact that the transmit antenna concentrates energy in the direction of the receiver. **Step 2:** The receive antenna collects power according to its effective aperture $A_{e,r}$: $$ P_{r} = S(d) \cdot A_{e,r} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2: $$ P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} G_{t}}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot A_{e,r} $$ **Step 4:** Replace $A_{e,r}$ using the gain–aperture relation $A_{e} = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$: $$ A_{e,r} = \frac{G_{r} \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute into the expression for $P_{r}$: $$ P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} G_{t}}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_{r} \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} $$ **Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the canonical Friis transmission equation: $$ P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{2} $$ **Step 7 (KTU-friendly dB form):** Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides: $$ P_{r,\text{dBm}} = P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_{t,\text{dBi}} + G_{r,\text{dBi}} - L_{FS,\text{dB}} $$ where the free-space path loss in dB is: $$ L_{FS,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) $$ This completes the derivation. Every line above is what a KTU examiner expects to see in a 7-mark derivation sub-question. ## 3.2 Derivation of Polarization Loss Factor (PLF) **Statement:** When the transmit and receive antenna polarization unit vectors are not perfectly aligned, only the projection of one onto the other contributes to power transfer. **Step 1:** Let the transmit antenna polarization be represented by the complex unit vector: $$ \hat{\rho}_{t} = \hat{\theta}\,\cos\alpha\, e^{j\delta_{t}} $$ **Step 2:** Let the receive antenna polarization be: $$ \hat{\rho}_{r} = \hat{\theta}\,\cos\beta\, e^{j\delta_{r}} $$ **Step 3:** The complex inner product captures both orientation and phase mismatch: $$ \hat{\rho}_{t} \cdot \hat{\rho}_{r}^{*} = \cos\psi \, e^{j\Delta\delta} $$ where $\psi$ is the angular misalignment and $\Delta\delta = \delta_{t} - \delta_{r}$ is the phase difference. **Step 4:** The magnitude squared of this inner product is the polarization loss factor: $$ \text{PLF} = \vert \hat{\rho}_{t} \cdot \hat{\rho}_{r}^{*} \vert^{2} = \cos^{2}\psi $$ **Step 5 — Special cases for the KTU viva:** - Perfectly aligned linear polarizations ($\psi = 0°$): PLF = 1 (no loss). - Perpendicular linear polarizations ($\psi = 90°$): PLF = 0 (total loss). - Circular-to-linear mismatch: PLF = 1/2 = −3 dB. - Dual orthogonal linear at the receiver (polarization diversity): combined PLF = 1 always. ## 3.3 Derivation of Mean Path Loss with Log-Distance Model and Shadow Fading **Statement:** The mean path loss in a non-free-space channel is empirical but takes a clean analytical form. **Step 1:** At reference distance $d_{0}$ in the far field, measure or compute the path loss $L_{p}(d_{0})$. For free space at $d_{0}$: $$ L_{p}(d_{0}) = 20\log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi d_{0}}{\lambda} \right) $$ **Step 2:** The path loss exponent $n$ captures the additional loss due to terrain, buildings, foliage. Integrating over a logarithmic distance: $$ L_{p}(d) = L_{p}(d_{0}) + 10\, n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right) $$ **Step 3:** Empirical values of $n$: - Free space: $n = 2$ - Urban macrocell: $n = 3.0$ – $3.5$ - Urban microcell LOS: $n = 2.5$ – $2.7$ - Indoor LOS: $n = 1.6$ – $1.8$ - Indoor NLOS / obstructed: $n = 3.0$ – $4.0$ **Step 4:** Shadow fading is modelled as a zero-mean Gaussian random variable in the log-domain (i.e., log-normal in linear scale): $$ L_{p}(d) = L_{p}(d_{0}) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right) + X_{\sigma}, \quad X_{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2}) $$ Typical $\sigma$ ranges from **6 dB (light shadowing)** to **12 dB (heavy shadowing)**. **Step 5 — Fading margin for a desired outage probability $P_{\text{out}}$:** The fade margin $F_{m}$ required to ensure $P_{\text{out}}$ is: $$ F_{m} = \sigma \, Q^{-1}(P_{\text{out}}) $$ For example, with $\sigma = 8$ dB and $P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$ (90% coverage), $Q^{-1}(0.1) \approx 1.28$, giving $F_{m} \approx 10.24$ dB. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Link Budget (KTU-style) **Problem:** A transmitter delivers $P_{t} = 10$ W at $f = 900$ MHz into a base-station antenna of gain $G_{t} = 14$ dBi. The mobile receiver uses an antenna of gain $G_{r} = 1.5$ dBi at distance $d = 5$ km. Polarization mismatch angle is $30°$. Find the received power. **Solution walkthrough:** **Step 1 — Free-space path loss (dB form):** $$ L_{FS} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(900) $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(5) = 13.98 \text{ dB}, \quad 20\log_{10}(900) = 59.08 \text{ dB} $$ $$ L_{FS} = 32.44 + 13.98 + 59.08 = 105.50 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2 — Polarization loss factor in dB:** $$ \text{PLF} = \cos^{2}(30°) = (\sqrt{3}/2)^{2} = 0.75 $$ $$ \text{PLF}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(0.75) = -1.25 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 3 — Transmit power in dBm:** $$ P_{t} = 10 \text{ W} = 10\,000 \text{ mW} \Rightarrow P_{t,\text{dBm}} = 10\log_{10}(10\,000) = 40 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Friis link equation in dB:** $$ P_{r,\text{dBm}} = P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_{t,\text{dBi}} + G_{r,\text{dBi}} - L_{FS,\text{dB}} + \text{PLF}_{\text{dB}} $$ $$ P_{r,\text{dBm}} = 40 + 14 + 1.5 - 105.50 - 1.25 = -51.25 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to linear:** $$ P_{r} = 10^{-51.25/10} \text{ mW} \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mW} = 7.5 \text{ nW} $$ This is a realistic value for cellular uplink margin calculation. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Antenna Choice vs. Scenario | Scenario | Frequency | Recommended Antenna | Typical Gain | Rationale | |---|---|---|---|---| | Macrocell BTS | 900 MHz – 2.6 GHz | Sectorized collinear array | 14–18 dBi | High EIRP needed for long reach | | Indoor small cell | 2.4 / 5 GHz | Omnidirectional dipole / patch | 2–5 dBi | Uniform azimuth coverage | | Fixed wireless access | 5.8 GHz | Parabolic dish / grid | 24–30 dBi | Narrow beam, Pt-Pt links | | Satellite ground terminal | 4–12 GHz | Parabolic reflector | 35–50 dBi | Compensates extreme path loss | | IoT / wearable | Sub-1 GHz / 2.4 GHz | PIFA / chip antenna | −2 to +2 dBi | Body-worn, omnidirectional | | mmWave 5G | 28 / 38 GHz | Patch array with beamforming | 20–25 dBi | High free-space path loss | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Channel–Antenna System The diagram below maps the joint operation of the antenna subsystem and the propagation channel subsystem, with all relevant design parameters annotated at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR TX["Transmitter

The arrows show the *direction of signal flow* and the dotted arrows represent *control / feedback*. This is the KTU-accepted convention for representing antenna-channel subsystems on the answer sheet when physical pictorial sketches are not feasible. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] > **Define the following terms related to channel-antenna systems:** > (i) Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP) > (ii) Polarization Loss Factor (PLF) > (iii) Effective Aperture of an antenna **Model Answer:** (i) **EIRP** is the product of the power supplied to the transmitting antenna and its gain with respect to an isotropic radiator. It represents the equivalent power that an isotropic antenna would need to radiate to produce the same field strength in the direction of maximum radiation. $$ \text{EIRP} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} $$ In dB: $\text{EIRP}_{\text{dBm}} = P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_{t,\text{dBi}}$. *(1 mark)* (ii) **PLF** is the factor by which the received power is reduced when the polarization of the receive antenna does not match the polarization of the incoming wave. For two linearly polarized antennas with misalignment angle $\psi$: $$ \text{PLF} = \cos^{2}\psi $$ It is a dimensionless factor between 0 and 1. *(1 mark)* (iii) **Effective Aperture** $A_{e}$ of a receiving antenna is the ratio of the power delivered to the load (matched) to the incident power flux density at the antenna. It is related to gain by $A_{e} = G \lambda^{2} / (4\pi)$. *(1 mark)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] > **Why is the Friis transmission equation valid only in the far-field region of the transmit antenna? Mention the condition that defines this region.** **Model Answer:** The Friis transmission equation assumes that the radiated wavefront has become a *uniform spherical surface* over which power is distributed uniformly (in the isotropic reference sense). This approximation holds only in the **far-field (Fraunhofer) region** of the antenna, beyond which the angular distribution of radiated power is independent of distance. The Fraunhofer distance condition is: $$ d_{F} = \frac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda} $$ where $D$ is the largest physical dimension of the antenna and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. For $d \ge d_{F}$, the antenna pattern is stable with distance and the free-space spherical wave assumption of Friis holds. *(2 marks for the explanation + 1 mark for the condition)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE follows a strict Module-Internal Choice pattern. Both options belong to **Module 2**. --- ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern] **(a)** Derive the Friis transmission equation in free space, clearly stating the role of effective aperture and antenna gains. Show the equivalent dB form. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A microwave link operates at $f = 6$ GHz. The transmitter delivers $P_{t} = 1$ W into an antenna of gain $G_{t} = 30$ dBi. The receiver at $d = 50$ km uses an antenna of gain $G_{r} = 35$ dBi. Calculate: (i) the free-space path loss in dB, (ii) the EIRP in dBW, (iii) the received power in dBm and in nanowatts. Assume perfect polarization match. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** Refer to the full derivation given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key milestones expected in the answer script are: 1. Define power flux density from isotropic radiator: $S = P_{t}/(4\pi d^{2})$ *(1 mark)* 2. Multiply by transmit antenna gain $G_{t}$ to obtain actual $S$ at distance $d$ *(1 mark)* 3. Receive antenna captures power proportional to its effective aperture $A_{e,r}$ *(1 mark)* 4. Substitute $A_{e,r} = G_{r}\lambda^{2}/(4\pi)$ derived from the gain–aperture relation *(2 marks)* 5. Combine and rearrange to obtain canonical Friis form *(1 mark)* 6. Convert to dB form: $P_{r,\text{dBm}} = P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_{t,\text{dBi}} + G_{r,\text{dBi}} - L_{FS,\text{dB}}$ *(1 mark)* **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Free-space path loss:** Using $L_{FS} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$: $$ L_{FS} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(50) + 20\log_{10}(6000) $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(50) = 33.98 \text{ dB}, \quad 20\log_{10}(6000) = 75.56 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{L_{FS} = 32.44 + 33.98 + 75.56 = 141.98 \text{ dB}} \quad \text{*(2 marks)*} $$ **(ii) EIRP in dBW:** $P_{t} = 1$ W $= 0$ dBW. With $G_{t} = 30$ dBi: $$ \boxed{\text{EIRP} = 0 \text{ dBW} + 30 \text{ dBi} = 30 \text{ dBW}} \quad \text{*(2 marks)*} $$ **(iii) Received power:** In dBm: $P_{t} = 1$ W $= 30$ dBm. $$ P_{r,\text{dBm}} = 30 + 30 + 35 - 141.98 = -46.98 \text{ dBm} $$ Converting to nanowatts: $$ P_{r} = 10^{-46.98/10} \text{ mW} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mW} = 20 \text{ nW} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{r} \approx -46.98 \text{ dBm} \approx 20 \text{ nW}} \quad \text{*(3 marks)*} $$ --- ### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern] **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain how the antenna subsystem and the channel subsystem jointly determine the received signal quality in a wireless link. Highlight the role of EIRP, polarization match and path loss exponent. *(7 marks)* **(b)** In an urban microcell environment, the path loss at $d_{0} = 100$ m is measured as 80 dB at $f = 1800$ MHz. The path loss exponent is $n = 3.2$ and shadow fading standard deviation is $\sigma = 8$ dB. For a desired outage probability of $P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$ at $d = 500$ m, calculate: (i) the mean path loss, (ii) the fade margin required, (iii) the total path loss that must be budgeted including the fade margin. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** The expected answer should follow the block diagram flow: - **Block 1 — Transmitter**: Feeds $P_{t}$ into matching network; *(0.5 mark)* - **Block 2 — TX Antenna**: Imposes directivity, gain $G_{t}$, polarization $\hat{\rho}_{t}$; produces EIRP $= P_{t}G_{t}$; *(1 mark)* - **Block 3 — Channel**: Imposes path loss $L_{p}(d) = L_{p}(d_{0}) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_{0}) + X_{\sigma}$, multipath, fading; *(1 mark)* - **Block 4 — RX Antenna**: Imposes gain $G_{r}$, polarization $\hat{\rho}_{r}$, PLF; receives flux density and delivers $P_{r}$; *(1 mark)* - **Block 5 — Receiver**: Determines SNR via thermal noise $kT B$. *(0.5 mark)* **Discussion points expected (3 marks split):** - **EIRP** combines TX power and antenna gain; it is the only metric that drives the field strength into the channel. - **Polarization mismatch** is captured by PLF $= \cos^{2}\psi$; a 3 dB loss occurs when a circular wave hits a linearly polarized antenna. - **Path loss exponent** $n$ governs how rapidly signal decays with distance; in NLOS urban microcells $n$ can reach 3.5, requiring taller antennas or higher TX power to compensate. **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Mean path loss:** $$ L_{p}(d) = L_{p}(d_{0}) + 10n\log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right) $$ $$ 10n\log_{10}(500/100) = 10 \cdot 3.2 \cdot \log_{10}(5) = 32 \cdot 0.699 = 22.37 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{L_{p}(500\,\text{m}) = 80 + 22.37 = 102.37 \text{ dB}} \quad \text{*(2 marks)*} $$ **(ii) Fade margin for $P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$:** The Q-function inverse: $Q^{-1}(0.1) \approx 1.2816$. $$ F_{m} = \sigma \cdot Q^{-1}(P_{\text{out}}) = 8 \cdot 1.2816 = 10.25 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{F_{m} \approx 10.25 \text{ dB}} \quad \text{*(2 marks)*} $$ **(iii) Total path loss including fade margin:** $$ L_{\text{total}} = L_{p}(d) + F_{m} = 102.37 + 10.25 = 112.62 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{L_{\text{total}} \approx 112.62 \text{ dB}} \quad \text{*(3 marks)*} $$ This total loss must be overcome by the link budget — i.e., EIRP + RX antenna gain − RX sensitivity ≥ $L_{\text{total}}$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in channel-antenna design questions:** > > 1. **Unit mismatch in Friis (3 marks lost frequently)** — using $d$ in metres with the 32.44 constant. Always verify whether $d$ is in **km** and $f$ in **MHz** (constant = 32.44) or $d$ in m and $f$ in Hz (constant = $-147.55$). > > 2. **dBi vs dBd confusion (1–2 marks lost)** — $G_{\text{dBi}} = G_{\text{dBd}} + 2.15$. The KTU paper almost always expects dBi. A dipole gain of 2.15 dBi becomes 0 dBd. > > 3. **Forgetting polarization loss** — Many students write Friis in dB and forget the PLF term. If the question mentions any polarization mismatch angle, **you must include the PLF term**. > > 4. **Using $G$ instead of realized gain $G_{\text{realized}} = \eta_{r} \cdot D$** — If the question specifies a radiation efficiency $\eta_{r} < 1$, failing to apply it loses 1–2 marks. > > 5. **Confusing shadow fading margin with link margin** — Fade margin is computed using $\sigma$ and the inverse Q-function for the desired outage probability; do not confuse it with the system link margin which includes all other reserves. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Channel Antenna System Design** - ☐ **Friis transmission equation** is the cornerstone of all link budget calculations: $P_{r} = P_{t}G_{t}G_{r}(\lambda/4\pi d)^{2}$. - ☐ **EIRP** $= P_{t}G_{t}$; always expressed in **dBm or dBW** in KTU papers. - ☐ **Free-space path loss** at $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz: $L_{FS} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}d + 20\log_{10}f$. - ☐ **Effective aperture** $A_{e} = G\lambda^{2}/(4\pi)$; for isotropic antenna, $A_{e} = \lambda^{2}/(4\pi)$. - ☐ **Polarization loss factor** $\text{PLF} = \cos^{2}\psi$; = 0.5 (−3 dB) for circular-to-linear mismatch. - ☐ **Far-field condition** $d_{F} = 2D^{2}/\lambda$ — never apply Friis in the near field. - ☐ **Log-distance path loss** $L_{p}(d) = L_{p}(d_{0}) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_{0}) + X_{\sigma}$. - ☐ **Fade margin** $F_{m} = \sigma Q^{-1}(P_{\text{out}})$; e.g., for 90% coverage, $Q^{-1}(0.1) \approx 1.28$. - ☐ **Antenna height** improves path loss logarithmically in Hata/COST models — raising BTS by 10× gives 10–15 dB gain in suburban areas. - ☐ **Coherence distance** $F_{n} = \lambda/(2\sin\theta_{s})$ determines minimum antenna spacing for diversity. - ☐ **VSWR < 1.5** is a typical design target for broadband antennas; < 2.0 is the looser KTU minimum. - ☐ **Directivity approximation** $D \approx 41253 / (\theta_{1}\theta_{2})$ where beamwidths are in degrees. - ☐ **Polarization diversity** achieves decorrelation at only $\lambda/10$ spacing — a key advantage in mobile handsets. - ☐ **EIRP regulatory limits** exist for ISM bands (e.g., 36 dBm for 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi with high-gain antennas in some regions). - ☐ **Link budget flow:** EIRP → Free-space loss → Polarization loss → RX antenna gain → RX power vs RX sensitivity. - ☐ **Practical antenna choices:** BTS sector antenna (14–18 dBi), handset PIFA (−2 to +2 dBi), satellite dish (35–50 dBi), IoT chip antenna (−5 to 0 dBi).

topics.title: Large Scale Path Loss
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Large Scale Path Loss

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large-Scale Path Loss (LSPL)** is the average attenuation experienced by a radio signal as it propagates over a **large distance** (typically several hundreds or thousands of wavelengths) between the transmitter and the receiver. It represents the gradual, distance-dependent reduction in signal power due to free-space dispersion, ground reflection, diffraction over obstacles, and the macroscopic contour of the terrain.

In contrast to **small-scale fading** (which occurs over half-wavelength distances due to multipath interference), **large-scale path loss** captures the **macroscopic signal level trend** as a function of transmitter-receiver separation $d$. The general form is:

$$PL(d) \propto d^{n}$$

where $n$ is the **path loss exponent** whose value depends on the propagation environment (free space, urban, suburban, indoor, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Large-Scale Path Loss belongs to **Module 3: Radio Wave Propagation**, and it specifically addresses the mechanisms behind long-distance signal attenuation in cellular, Wi-Fi, satellite, and IoT systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **shouting to a friend**:

- 🏟️ **Inside an empty football stadium (Free Space):** Your voice travels almost equally well in all directions; attenuation is smooth and depends only on distance — this is analogous to **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)**.

- 🌳 **In a dense forest:** Your voice loses energy as it passes through trees, gets scattered by leaves, and blocked by trunks — this is similar to **obstructed urban/indoor path loss** where $n > 2$.

- 🏙️ **In a city street with tall buildings:** Your voice bends slightly around corners (diffraction), reflects off glass walls, and loses energy at every bounce — this resembles the **Okumura-Hata urban model**.

The key insight: the **farther** and **more obstructed** the propagation path, the **steeper** the path loss curve becomes.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Propagation Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Physical Cause | Impact on Path Loss |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Reflection** | Signal bounces off surfaces larger than $\lambda$ (buildings, ground) | Creates **multipath**, alters effective path length |
| **Diffraction** | Signal bends around sharp edges (rooftops, corners) | Allows reception in **shadowed regions** |
| **Scattering** | Signal disperses off small objects (foliage, lamp posts) | Causes **energy spread**, depolarization |
| **Free-space dispersion** | Spherical wavefront spreading | Dominant mechanism at **short LOS distances** |

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> Standard physical quantities used throughout this module:
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \;\text{m/s}$
> - **Wavelength relation:** $\lambda = c / f$
> - **Reference distance:** $d_{0}$ (typically **1 m** indoors, **100 m** or **1 km** outdoors)
> - **Path loss exponent:** $n$ (dimensionless, $\geq 2$)
> - **Operating frequency range:** $f \in [\text{300 MHz},\; \text{300 GHz}]$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Path Loss Modeling Approach

Modern wireless engineers model large-scale path loss in **three escalating layers** of accuracy:

### 🔹 Tier 1: Deterministic Free-Space Model
- **When to use:** Clear line-of-sight (LOS) conditions, no obstructions.
- **Formula core:** Inverse-square law from Friis equation.

### 🔹 Tier 2: Empirical / Statistical Models
- **When to use:** Macrocell (cell radius $\geq 1$ km) urban/suburban deployments.
- **Examples:** Okumura-Hata, COST-231 Hata, Longley-Rice.

### 🔹 Tier 3: Site-Specific Ray-Tracing Models
- **When to use:** Microcell/picocell indoor or dense urban scenarios.
- **Examples:** ITU Indoor Model, Dominant Path Model, 3GPP Ray Tracing.

---

## 2.2 Why Path Loss Occurs — Step-by-Step Physics

1. **Wavefront Spreading:** As an electromagnetic wave travels outward from an isotropic radiator, the energy is distributed over an expanding spherical surface of area $4\pi d^{2}$.
2. **Absorption:** Atmospheric gases (oxygen, water vapor) and precipitation absorb RF energy at certain frequency bands.
3. **Obstruction:** Buildings, foliage, and terrain block or attenuate the wave.
4. **Multipath Energy Loss:** Power arriving via scattered paths rarely adds coherently, leading to **effective** path loss even if total energy is conserved.
5. **Ground Reflection:** Destructive interference between direct and ground-reflected rays produces **additional loss** beyond free-space.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for solving KTU university questions on Large-Scale Path Loss.

| # | Model / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Friis Free-Space Eqn** | $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{2}$ | Watts; $G_{t}, G_{r}$ linear |
| 2 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $PL_{FS} = \left( \dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2} = \left( \dfrac{4\pi d f}{c} \right)^{2}$ | Power ratio (linear) |
| 3 | **FSPL in dB** | $PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 4 | **Effective Aperture** | $A_{e} = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$ | m²; relates area to gain |
| 5 | **Log-Distance Path Loss** | $PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{d}{d_{0}} \right)$ | $d_{0}$ reference distance |
| 6 | **Log-Normal Shadowing** | $PL(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_{0}) + 10n\log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{d}{d_{0}} \right) + X_{\sigma}$ | $X_{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2})$ |
| 7 | **Received Power (Log-normal)** | $P_{r}(d)[\text{dBm}] = P_{t}[\text{dBm}] - PL(d)[\text{dB}]$ | minus gains as needed |
| 8 | **Okumura-Hata (Urban)** | $L_{50} = 69.55 + 26.16\log_{10}(f_{c}) - 13.82\log_{10}(h_{te}) - a(h_{re}) + [44.9 - 6.55\log_{10}(h_{te})]\log_{10}(d)$ | dB; $f_{c}$ in MHz, $d$ in km |
| 9 | **Mobile Antenna Correction** (small/medium city) | $a(h_{re}) = [1.1\log_{10}(f_{c}) - 0.7] h_{re} - [1.56\log_{10}(f_{c}) - 0.8]$ | $h_{re}$ in metres |
| 10 | **Mobile Antenna Correction** (large city, $f_{c} \geq 300$ MHz) | $a(h_{re}) = 8.29[\log_{10}(1.54 h_{re})]^{2} - 1.1$ | dB |
| 11 | **COST-231 Hata** | $L_{50} = 46.3 + 33.9\log_{10}(f_{c}) - 13.82\log_{10}(h_{te}) - a(h_{re}) + [44.9 - 6.55\log_{10}(h_{te})]\log_{10}(d) + C_{M}$ | $C_{M}=0$ (suburban), $3$ dB (urban/metropolitan) |
| 12 | **ITU Indoor Path Loss** | $L_{\text{total}} = 20\log_{10}(f) + N\log_{10}(d) + L_{f}(n) - 28$ | $f$ in MHz, $d$ in m, $N$ distance-loss coefficient |
| 13 | **Floor Penetration Loss** | $L_{f}(n) = n \cdot L_{\text{per-floor}}$ | $L_{\text{per-floor}} \approx 4$–$6$ dB/floor (typical) |
| 14 | **Path Loss Exponent — Typical Values** | $n = 2$ (free space), $1.6$–$1.8$ (LOS indoor), $4$–$6$ (NLOS obstructed indoor), $3.7$–$6.5$ (urban macrocell) | dimensionless |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Path Loss Matters |
|--------|----------------------|
| **5G/6G Network Planning** | Determines **cell radius**, **gNodeB placement**, **handover boundaries**. |
| **IoT / LPWAN (LoRa, NB-IoT)** | Affects **link budget**; rural deployments use $n \approx 2.5$. |
| **Satellite Communications** | FSPL at GHz frequencies is extreme — drives **dish size** and **EIRP** requirements. |
| **Indoor Wi-Fi Design** | Floor attenuation determines **AP placement** across multi-storey buildings. |
| **Radar Systems** | $R^{4}$ round-trip loss in monostatic radar originates from path loss on Tx and Rx paths. |
| **Military / Tactical Comms** | Terrain-aware path loss models (TIREM, Longley-Rice) used for **EW planning**. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss from Friis Equation

**Goal:** Derive $PL_{FS}$ in dB form as $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Friis transmission equation:**

$$P_{r}(d) = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define path loss as the ratio of transmitted to received power (assuming unity gains):**

$$PL_{FS}(d) = \frac{P_{t}}{P_{r}} = \frac{1}{G_{t} G_{r}} \left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2}$$

Setting $G_{t} = G_{r} = 1$ (isotropic reference antennas):

$$PL_{FS}(d) = \left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$:**

$$PL_{FS}(d) = \left( \frac{4\pi d \, f}{c} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels (10·log₁₀ of power ratio):**

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[ \left( \frac{4\pi d f}{c} \right)^{2} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Use the log-power identity $10\log_{10}(x^{2}) = 20\log_{10}(x)$:**

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi d f}{c} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Expand using $\log(abc) = \log a + \log b + \log c$:**

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(c)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the constant using $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:**

$$20\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(3 \times 10^{8}) = 21.98 - 169.54 = -147.56$$

This yields the form with $d$ in metres and $f$ in Hz:

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = -147.56 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{m}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{Hz}})$$

**Step 8 — Re-normalize to convenient units ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz):**

Substituting $d_{\text{m}} = 10^{3} d_{\text{km}}$ and $f_{\text{Hz}} = 10^{6} f_{\text{MHz}}$:

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = -147.56 + 20\log_{10}(10^{3} d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(10^{6} f_{\text{MHz}})$$

$$\Rightarrow PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = -147.56 + 60 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 120 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$$

$$\boxed{PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Log-Distance Path Loss Model from Power Fall-Off

**Step 1 — Start with the generic power-law fall-off:**

$$P_{r}(d) \propto \frac{P_{t}}{d^{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio with respect to a reference distance $d_{0}$:**

$$\frac{P_{r}(d)}{P_{r}(d_{0})} = \left( \frac{d_{0}}{d} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the received power ratio and express as a path loss:**

$$PL(d) = \frac{P_{r}(d_{0})}{P_{r}(d)} = \left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB:**

$$PL(d)[\text{dB}] = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right)^{n} = 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Add the reference path loss $PL(d_{0})$ to anchor the curve:**

$$PL(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_{0})[\text{dB}] + 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the zero-mean Gaussian shadow-fading term $X_{\sigma}$ (variance $\sigma^{2}$):**

$$\boxed{PL(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_{0})[\text{dB}] + 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right) + X_{\sigma}, \quad X_{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})}$$

The random term $X_{\sigma}$ is what produces the **scattered dB pattern** around the mean trend — observable in any drive-test dataset.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Okumura-Hata Path Loss Exponent Behaviour

**Step 1 — Rewrite Okumura-Hata's $L_{50}$ expression:**

$$L_{50}(d) = A + B \log_{10}(d)$$

where the constant $A$ absorbs all terms independent of $d$, and the slope $B = 44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(h_{te})$ captures the dependence on transmitter height $h_{te}$.

**Step 2 — Compare with the log-distance model:**

$$PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Match coefficients:** the equivalent path-loss exponent is:

$$10n = B = 44.9 - 6.55\log_{10}(h_{te})$$

$$\boxed{n_{\text{eff}} = 4.49 - 0.655 \log_{10}(h_{te})}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check at $h_{te} = 100$ m:**

$$n_{\text{eff}} = 4.49 - 0.655 \log_{10}(100) = 4.49 - 1.31 = 3.18$$

This is consistent with measured urban macrocell values of $n \in [3, 4]$, confirming the **Okumura-Hata model reduces to a log-distance form** with an environment-dependent slope.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | Free-Space | Log-Distance | Okumura-Hata | ITU Indoor | COST-231 Hata |
|--------|-----------|--------------|--------------|------------|---------------|
| **Frequency Range** | Any | Any | 150–1500 MHz | 900 MHz – 5.2 GHz | 1500–2000 MHz |
| **Distance Range** | LOS only | Any | 1–20 km | Indoor: 10–100 m | 1–20 km |
| **$h_{te}$ Range** | N/A | N/A | 30–200 m | N/A | 30–200 m |
| **$h_{re}$ Range** | N/A | N/A | 1–10 m | N/A | 1–10 m |
| **Path-Loss Exponent $n$** | Exactly 2 | Tunable (1.6–6.5) | ~3–4 (urban) | Tunable (1.6–6) | ~3–4.5 |
| **Includes Shadowing?** | ❌ | ✅ ($X_{\sigma}$) | ❌ (mean only) | ❌ (mean only) | ❌ (mean only) |
| **KTU Typical Use** | Reference baseline | Generic link-budget | Macrocell (2G/3G/4G) | Wi-Fi / Indoor 5G | PCS / LTE / 5G NR |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define Free-Space Path Loss. Derive its expression in dB as a function of distance and frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Free-Space Path Loss is the attenuation experienced by an electromagnetic wave propagating in an unobstructed line-of-sight path, caused purely by spherical wavefront spreading. It is given by:

$$PL_{FS}(d) = \left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2}$$

Expressing in decibels with $\lambda = c/f$:

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

where $d$ is in km and $f$ in MHz. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is meant by path loss exponent? List typical values for free space, urban macrocell, and indoor NLOS environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **path loss exponent $n$** describes how rapidly the received signal power decays with distance in the log-distance model:

$$PL(d) \propto d^{n}$$

Typical values:

| Environment | $n$ |
|-------------|-----|
| Free space | 2.0 |
| Urban macrocell | 3.7 – 6.5 |
| Indoor NLOS (obstructed) | 4.0 – 6.0 |

A higher $n$ indicates **harsher attenuation** due to obstructions and multipath. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

#### (a) Derive the Free-Space Path Loss formula from first principles using the Friis transmission equation. A transmitter radiates 30 dBm at 900 MHz with a 6 dBi antenna. The receiver uses a 3 dBi antenna at 5 km in free space. Compute the received power in dBm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Friis transmission equation:**

$$P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define path loss with $G_{t} = G_{r} = 1$:**

$$PL_{FS} = \left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2} = \left( \frac{4\pi d f}{c} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dB:**

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $d = 5$ km and $f = 900$ MHz:**

$$PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(900)$$

$$= 32.44 + 13.98 + 59.08 = 105.50 \;\text{dB}$$

**Step 5 — Apply received power formula in dB:**

$$P_{r}(\text{dBm}) = P_{t}(\text{dBm}) + G_{t}(\text{dBi}) + G_{r}(\text{dBi}) - PL_{FS}(\text{dB})$$

$$P_{r} = 30 + 6 + 3 - 105.50 = -66.50 \;\text{dBm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P_{r} = -66.50 \;\text{dBm}}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Explain the Log-Normal Shadowing model with its mathematical formulation. Why is a Gaussian random variable added to the deterministic path loss? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Deterministic Log-Distance Model:**

The average large-scale path loss at distance $d$ from a reference distance $d_{0}$ is:

$$PL(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_{0})[\text{dB}] + 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right)$$

This models **mean attenuation** but ignores the random fluctuations observed in real measurements.

**Step 2 — Adding the Shadow Fading Term:**

Real measurements show that path loss values at the same distance $d$ vary across the coverage area due to different obstructions (buildings, trees, vehicles). This variation is modeled as a zero-mean Gaussian random variable:

$$PL(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_{0})[\text{dB}] + 10n \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{d}{d_{0}} \right) + X_{\sigma}$$

where $X_{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$ in dB.

**Step 3 — Physical Justification:**

- $X_{\sigma}$ captures the **shadowing effect** caused by large obstacles between Tx and Rx.
- The "log-normal" name arises because $PL$ in dB is normally distributed, hence **PL in linear scale is log-normally distributed**.
- Standard deviation $\sigma$ typically ranges from **6 dB (light shadowing)** to **12 dB (heavy shadowing)**.

**Step 4 — Engineering Use:**

- Used for **coverage probability analysis** in cellular planning.
- Required for calculating **outage probability** $P_{\text{out}} = Q(\gamma_{\text{th}}/\bar{\gamma})$.
- Essential for **handover margin** calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### (a) Describe the Okumura-Hata path loss model with its area-to-area correction factor for mobile antenna height. Compute the median path loss for a suburban macrocell with $f_{c} = 900$ MHz, $h_{te} = 50$ m, $h_{re} = 2$ m, and $d = 10$ km. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Okumura-Hata Urban Formula:**

$$L_{50}(\text{dB}) = 69.55 + 26.16 \log_{10}(f_{c}) - 13.82 \log_{10}(h_{te}) - a(h_{re}) + [44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(h_{te})] \log_{10}(d)$$

**Step 2 — Mobile Antenna Correction (small/medium city):**

$$a(h_{re}) = [1.1 \log_{10}(f_{c}) - 0.7] h_{re} - [1.56 \log_{10}(f_{c}) - 0.8]$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a(h_{re})$ at $f_{c} = 900$ MHz, $h_{re} = 2$ m:**

$$a(h_{re}) = [1.1 \log_{10}(900) - 0.7] \times 2 - [1.56 \log_{10}(900) - 0.8]$$

$$\log_{10}(900) = 2.954$$

$$a(h_{re}) = [1.1 \times 2.954 - 0.7] \times 2 - [1.56 \times 2.954 - 0.8]$$

$$= [3.249 - 0.7] \times 2 - [4.609 - 0.8]$$

$$= 2.549 \times 2 - 3.809 = 5.098 - 3.809 = 1.289 \;\text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into main formula at $h_{te} = 50$ m, $d = 10$ km:**

$$\log_{10}(50) = 1.699, \quad \log_{10}(10) = 1$$

$$L_{50} = 69.55 + 26.16 \times 2.954 - 13.82 \times 1.699 - 1.289 + [44.9 - 6.55 \times 1.699] \times 1$$

$$= 69.55 + 77.30 - 23.48 - 1.29 + [44.9 - 11.13]$$

$$= 122.08 + 33.77 = 155.85 \;\text{dB}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Urban → Suburban Correction:**

Suburban correction: $L_{\text{sub}} = L_{\text{urban}} - 2[\log_{10}(f_{c}/28)]^{2} - 5.4$

$$L_{\text{sub}} = 155.85 - 2[\log_{10}(900/28)]^{2} - 5.4$$

$$= 155.85 - 2[\log_{10}(32.14)]^{2} - 5.4 = 155.85 - 2(1.507)^{2} - 5.4$$

$$= 155.85 - 4.54 - 5.4 = 145.91 \;\text{dB}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_{50,\text{sub}} \approx 145.91 \;\text{dB}}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Explain the ITU Indoor Path Loss model with floor penetration loss factor. How does the distance-power loss coefficient $N$ vary across LOS and NLOS indoor scenarios? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — ITU Indoor Path Loss Formula:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 20\log_{10}(f) + N \log_{10}(d) + L_{f}(n) - 28$$

where:
- $f$ = frequency in MHz
- $d$ = distance in metres (must satisfy $d > 1$ m)
- $N$ = distance-power loss coefficient
- $L_{f}(n)$ = floor penetration loss factor for $n$ floors between Tx and Rx
- Constant **–28** arises from aggregating $20\log_{10}(c/4\pi)$ and unit conversions.

**Step 2 — Typical Values of $N$:**

| Environment | $N$ |
|-------------|-----|
| **Same floor, LOS** (corridor, hall) | 18 – 20 |
| **Same floor, NLOS** (through walls) | 28 – 32 |
| **Across one floor** (through slab) | 30 + floor loss |
| **Multi-floor NLOS** (across multiple slabs) | 38 – 44 |

**Step 3 — Floor Penetration Loss $L_{f}(n)$:**

Typical per-floor attenuation in modern buildings:

$$L_{\text{per floor}} \approx 4 \text{ to } 6 \;\text{dB}$$

For $n$ floors between transmitter and receiver:

$$L_{f}(n) = n \times L_{\text{per floor}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- The **$N\log_{10}(d)$** term is the **distance attenuation** within a floor.
- The **$L_{f}(n)$** term models **vertical penetration** through concrete slabs, metalized floors, and reinforced structures.
- LOS indoor propagation is **closest to free space** ($N \approx 20$, comparable to FSPL's $20\log_{10}(d)$ slope).
- NLOS shows **steeper slope** ($N \approx 30$) and additional penetration loss. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Large-Scale Path Loss Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 to –3 marks):** Mixing $d$ in metres with the **km-formula** constant 32.44, or $f$ in GHz with MHz formula. Always re-verify units before plugging numbers.
> 2. **Forgetting the Suburban Correction:** When the question says "suburban", many students use the **urban Okumura-Hata** formula directly. The proper approach: compute urban $L_{50}$, then subtract the suburban correction term.
> 3. **Skipping the Antenna Correction $a(h_{re})$:** Many students drop the $a(h_{re})$ term in Okumura-Hata. This costs **at least 2 marks**.
> 4. **Using $\log$ instead of $\log_{10}$:** In numerical computation, $\log$ on a calculator is sometimes $\log_{e}$ by default — switch to base-10 or use $\log_{10}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing Path Loss with Received Power:** Path loss (dB) is **positive** and large. Received power (dBm) is usually **negative** and small. The link-budget sign convention must be applied correctly.
> 6. **Shadow Fading Standard Deviation:** Always express $\sigma$ in dB and state its range (6–12 dB typical).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Large-Scale Path Loss (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Large-Scale Path Loss** models **average signal attenuation** over distances of tens to thousands of metres.
- ✅ **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):** $PL_{FS}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$.
- ✅ **Friis Equation:** $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} (\lambda/4\pi d)^{2}$ — anchor of all RF link budgets.
- ✅ **Log-Distance Model:** $PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_{0})$ — generic and tunable.
- ✅ **Log-Normal Shadowing:** Adds $X_{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$ in dB to capture terrain variability.
- ✅ **Path Loss Exponent $n$:** $\geq 2$; higher in obstructed/urban environments.
- ✅ **Okumura-Hata:** Valid for **150–1500 MHz**, **1–20 km**, macrocell. Includes $a(h_{re})$ correction.
- ✅ **COST-231 Hata:** Extension to **1500–2000 MHz** with $C_{M}$ correction (0 suburban / 3 dB urban).
- ✅ **ITU Indoor Model:** $L_{\text{total}} = 20\log(f) + N\log(d) + L_{f}(n) - 28$; uses $d$ in metres and $f$ in MHz.
- ✅ **Reference Distance $d_{0}$:** 1 m (indoor), 100 m (outdoor microcell), 1 km (macrocell).
- ✅ **Receive Power (dBm):** $P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - PL$ (all in dB).
- ✅ **Typical $\sigma$ for shadowing:** 6–12 dB.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications:** 5G NR cell planning, IoT link budgets, satellite FSPL, indoor Wi-Fi AP placement.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Always (i) state the model validity range, (ii) use consistent units, (iii) include antenna corrections where applicable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Space Propagation Loss Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Free Space Propagation Loss Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Free Space Propagation Loss Equation** (also called the **Friis Transmission Equation**) quantifies the ratio of transmitted power to received power between two antennas separated by a distance in an ideal, unobstructed propagation environment — a perfect vacuum or uniform medium with no absorption, reflection, or refraction.

In a free-space model, electromagnetic waves radiate spherically from an **isotropic radiator**, and the power received by a distant antenna depends on:
- Transmitted power ($P_t$)
- Gains of transmit and receive antennas ($G_t$, $G_r$)
- Distance between antennas ($d$)
- Operating wavelength ($\lambda$) or equivalently, frequency ($f$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The free-space model is the foundational benchmark against which all real-world propagation models (two-ray ground reflection, Okumura-Hata, COST-231, log-normal shadowing) are calibrated. Mastering it is non-negotiable for Module 3.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Invisible Lightbulb"

Imagine a bare lightbulb glowing in a perfectly dark, fogless room. The bulb radiates light **equally in every direction** (that's an isotropic radiator). If you walk further away from the bulb, the light that hits your eye becomes dimmer — not because the bulb got weaker, but because the **same total light is being spread over a larger sphere**.

The surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is $4\pi d^2$. So the **power density** (watts per square meter) at distance $d$ is:

$$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

Your eye, acting like an antenna, can only "catch" a small area of that expanding sphere. A **bigger eye (larger antenna)** catches more, and a **concentrated beam (high gain)** focuses power in a particular direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Free space loss is NOT loss due to atmosphere — it is purely the **geometric spreading** of energy over a larger surface as distance increases. No energy is actually "lost"; it is just spread thinner.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Metrics Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Typical Value |
|--------|----------|------|---------------|
| $P_t$ | Transmitted power | **Watts (W)** or **dBm** | 0.1 – 100 W |
| $P_r$ | Received power | **Watts (W)** or **dBm** | μW to pW range |
| $G_t, G_r$ | Antenna gains (linear) | dimensionless | 1 – 1000+ |
| $d$ | Distance | **meters (m)** or **km** | 1 m – 100 km |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength | **meters (m)** | 0.01 – 10 m |
| $f$ | Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** | 30 MHz – 300 GHz |
| $c$ | Speed of light | **m/s** | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is treated as exact for all KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Buildup

### Step 1: Idealized Isotropic Radiator
An **isotropic radiator** is a hypothetical, lossless antenna that radiates power **uniformly in all directions** in 3D space. Its gain $G_i = 1$ (or 0 dBi).

### Step 2: Power Density at Distance $d$
The transmitted power $P_t$ is distributed over the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$:

$$S_d = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

This is the **Poynting vector magnitude** for a spherical wavefront.

### Step 3: Effective Aperture of Receiving Antenna
A receiving antenna intercepts power based on its **effective aperture** $A_e$, defined as:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi} \quad \text{[m}^2\text{]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $A_e$:** It is the "collecting area" of the antenna. A larger aperture collects more energy.

### Step 4: Received Power Calculation
The received power is the product of incident power density and effective aperture:

$$P_r = S_d \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Friis Transmission Equation** — the heart of the free space propagation model.

### Step 5: Converting to Path Loss (dB)
The **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)** is defined as the ratio $P_t/P_r$ when both antennas are isotropic ($G_t = G_r = 1$):

$$\boxed{L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2}$$

In decibels:

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d\,\text{[km]}) + 20\log_{10}(f\,\text{[MHz]})$$

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

## 2.2 Why and How — The Reasoning Behind the Math

| Concept | Why It Matters | How It's Used |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| $1/(4\pi d^2)$ term | Geometric spreading of spherical wave | Determines basic distance dependence |
| $\lambda^2$ term | Reflects antenna aperture scaling | Higher frequency (smaller $\lambda$) → more loss! |
| $(4\pi)^2$ denominator | Combined effect of spreading + aperture | Constant normalization |
| 20 log d (not 10 log d) | Loss is **squared** in linear, so **doubled** in dB | Doubling distance increases loss by **6 dB** |
| 20 log f | Higher frequency = more path loss | Doubling frequency increases loss by **6 dB** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Power density | $S_d = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$ | W/m² |
| 2 | Effective aperture | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$ | m² |
| 3 | Friis transmission equation | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$ | Watts |
| 4 | Free space path loss (linear) | $L_{fs} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | FSPL in dB (SI units) | $L_{fs} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$ | dB |
| 6 | FSPL in dB (practical) | $L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | dB |
| 7 | Received power in dBm | $P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t\,[\text{dBi}] + G_r\,[\text{dBi}] - L_{fs}\,[\text{dB}]$ | dBm |
| 8 | Doubling distance | $\Delta L = +6$ dB | — |
| 9 | Doubling frequency | $\Delta L = +6$ dB | — |
| 10 | Wavelength relation | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{f}$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** When using formula (6), $d$ must be in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**. If you mix units (e.g., $d$ in m and $f$ in Hz), the constant 32.44 will be wrong. Always verify units first!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The free space equation is essential for:
- **Link budget analysis** in satellite communication (e.g., VSAT, GPS)
- **Radar range equation** derivations
- **5G/6G mmWave** cell planning (where high $f$ makes loss severe)
- **Antenna range characterization** in anechoic chambers
- **Reference baseline** for comparing urban/rural propagation measurements
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Isotropic Radiation to Friis Equation

### Derivation 1: Power Density at Distance $d$

**Starting concept:** An isotropic radiator emits power $P_t$ uniformly through a sphere of radius $d$.

The total surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is $A = 4\pi d^2$.

Since the power is distributed uniformly:

$$S_d = \frac{P_t}{\text{Surface area of sphere}} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

For a **directional transmit antenna** with gain $G_t$ (which concentrates power along a preferred axis), the on-axis power density becomes:

$$\boxed{S_d = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}}$$

### Derivation 2: Effective Aperture of a Receiving Antenna

The **effective aperture** $A_e$ relates an antenna's physical capture cross-section to its gain and the operating wavelength. From electromagnetic theory, the maximum effective aperture of any antenna is:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

**Reasoning:** The gain $G_r$ describes how much better the antenna collects energy compared to an isotropic radiator. The factor $\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ represents the natural "aperture" of an isotropic antenna at wavelength $\lambda$.

### Derivation 3: Complete Friis Transmission Equation

The received power is the product of the incident power density and the effective collecting area:

$$P_r = S_d \cdot A_e$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$P_r = \left(\frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}}$$

This is the **Friis Free Space Transmission Equation**.

### Derivation 4: Free Space Path Loss in dB

The path loss is defined as:

$$L_{fs} = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{(4\pi)^2 d^2}{G_t G_r \lambda^2}$$

Assuming isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$):

$$L_{fs} = \frac{(4\pi d)^2}{\lambda^2} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

Taking $10\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

Substituting $\lambda = c/f$:

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

Converting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s to convenient units:

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(3 \times 10^8)$$

Computing the constant terms:
- $20\log_{10}(4\pi) = 20 \times 1.0969 = 21.94$ dB
- $-20\log_{10}(3 \times 10^8) = -20 \times 8.477 = -169.54$ dB
- Combined: $21.94 - 169.54 = -147.60$ dB

So with $d$ in meters and $f$ in Hz:

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = -147.60 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$$

When $d$ is in **km** ($d_{km} = d_m/1000$) and $f$ is in **MHz** ($f_{MHz} = f_{Hz}/10^6$):

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = -147.60 + 20\log_{10}(1000 \cdot d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(10^6 \cdot f_{MHz})$$

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = -147.60 + 60 + 120 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

$$\boxed{L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Changes

| Change | Effect on $L_{fs}$ (dB) | Reason |
|--------|------------------------|--------|
| Distance $d$ doubles | $+6$ dB | $20\log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \approx 6$ |
| Distance $d$ × 10 | $+20$ dB | $20\log_{10}(10) = 20$ |
| Frequency $f$ doubles | $+6$ dB | $20\log_{10}(2) = 6.02$ |
| Frequency $f$ × 10 | $+20$ dB | $20\log_{10}(10) = 20$ |
| Frequency $f$ → 10$f$ and $d$ → 10$d$ | $+40$ dB | $20+20$ |
| Antenna gain doubles | $-3$ dB (less loss) | $10\log_{10}(2) = 3.01$ |
| Wavelength halves (freq doubles) | $+6$ dB | Inverse $\lambda^2$ dependence |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A transmitter operating at **2.4 GHz** radiates **10 W** through a transmit antenna of gain **12 dBi**. The receiver is located **5 km** away with a receive antenna gain of **8 dBi**. Calculate:
(a) The free space path loss in dB.
(b) The received power in dBm.

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Using the practical formula with $f = 2400$ MHz, $d = 5$ km:

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(2400)$$

$$20\log_{10}(5) = 20 \times 0.69897 = 13.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.60 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 13.98 + 67.60 = 114.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Part (b):** Convert $P_t = 10$ W to dBm:

$$P_t\,[\text{dBm}] = 10\log_{10}(10{,}000) = 40 \text{ dBm}$$

Using the link budget equation:

$$P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}$$

$$P_r = 40 + 12 + 8 - 114.02 = -54.02 \text{ dBm}$$

Converting back to Watts:

$$P_r = 10^{(-54.02 - 30)/10} = 10^{-8.402} \approx 3.96 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 3.96 \text{ nW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **realistic** received power level for a Wi-Fi link at 5 km — extremely weak, illustrating why high-gain antennas and sensitive receivers are essential.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Friis Equation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transmitter Pt watts] --> B[Transmit Antenna Gain Gt linear]
    B --> C[Power Density at Distance d]
    C --> D[Effective Aperture of Rx Antenna]

    F[Frequency f Hz] --> G[Wavelength lambda c over f]
    G --> D
    H[Distance d meters] --> C
    H --> I[Path Loss Ratio Pt over Pr]
    I --> J[Path Loss in dB]

    F --> J

    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Variables and Their Interactions

```mermaid

    A[Distance d] -->|increases| B[Path Loss Lfs]

    E --> F
    F -->|net effect| G[Final Link Quality]

    style A fill:#E0FFFF
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#E0FFFF
    style D fill:#E6E6FA
    style E fill:#98FB98
    style F fill:#FFA07A
    style G fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.4 Path Loss Behavior vs Distance (Log-Log Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Log Distance d] -->|20 dB per decade| B[Log Path Loss Lfs]
    C[Distance 10 m] --> D[Lfs reference]
    E[Distance 100 m] --> F[Lfs plus 20 dB]
    G[Distance 1 km] --> H[Lfs plus 40 dB]
    I[Distance 10 km] --> J[Lfs plus 60 dB]

    style A fill:#B0E0E6
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Free Space Path Loss. State the Friis transmission equation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Free Space Path Loss (FSPL):** It is the loss in signal strength of an electromagnetic wave due to geometric spreading of the wavefront as it propagates through free space (vacuum or ideal medium with no absorption, reflection, or scattering). It is a function of distance and frequency only.

**Friis Transmission Equation:**

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$$

where $P_r$ = received power, $P_t$ = transmitted power, $G_t$ and $G_r$ = transmit and receive antenna gains, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $d$ = distance between antennas.

**Free Space Path Loss (isotropic case):**

$$L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \quad \text{or} \quad L_{fs}[\text{dB}] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

---

### Question 2: Why does free space path loss increase with frequency? What is the effect of doubling the distance? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Reason for frequency dependence:** The effective aperture of a receiving antenna is given by $A_e = \lambda^2 G_r/(4\pi)$. Since $\lambda = c/f$, a higher frequency means a smaller wavelength, which reduces the antenna's effective collecting area. Since $P_r \propto \lambda^2$, doubling the frequency (halving the wavelength) reduces received power by a factor of 4, i.e., **+6 dB more loss**.

**Effect of doubling distance:** From $L_{fs} = (4\pi d/\lambda)^2$, doubling $d$ quadruples the path loss, which corresponds to an increase of $20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6$ dB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write "doubling distance doubles the loss" — this is **wrong**. Loss is a power ratio and **squares** with distance, giving a **6 dB increase** per doubling in free space.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Derive the Friis free space transmission equation. A 5 GHz microwave link uses a transmitter with output power of 1 W and transmit antenna gain of 20 dBi. The receiver is located 50 km away and has an antenna gain of 15 dBi. Calculate:**
**(a)** The free space path loss in dB. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The received power in dBm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Derivation of Friis Equation:**

Consider an isotropic radiator transmitting power $P_t$. At a distance $d$, the power is uniformly distributed over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$.

The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) is:

$$S_d = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

If a directional transmit antenna with gain $G_t$ is used, the on-axis power density becomes:

$$S_d = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

The receiving antenna intercepts this power density through its effective aperture:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

The received power is the product of power density and effective aperture:

$$P_r = S_d \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$$

This is the **Friis Transmission Equation**.

**(a) Free Space Path Loss:**

Given: $f = 5$ GHz $= 5000$ MHz, $d = 50$ km.

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(50) + 20\log_{10}(5000)$$

$$20\log_{10}(50) = 20 \times 1.6990 = 33.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(5000) = 20 \times 3.6990 = 73.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 33.98 + 73.98 = 140.40 \text{ dB}$$

**(b) Received Power:**

Convert transmitted power to dBm:

$$P_t = 1 \text{ W} = 1000 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_t\,[\text{dBm}] = 10\log_{10}(1000) = 30 \text{ dBm}$$

Link budget:

$$P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}$$

$$P_r = 30 + 20 + 15 - 140.40 = -75.40 \text{ dBm}$$

Converting to Watts:

$$P_r = 10^{(-75.40 - 30)/10} = 10^{-10.54} \approx 2.88 \times 10^{-11} \text{ W} = 28.8 \text{ pW}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Explain the concept of effective aperture of an antenna. With the help of the Friis transmission equation, derive the free space path loss equation. A satellite at a height of 36,000 km transmits at 4 GHz with 10 W power. The satellite antenna has a gain of 30 dBi and the ground station antenna has a gain of 50 dBi. Calculate:**
**(a)** The free space path loss. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The received power at the ground station. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Effective Aperture Concept:**

The effective aperture $A_e$ of a receiving antenna is the equivalent area that, when multiplied by the incident power density, gives the power delivered to the matched load. It is related to the antenna gain and wavelength by:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

**Derivation of FSPL:**

From the Friis equation:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$$

The path loss (assuming isotropic antennas, $G_t = G_r = 1$) is:

$$L_{fs} = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{(4\pi)^2 d^2}{\lambda^2} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\lambda = c/f$:

$$L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$

In dB:

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

$$= 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(c)$$

For $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz (with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s):

$$L_{fs}\,\text{[dB]} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

**(a) Free Space Path Loss:**

Given: $d = 36{,}000$ km, $f = 4$ GHz $= 4000$ MHz.

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(36000) + 20\log_{10}(4000)$$

$$20\log_{10}(36000) = 20 \times 4.5563 = 91.13 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(4000) = 20 \times 3.6021 = 72.04 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 91.13 + 72.04 = 195.61 \text{ dB}$$

**(b) Received Power:**

Convert $P_t = 10$ W to dBm:

$$P_t\,[\text{dBm}] = 10\log_{10}(10000) = 40 \text{ dBm}$$

Link budget:

$$P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}$$

$$P_r = 40 + 30 + 50 - 195.61 = -75.61 \text{ dBm}$$

Converting to Watts:

$$P_r = 10^{(-75.61 - 30)/10} = 10^{-10.561} \approx 2.75 \times 10^{-11} \text{ W} \approx 27.5 \text{ pW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch (2 marks lost):** Mixing $d$ in m with the "32.44" formula, or forgetting to convert GHz to MHz. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **dBm vs dBW confusion (2 marks lost):** $10\text{ W} = 40$ dBm $= 10$ dBW. Mixing these costs marks.
> 3. **Skipping derivation (3-5 marks lost):** Part (a) always carries the derivation. Writing only the final formula without showing the path from Friis to FSPL will cost the full 7 marks.
> 4. **Antenna gain in dBi vs linear:** Conversions must be shown: $G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(G_{linear})$ and vice versa.
> 5. **Forgetting the $(4\pi)^2$ denominator:** A common error when rushing — leads to off-by-roughly-22 dB mistakes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [ ] **Friis Transmission Equation** (memorize): $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$
- [ ] **FSPL formula (memorize):** $L_{fs}[\text{dB}] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$
- [ ] **Units contract:** $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz** when using constant 32.44. Don't use this constant with SI units.
- [ ] **Path loss exponent in free space = 2:** Path loss is proportional to $d^2$ (not 10$d$). In log scale, $20\log_{10}(d)$ dominates.
- [ ] **Distance-doubling rule:** $+6$ dB loss per doubling of distance.
- [ ] **Frequency-doubling rule:** $+6$ dB loss per doubling of frequency.
- [ ] **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (constant for all KTU problems).
- [ ] **Effective aperture:** $A_e = \lambda^2 G_r / (4\pi)$ — smaller $\lambda$ means smaller antenna aperture.
- [ ] **dBm conversion:** $P[\text{dBm}] = 10\log_{10}(P[\text{mW}])$.
- [ ] **Link budget order:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}$ (all in dB/dBi/dBm).
- [ ] **Why 32.44?** Because $20\log_{10}(4\pi \cdot 10^3 / 3 \cdot 10^8) \cdot 10^6$ — emerges from unit conversion factors. Don't just memorize, understand the units!
- [ ] **Free space is a benchmark:** Real-world path loss is always higher due to ground reflection, obstacles, atmospheric absorption.
- [ ] **High frequency, high loss:** mmWave (28 GHz, 60 GHz) suffers enormous free-space loss — drives 5G small-cell densification.
- [ ] **Antenna gain directionality matters:** A high-gain antenna must be aimed accurately to deliver the gain benefit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Loss of NLOS and LOS Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Path Loss of NLOS and LOS Systems ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Path Loss? **Path Loss (PL)** is the reduction in power density (or signal strength) of an electromagnetic wave as it propagates through space between a transmitter and a receiver. It is a deterministic measure of the attenuation experienced by the signal due to the propagation channel, and is the most fundamental quantity in wireless link budget design. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term "Path Loss" is formally defined as the ratio (in dB) of the transmitted power to the received power, assuming isotropic transmit and receive antennas: $$PL(dB) = 10 \log_{10}\frac{P_t}{P_r}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim-style):** > Path loss is defined as the difference (in decibels) between the effective transmitted power and the received power, representing the attenuation suffered by the signal as a function of distance, frequency, and the surrounding environment. --- ### 1.2 LOS vs NLOS — The Two Fundamental Propagation Regimes **1. Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation** A **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** link exists when the transmitter (Tx) and the receiver (Rx) are connected by an unobstructed, straight electromagnetic path. The first Fresnel zone is clear of obstacles, and the dominant received signal energy travels along the direct geometric ray. LOS is the foundational model in **satellite communication, microwave line-of-sight links, and rural macrocell deployments**. **2. Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) Propagation** In a **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS)** scenario, the direct geometric ray is blocked by a building, terrain, foliage, or other obstruction. The receiver picks up energy from **reflected, diffracted, and scattered** replicas of the transmitted signal. NLOS is the rule in **dense urban cellular systems, indoor Wi-Fi, and vehicular networks**, and is the principal cause of the so-called "coverage holes" in cellular networks. --- ### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Stadium Wave" 🎤 Imagine you are cheering in a large circular stadium: - **LOS (Front row seats):** Your voice travels directly to a friend sitting in the front row, unobstructed. The friend hears you **loud and clear**, much like an LOS microwave link. - **NLOS (Behind the pillar):** Your friend moves behind a concrete pillar. They can no longer see you, but they still **hear a muffled, bouncing echo** of your voice — the sound has reflected off walls, ceilings, and other surfaces. This is analogous to NLOS reception: the signal is weaker, delayed, and distorted. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Hook:** LOS = Direct ray dominates. NLOS = Direct ray blocked; only reflections, diffraction, and scattering survive. Path loss is the price the signal pays for the journey. --- ### 1.4 Why Path Loss Modeling Matters in Engineering - **Cellular network planning:** Determining cell radius, BS antenna height, and transmit power. - **Wi-Fi deployment:** Optimal AP placement in offices and homes. - **5G/6G mmWave design:** Critical at 28 GHz, 38 GHz, and 60 GHz bands. - **Satellite link budgets:** Power budgeting for GEO/MEO/LEO constellations. - **IoT and LPWAN:** Range estimation for LoRa, NB-IoT, Sigfox deployments. - **Public safety and military comms:** Predicting coverage in challenging terrain. The standard reference parameters used throughout this module are: - **Carrier frequency (f)** in **MHz** or **GHz** - **Transmitter–Receiver separation (d)** in **km** or **m** - **Path Loss Exponent (n)** — dimensionless, environment-dependent - **Path Loss (PL)** in **dB** ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Path Loss Models KTU 2024 Scheme classifies path loss models into the following three tiers: | Tier | Model Class | Application | |------|-------------|-------------| | **1. Deterministic LOS Models** | Free-Space (Friis), Two-Ray Ground Reflection | Saturated LOS, macrocell, satellite | | **2. Empirical / Statistical Models** | Log-Distance, Log-Normal Shadowing | General outdoor and indoor prediction | | **3. Empirical Outdoor Models** | Okumura–Hata, COST-231 Hata, Walfisch–Ikegami | Urban / suburban macrocells, 150–2000 MHz and up to 5 GHz | --- ### 2.2 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — The LOS Benchmark The Friis free-space equation states that the received power over distance *d* at wavelength *λ* is: $$P_r(d) = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ The corresponding free-space path loss is: $$FSPL(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$ - Valid only when **d > d₀** (the far-field Fraunhofer distance, $d_0 = 2D^2/\lambda$). - Implies path loss exponent **n = 2**. --- ### 2.3 Two-Ray Ground Reflection Model (LOS, planar earth) When a **ground-reflected ray** adds constructively/destructively to the LOS ray at the receiver, the average received power (for **d > h_t h_r / λ**) simplifies to: $$P_r(d) \approx P_t G_t G_r \frac{h_t^{2} h_r^{2}}{d^{4}}$$ Equivalently, the path loss exponent becomes **n = 4**, which is much faster decay than free space: $$PL_{2-ray}(dB) \approx 40\log_{10}(d) - 10\log_{10}(h_t^{2} h_r^{2})$$ --- ### 2.4 Log-Distance Path Loss Model The general empirical log-distance model is: $$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$ where: - **PL(d₀)** = known path loss at reference distance *d₀* (typically 1 m or 1 km) - **n** = path loss exponent (empirically measured) - **d** = T-R separation --- ### 2.5 Log-Normal Shadowing (NLOS) Real channels are random; shadowing from large obstructions causes a **log-normal** distribution of received power about the mean: $$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}$$ where $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2})$ is a **zero-mean Gaussian random variable** (in dB). Typical σ is **6 to 12 dB** for outdoor and **4 to 10 dB** for indoor. --- ### 2.6 Okumura–Hata Model (Outdoor Urban/Suburban) The Okumura model was empirically derived in **1968** in Tokyo. Hata (1980) converted it to closed-form expressions. Valid for **150 MHz ≤ f ≤ 1500 MHz**, **1 km ≤ d ≤ 20 km**. Standard formula: $$PL_{urban}(dB) = 69.55 + 26.16\log_{10}(f_c) - 13.82\log_{10}(h_b) - a(h_m) + [44.9 - 6.55\log_{10}(h_b)]\log_{10}(d)$$ where the mobile antenna height correction factor is: $$a(h_m) = [1.1 \log_{10}(f_c) - 0.7] h_m - [1.56 \log_{10}(f_c) - 0.8]$$ Corrections for other environments: - **Suburban:** $-2 [\log_{10}(f_c/28)]^{2} - 5.4$ - **Open rural:** $-4.78 [\log_{10}(f_c)]^{2} + 18.33 \log_{10}(f_c) - 40.94$ --- ### 2.7 COST-231 Hata Extension (Up to 2 GHz) Extends Okumura–Hata to **1500 MHz – 2000 MHz**: $$PL_{COST}(dB) = 46.3 + 33.9\log_{10}(f_c) - 13.82\log_{10}(h_b) - a(h_m) + [44.9 - 6.55\log_{10}(h_b)]\log_{10}(d) + C$$ where **C = 0 dB** for suburban and **C = 3 dB** for urban metropolitan centers. --- ### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **master reference** for the Module 3 board exam. Memorize every row. | # | Model | Formula (Compact Form) | Path Loss Exponent (n) | Typical σ (dB) | Frequency / Range | |---|-------|-----------------------|------------------------|----------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Free Space (Friis) — LOS** | $PL = 32.44 + 20\log d + 20\log f$ | 2 | 0 | All, d > Fraunhofer | | 2 | **Two-Ray Ground — LOS** | $PL \approx 40\log d - 20\log(h_t h_r)$ | 4 | 0 | LOS with ground bounce | | 3 | **Log-Distance** | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log(d/d_0)$ | 1.6 – 6 | 0 | General | | 4 | **Log-Normal Shadowing — NLOS** | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$ | 1.6 – 6 | 4 – 12 | Outdoor/indoor | | 5 | **Okumura–Hata Urban** | $69.55 + 26.16\log f - 13.82\log h_b - a(h_m) + [44.9 - 6.55\log h_b]\log d$ | ~3.5 | 8 – 10 | 150 – 1500 MHz, 1–20 km | | 6 | **COST-231 Hata** | $46.3 + 33.9\log f - 13.82\log h_b - a(h_m) + [44.9 - 6.55\log h_b]\log d + C$ | ~3.5 | 8 – 10 | 1500 – 2000 MHz | **Typical Path Loss Exponent Values (KTU Favorite Table):** | Environment | n | σ (dB) | |-------------|---|--------| | Free space | 2 | 0 | | Urban cellular | 2.7 – 3.5 | 6 – 12 | | Shadowed urban | 3 – 5 | 6 – 12 | | In LOS building | 1.6 – 1.8 | 4 – 6 | | Obstructed building | 4 – 6 | ~7 | | Indoor LOS | 1.6 | 3 – 6 | | Indoor NLOS | 3 – 4 | 7 – 12 | --- ### 2.9 The "Why" Behind the Path Loss Exponent - **n = 2 → Free space:** Power spreads over the surface of a sphere, $\propto d^2$. - **n = 3 → Urban canyon (single ground bounce + wall reflections):** Power spreads as $\propto d^3$. - **n = 4 → Two-ray ground model:** When the ground-reflected ray cancels the LOS ray, the dominant energy follows $d^4$. -
