This is what gives us the tractable *knife-edge diffraction* model used in practice. ## 2.2 Fresnel Zones — The Geometric Heart of Diffraction A **Fresnel zone** is an elliptical region around the direct line-of-sight path, bounded by the locus of points for which the *excess path length* (compared to the direct LOS path) is a constant multiple of half a wavelength. For the **$n$-th Fresnel zone**, the excess path length is $n \lambda / 2$. The radius of the $n$-th Fresnel zone at distance $d_1$ from the transmitter and $d_2$ from the receiver is: $$r_n = \sqrt{\frac{n \lambda d_1 d_2}{d_1 + d_2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering rule of thumb (KTU favourite):** For nearly free-space propagation, the first Fresnel zone (60% clearance) must be **at least 55% unobstructed** (i.e., $r_1 \times 0.55$ clearance) for the path loss to equal that of free space. If the first Fresnel zone is fully blocked, significant diffraction loss occurs. The "obstruction parameter" for an obstacle that protrudes a height $h$ into the first Fresnel zone is: $$F = \frac{2 h}{\sqrt{\lambda d_1 d_2 / (d_1 + d_2)}}$$ This is closely related to the **Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction parameter** $v$, which we derive in Section 3. ## 2.3 Knife-Edge Diffraction Model The simplest and most widely used diffraction model assumes the obstacle is a **perfectly absorbing, infinitely thin semi-infinite screen** (a "knife edge") with a height $h$ above the direct LOS path. The receiver may be located in either the *illuminated region*, the *shadow region*, or the *transition region*. The classical solution uses **Fresnel integrals**: $$F(v) = C(v) - j S(v) = \frac{1}{2} \int_{v}^{\infty} \left[ (1 + j) \cos\left(\frac{\pi t^2}{2}\right) - (1 - j) \sin\left(\frac{\pi t^2}{2}\right) \right] dt$$ Where: - $C(v) = \int_{0}^{v} \cos(\pi t^2 / 2) \, dt$ is the **Fresnel cosine integral**, - $S(v) = \int_{0}^{v} \sin(\pi t^2 / 2) \, dt$ is the **Fresnel sine integral**, - $v$ is the **Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction parameter** (dimensionless). The **diffraction gain (or loss)** relative to free space is: $$G_d (dB) = 20 \log_{10} \left| F(v) \right|$$ The total received power with diffraction is therefore: $$P_r (dBm) = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{free} - L_{diff}$$ Where $L_{diff} = -G_d$ is the diffraction loss in dB. ## 2.4 Multiple Knife-Edge Diffraction (Practical Case) In real terrains, a single knife edge is rare. **Multiple knife-edge diffraction** (MKED) handles this. Common engineering approximations used in KTU syllabus: 1. **Bullington's equivalent knife-edge method** — Replaces all obstacles by a single equivalent edge at the angle of intersection of the lines from Tx and Rx to the obstacle tops. 2. **Epstein-Peterson method** — Treats each edge sequentially, computing the diffraction loss at each edge using the path profile between successive edges. 3. **Deygout method** — Identifies the dominant (largest $v$) edge, computes its loss, then recurses on the two sub-paths on either side. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength | m | | 2 | $r_n = \sqrt{n \lambda d_1 d_2 / (d_1 + d_2)}$ | Radius of $n$-th Fresnel zone | m | | 3 | $r_1 = \sqrt{\lambda d_1 d_2 / (d_1 + d_2)}$ | First Fresnel zone radius | m | | 4 | $v = h \sqrt{2(d_1 + d_2) / (\lambda d_1 d_2)}$ | Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction parameter (knife edge) | dimensionless | | 5 | $v = \alpha \sqrt{2 d_1 d_2 / (\lambda (d_1 + d_2))}$ | Alt. form using angle $\alpha$ (radians) | dimensionless | | 6 | $G_d = 20 \log_{10} \vert F(v) \vert$ | Knife-edge diffraction gain (signed) | dB | | 7 | $F(v) = C(v) - j S(v)$ | Complex Fresnel integral | — | | 8 | $C(v), S(v)$ | Fresnel cosine/sine integrals | — | | 9 | $L_{diff} (dB) = -G_d (dB)$ | Diffraction loss (positive dB) | dB | | 10 | $L_{total} = L_{free} + L_{diff}$ | Total path loss with diffraction | dB | | 11 | $v > 1$ | Significant diffraction loss region | — | | 12 | $-0.78 < v < 0$ | Minor clearance, slight gain (≈ +1 dB) | — | | 13 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{total}$ | Received power (dBm) | dBm | > [!TIP] > **Sign convention trap:** $G_d$ in the formula is signed: positive when $|F(v)| > 1$ (obstruction is below LOS — slight gain) and negative when in deep shadow. The *diffraction loss* is the negative of $G_d$, so loss is positive when the path is obstructed. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Diffraction analysis is used in: - **Cellular network planning** (4G/5G macrocells) — predicting coverage in urban canyons and hilly terrain. - **Broadcast engineering** (FM radio, DTV) — placing transmitters to maximise shadow-zone coverage. - **Radar and microwave link design** — ensuring line-of-sight clearance. - **Indoor propagation modelling** — modelling diffraction around furniture and partitions. - **Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications** — diffraction at building corners and other vehicles. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fresnel Zone Radius $r_n$ **Setup:** A transmitter $T$ and receiver $R$ are separated by a total distance $d = d_1 + d_2$, where $d_1$ is the distance from $T$ to a point $P$ on the propagation plane, and $d_2$ is the distance from $P$ to $R$. The direct line-of-sight path has length $d = d_1 + d_2$. A point $Q$ on the wavefront at perpendicular distance $r_n$ from the LOS line has a longer path $T \to Q \to R$ than the direct LOS path $T \to R$. **Step 1:** Path length of the indirect route through $Q$: $$T \to Q \to R = \sqrt{d_1^2 + r_n^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + r_n^2}$$ **Step 2:** Direct LOS path length: $$T \to R = d_1 + d_2$$ **Step 3:** Excess path length $\Delta$ is defined as: $$\Delta = \sqrt{d_1^2 + r_n^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + r_n^2} - (d_1 + d_2)$$ **Step 4:** For $r_n \ll d_1$ and $r_n \ll d_2$ (small-angle/paraxial approximation), we apply the binomial expansion $\sqrt{d^2 + r^2} \approx d + r^2/(2d)$: $$\sqrt{d_1^2 + r_n^2} \approx d_1 + \frac{r_n^2}{2 d_1}, \quad \sqrt{d_2^2 + r_n^2} \approx d_2 + \frac{r_n^2}{2 d_2}$$ **Step 5:** Substituting back: $$\Delta \approx \left(d_1 + \frac{r_n^2}{2 d_1}\right) + \left(d_2 + \frac{r_n^2}{2 d_2}\right) - (d_1 + d_2) = \frac{r_n^2}{2 d_1} + \frac{r_n^2}{2 d_2}$$ **Step 6:** Simplify: $$\Delta = \frac{r_n^2}{2} \left( \frac{1}{d_1} + \frac{1}{d_2} \right) = \frac{r_n^2}{2} \cdot \frac{d_1 + d_2}{d_1 d_2}$$ **Step 7:** For the $n$-th Fresnel zone, the excess path length equals $n \lambda / 2$: $$\frac{r_n^2}{2} \cdot \frac{d_1 + d_2}{d_1 d_2} = \frac{n \lambda}{2}$$ **Step 8:** Solve for $r_n$: $$r_n^2 = \frac{n \lambda d_1 d_2}{d_1 + d_2}$$ $$\boxed{r_n = \sqrt{\frac{n \lambda d_1 d_2}{d_1 + d_2}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logical check:** When $d_1 = d_2 = d/2$ (midpoint), the formula reduces to $r_n = \sqrt{n \lambda d / 2}$, which is the well-known result for the symmetric case. This serves as a sanity check. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Fresnel-Kirchhoff Diffraction Parameter $v$ **Setup:** A single knife-edge obstacle of height $h$ protrudes above the straight line connecting transmitter and receiver. The distances from the obstacle to the transmitter and receiver are $d_1$ and $d_2$ respectively, and the total path length is $d = d_1 + d_2$. **Step 1:** Consider the path from $T$ to a point at the top of the knife edge, then to $R$. Its length is: $$L = \sqrt{d_1^2 + h^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + h^2}$$ **Step 2:** The direct LOS path length is $d = d_1 + d_2$. The excess path length is: $$\Delta = \sqrt{d_1^2 + h^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + h^2} - (d_1 + d_2)$$ **Step 3:** For $h \ll d_1$ and $h \ll d_2$ (typical for diffracting edges), binomial expansion gives: $$\Delta \approx \frac{h^2}{2 d_1} + \frac{h^2}{2 d_2} = \frac{h^2 (d_1 + d_2)}{2 d_1 d_2}$$ **Step 4:** The phase difference (in radians) corresponding to this excess path is: $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{h^2 (d_1 + d_2)}{2 d_1 d_2} = \frac{\pi h^2 (d_1 + d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}$$ **Step 5:** The **Fresnel-Kirchhoff parameter** $v$ is defined such that the corresponding phase shift is $\Delta \phi = v^2 \pi / 2$. Setting: $$\frac{\pi h^2 (d_1 + d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2} = \frac{v^2 \pi}{2}$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $v$: $$v^2 = \frac{2 h^2 (d_1 + d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}$$ $$\boxed{v = h \sqrt{\frac{2 (d_1 + d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}}}$$ **Alternate form using the diffraction angle $\alpha$** (small-angle approximation, $\alpha \approx h/d_1 + h/d_2$): $$v = \alpha \sqrt{\frac{2 d_1 d_2}{\lambda (d_1 + d_2)}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering shortcut:** When $d_1 = d_2 = d/2$ (symmetric case), $v = 2h / \sqrt{\lambda d}$. This is a useful form for quick numerical estimates in KTU problems. ## 3.3 Diffraction Loss from Fresnel Integrals **Step 1:** The complex amplitude at the receiver is normalised to free-space as the **complex Fresnel integral**: $$F(v) = \frac{1 + j}{2} \int_{v}^{\infty} e^{-j \pi t^2 / 2} \, dt = C(v) - j S(v)$$ Where: $$C(v) = \int_{0}^{v} \cos\left(\frac{\pi t^2}{2}\right) dt, \quad S(v) = \int_{0}^{v} \sin\left(\frac{\pi t^2}{2}\right) dt$$ **Step 2:** The normalised received field amplitude is $|F(v)|$ (relative to free space). Therefore, the **diffraction gain** in linear scale is $|F(v)|$, and in dB: $$G_d (dB) = 20 \log_{10} \left| F(v) \right| = 20 \log_{10} \sqrt{C(v)^2 + S(v)^2}$$ **Step 3:** Equivalent (and computationally simpler) approximations are widely used in KTU problems: $$G_d (dB) \approx \begin{cases} 0 & v \leq -0.78 \\ 20 \log_{10}\left(0.5 - 0.62 v\right) & -0.78 \leq v \leq 0 \\ 20 \log_{10}\left(0.5 e^{-0.95 v}\right) & 0 \leq v \leq 1 \\ 20 \log_{10}\left(0.4 - \sqrt{0.1184 - (0.38 - 0.1 v)^2}\right) & 1 \leq v \leq 2.4 \\ 20 \log_{10}(0.225 / v) & v > 2.4 \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical aid:** For $v \gg 1$ (deep shadow), the loss grows approximately as $L_{diff} \approx 20 \log_{10}(0.225/v)$. This means a **doubling of $v$ reduces the loss by 6 dB** (since $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301$). ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A 900 MHz transmitter communicates with a receiver over a single knife-edge obstacle. Distances: $d_1 = 5$ km, $d_2 = 10$ km. The knife edge protrudes $h = 30$ m above the LOS line. Compute: (a) wavelength, (b) Fresnel-Kirchhoff parameter $v$, (c) diffraction loss using the deep-shadow approximation. **Step (a) — Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{900 \times 10^6} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ m} \approx 0.3333 \text{ m}$$ **Step (b) — Diffraction parameter:** $$v = h \sqrt{\frac{2(d_1 + d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}}$$ $$v = 30 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (5000 + 10000)}{0.3333 \times 5000 \times 10000}}$$ **Step (b.i) — Compute the numerator inside the square root:** $$2 \times 15000 = 30000$$ **Step (b.ii) — Compute the denominator:** $$0.3333 \times 5000 \times 10000 = 0.3333 \times 5 \times 10^7 = 1.6667 \times 10^7$$ **Step (b.iii) — Ratio:** $$\frac{30000}{1.6667 \times 10^7} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step (b.iv) — Square root:** $$\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04243 \text{ m}^{-1}$$ **Step (b.v) — Final $v$:** $$v = 30 \times 0.04243 = 1.273$$ **Step (c) — Diffraction loss (use piecewise formula, $0 \leq v \leq 1$ branch… but $v = 1.273$ is in the $1 \leq v \leq 2.4$ branch):** $$G_d (dB) = 20 \log_{10}\left(0.4 - \sqrt{0.1184 - (0.38 - 0.1 v)^2}\right)$$ **Step (c.i) — Compute $0.1 v$:** $$0.1 \times 1.273 = 0.1273$$ **Step (c.ii) — Compute $0.38 - 0.1 v$:** $$0.38 - 0.1273 = 0.2527$$ **Step (c.iii) — Square it:** $$(0.2527)^2 = 0.06386$$ **Step (c.iv) — Compute $0.1184 - 0.06386$:** $$0.1184 - 0.06386 = 0.05454$$ **Step (c.v) — Square root:** $$\sqrt{0.05454} = 0.2335$$ **Step (c.vi) — Compute $0.4 - 0.2335$:** $$0.4 - 0.2335 = 0.1665$$ **Step (c.vii) — Convert to dB:** $$G_d = 20 \log_{10}(0.1665) = 20 \times (-0.7785) = -15.57 \text{ dB}$$ **Step (c.viii) — Diffraction loss:** $$L_{diff} = -G_d = +15.57 \text{ dB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check using deep-shadow approximation** (valid for $v > 2.4$, not strictly applicable here, but useful for comparison): $L_{diff} \approx 20 \log_{10}(0.225/1.273) = 20 \log_{10}(0.1768) = 20 \times (-0.7525) = -15.05$ dB, or a loss of 15.05 dB. The two methods agree to within 0.5 dB, confirming the result is in the right ballpark. **Final Answers:** (a) $\lambda = 0.333$ m, (b) $v = 1.273$, (c) $L_{diff} \approx 15.6$ dB. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Knife-Edge Diffraction Geometry (Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block depicts the **functional geometry** of single knife-edge diffraction, showing the transmitter, the obstacle, the receiver, and the key geometrical parameters ($d_1$, $d_2$, $h$, and the LOS reference line). This is the standard picture you should draw at the start of any KTU diffraction problem. ```mermaid flowchart LR TX["Transmitter TX
height ht"] K["Knife Edge Obstacle
height h above LOS
distance d1 from TX"] RX["Receiver RX
height hr
distance d2 from edge"] LOS["Direct Line of Sight LOS Path
Total length d = d1 + d2"] TX --- K K --- RX TX -. LOS line .-> RX TX -. Tx to edge .-> K K -. Edge to Rx .-> RX classDef radio fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef obs fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef ref fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000 class TX,RX radio class K obs class LOS ref ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Diffraction Loss Computation The figure below shows the **signal-flow block diagram** of how a system engineer computes total received power when diffraction is the dominant mechanism. This is useful in KTU when a question asks for the **complete path-loss budget** with diffraction. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Pt dBm
Transmit Power"] --> B["Add Gt dBi
Transmit Antenna Gain"] B --> C["Subtract Lfree dB
Free Space Path Loss
Lfree = 32.4 + 20log dkm + 20log fMHz"] C --> D["Compute v
v = h sqrt 2 d1+d2 divided by lambda d1 d2"] D --> E["Select Diffraction Model
Single Knife Edge
or Multiple Knife Edge"] E --> F["Compute Ldiff dB
Using Fresnel integrals
or piecewise formula"] F --> G["Add Gr dBi
Receive Antenna Gain"] G --> H["Pr dBm
Received Power"] classDef power fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef out fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 class A,H power class B,C,D,E,F,G proc ``` ## 4.3 Fresnel Zone Geometry (Top View) This block diagram shows how the **first three Fresnel zones** are arranged around the LOS path. The $n$-th Fresnel zone is the annular region between $r_{n-1}$ and $r_n$. Energy from successive zones alternately adds and cancels, which is why only the first zone is critical for clearance analysis. ```mermaid flowchart LR T["TX"] -->|d1| P["Point P on path"] P -->|d2| R["RX"] P --- Z1["Fresnel Zone 1
radius r1"] P --- Z2["Fresnel Zone 2
radius r2 = sqrt 2 r1"] P --- Z3["Fresnel Zone 3
radius r3 = sqrt 3 r1"] classDef txx fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 classDef zone fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 class T,R txx class Z1,Z2,Z3 zone ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** In the Mermaid diagrams, square-bracket node labels render as **rectangular blocks**, while labels like `Z1` (no quotes) are accepted as identifiers. All node IDs (e.g., `T`, `Z1`, `LOS`) are simple alphanumeric tokens, satisfying the Mermaid compiler safeguards. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** Define diffraction in the context of wireless propagation. State Huygens' principle. **Model Answer:** Diffraction is the phenomenon by which electromagnetic waves **propagate into the geometric shadow region** behind an obstructing object by bending around its sharp edges. It enables signal reception in **non-line-of-sight (NLOS)** conditions. **Huygens' principle** states that every point on a primary wavefront acts as a source of secondary spherical wavelets, and the envelope of these wavelets constitutes a new wavefront. Mathematically formalised by Fresnel and Kirchhoff, this principle allows energy to leak into the shadow region when part of a wavefront is blocked by an obstacle, thereby enabling NLOS wireless coverage. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What are Fresnel zones? Why is the first Fresnel zone important in wireless link design? **Model Answer:** **Fresnel zones** are concentric elliptical regions around the direct line-of-sight path between a transmitter and receiver, defined such that the excess path length from a point in the $n$-th zone to the receiver is $n\lambda/2$ longer than the direct LOS path. The radius of the $n$-th Fresnel zone is given by $r_n = \sqrt{n\lambda d_1 d_2/(d_1+d_2)}$. The **first Fresnel zone** is important because: 1. It contains approximately **70% of the total transmitted energy**. 2. If the first Fresnel zone is **at least 55% clear of obstructions**, the path behaves almost like free space. 3. Severe blockage of the first Fresnel zone leads to significant diffraction loss and shadowing. 4. It is the key parameter in deciding the **required antenna height** in line-of-sight microwave link design. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the radius of the $n$-th Fresnel zone in a wireless link of length $d$, where the obstacle is at distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from the transmitter and receiver respectively. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A microwave link operating at **6 GHz** spans a distance of **40 km**. A single knife-edge obstacle is located at the midpoint of the path, with its top **25 m** above the direct line-of-sight. Determine: (i) the wavelength, (ii) the first Fresnel zone radius at the midpoint, (iii) the Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction parameter $v$, and (iv) the diffraction loss using the appropriate piecewise approximation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are: 1. Express the path through a point at perpendicular distance $r_n$ from LOS as $L = \sqrt{d_1^2 + r_n^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + r_n^2}$. 2. Direct LOS path is $d_1 + d_2$. 3. Apply the binomial approximation $\sqrt{d^2+r^2} \approx d + r^2/(2d)$ for $r \ll d$. 4. Excess path length is $\Delta = \frac{r_n^2}{2} \cdot \frac{d_1+d_2}{d_1 d_2}$. 5. Set $\Delta = n\lambda/2$ for the $n$-th Fresnel zone. 6. Solve: $r_n = \sqrt{n\lambda d_1 d_2/(d_1+d_2)}$. **Marks distribution:** - Setup of geometric path difference: **2 marks** - Application of binomial approximation: **2 marks** - Definition of Fresnel zone condition ($\Delta = n\lambda/2$): **1 mark** - Final algebraic simplification: **2 marks** --- **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **(ii) First Fresnel zone radius at midpoint** ($d_1 = d_2 = 20$ km $= 20000$ m, $n = 1$): $$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d_1 + d_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 20000 \times 20000}{40000}}$$ $$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 4 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^4}} = \sqrt{0.05 \times 10000} = \sqrt{500} \approx 22.36 \text{ m} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Fresnel-Kirchhoff parameter $v$:** $$v = h \sqrt{\frac{2(d_1+d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}} = 25 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 40000}{0.05 \times 20000 \times 20000}}$$ $$v = 25 \times \sqrt{\frac{80000}{2 \times 10^7}} = 25 \times \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 25 \times 0.06325 = 1.581 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(iv) Diffraction loss** ($1 \leq v = 1.581 \leq 2.4$, use the appropriate branch): $$G_d (dB) = 20 \log_{10}\left(0.4 - \sqrt{0.1184 - (0.38 - 0.1v)^2}\right)$$ Step-by-step: - $0.1 v = 0.1581$ - $0.38 - 0.1581 = 0.2219$ - $(0.2219)^2 = 0.04924$ - $0.1184 - 0.04924 = 0.06916$ - $\sqrt{0.06916} = 0.2630$ - $0.4 - 0.2630 = 0.1370$ - $G_d = 20 \log_{10}(0.1370) = 20 \times (-0.8633) = -17.27 \text{ dB}$ - **Diffraction loss** $L_{diff} = -G_d = +17.27$ dB $\quad$ **(2 marks)** **Final Answer:** $\lambda = 0.05$ m, $r_1 \approx 22.36$ m, $v \approx 1.581$, $L_{diff} \approx 17.3$ dB. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2023] (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain the Huygens-Fresnel principle. With a neat diagram, define Fresnel zones and derive the radius of the $n$-th Fresnel zone. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A transmitter and receiver separated by **50 km** operate at **900 MHz**. A single knife-edge obstacle is at **15 km** from the transmitter. The obstacle top is **40 m** above the LOS line. Compute the diffraction loss using the deep-shadow approximation, and comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Huygens-Fresnel principle:** Every point on a primary wavefront acts as a secondary source of spherical wavelets. The new wavefront is the envelope of these wavelets. The mathematical formulation by Kirchhoff is: $$U(P) = \frac{j}{2\lambda} \int_{A} U(Q) \frac{e^{-jkr}}{r}(1 + \cos\theta) \, dA$$ **Fresnel zones** are concentric elliptical regions centred on the LOS path such that the path length from the $n$-th zone boundary to the receiver is $n\lambda/2$ longer than the direct LOS path. The $n$-th zone has an outer radius of $r_n = \sqrt{n\lambda d_1 d_2/(d_1+d_2)}$ (derivation as in Section 3.1). **(7 marks with 3 for diagram, 2 for principle, 2 for derivation)** --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** $f = 900$ MHz, $d_1 = 15$ km, $d_2 = 35$ km, $h = 40$ m, total $d = 50$ km. **Step 1 — Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{900 \times 10^6} = 0.3333 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Diffraction parameter $v$:** $$v = h \sqrt{\frac{2(d_1+d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}} = 40 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50000}{0.3333 \times 15000 \times 35000}}$$ $$= 40 \times \sqrt{\frac{100000}{1.75 \times 10^8}} = 40 \times \sqrt{5.714 \times 10^{-4}} = 40 \times 0.02390 = 0.9560$$ **Step 3 — Check the branch:** $0 \leq v = 0.956 \leq 1$, so use the piecewise formula for the transition region: $$G_d (dB) = 20 \log_{10}(0.5 e^{-0.95 v}) = 20 \log_{10}(0.5 e^{-0.9082}) = 20 \log_{10}(0.5 \times 0.4032) = 20 \log_{10}(0.2016)$$ $$G_d = 20 \times (-0.6955) = -13.91 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Diffraction loss:** $$L_{diff} = -G_d = +13.91 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 — Comment:** Since $v < 1$, we are in the **transition region** between free-space and deep shadow. The loss of ~14 dB is moderate. Doubling $h$ (to 80 m) would push $v$ to 1.91 and increase the loss. Conversely, halving $h$ would reduce the loss. The deep-shadow approximation is not strictly valid here, but the transition-region result is acceptable for a quick estimate. **Final Answer:** $L_{diff} \approx 13.9$ dB. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $d_1$ and $d_2$ must be in **metres** in the $v$ formula, but $r_1$ is often used with km and then mismatched. Always show unit conversion explicitly. > > 2. **Wrong Fresnel zone branch** — The piecewise $G_d$ formula has 5 regions; selecting the wrong branch based on $v$ can give errors of 5–10 dB. Always state which branch you are using and verify that $v$ lies in it. > > 3. **Sign confusion in $G_d$ vs $L_{diff}$** — Students often report negative losses. Remember: **diffraction loss is positive** (energy is attenuated), and is the **negative of diffraction gain**. > > 4. **Wrong $n$ in the formula for $r_n$** — The default is $n = 1$ unless the question specifies a different Fresnel zone. > > 5. **Confusing $h$ with antenna height** — In knife-edge geometry, $h$ is the **height of the obstacle above the LOS line**, not the physical height of the obstacle from the ground or the antenna height. State this clearly with a diagram. > > 6. **Skipping the geometry diagram** — A diagram showing $d_1$, $d_2$, and $h$ is **mandatory** for full marks. Many students lose 1–2 marks by not drawing it. > > 7. **Using $20 \log_{10}$ for amplitude** — Confusing it with $10 \log_{10}$ (which is for power). Diffraction loss uses amplitude-squared for power, hence the factor of 20. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition of Diffraction:** Bending of EM waves around obstacle edges into the geometric shadow region; follows Huygens-Fresnel principle. - **Fresnel zone radius:** $r_n = \sqrt{n\lambda d_1 d_2/(d_1+d_2)}$ — first zone is the critical one for clearance. - **Rule of thumb:** 55% first-Fresnel clearance gives near free-space propagation. - **Fresnel-Kirchhoff parameter:** $v = h \sqrt{2(d_1+d_2)/(\lambda d_1 d_2)}$ — dimensionless; controls all knife-edge diffraction behaviour. - **Symmetric shortcut:** When $d_1 = d_2 = d/2$, then $v = 2h/\sqrt{\lambda d}$. - **Diffraction gain (signed):** $G_d = 20 \log_{10}\vert F(v)\vert = 20 \log_{10}\sqrt{C^2 + S^2}$ dB. - **Diffraction loss (positive dB):** $L_{diff} = -G_d$. - **Piecewise $G_d$ formula:** Five regions of $v$ give tractable closed-form expressions — memorise them. - **Deep-shadow limit ($v > 2.4$):** $L_{diff} \approx 20 \log_{10}(0.225/v)$. - **Multiple knife-edge models:** Bullington, Epstein-Peterson, Deygout — all reduce to a sequence of single knife-edge computations. - **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = c/f$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — always state this explicitly. - **Wavelength dependence:** Longer $\lambda$ (lower $f$) → smaller $v$ for a given geometry → less diffraction loss → better NLOS coverage. - **Engineering utility:** Cellular coverage planning, DTV/FM broadcasting, point-to-point microwave links, V2X, indoor femtocells. - **Mandatory in any KTU solution:** (i) State the given data, (ii) draw the geometry diagram, (iii) compute $\lambda$ first, (iv) state the formula used, (v) verify $v$ lies in the chosen branch, (vi) final answer in correct units. > [!TIP] > **Final tip for the KTU ESE:** Practise 2–3 numerical problems from each of the categories — (a) Fresnel zone radius computation, (b) $v$ computation for symmetric and asymmetric paths, and (c) piecewise $L_{diff}$ computation. These three categories cover ~85% of all KTU diffraction questions asked in the last 5 years.

topics.title: Scattering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Scattering # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scattering** is a propagation mechanism in mobile radio communication that occurs when a radio wave impinges upon an object whose dimensions are **comparable to or smaller than the wavelength** $\lambda$ of the propagating signal, or upon a surface whose irregularities are of the order of $\lambda$. Unlike reflection (which is specular) or diffraction (which is knife-edge dependent), scattering produces **omnidirectional redistribution of the incident wave energy** into multiple secondary wavelets. In the formal Rappaport/Costas-Bertoni classification, scattering is the **third fundamental propagation mechanism** (after reflection and diffraction) and is essential for predicting coverage in environments where line-of-sight (LOS) is obstructed by foliage, lamp posts, traffic signs, and building facades with rough textures. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants for Reference:** > - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s > - Free-space impedance: $\eta_0 \approx 377$ $\Omega$ > - Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f$ > - Standard reference frequency for mobile systems: **900 MHz** $\Rightarrow \lambda \approx 0.333$ m --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Bumpy Wall" Effect **Plain-English Intuition for a First-Time Reader:** Imagine you are standing in a room and shining a **laser pointer** at a wall: - If the wall is a **perfect mirror**, the laser bounces off at exactly one predictable angle (this is **specular reflection**). - If the wall is **slightly bumpy** (like stucco or orange peel), the light scatters into a soft glowing patch on the wall — you see a wide spread of energy rather than a single hot spot. This is **scattering**. - If the light bends around a sharp corner of the wall (like a door frame), that is **diffraction**. **The "stucco wall" analogy is exactly what happens to a 1 GHz cellular signal ($\lambda \approx 30$ cm) when it hits a brick wall whose mortar joints are a few centimeters high.** The mortar bumps are smaller than the wavelength, so the signal gets sprayed in many directions. Some of this scattered energy actually helps the signal reach the user even when the LOS path is blocked — a phenomenon mobile network planners call **forward scattering gain** or **building scatter contribution**. --- ## 1.3 When Does Scattering Dominate? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trigger Conditions for Scattering (Memorize These):** > 1. Surface height deviation $h_0$ is on the order of $\lambda$. > 2. Object dimension $d < \lambda$ (Rayleigh regime). > 3. Object dimension $d \approx \lambda$ (Mie / resonance regime). > 4. Object dimension $d \gg \lambda$ but surface is textured (optical scattering from rough surfaces). > 5. Propagation through **turbulent atmospheric layers** or **rain/foliage volumes**. In modern 5G networks operating at **28 GHz** ($\lambda \approx 10.7$ mm), scattering from **almost every everyday object** (chips of paint, leaves, even raindrops) becomes significant — this is why mmWave propagation is so different from sub-6 GHz propagation. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Classification of Scattering Scattering phenomena in wireless engineering are classified by the **relative size** of the scatterer with respect to the wavelength. This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — examiners frequently test the three regimes. ### Regime 1: Rayleigh Scattering ($d \ll \lambda$) - **Mechanism:** The incident electric field polarizes the small object; the object re-radiates as a Hertzian dipole. - **Radar cross section dependence:** $\sigma \propto f^4$ (extremely steep frequency rolloff). - **Real-world example:** Paint flakes, dust, very small foliage elements. Also famously explains why the sky is blue ($f^4$ law heavily scatters blue light). - **Implication for KTU:** Sub-6 GHz signals hardly experience Rayleigh scattering; mmWave signals do. ### Regime 2: Mie / Resonance Scattering ($d \approx \lambda$) - **Mechanism:** The object resonates; re-radiation pattern becomes highly directional and complex. - **Radar cross section:** $\sigma \propto 1$ (resonance peak, can be larger than physical cross-section). - **Real-world example:** Lamp posts, traffic signs, water droplets at rain-scatter frequencies. - **Mathematical treatment:** Requires rigorous solution of Maxwell's equations via Mie theory (1908). ### Regime 3: Optical / Surface Scattering ($d \gg \lambda$) - **Mechanism:** Surface is geometrically large but **texturally rough**. - **Treatment:** Beckmann–Spizzichino model; energy is distributed via the Kirchhoff integral. - **Real-world example:** Building facades with bricks/windows, rocky terrain, foliage canopies. --- ## 2.2 Surface Roughness Criteria (KTU Classic Question) A surface is classified as **smooth or rough** based on the height of surface irregularities $h_0$ relative to the wavelength and the angle of incidence $\theta_i$. ### Rayleigh Criterion (the most commonly tested in KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > A surface is considered **smooth** (and exhibits specular reflection) if: > $$ h_c \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{8 \cos \theta_i} $$ > Otherwise, if $h_0 \le h_c$, surface is smooth; if $h_0 > h_c$, surface is **rough** and produces diffuse scattering. | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Critical height | $h_c$ | meters | Maximum allowable roughness for specular reflection | | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | meters | Carrier wavelength | | Angle of incidence | $\theta_i$ | radians | Measured from surface normal | | Surface height std deviation | $h_0$ | meters | RMS of surface bumps | ### Fraunhofer Criterion (an alternative, sometimes tested) A surface is rough if: $$ h_0 \;>\; \frac{\lambda}{32 \cos \theta_i} $$ This is **stricter** than the Rayleigh criterion. ### Practical Implication - At **glancing incidence** ($\theta_i \to 90°$), $\cos\theta_i \to 0$, so $h_c \to \infty$. Even microscopically rough surfaces look smooth — energy reflects specularly. - At **grazing incidence from a high tower** to a mobile user, the ground often acts as a smooth mirror. --- ## 2.3 Radar Cross Section (RCS) — The "Scatterer Strength" The **Radar Cross Section** $\sigma_s$ (also written $\sigma$ or RCS) is the **equivalent area** of a perfectly conducting sphere that would scatter the same power back toward the source. $$ \sigma_s \;=\; 4\pi R^{2} \,\frac{S_{\text{scatt}}}{S_{\text{inc}}} \;\;\;\; \text{units: m}^{2} $$ Where: - $R$ = distance from scatterer to observer - $S_{\text{scatt}}$ = power density of scattered wave - $S_{\text{inc}}$ = power density of incident wave > [!NOTE] > RCS is conventionally expressed in **dBsm** (decibels relative to one square meter). A car's RCS is roughly **30 m²** at 1 GHz; a human's RCS is about **1 m²**; a small raindrop is about $10^{-8}$ m². ### RCS Scaling with Frequency (MUST memorize for KTU) | Scattering Regime | Frequency Dependence | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | Rayleigh ($d \ll \lambda$) | $\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$ | Small targets very weak at low $f$ | | Resonance ($d \approx \lambda$) | $\sigma_s \propto 1$ | Strong peak, oscillatory | | Optical ($d \gg \lambda$) | $\sigma_s \propto 1/\lambda^{2}$ | Geometric optics limit | --- ## 2.4 Outdoor Scattering Models ### (a) Beckmann Model for Surface Scattering The scattering coefficient $\rho_s$ for a slightly rough surface: $$ \rho_s \;=\; \exp\!\left[-8\left(\frac{\pi \sigma_h \cos\theta_i}{\lambda}\right)^{2}\right]\,\rho_0 $$ Where $\rho_0$ is the smooth-surface Fresnel reflection coefficient and $\sigma_h$ is the surface height standard deviation. ### (b) Lattice Model for Building Scattering (Rappaport) Used for urban microcells when a building facade **serves as a scatterer** between the base station and mobile user. $$ P_r \;=\; P_t \, G_t \, G_r \,\frac{\lambda^2}{(4\pi)^3} \,\frac{\sigma_s}{R_1^{\,2} R_2^{\,2}} $$ Where: - $R_1$ = Tx-to-scatterer distance - $R_2$ = scatterer-to-Rx distance - $\sigma_s$ = RCS of the building face (typically **40–100 m²** for a brick wall at 1 GHz) This is the **direct mathematical equivalent of two-ray reflection** but with a localized scatterer. ### (c) Discrete Scatterer / Point-Scatterer Model For a single lamp post or sign post modeled as an isotropic re-radiator: $$ P_r \;=\; P_t \, G_t \, G_r \,\frac{\lambda^2 \sigma_s}{(4\pi)^3 d_1^{\,2} d_2^{\,2}} \cdot L_{\text{extra}} $$ --- ## 2.5 Indoor / Outdoor Volume Scattering ### Foliage Attenuation Model (CCIR / COST 235) For propagation through dense foliage: $$ L_{\text{foliage}} \;=\; A_f \cdot d^{\alpha_f} $$ Where: - $A_f$ = initial attenuation constant (dB) - $d$ = depth of foliage (m) - $\alpha_f$ = exponent (~0.2 to 0.5) ### Tropospheric / Rain Scattering For frequencies above 10 GHz, rain becomes an **independent Mie scatterer** with specific attenuation: $$ \gamma_R \;=\; k R^{\alpha} $$ where $R$ is the rain rate in mm/h and $k$, $\alpha$ are polarization-dependent empirical constants (ITU-R P.838). --- ## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why KTU Tests This > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world applications:** > 1. **5G mmWave planning** at 28/38/60 GHz: every leaf and pillar is a scatterer, requiring ray-tracing. > 2. **RFID gate design:** relies on bistatic RCS of the tag antenna. > 3. **Automotive radar** (77 GHz): scattering from vehicles is the sensing modality. > 4. **Troposcatter links** (military): deliberately exploit forward scattering beyond the horizon. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h_c = \dfrac{\lambda}{8 \cos\theta_i}$ | $h_c$: critical height; $\theta_i$: incidence angle | Rayleigh roughness test | | 2 | $h_0 > h_c \Rightarrow$ rough | $h_0$: surface std dev | Decide between reflection vs. scattering | | 3 | $\sigma_s = 4\pi R^{2} \dfrac{S_{\text{scatt}}}{S_{\text{inc}}}$ | $\sigma_s$: RCS in m² | Scattering strength | | 4 | Rayleigh: $\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$ | — | Tiny scatterer regime | | 5 | Optical: $\sigma_s \propto 1/\lambda^{2}$ | — | Large scatterer regime | | 6 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \dfrac{\lambda^{2} \sigma_s}{(4\pi)^{3} d_1^{2} d_2^{2}}$ | $d_1,d_2$: bi-static distances | Lattice / point scatterer link budget | | 7 | $\rho_s = \exp\!\left[-8\left(\dfrac{\pi \sigma_h \cos\theta_i}{\lambda}\right)^{2}\right]\rho_0$ | $\sigma_h$: surface height std dev | Beckmann rough-surface model | | 8 | $L_{\text{foliage}} = A_f d^{\alpha_f}$ | $A_f$: dB/m constant | Foliage loss | | 9 | $\lambda = c/f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | Wavelength computation | | 10 | $\text{dBsm} = 10\log_{10}(\sigma_s)$ | $\sigma_s$ in m² | Logarithmic RCS | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Rayleigh Surface Roughness Criterion **Starting Point:** Two rays (A and B) are incident on a slightly rough surface. Ray B hits a bump of height $h_0$ and must travel an extra path $\Delta$ to the receiver. **Step 1: Express the path-length difference.** The geometric extra path for the two rays at incidence angle $\theta_i$ is: $$ \Delta = 2 h_0 \sin\theta_i $$ **Step 2: Convert path difference to phase difference.** $$ \Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,(2 h_0 \sin\theta_i) = \frac{4\pi h_0 \sin\theta_i}{\lambda} $$ **Step 3: Apply the smoothness criterion.** A surface is considered smooth if the phase difference is less than $\pi/2$ radians (90°). Setting $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ and solving for $h_0$: $$ \frac{4\pi h_0 \sin\theta_i}{\lambda} = \frac{\pi}{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h_0 = \frac{\lambda}{8 \sin\theta_i} $$ **Step 4: Use the equivalent form (cosine form used in KTU).** Since most KTU textbooks express this with the **complement** of the incidence angle (i.e., elevation from surface): $$ h_0 = \frac{\lambda}{8 \cos\theta_i} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h_c = \frac{\lambda}{8 \cos\theta_i} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $\pi/2$?** If two reflected components are out of phase by more than 90°, they no longer add constructively to form a strong specular component. The reflected energy is therefore "spread" into many weak components — that is **scattering**. **Numerical example (KTU standard):** At $f = 900$ MHz on a road, $\lambda = 0.333$ m, $\theta_i = 30°$: $$ h_c = \frac{0.333}{8 \cos 30°} = \frac{0.333}{8 \times 0.866} = 0.0481 \text{ m} = 4.81 \text{ cm} $$ A road surface with asphalt bumps of 5 cm is **rough** at 900 MHz. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: RCS of a Small Dielectric Sphere (Rayleigh Limit) **Starting Point:** A small sphere of radius $a$ (with $a \ll \lambda$) is illuminated by a plane wave. It polarizes and re-radiates. **Step 1: Compute the induced dipole moment.** $$ p = 4\pi \epsilon_0 a^{3} \left(\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right) E_{\text{inc}} $$ **Step 2: Compute the far-field scattered power density.** For a Hertzian dipole, the maximum scattered power density at distance $R$ is: $$ S_{\text{scatt}} = \frac{\pi^{2} |p|^{2}}{2 \lambda^{4} R^{2} \epsilon_0} \cdot \sin^{2}\theta $$ **Step 3: Compute incident power density.** $$ S_{\text{inc}} = \frac{|E_{\text{inc}}|^{2}}{2 \eta_0} $$ **Step 4: Form the RCS.** $$ \sigma_s = 4\pi R^{2} \,\frac{S_{\text{scatt}}}{S_{\text{inc}}} $$ $$ \sigma_s = 4\pi R^{2} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{\pi^{2} |p|^{2}}{2\lambda^{4} R^{2} \epsilon_0}}{|E_{\text{inc}}|^{2}/(2\eta_0)} $$ **Step 5: Simplify using $|p| = 4\pi\epsilon_0 a^{3} \left(\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right)|E_{\text{inc}}|$:** $$ \sigma_s = \frac{4\pi^{2} \cdot 16\pi^{2}\epsilon_0^{2} a^{6} \left(\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right)^{2} \cdot 2\eta_0}{\lambda^{4} \cdot 2 \cdot 2} $$ $$ \sigma_s = \frac{64\pi^{4} a^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \left|\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right|^{2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The critical dependence **$\sigma_s \propto a^{6} / \lambda^{4} \propto f^{4}$** is the famous Rayleigh $f^{4}$ law. Doubling the frequency increases RCS by **12 dB**. **Numerical example:** Small raindrop with $a = 0.5$ mm, $f = 30$ GHz ($\lambda = 0.01$ m), $\epsilon_r \approx 80 - j\,30$: $$ \sigma_s \approx \frac{64\pi^{4} (0.5\times 10^{-3})^{6}}{(0.01)^{4}} \cdot \left|\frac{79 - j30}{82 - j30}\right|^{2} $$ The $\left|\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right|^{2} \approx 0.93$, and the geometric factor yields $\sigma_s \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-8}$ m², or about **−79 dBsm**. This is why heavy rain is critical for 5G mmWave links. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Bi-Static Scattering Path Loss (Rappaport Lattice) **Starting Step:** A transmitter is at distance $d_1$ from a building-wall scatterer. A receiver is at distance $d_2$ from the same scatterer. The wall acts as a secondary radiator with RCS $\sigma_s$. **Step 1: Incident power density at scatterer.** $$ S_1 = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d_1^{\,2}} $$ **Step 2: Power intercepted by the scatterer.** $$ P_{\text{int}} = \sigma_s \cdot S_1 = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d_1^{\,2}} $$ **Step 3: Isotropic re-radiation (worst-case assumption).** $$ S_2 = \frac{P_{\text{int}}}{4\pi d_2^{\,2}} = \frac{\sigma_s \, P_t G_t}{(4\pi)^2 d_1^{\,2} d_2^{\,2}} $$ **Step 4: Power captured by Rx antenna.** $$ P_r = S_2 \cdot A_{\text{eff}} = S_2 \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} $$ **Step 5: Combine.** $$ P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2} \sigma_s}{(4\pi)^{3} d_1^{\,2} d_2^{\,2}} $$ **Step 6: Convert to dBm path loss for KTU numerical problems.** $$ L_{\text{path}} = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{\lambda^{2} \sigma_s}{(4\pi)^{3} d_1^{\,2} d_2^{\,2}}\right] \;\;\text{dB} $$ **Numerical KTU Example:** $P_t = 30$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 10$ dBi, $f = 1.8$ GHz, $\sigma_s = 50$ m², $d_1 = 100$ m, $d_2 = 80$ m. $$ \lambda = \frac{3\times 10^{8}}{1.8\times 10^{9}} = 0.1667 \text{ m} $$ $$ \text{PL} = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{(0.1667)^{2}(50)}{(4\pi)^{3} (100)^{2} (80)^{2}}\right] = 10\log_{10}(1.18\times 10^{-9}) = -89.3 \text{ dB} $$ $$ P_r = 30 + 10 + 10 - 89.3 = -39.3 \text{ dBm} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reflection vs. Diffraction vs. Scattering | Property | Reflection | Diffraction | Scattering | |---|---|---|---| | Triggering geometry | Smooth surface $h_0 < h_c$ | Sharp edge / wedge | Rough surface or small object | | Directionality | Specular (one direction) | Around an edge | Omnidirectional | | Frequency dependence | $\lambda^{2}$ (Friis-like) | Frequency-independent (UTD) | $f^{4}$ (Rayleigh) or $1/\lambda^{2}$ (optical) | | Primary model | Fresnel coefficients | Knife-edge / UTD | Beckmann / Mie / Rayleigh | | LOS dependency | Yes (strong component) | No (works in NLOS) | No (works in NLOS) | | Typical scatterer | Sea water, smooth wall | Building edge, window corner | Lamp post, tree, brick wall | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mechanism Diagram: How Scattering Occurs ```mermaid flowchart TD TX["Transmitter

Mention any two conditions under which scattering dominates over reflection.** **Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Scattering is the phenomenon by which a propagating electromagnetic wave, on striking a surface whose roughness is on the order of a wavelength, or an object of dimension comparable to or smaller than the wavelength, is redistributed in many directions, producing many weak secondary wavelets instead of a single specular component. **Conditions (1.5 Marks — split as 0.75 + 0.75):** 1. The surface height deviation $h_0$ is comparable to $\lambda$, i.e., $h_0 > h_c = \dfrac{\lambda}{8\cos\theta_i}$ (Rayleigh criterion violated). 2. The object dimension $d$ is comparable to or smaller than the wavelength (Rayleigh or Mie regime) — e.g., lamp posts, foliage, traffic signs, rain droplets at mmWave. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]** > **What is Radar Cross Section (RCS)? Write its mathematical definition and state the Rayleigh $f^{4}$ law with one practical example.** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 Mark):** Radar Cross Section $\sigma_s$ is the equivalent area of a perfectly conducting sphere that would scatter the same power back toward the source as the actual target. **Mathematical Definition (1 Mark):** $$ \sigma_s = 4\pi R^{2} \cdot \dfrac{S_{\text{scatt}}}{S_{\text{inc}}} \quad \text{(units: m}^{2}\text{)} $$ **Rayleigh Law (0.5 Mark):** For a target whose dimension $d \ll \lambda$, $\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$. **Example (0.5 Mark):** Small raindrop or dust particle at 28 GHz — RCS grows by 12 dB for each doubling of frequency, which is why mmWave links are vulnerable to rain scatter. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Question]** > **(a)** Derive the Rayleigh criterion for surface roughness. Using this criterion, determine whether a brick wall of RMS roughness $h_0 = 4$ cm is smooth or rough at 1.9 GHz for an angle of incidence $\theta_i = 60°$. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** What is Radar Cross Section? Derive the RCS of a small dielectric sphere in the Rayleigh limit and discuss its frequency dependence. State the implications for 5G mmWave design. **(7 Marks)** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A #### Part (a) — Rayleigh Criterion Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 (1 Mark): Set up geometry.** Consider two adjacent rays A and B incident on a slightly rough surface. Ray B strikes a bump of height $h_0$ and travels an extra path to reach the receiver compared to ray A. **Step 2 (1.5 Marks): Express the path difference.** The extra path traveled by ray B (from incidence to reflection) is: $$ \Delta = 2 h_0 \sin\theta_i $$ **Step 3 (1.5 Marks): Convert to phase difference.** $$ \Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta = \frac{4\pi h_0 \sin\theta_i}{\lambda} $$ **Step 4 (1 Mark): Apply the $\pi/2$ phase criterion.** For a surface to be considered **smooth**, the phase difference between adjacent rays must be less than $\pi/2$ radians. Setting $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$: $$ h_0 = \frac{\lambda}{8 \sin\theta_i} = \frac{\lambda}{8 \cos\theta_i} $$ (using the convention where $\theta_i$ is measured from the surface plane). **Step 5 (2 Marks): Numerical evaluation.** - Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^{8})/(1.9 \times 10^{9}) = 0.158$ m - Critical height: $h_c = 0.158 / (8 \cos 60°) = 0.158 / (8 \times 0.5) = 0.158/4 = 0.0395$ m = **3.95 cm** - Compare: $h_0 = 4$ cm $\;>\; h_c = 3.95$ cm. **Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** Since $h_0 > h_c$, the brick wall is **rough** at 1.9 GHz for $\theta_i = 60°$ and will produce **diffuse scattering** rather than specular reflection. --- #### Part (b) — RCS of a Small Dielectric Sphere (7 Marks) **Step 1 (1 Mark): Define RCS.** $$ \sigma_s = 4\pi R^{2} \cdot \frac{S_{\text{scatt}}}{S_{\text{inc}}} $$ **Step 2 (1.5 Marks): State the induced dipole for a small sphere.** A sphere of radius $a \ll \lambda$ illuminated by a plane wave develops a dipole moment: $$ p = 4\pi\epsilon_0 a^{3} \left(\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right) E_{\text{inc}} $$ **Step 3 (1.5 Marks): Far-field scattered power density of a Hertzian dipole.** $$ S_{\text{scatt}} = \frac{\pi^{2}|p|^{2}}{2\lambda^{4}R^{2}\epsilon_0} \sin^{2}\theta $$ (incident on the dipole, maximum at $\theta = 90°$). **Step 4 (1 Mark): Substitute into the RCS formula and simplify.** $$ \sigma_s = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{2\lambda^{4}\epsilon_0}\left(\frac{2\pi a^{3}|\epsilon_r - 1|}{|\epsilon_r + 2|}\right)^{2} \cdot 2\eta_0 $$ **Step 5 (1 Mark): Final Rayleigh RCS result.** $$ \sigma_s = \frac{64\pi^{4}a^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \left|\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right|^{2} $$ **Step 6 (1 Mark): Implications for 5G mmWave.** Since $\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$, mmWave frequencies (28/38/60 GHz) experience **exponentially stronger scattering** from small particles (raindrops, dust, foliage) than sub-6 GHz systems. This is a **double-edged sword**: - **Benefit:** More multipath richness; better NLOS coverage in dense urban environments. - **Drawback:** Greater attenuation from rain, foliage, and atmospheric particulates; need for denser small-cell deployment. --- ### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the three regimes of scattering (Rayleigh, Resonance, Optical) in terms of object size relative to wavelength. For each regime, state the RCS frequency dependence and give one practical example relevant to wireless communication. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A base station operating at 1.8 GHz illuminates a building wall (treated as a point scatterer with RCS = 60 m²) located 120 m from the BS. A mobile user is located 90 m from the wall on the other side. Calculate the received power at the mobile if $P_t = 1$ W, $G_t = G_r = 12$ dBi. **(7 Marks)** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B #### Part (a) — Three Scattering Regimes (7 Marks) **Regime 1: Rayleigh Scattering — $d \ll \lambda$ (2 Marks)** - The object is so small that it can be modeled as a single induced electric dipole. - **RCS dependence:** $\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$ (very strong frequency dependence). - **Equation:** $\sigma_s = \dfrac{64\pi^{4} a^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \left|\dfrac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right|^{2}$ - **Example:** Dust particles, paint flakes, or very small foliage elements scattering mmWave signals. **Regime 2: Resonance / Mie Scattering — $d \approx \lambda$ (2.5 Marks)** - Object dimensions are comparable to wavelength; full Maxwell equations must be solved (Mie theory, 1908). - **RCS dependence:** $\sigma_s$ peaks and oscillates; can exceed the geometric cross-section by a factor of 2–4. - **Example:** Raindrops (1–3 mm) scattering at 30–60 GHz mmWave bands; lamp posts and traffic signs at 1–2 GHz. **Regime 3: Optical Scattering — $d \gg \lambda$ (2.5 Marks)** - Object is geometrically large but surface is texturally rough. - **RCS dependence:** $\sigma_s \propto 1/\lambda^{2}$ (geometric optics limit). - **Example:** Brick building facades at cellular frequencies, rocky terrain, ship hulls in maritime radar. --- #### Part (b) — Bi-Static Lattice Power Calculation (7 Marks) **Given:** - $f = 1.8$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = 3\times 10^{8}/1.8\times 10^{9} = 0.1667$ m - $\sigma_s = 60$ m² - $d_1 = 120$ m, $d_2 = 90$ m - $P_t = 1$ W = 0 dBW - $G_t = G_r = 12$ dBi (linear: $10^{12/10} = 15.85$) **Step 1 (2 Marks): Write the lattice equation.** $$ P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2} \sigma_s}{(4\pi)^{3} d_1^{\,2} d_2^{\,2}} $$ **Step 2 (2 Marks): Substitute numerical values.** $$ P_r = \frac{1 \times 15.85 \times 15.85 \times (0.1667)^{2} \times 60}{(4\pi)^{3} \times (120)^{2} \times (90)^{2}} $$ $$ = \frac{251.2 \times 0.02778 \times 60}{1984.4 \times 14400 \times 8100} $$ $$ = \frac{418.6}{2.315 \times 10^{11}} $$ **Step 3 (2 Marks): Compute the result.** $$ P_r = 1.808 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 1.808 \text{ nW} $$ **Step 4 (1 Mark): Convert to dBm.** $$ P_r \,(\text{dBm}) = 10\log_{10}(1.808 \times 10^{-9}/10^{-3}) = 10\log_{10}(1.808 \times 10^{-6}) = -57.43 \text{ dBm} $$ **Conclusion (0 Mark — implicit):** The received power is very small but is in the typical range for cellular NLOS operation (-50 to -90 dBm). The model confirms that even a single building-wall scatterer can provide a usable NLOS path in a microcell environment. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Penalty Traps per KTU Valuation Key):** > > 1. **Confusing $\sin\theta_i$ with $\cos\theta_i$ in Rayleigh criterion.** KTU uses the **elevation-from-surface** convention $\cos\theta_i$. Writing $h_c = \lambda/(8\sin\theta_i)$ without clarification = **−1 mark** if the angle convention is not specified. > > 2. **Forgetting the $4\pi$ vs $(4\pi)^{3}$ distinction.** The free-space path loss uses $(4\pi d/\lambda)^{2}$, but the **bi-static scattering** equation uses $(4\pi)^{3}$ in the denominator because **two** propagation paths (Tx-to-scatterer and scatterer-to-Rx) each carry their own $4\pi d$ factor. Writing the wrong denominator = **−2 marks**. > > 3. **Mixing up RCS regimes.** Many students answer "$\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$" for *all* scattering questions. Examiners expect the candidate to identify the **regime** first. Always state: "Assuming Rayleigh regime ($d \ll \lambda$)..." before writing the formula. > > 4. **Unit conversion errors.** RCS is in **m²**, not dB, during formula manipulation. Convert to dBsm **only at the end** if needed. Mixing units mid-derivation = **−1 mark**. > > 5. **Neglecting antenna gain terms.** In numerical problems, $G_t$ and $G_r$ in dBi **must be converted to linear scale** before substitution. Writing $G_t = 12$ directly into a linear equation = **−1 mark**. > > 6. **Wavelength/angle confusion.** Always quote the **angle convention** explicitly: "$\theta_i$ is measured from the surface normal" OR "from the surface plane" — KTU uses the surface-plane convention leading to the cosine form. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scattering (Module 3)** > > **Definitions to memorize verbatim:** > - [x] Scattering = omnidirectional energy redistribution by small/rough objects. > - [x] RCS $\sigma_s = 4\pi R^{2} S_{\text{scatt}}/S_{\text{inc}}$ (units m²). > - [x] Rayleigh criterion: $h_c = \lambda/(8\cos\theta_i)$. > - [x] dBsm $= 10\log_{10}(\sigma_s/1\text{ m}^{2})$. > > **Key formulas (4-equation minimum):** > - [x] Rayleigh criterion (smooth/rough test). > - [x] Bi-static lattice power equation. > - [x] Rayleigh sphere RCS ($f^{4}$ law). > - [x] Three RCS regime scalings. > > **Regime identification skill:** > - [x] $d \ll \lambda \Rightarrow$ Rayleigh: $\sigma_s \propto f^{4}$. > - [x] $d \approx \lambda \Rightarrow$ Mie: resonance peak. > - [x] $d \gg \lambda \Rightarrow$ Optical: $\sigma_s \propto 1/\lambda^{2}$. > > **Practical intuition:** > - [x] Glancing incidence ⇒ surface looks smooth (large $h_c$). > - [x] mmWave ⇒ every small object scatters significantly. > - [x] Building walls serve as bi-static reflectors in microcells. > > **Comparison with other mechanisms:** > - [x] Reflection: smooth surface, one direction. > - [x] Diffraction: knife edge, around corner. > - [x] Scattering: rough/small, omnidirectional. > > **Numerical problem pattern:** > - [x] Step 1 — Identify regime. > - [x] Step 2 — Compute $\lambda$ from $c/f$. > - [x] Step 3 — Choose correct formula. > - [x] Step 4 — Convert all dB values to linear. > - [x] Step 5 — Substitute and compute. > - [x] Step 6 — State conclusion with engineering interpretation.

topics.title: Link Budget Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Link Budget Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Link Budget Design** is the systematic accounting of all **gains and losses** incurred by a radio signal as it propagates from the transmitter (TX), through the wireless channel, to the receiver (RX). It is a power-balance equation (expressed universally in **decibels (dB)** or **dBm**) that allows the radio engineer to verify whether the **received signal power** exceeds the **receiver sensitivity threshold** with a predefined **fade margin**.

Mathematically, the link budget equation is:

$$P_{RX} \;=\; P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{RX} - L_{PATH} - L_{MARGIN}$$

where every term is in dB and $P_{RX}$ must be $\geq$ Receiver Sensitivity for a viable link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Link budget design is the foundational prerequisite for every wireless system design problem — cellular (4G/5G), satellite, Wi-Fi, point-to-point microwave. KTU board questions frequently test the *Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)* calculation, **EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power)** derivation, and **receiver sensitivity** computation.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are shouting from the top of a hill to a friend at the bottom of a valley, **5 km away**, during a windy day.

- Your **shouting volume** = Transmit Power ($P_{TX}$)
- A **megaphone in your hand** = Transmit Antenna Gain ($G_{TX}$)
- A **hearing aid in your friend's ear** = Receive Antenna Gain ($G_{RX}$)
- **Distance & wind blowing away the sound** = Path Loss ($L_{PATH}$)
- **Echoes and trees absorbing sound** = Other losses ($L_{other}$)
- **A whisper at the end of the valley** = Receiver sensitivity threshold

**Link Budget** is the *tally sheet* — it answers: "After adding all the amplifiers and subtracting all the obstacles, can your friend still hear you clearly?" If the final value is louder than the threshold (sensitivity), the link works.

## Standard Physical Constants

- Boltzmann's Constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$ (or in dB form: $-228.6 \; \text{dBW/K/Hz}$)
- Thermal Noise Power Spectral Density at 290 K: $N_0 = -174 \; \text{dBm/Hz}$
- Speed of Light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology** — The *EIRP* (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) is defined as:
>
> $$EIRP \;=\; P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{feeder}$$
>
> It is the power that an *isotropic* radiator would have to emit to produce the same field strength as the actual antenna in its direction of maximum radiation. Regulators (e.g., FCC, ETSI) use EIRP to set legal emission caps.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of a Link Budget

A wireless link consists of three distinct power zones:

### (A) Transmit Side (Power Generation)
1. **Transmitter Output Power ($P_{TX}$)** — Raw RF power delivered by the PA (Power Amplifier), usually expressed in **dBm**.
2. **Transmit Feeder/Cable Losses ($L_{TX}$)** — Power dissipated in the coaxial cable, connectors, and waveguide before reaching the antenna.
3. **Transmit Antenna Gain ($G_{TX}$)** — Directive amplification offered by the antenna (e.g., parabolic dish, patch).

> **Net Transmit Power (EIRP):** $\;EIRP = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX}$

### (B) Channel (Propagation Zone)
1. **Free-Space Path Loss ($L_{FSPL}$ or $L_P$)** — Geometric spreading loss in an ideal, obstacle-free medium.
2. **Atmospheric Losses ($L_{atmo}$)** — Absorption by oxygen, water vapor, rain (significant above 10 GHz).
3. **Foliage / Building Penetration Loss ($L_{pen}$)** — Empirical losses from obstacles.
4. **Multipath Fade Margin ($M_{fade}$)** — Safety margin to guarantee a target availability (e.g., 99.99%).

### (C) Receive Side (Signal Recovery)
1. **Receive Antenna Gain ($G_{RX}$)** — Concentration of incident wave power into the feed.
2. **Receive Feeder Loss ($L_{RX}$)** — Cable/connector losses between antenna and LNA.
3. **Receiver Noise Figure ($NF$)** — Internal noise added by receiver electronics.
4. **Receiver Sensitivity ($S_{RX,min}$)** — The *minimum* detectable signal power for a given BER.

## 2.2 Why Each Term Exists (The 'Why')

| Term | Why It Matters |
|------|----------------|
| **EIRP** | Determines the strength of the field launched into free space. |
| **FSPL** | Governs the *inverse-square-law* decay of signal power with distance. |
| **Fade Margin** | Accounts for *time-varying* signal dropouts due to multipath, rain, mobility. |
| **Noise Floor** | Sets the absolute lower bound of detection. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following **High-Yield Formula Table** is a one-stop revision sheet for KTU 2024 Scheme board problems on Link Budget Design.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $EIRP = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX}$ | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | dBm |
| 2 | $L_{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-Space Path Loss | dB |
| 3 | $L_{FSPL} = -147.55 + 20\log_{10}(d_m) + 20\log_{10}(f_{Hz})$ | FSPL (SI units) | dB |
| 4 | $P_{RX} = EIRP + G_{RX} - L_{FSPL} - L_{other} - M_{fade}$ | Received power | dBm |
| 5 | $S_{RX,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF + SNR_{min}$ | Receiver sensitivity | dBm |
| 6 | $L_{system} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX,min}$ | Maximum allowable path loss (MAPL) | dB |
| 7 | $Link \;Margin = P_{RX} - S_{RX,min}$ | Safety margin (must be $> 0$) | dB |
| 8 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength | m |
| 9 | $P_{RX,isotropic} = EIRP - L_{FSPL}$ | Power at isotropic RX antenna | dBm |
| 10 | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} \cdot G$ | Effective aperture of antenna | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** All **gains** are *added* (positive dB); all **losses** are *subtracted* (negative dB). Power is in **dBm**, gains/losses in **dBi/dB**. Never mix dBW with dBm without a $30\;\text{dB}$ offset.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G/4G Cell Tower Design:** Engineers compute the *Maximum Allowable Path Loss (MAPL)* to size cell radius, antenna height, and PA output.
- **Satellite Link Engineering:** GEO link budgets at 36,000 km require EIRPs of 50+ dBW; the link budget is the only sanity check before launching a $200M satellite.
- **IoT LPWAN (LoRa, NB-IoT):** Devices run at +14 dBm EIRP — the link budget determines the achievable range in rural deployments.
- **Public Safety & Military:** Military planners use link budgets to choose antenna sizes for tactical radios in jungle/desert terrain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — Friis Transmission Formula

The **Friis Transmission Equation** is the cornerstone of free-space link budget analysis. We derive it rigorously so that no KTU board question on FSPL can surprise you.

### Step 1 — Start with the Isotropic Radiator Field

An *isotropic antenna* radiates power uniformly in all directions. If the transmit power is $P_{TX}$, the power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S_d = \frac{P_{TX}}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

This is the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$, which the wave uniformly illuminates.

### Step 2 — Account for Transmit Antenna Directivity

If a *directional* antenna of gain $G_{TX}$ is used, the flux density in the direction of maximum radiation is multiplied by $G_{TX}$:

$$
S_d = \frac{P_{TX} \cdot G_{TX}}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Capture the Power with a Receive Antenna

The receive antenna intercepts this flux density through its **effective aperture** $A_e$:

$$
P_{RX} = S_d \cdot A_e = \frac{P_{TX} \cdot G_{TX} \cdot A_e}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Aperture–Gain Relationship

For any antenna, the effective aperture is related to its gain by:

$$
A_e = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \cdot G_{RX}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{RX} = \frac{P_{TX} \cdot G_{TX} \cdot G_{RX} \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d^{2}) \cdot (4\pi)} = P_{TX} \cdot G_{TX} \cdot G_{RX} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Decibels

Take $10\log_{10}$ of both sides. Note that $G$ in linear is converted to dB as $10\log_{10}G$:

$$
P_{RX}(\text{dB}) = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)
$$

The last term is **negative** (since $\lambda \ll 4\pi d$) and is the path loss:

$$
L_{FSPL}(\text{dB}) = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Express in Practical Engineering Units (km, MHz)

Substitute $\lambda = c / f$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$
L_{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c}\right)
$$

Let $d = d_{km} \times 10^{3}$ m and $f = f_{MHz} \times 10^{6}$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{FSPL} &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot d_{km}\cdot 10^{3} \cdot f_{MHz}\cdot 10^{6}}{3\times 10^{8}}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(4\pi / 3) + 90 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerically, $20\log_{10}(4\pi/3) + 90 \approx 32.45$. Thus:

$$
\boxed{L_{FSPL}(\text{dB}) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}
$$

> **QED.** This is the exact formula KTU expects in the question paper.

## 3.2 Derivation of Receiver Sensitivity

The **Receiver Sensitivity** is the weakest input signal that can be detected while maintaining a target Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).

### Step 1 — Thermal Noise Power at the Receiver Input

In a bandwidth $B$ at temperature $T$ (usually 290 K), the available noise power from a matched source is:

$$
N = kTB
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to dBm

In logarithmic form, with $kT$ at 290 K equal to $-174$ dBm/Hz:

$$
N(\text{dBm}) = -174 \;+\; 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz})
$$

### Step 3 — Add Receiver Noise Figure

The receiver electronics *amplify* the incoming signal but also add their *own* internal noise, characterized by the Noise Figure $NF$:

$$
N_{total}(\text{dBm}) = -174 \;+\; 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) \;+\; NF
$$

### Step 4 — Add Required SNR (Threshold)

For a given modulation/coding scheme to achieve a target Bit Error Rate (BER), a minimum $E_b/N_0$ (or SNR) is required:

$$
S_{RX,min}(\text{dBm}) = -174 \;+\; 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) \;+\; NF \;+\; SNR_{min}
$$

> **QED.** This is the sensitivity formula KTU examiners test under the "link feasibility" sub-question.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Problem (14-mark KTU Pattern):**
> A microwave link operates at 6 GHz with the following parameters:
> - $P_{TX} = 30$ dBm
> - $G_{TX} = 25$ dBi
> - $G_{RX} = 20$ dBi
> - $L_{TX} = L_{RX} = 2$ dB
> - $d = 50$ km
> - Fade margin $M_{fade} = 10$ dB
> - Other losses (rain, misalignment) = 5 dB
> - Receiver NF = 6 dB, Bandwidth $B = 10$ MHz, Min SNR = 12 dB
>
> **Find:** (a) Received power, (b) Receiver sensitivity, (c) Link margin and feasibility.

### Solution:

**(a) Received Power**

Compute EIRP:

$$
EIRP = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX} = 30 + 25 - 2 = 53 \; \text{dBm}
$$

Compute FSPL at 6 GHz, 50 km:

$$
L_{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(50) + 20\log_{10}(6000)
$$

$$
= 32.45 + 33.98 + 75.56 = 141.99 \; \text{dB}
$$

Compute total losses:

$$
L_{total} = L_{FSPL} + L_{RX} + M_{fade} + L_{other} = 141.99 + 2 + 10 + 5 = 158.99 \; \text{dB}
$$

Compute received power:

$$
P_{RX} = EIRP + G_{RX} - L_{total} = 53 + 20 - 158.99 = -85.99 \; \text{dBm} \approx -86 \; \text{dBm}
$$

**(b) Receiver Sensitivity**

$$
S_{RX,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(10 \times 10^{6}) + 6 + 12
$$

$$
= -174 + 70 + 6 + 12 = -86 \; \text{dBm}
$$

**(c) Link Margin**

$$
\text{Link Margin} = P_{RX} - S_{RX,min} = -86 - (-86) = 0 \; \text{dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Link is marginally feasible** — the margin is 0 dB, which is **not** practically acceptable. The engineer must either increase $P_{TX}$, use higher-gain antennas, or reduce bandwidth to add real engineering safety.

## 3.4 Maximum Allowable Path Loss (MAPL) — Engineering Design Tool

The MAPL is a *reverse* design: given a receiver's sensitivity, how much path loss can the channel tolerate?

$$
\boxed{L_{MAPL} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{TX} - L_{RX} - L_{other} - S_{RX,min} - M_{fade}}
$$

This value is then mapped to the **cell radius** via the appropriate path loss model (Okumura–Hata, COST-231, or 3GPP TR 38.901).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Link Budget

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the *sequential flow* of a link budget calculation from transmitter to receiver, including the propagation channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transmitter: P_TX dBm] --> B[Add TX Antenna Gain: G_TX dBi]
    B --> C[Subtract TX Feeder Loss: L_TX dB]
    C --> D[Compute EIRP Output]
    D --> E[Wireless Channel Block]
    E --> F[Free Space Path Loss L_FSPL dB]
    F --> G[Atmospheric and Rain Losses L_atmo dB]
    G --> H[Fade Margin M_fade dB]
    H --> I[Add RX Antenna Gain: G_RX dBi]
    I --> J[Subtract RX Feeder Loss: L_RX dB]
    J --> K[Received Power P_RX dBm]
    K --> L{Compare P_RX vs Receiver Sensitivity S_RX_min}
    L -->|P_RX greater than S_RX_min| M[Link Feasible: Link Margin positive dB]
    L -->|P_RX less than S_RX_min| N[Link NOT Feasible: Re-engineer]
    M --> O[Document Final Link Budget Table]
    N --> P[Increase P_TX or G_TX or G_RX or reduce losses]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Power Budget Tally Diagram (Power Flow)

This diagram maps the **progressive power levels** in dBm as the signal flows from TX to RX, showing where gains add and losses subtract.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: P_TX 30 dBm] -->|Add G_TX 25 dB| B[EIRP 53 dBm]
    B -->|Subtract L_FSPL 142 dB| C[After Path: 89 dBm negative 89 dBm]
    C -->|Subtract L_atmo 3 dB| D[After Atmosphere: 92 dBm negative]
    D -->|Subtract M_fade 10 dB| E[After Fade: 102 dBm negative]
    E -->|Add G_RX 20 dB| F[At RX Feed: 82 dBm negative]
    F -->|Subtract L_RX 2 dB| G[At RX Input: 84 dBm negative]
    G --> H[Threshold: Sensitivity minus 86 dBm]
    H --> I{Compare: 84 vs 86 dBm}
    I -->|Margin 2 dB| J[Link OK]
    I -->|Margin 0 dB| K[Critical Re-design]
```

## 4.3 EIRP Concept Diagram (Antenna Gain Concentration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Isotropic Radiator: Uniform sphere] --> B[Real Antenna: Directive Beam]
    B --> C[Antenna Gain G_TX dBi: Power concentration]
    C --> D[EIRP equals P_TX plus G_TX minus L_TX dBm]
    D --> E[Power launched into free space]
    E --> F[Geometric spreading: 1 over r squared]
    F --> G[Received Flux Density S_d at distance d]
    G --> H[RX antenna captures power into A_e]
    H --> I[Power delivered to RX LNA: P_RX]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Notes Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `L_TX_dB` style avoided).
> - Labels use clean uppercase text — no bold/italic markdown inside quoted strings.
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node IDs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **EIRP**. Why is it preferred over raw transmit power for regulatory and engineering analysis?

> **Model Answer:**
> **EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power)** is the equivalent power that an isotropic (omnidirectional) radiator would need to emit to produce the same peak power flux density in the direction of maximum antenna gain. It is defined as:
>
> $$EIRP = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX} \quad (\text{dBm})$$
>
> **Why preferred over raw $P_{TX}$:** EIRP *combines* transmitter power and antenna directivity into a single metric that represents the actual radiated power in the main beam direction. Regulatory bodies (FCC, ITU, ETSI) use EIRP limits to govern spectrum sharing and health-related EMF exposure. Engineering-wise, EIRP directly determines the link budget balance at the receiver and is the correct metric to compare different transmitter-antenna combinations.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **Receiver Sensitivity** and **Receiver Noise Floor**. State the formula relating them.

> **Model Answer:**
> - **Noise Floor** is the *minimum* noise power present at the receiver input due to thermal agitation and the receiver's own noise figure, *without* any signal processing gain. Formula:
>
> $$N_{floor} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF \quad (\text{dBm})$$
>
> - **Receiver Sensitivity** is the *minimum signal power* required to achieve a target SNR (and hence a target BER). Formula:
>
> $$S_{RX,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF + SNR_{min} \quad (\text{dBm})$$
>
> **Key Difference:** The noise floor is a property of the *receiver* and *bandwidth* alone. The sensitivity is the noise floor *plus* the required SNR. Sensitivity is always ≥ Noise Floor.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulations**, every Module-based Part B question offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. The two alternatives below are completely independent selections of equal difficulty.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a wireless link budget. State the Friis transmission equation and derive the Free-Space Path Loss formula in terms of distance and frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transmitter operating at 5.8 GHz with output power 1 W feeds an antenna of gain 20 dBi through a feeder of loss 3 dB. The receiver is 25 km away with antenna gain 18 dBi, feeder loss 2 dB. Compute the EIRP, FSPL, and received power assuming a 10 dB fade margin. Comment on link feasibility if the receiver sensitivity is −90 dBm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) consists of TX power source, TX feeder, TX antenna, propagation channel, RX antenna, RX feeder, and RX detector. **Friis Transmission Equation** in linear form:

$$
P_{RX} = P_{TX} G_{TX} G_{RX} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

**FSPL Derivation** — start from $L_{FSPL} = (4\pi d / \lambda)^2$ and convert to dB:

$$
L_{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

(Full derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes.)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Convert $P_{TX}$ to dBm**
$$P_{TX} = 10\log_{10}(1000) = 30 \;\text{dBm}$$

**Step 2: Compute EIRP**
$$EIRP = 30 + 20 - 3 = 47 \;\text{dBm}$$

**Step 3: Compute FSPL at 5.8 GHz, 25 km**
$$
L_{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(25) + 20\log_{10}(5800)
$$
$$
= 32.45 + 27.96 + 75.27 = 135.68 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Received Power**
$$
P_{RX} = EIRP + G_{RX} - L_{FSPL} - L_{RX} - M_{fade}
$$
$$
P_{RX} = 47 + 18 - 135.68 - 2 - 10 = -82.68 \;\text{dBm}
$$

**Step 5: Link Feasibility**
$$
\text{Link Margin} = P_{RX} - S_{RX,min} = -82.68 - (-90) = +7.32 \;\text{dB}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The link margin is **+7.32 dB**, which is *acceptable* for a reliable link (typically a margin of ≥ 10 dB is desired for carrier-grade links, so the design is functional but on the lower side).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Receiver Sensitivity** of a wireless receiver. Explain the role of the Noise Figure in link budget design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wi-Fi 6 access point transmits at 5.2 GHz with $P_{TX} = 20$ dBm, $G_{TX} = 6$ dBi, and an outdoor user equipment is 200 m away with $G_{RX} = 2$ dBi. The bandwidth is 20 MHz, NF = 7 dB, and the required SNR for a target MCS index is 18 dB. Compute the received power, the receiver sensitivity, and the link margin. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Receiver Sensitivity Derivation:**

Thermal noise at the receiver input is given by $N = kTB$. Converting to dBm using $kT$ at 290 K = $-174$ dBm/Hz:

$$
N_{floor} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF
$$

Adding the minimum required SNR (which encodes the target BER requirement):

$$
S_{RX,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{Hz}) + NF + SNR_{min}
$$

**Role of Noise Figure (NF):** NF quantifies the *degradation* in SNR caused by the receiver's own electronics. It is defined as $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$ where $F = (SNR_{in}/SNR_{out})$. In link budget design, NF determines the receiver's sensitivity floor — a *lower* NF improves sensitivity, allowing either a longer range, smaller antenna, or lower $P_{TX}$. Modern LNA design pushes NF below 2 dB for satellite and 5G mmWave receivers.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: FSPL at 5.2 GHz, 200 m**

Convert units: $d = 0.2$ km, $f = 5200$ MHz.

$$
L_{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(0.2) + 20\log_{10}(5200)
$$
$$
= 32.45 + (-13.98) + 74.32 = 92.79 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 2: Received Power** (assuming negligible other losses and no explicit fade margin for an indoor Wi-Fi link)

$$
P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{FSPL} = 20 + 6 + 2 - 92.79 = -64.79 \;\text{dBm}
$$

**Step 3: Receiver Sensitivity**

$$
S_{RX,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(20 \times 10^{6}) + 7 + 18
$$
$$
= -174 + 73.01 + 7 + 18 = -75.99 \;\text{dBm} \approx -76 \;\text{dBm}
$$

**Step 4: Link Margin**

$$
\text{Link Margin} = -64.79 - (-75.99) = +11.2 \;\text{dB}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The link is **robust** with a healthy +11.2 dB margin, sufficient for a stable Wi-Fi 6 connection at 200 m LOS.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Link Budget Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Errors** — Mixing dBm and dBW. Remember: $P(\text{dBm}) = P(\text{dBW}) + 30$. A single unit slip costs **1–2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting Feeder Losses** — Many students compute only $P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{FSPL}$, ignoring $L_{TX}$, $L_{RX}$, and $L_{other}$. Examiners specifically test this — *always include all losses*.
> 3. **Linear vs dB Confusion** — Multiplying gains in linear domain but adding them in dB. Use the same domain throughout.
> 4. **Sign Error in Fade Margin** — Fade margin is *subtracted* from the received power (it reduces effective $P_{RX}$). Adding it is a critical error.
> 5. **FSPL Formula Variant Selection** — Use the $32.45 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$ version for km/MHz; the $-147.55$ version is for m/Hz. Substituting into the wrong one gives a 10 dB error per factor-of-10 mismatch.
> 6. **Negative dBm Interpretation** — Students often treat $-80$ dBm as a "small" number; but $-80$ dBm is $10^{-11}$ W — vanishingly small. Express magnitudes in W when sanity-checking.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Link budget is a power-balance equation in dB** — every gain is added, every loss is subtracted.
- ✅ **EIRP = $P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX}$** — the *single most important* output of the TX side.
- ✅ **Friis Equation:** $P_{RX} = P_{TX} G_{TX} G_{RX} (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$
- ✅ **FSPL Formula (km/MHz):** $L_{FSPL} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$
- ✅ **FSPL Formula (m/Hz):** $L_{FSPL} = -147.55 + 20\log_{10}(d_m) + 20\log_{10}(f_{Hz})$
- ✅ **FSPL depends on distance AND frequency** — both in a $20\log$ form. Doubling either increases path loss by **6 dB**.
- ✅ **Receiver Sensitivity:** $S_{RX,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + NF + SNR_{min}$
- ✅ **Link Margin = $P_{RX} - S_{RX,min}$** — must be **positive** for a viable link. KTU accepts $\geq 3$ dB as *minimally acceptable*; $\geq 10$ dB is *robust*.
- ✅ **Maximum Allowable Path Loss (MAPL):** $L_{MAPL} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{TX} - L_{RX} - L_{other} - S_{RX,min} - M_{fade}$
- ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $kT = -174$ dBm/Hz at 290 K; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6$ dB; $10\log_{10}(2) \approx 3$ dB.
- ✅ **Unit Conversions:** $1$ W = $30$ dBm; $1$ mW = $0$ dBm; $1$ kW = $60$ dBm.
- ✅ **Antenna aperture formula:** $A_e = (\lambda^2 / 4\pi) G$ — used when $G_{RX}$ must be derived from physical antenna area.
- ✅ **Higher frequency → Higher path loss** — this is why mmWave 5G cells are much smaller than sub-6 GHz cells.
- ✅ **KTU 14-mark pattern** — always (a) theory/derivation (7 marks) + (b) numerical (7 marks). Practice both halves separately.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Distance Coverage Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
