<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCCPT502 – Introduction to Wireless Communication**, **Module 4** is dedicated to the study of **Multiple Access Techniques (MATs)** that enable a finite pool of radio resources (frequency, time, code, space) to be shared simultaneously among a large number of mobile subscribers without mutually destructive interference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Access (MA)** is a technique by which **multiple users** (or signals) share a **common communication channel** such that each user can communicate independently with an acceptable quality of service (QoS) and minimal cross-interference.

The **primary radio resources** that can be shared are:

1. **Frequency Spectrum (F)**
2. **Time (T)**
3. **Code (C)** — a unique pseudorandom spreading sequence
4. **Space (S)** — antenna sectoring, SDMA, MIMO
5. **Hybrid combinations** of the above

The fundamental *multiple access equation* (the radio resource budget) is formally expressed as:

$$N \;\propto\; \frac{F \times T \times C \times S}{\Delta \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{threshold}}}$$

where **N** is the number of simultaneous users, **Δ** is the channel bandwidth, and **SNR_threshold** is the minimum signal-to-noise ratio required for an acceptable bit-error-rate (BER), typically **BER ≤ 10⁻³** for voice and **BER ≤ 10⁻⁶** for data services.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Highway and Toll-Booth" Model**

Imagine a **single radio channel** as a **single-lane bridge** connecting two cities. If every car (user) tried to use the bridge at the same time, there would be a **traffic jam (collision)** and no one would cross efficiently.

* **FDMA (Frequency Division MA):** Build **multiple parallel bridges** at different altitudes — each car uses its own bridge. Many users, separate frequencies, simultaneous.
* **TDMA (Time Division MA):** Keep a **single bridge**, but allow only **one car at a time**, cycling cars in **time slots** (round-robin).
* **CDMA (Code Division MA):** Keep a **single bridge**, but give every car a **unique "secret whistle"** (spreading code) so all cars cross together; the receiver filters using the matching whistle.
* **SDMA (Space Division MA):** Use **directional lanes and smart antenna beams** so spatially separated users can be served by the **same frequency at the same time**.
* **OFDMA (Orthogonal FDMA):** Subdivide the bridge into many **sub-bridges** (subcarriers) and let each user occupy a subset — this is the heart of **4G LTE and 5G NR**.

This is the *intuitive essence* of Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Scope of Module 4 (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Module 4** of PCCPT502 systematically covers the following multiple-access families:

| Sub-Topic | Symbol | Resource Shared | Channel Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Division MA | **FDMA** | Frequency | Narrowband |
| Time Division MA | **TDMA** | Time | Narrowband |
| Code Division MA | **CDMA** | Code | Spread-Spectrum (Wideband) |
| Space Division MA | **SDMA** | Space (sector / beam) | Narrowband / Wideband |
| Orthogonal FDMA | **OFDMA** | Frequency + Time | Wideband / Multicarrier |
| Hybrid (e.g., TDMA/FDMA, OFDMA) | — | Combined | Modern 4G/5G |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The introductory lecture (this note) lays the conceptual foundation for **why** multiple access is necessary, the **classification** of techniques, and the **performance metrics** used to compare them (capacity, spectral efficiency, delay, fairness, complexity).

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Performance Metrics (Key Vocabulary)

These are the **yardsticks** KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Spectral Efficiency (η)** — bits per second per Hertz per cell; units: **bps/Hz/cell**.
2. **Channel Capacity (C)** — maximum error-free data rate; units: **bps**.
3. **Number of Simultaneous Users (N)** — capacity of the system in user count.
4. **Bit-Error-Rate (BER)** — probability of bit error, target **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶**.
5. **Throughput (T)** — useful data delivered per unit time, accounting for overheads.
6. **Delay (τ)** — end-to-end latency.
7. **Jitter (σ_τ)** — variability in delay.
8. **Fairness Index (FI)** — Jain's fairness index ∈ [0, 1].

The **Shannon-Hartley theorem** (1948) bounds channel capacity:

$$C \;=\; B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\;\;\text{[bps]}$$

where **B** is bandwidth in Hz, **S** is signal power, and **N** is noise power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Problem of Multiple Access

In a **cellular wireless system**, the base station (BS) must serve **M mobile stations (MS)** in its coverage area using a **limited radio spectrum**. The system designer must choose *how* to partition the spectrum.

The **resource hypercube** of a wireless system is defined by the four axes:

$$
\boxed{
\mathcal{R} \;=\; \{ F,\;T,\;C,\;S \}
}
$$

Each multiple-access technique **assigns a unique tuple** from this 4-D space to every user:

| Technique | User *k* Address Tuple |
|---|---|
| FDMA | $(f_k, -, -, -)$ |
| TDMA | $(-, t_k, -, -)$ |
| CDMA | $(-, -, c_k, -)$ |
| SDMA | $(-, -, -, s_k)$ |
| OFDMA | $(f_k, t_k, -, -)$ |
| Hybrid CDMA/FDMA | $(f_k, -, c_k, -)$ |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Multiple Access Techniques

### 2.2.1 Narrowband vs Wideband Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Narrowband System:** A system is *narrowband* if the **coherence bandwidth (Bc) of the channel** is much greater than the signal bandwidth **W**, i.e., $W \ll B_c$. Channels are typically **flat-fading**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wideband System:** $W > B_c$, the signal experiences **frequency-selective fading**; equalization or spread-spectrum processing is required.

### 2.2.2 The Two Major Families

```
Multiple Access Techniques
├── Narrowband MA  (channel BW < coherence BW)
│     ├── FDMA  (1G AMPS)
│     └── TDMA  (2G GSM)
└── Wideband / Spread-Spectrum MA  (channel BW >> coherence BW)
      ├── Direct-Sequence CDMA  (DS-CDMA, IS-95, 3G WCDMA)
      ├── Frequency-Hopping CDMA  (FH-CDMA, Bluetooth)
      └── Hybrid CDMA (used in 3G)
Modern Systems (4G/5G)
  └── OFDMA + SC-FDMA + Massive MIMO
```

---

## 2.3 Why Multiple Access is Indispensable — The Capacity Argument

By **Shannon's theorem**, the capacity of a single channel of bandwidth **B** is:

$$C_{\text{single}} \;=\; B \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right) \;\;\text{bps}$$

If this *single* channel is shared among **N users** in FDMA, each user gets a slice $B/N$ (ignoring guard bands), so the per-user capacity is:

$$C_{\text{FDMA, user}} \;=\; \frac{B}{N} \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}_u\right)$$

For CDMA, all users share the *full* bandwidth **B** simultaneously, but the effective SNR is reduced by the processing gain and the multiple-access interference (MAI):

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{eff, CDMA}} \;=\; \frac{\text{SNR}_u}{1 + (N-1)\cdot \rho}$$

where **ρ** is the cross-correlation between codes (ideally 0 for orthogonal codes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must understand that **no single MA technique is universally optimal** — the choice depends on the **traffic profile**, **mobility**, **propagation environment**, **hardware complexity**, and **standard** (GSM, IS-95, LTE, 5G NR).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Upper bound on any channel |
| 2 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = C/B$ | bps/Hz | Key 4G/5G metric |
| 3 | FDMA Channel Count | $N_{\text{FDMA}} = \lfloor B / (B_c + B_g) \rfloor$ | channels | $B_g$ = guard band |
| 4 | TDMA Time-Slot Users | $N_{\text{TDMA}} = N_f \times N_t$ | users | $N_f$ = freq × $N_t$ = slots |
| 5 | CDMA Processing Gain | $G_p = W / R$ | dimensionless | $W$=chip rate, $R$=data rate |
| 6 | CDMA Capacity (ideal) | $N_{\text{CDMA}} \approx 1 + W/R$ | users | Pole capacity, no voice activity |
| 7 | CDMA Capacity (real) | $N \approx \frac{G_p}{E_b/N_0} \cdot \frac{1}{V \cdot (1+f)}$ | users | VAF, frequency reuse factor |
| 8 | OFDMA Subcarriers | $N_{\text{sc}} = \text{FFT size}$ (e.g., 512, 1024, 2048) | subcarriers | LTE: 72 subcarriers per RB |
| 9 | OFDMA Symbol Duration | $T_u = N_{\text{sc}} / \Delta f$ | seconds | LTE: 66.7 μs |
| 10 | Bit-Energy / Noise Density | $E_b / N_0 = (S/N) \cdot (B/R)$ | dB or linear | Key CDMA link-budget metric |
| 11 | Jain's Fairness Index | $FI = \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{n \sum x_i^2}$ | [0, 1] | 1 = perfectly fair |
| 12 | Multiple Access Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{MA}} = N \cdot R_u / B_{\text{total}}$ | bps/Hz | Comparison across MA schemes |

> [!CAUTION]
> KTU exam trap: students often confuse **processing gain** $G_p = W/R$ (chip rate / data rate) with the **spreading factor (SF)**. In DS-CDMA, the SF is the number of chips per data bit, **identical in value** to $G_p$ in DS systems — but in FH-CDMA they may differ.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Multiple Access |
|---|---|
| **1G AMPS** (1980s) | FDMA — 30 kHz channels per user |
| **2G GSM** (1990s) | TDMA on FDMA — 8 slots/frame, 200 kHz carriers |
| **3G UMTS / WCDMA** (2000s) | Direct-Sequence CDMA — 5 MHz carriers |
| **4G LTE** (2010s) | **OFDMA in downlink**, **SC-FDMA in uplink** |
| **5G NR** (2020s) | **Flexible OFDMA + Massive MIMO + mmWave** |
| **Satellite (GPS)** | CDMA — all 24+ satellites share same band |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | CSMA/CA (random access), OFDM in PHY |
| **Bluetooth** | FH-CDMA (1600 hops/s) |
| **IoT (LoRa, Sigfox)** | Chirp-Spread-Spectrum / Ultra-Narrowband |
| **Military / Anti-Jam** | Spread-Spectrum CDMA (low probability of intercept) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **explicit algebraic walk-throughs** of the most heavily tested derivations in Module 4's introductory concepts.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Shannon Capacity of an FDMA Channel per User

**Starting point:** Shannon's capacity theorem for an AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and SNR = $S/N$:

$$C \;=\; B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bps]}$$

**Step 1.** In **FDMA**, the total system bandwidth $B_{\text{total}}$ is partitioned into $N$ non-overlapping sub-channels, each of width:

$$
B_u \;=\; \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{N} \quad \text{(ignoring guard bands for now)}
$$

**Step 2.** Assume each user transmits with the **same power** $S_u = S_{\text{total}}/N$ (uniform power allocation — a common assumption in textbook problems). The noise power per user is:

$$N_u \;=\; kTB_u \;=\; kT \cdot \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{N}$$

**Step 3.** The per-user SNR is then:

$$
\text{SNR}_u \;=\; \frac{S_u}{N_u} \;=\; \frac{S_{\text{total}}/N}{kT \cdot B_{\text{total}}/N} \;=\; \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{kT B_{\text{total}}} \;=\; \text{SNR}_{\text{total}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Under ideal uniform power allocation, the per-user SNR in FDMA equals the total system SNR. (This is a **key result** frequently tested by KTU.)

**Step 4.** Per-user capacity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u^{\text{FDMA}} &= B_u \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}_u\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{N} \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{total}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{N} \cdot C_{\text{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Total system capacity (sum over N users):

$$
\boxed{
C_{\text{system}}^{\text{FDMA}} \;=\; B_{\text{total}} \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{N kT B_{\text{total}}}\right)
}
$$

Compare with **TDMA** under identical assumptions (block-fading, perfect synchronization):

$$
\boxed{
C_{\text{system}}^{\text{TDMA}} \;=\; B_{\text{total}} \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{kT B_{\text{total}}}\right)
}
$$

> **Critical observation:** TDMA *concentrates* all transmit power into a fraction $1/N$ of the time, so during the active slot, the **transmit power per user is N times higher** than in FDMA. Therefore:

$$\text{SNR}_u^{\text{TDMA}} \;=\; N \cdot \text{SNR}_u^{\text{FDMA}}$$

This is the **fundamental FDMA-vs-TDMA tradeoff** that KTU frequently tests.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CDMA Pole Capacity

**Starting point:** In a synchronous DS-CDMA system with processing gain $G_p = W/R$ and a voice activity factor $V$ (typically 0.4–0.5), the bit-energy-to-noise-density ratio for user $i$ is:

$$
\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_i \;=\; \frac{G_p}{(N-1) \cdot V \cdot (1 + f)} \quad \text{(for equal-power users)}
$$

where $f$ is the **other-cell interference factor** (typically 0.55 for omnidirectional, 0.27 for 3-sectored cells).

**Step 1.** To support the minimum required $E_b/N_0 = \beta$ (e.g., $\beta = 7$ dB for voice):

$$
\beta \;\leq\; \frac{G_p}{(N-1) V (1+f)}
$$

**Step 2.** Solve for the maximum N (the **pole capacity**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N - 1 \;&\leq\; \frac{G_p}{\beta \cdot V \cdot (1+f)} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{pole}} \;&=\; 1 + \frac{G_p}{\beta \cdot V \cdot (1+f)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute typical values for IS-95: $G_p = 128$ (chip rate 1.228 MHz, data rate 9.6 kbps), $\beta = 7$ dB $= 5.01$, $V = 0.5$, $f = 0.55$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{pole}} &= 1 + \frac{128}{5.01 \times 0.5 \times 1.55} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + \frac{128}{3.883} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 32.97 \\[4pt]
&\approx 34 \text{ users per cell}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the textbook value (~32–35 voice users per cell for IS-95).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\boxed{N_{\text{pole}}^{\text{CDMA}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{G_p}{\beta V (1+f)}}$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: FDMA vs TDMA vs CDMA

| Property | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resource Shared | Frequency | Time | Code |
| Channel Type | Narrowband | Narrowband | Wideband (Spread-Spectrum) |
| Guard Bands | Yes (essential) | No (time-separated) | No (code-separated) |
| Synchronization | Frequency only | **Strict time sync** | **Strict code sync** |
| Power Control | Less critical | Moderate | **Critical** (near-far problem) |
| Multipath / Fading | Susceptible | Susceptible | **Resistant** (Rake receiver) |
| Handoff Complexity | Moderate | High | **Soft handoff** simpler |
| Capacity (fixed BW) | Hard limit | Hard limit | **Soft limit** (interference-limited) |
| Privacy / Security | Low | Low | **High** (pseudo-random code) |
| Channel Reuse | N-cell reuse | N-cell reuse | **Universal** (1-cell reuse) |
| Generation | **1G** (AMPS) | **2G** (GSM) | **3G** (WCDMA) |
| Hardware Complexity | Low | Moderate | **High** (Rake, power control) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: OFDMA Subcarrier Count and Symbol Time

**Given:** A 4G LTE system uses an FFT size of $N_{\text{FFT}} = 1024$ for a 10 MHz channel, with subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 15$ kHz.

**Step 1.** Compute the useful OFDM symbol time:

$$
T_u \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta f} \;=\; \frac{1}{15{,}000} \;\approx\; 66.67 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2.** Add cyclic prefix (CP) — LTE normal CP = $T_{\text{CP}} = 4.69 \,\mu\text{s}$ (first symbol) or 5.21 μs (others). Total OFDM symbol time:

$$
T_{\text{sym}} \;=\; T_u + T_{\text{CP}} \;\approx\; 71.4 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3.** Number of subcarriers allocated per Resource Block (RB):

$$
N_{\text{sc}}^{\text{RB}} \;=\; 12 \text{ subcarriers per RB}
$$

**Step 4.** For a 10 MHz LTE channel, total usable subcarriers:

$$
N_{\text{usable}} \;=\; 600 \text{ subcarriers} \;\Rightarrow\; 50 \text{ RBs}
$$

> **Why 600 and not 1024?** The remaining 424 subcarriers are **guard band / DC** (unused to avoid adjacent-channel interference and DC-offset problems).

**Step 5.** Total occupied bandwidth:

$$
B_{\text{occupied}} \;=\; 600 \times 15 \text{ kHz} \;=\; 9 \text{ MHz} \quad (\text{within a 10 MHz channel})
$$

This explains the **"occupied vs allocated"** bandwidth distinction frequently tested.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

Define **Multiple Access Technique**. List any **four** multiple-access schemes used in modern wireless communication systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Multiple Access is a method by which a large number of users share a **finite radio resource** (frequency/time/code/space) such that each user can communicate independently with acceptable quality of service.

**Four MA schemes (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **FDMA** — Frequency Division Multiple Access
2. **TDMA** — Time Division Multiple Access
3. **CDMA** — Code Division Multiple Access
4. **SDMA** — Space Division Multiple Access

(Acceptable extras: OFDMA, CSMA, ALOHA, Hybrid MA.)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

Differentiate between **narrowband** and **wideband** multiple access systems. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Narrowband MA | Wideband MA (Spread-Spectrum) |
|---|---|---|
| Signal BW vs Coherence BW | $W \ll B_c$ | $W \gg B_c$ |
| Fading Type | Flat fading | Frequency-selective fading |
| Spectral Density | High | Low (spread) |
| Example | FDMA, TDMA (GSM) | CDMA (IS-95, WCDMA) |
| Interference | Co-channel | Multiple-access interference |

**Examples (1 mark):**
* Narrowband: **GSM (2G)** uses TDMA with 200 kHz carriers.
* Wideband: **IS-95 (3G)** uses DS-CDMA with 1.228 MHz chip rate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the fundamental **principle of FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA** with neat block diagrams. Compare their **advantages and disadvantages** in a tabular form. (7 marks)

**Q.A (b)** A wireless system has a total bandwidth of **25 MHz**. Each user requires a channel of **30 kHz** with a guard band of **10 kHz**. If the system uses FDMA, calculate:
  (i) The **number of simultaneous users** per cell.
  (ii) The **spectral efficiency** if each user transmits at 9.6 kbps.
  (iii) The **total system throughput** for 120 cells in a 7-cell reuse cluster. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

**FDMA (2 marks):**
* Each user is assigned a **unique frequency band** of width $B_c$.
* Guard bands $B_g$ separate adjacent channels.
* All users transmit **simultaneously** but at different frequencies.
* Used in 1G AMPS (30 kHz channels per user).

**TDMA (2 marks):**
* Each user is assigned a **unique time slot** within a recurring frame.
* All users share the **same frequency band**.
* Requires **precise time synchronization** between BS and MS.
* Used in 2G GSM (8 slots/frame, 4.615 ms frame).

**CDMA (2 marks):**
* All users transmit **simultaneously on the same frequency**.
* Each user is identified by a **unique pseudorandom spreading code**.
* Receiver correlates with the desired code; others appear as noise.
* Used in 3G WCDMA, GPS, IS-95.

**Comparison table (1 mark):**

| Feature | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resource | Frequency | Time | Code |
| Synchronization | Frequency only | Strict time | Strict code |
| Guard bands | Required | Not required | Not required |
| Multipath resistance | Poor | Poor | Good (Rake) |
| Handoff | Hard | Hard | Soft |
| Capacity limit | Hard | Hard | Soft (interference) |
| Privacy | Low | Low | High |

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

**Given:** $B_{\text{total}} = 25$ MHz, $B_c = 30$ kHz per user, $B_g = 10$ kHz, $R_u = 9.6$ kbps.

**Step 1 — Bandwidth per user including guard band:**

$$
B_{\text{user}} = B_c + B_g = 30 + 10 = 40 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of users per cell (i):**

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_{\text{user}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{25{,}000 \text{ kHz}}{40 \text{ kHz}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 625 \rfloor
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 625 \text{ users per cell}}
$$

**Step 3 — Spectral efficiency (ii):**

$$
\eta = \frac{N \times R_u}{B_{\text{total}}} = \frac{625 \times 9.6 \text{ kbps}}{25{,}000 \text{ kHz}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta = 0.24 \text{ bps/Hz/cell}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total throughput with 7-cell reuse cluster (iii):**

With cluster size $N_{\text{cluster}} = 7$, the spectrum is **reused every 7 cells**, so total cells in 120-cell deployment:

$$
N_{\text{cell-groups}} = \frac{120}{7} \approx 17.14
$$

Taking integer:

$$
N_{\text{cell-groups}} = 17 \text{ full clusters} = 17 \times 7 = 119 \text{ cells}
$$

Users per cluster = $7 \times 625 = 4{,}375$ users.

Total system throughput:

$$
T_{\text{system}} = 17 \times 4375 \times 9.6 \text{ kbps} = 17 \times 42{,}000 \text{ kbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{system}} = 714{,}000 \text{ kbps} = 714 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.B (a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the working of a **Direct-Sequence CDMA (DS-CDMA)** transmitter and receiver. Derive the expression for **processing gain** and explain how it provides **interference rejection**. (7 marks)

**Q.B (b)** A DS-CDMA system has a chip rate of **1.228 Mcps** and a data rate of **9.6 kbps**. The required $E_b/N_0$ for acceptable voice quality is **7 dB**. Assuming voice activity factor $V = 0.4$ and other-cell interference factor $f = 0.6$, calculate the **pole capacity** of the system. If the operator wants to support **40 users per cell**, what **$E_b/N_0$** must be achieved? (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

**DS-CDMA Transmitter (2 marks):**
* Source data $b(t)$ at rate $R$ bps is multiplied by a **pseudorandom (PN) code** $c(t)$ at chip rate $W$ chips/s.
* Spreading factor (processing gain) is $G_p = W/R$.
* Modulated onto an RF carrier and transmitted.

**DS-CDMA Receiver (2 marks):**
* The received signal is the **sum of all users'** spread signals plus noise.
* Receiver **re-multiplies** by the *same* PN code of the desired user (synchronized).
* After correlation, the desired signal **despreads** (returns to $R$ bps); other users' signals remain spread (low PSD).
* A bandpass filter at the data bandwidth removes the spread interference.

**Processing Gain derivation (2 marks):**

$$
G_p = \frac{W}{R} = \frac{\text{Chip rate}}{\text{Data rate}} = \frac{1.228 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} \approx 128
$$

**Interference rejection (1 mark):**
The desired user's signal bandwidth is **reduced from W to R** during despreading, while interferers remain spread over W. The **narrowband post-correlation filter** attenuates interferers by the factor $G_p$, providing a **processing gain of 21 dB** (= $10 \log_{10} 128$).

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**Given:** $W = 1.228$ Mcps, $R = 9.6$ kbps, $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB, $V = 0.4$, $f = 0.6$.

**Step 1 — Processing gain:**

$$
G_p = \frac{W}{R} = \frac{1.228 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 127.92 \approx 128
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $E_b/N_0$ to linear:**

$$
\beta = 10^{7/10} = 10^{0.7} = 5.012
$$

**Step 3 — Apply pole capacity formula:**

$$
N_{\text{pole}} = 1 + \frac{G_p}{\beta \cdot V \cdot (1+f)} = 1 + \frac{128}{5.012 \times 0.4 \times 1.6}
$$

Denominator:

$$
5.012 \times 0.4 = 2.0048; \quad 2.0048 \times 1.6 = 3.2077
$$

$$
N_{\text{pole}} = 1 + \frac{128}{3.2077} = 1 + 39.90
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{pole}} \approx 40.9 \;\Rightarrow\; 40 \text{ users per cell}}
$$

**Step 4 — If the operator wants $N = 40$ users, find required $E_b/N_0$:**

Rearranging:

$$
\beta_{\text{req}} = \frac{G_p}{(N - 1) \cdot V \cdot (1+f)} = \frac{128}{39 \times 0.4 \times 1.6}
$$

Denominator: $39 \times 0.64 = 24.96$

$$
\beta_{\text{req}} = \frac{128}{24.96} = 5.128
$$

In dB:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{req}} = 10 \log_{10}(5.128) = 7.10 \text{ dB}}
$$

The system is **operating right at its pole capacity** — any additional user or additional interference will degrade BER catastrophically (the **CDMA "cliff effect"**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1"** in $N - 1$. Many students write $N = G_p / (\beta V (1+f))$ and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing dB and linear**: $\beta$ in the pole capacity formula MUST be linear. KTU examiners explicitly test this conversion.
> 3. **Confusing processing gain with spreading factor**: They are numerically equal in DS-CDMA but conceptually different — define both.
> 4. **Ignoring voice activity factor (VAF)**: Real systems *always* have VAF < 1; omitting it inflates capacity by 2× and is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 5. **No unit consistency**: Always state $W$ in chips/s, $R$ in bps, $B$ in Hz.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet for the Module 4 Introduction.**

### **A. Core Concepts You MUST Know**
- [x] Multiple Access = sharing of $\{F, T, C, S\}$ resources among users.
- [x] FDMA = Frequency; TDMA = Time; CDMA = Code; SDMA = Space; OFDMA = Frequency + Time.
- [x] Narrowband MA: $W \ll B_c$ (flat fading).
- [x] Wideband MA: $W \gg B_c$ (frequency-selective, spread-spectrum).
- [x] Processing Gain $G_p = W/R$ is the cornerstone of CDMA.
- [x] Pole Capacity $N_{\text{pole}} = 1 + G_p / [\beta V (1+f)]$.
- [x] Shannon capacity $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- [x] Spectral efficiency $\eta = C/B$ in bps/Hz.
- [x] FDMA users need guard bands; TDMA needs time sync; CDMA needs code & power control.

### **B. Generation-MA Mapping (Always Tested)**
- [x] 1G → FDMA (analog)
- [x] 2G → TDMA/FDMA (GSM)
- [x] 3G → WCDMA / CDMA2000
- [x] 4G → OFDMA (downlink), SC-FDMA (uplink)
- [x] 5G → OFDMA + Massive MIMO + mmWave

### **C. Key Tradeoffs**
- [x] FDMA has continuous transmission; TDMA has bursty transmission.
- [x] TDMA concentrates power → higher instantaneous SNR per user.
- [x] CDMA is **interference-limited**, not bandwidth-limited → "soft capacity."
- [x] CDMA is prone to **near-far problem** → requires tight power control (e.g., 800 Hz update rate in WCDMA).
- [x] OFDMA adds **cyclic prefix** to combat ISI; CP overhead is $\sim 7\%$ (LTE normal CP).

### **D. Common Numerical Values to Memorize**
- [x] AMPS channel BW: 30 kHz per user (FDMA).
- [x] GSM channel BW: 200 kHz, 8 slots/frame, 4.615 ms frame, 270.833 kbps carrier rate.
- [x] IS-95 chip rate: 1.228 Mcps, $G_p = 128$.
- [x] LTE subcarrier spacing: 15 kHz; FFT size: 2048 (for 20 MHz).
- [x] LTE normal CP: 4.69 μs (symbol 0) / 5.21 μs (others); useful symbol: 66.67 μs.
- [x] LTE Resource Block: 12 subcarriers × 7 OFDM symbols = 84 REs.
- [x] Voice $E_b/N_0$ requirement: ~7 dB; data (QPSK): ~5 dB; 64-QAM: ~15 dB.
- [x] Voice activity factor VAF: ~0.4 (in duplex conversation).
- [x] Other-cell interference factor: 0.55 (omni) / 0.27 (3-sector).

### **E. Pitfalls (Re-stated for Final Warning)**
- [x] Always convert dB to linear for capacity formulas.
- [x] Always include the "+1" or "N-1" carefully in CDMA capacity.
- [x] Don't confuse *bandwidth per user* (FDMA) with *chip rate* (CDMA).
- [x] Don't write $N = B/B_c$ without guard bands in FDMA problems.
- [x] Don't claim CDMA "has unlimited capacity" — it is **interference-limited**.

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Access (MA)** is a set of techniques that allow a large number of mobile users (subscribers) to share the finite allocated radio spectrum efficiently so that the **total capacity of the system is maximized**, while maintaining an acceptable quality of service (QoS) — measured in terms of carrier-to-interference ratio (C/I), bit error rate (BER), and spectral efficiency (η).

In a wireless cellular system, the available **bandwidth (BW)** is the most scarce resource. Multiple Access Strategies (also called **Channelization** or **Multiuser Access** schemes) define the *orthogonal dimensions* along which the spectrum is partitioned so that independent users can communicate simultaneously with the **Base Station (BS)** without mutual interference.

The four classical strategies compared in this module are:

| Strategy | Orthogonal Dimension | Standard Use |
|---|---|---|
| **FDMA** | Frequency | 1G Analog (AMPS) |
| **TDMA** | Time | 2G (GSM) |
| **CDMA** | Code (Spreading) | 3G (IS-95, WCDMA) |
| **SDMA** | Space (Direction) | 4G/5G Beamforming |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Hall" Analogy

Imagine a **massive banquet hall (the cellular spectrum)** that must host **1,000 guests (mobile users)** who all need to place orders and receive food (transmit/receive data) at the same time.

- **FDMA** is like dividing the hall into **100 small private rooms** (frequency sub-bands). Each room has its own waiter. Guests in different rooms never hear each other. *Easy to manage, but you waste space because each room needs walls (guard bands) and the rooms are never perfectly full.*
- **TDMA** is like leaving the hall as **one large open space**, but seating is strictly scheduled. Each guest gets exactly **5 minutes** (a time slot) to order, then the next guest uses the same chair. *Very efficient use of chairs, but everyone must be perfectly synchronized to a master clock (network synchronization).*
- **CDMA** is like letting **all 1,000 guests talk at once in the same hall**, but each one speaks a **different language** (a unique code). The waiter (receiver) understands *only one language*, so the other 999 conversations sound like background noise. *Spectrally efficient, but everyone must learn their unique language, and one loud guest can drown out others (the near-far problem).*
- **SDMA** is like using **directional microphones and speakers** aimed only at specific tables. The same frequency and time are reused, but the signal only "hears" the intended direction. *Requires smart antennas and is the foundation of 5G Massive MIMO.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The exam almost always asks for a *comparison table* followed by the *strengths/weaknesses* of at least two schemes. Knowing the **near-far problem**, **guard band overhead**, and **synchronization requirement** is mandatory.

## 1.3 Why Comparison is Essential

Modern cellular systems are **hybrid** — for example, **3G uses CDMA + TDMA**, **4G LTE uses OFDMA + SC-FDMA + SDMA (MIMO)**, and **5G NR uses flexible OFDMA + SDMA**. A system engineer must compare multiple access strategies on standardized metrics before choosing one for deployment. The next sections derive these comparison parameters rigorously.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Trade-off (The "Why" Behind Every Strategy)

Every multiple access scheme trades one resource against another. The master relationship for a system of $N$ users with a total bandwidth $B$ Hz and total symbol duration $T$ seconds is given by the **Time-Frequency-Code Resource Cube**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Resources} &= B \times T \times C \geq N \times R_b
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $B$ = total available bandwidth (Hz)
- $T$ = total frame time (s)
- $C$ = number of orthogonal codes
- $N$ = number of users
- $R_b$ = per-user bit rate (bps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Multi-Access Equation (Kishore Trivedi):** $\frac{N}{B} \cdot \frac{R_b}{T} \leq 1$. This is the core inequality the examiner tests. It states that the *product of spectral load and time-load cannot exceed 1* in an orthogonal system.

## 2.2 Key Comparison Parameters (Engineering Metrics)

A KTU answer should compare the four strategies along these **eight** parameters:

1. **Spectral Efficiency (η)** — bits/second/Hz per cell
2. **Guard Band / Guard Time Overhead**
3. **Synchronization Requirement** (carrier, slot, frame)
4. **Power Control Necessity** (near-far problem)
5. **Co-channel Interference (CCI) Handling**
6. **Multipath / Fading Robustness**
7. **Cell Capacity Formula**
8. **Implementation Complexity**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` notation (not `|`) for absolute values to keep Markdown tables intact.

| # | Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | SDMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capacity per Cell (C)** | $C = \frac{M}{k}$ (Channels) | $C = \frac{M}{k}$ (Channels) | $C = M$ (Users, ideal) | $C = M \times N_{beam}$ |
| 2 | **Spectral Efficiency η** | $\eta_{FDMA} = \frac{N_c}{B_{total}}$ | $\eta_{TDMA} = \frac{N_t \cdot R_b}{B_{slot}}$ | $\eta_{CDMA} = \frac{R_b}{W} \cdot \frac{E_b}{N_0}$ | $\eta_{SDMA} = N_{ant} \cdot \eta_{cell}$ |
| 3 | **Capacity Bound (Shannon)** | $C = B \log_2\!\big(1 + \text{SNR}\big)$ | Same | $C = W \log_2\!\big(1 + \frac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{N}{W}\big)$ | $C = B \log_2 \det\!\big(\mathbf{I} + \text{SNR}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}^\dagger\big)$ |
| 4 | **Reuse Factor (N)** | $N = \frac{i^2 + ij + j^2}{3}$ | Same | $N = 1$ (Universal) | $N$ flexible, beam-based |
| 5 | **Guard Overhead** | Guard Bands $B_g$ | Guard Time $t_g$ | None (spread spectrum) | Beam isolation |
| 6 | **Sync Required** | Frequency | Slot / Frame | Code (PN) | Spatial |
| 7 | **Near-Far Problem** | Low | Low | **Severe** (need power control) | Low (beam nulling) |
| 8 | **Multipath** | Vulnerable | Vulnerable (use equalizer) | **Rake receiver helps** | Beam steering |
| 9 | **Required C/I** | $\geq 18$ dB | $\geq 12\text{–}14$ dB | **Negative dB** (self-interference limited) | Depends on beamwidth |
| 10 | **Hardware** | Filters | DSP / DSP | Correlator / Rake | Smart Antenna Array |
| 11 | **Power Control Rate** | Slow | Slow | **Fast (≥ 800 Hz)** | Medium |
| 12 | **Complexity (BTS)** | Low | Medium | **High** | High (Array DSP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Symbols Used in the Table:**
> - $M$ = total channels/codes available per cluster
> - $k$ = channels per cell in the cluster
> - $N_c$ = number of frequency channels
> - $W$ = spread spectrum bandwidth
> - $E_b/N_0$ = bit-energy-to-noise-spectral-density
> $\mathbf{H}$ = MIMO channel matrix
> - $N_{ant}$ = number of antenna elements
> - $N_{beam}$ = number of orthogonal beams

## 2.4 Why These Metrics Matter (Engineering Utility)

- **Spectral Efficiency (η)** determines the operator's revenue per MHz of purchased spectrum.
- **Guard Overhead** translates directly to **wasted spectrum** and lower ARPU.
- **Near-Far Problem** dictates whether tight (≥ 800 Hz) power control loops are required, which drain **mobile battery life**.
- **Reuse Factor $N=1$** in CDMA is what makes **soft handoff** possible, giving 3G its "no dropped calls" reputation.
- **Smart antennas in SDMA** form the basis of **Massive MIMO** in 5G NR, the single most important technology in modern wireless.

## 2.5 Qualitative Comparison Narrative

> **FDMA** is the simplest but suffers from **inflexible bandwidth allocation** and **inflexible guard bands**.
> **TDMA** improved spectral efficiency by 3× over FDMA but required **precise synchronization**, leading to GSM's tight timing advance loops.
> **CDMA** traded spectrum for *processing gain* and achieved **universal frequency reuse** at the cost of a strict **power control problem**.
> **SDMA** is the modern frontier — it requires no extra spectrum or time, but exploits **spatial orthogonality** via beamforming.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the full mathematical derivations of the comparison parameters for each multiple access scheme.

## 3.1 Derivation: Total System Bandwidth in FDMA

In FDMA, the total bandwidth $B_s$ available to a cell is divided into $N$ channels, each of bandwidth $B_c$, separated by a guard band $B_g$.

**Step 1** — Write the total bandwidth expression:

$$
B_s = N \cdot B_c + (N - 1) \cdot B_g
$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the per-user channel bandwidth (neglecting the $-1$ for large $N$, KTU standard approximation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_s &\approx N \cdot B_c + N \cdot B_g \\
\frac{B_s}{N} &= B_c + B_g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Therefore the per-user **effective** information bandwidth is:

$$
\boxed{B_{eff} = B_c = \frac{B_s}{N} - B_g}
$$

> **Logic:** Each user is allocated a *narrow* sub-channel. The wider the guard band $B_g$ is to prevent inter-channel interference, the *less* information bandwidth each user has.

## 3.2 Derivation: TDMA Frame Structure & Efficiency

In TDMA, $N$ users share one wideband carrier. A frame of duration $T_f$ contains $N$ slots, each of duration $t_s$, with a guard time $t_g$ between slots.

**Step 1** — Frame equation:

$$
T_f = N \cdot t_s + (N - 1) \cdot t_g + T_{pre}
$$

**Step 2** — where $T_{pre}$ is the preamble (training sequence) required for equalization.

**Step 3** — Frame efficiency is the ratio of *useful payload time* to *total frame time*:

$$
\eta_{frame} = \frac{N \cdot t_s}{T_f}
$$

**Step 4** — Assuming $N \gg 1$ and the preamble is negligible:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{frame} \approx \frac{1}{1 + \frac{t_g}{t_s}}}
$$

> **Logic:** If the guard time $t_g$ is 5 % of the slot time $t_s$, the frame efficiency is only $1/1.05 = 95.2\%$. This is the *spectral overhead* KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Derivation: CDMA Processing Gain & Capacity

In a Direct Sequence CDMA system, each user's data bit of duration $T_b$ is multiplied by a chip sequence of duration $T_c$, where $T_c \ll T_b$.

**Step 1** — Define the **Processing Gain** $G_p$:

$$
G_p = \frac{T_b}{T_c} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{W}{R_b}
$$

Where $W$ is the spread bandwidth and $R_c$ is the chip rate.

**Step 2** — The **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)** at the base station receiver is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SIR} &= \frac{1}{(N-1)} \cdot \frac{G_p}{1} \\
&\approx \frac{G_p}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The signal of the desired user is spread by $G_p$, but interference from $N-1$ other users is *not* despread, so its effective energy is reduced by $G_p$.

**Step 3** — The **bit-energy-to-noise-density ratio** is:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \text{SIR} \cdot G_p = \frac{G_p^2}{N}
$$

**Step 4** — Solving for the **pole capacity** (number of users supported in a single cell, with $N_0 = 0$, ideal):

$$
\boxed{N_{max} = 1 + \frac{G_p}{\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req}}}
$$

**Step 5** — Example: GSM voice uses $G_p = W/R_b = 1.25\,\text{MHz} / 9.6\,\text{kbps} = 130$. With $(E_b/N_0)_{req} = 7$ (linear, ≈ 8.45 dB):

$$
N_{max} = 1 + \frac{130}{7} \approx 19 \text{ users per cell}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world (IS-95) capacity** is lower because of **voice activity factor (≈ 0.45)**, **sectoring (gain 2.5)**, and **inter-cell interference (factor 1.6)**:
> $$N_{real} \approx \frac{N_{max} \times \text{Sectoring Gain}}{\text{Voice Activity}} \times \frac{1}{1+\text{Interference Factor}} \approx \frac{19 \times 2.5}{0.45 \times 2.6} \approx 40 \text{ users/cell}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: SDMA Beamforming Gain

In SDMA, an antenna array of $M$ elements is used to form $M$ simultaneous beams.

**Step 1** — The array factor for a Uniform Linear Array (ULA) is:

$$
AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w_n \, e^{j \, n \, k \, d \, \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 2** — For a linear-phase beamformer steering to angle $\theta_0$, the weight is $w_n = e^{-j \, n \, k \, d \, \sin\theta_0}$, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF(\theta) &= \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} e^{j \, n \, k \, d \, (\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0)} \\
&= \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{M \pi d}{\lambda}(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0)\right)}{\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi d}{\lambda}(\sin\theta - \sin\theta_0)\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — The **3 dB beamwidth** is approximately:

$$
\boxed{\Delta\theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{0.886 \, \lambda}{M \, d} \text{ radians}}
$$

**Step 4** — Array gain (SIR improvement) is:

$$
G_{array} = M
$$

> **Logic:** An 8-element array narrows the beam to ~13° and gives an 8× (= 9 dB) array gain. This is the **physical foundation of Massive MIMO** in 5G.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: The Master Comparison

| Property | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | SDMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain Partitioned** | Frequency | Time | Code | Space |
| **Data Rate per User** | Low (narrowband) | High during slot | Low to Medium | Same as underlying scheme |
| **Handoff** | Hard | Hard | **Soft** | Hard / Soft |
| **Privacy / Security** | Low | Low | **High** (code required) | Medium |
| **Battery Life** | Good | Good (sleep slots) | Poor (continuous power control) | Good |
| **Standard** | AMPS, ETACS | GSM, IS-54 | IS-95, WCDMA, CDMA2000 | 4G LTE, 5G NR |
| **Spectral Efficiency (η)** | ~0.2 b/s/Hz | ~0.5 b/s/Hz | ~0.8 b/s/Hz | > 5 b/s/Hz (with MIMO) |
| **Doppler Sensitivity** | Low | High | Medium | Medium |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Time-Frequency-Code-Space Resource Cube

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **4D resource space** of a wireless channel and how each Multiple Access strategy slices it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Total Radio Resource Cube: B x T x C x S] --> nodeB[FDMA: Slice along Frequency Axis B]
    nodeA --> nodeC[TDMA: Slice along Time Axis T]
    nodeA --> nodeD[CDMA: Slice along Code Axis C]
    nodeA --> nodeE[SDMA: Slice along Space Axis S]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Each user gets one B-sub-band continuously]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Each user gets one T-slot periodically]
    nodeD --> nodeH[All users share full B and T using unique codes]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Users in different angular directions are isolated]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[1G: AMPS]
    nodeG --> nodeK[2G: GSM]
    nodeH --> nodeL[3G: IS-95, WCDMA]
    nodeI --> nodeM[4G/5G: LTE, NR with MIMO]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hybrid 4G/5G Transceiver

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[User Data 1] --> en1[Channel Encoder]
    src2[User Data 2] --> en2[Channel Encoder]
    src3[User Data N] --> enN[Channel Encoder]
    en1 --> subc1[Sub-carrier Map]
    en2 --> subc2[Sub-carrier Map]
    enN --> subcN[Sub-carrier Map]
    subc1 --> mimo1[Precoder: SDMA]
    subc2 --> mimo1
    subcN --> mimo1
    mimo1 --> anta[Antenna 1]
    mimo1 --> antb[Antenna 2]
    mimo1 --> antc[Antenna M]
    anta --> chf[Multipath Fading Channel]
    antb --> chf
    antc --> chf
    chf --> rxa[Antenna Array at BS]
    rxa --> mmses[MMSE / MRC Combiner]
    mmses --> demap[Sub-carrier De-map]
    demap --> dec1[Decoder 1]
    demap --> dec2[Decoder 2]
    demap --> decN[Decoder N]
    dec1 --> outsink1[User 1 Data]
    dec2 --> outsink2[User 2 Data]
    decN --> outsinkN[User N Data]
```

> **Block interpretation:** This is a simplified **OFDMA + SDMA** architecture. The 4D resource allocator (OFDM) performs **TDMA within each sub-carrier, FDMA across sub-carriers**, while the **MIMO precoder** performs **SDMA in space**. The 4G/5G layer maps code-domain multiple access (CDMA) on top using scrambling.

## 4.3 Capacity vs. Complexity Trade-off Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX[Increasing User Capacity] --> op1[FDMA: Linear in Spectrum, Low Complexity]
    axisX --> op2[TDMA: Linear in Time, Medium Complexity]
    axisX --> op3[CDMA: Capacity limited by Noise Rise, High Complexity]
    axisX --> op4[SDMA: Capacity grows with Antennas, Very High Complexity]
    op1 --> note1[Limitation: Guard Band Overhead]
    op2 --> note2[Limitation: Synchronization Overhead]
    op3 --> note3[Limitation: Near-Far Power Control]
    op4 --> note4[Limitation: Channel State Information Accuracy]
    note1 --> final[Choice Depends on System Constraints]
    note2 --> final
    note3 --> final
    note4 --> final
```

## 4.4 Near-Far Problem Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    closeMS[Mobile A at 100 m] --> bts[Base Station]
    farMS[Mobile B at 10 km] --> bts
    closeMS -. Signal Strong 10000x .-> bts
    farMS -. Signal Weak 1x .-> bts
    bts --> problem[Near Far Problem in CDMA]
    problem --> sol1[Power Control at 800 Hz]
    problem --> sol2[Multiuser Detection MUD]
    sol1 --> bts
    sol2 --> bts
```

> **Explanation:** In CDMA, all users share the same frequency at the same time. If Mobile A is 100× closer than Mobile B, A's signal at the BTS is $10,000$× stronger. Without power control, A *jams* B. This problem **does not exist in FDMA/TDMA** (orthogonal) and is **greatly reduced in SDMA** (beam nulling).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Multiple Access and list the four classical multiple access strategies used in wireless cellular systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multiple Access is the technique used to allow a large number of subscribers to share the finite allocated radio spectrum so that maximum capacity is achieved with acceptable Quality of Service.

The four classical multiple access strategies are:
1. **FDMA — Frequency Division Multiple Access:** The available bandwidth is divided into non-overlapping frequency channels; each user is permanently allocated one sub-band.
2. **TDMA — Time Division Multiple Access:** Each user is allocated a periodic time slot on a common wideband carrier.
3. **CDMA — Code Division Multiple Access:** All users share the full bandwidth and full time, but are separated by unique orthogonal/quasi-orthogonal spreading codes.
4. **SDMA — Space Division Multiple Access:** Users are separated by their spatial location using directional/smart antenna beams.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing names + 1 mark for brief description of each.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the near-far problem? In which multiple access strategy is it most severe and how is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **near-far problem** is the phenomenon in which a mobile station close to the base station (BTS) generates a much stronger signal than a far mobile, drowning out the weaker signal at the receiver.

- **Most severe in CDMA** (1 mark): because all users transmit on the same frequency and same time, so the strong user directly interferes with the weak user's signal at the correlator output.
- **Mitigations** (1 mark each, any two): open-loop and closed-loop **fast power control** (800 Hz update in IS-95, 1500 Hz in WCDMA), **multiuser detection (MUD)**, and **sectored antennas**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question in Module 4 contains 2 sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Choice A

#### Question A(a). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA with respect to: (i) bandwidth efficiency, (ii) synchronization requirement, (iii) near-far problem, (iv) guard band/time overhead, (v) handoff mechanism, (vi) power control, and (vii) cell capacity formula. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Bandwidth Efficiency** | Lowest (guard bands waste spectrum) | Higher (no guard band) | Highest (no guard band/time) |
| **(ii) Synchronization** | Frequency accuracy only | Strict slot & frame timing | Code synchronization (PN sequence) |
| **(iii) Near-Far Problem** | Negligible | Negligible | **Most severe** — needs 800 Hz power control |
| **(iv) Overhead** | Guard bands $B_g$ between channels | Guard time $t_g$ between slots; training sequence | Pilot channel overhead; chip rate overhead |
| **(v) Handoff** | Hard handoff (break before make) | Hard handoff | **Soft handoff** (make before break) |
| **(vi) Power Control** | Slow; not critical | Slow; not critical | **Fast and strict** |
| **(vii) Capacity Formula** | $C = M/k$ (channels) | $C = M/k$ (time slots) | $C = 1 + G_p / (E_b/N_0)_{req}$ |

**Final cell capacity comparison** (1 mark):
FDMA and TDMA are **interference limited** with $N = 7$ reuse typical; CDMA is **noise-limited** with $N = 1$ universal reuse.

#### Question A(b). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**A CDMA cellular system uses a spread bandwidth $W = 1.25$ MHz, voice data rate $R_b = 9.6$ kbps, and a required $E_b/N_0 = 7$ (linear). Calculate (i) processing gain, (ii) pole capacity, (iii) practical capacity with voice activity factor of 0.45, sectoring gain of 2.5, and inter-cell interference factor of 1.6. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Processing Gain** (2 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p &= \frac{W}{R_b} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} \\
    &= 130.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pole Capacity** (2 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{max} &= 1 + \frac{G_p}{(E_b/N_0)_{req}} = 1 + \frac{130.2}{7} \\
        &= 19.6 \approx 19 \text{ users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Practical Capacity** (3 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{practical} &= \frac{N_{max} \times G_{sector}}{V \times (1 + f)} \\
              &= \frac{19.6 \times 2.5}{0.45 \times (1 + 1.6)} \\
              &= \frac{49.0}{0.45 \times 2.6} \\
              &= \frac{49.0}{1.17} \\
              &= 41.9 \approx 42 \text{ users per cell}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $G_{sector} = 2.5$ is the sectoring gain (3-sector antenna).
- $V = 0.45$ is the voice activity factor.
- $(1 + f) = 2.6$ accounts for inter-cell interference ($f = 0.6$).
- **Final Answer: ≈ 42 users per cell.**

---

### Choice B (Alternative to Choice A)

#### Question B(a). `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Explain the operational principle of SDMA. How does beamforming with a smart antenna array improve spectral efficiency? Derive the array gain for an $M$-element Uniform Linear Array. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Operational Principle of SDMA** (2 marks):
SDMA exploits the *spatial* dimension. By using an antenna array, the base station can form $M$ narrow, directional beams that point to different users simultaneously, even on the *same frequency, same time, same code*. Users separated in angle are orthogonal to each other.

**Beamforming & Spectral Efficiency** (2 marks):
The beamformer applies complex weights $w_n$ to each antenna element:

$$
y(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w_n^* \, x_n(t)
$$

By adjusting $w_n$, the beam is steered. The narrower the beam, the *higher* the antenna gain and the *lower* the co-channel interference, allowing more aggressive frequency reuse.

**Array Gain Derivation** (3 marks):
For a signal arriving at angle $\theta_0$, with weights $w_n = e^{-j n k d \sin\theta_0}$ (matched filter), the array output power is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} e^{-j n k d \sin\theta_0} \cdot e^{j n k d \sin\theta_0} \right\vert^2 \cdot P_{in} \\
        &= \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} 1 \right\vert^2 \cdot P_{in} \\
        &= M^2 \cdot P_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

The noise is uncorrelated across the $M$ branches, so noise power is only $M \cdot \sigma^2$. Therefore **output SNR = $M$ × input SNR**:

$$
\boxed{G_{array} = M}
$$

For $M = 64$ (5G Massive MIMO), the array gain is $10 \log_{10}(64) = 18$ dB, allowing $N = 1$ frequency reuse across cells.

#### Question B(b). `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**A TDMA system has a frame of $40$ ms duration and supports 8 users. If each time slot has a guard time of $30\,\mu$s and a preamble of $2$ ms, calculate (i) the slot duration, (ii) the frame efficiency, and (iii) the effective per-user information rate if the data modulation rate is $271$ kbps. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Slot Duration** (2 marks):
Frame equation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= N \cdot t_s + (N-1) \cdot t_g + T_{pre} \\
40 \times 10^{-3} &= 8 \cdot t_s + 7 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-3} \\
40 \times 10^{-3} - 0.21 \times 10^{-3} - 2 \times 10^{-3} &= 8 \cdot t_s \\
37.79 \times 10^{-3} &= 8 \cdot t_s \\
t_s &= 4.72 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Frame Efficiency** (2 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{frame} &= \frac{N \cdot t_s}{T_f} = \frac{8 \times 4.72 \text{ ms}}{40 \text{ ms}} \\
              &= \frac{37.76}{40} = 0.944 = 94.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Effective Per-User Information Rate** (3 marks):
Per-slot data bits = $R_b \times t_s = 271 \times 10^3 \times 4.72 \times 10^{-3} = 1279$ bits.
Per-frame useful bits per user = $1279$ bits (one slot per frame).
Bits per second = $1279 / 0.04 = 31{,}975$ bps.
Alternatively, using frame efficiency:

$$
\boxed{R_{eff} = 271 \text{ kbps} \times 0.944 = 255.8 \text{ kbps}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4 — Multiple Access):**
> 1. **Forgetting the guard-band term** in FDMA: many students write $B_s = N \cdot B_c$, but the correct form includes guard bands $B_g$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing reuse factor $N$** with cluster size: $N$ is the number of *cells* in a cluster, not the number of *channels*. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Writing $(E_b/N_0)$ in dB** in CDMA capacity formula: the formula $N_{max} = 1 + G_p/(E_b/N_0)$ requires *linear* $E_b/N_0$. Convert from dB by $10^{(dB/10)}$ first. **Lose 2 marks if wrong.**
> 4. **Forgetting voice activity factor** in practical CDMA: pole capacity is *academic*; examiners expect the practical formula. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Mixing up Processing Gain and Spreading Factor**: for IS-95, $G_p = W/R_b = 128$ (not 64). Always show the substitution.
> 6. **Not specifying "synchronous" vs "asynchronous"** in CDMA: IS-95 is *synchronous* (BS-to-MS), WCDMA is *asynchronous*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 — Comparisons of Multiple Access Strategies: Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Four Classical MA Strategies**: FDMA (frequency), TDMA (time), CDMA (code), SDMA (space). The 4D resource cube is $B \times T \times C \times S$.
- [x] **FDMA capacity**: limited by guard bands; **TDMA capacity**: limited by guard time + training overhead; **CDMA capacity**: $N = 1 + G_p/(E_b/N_0)$ (pole) and $N_{real} = N_{pole} \cdot G_{sec} / (V \cdot (1+f))$ (practical).
- [x] **Processing Gain** of CDMA: $G_p = W/R_b = R_c/R_b$.
- [x] **Near-Far Problem** is unique to CDMA; mitigated by **fast power control** (≥ 800 Hz) and **multiuser detection**.
- [x] **Reuse Factor** $N=1$ only in **CDMA** (universal frequency reuse) and **SDMA** (with beam isolation).
- [x] **Soft Handoff** in CDMA; **Hard Handoff** in FDMA/TDMA/SDMA.
- [x] **Spectral Efficiency** ranking: SDMA > CDMA > TDMA > FDMA.
- [x] **Frame Efficiency** of TDMA: $\eta = 1 / (1 + t_g/t_s)$.
- [x] **SDMA array gain** = $M$ (for $M$-element ULA with perfect beamforming); 3 dB beamwidth $\approx 0.886 \lambda / (M d)$ rad.
- [x] **1G = FDMA**, **2G = TDMA**, **3G = CDMA**, **4G/5G = OFDMA + SDMA (MIMO)**.
- [x] **Standard 2G GSM** uses $G_p \approx 130$ and $(E_b/N_0)_{req} \approx 7$ → pole capacity ≈ 19 users.
- [x] **Narrowband vs Wideband**: FDMA = narrowband, TDMA/CDMA = wideband (spread).
- [x] **Doppler / Multipath**: CDMA uses a **Rake receiver** to combine multipath echoes; TDMA needs an **equalizer**; FDMA is most vulnerable to selective fading.
- [x] **Voice Activity Factor** $V \approx 0.45$ — assumes 45 % of users are actually talking; this **doubles** practical CDMA capacity.
- [x] **Sectoring Gain** $G_{sec} = 2.5$ for 120° sectors; multiplies CDMA capacity.
- [x] **Hardcoded KTU values to memorize**: $W = 1.25$ MHz, $R_b = 9.6$ kbps (IS-95), $G_p = 130.2$, $V = 0.45$, sector gain = 2.5.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always answer the *comparison* question with a **table + narrative**. Tables are worth 4–5 marks and the narrative 2–3 marks. The numerical question almost always tests **CDMA pole capacity** or **TDMA frame efficiency**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TDMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TDMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)**
> TDMA is a **multiple access technique** in which a single carrier frequency (or a narrow frequency band) is shared among multiple users by allocating **disjoint, non-overlapping time slots** to each user within a periodic structure called a **TDMA frame**. Each user transmits (uplink) and receives (downlink) only during its designated time slot, allowing **N** users to coexist on the same RF channel using a single frequency or a pair of frequencies (FDD) or a single frequency (TDD).

In KTU 2024 terminology aligned with the standard text (Rappaport's *Wireless Communications*), TDMA is classified as a **narrowband multiple access scheme** because the transmitted signal bandwidth (after modulation) is much smaller than the coherence bandwidth of the channel — typically the data rate is supported in a **channel bandwidth ≤ 30 kHz** in legacy systems, although modern broadband variants exist.

> [!NOTE] **Key Terminology You Must Memorise**
> - **TDMA Frame:** The fundamental repeating time unit that contains all the time slots.
> - **Time Slot (Burst):** The smallest unit of time allotted to a single user.
> - **Guard Period (Guard Time):** Idle time between successive slots to avoid overlap due to propagation delay uncertainty.
> - **Preamble / Training Sequence:** Known bit pattern at the start of a burst used for synchronization, equalization, and channel estimation.
> - **Uplink (Reverse Link) vs. Downlink (Forward Link):** Mobile-to-BS direction vs. BS-to-Mobile direction.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Round-Table Meeting"

Imagine a **round-table meeting** with one large table and one microphone. The chairman has fixed the rule:

> *“Each of the 8 speakers gets exactly 1 minute of microphone time, in strict rotation. The moment your minute ends, the mic instantly switches to the next speaker. Nobody else may interrupt.”*

In this analogy:
- The **microphone channel** = the single RF carrier frequency.
- The **8 speakers** = the 8 mobile users sharing that carrier.
- The **1-minute slot** = one TDMA time slot.
- The **full round (8 minutes)** = one TDMA frame.
- The **tiny pause** between speaker switches = the **guard time**, accounting for the fact that one speaker is far from the mic (delayed arrival) while another is close.

This is **exactly** how TDMA works: a base station acts as the chairman, allotting identical, repeating slices of time to many mobiles, all transmitting on the **same frequency** but at **different instants**.

### 1.3 Physical and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value / Standard | System Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Channel bandwidth | **30 kHz** | USDC / IS-54 |
| Users per carrier | **3** | USDC / IS-54 |
| Channel bandwidth | **200 kHz** | GSM |
| Users per carrier | **8** | GSM |
| Frame duration | **4.615 ms** | GSM |
| Time slot duration | **577 µs** | GSM |
| Modulation | GMSK (BT = 0.3) | GSM |

> [!TIP] **Why TDMA matters in 2024 KTU context**
> Almost every 2G cellular system (GSM, IS-54, PDC) and many private radio systems (DECT, TETRA) are built on TDMA. It is also the **spine** on which 3G/4G/5G evolved — these systems use a hybrid OFDMA/TDMA framework. Understanding pure TDMA is therefore a mandatory prerequisite for any 5G NR study.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

The TDMA process can be broken into seven logical operations, every one of which appears in KTU exam questions:

1. **Frequency Reuse Across Time:** All users in a cell share the **same carrier frequency**. This is the central difference from FDMA, which segregates users by frequency. (In practice, FDD systems use *two* carriers — one for uplink, one for downlink — but each carrier itself is TDMA-shared.)
2. **Frame Construction:** The total time axis is divided into periodic **TDMA frames** of duration $T_f$. Each frame contains $N$ time slots of nominal duration $T_s$.
3. **Slot Allocation:** A central scheduler (the base station) assigns slot $k$ to user $k$ for every frame. A user transmits only during its assigned slot and is **idle** (can power-down its RF chain) otherwise.
4. **Burst Formation:** Within each slot, the user's data is packaged as a **burst** containing:
   - **Preamble (training/known sequence)** — for synchronization, equalizer training, and channel estimation.
   - **Information bits (payload)** — the actual user data.
   - **Trail / tail bits** — for graceful ramp-down.
   - **Guard time** — a small idle window preventing slot-to-slot collision.
5. **Uplink–Downlink Separation:** Two methods are used in real systems:
   - **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD):** Uplink and downlink use *two separate* carrier bands; each is independently TDMA-shared. (e.g., GSM, IS-54)
   - **Time Division Duplex (TDD):** A *single* carrier is used; half the slots are downlink, half are uplink. (e.g., DECT)
6. **Re-synchronization:** Because users are at varying distances, the base station transmits a **beacon/broadcast control channel** to which every mobile times its slot transmission. This is called *timing advance* in GSM.
7. **Re-use Across Cells:** Different cells use different carrier *frequency sets*, but within a cell, the same carrier serves many users via TDMA. This is identical in concept to FDMA's frequency planning.

### 2.2 Why TDMA? — Engineering Justification

- **Bandwidth Efficiency vs FDMA:** TDMA can use a wider channel bandwidth per user, supporting higher per-user data rates while sharing the same total spectrum pool. Example: GSM gives each user **25 kHz × 8 = 200 kHz of effective bandwidth** within a single 200 kHz carrier — impossible in pure FDMA.
- **No Intermodulation Products:** Since only one user transmits at a time on a given carrier, there are no intermodulation (IM) spurs from nonlinear PA operation — a major headache in FDMA.
- **Dynamic Capacity Allocation:** Slots can be reassigned per frame, supporting **variable-rate** services (e.g., GSM's half-rate vs full-rate codec).
- **Easier Handoff:** A mobile can be re-timed into a neighbouring cell's slot structure with a one-bit change in offset.
- **Battery Savings:** The mobile's RF transmitter is ON for only ~1/8 of the frame in GSM — improving talk-time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield TDMA Equations — Memorise Every One**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ | Frame duration = (number of slots) × (slot duration) | seconds |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{T_f}{T_s}$ | Number of time slots per TDMA frame | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_c}$ | Number of carriers (FDMA layer) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N_{\text{users}} = N_{\text{carriers}} \times N_{\text{slots/carrier}}$ | Total users supported per cell | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_b = \dfrac{b}{T_s}$ | Bit rate per user (b = bits per slot) | bps |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot b_{\text{info}}}{N \cdot b_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | TDMA frame efficiency (information bits ÷ total bits) | % |
| 7 | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{N \cdot t_{\text{overhead}}}{T_f}\right) \times 100\%$ | Efficiency expressed via overhead time | % |
| 8 | $t_{\text{guard}} \geq 2 \cdot \dfrac{R}{c}$ | Minimum guard time (R = max cell radius, c = speed of light) | seconds |
| 9 | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{\text{guard}}}{2}$ | Maximum cell radius supported by a given guard time | meters |
| 10 | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon capacity (per-user or per-slot) | bps |
| 11 | $C_{\text{TDMA}} = N \cdot C_{\text{slot}}$ | Total TDMA channel capacity (N independent slots) | bps |

> [!WARNING] **Critical Notation Rule for KTU 2024**
> In efficiency formulas, **bits per slot = information bits + preamble bits + trail bits + guard bits**. Do NOT confuse $b_{\text{info}}$ (payload only) with $b_{\text{total}}$ (full burst).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

TDMA is the **architectural backbone** of:
- **GSM** (Global System for Mobile) — 8 slots/frame, GMSK modulation.
- **IS-54 / USDC** (US Digital Cellular) — 3 slots/frame, π/4 DQPSK.
- **PDC** (Personal Digital Cellular, Japan) — 3 slots/frame.
- **DECT** (Digital Enhanced Cordless Telecommunications) — 24 slots/frame, TDD.
- **TETRA** (Terrestrial Trunked Radio) — 4 slots/frame, π/4 DQPSK.
- **Low-Earth-Orbit (LEO) satellite** systems (e.g., early IRIDIUM) — TDMA overlay.
- **Underwater acoustic networks** — where propagation delay makes CDMA impractical.

In every one of these systems, the **frame efficiency equation** is the key performance metric that determines how much real user data can be carried per frame — and that is exactly what KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: TDMA Frame Efficiency $\eta$

**Statement to prove:**

$$
\eta = \left(1 - \frac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_f}\right) \times 100\%
$$

where $T_{\text{overhead}}$ is the *total* time per frame consumed by preambles, trails, and guard bands across all slots.

**Step 1 — Define the frame.**
A TDMA frame of duration $T_f$ contains $N$ time slots. Each slot has a nominal duration $T_s$ such that:

$$
T_f = N \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose a single slot.**
Each slot is partitioned into:

$$
T_s = t_{\text{preamble}} + t_{\text{info}} + t_{\text{trail}} + t_{\text{guard}}
$$

The "useful" part of a slot is the information-carrying duration $t_{\text{info}}$. Everything else is overhead.

**Step 3 — Compute total useful time per frame.**

$$
T_{\text{useful}} = N \cdot t_{\text{info}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total overhead per frame.**

$$
T_{\text{overhead}} = N \cdot (t_{\text{preamble}} + t_{\text{trail}} + t_{\text{guard}})
$$

**Step 5 — Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_f} = \frac{N \cdot t_{\text{info}}}{T_f}
$$

Substitute $T_f = N \cdot T_s$:

$$
\eta = \frac{N \cdot t_{\text{info}}}{N \cdot T_s} = \frac{t_{\text{info}}}{T_s}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of total overhead.**

Since $T_f = T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{overhead}}$:

$$
\eta = \frac{T_f - T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_f} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_f}
$$

In percentage form:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \left(1 - \frac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_f}\right) \times 100\%}
$$

This is the **canonical TDMA efficiency formula** that appears verbatim in KTU question papers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Guard Time for a Cell of Radius $R$

**Statement to prove:**

$$
t_{\text{guard}} \geq \frac{2R}{c}
$$

**Step 1 — Worst-case scenario.**
Consider a user at the **cell edge**, distance $R$ from the base station. Its signal takes the **maximum** propagation delay to arrive at the BS:

$$
t_{\text{p,max}} = \frac{R}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Best-case scenario.**
A user at the base station's foot (distance ≈ 0) suffers **zero** delay:

$$
t_{\text{p,min}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — The differential delay.**
If the user-at-edge transmits just as a new slot begins, its signal arrives at the BS $\frac{R}{c}$ late. The *next* user (near the BS) starts transmitting immediately. To prevent the *previous* user's tail from colliding with the *next* user's preamble, the guard time must absorb the worst-case delay:

$$
t_{\text{guard}} = t_{\text{p,max}} - t_{\text{p,min}} = \frac{R}{c} - 0 = \frac{R}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for the round-trip nature.**
However, the BS is the central scheduler in **FDD/TDMA systems**. The BS uses *timing advance* to pre-compensate the uplink. The standard guard-time budget must still cover the **residual** timing uncertainty, which (without timing advance) is the full **round-trip** delay:

$$
t_{\text{guard,min}} = 2 \cdot \frac{R}{c}
$$

This is the KTU textbook expression.

**Step 5 — Solve for maximum cell radius.**

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot t_{\text{guard}}}{2}}
$$

For GSM: $t_{\text{guard}} = 30\ \mu s \Rightarrow R_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 4.5\ \text{km}$, consistent with GSM's historical cell radius of ~35 km (extended via timing advance).

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Typical 7-Mark Problem

**Problem (KTU style):**  
A TDMA system supports **8 users per carrier**, has a **frame duration** of $4.615\ \text{ms}$, and each **time slot** contains **156.25 bits** of which **40 bits** are overhead (preamble + trail + guard).  
Calculate:  
(a) The bit duration per user.  
(b) The total bit rate of the RF channel.  
(c) The frame efficiency.

**Solution (a) — Bit duration per user:**

The time-slot duration is:

$$
T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{4.615 \times 10^{-3}}{8} = 5.769 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 576.9\ \mu s
$$

This matches GSM's slot duration of 577 µs.

The bit duration is:

$$
T_b = \frac{T_s}{156.25} = \frac{5.769 \times 10^{-4}}{156.25} = 3.692 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 3.69\ \mu s
$$

Equivalently, the per-user bit rate is:

$$
R_b = \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{156.25}{5.769 \times 10^{-4}} = 270.83\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Solution (b) — Total bit rate of the RF channel:**

Total bits per frame = $8 \times 156.25 = 1250$ bits.

$$
R_{\text{total}} = \frac{1250}{4.615 \times 10^{-3}} = 270.83 \times 10^3\ \text{bps} = 270.83\ \text{kbps}
$$

Note that all 8 users collectively produce 270.83 kbps, and each gets $270.83 / 8 = 33.85$ kbps of *information* rate (after subtracting overhead bits).

**Solution (c) — Frame efficiency:**

Information bits per frame = $8 \times (156.25 - 40) = 8 \times 116.25 = 930$ bits.

$$
\eta = \frac{930}{1250} \times 100\% = 74.4\%
$$

> [!TIP] **Sanity check:** Pure GSM with 26-frame multiframe gives ~97% efficiency per single frame is too high — the *single-frame* efficiency in GSM is around 74%–78% as computed above, consistent with KTU reference solutions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Duplexing Methods within TDMA

| Property | TDD-TDMA | FDD-TDMA |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier count | **1** (alternating up/down) | **2** (separate up & down) |
| Hardware complexity | Single synthesizer, fast switch | Two synthesizers, duplexer filter |
| Latency symmetry | Slot-by-slot balance possible | Inherently symmetric (continuous) |
| Best for | Short-range, asymmetric traffic (DECT, TETRA) | Long-range, symmetric voice (GSM, IS-54) |
| Guard time | Critical (must cover round-trip) | Critical on uplink only |
| Examples | DECT, TETRA, half-duplex PTT | GSM, IS-54/136, PDC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TDMA Frame Structure — Burst Composition

The following block-level architecture shows how a single TDMA burst is structured, what each segment does, and how $N$ such bursts compose a complete frame.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TDMA FRAME  T_f = 4.615 ms"] --> B["SLOT 1  User 1"]
    A --> C["SLOT 2  User 2"]
    A --> D["SLOT 3  User 3"]
    A --> E["SLOT 4  User 4"]
    A --> F["SLOT 5  User 5"]
    A --> G["SLOT 6  User 6"]
    A --> H["SLOT 7  User 7"]
    A --> I["SLOT 8  User 8"]

    B --> B1["PREAMBLE  3 guard bits + 41 sync bits"]
    B --> B2["INFO BITS  58 coded + 26 training + 58 coded = 142 bits"]
    B --> B3["TRAIL BITS  3 bits"]
    B --> B4["GUARD TIME  8.25 bit periods"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The frame is sliced horizontally into 8 equal slots, each slot being a **burst** of 156.25 bit periods. Inside every burst, the structure is: **preamble (44 bits) → information (142 bits) → trail (3 bits) → guard (8.25 bits)**. This is the exact GSM normal burst layout.

---

### 4.2 Uplink / Downlink Timing in FDD-TDMA

In a Frequency Division Duplex system, two frequency bands exist — one for uplink, one for downlink. The base station transmits continuously on the downlink carrier while mobiles transmit in burst fashion on the uplink.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOWN["DOWNLINK CARRIER  BS to Mobile  F1"]
        D1["FRAME 1"]
        D2["FRAME 2"]
        D3["FRAME 3"]
    end

    subgraph UP["UPLINK CARRIER  Mobile to BS  F2"]
        U1["FRAME 1"]
        U2["FRAME 2"]
        U3["FRAME 3"]
    end

    MOBILE1["MOBILE 1"] -->|Slot 1| U1
    MOBILE2["MOBILE 2"] -->|Slot 2| U1
    MOBILE3["MOBILE 3"] -->|Slot 3| U1

    D1 -->|Slot 1| MOBILE1
    D2 -->|Slot 2| MOBILE2
    D3 -->|Slot 3| MOBILE3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The base station broadcasts a continuous TDMA frame on the downlink. Each mobile extracts only the slot addressed to it. On the uplink, all mobiles transmit on the *same* frequency, but in different time slots — coordinated by the base station's timing-advance commands.

---

### 4.3 TDD-TDMA Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FRAME["TDMA FRAME 12 slots total"] --> DL["DOWNLINK HALF  6 slots"]
    FRAME --> UL["UPLINK HALF  6 slots"]

    DL --> DL1["DL Slot 1  Broadcast"]
    DL --> DL2["DL Slot 2  User A downlink"]
    DL --> DL3["DL Slot 3  User B downlink"]
    DL --> DL4["DL Slot 4  User C downlink"]
    DL --> DL5["DL Slot 5  User D downlink"]
    DL --> DL6["DL Slot 6  Common control"]

    UL --> UL1["UL Slot 1  User A uplink"]
    UL --> UL2["UL Slot 2  User B uplink"]
    UL --> UL3["UL Slot 3  User C uplink"]
    UL --> UL4["UL Slot 4  User D uplink"]
    UL --> UL5["UL Slot 5  Random access"]
    UL --> UL6["UL Slot 6  Reserved"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** In a TDD-TDMA system, a *single* carrier is shared and the frame is split in time between transmit and receive. This is what DECT (24 slots, 12 + 12) and TETRA use. The advantage is that the split can be made *asymmetric* — e.g., more downlink slots for web browsing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA). List any **four** advantages of TDMA over FDMA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TDMA is a multiple access technique in which multiple users share a single carrier frequency by being allocated **distinct, non-overlapping time slots** within a recurring TDMA frame. Each user transmits only during its assigned slot.

**Four advantages over FDMA:**
1. **No intermodulation products** — only one user transmits per carrier at any instant, eliminating IM distortion in nonlinear PAs.
2. **Higher bandwidth efficiency** — a wider channel per user supports higher data rates and bursts.
3. **Flexible capacity allocation** — slots can be dynamically reassigned per frame to support variable-rate services.
4. **Easier handoff and synchronization** — slot retiming is a one-bit parameter change; no frequency retuning.
5. *(Bonus — can use)* Lower mobile battery consumption due to transmitter duty cycling.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the **purpose of the guard time** in a TDMA slot? Derive the relation between the maximum cell radius $R$ and the guard time $t_g$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **guard time** is an idle interval inserted at the end of each TDMA burst to prevent overlap between bursts from different users arriving with different propagation delays. It compensates for the worst-case differential delay.

Derivation: Worst-case delay difference between a user at cell edge ($t_{p,\max} = R/c$) and a user at the base station ($t_{p,\min} = 0$) is $R/c$. Including the round-trip uncertainty, the minimum required guard time is:

$$
t_g = \frac{2R}{c}
$$

Solving for the maximum cell radius:

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot t_g}{2}}
$$

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with internal choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TDMA frame and burst structure** used in the GSM system. Clearly label the preamble, information, trail, and guard regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a TDMA system, the total RF bandwidth is **25 MHz**, the channel bandwidth is **200 kHz**, the frame duration is **4.615 ms**, and each time slot contains **156.25 bits** with **40 bits of overhead**. Compute:
- (i) Number of carriers available.
- (ii) Number of users per carrier.
- (iii) Per-user information bit rate.
- (iv) Frame efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The GSM TDMA frame is a recurring structure of duration $T_f = 4.615$ ms containing $N = 8$ time slots, each of duration $T_s = 577\ \mu s$.

Each slot (a "burst") consists of 156.25 bit periods arranged as:

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Guard bits (tail) | 3 bits | Ramp-down / fade-out |
| Training sequence | 26 bits | Equalizer training |
| Information bits | 58 + 58 = 116 bits | User payload (two halves around training) |
| Stealing bits | 2 bits | Indicate FACCH hijack |
| Guard period | 8.25 bit periods | Prevent slot collision |

**Plus a 3-bit** tail/guard at the start → totaling 148 payload bits + 8.25 bit guard ≈ 156.25 bits.

(Draw the diagram with fields aligned and labelled; mark each transition with its bit count.)

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Number of carriers:**

$$
N_c = \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_c} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} = 125\ \text{carriers}
$$

**(ii) Number of users per carrier:**
GSM uses $N = 8$ time slots per carrier, so:

$$
N_{\text{users/carrier}} = 8
$$

**(iii) Per-user information bit rate:**
Time-slot duration:

$$
T_s = \frac{4.615 \times 10^{-3}}{8} = 5.769 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}
$$

Per-user bit rate (raw, including overhead):

$$
R_b = \frac{156.25}{5.769 \times 10^{-4}} = 270.83\ \text{kbps}
$$

Information-only bit rate (subtracting 40 overhead bits):

$$
R_{\text{info}} = \frac{156.25 - 40}{5.769 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{116.25}{5.769 \times 10^{-4}} = 201.5\ \text{kbps}
$$

**(iv) Frame efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{116.25}{156.25} \times 100\% = 74.4\%
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Supplementary]**

**(a)** Compare **TDD-TDMA** and **FDD-TDMA** duplexing schemes. State two real-world systems that use each. Derive the expression for **minimum guard time** required in a TDMA system for a cell of radius $R$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GSM-like TDMA system operates with a **30 kHz channel bandwidth** and a **frame efficiency of 75%**. If the system must support a total capacity of **120 users per cell** and the total available spectrum is **15 MHz**, calculate:
- (i) The number of carriers (FDMA layer).
- (ii) The number of TDMA slots per carrier.
- (iii) The per-user gross bit rate, given the symbol rate equals twice the channel bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | TDD-TDMA | FDD-TDMA |
|---|---|---|
| Carriers | 1 | 2 (separate up & down) |
| Duplex method | Time-based alternation | Frequency-based separation |
| Hardware | Simpler (single synthesizer) | Complex (duplexer + 2 synths) |
| Best for | Asymmetric, short range | Symmetric, long range |
| Examples | DECT, TETRA, half-duplex PTT | GSM, IS-54, PDC |

**Guard time derivation** (as in Section 3.2):
The worst-case propagation delay difference between a cell-edge user and a base-station-adjacent user is $R/c$. For full safety against round-trip uncertainty:

$$
t_{g,\min} = \frac{2R}{c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot t_g}{2}
$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Number of carriers (FDMA layer):**

$$
N_c = \frac{15 \times 10^6}{30 \times 10^3} = 500\ \text{carriers}
$$

**(ii) Number of TDMA slots per carrier:**

$$
N_s = \frac{N_{\text{users}}}{N_c} = \frac{120}{500} = 0.24
$$

Since $N_s$ must be an integer, this system design is **infeasible** as stated. The student should comment that the numbers need revision — for example, if the channel bandwidth were 200 kHz (as in GSM), then $N_c = 75$ and $N_s = 120/75 = 1.6$, still infeasible, indicating the requirement is for **a cluster of cells** or a different system parameter set.

*Examiner note: For the intended calculation, accept $N_s$ as the slot allocation and proceed.*

**(iii) Per-user gross bit rate:**
Symbol rate per carrier $= 2 \times B_c = 2 \times 30\ \text{kHz} = 60\ \text{ksps}$.
