# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OFDM Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SERIAL BITSTREAM] --> B[SERIAL TO PARALLEL CONVERTER]
    B --> C1[CONSTELLATION MAPPER 1]
    B --> C2[CONSTELLATION MAPPER 2]
    B --> C3[CONSTELLATION MAPPER 3]
    B --> CN[CONSTELLATION MAPPER N]
    C1 --> D[N POINT IFFT BLOCK]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    CN --> D
    D --> E[CYCLIC PREFIX INSERTION]
    E --> F[DIGITAL TO ANALOG CONVERTER]
    F --> G[RF UP CONVERSION AND POWER AMPLIFIER]
    G --> H[TIMING AND FREQUENCY SYNCHRONIZATION]
    H --> I[WIRELESS CHANNEL H OF TAU]
```

> **Interpretation:** Data flows from a single high-rate source, is split into $N$ parallel low-rate substreams, mapped to constellation points, summed via IFFT, padded with a cyclic prefix, and finally transmitted.

---

## 4.2 OFDM Receiver — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RECEIVED RF SIGNAL] --> B[RF DOWN CONVERSION AND LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER]
    B --> C[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    C --> D[TIMING AND FREQUENCY SYNCHRONIZATION]
    D --> E[CYCLIC PREFIX REMOVAL]
    E --> F[N POINT FFT BLOCK]
    F --> G1[CHANNEL ESTIMATION H K]
    F --> H1[ONE TAP EQUALIZER SUBCARRIER 1]
    F --> H2[ONE TAP EQUALIZER SUBCARRIER 2]
    F --> HN[ONE TAP EQUALIZER SUBCARRIER N]
    G1 --> H1
    G1 --> H2
    G1 --> HN
    H1 --> I1[CONSTELLATION DEMAPPER 1]
    H2 --> I2[CONSTELLATION DEMAPPER 2]
    HN --> IN[CONSTELLATION DEMAPPER N]
    I1 --> J[PARALLEL TO SERIAL CONVERTER]
    I2 --> J
    IN --> J
    J --> K[OUTPUT BITSTREAM]
```

> **Key Point:** The receiver is the structural mirror of the transmitter. Note the use of **$N$ parallel one-tap equalizers** instead of a single complex multi-tap time-domain equalizer.

---

## 4.3 OFDM Signal Generation Process — IFFT Perspective

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT SYMBOLS X0 X1 X2 UP TO X N-1] --> B[ASSIGN TO FREQUENCY BINS]
    B --> C[N POINT IFFT COMPUTATION]
    C --> D[TIME DOMAIN SAMPLES X 0 X 1 UP TO X N-1]
    D --> E[COPY LAST NG SAMPLES]
    E --> F[PREPEND AS CYCLIC PREFIX]
    F --> G[TIMESERIES XG 0 TO XG NG-1 THEN X 0 TO X N-1]
    G --> H[TRANSMIT THROUGH CHANNEL H OF TAU]
```

> **Conceptual Note:** The IFFT transforms the symbol from the *frequency domain* (one value per subcarrier) to the *time domain* (samples of the composite OFDM waveform). The cyclic prefix is the *last $N_g$ samples copied to the front* — this simple operation is what gives OFDM its multipath immunity.

---

## 4.4 Orthogonality in Frequency Domain — Sinc Subcarriers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SUB CARRIER 0 PEAK AT F0] --> B[SUB CARRIER 1 PEAK AT F1 NULL AT F0]
    B --> C[SUB CARRIER 2 PEAK AT F2 NULLS AT F0 AND F1]
    C --> D[SUB CARRIER 3 PEAK AT F3 NULLS AT F0 F1 F2]
    D --> E[OFDM SPECTRUM SUM OF ALL SINC SUB CHANNELS]
```

> **Why this matters:** Each subcarrier's spectrum is a sinc function. The peak of one subcarrier coincides with the *zeros* of all others, allowing **spectral overlap of 50%** without ICI. This is the geometric essence of OFDM orthogonality.

---

## 4.5 Cyclic Prefix — Time-Domain Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IFFT OUTPUT X0 X1 UP TO X N-1] --> B[TAKE LAST NG SAMPLES]
    B --> C[COPY TO FRONT OF BLOCK]
    C --> D[FINAL BLOCK X NG TO X N-1 X 0 X 1 UP TO X NG-1]
    D --> E[CHANNEL CONVOLUTION BECOMES CIRCULAR]
    E --> F[FFT AT RECEIVER DIAGONALIZES INTO H K TIMES X K]
```

> **Critical Takeaway:** Without the cyclic prefix, the linear convolution of the channel with each OFDM block would *spill* into adjacent symbols (ISI) and would *destroy* the subcarrier orthogonality (ICI). The CP is what makes OFDM practically realizable.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **Explain the concept of orthogonality among subcarriers in OFDM. Why is it important for spectral efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In OFDM, subcarriers are said to be **orthogonal** when their mutual inner product over the symbol duration $T_{sym}$ is zero:

$$\int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2 \pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j 2 \pi f_m t} \, dt = 0, \quad k \ne m$$

This is satisfied when the subcarrier spacing is $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$.

**Importance (for spectral efficiency):**
- The spectra of individual subcarriers (sinc-shaped) may **overlap by 50%** without causing interference.
- This allows OFDM to use the available bandwidth far more efficiently than classical FDM, which requires non-overlapping guard bands.
- Hence OFDM achieves near-ideal **spectral efficiency** approaching the Nyquist rate.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **What is a cyclic prefix in OFDM? Why is it added?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cyclic prefix (CP)** is a copy of the last $N_g$ samples of the OFDM symbol prepended to the transmitted block, making the total block duration $T_{total} = T_{sym} + T_g$.

**Reasons for adding the CP:**

1. **ISI elimination:** The CP acts as a guard interval. If $T_g \ge \tau_{max}$ (channel's maximum delay spread), the multipath echoes from the previous symbol cannot corrupt the current useful symbol.
2. **Circular convolution:** The CP converts the channel's *linear* convolution into a *circular* convolution, which is diagonalized by the FFT.
3. **One-tap equalization:** This enables trivial frequency-domain equalization — a single complex division per subcarrier.

**Drawback:** The CP carries **no new data**; it introduces a power and bandwidth overhead of $N_g / (N + N_g)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with internal choice. Both choices A and B are provided below.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Derive the mathematical expression for an OFDM signal with N subcarriers. Show that the discrete-time OFDM symbol can be generated using the IFFT operation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** An OFDM signal modulates $N$ parallel data symbols $\{X_0, X_1, \dots, X_{N-1}\}$ onto $N$ orthogonal subcarriers. The continuous-time complex baseband representation is:

$$s(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2 \pi f_k t}, \quad 0 \le t \le T_{sym}$$

**Step 2:** Set the lowest subcarrier frequency to zero (baseband) and define subcarrier spacing as $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$, giving $f_k = k/T_{sym}$:

$$s(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2 \pi k t / T_{sym}}$$

**Step 3:** Sample the signal at $t = n T_s = n T_{sym}/N$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2 \pi k n / N}$$

**Step 4:** Recognizing this as the standard $N$-point IFFT (up to a scaling constant), we conclude:

$$\boxed{x[n] = \text{IFFT}\{X_k\}}$$

The IFFT therefore implements multicarrier OFDM modulation, with computational complexity $O(N \log N)$ — the very reason OFDM is practical.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Explain the role of the cyclic prefix in OFDM. Derive the expression $Y_k = H_k X_k + W_k$ at the receiver, showing that equalization reduces to a one-tap operation per subcarrier.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The transmitted OFDM block with cyclic prefix (CP of length $N_g$) is:

$$\tilde{x}[n] = x[(n - N_g) \bmod N], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N + N_g - 1$$

**Step 2:** The received signal is the linear convolution with the channel impulse response $h[n]$ of length $L$:

$$\tilde{y}[n] = \sum_{\ell=0}^{L-1} h[\ell] \, \tilde{x}[n - \ell] + w[n]$$

**Step 3:** Discarding the first $N_g$ samples (CP) leaves $N$ samples $y[m]$, $m = 0, \dots, N-1$. Because the CP contains a copy of the tail, this operation produces a **circular convolution**:

$$y[m] = \sum_{\ell=0}^{L-1} h[\ell] \, x[(m - \ell) \bmod N] + w[m]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the $N$-point FFT. The DFT of a circular convolution equals the pointwise product of DFTs:

$$Y_k = H_k X_k + W_k$$

where $H_k = \sum_{\ell=0}^{L-1} h[\ell] e^{-j 2 \pi k \ell / N}$ and $W_k = \text{FFT}\{w[m]\}$.

**Step 5:** Equalization. With channel estimate $\hat{H}_k$, the receiver applies a single complex multiplication:

$$\hat{X}_k = \frac{Y_k}{\hat{H}_k} = X_k + \frac{W_k}{\hat{H}_k}$$

This **one-tap equalizer per subcarrier** replaces the complex time-domain equalizer of single-carrier systems — a major OFDM advantage.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **With a neat block diagram, explain the OFDM transmitter. Discuss the function of each block.**

**Model Solution:**

The OFDM transmitter consists of the following sequential blocks (see Section 4.1 diagram for the visual reference):

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Serial-to-Parallel Converter** | Splits high-rate input bits into $N$ low-rate parallel streams. Lengthens symbol duration. |
| **Constellation Mapper** | Maps each parallel stream's bits to complex symbols (BPSK/QPSK/QAM). Output: $\{X_0, X_1, \dots, X_{N-1}\}$. |
| **N-point IFFT** | Synthesizes the time-domain OFDM sample vector from the frequency-domain symbols. |
| **Cyclic Prefix Insertion** | Copies the last $N_g$ samples and prepends them, ensuring ISI-free transmission. |
| **D/A Converter** | Converts digital samples to analog waveform. |
| **RF Up-conversion & PA** | Mixes to carrier frequency and amplifies for transmission. |
| **Synchronization Block** | Adds pilot symbols, preambles for receiver timing/CFO estimation. |

The mathematical output of the IFFT block is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2 \pi k n / N}$$

The final transmitted block is $\tilde{x}[n] = [x[N - N_g], \dots, x[N-1], x[0], \dots, x[N-1]]$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **An OFDM system uses N = 64 subcarriers with 16-QAM modulation, subcarrier spacing of 15 kHz, and a cyclic prefix of length 16 samples. Compute (i) useful symbol duration, (ii) total symbol duration, (iii) bit rate, and (iv) bandwidth efficiency.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 64$, $M = 16$ (16-QAM $\Rightarrow \log_2 16 = 4$ bits/symbol), $\Delta f = 15$ kHz, $N_g = 16$.

**(i) Useful symbol duration:**
$$T_{sym} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{15{,}000} = 66.67 \text{ μs}$$

**Sampling period:**
$$T_s = \frac{T_{sym}}{N} = \frac{66.67 \text{ μs}}{64} = 1.042 \text{ μs}$$

**CP duration:**
$$T_g = N_g \cdot T_s = 16 \times 1.042 \text{ μs} = 16.67 \text{ μs}$$

**(ii) Total symbol duration:**
$$T_{total} = T_{sym} + T_g = 66.67 + 16.67 = 83.33 \text{ μs}$$

**(iii) Bit rate:**
$$R_b = \frac{N \cdot \log_2 M}{T_{total}} = \frac{64 \times 4}{83.33 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{256}{83.33 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.072 \text{ Mbps}$$

**(iv) Bandwidth efficiency:**
$$B = N \cdot \Delta f = 64 \times 15{,}000 = 960 \text{ kHz} = 0.96 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{3.072 \text{ Mbps}}{0.96 \text{ MHz}} = 3.2 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sym} = 66.67 \text{ μs}, \quad T_{total} = 83.33 \text{ μs}, \quad R_b = 3.072 \text{ Mbps}, \quad \eta = 3.2 \text{ bits/s/Hz}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in OFDM Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_{sym}$ with $T_{total}$:** Many students use $T_{total}$ in the numerator but forget that the **bit rate uses the *full* symbol period including CP**. Always divide by $T_{total} = T_{sym} + T_g$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{N}$ scaling factor in the IFFT:** Different textbooks use different conventions. State your convention explicitly at the start of the answer to avoid losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Cyclic prefix = "guard band" mistake:** The CP is a *time-domain* guard interval, not a *frequency-domain* guard band. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
>
> 4. **PAPR formula error:** PAPR (linear) = $N$, but PAPR (dB) = $10 \log_{10}(N)$. Don't write $N$ dB. Example: $N = 64 \Rightarrow 18$ dB, not 64 dB.
>
> 5. **Not stating the CP length condition:** Always mention that **$N_g \ge L$** (samples) or equivalently **$T_g \ge \tau_{max}$** to claim ISI elimination.
>
> 6. **Missing the "One-tap equalizer" punchline:** In any derivation of $Y_k = H_k X_k + W_k$, the examiner expects the final comment that *equalization reduces to a single complex division per subcarrier*. This single sentence is often worth 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Subcarrier spacing vs symbol duration mix-up:** $\Delta f \cdot T_{sym} = 1$. If you write $\Delta f = T_{sym}$, you will lose the entire problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — OFDM Module 4**

- [x] **OFDM** = Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing — a multicarrier modulation scheme.
- [x] **Core idea:** Split a high-rate stream into $N$ parallel low-rate streams over orthogonal subcarriers.
- [x] **Orthogonality condition:** $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$.
- [x] **Cyclic Prefix (CP):** Length $\ge \tau_{max}$; converts linear convolution to circular convolution.
- [x] **IFFT/FFT Implementation:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j 2 \pi k n / N}$.
- [x] **Frequency-domain received symbol:** $Y_k = H_k X_k + W_k$.
- [x] **One-tap equalizer:** $\hat{X}_k = Y_k / H_k$ — no complex multi-tap equalizer needed.
- [x] **PAPR:** $N$ (linear) or $10 \log_{10} N$ dB. Mitigation: clipping, SLM, PTS, DFT-spreading.
- [x] **Bit rate formula:** $R_b = N \log_2 M / T_{total}$ where $T_{total} = T_{sym} + T_g$.
- [x] **Spectral efficiency (no CP):** $\eta = \log_2 M$ bits/s/Hz. **With CP:** $\eta = \log_2 M \cdot N/(N+N_g)$.
- [x] **Advantages:** ISI mitigation, simple equalization, high spectral efficiency, robust to multipath, MIMO-friendly.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** High PAPR, sensitive to carrier frequency offset (CFO) and phase noise, strict synchronization needed.
- [x] **Applications:** Wi-Fi (802.11), 4G LTE, 5G NR, DVB, WiMAX, ADSL, DAB.
- [x] **5G NR numerology:** Subcarrier spacings 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz; FFT sizes up to 4096.
- [x] **Must-derive equations for KTU ESE:**
  1. $x[n] = \text{IFFT}\{X_k\}$ derivation.
  2. $Y_k = H_k X_k + W_k$ with CP justification.
  3. PAPR = $N$ derivation.
- [x] **Common numericals:** Bit rate, spectral efficiency, PAPR in dB, total block duration.
- [x] **Block diagrams to draw from memory:** Transmitter chain, Receiver chain, CP insertion, OFDM spectrum (sinc subcarriers).
- [x] **One-line definitions to memorize:**
  - OFDM = "multicarrier modulation with orthogonal overlapping sinc subcarriers"
  - Cyclic prefix = "guard interval that converts linear convolution into circular convolution"
  - Subcarrier orthogonality = "$\int_0^{T_{sym}} \phi_k(t) \phi_m^*(t) dt = 0$ for $k \ne m$"
  - OFDM's main advantage over single-carrier = "Frequency-selective channel becomes $N$ flat-fading channels"
- [x] **Pitfall:** Don't confuse "subcarrier spacing" $\Delta f$ with "carrier frequency" $f_c$.
- [x] **Pitfall:** PAPR is computed per OFDM symbol, not per subcarrier.
- [x] **Pitfall:** The CP overhead fraction is $T_g / T_{total} = N_g / (N + N_g)$, not $N_g / N$.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, 80% of OFDM marks come from **(a) deriving the IFFT formulation**, **(b) explaining the cyclic prefix with $Y_k = H_k X_k + W_k$**, and **(c) numerical bit-rate/spectral-efficiency problems**. Master these three and you are guaranteed to score full marks in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CSMA Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CSMA Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 CSMA Protocols — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)** is a probabilistic **Media Access Control (MAC)** protocol in which a node wishing to transmit first *listens* to (i.e., senses) the shared communication channel to determine whether another transmission is already in progress. Only if the channel is sensed **idle** (or, depending on the variant, *probably idle*) does the node begin transmission. This is a foundational improvement over the earlier **ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA** random access schemes because it drastically reduces the probability of collisions by exploiting the property that propagation delay is small compared to frame transmission time in many practical networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CSMA belongs to the family of **Contention-Based Random Access Protocols** (Module 4). The three canonical variants examined in KTU examinations are: **(i) 1-Persistent CSMA, (ii) Non-Persistent CSMA, and (iii) p-Persistent CSMA.** The two extended derivatives — **CSMA/CD** and **CSMA/CA** — are also high-yield topics, especially CSMA/CA in the context of wireless LANs (IEEE 802.11).

## 1.2 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Carrier Sense** | Listening for the presence of a signal (carrier) on the medium. |
| **Multiple Access** | Many independent stations share one common broadcast channel. |
| **Collision** | Overlap of two or more transmitted frames causing signal corruption. |
| **Propagation Delay ($T_p$)** | Time for a signal to traverse the physical medium from sender to receiver. |
| **Transmission Time ($T_t$)** | Time required to push an entire frame onto the medium. |
| **Vulnerable Period** | Window of time during which a collision can occur. |
| **Throughput (S)** | Fraction of channel capacity used for successful transmissions. |
| **Offered Load (G)** | Average number of transmission attempts per frame time. |

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Polite Conversation"

Imagine a group of friends standing in a hallway trying to talk. In a pure **ALOHA** world, each person simply starts talking whenever they feel like it, and collisions (people talking over each other) are constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Polite Conversation Model**
> - **Pure ALOHA** = Everyone talks at the same time, no listening. Total chaos.
> - **Slotted ALOHA** = Everyone agrees to only start talking at the *start* of fixed time slots. Still chaotic, but a bit more orderly.
> - **CSMA** = Before speaking, each person **listens** to the hallway. If they hear someone talking, they wait. Only when silence is detected do they begin.
> - **1-Persistent CSMA** = The eager person: as soon as silence appears, they immediately start talking (and if two people do this at the same moment, they collide).
> - **Non-Persistent CSMA** = The patient person: if the hallway is busy, they wait a *random* amount of time before checking again — reducing collisions but adding delay.
> - **p-Persistent CSMA** = The probabilistic person: with probability *p* they start talking in the next slot, and with probability *(1−p)* they wait one slot. Useful in slotted channels.

The physical intuition is simple: **listening before talking saves everyone a lot of trouble**, but it cannot eliminate collisions completely because of finite signal propagation speed.

## 1.4 Why CSMA Exists — The Mathematical Motivation

In ALOHA protocols, the **vulnerable period** is the time during which a collision can corrupt a frame. For a frame of transmission time $T_t$:

$$\text{Pure ALOHA Vulnerable Period} = 2 \cdot T_t$$

$$\text{Slotted ALOHA Vulnerable Period} = T_t$$

CSMA reduces this vulnerable period dramatically. In the ideal case where every station can instantaneously detect the carrier of any other station, the vulnerable period collapses to just the **propagation delay** $T_p$, because the moment a station starts transmitting, all other stations will detect this carrier after at most $T_p$ seconds and will refrain from transmitting.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** "CSMA trades *channel idle time* for *collision reduction*." The station may wait, but the *successful* throughput is far higher.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in CSMA

- **Speed of light in free space** ($c$) $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Speed of signal in copper coax** $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (≈ **2/3 of c**)
- **Speed of signal in optical fiber** $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Standard frame transmission unit:** 1 frame-time is normalized such that $T_t = 1$ for analysis
- **Typical 802.11 Slot Time:** **9 μs** (legacy DSSS) or **20 μs** (802.11n)
- **Ethernet (802.3) minimum frame size:** **64 bytes** (to ensure collision detection across maximum 2500 m segment)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ CSMA Protocols — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of CSMA Variants

CSMA protocols differ in *how* a station behaves when it senses the channel. The classification is shown below.

### 2.1.1 1-Persistent CSMA

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step):**
1. Station has a frame ready to transmit.
2. It senses the channel continuously.
3. If channel is **idle** → transmit **immediately** (with probability = 1).
4. If channel is **busy** → wait until the channel becomes idle, *then* transmit immediately.
5. If a collision occurs → wait a random backoff time, then re-attempt from step 1.

**Why "1-Persistent"?** The probability of transmitting the instant the channel is sensed idle is **1** (i.e., 100%).

**Advantages:** Low delay when traffic is light.

**Disadvantages:** If two or more stations are waiting for the channel to go idle, they will *all* transmit simultaneously when the channel becomes free, causing a **collision**. Performance degrades sharply under high load.

### 2.1.2 Non-Persistent CSMA

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step):**
1. Station has a frame ready to transmit.
2. It senses the channel.
3. If channel is **idle** → transmit immediately.
4. If channel is **busy** → do **not** continuously sense. Instead, wait a *random* amount of time, then re-sense the channel from step 2.

**Advantages:** Reduces the chance of collisions dramatically (no synchronized collision storm when channel becomes free).

**Disadvantages:** Introduces additional idle time because the station may be waiting randomly while the channel is actually idle → higher average delay.

### 2.1.3 p-Persistent CSMA (Slotted Channel)

This variant applies to **slotted** channels (e.g., systems divided into time slots, like Slotted ALOHA).

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step):**
1. Station has a frame ready to transmit.
2. It senses the channel at the **start of a slot**.
3. If channel is **idle** → transmit with probability **$p$**; defer to the next slot with probability **$(1-p)$**.
4. If channel is **busy** → wait until the start of the *next* slot, then re-apply step 3.
5. If a collision occurs → wait a random backoff, then re-attempt.

**Limiting Behavior:**
- When $p \to 1$ → behaves like **1-Persistent CSMA**.
- When $p \to 0$ → channel is grossly underutilized (too much waiting).
- Optimum **$p$** lies between 0 and 1, depending on offered load $G$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The p-Persistent protocol is *only* defined for slotted channels. If a question asks about p-Persistent CSMA in an unslotted context, the answer is "not defined / not applicable in classical form."

## 2.2 The Two Critical Derivatives: CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA

### 2.2.1 CSMA/CD — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection

**Domain:** Wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3).

**Mechanism:**
1. Sense the carrier; transmit only if idle.
2. **While transmitting, simultaneously monitor the medium.**
3. If a collision is detected (voltage anomaly on the cable), abort transmission immediately.
4. Send a **Jam Signal** to ensure all stations know about the collision.
5. Apply the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm: wait a random number of slot times chosen from $[0, 2^i - 1]$, where $i$ is the number of collisions experienced (capped at $i = 10$).
6. Re-attempt transmission.

**Requirement:** The minimum frame size must be long enough that transmission time $T_t \geq 2 \cdot T_p$ (round-trip propagation). This guarantees that a sending station is still transmitting when the collision signal returns. In standard Ethernet: minimum frame = 64 bytes; maximum segment = 2500 m → $2T_p \approx 51.2$ μs → exactly the time to send 64 bytes at 10 Mbps.

### 2.2.2 CSMA/CA — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance

**Domain:** Wireless LANs (IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi). Wireless nodes **cannot** detect collisions effectively because:
- The transmitted signal is ~100,000× stronger than any received signal at the same node → the antenna cannot hear others while talking.
- Hidden node problem.

**Mechanism (DCF — Distributed Coordination Function):**
1. Sense the channel; if idle for a **DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space)** period, transmit.
2. If channel is busy → wait until idle + DIFS, then start a **backoff counter** with a random value in $[0, CW]$ (Contention Window).
3. Decrement the counter for every idle slot; freeze it if a transmission is sensed.
4. When counter reaches 0, transmit.
5. Wait for **ACK** from receiver within a **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) window.
6. If no ACK → assume collision, double $CW$, and retry.

**Three additional defenses** against hidden nodes:
- **RTS/CTS Handshake:** Request-To-Send and Clear-To-Send frames reserve the channel.
- **NAV (Network Allocation Vector):** Virtual carrier-sense timer maintained in every node.
- **ACK:** Every unicast data frame must be acknowledged.

## 2.3 The Vulnerable Period in CSMA

In CSMA, the vulnerable period is the time during which, if another station starts transmitting, a collision is unavoidable. For non-persistent CSMA with perfect carrier sensing:

$$\text{Vulnerable Period}_{CSMA} = T_p$$

For 1-persistent CSMA, the effective vulnerable period is larger (up to $\approx T_p + T_t$ in worst-case synchronization).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "vulnerable period of CSMA = 0." It is **NOT zero** — it is the propagation delay $T_p$, because during the first $T_p$ seconds of a transmission, *other* stations may not yet have sensed the carrier.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` in formulas below for absolute value to avoid breaking the markdown table.**

| # | Formula | Description | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{1 + G}$ | Slotted Non-Persistent CSMA throughput | Slotted NP-CSMA |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | Unslotted Non-Persistent CSMA throughput | Unslotted NP-CSMA |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{G(1 + G) \cdot e^{-G}(1 + G)}{G + e^{-G} \cdot (1 + 2G)}$ | Slotted 1-Persistent CSMA throughput | Slotted 1-P-CSMA |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}(1 + G)}{1 + e^{-G}}$ | Unslotted 1-Persistent CSMA throughput | Unslotted 1-P-CSMA |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}(1 + p)}{(1 + p) + G \cdot e^{-G}}$ | Slotted p-Persistent CSMA (optimum) | Slotted p-CSMA |
| 6 | $V_{ALOHA} = 2T_t$ | Vulnerable period — Pure ALOHA | ALOHA |
| 7 | $V_{S-ALOHA} = T_t$ | Vulnerable period — Slotted ALOHA | S-ALOHA |
| 8 | $V_{CSMA} = T_p$ | Vulnerable period — CSMA (perfect sensing) | CSMA |
| 9 | $S_{max} \approx 0.538$ (at $G=1$) | Maximum Slotted ALOHA throughput | S-ALOHA |
| 10 | $S_{max} \approx 0.184$ (at $G=0.5$) | Maximum Pure ALOHA throughput | Pure ALOHA |
| 11 | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Normalized propagation delay (critical parameter) | All CSMA |
| 12 | $CW_{i+1} = 2 \cdot (CW_i + 1) - 1$ | Binary Exponential Backoff (802.11) | CSMA/CA |
| 13 | $T_{min\_frame} = 2 \cdot T_p$ | Minimum frame transmission time (CD requirement) | CSMA/CD |
| 14 | $G = S + \text{(collisions per unit time)}$ | Fundamental traffic identity | All |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ethernet (802.3):** Uses 1-Persistent CSMA/CD on legacy coax/twisted-pair; modern switched Ethernet operates in full-duplex (CSMA/CD effectively disabled).
- **Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax):** Uses CSMA/CA with DCF and RTS/CTS.
- **CAN Bus (Automotive):** Uses a CSMA/CD + bit-wise arbitration variant (CSMA/BA) — non-destructive.
- **Satellite Channels (VSAT):** Use Slotted ALOHA for low-traffic reservation systems.
- **IoT (LoRaWAN, NB-IoT):** Use ALOHA-based MAC due to extreme duty-cycle constraints.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice between ALOHA family and CSMA family depends on **propagation delay relative to frame time ($a = T_p/T_t$)**. For $a \ll 1$ (LANs, Wi-Fi), CSMA dominates. For $a \geq 1$ (satellite, deep-space), ALOHA is often preferred because carrier sensing becomes less useful when the round-trip delay is comparable to the frame duration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 CSMA Protocols — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Slotted Non-Persistent CSMA Throughput

We model the system as a discrete-time channel where each slot has duration equal to one frame transmission time $T_t = 1$ (normalized). A station can transmit only at the *beginning* of a slot. Let $G$ be the average number of attempted transmissions per slot (Poisson arrivals).

**Step 1 — Probability of zero attempts in a slot:**

$$P(\text{no arrival in slot}) = e^{-G}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of successful transmission:**

A transmission is successful if **exactly one** station attempts in the slot, AND no other station begins a transmission in the same slot (because in slotted non-persistent CSMA, stations check the channel at the start of the slot only).

$$P(\text{success}) = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of throughput:**

Throughput $S$ is the average number of successful transmissions per slot. Since $T_t = 1$, the maximum $S$ is 1.

$$S = P(\text{success}) = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is identical in form to Slotted ALOHA — but the channel model is different. In Slotted ALOHA, every station transmits blindly if it has a frame; in slotted NP-CSMA, stations first sense.

**Step 4 — Refined non-persistent formula (with carrier-sensing delay parameter $a$):**

When propagation delay $T_p = a \cdot T_t$ is non-negligible, the channel may not be sensed as busy immediately. A station's transmission can collide with another that started up to $a$ slots earlier. The refined throughput is:

$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$$

**Detailed Expansion of Denominator:**
- $G$ → arrivals in the *current* slot.
- $2aG$ → arrivals in the *previous and next* slots within the propagation window (these cannot yet be sensed and may collide).
- $e^{-aG}$ → probability that the slot prior to the one in question has no arrivals (so no collision from that side).

**Step 5 — Locate the maximum throughput (calculus):**

Take the derivative $\dfrac{dS}{dG} = 0$ and solve. For small $a$ (typical LAN), the maximum occurs near $G \approx 1$, and the peak throughput approaches:

$$S_{max} \to 1 \text{ as } a \to 0$$

> This shows that as propagation delay shrinks (faster mediums, shorter cables), CSMA's maximum throughput approaches the theoretical limit of 1 — a vast improvement over ALOHA's 0.368 (slotted) and 0.184 (pure).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unslotted 1-Persistent CSMA Throughput

In unslotted 1-Persistent CSMA, a station transmits the instant the channel becomes idle. Collisions occur when two or more stations sense the channel as idle simultaneously. Assume Poisson arrivals with mean rate $G$ per frame-time.

**Step 1 — Define the conditional states:**

Let $U$ = the event that the channel is idle at a given instant.
Let $B$ = the event that the channel is busy.

The throughput is the fraction of time the channel carries useful, uncorrupted data:

$$S = \frac{P(\text{one frame in vulnerable period}) \cdot P(\text{success}) \cdot 1 \text{ (frame-time)}}{E[\text{cycle length}]}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle structure:**

A cycle consists of:
- A *busy period* (one frame-time if successful, or one frame-time if collision — collision is detected after the full frame is sent in unslotted 1-P).
- Followed by an *idle period*.

**Step 3 — Standard result (Kleinrock, 1975):**

$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-G} \cdot (1 + G)}{1 + e^{-G}}$$

**Interpretation of each factor:**
- $G \cdot e^{-G}$ → probability of *exactly one* station attempting in the vulnerable period.
- $(1 + G)$ → adjustment for the period immediately *after* the start of the frame (other stations may still attempt before sensing the carrier).
- $1 / (1 + e^{-G})$ → expected channel utilization fraction.

**Step 4 — Asymptotic limit:**

As $G \to 0$ (very light load): $S \to G$ (the channel carries whatever is offered, almost no collisions).

As $G \to \infty$ (heavy load): $S \to \dfrac{G(1+G)}{1} \cdot 0 \to$ drops because of massive collision storms. The peak occurs at moderate $G \approx 1$, with $S_{max} \approx 0.529$ for unslotted 1-persistent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The 1-Persistent CSMA — Why the "1" Matters Mathematically

The **persistence** value is the conditional probability of transmission given an idle channel sensing. In 1-persistent:

$$P(\text{transmit} \mid \text{channel idle}) = 1$$

For non-persistent, this conditional probability becomes the geometric decay due to random waiting. The expected *additional* delay $E[D_{extra}]$ in non-persistent CSMA for sensing delay parameter $a$ is:

$$E[D_{extra}] = a \cdot T_t \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-G}}{G}$$

As $G \to 0$, $E[D_{extra}] \to a \cdot T_t$, which is the propagation delay — the station waits one round-trip's worth of time on average.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vulnerable Period of CSMA — Geometric Argument

Consider a frame of duration $T_t$ transmitted by Station A starting at $t = 0$. The last bit of A's frame reaches the most distant station at $t = T_p$ (worst case). If another station B starts transmitting anywhere in the interval $(-T_p, T_p)$ relative to A's start, a collision occurs.

> Length of overlap window = $2T_p$ for a *single* direction, but for *symmetric* collision detection, we need to consider the propagation both ways, giving an effective vulnerable window of $T_p$ on each side.

Hence, the total vulnerable period is $T_p$ (or expressed in frame-time units: $a = T_p / T_t$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Comparison Table:**

| Protocol | Vulnerable Period | $S_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Pure ALOHA | $2T_t$ | 0.184 |
| Slotted ALOHA | $T_t$ | 0.368 |
| Slotted NP-CSMA ($a \to 0$) | $\approx 0$ | $\to 1$ |
| Unslotted NP-CSMA ($a = 0.01$) | $\approx 0.01T_t$ | $\approx 0.97$ |
| CSMA/CD (Ethernet) | $T_p$ (detected quickly) | $> 0.9$ in practice |

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Minimum Frame Size for CSMA/CD (The Round-Trip Constraint)

**Given:** Cable length $L = 2500$ m, signal speed $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, data rate $R = 10$ Mbps.

**Step 1 — One-way propagation delay:**

$$T_p = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 12.5 \ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip propagation delay (worst case, two stations at far ends):**

$$T_{rt} = 2 \cdot T_p = 25 \ \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Minimum frame transmission time to detect collision:**

For the sender to detect a collision, it must still be transmitting when the collision signal returns. Therefore:

$$T_{t,min} \geq T_{rt} = 25 \ \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Minimum frame size in bits:**

$$N_{min} = R \cdot T_{t,min} = 10^7 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 250 \ \text{bits}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bytes:**

$$N_{min} = \frac{250}{8} = 31.25 \ \text{bytes}$$

**Step 6 — Why IEEE 802.3 specifies 64 bytes:**

To provide a safety margin (4× safety factor) for repeaters, transceivers, and signal rise times, IEEE 802.3 mandates a minimum frame size of **64 bytes = 512 bits**, giving an effective collision detection window of $T_{rt} = 51.2 \ \mu s$ at 10 Mbps.

> [!NOTE]
> **This 64-byte minimum is one of the most frequently asked KTU numerical problems** — typically framed as: "Compute the minimum frame size for an Ethernet network of length X meters at data rate Y bps."

## 3.6 Performance Comparison Summary Table (The KTU Comparison Matrix)

| Property | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | 1-P CSMA | NP-CSMA | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carrier Sense** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Slots Required** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Optional |
| **Collision Detection** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (avoidance only) |
| **Collision Avoidance** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Partial | ❌ | ✅ |
| **$S_{max}$** | 0.184 | 0.368 | 0.529 | →1 (small $a$) | High | ~0.5–0.7 |
| **Domain** | Legacy Sat. | Slotted sat. | LAN (old) | LAN | Ethernet | Wi-Fi |
| **Persistence** | N/A | N/A | 1 | 0 (random wait) | 1 | 1 (with backoff) |
| **Hidden Node Handling** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (RTS/CTS) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ CSMA Protocols — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — CSMA Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Random Access MAC Protocols] --> B0[ALOHA Family]
    A0 --> C0[CSMA Family]
    B0 --> B1[Pure ALOHA]
    B0 --> B2[Slotted ALOHA]
    C0 --> C1[1 Persistent CSMA]
    C0 --> C2[Non Persistent CSMA]
    C0 --> C3[p Persistent CSMA]
    C0 --> C4[CSMA CD]
    C0 --> C5[CSMA CA]
    C4 --> D1[Ethernet IEEE 802.3]
    C5 --> D2[Wi-Fi IEEE 802.11]
    C5 --> D3[DCF Mode]
    C5 --> D4[PCF Mode]
    style A0 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C0 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B0 fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — 1-Persistent CSMA Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Station has frame to send] --> S1{Sense Channel}
    S1 -- Busy --> S2[Wait until channel becomes IDLE]
    S2 --> S1
    S1 -- Idle --> S3[Transmit IMMEDIATELY probability equals 1]
    S3 --> S4{Collision Detected}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Transmission SUCCESS]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[Send Jam Signal in CD]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Binary Exponential Backoff]
    S7 --> S1
    style S0 fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000
    style S1 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#388e3c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Non-Persistent CSMA Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Station has frame to send] --> N1{Sense Channel}
    N1 -- Busy --> N2[Do NOT wait continuously]
    N2 --> N3[Wait RANDOM backoff time]
    N3 --> N1
    N1 -- Idle --> N4[Transmit immediately]
    N4 --> N5{Collision?}
    N5 -- Yes --> N6[Wait random backoff then retry]
    N5 -- No --> N7[Transmission SUCCESS]
    N6 --> N1
    style N0 fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000
    style N2 fill:#e64a19,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style N3 fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style N7 fill:#388e3c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — p-Persistent CSMA Slotted Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Frame ready at start of slot] --> P1{Slot begins<br/>Channel idle}
    P1 -- No --> P2[Wait for next slot]
    P2 --> P1
    P1 -- Yes --> P3[Generate random number r in 0 1]
    P3 --> P4{r less than p}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Transmit in this slot]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Defer to next slot]
    P6 --> P1
    P5 --> P7{Collision?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Random backoff and restart]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Success]
    style P0 fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000
    style P4 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P9 fill:#388e3c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — CSMA/CA IEEE 802.11 DCF Timing Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender STA
    participant Channel as Wireless Channel
    participant Receiver as Receiver STA
    participant Others as Other STAs

    Note over Channel: Channel is BUSY due to other transmission
    Sender->>Sender: Sense channel and defer
    Note over Channel: Channel becomes IDLE
    Note over Sender: Wait DIFS period 34 microseconds
    Sender->>Sender: Pick random backoff counter in 0 to CW
    loop While counter greater than 0
        Sender->>Sender: Decrement counter per idle slot
        Channel-->>Sender: If channel becomes busy FREEZE counter
    end
    Note over Sender: Counter reached zero
    Sender->>Channel: Transmit RTS frame
    Channel->>Receiver: RTS received
    Receiver->>Channel: Send CTS after SIFS 16 microseconds
    Channel-->>Others: All overhearing STAs set NAV
    Sender->>Channel: Send DATA frame after SIFS
    Receiver->>Sender: Send ACK after SIFS
    Note over Sender: Transmission complete SUCCESS
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Vulnerable Period Timeline (CSMA vs ALOHA)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Vulnerable Period Comparison Across Random Access Protocols
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Pure ALOHA
    Frame X vulnerable window minus 1 to plus 1 :a1, 0, 2
    section Slotted ALOHA
    Frame Y slot only 0 to 1 :a2, 0, 1
    section CSMA ideal
    Detection only Tp :a3, 0, 1
    section CSMA realistic 1 persistent
    Effective window 0 to Tp plus Tt :a4, 0, 1
```

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram 7 — Block Architecture of CSMA/CA Transceiver (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Application Layer<br/>Data Source] --> A2[MAC Sublayer<br/>CSMA CA Engine]
    A2 --> A3[Backoff Controller<br/>Binary Exponential]
    A2 --> A4[DIFS SIFS Timer]
    A2 --> A5[NAV Manager<br/>Virtual Carrier Sense]
    A2 --> A6[ACK Manager]
    A3 --> A7[PHY Layer<br/>Radio Transceiver]
    A4 --> A7
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Antenna<br/>2.4 or 5 GHz]
    A8 --> A9[Wireless Medium<br/>Shared Channel]
    A9 --> A8
    style A2 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style A7 fill:#388e3c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style A9 fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000
```

# 🎯 CSMA Protocols — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define CSMA. Why is the vulnerable period in CSMA less than that in Pure ALOHA?

**Model Answer:**

**Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)** is a contention-based random access MAC protocol in which a station, before transmitting a frame, *listens* to the shared medium to detect the presence of a carrier signal. Transmission is initiated only if the channel is sensed *idle* (or, in persistent variants, when it becomes idle).

**Comparison of Vulnerable Periods:**

| Protocol | Vulnerable Period | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Pure ALOHA | $2T_t$ | No sensing; any overlap of two frame-times causes corruption. |
| Slotted ALOHA | $T_t$ | Slotting halves the window, but no sensing still. |
| CSMA | $\approx T_p$ | Stations sense and defer; collision window is just the time to detect a busy channel ($T_p$). |

Since $T_p \ll T_t$ in LANs, CSMA's vulnerable period is **far smaller**, leading to a much higher maximum throughput (approaching 1) compared to ALOHA (max 0.184 or 0.368).

> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** Definition — 1.5 marks, Comparison — 1.5 marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA. State one practical system where each is used.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with **Collision Detection** | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with **Collision Avoidance** |
| **Strategy** | Transmit, monitor, abort if collision detected | Transmit only after RTS/CTS reservation + backoff |
| **Collision Handling** | Detect mid-transmission, send jam, backoff | Avoid via random backoff + virtual sensing (NAV) |
| **Hardware Requirement** | Must be able to TX and RX simultaneously | No simultaneous TX/RX needed |
| **Domain** | **Wired** networks | **Wireless** networks |
| **Practical System** | Classical **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** | **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** |
| **Why Domain Mismatch?** | In wired, collision fragments are easily detected by voltage anomalies on the cable. | In wireless, transmitted power overwhelms received power, so collision detection is impossible. |

> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** Tabular distinction — 2 marks, Example — 1 mark.

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain the operation of **1-Persistent CSMA** and **Non-Persistent CSMA** with the help of a flow diagram. Compare their throughput performance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A CSMA/CD network operates at a data rate of **1 Gbps** over a cable of maximum length **250 m** with a signal propagation speed of **$2 \times 10^8$ m/s**. Frames are **512 bytes** long. Determine:
> (i) One-way propagation delay
> (ii) Round-trip propagation delay
> (iii) Whether the minimum frame size requirement for collision detection is satisfied. If not, by what factor must the frame size be increased? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1-Persistent CSMA Operation:**
The station continuously monitors the channel. If idle, it transmits *immediately* (persistence = 1). If busy, it waits for the channel to become idle, then transmits with probability 1. On collision, it backs off randomly and retries. *Drawback:* When multiple stations are waiting, they all transmit at the same instant when the channel becomes free, causing a collision storm.

**Non-Persistent CSMA Operation:**
The station senses the channel. If idle, it transmits. If busy, it does *not* continuously sense — instead, it waits a *random* amount of time and re-senses. *Advantage:* Greatly reduces collision probability because no synchronized storm. *Drawback:* Increased idle time and delay.

**Throughput Comparison:**

| Parameter | 1-Persistent | Non-Persistent |
|---|---|---|
| **Throughput eq. (slotted)** | $S = \dfrac{G e^{-G}(1+G)}{1 + e^{-G}}$ | $S = \dfrac{G e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ |
| **Max throughput** | $\approx 0.529$ | $\to 1$ as $a \to 0$ |
| **Collision rate at high $G$** | Very high | Lower |
| **Idle time at high $G$** | Low | Higher |
| **Best for** | Moderate, predictable load | Variable, bursty load |

*Flow diagram (refer to Section 4.2 and 4.3 of these notes).*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Data rate $R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$
- Cable length $L = 250 \text{ m}$
- Propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- Frame size $F = 512 \text{ bytes} = 4096 \text{ bits}$

**Sub-part (i) — One-way propagation delay:**

$$T_p = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{250}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.25 \ \mu s$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Round-trip propagation delay:**

$$T_{rt} = 2 \cdot T_p = 2 \times 1.25 \ \mu s = 2.5 \ \mu s$$

**Sub-part (iii) — Frame transmission time and minimum-frame check:**

$$T_t = \frac{F}{R} = \frac{4096}{10^9} = 4.096 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 4.096 \ \mu s$$

**Compare:** $T_t$ vs $T_{rt}$

$$T_t = 4.096 \ \mu s \geq T_{rt} = 2.5 \ \mu s \quad ✅$$

The minimum frame size requirement **is satisfied** because the frame transmission time exceeds the round-trip propagation delay. The sending station is still transmitting when the collision signal returns.

**Ratio / Safety Factor:**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{T_t}{T_{rt}} = \frac{4.096}{2.5} = 1.6384$$

So the frame size is approximately **1.64 times** the theoretical minimum required (which would be $R \cdot T_{rt} = 10^9 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 2500$ bits = 312.5 bytes). The actual 512-byte frame provides a comfortable safety margin.

> **Mark Allocation (b):** (i) — 2 marks, (ii) — 2 marks, (iii) — 3 marks.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample QP aligned to 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Describe the working of **CSMA/CA** as used in IEEE 802.11 wireless LANs. Explain the role of **DIFS, SIFS, NAV, RTS/CTS**, and **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)**. Why is CSMA/CD unsuitable for wireless media? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A wireless network uses **slotted non-persistent CSMA** with frame transmission time $T_t = 20$ ms and one-way propagation delay $T_p = 2$ ms. The offered traffic follows a Poisson distribution with mean rate $G = 0.5$ attempts per frame-time. Compute the throughput and compare it with slotted ALOHA at the same load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Why CSMA/CD is unsuitable for wireless:**

In wireless media, a station *cannot* detect collisions while transmitting because:
1. The transmitted signal power at the antenna (~**20 dBm** = 100 mW) is **vastly stronger** than any received signal (~**−80 dBm** = 0.00000001 mW) — a difference of ~100 dB or **10 billion times**. The receiver's sensitive front-end is saturated by the station's own transmission.
2. The **Hidden Node Problem**: Two stations (A and C) are out of range of each other, but both can reach station B. They sense the channel as idle simultaneously, transmit, and collide at B — but neither A nor C detects the collision.
3. Wireless signal strength varies unpredictably due to fading, multipath, and mobility, making carrier sensing unreliable.

**CSMA/CA Mechanism (IEEE 802.11 DCF):**

| Element | Function |
|---|---|
| **DIFS** (Distributed Inter-Frame Space) | Minimum idle time before a station may attempt transmission (34 μs in DSSS). Gives priority to data frames. |
| **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) | Shortest inter-frame gap, used for ACK, CTS, and segmented frame responses (16 μs). Ensures control frames jump ahead of data contention. |
| **NAV** (Network Allocation Vector) | Virtual carrier-sense timer. Each frame's duration field tells other nodes how long the medium will be busy, so they "logically" defer. |
| **RTS/CTS** | Sender requests the channel (RTS); receiver confirms (CTS). Both announce duration in headers → all nodes (even hidden ones) update NAV. |
| **BEB** (Binary Exponential Backoff) | After collision, contention window $CW$ doubles: $CW = 2^{i+1} - 1$ up to a max of 1023 slots. Reduces collision probability under heavy load. |

**CSMA/CA Step-by-Step:**
1. Sense channel; if idle for **DIFS** → start backoff counter with random value in $[0, CW]$.
2. Decrement counter every idle slot; **freeze** if a transmission is sensed.
3. When counter = 0, send **RTS**; wait for **CTS** from receiver.
4. After receiving CTS (or after SIFS if no RTS/CTS), send **DATA**; wait for **ACK** within SIFS.
5. If no ACK → assume failure, double $CW$, repeat.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $T_t = 20$ ms
- $T_p = 2$ ms
- $G = 0.5$ attempts/frame-time (slotted)
- Slotted Non-Persistent CSMA

**Step 1 — Normalized propagation parameter:**

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{2}{20} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Apply Slotted NP-CSMA throughput formula:**

$$S_{CSMA} = \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$$

Substitute $G = 0.5$, $a = 0.1$:

$$S_{CSMA} = \frac{0.5 \cdot e^{-0.05}}{0.5(1 + 0.2) + e^{-0.05}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$e^{-0.05} = 0.9512$$

$$S_{CSMA} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.9512}{0.5 \times 1.2 + 0.9512} = \frac{0.4756}{0.6 + 0.9512} = \frac{0.4756}{1.5512}$$

$$\boxed{S_{CSMA} \approx 0.3066}$$

**Step 4 — Slotted ALOHA throughput at same load:**

$$S_{ALOHA} = G \cdot e^{-G} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 0.5 \times 0.6065 = 0.3033$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

| Protocol | Throughput at $G = 0.5$ |
|---|---|
| Slotted ALOHA | $0.3033$ |
| Slotted Non-Persistent CSMA | $\mathbf{0.3066}$ |
| **Improvement** | **~1.09%** |

**Interpretation:** At low-to-moderate load ($G = 0.5$), the improvement of CSMA over Slotted ALOHA is small because collisions are already infrequent. The benefit of CSMA becomes dramatic at higher loads (e.g., $G = 1$, $G = 2$), where Slotted ALOHA's throughput collapses but CSMA maintains reasonable performance.

> **Mark Allocation (b):** Formula setup — 2 marks, Computation — 3 marks, Comparison — 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse "vulnerable period" with "frame time."** CSMA's vulnerable period is **$T_p$**, NOT $T_t$. Students who write "$V_{CSMA} = T_t$" lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Always compute $a = T_p / T_t$ first** in any CSMA numerical — it's the keystone parameter.
> 3. **In CSMA/CD numericals, the round-trip delay is $2T_p$**, not $T_p$. Minimum frame condition: $T_t \geq 2T_p$.
> 4. **1-Persistent CSMA is NOT suitable for wireless** — it would cause collision storms. CSMA/CA is used.
> 5. **p-Persistent CSMA applies only to slotted channels** — for unslotted, answer is "Not Applicable."
> 6. **The factor of 2 in $2T_t$ for Pure ALOHA** comes from both *leading* and *trailing* vulnerable windows. Students often forget the leading half.
> 7. **For 802.11, SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS** — remember this ordering for time-slot hierarchy questions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist 5 minutes before the exam for a final memory refresh.**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember

- ⭐ **CSMA = Sense before Transmit.** Reduces vulnerable period from $2T_t$ (ALOHA) to $T_p$.
- ⭐ **Three CSMA Variants:** 1-Persistent, Non-Persistent, p-Persistent.
- ⭐ **1-Persistent:** Aggressive, transmit immediately on idle — high collision under load.
- ⭐ **Non-Persistent:** Patient, random backoff on busy — fewer collisions, more delay.
- ⭐ **p-Persistent:** Probabilistic in slotted channel — opt for $p$ depends on $G$.
- ⭐ **CSMA/CD = Detect.** Used in wired Ethernet. Needs $T_t \geq 2T_p$ and minimum 64-byte frame.
- ⭐ **CSMA/CA = Avoid.** Used in Wi-Fi. Uses DIFS/SIFS, NAV, RTS/CTS, BEB.
- ⭐ **Hidden Node Problem** → solved only by RTS/CTS in wireless.
- ⭐ **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** $CW$ doubles after each failure, max 1023.
- ⭐ **Maximum throughput ranking:** NP-CSMA > 1-P CSMA > Slotted ALOHA > Pure ALOHA.

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize)

- ⭐ Pure ALOHA: $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, $S_{max} = 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$
- ⭐ Slotted ALOHA: $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$, $S_{max} = 0.368$ at $G = 1$
- ⭐ NP-CSMA slotted: $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{1 + G}$
- ⭐ NP-CSMA unslotted: $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$
- ⭐ 1-P CSMA unslotted: $S = \dfrac{G(1+G) e^{-G}}{1 + e^{-G}}$
- ⭐ Minimum frame size: $F_{min} = 2 \cdot T_p \cdot R$ bits
- ⭐ Throughput identity: $G = S + (\text{collisions/time})$

### ✅ Engineering Rule of Thumb

- ⭐ When $a = T_p/T_t$ is **small** (LAN, Wi-Fi) → **CSMA is far better than ALOHA**.
- ⭐ When $a$ is **large** (satellite, deep-space) → **ALOHA is competitive**; CSMA's gain shrinks.
- ⭐ **Ethernet legacy:** 10 Mbps, 2500 m, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, 64-byte minimum frame.
- ⭐ **Wi-Fi (802.11):** SIFS = 16 μs, PIFS = 25 μs, DIFS = 34 μs (DSSS legacy), slot time = 9 μs.

### ✅ Exam Strategy

- ⭐ Always **draw the flowchart** in protocol explanation questions — earns easy 2 marks.
- ⭐ In numericals, **state the formula first**, then substitute, then compute.
- ⭐ **Mention real-world systems** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi) — examiners reward contextual awareness.
- ⭐ Use **tabulated comparisons** rather than paragraphs wherever possible.
- ⭐ For "compare" questions, **make a table with at least 4 rows** of contrasting features.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Systems: GSM System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Systems: GSM System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Global System for Mobile Communications (GSM)** is a second-generation (2G) digital cellular standard developed by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) to provide ubiquitous, secure, and high-capacity mobile voice and data services. Architecturally, GSM is partitioned into three tightly coupled subsystems that collectively manage the radio link, the switching backbone, and the user/service databases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** GSM is a TDMA/FDMA-based cellular system operating in the **900 MHz** and **1800 MHz** bands (with **850 MHz** and **1900 MHz** variants in the Americas). Each RF channel of **200 kHz** bandwidth is divided into **8 TDMA time slots**, supporting **8 simultaneous users per carrier**.

The three principal subsystems of the GSM System Architecture (Network) are:

1. **Mobile Station (MS)** — The user-side equipment (handset + SIM).
2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS)** — Manages the radio interface (BTS + BSC + TCU).
3. **Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)** — The fixed backbone that performs switching, routing, mobility management, and subscriber authentication (MSC, HLR, VLR, AUC, EIR).
4. **Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS)** — Network monitoring, fault management, and billing.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "GSM = A City Post Office"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A City Postal Network**
> 
> - The **Mobile Station (MS)** is the *citizen* (the person holding a letter/SIM card).
> - The **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)** is the *local post box* on the street corner — it is the first physical point of contact.
> - The **Base Station Controller (BSC)** is the *neighbourhood post office supervisor* — it routes and supervises several post boxes.
> - The **Mobile Switching Center (MSC)** is the *main city sorting office* — it decides which city/locality the letter must go to.
> - The **HLR** (Home Location Register) is the *permanent home address registry* of the citizen.
> - The **VLR** (Visitor Location Register) is the *temporary hotel guest book* — "Mr X is currently in our city."
> - The **AUC** (Authentication Center) is the *passport/security office*.
> - The **EIR** (Equipment Identity Register) is the *customs checkpoint* — checking if the *device* (not the user) is blacklisted/stolen.

When a citizen moves from City A to City B, the city office (MSC-B) registers him in the VLR (guest book) and informs his hometown registry (HLR). When he makes a call, the local sorting office (MSC) checks the HLR/VLR before connecting the call.

---

### 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Uplink Frequency Band | **890 – 915 MHz** (GSM 900) |
| Downlink Frequency Band | **935 – 960 MHz** (GSM 900) |
| Duplex Spacing | **45 MHz** |
| Carrier Bandwidth | **200 kHz** |
| Number of Carriers | **124** (in GSM 900) |
| Users per Carrier | **8** (TDMA) |
| Modulation | **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying, BT = 0.3) |
| Bit Rate per Carrier | **270.833 kbps** |
| Speech Coding | **RPE-LTP** (13 kbps) |
| Frame Duration | **4.615 ms** |
| Slot Duration | **577 µs** (156.25 bits) |
| Cell Radius | **0.5 km – 35 km** (typical) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture — The Three Subsystems

#### 2.1.1 Mobile Station (MS)

The MS is the only part the user directly interacts with. It has two distinct components:

- **Mobile Equipment (ME):** The physical handset, identified globally by a unique **15-digit IMEI** (International Mobile Equipment Identity).
- **Subscriber Identity Module (SIM):** A smart card containing the **IMSI** (International Mobile Subscriber Identity), the **Ki** (authentication key), and personal service data.

The MS is responsible for radio transmission/reception, speech coding/decoding, and signalling to the BTS over the **Um** (air) interface.

#### 2.1.2 Base Station Subsystem (BSS)

The BSS is the radio access network of GSM. It contains:

- **Base Transceiver Station (BTS):** Houses the radio transceivers and antennas of one cell. It performs channel coding, encryption, and modulation/demodulation. It terminates the **Um** interface on the network side.
- **Base Station Controller (BSC):** A small switch that controls multiple BTSs. It manages **radio channel allocation** (handover decisions), **frequency hopping**, and **power control** for the BTSs under its command. Typically, one BSC manages **30 – 200 BTSs**.
- **Transcoding and Rate Adaptation Unit (TRAU/TCU):** Converts the **13 kbps** RPE-LTP speech from the BTS to the standard **64 kbps** PCM format of the PSTN, located between BSC and MSC.

The BSS terminates the **Abis** interface (between BTS and BSC) and the **A** interface (between BSC and MSC).

#### 2.1.3 Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)

Often called the "heart" of GSM, the NSS performs call switching, mobility management, and subscriber management.

- **Mobile Switching Center (MSC):** The primary switching node. Sets up, routes, and releases calls. Implements **HLR queries**, **VLR coordination**, and inter-MSC handovers. It also interfaces with the PSTN via the **Gateway MSC (GMSC)**.
- **Home Location Register (HLR):** The **master database** of all subscribers permanently registered in the home network. Stores IMSI, MSISDN (phone number), current VLR address, and subscribed services.
- **Visitor Location Register (VLR):** A **temporary database** of subscribers currently roaming in the MSC's area. Avoids repeated HLR queries.
- **Authentication Center (AuC):** Stores the **Ki** (128-bit secret key) and generates **SRES** triplets for subscriber authentication.
- **Equipment Identity Register (EIR):** Maintains a database of IMEIs: white list, black list, and grey list.

#### 2.1.4 Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS)

The OSS is the central nervous system for network operators. It performs:
- Network monitoring and fault management.
- Subscriber billing (via the **Billing Center**).
- Performance management.
- Security and alarm management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Many students confuse the HLR and VLR. A simple rule:
> - **HLR = Permanent** (lives at home network).
> - **VLR = Temporary** (lives at visited network while roaming).
> When the user returns home, the VLR entry is **deleted**, while the HLR entry **never** changes unless the user subscribes/unsubscribes.

---

### 2.2 The Four Standard GSM Interfaces

| Interface | Location | Protocol | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Um** | MS ↔ BTS | Radio (Physical + LAPDm) | Air interface, signalling + traffic |
| **Abis** | BTS ↔ BSC | LAPD | Internal BSS signalling, traffic |
| **A** | BSC ↔ MSC | BSSAP/SS7 (MTP/SCCP) | BSS-to-NSS signalling |
| **MAP** | MSC ↔ HLR/VLR/AuC | SS7/MAP | Mobility + Authentication signalling |

---

### 2.3 GSM Radio Frame Structure — The KTU Numerical Backbone

The GSM TDMA hierarchy is a **multiframe hierarchy** with 5 levels:

$$
\boxed{\text{Hyperframe} \;(2715648) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Superframe} \;(1326) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Multiframe} \;(26 \text{ or } 51) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Frame} \;(8) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Slot} \;(156.25 \text{ bits})}
$$

There are **two types of multiframes**:
- **Traffic multiframe:** 26 frames, duration = **120 ms**.
- **Control multiframe:** 51 frames, duration = **235.4 ms**.

Each **frame = 8 time slots = 4.615 ms**. Each **time slot = 156.25 bits = 577 µs**.

The **TDMA frame number (FN)** cycles over the **hyperframe** of duration:

$$
T_{\text{hyper}} = 2715648 \text{ frames} \times 4.615 \text{ ms/frame} \approx 12533.3 \text{ seconds} \approx 3 \text{ hours } 28 \text{ min } 53 \text{ s}
$$

This hyperframe is used for **encryption** (ciphering sequence) and **frequency hopping**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_{\text{carrier}} = 200 \text{ kHz}$ | RF carrier bandwidth | kHz |
| 2 | $R_{\text{carrier}} = 270.833 \text{ kbps}$ | Gross bit rate per carrier | kbps |
| 3 | $T_{\text{frame}} = 4.615 \text{ ms}$ | TDMA frame duration | ms |
| 4 | $T_{\text{slot}} = 577 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ | Time-slot duration | µs |
| 5 | $N_{\text{slot}} = 8$ | Slots per frame | — |
| 6 | $T_{\text{bit}} = T_{\text{slot}} / 156.25 = 3.69 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ | Bit duration | µs |
| 7 | $T_{\text{multiframe (T)}} = 26 \times 4.615 = 120 \text{ ms}$ | Traffic multiframe | ms |
| 8 | $T_{\text{multiframe (C)}} = 51 \times 4.615 = 235.4 \text{ ms}$ | Control multiframe | ms |
| 9 | $T_{\text{hyper}} \approx 12533.3 \text{ s} \approx 3.48 \text{ h}$ | Hyperframe period | s |
| 10 | $R_{\text{speech}} = 13 \text{ kbps}$ | RPE-LTP codec rate | kbps |
| 11 | $R_{\text{channel}} = R_{\text{speech}} + \text{overhead} \approx 22.8 \text{ kbps}$ | Full-rate traffic channel | kbps |
| 12 | $N_{\text{users/carrier}} = 8$ | Users per carrier | — |
| 13 | $N_{\text{carriers (GSM 900)}} = 124$ | Available carriers | — |
| 14 | $\text{Duplex spacing} = 45 \text{ MHz}$ | Tx–Rx gap | MHz |
| 15 | $E_b/N_0$ for GMSK (typical) | **≥ 9 dB** for BER $10^{-3}$ | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** GSM's 4-level frame structure is the blueprint for all later TDMA-derived systems (GPRS, EDGE). The hyperframe's **3 h 28 min** cycle is reused for **A5/1 ciphering** — every 4.615 ms, a fresh cipher block is XORed with the user data, producing roughly **217 encrypted bursts per second** per user. Modern IoT LTE-M and NB-IoT systems trace their **scheduling** architectures directly back to GSM's 26-frame traffic multiframe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit Rate of a GSM Carrier

**Given:**
- Carrier bandwidth $B = 200$ kHz
- GMSK modulation with bandwidth-time product $BT = 0.3$
- 8 time slots per frame

**Step 1: Compute the symbol (baud) rate.**

For GMSK, the **3 dB bandwidth** of a Gaussian pulse is related to the bit rate by:

$$
B \cdot T = 0.3 \quad \text{(GMSK parameter)}
$$

Substituting $B = 200$ kHz:

$$
T = \frac{0.3}{200 \times 10^3} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.5 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the symbol (baud) rate.**

$$
R_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{1.5 \text{ µs}} = 666.667 \text{ kbaud}
$$

**Step 3: GMSK is a binary modulation (1 bit per symbol).**

$$
R_b = R_s \times 1 = 666.667 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4: Account for framing overhead and guard periods.**

GSM has 8 slots per frame and a fixed **guard period** of 8.25 bits per slot. The effective information per slot is therefore **148 bits**. With 8 slots, 4.615 ms frame, 1 burst every 8 slots per user:

$$
R_{\text{channel}} = \frac{148 \text{ bits}}{4.615 \text{ ms}} = 32.05 \text{ kbps}
$$

But because each user gets **1 slot out of 8**, the actual user data rate per slot is:

$$
R_{\text{user}} = \frac{148 \text{ bits}}{8 \times 4.615 \text{ ms}} = \frac{148}{36.92 \text{ ms}} = 4.0 \text{ kbps per direction}
$$

**Step 5: Add 4 bursts of SACCH and idle/reserved — the user gets an effective channel rate.**

The actual **full-rate traffic channel (TCH/F)** has a total gross rate of:

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{TCH}} = 22.8 \text{ kbps}}
$$

which carries **13 kbps of RPE-LTP speech** plus error correction (9.8 kbps convolutional FEC at rate 1/2 with puncturing).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multiframe and Hyperframe Periods

**Given:** 8 slots/frame, multiframe structure as defined by GSM 05.01.

**Step 1: Traffic multiframe period.**

A traffic multiframe contains 26 consecutive frames:

$$
T_{\text{T-multiframe}} = 26 \times T_{\text{frame}} = 26 \times 4.615 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{T-multiframe}} = 120 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 2: Control multiframe period.**

A control multiframe contains 51 consecutive frames:

$$
T_{\text{C-multiframe}} = 51 \times 4.615 \text{ ms} = 235.38 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{C-multiframe}} \approx 235.4 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 3: Superframe period.**

A superframe = 51 traffic multiframes (or 26 control multiframes):

$$
T_{\text{superframe}} = 51 \times 120 \text{ ms} = 6.12 \text{ s}
$$

Equivalently: $T_{\text{superframe}} = 26 \times 235.4 \text{ ms} = 6.12 \text{ s}$ ✓

**Step 4: Hyperframe period.**

A hyperframe = 2048 superframes:

$$
T_{\text{hyper}} = 2048 \times 6.12 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{hyper}} = 12533.76 \text{ s} \approx 3 \text{ h } 28 \text{ min } 53.76 \text{ s}}
$$

This is **3 hours 28 minutes 53 seconds 760 ms**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — User Capacity per Cell

**Given:** GSM 900 with 124 carriers, 8 users/carrier.

**Step 1: Total traffic channels per cell (sector) under ideal conditions.**

$$
N_{\text{users}} = N_{\text{carriers}} \times N_{\text{slots}} = 124 \times 8 = 992 \text{ users per cell (FDMA+TDMA)}
$$

**Step 2: Half-rate (HR) codec scenario.**

With half-rate codec (5.6 kbps RPE-LTP), each slot can serve 2 users via **sub-channels** (submultiplexing), so:

$$
N_{\text{HR users}} = 124 \times 8 \times 2 = 1984 \text{ users per cell}
$$

**Step 3: Total Erlangs supported (using Erlang B table).**

If the Grade of Service (GoS) is 2% blocking and 992 channels, the carried traffic:

$$
A \approx 870 \text{ Erlangs per cell}
$$

Assuming 30 mErlang per user:

$$
N_{\text{subscribers}} = \frac{870}{0.030} \approx 29{,}000 \text{ subscribers per cell}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — GSM Authentication Triplet Generation

**Step 1: The subscriber identity — IMSI.**

IMSI = MCC (3 digits) + MNC (2-3 digits) + MSIN (up to 10 digits) → up to **15 digits**.

**Step 2: Authentication triplet (SRES, Kc, RAND).**

- AuC generates a 128-bit random number **RAND**.
- Using the **A3 algorithm**, AuC computes:
$$
\text{SRES} = A_3(K_i, \text{RAND}) \quad (\text{32 bits})
$$
- Using the **A8 algorithm**, AuC computes:
$$
K_c = A_8(K_i, \text{RAND}) \quad (\text{64-bit session key})
$$
- The triplet **(RAND, SRES, Kc)** is sent to the VLR.

**Step 3: At the MS side, the SIM repeats the same computation.**

The MS computes:

$$
\text{SRES}_{\text{MS}} = A_3(K_i, \text{RAND})
$$

**Step 4: Compare.**

If $\text{SRES}_{\text{MS}} = \text{SRES}_{\text{AuC}}$, authentication is **successful**, and the session key $K_c$ is used for **A5 ciphering**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Channel Types in GSM

| Channel Category | Logical Channel | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Traffic (TCH)** | TCH/F, TCH/H | Both | Speech/data payload |
| **Broadcast (BCH)** | BCCH, PCH, SCH, FCCH | DL → MS | Cell info, freq/time sync |
| **Common Control (CCCH)** | RACH, AGCH, PCH, CBCH | UL/DL | Access grant, paging |
| **Dedicated Control (DCCH)** | SDCCH, SACCH, FACCH | Both | Signalling during call |
| **Idle Beacons** | CBCH | DL | Cell broadcast (SMS-CB) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GSM System Architecture — Top-Level Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS1[Mobile Station MS]
    SIM[SIM Card]
    IMEI[IMEI Hardware ID]
    MS1 --> SIM
    MS1 --> IMEI
    
    BSS[Base Station Subsystem BSS]
    BTS1[BTS Cell 1]
    BTS2[BTS Cell 2]
    BTS3[BTS Cell 3]
    BSC1[Base Station Controller BSC]
    TCU[TRAU Transcoding Unit]
    BSS --> BTS1
    BSS --> BTS2
    BSS --> BTS3
    BTS1 --> BSC1
    BTS2 --> BSC1
    BTS3 --> BSC1
    BSC1 --> TCU
    
    NSS[Network and Switching Subsystem NSS]
    MSC[Mobile Switching Center MSC]
    GMSC[Gateway MSC GMSC]
    HLR[Home Location Register HLR]
    VLR[Visitor Location Register VLR]
    AUC[Authentication Center AuC]
    EIR[Equipment Identity Register EIR]
    NSS --> MSC
    NSS --> GMSC
    NSS --> HLR
    NSS --> VLR
    NSS --> AUC
    NSS --> EIR
    
    OSS[Operation and Support Subsystem OSS]
    OMC[Operations and Maintenance Center]
    BC[Billing Center]
    NMC[Network Management Center]
    OSS --> OMC
    OSS --> BC
    OSS --> NMC
    
    MS1 -->|Um Radio| BTS1
    BSC1 -->|Abis| BTS1
    BSC1 -->|A Interface| MSC
    MSC -->|SS7 MAP| HLR
    MSC -->|SS7 MAP| VLR
    HLR --> AUC
    MSC --> GMSC
    GMSC <-->|PSTN| PSTN[Public Switched Telephone Network]
```

### 4.2 GSM Interfaces and Their Positions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS_A[Mobile Station]
    BTS_A[Base Transceiver Station]
    BSC_A[Base Station Controller]
    MSC_A[Mobile Switching Center]
    
    MS_A -->|Um Air Interface| BTS_A
    BTS_A -->|Abis Interface| BSC_A
    BSC_A -->|A Interface| MSC_A
    MSC_A -->|MAP SS7| HLR_A[HLR]
    MSC_A -->|MAP SS7| VLR_A[VLR]
    MSC_A -->|MAP SS7| AUC_A[AuC]
    MSC_A -->|MAP SS7| EIR_A[EIR]
```

### 4.3 GSM Call Flow — Mobile-Originated Call (MOC) to PSTN

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MS as Mobile Station
    participant BTS as BTS
    participant BSC as BSC
    participant MSC as MSC
    participant VLR as VLR
    participant HLR as HLR
    participant AUC as AuC
    participant GMSC as GMSC
    participant PSTN as PSTN
    
    MS->>BTS: 1. RACH Channel Request
    BTS->>BSC: 2. Channel Required
    BSC->>BTS: 3. Activate SDCCH
    BTS->>MS: 4. AGCH Immediate Assignment
    MS->>BTS: 5. SDCCH CM Service Request
    BTS->>MSC: 6. CM Service Request via BSC
    MSC->>VLR: 7. Query for IMSI
    VLR->>HLR: 8. Get Authentication Triplet
    HLR->>AUC: 9. Generate SRES, Kc, RAND
    AUC-->>HLR: 10. Return Triplet
    HLR-->>VLR: 11. Triplet
    VLR-->>MSC: 12. Triplet
    MSC->>MS: 13. AUTH REQUEST RAND
    MS->>MSC: 14. AUTH RESPONSE SRES
    MSC->>MS: 15. CIPHER MODE COMMAND
    MS->>MSC: 16. CIPHER MODE COMPLETE
    MS->>MSC: 17. SETUP
    MSC->>GMSC: 18. IAM Initial Address Message
    GMSC->>PSTN: 19. Route to Called Party
    PSTN-->>GMSC: 20. ACM Address Complete
    GMSC-->>MSC: 21. ACM
    MSC->>MS: 22. CALL PROCEEDING + ALERTING
    MS-->>MSC: 23. CONNECT
    MSC-->>GMSC: 24. ANM Answer
    GMSC-->>PSTN: 25. ANM
    MS-->>BTS: 26. TCH Assigned Voice Path Open
```

### 4.4 GSM TDMA Frame Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Hyperframe 2715648 frames / 3 h 28 min 53 s]
    S[Superframe 1326 frames / 6.12 s]
    M[Multiframe 26 frames or 51 frames]
    F[TDMA Frame 8 slots / 4.615 ms]
    SL[Time Slot 156.25 bits / 577 us]
    
    H -->|2048 superframes| S
    S -->|1326 multiframes| M
    M -->|26 or 51 frames| F
    F -->|8 slots| SL
```

### 4.5 GSM Authentication Triplet Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIM[SIM Card with Ki]
    AUDC[AuC with Ki]
    
    AUDC -->|Generate RAND 128 bit| RAND[RAND Random]
    AUDC -->|A3 Algorithm| SRES_AU[SRES 32 bit]
    AUDC -->|A8 Algorithm| KC[Kc 64 bit Session Key]
    
    RAND --> MS_AUTH
    SRES_AU --> VLR_STORAGE[VLR Stores Triplet]
    KC --> VLR_STORAGE
    
    SIM -->|A3 with Ki| SRES_MS[SRES 32 bit MS]
    SRES_MS --> COMPARE{Check}
    SRES_AU --> COMPARE
    COMPARE -->|Match| SUCCESS[Authenticated]
    COMPARE -->|No Match| FAIL[Reject]
    
    SUCCESS -->|A5 Ciphering| KCU[Use Kc for Encryption]
```
