**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> Define the term *Protocol* in the context of wireless communication. List any four functions performed by a wireless protocol.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *protocol* is a formalized set of rules and conventions that govern how data is transmitted, received, and interpreted between two or more wireless devices. It ensures orderly and reliable communication over a shared, lossy wireless medium.

**Four key functions of a wireless protocol:**

1. **Addressing and identification** — assigning unique identifiers (MAC addresses, device IDs) to each node.
2. **Medium access control** — regulating when a device may transmit (e.g., CSMA/CA, TDMA, FDMA).
3. **Error detection and correction** — ensuring data integrity using CRC, FEC, ARQ.
4. **Connection management** — establishing, maintaining, and terminating communication sessions (e.g., Bluetooth connection states).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> Differentiate between **Bluetooth**, **Wi-Fi**, and **ZigBee** based on (i) frequency band, (ii) maximum data rate, and (iii) typical range.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Bluetooth | Wi-Fi | ZigBee |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Frequency Band | **2.4 GHz** (ISM) | **2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz** | **2.4 GHz / 915 MHz / 868 MHz** |
| (ii) Max Data Rate | **1–3 Mbps** (BR/EDR) | **11 Mbps to 9.6 Gbps** | **250 kbps** |
| (iii) Typical Range | **10 m** (Class 2) | **30–100 m** indoor | **10–100 m** |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> Explain the **Bluetooth protocol stack** in detail with a neat diagram. Describe the functions of the **L2CAP** and **LMP** layers.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The Bluetooth protocol stack is organized into **three functional groups** (refer to the diagram in Section 4.1):

**1. Transport Protocol Group (Lower Layers):**

- **Radio Layer (PHY):** Operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band with 79 channels of 1 MHz each. Uses **FHSS** at **1600 hops/second** with **GFSK** modulation.
- **Baseband Layer:** Defines the **Piconet** structure (1 master + up to 7 active slaves), manages ACL (data) and SCO (voice) links, performs error correction via **FEC** and **ARQ**.
- **Link Manager Protocol (LMP):** Handles **link setup**, **authentication**, **pairing**, **encryption**, and **power mode negotiation** (Active, Sniff, Hold, Park).

**2. Middleware Protocol Group:**

- **HCI (Host Controller Interface):** Standardized interface between host (computer) and Bluetooth controller.
- **L2CAP (Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol):** Performs **segmentation and reassembly** of large packets (up to 65,535 bytes in Bluetooth 5.0), provides **connection-oriented** and **connectionless** services, and enables **QoS flow specification**.
- **SDP (Service Discovery Protocol):** Allows devices to discover available services.

**3. Application Protocol Group:**

- **RFCOMM:** Emulates RS-232 serial ports for cable replacement.
- **OBEX:** Object exchange for file/contact transfer.
- **TCS BIN:** Telephony control for voice calls.

**Specific roles of L2CAP and LMP:**

- **LMP** operates between the Baseband and L2CAP layers; it controls the **radio link** between two Bluetooth devices, manages **pairing**, **authentication**, and **low-power mode transitions**.
- **L2CAP** sits above LMP and provides **logical channel multiplexing**, allowing multiple higher-layer protocols to share a single ACL link. It also handles **packet segmentation** when an upper-layer PDU exceeds the Baseband's MTU.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **CSMA/CA mechanism** used in IEEE 802.11 MAC layer. State the role of **DIFS**, **SIFS**, and **backoff counter**.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The IEEE 802.11 MAC layer uses the **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** based on **CSMA/CA** to regulate channel access (refer to Section 4.5 for the flow diagram).

**Working of CSMA/CA:**

1. The station with a frame to transmit **senses the channel**.
2. If the channel is **idle for DIFS**, the station proceeds; otherwise, it **waits** until the channel becomes idle.
3. Once DIFS expires, the station **generates a random backoff counter** uniformly in $[0, CW]$.
4. The counter is **decremented by 1 every slot time** while the channel remains idle. If the channel becomes busy, the counter is **frozen**.
5. When the counter reaches **zero**, the station **transmits the frame**.
6. After **SIFS**, the receiver sends an **ACK** to confirm successful reception.
7. If no ACK is received, the station assumes collision and **doubles CW** (up to $CW_{max}$) and retries.

**Role of Key Timing Parameters:**

- **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space):** The shortest interval used for **immediate responses** (e.g., ACK, CTS). It allows the radio hardware to switch from receive to transmit mode. Value = **10 µs** in 802.11b.
- **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space):** The interval a data frame must wait before contending for the channel. Always **greater than SIFS** to give priority to control frames. Value = **50 µs** in 802.11b (computed as $SIFS + 2 \cdot T_{slot} = 10 + 2 \times 20 = 50$ µs).
- **Backoff Counter:** A **random number of slot times** selected uniformly from $[0, CW]$. It minimizes the probability of simultaneous transmission attempts by multiple stations, thus **avoiding collisions**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **CSMA/CA** with **CSMA/CD** (used in Ethernet). Wireless networks use **CA (Collision Avoidance)** because a device *cannot detect a collision while transmitting* (it cannot listen on the same frequency it is transmitting on — known as the *near-far problem*).
> - Always state the SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS ordering in the MAC layer hierarchy. Examiners specifically test this ordering.
> - Forgetting to **freeze the backoff counter** when the channel becomes busy is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> Explain the **ZigBee protocol stack** with a neat diagram. Compare it with the Bluetooth protocol stack in terms of power consumption, range, and data rate.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The ZigBee protocol stack is a **two-part architecture**: the lower two layers are defined by **IEEE 802.15.4**, and the upper layers by the **ZigBee Alliance** (refer to the diagram in Section 4.3).

**Layers of the ZigBee Stack:**

1. **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY Layer:**
   - Frequency bands: 2.4 GHz (16 channels), 915 MHz (10 channels), 868 MHz (1 channel).
   - Modulation: **O-QPSK** at 2.4 GHz.
   - Bit rate: **250 kbps** at 2.4 GHz.

2. **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Layer:**
   - Uses **slotted CSMA/CA** with optional **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)**.
   - Supports three device roles: **FFD (Full-Function Device)** acting as Coordinator or Router, and **RFD (Reduced-Function Device)** as End Device.

3. **ZigBee NWK (Network Layer):**
   - Handles **mesh routing**, **network formation**, and **tree/mesh addressing**.

4. **APS (Application Support Sublayer):**
   - Manages **binding tables** between endpoints and provides **indirect addressing**.

5. **ZDO (ZigBee Device Object):**
   - Responsible for **device discovery**, **security key management**, and **network management**.

6. **Application Framework (AF):**
   - Hosts up to **240 application endpoints** defined by the application developer.

**Comparison with Bluetooth:**

| Parameter | ZigBee | Bluetooth |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Consumption** | **Ultra-low** (months–years on coin cell) | **Low** (days–weeks) |
| **Range** | **10–100 m** | **10 m** (Class 2 typical) |
| **Data Rate** | **250 kbps** | **1–3 Mbps** |
| **Network Size** | Up to **65,000 nodes** | Up to **8 active** per piconet |
| **Topology** | Star, Mesh, Cluster Tree | Piconet, Scatternet |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> An IEEE 802.11b WLAN uses **DSSS** with a data rate of **11 Mbps**. A station transmits a **1500-byte MSDU** using basic rate ACK of **1 Mbps**. Compute the **total channel occupancy time** (in µs) for this exchange. Assume $SIFS = 10$ µs, $DIFS = 50$ µs, $T_{slot} = 20$ µs, PHY preamble = 144 µs, and PLCP header = 48 µs. ACK frame size = 14 bytes.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — MAC Frame Size:**
$$
L_{MAC} = 34 + 1500 + 4 \text{ (FCS)} \quad \text{(Wait — FCS is part of the standard 34-byte overhead)}
$$
Correction:
$$
L_{MAC} = 2(\text{FC}) + 2(\text{Dur}) + 4(6)(\text{Addrs}) + 2(\text{SeqCtrl}) + 1500(\text{MSDU}) + 4(\text{FCS}) = 1534 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Data Transmission Time at 11 Mbps:**
$$
T_{data} = \frac{1534 \times 8}{11 \times 10^6} = \frac{12{,}272}{11{,}000{,}000} = 1.1156 \text{ ms} = 1115.6 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3 — PHY Overhead:**
$$
T_{PHY} = 144 + 48 = 192 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 4 — ACK Transmission Time at 1 Mbps:**
$$
T_{ACK} = \frac{14 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} + T_{PHY} = 112 \text{ µs} + 192 \text{ µs} = 304 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Channel Occupancy:**
$$
T_{total} = DIFS + T_{data} + T_{PHY} + SIFS + T_{ACK}
$$
$$
T_{total} = 50 + 1115.6 + 192 + 10 + 304
$$
$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 1671.6 \text{ µs} \approx 1.67 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Throughput:**
$$
\eta = \frac{1500 \times 8}{1671.6 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{12{,}000}{1.6716 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 7.18 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Efficiency ratio:** $\frac{7.18}{11} \approx 65.3\%$ — confirms that the nominal 11 Mbps data rate delivers only ~65% efficiency in practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting the **FCS (4 bytes)** in the MAC frame size → lose 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using 11 Mbps for the ACK transmission → ACK is always at **basic rate (1 Mbps)**. Lose 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Omitting the **PHY preamble and PLCP header overhead** when calculating $T_{data}$. Lose 2 marks.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Including the backoff counter time as part of "channel occupancy" — backoff time is **before** transmission, not *during* it.

---

### 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Protocols**

**Core Definitions:**
- A **protocol** is a standardized set of rules governing wireless communication.
- A **protocol stack** is a layered architecture with vertical inter-layer and horizontal peer-layer communication.
- A **Piconet** = 1 master + up to 7 active slaves in Bluetooth.
- A **BSS** (Basic Service Set) = 1 AP + associated clients in Wi-Fi.
- A **PAN** (Personal Area Network) = Bluetooth domain.
- A **WSN** (Wireless Sensor Network) = ZigBee domain.

**Key Numerical Values (must memorize):**
- Bluetooth: 79 channels, 1 MHz each, 1600 hops/sec, GFSK modulation, 1 Mbps BR, 10 m (Class 2).
- Wi-Fi 802.11b: 11 Mbps DSSS, SIFS = 10 µs, PIFS = 30 µs, DIFS = 50 µs, Slot = 20 µs.
- ZigBee: 16 channels in 2.4 GHz, 5 MHz spacing, 250 kbps, O-QPSK.

**Critical Timing Relationships:**
$$
SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS
$$
$$
PIFS = SIFS + T_{slot}
$$
$$
DIFS = SIFS + 2 \cdot T_{slot}
$$

**MAC Address Sizes:**
- Bluetooth: 48-bit BD_ADDR.
- Wi-Fi: 48-bit MAC address.
- ZigBee: 64-bit IEEE address (short 16-bit also used).

**CSMA/CA vs. CSMA/CD:**
- **CSMA/CA** → Wi-Fi, ZigBee (Collision **Avoidance** — used in wireless).
- **CSMA/CD** → Ethernet (Collision **Detection** — used in wired).

**Bluetooth Hop Pattern:**
- Hop range: 2402 MHz to 2480 MHz, 79 carriers spaced 1 MHz apart.
- Master determines hopping sequence using its clock and BD_ADDR.

**Wi-Fi Frame Structure (remember the 4-address format):**
- 4 × MAC addresses enable distribution system bridging across multiple BSSes.

**ZigBee Network Capacity:**
- Theoretical maximum: **65,000 nodes** per ZigBee coordinator.
- Each node can have up to 240 endpoints.

**Profile-Based Thinking:**
- Bluetooth uses **profiles** (A2DP, HFP, HID, SPP) to define application-level behavior.
- Wi-Fi uses **802.11 amendments** (a/b/g/n/ac/ax) to evolve.
- ZigBee uses **public application profiles** (Home Automation, ZigBee Light Link, ZigBee IP).

**Examiner Pattern Alert:**
- Always write the **standard body** alongside the protocol name (Bluetooth SIG, IEEE 802.11, IEEE 802.15.4 + ZigBee Alliance).
- Always justify **why** a protocol is chosen for a given use case.
- Always draw **block diagrams** in 14-mark answers — answers without diagrams typically lose **2–3 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization and Calling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization and Calling

# Localization and Calling in Wireless Communication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic is the backbone of cellular mobility management. It answers two fundamental questions: *"Where is the user?"* (Localization) and *"How does a call reach that user?"* (Calling). Mastery of this topic is mandatory for ESE and Module-level assessments.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Localization** in wireless communication refers to the set of mechanisms a cellular network uses to **track, register, and update the current position of a Mobile Station (MS)** within the Public Land Mobile Network (PLMN). It is a subset of *Location Management* and is implemented through the combined operation of **Location Updating, IMSI Attach/Detach, and Paging** procedures.

**Calling** refers to the **end-to-end signaling and traffic channel allocation procedures** that establish, maintain, and release a voice or data session between two subscribers. Within the mobility context, it is classified into:

- **Mobile Originated Call (MOC):** Call initiated *from* the mobile user.
- **Mobile Terminated Call (MTC):** Call destined *to* the mobile user.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Snapshot:**
> - **LA (Location Area):** A group of cells identified by a unique *Location Area Identity (LAI)*.
> - **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent database of subscribers belonging to the network.
> - **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database of subscribers currently roaming in its area.
> - **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Core switch handling call routing, signaling, and inter-BSS coordination.
> - **IMSI (International Mobile Subscriber Identity):** Unique 15-digit permanent identity of a subscriber.
> - **TMSI (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity):** 4-byte alias assigned by VLR to protect subscriber identity over the air interface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the cellular network as a massive, distributed hotel chain:**

Imagine you are a guest (the **Mobile Station**) registered at the **Head Office** (the **HLR**) of a hotel chain. The Head Office only knows your *permanent home city*, not the specific hotel branch you are currently in. Now consider the following:

| Real-World Analogy | Wireless Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Checking into a hotel branch | **IMSI Attach / Location Update** to VLR |
| Hotel branch front desk (knows local guests) | **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** |
| Calling the hotel chain to find a guest | **HLR query during MTC** |
| Hotel staff paging speakers in the lobby | **Paging message on PCH (Paging Channel)** |
| Guest changing rooms | **Cell reselection (no signaling needed)** |
| Guest moving to a different hotel branch | **Location Update at LA boundary** |
| Dialling out from your room | **Mobile Originated Call (MOC)** |
| Receiving a call from outside | **Mobile Terminated Call (MTC)** |

This is exactly how GSM works. The network must *continuously know* in which Location Area (LA) you are, so when someone calls you, it can **page** only that LA instead of the entire country — saving enormous bandwidth and battery life.

### 1.3 Physical & System Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Localization:**
> - **LAI (Location Area Identity)** = MCC (3 digits) + MNC (2 digits) + LAC (up to 5 digits) → uniquely identifies an LA globally.
> - **IMSI length:** **15 digits** (max).
> - **TMSI length:** **4 octets (32 bits)** — assigned by VLR.
> - **MSISDN length:** **15 digits** (E.164 international format).
> - **Periodic Location Update timer (T3212):** typically broadcast by the network (range: 1 deci-hour to 255 deci-hours).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Localization

The localization framework rests on **three core procedures** that operate concurrently:

### Pillar 1 — IMSI Attach
- Executed when the MS **switches ON** and registers for the first time in a new VLR area.
- Carries the **IMSI** to the network so the VLR can mark the subscriber as *active*.
- Reverse procedure is **IMSI Detach**, executed at power-off.
- Significance: Prevents unnecessary paging of switched-off mobiles.

### Pillar 2 — Location Update
Triggered by **one of three events**:

1. **LA Boundary Crossing** — MS moves into a cell whose LAI differs from the stored LAI.
2. **Periodic Update** — Timer T3212 expires; MS re-registers to declare it is still alive (used for fault tolerance).
3. **IMSI Attach on Power-On** — If the LA has changed since last switch-off.

### Pillar 3 — Paging
- Executed by the network during a **Mobile Terminated Call (MTC)**.
- The MSC/VLR sends a **PAGING REQUEST** message on the **Paging Channel (PCH)** of *every cell* in the target LA.
- The MS monitors the PCH and, upon recognising its IMSI (or TMSI), responds on the **Random Access Channel (RACH)** with a **CHANNEL REQUEST**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LA-based paging instead of global paging?**
> Paging is the **most expensive downlink broadcast** in a cellular system. By restricting paging to one LA, the **paging load per cell is reduced by a factor of N**, where **N = (Total number of cells in PLMN) / (Number of cells in one LA)**. This is a classic KTU short-answer favourite.

## 2.2 The Calling Procedures

### 2.2.1 Mobile Originated Call (MOC)

The MOC flow proceeds as follows:

1. **User dials digits** and presses SEND.
2. MS requests a **signaling channel** → sends `CHANNEL REQUEST` on RACH.
3. Network allocates a **Standalone Dedicated Control Channel (SDCCH)** → responds with `IMMEDIATE ASSIGNMENT`.
4. MS establishes **SABM (Set Asynchronous Balanced Mode)** link on SDCCH.
5. **Service Request** + **CM Service Request** sent by MS to VLR.
6. **Authentication** and **Ciphering** are performed.
7. MS sends the **dialled digits (Setup message)** to MSC.
8. MSC analyzes the B-number, queries the **HLR** (if needed for routing).
9. **Traffic Channel (TCH) assignment** is done — MS moves from SDCCH to TCH.
10. **Call Connect / Connect Acknowledge** → speech path established.

### 2.2.2 Mobile Terminated Call (MTC)

The MTC flow is more complex because the network must *first find* the MS:

1. Gateway MSC (**GMSC**) receives the call from PSTN.
2. GMSC queries the **HLR** using **Send Routing Information (SRI)**.
3. HLR queries the **current VLR** using **Provide Roaming Number (PRN)**.
4. VLR allocates a **Mobile Station Roaming Number (MSRN)** and returns it to HLR → GMSC.
5. GMSC routes the call to the **visited MSC (VMSC)** using the MSRN.
6. VMSC initiates **Paging** in the last known LA of the MS.
7. MS responds on RACH → SDCCH allocated.
8. **Paging Response → Authentication → Ciphering → Setup message** delivered to MS.
9. MS rings, user answers → **Connect** sent.
10. **TCH assignment** → two-way speech path complete.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic for MTC — "S-R-P-R-C-P-S-A-C-T":**
> *SRI → Route to GMSC → PRN → Roaming Number returned → Call routed to VMSC → Paging → Setup → Acknowledge/Connect → TCH assigned.*

## 2.3 Roles of Network Entities

| Entity | Role in Localization | Role in Calling |
|---|---|---|
| **MS (Mobile Station)** | Stores LAI, TMSI; triggers Location Update on LA change | Initiates MOC; responds to Paging for MTC |
| **BTS (Base Transceiver Station)** | Broadcasts LAI on BCCH | Provides radio access (Um interface) |
| **BSC (Base Station Controller)** | Manages cell resources; forwards signaling | Allocates SDCCH/TCH; performs handover |
| **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)** | Coordinates Location Update with VLR | Switches the call; routes to PSTN/other PLMN |
| **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** | Stores current LA of MS, TMSI mapping | Provides MSRN; authenticates MS |
| **HLR (Home Location Register)** | Stores permanent subscriber data + current VLR address | Provides routing information via SRI query |
| **EIR (Equipment Identity Register)** | Checks IMEI validity (optional) | Not directly involved |
| **AuC (Authentication Center)** | Provides authentication vectors (Kc, SRES, RAND) | Authenticates MS during call setup |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the *exact* set of formulas and identifiers you must memorise for the KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Identifier | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LAI structure | $\text{LAI} = \text{MCC} \;\vert\mid\; \text{MNC} \;\vert\mid\; \text{LAC}$ | MCC: 3 digits, MNC: 2 digits, LAC: ≤ 5 digits |
| 2 | IMSI structure | $\text{IMSI} = \text{MCC} \;\vert\mid\; \text{MNC} \;\vert\mid\; \text{MSIN}$ | Max 15 digits (3 + 2 + ≤ 10) |
| 3 | MSISDN | E.164 format, 15 digits | Country code + National code + Subscriber number |
| 4 | TMSI | 4 octets | Assigned by VLR; valid only within VLR area |
| 5 | MSRN | E.164 format, similar to MSISDN | Allocated dynamically by VLR per call |
| 6 | Paging load reduction factor | $N = \dfrac{C_{total}}{C_{LA}}$ | $C_{total}$ = total cells, $C_{LA}$ = cells in one LA |
| 7 | Paging rate per cell | $P_c = \dfrac{\lambda_{MTC}}{C_{LA}}$ | $\lambda_{MTC}$ = MTC arrival rate |
| 8 | Total signaling cost (LU + Paging) | $C_{total} = C_{LU} \cdot \mu_{LA} + C_{P} \cdot P_c$ | $\mu_{LA}$ = LA crossing rate |
| 9 | SDCCH allocation time | Typically 1–2 seconds | For MOC / MTC setup |
| 10 | TCH handoff delay | 100–200 ms | After Connect message |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emergency Services (E-911 / E-112):** Localization accuracy directly affects response time.
- **Roaming & Inter-PLMN Handover:** Without robust location tracking, international roaming is impossible.
- **Load Balancing & Capacity Planning:** Operators size LA boundaries based on traffic distribution to minimise Location Update vs Paging trade-off (a classic optimization problem in KTU Module 4).
- **IoT and M2M:** Modern LPWAN systems (NB-IoT, LTE-M) reuse GSM's attach/update framework for power-efficient registration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Location Area Size (KTU Favourite Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Given a cellular network with uniform hexagonal cell layout, derive the **optimal LA size** that minimises the total signaling cost (Location Update + Paging).

### 3.1.1 Defining the Cost Function

Let:

- $\lambda_c$ = call arrival rate per MS (calls/sec)
- $v$ = average velocity of MS
- $L$ = perimeter of one cell (meters)
- $N_{cell}$ = number of cells per Location Area
- $\mu_{LA}$ = LA crossing rate per MS

**Step 1 — LA crossing rate per MS:**

For a mobile crossing the boundary of an LA, the rate is inversely proportional to the time spent within the LA. With $N_{cell}$ cells per LA:

$$\mu_{LA} = \frac{v \cdot P_{LA}}{A_{cell} \cdot N_{cell}}$$

where $P_{LA}$ is the perimeter of the LA and $A_{cell}$ is the area of one hexagonal cell. We approximate the LA as a larger hexagon containing $N_{cell}$ smaller cells.

**Step 2 — Paging rate per LA:**

Each MTC triggers a paging broadcast across all cells in the LA:

$$P_{LA} = \lambda_c \cdot N_{cell}$$

**Step 3 — Total signaling cost:**

Let $C_{LU}$ = cost (in signaling messages) of one Location Update, and $C_{P}$ = cost of paging one cell. Then:

$$C_{total} = C_{LU} \cdot \mu_{LA} + C_{P} \cdot P_{LA}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the expressions:**

$$C_{total} = C_{LU} \cdot \frac{v \cdot P_{LA}}{A_{cell} \cdot N_{cell}} + C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c \cdot N_{cell}$$

For a hexagonal layout, the perimeter scales as $\sqrt{N_{cell}}$:

$$P_{LA} \approx K \cdot \sqrt{N_{cell}}$$

Therefore:

$$C_{total} = C_{LU} \cdot \frac{v \cdot K \cdot \sqrt{N_{cell}}}{A_{cell} \cdot N_{cell}} + C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c \cdot N_{cell}$$

$$C_{total} = \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{A_{cell}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{cell}}} + C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c \cdot N_{cell}$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $N_{cell}$ to find optimum:**

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dN_{cell}} = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{A_{cell}} \cdot N_{cell}^{-3/2} + C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N_{cell}^{*}$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{A_{cell}} \cdot N_{cell}^{-3/2} = C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c$$

$$N_{cell}^{3/2} = \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{2 \cdot A_{cell} \cdot C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c}$$

$$\boxed{N_{cell}^{*} = \left( \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{2 \cdot A_{cell} \cdot C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c} \right)^{2/3}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The optimal LA size $N_{cell}^{*}$ is **inversely proportional to $\lambda_c$** (more calls → smaller LA) and **directly proportional to $v$** (faster mobiles → larger LA). This is the *velocity-vs-call-rate trade-off* that every KTU examiner loves to test.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style 7-mark problem)

> **Given:** $C_{LU} = 10$ messages, $C_{P} = 1$ message, $v = 30$ m/s, $K = 6 \cdot R$ (hexagonal perimeter constant with cell radius $R = 500$ m), $A_{cell} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} R^2$, $\lambda_c = 0.001$ calls/sec.

**Compute $A_{cell}$:**

$$A_{cell} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 500^2 = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 250000$$

$$A_{cell} = 649{,}519.05 \; m^2$$

**Compute $K \cdot v$:**

$$K \cdot v = (6 \times 500) \times 30 = 90{,}000$$

**Compute numerator term:**

$$C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K = 10 \times 90{,}000 = 900{,}000$$

**Compute denominator term:**

$$2 \cdot A_{cell} \cdot C_{P} \cdot \lambda_c = 2 \times 649{,}519.05 \times 1 \times 0.001 = 1299.04$$

**Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{900{,}000}{1299.04} \approx 692.83$$

**Raise to power 2/3:**

$$N_{cell}^{*} = (692.83)^{2/3} \approx 78.0$$

**Result:** The optimal LA should contain approximately **78 cells** — i.e., the network designer should partition the coverage area into LAs of roughly 78 cells each to balance Location Update cost and Paging cost.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Triggering Events and Network Responses

| Event | Trigger Source | Signaling Sent | VLR Action | HLR Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power ON (new LA)** | MS | IMSI Attach + LU Request | Create VLR record, assign TMSI | Update VLR pointer |
| **Power ON (same LA)** | MS | IMSI Attach only | Mark MS as active | None |
| **Power OFF** | MS | IMSI Detach | Mark MS as inactive | None |
| **LA boundary crossed** | MS | LU Request (with new LAI) | Update LA pointer; assign TMSI | None (LA-level change) |
| **T3212 timer expires** | MS | Periodic LU Request | Refresh last contact time | None |
| **Incoming MTC** | Network | Paging Request on PCH | Look up LA of MS | None (already knows VLR) |
| **MS moves to new MSC/VLR** | MS | LU Request (new VLR) | Create new VLR record | Update HLR with new VLR address |
| **MS returns home VLR** | MS | LU Request (home VLR) | Update TMSI | Optionally clear foreign VLR |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Mobile Terminated Call (MTC) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PSTN Caller] --> B[GMSC Gateway MSC]
    B --> C[HLR Home Location Register]
    C --> D{Send Routing Info SRI}
    D --> C
    C --> E[VLR Visitor Location Register]
    E --> F{Provide Roaming Number PRN}
    F --> E
    E --> G[Return MSRN to HLR]
    G --> C
    C --> B
    B --> H[VMSC Visited MSC]
    H --> I[Paging in all cells of target LA]
    I --> J[MS Mobile Station receives Paging]
    J --> K[MS responds on RACH]
    K --> L[SDCCH allocated]
    L --> M[Authentication and Ciphering]
    M --> N[Setup message delivered to MS]
    N --> O[MS rings user answers]
    O --> P[TCH Traffic Channel assigned]
    P --> Q[Two way speech path established]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show signaling and traffic flow. The two *self-loops* (B→C→B and C→E→C) represent the **two-stage database query** (SRI then PRN) that is characteristic of GSM's MTC routing.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Location Update (LA Boundary Crossing) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MS monitors BCCH for LAI] --> B{LAI changed from stored LAI}
    B -- No --> Z[Stay idle no signaling]
    B -- Yes --> C[MS sends Channel Request on RACH]
    C --> D[Network allocates SDCCH]
    D --> E[MS sends Location Update Request with new LAI and TMSI or IMSI]
    E --> F[BSC forwards to VLR]
    F --> G{MS known in VLR}
    G -- No --> H[VLR contacts HLR for authentication vectors]
    H --> I[Authentication challenge sent to MS]
    I --> J[MS computes SRES using Ki and RAND]
    J --> K{VLR verifies SRES}
    G -- Yes --> L[VLR verifies TMSI]
    K -- Fail --> X[Update Rejected]
    K -- Pass --> M[Ciphering mode set up]
    L --> M
    M --> N[VLR assigns new TMSI]
    N --> O[Location Update Accept sent to MS]
    O --> P[HLR updated with new VLR pointer if VLR changed]
    P --> Q[MS releases SDCCH returns to idle]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Reading Hint:** The decision diamond `{LAI changed from stored LAI}` is the *heart* of localization. The MS continuously checks BCCH (Broadcast Control Channel) — a fundamental exam point.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — MOC vs MTC Side-by-Side Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MOC[Mobile Originated Call]
        A1[MS dials digits] --> A2[Channel Request on RACH]
        A2 --> A3[SDCCH allocated]
        A3 --> A4[CM Service Request to VLR]
        A4 --> A5[Authentication Ciphering]
        A5 --> A6[Setup with dialled digits to MSC]
        A6 --> A7[MSC routes to called party]
        A7 --> A8[TCH assigned]
    end

    subgraph MTC[Mobile Terminated Call]
        B1[PSTN caller dials MSISDN] --> B2[GMSC queries HLR via SRI]
        B2 --> B3[HLR queries VLR via PRN]
        B3 --> B4[MSRN returned and routed to VMSC]
        B4 --> B5[VMSC pages MS in target LA]
        B5 --> B6[MS responds on RACH]
        B6 --> B7[SDCCH and authentication]
        B7 --> B8[Setup delivered to MS]
        B8 --> B9[MS answers TCH assigned]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram 4 — Functional Architecture of Localization Entities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UE[User Equipment Domain]
        MS[Mobile Station with SIM]
    end

    subgraph RAN[Radio Access Network Domain]
        BTS1[BTS Cell A]
        BTS2[BTS Cell B]
        BTS3[BTS Cell C]
        BSC[Base Station Controller BSC]
    end

    subgraph CN[Core Network Domain]
        MSC[Mobile Switching Center MSC]
        VLR[Visitor Location Register VLR]
        HLR[Home Location Register HLR]
        AUC[Authentication Center AuC]
        EIR[Equipment Identity Register EIR]
    end

    MS -- Um radio interface --> BTS1
    MS -- Um radio interface --> BTS2
    MS -- Um radio interface --> BTS3
    BTS1 --> BSC
    BTS2 --> BSC
    BTS3 --> BSC
    BSC -- A bis interface --> MSC
    MSC <--> VLR
    MSC <--> HLR
    HLR <--> AUC
    MSC <--> EIR
    MSC -- PSTN gateway --> PSTN[(PSTN and ISDN)]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Block-Diagram Note:** Always label the interfaces (Um, Abis, A) when drawing this architecture. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for correct interface naming.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 3 marks — direct conceptual questions (Answer in 3–4 lines).
> - **Part B:** 14 marks — in-depth analytical questions with sub-parts (7 + 7). Internal choice mandatory.

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Location Update. Why is it necessary in a cellular network?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Location Update is the procedure by which a Mobile Station (MS) informs the serving VLR/MSC that it has changed its Location Area (LA) or that a periodic registration timer (T3212) has expired.

It is necessary because:

1. The network must always know the **current LA of the MS** to deliver incoming calls (MTC) via paging.
2. It enables **roaming** across cells and MSCs.
3. It supports **fault tolerance** through periodic updates — a silent MS can be declared unreachable.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons — 3 marks total.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the role of TMSI in GSM? Why is it preferred over IMSI for over-the-air transmission?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TMSI (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity) is a **4-octet alias** assigned by the VLR to identify an MS within its coverage area.

It is preferred over IMSI because:

1. **Security / Privacy:** TMSI prevents eavesdroppers from tracking the subscriber's permanent identity and location.
2. **Efficiency:** TMSI is shorter and quicker to transmit than the 15-digit IMSI.
3. **Frequent change:** VLR can re-allocate a new TMSI after every Location Update, breaking tracking continuity.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three advantages — 3 marks total.)*

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Mobile Terminated Call (MTC) procedure** in GSM. List all the signaling messages exchanged between PSTN, GMSC, HLR, VLR, and the MS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **Location Update procedure** when an MS moves to a new Location Area. Explain the role of TMSI reallocation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) MTC Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — PSTN Initiates the Call (1 mark)**
The PSTN caller dials the **MSISDN** of the GSM subscriber. The PSTN routes the call to the **GMSC** (Gateway MSC), which is the entry point into the PLMN.

**Step 2 — HLR Query via SRI (1.5 marks)**
The GMSC does not know where the MS is currently located. It sends a **Send Routing Information (SRI)** request to the **HLR**, including the MSISDN.

**Step 3 — VLR Query via PRN (1.5 marks)**
The HLR looks up its database to find the **current VLR address** for the MS. It then sends a **Provide Roaming Number (PRN)** request to that VLR.

**Step 4 — MSRN Allocation and Return (1 mark)**
The VLR dynamically allocates a **Mobile Station Roaming Number (MSRN)** (E.164 format) and returns it to the HLR, which forwards it to the GMSC.

**Step 5 — Call Routing to VMSC (0.5 mark)**
The GMSC uses the MSRN to route the call to the **Visited MSC (VMSC)** serving the MS's current LA.

**Step 6 — Paging (1 mark)**
The VMSC instructs all BTSs in the target LA to broadcast a **PAGING REQUEST** on the PCH containing the MS's TMSI (or IMSI).

**Step 7 — MS Response and Call Setup (0.5 mark)**
The MS monitors the PCH, recognises its identity, and responds on the RACH. SDCCH is allocated, **Authentication + Ciphering** is performed, and the **SETUP** message is delivered to the MS.

**Result:** Two-way speech path is established on a **TCH (Traffic Channel)**.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Location Update Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — LAI Monitoring (1 mark)**
The MS continuously monitors the **BCCH (Broadcast Control Channel)** of the serving cell. The BCCH broadcasts the **LAI (Location Area Identity)** of the cell.

**Step 2 — Detection of LA Change (1 mark)**
The MS compares the broadcast LAI with the **stored LAI** in its SIM. If different, the MS initiates a Location Update.

**Step 3 — Channel Request (0.5 mark)**
The MS sends a `CHANNEL REQUEST` on the **RACH**, requesting a signaling channel.

**Step 4 — SDCCH Allocation (0.5 mark)**
The BSC allocates a **Standalone Dedicated Control Channel (SDCCH)** and sends `IMMEDIATE ASSIGNMENT`.

**Step 5 — Location Update Request (1 mark)**
The MS sends a `LOCATION UPDATE REQUEST` to the VLR, containing the old LAI, the new LAI, and either the TMSI or IMSI.

**Step 6 — Authentication (1 mark)**
If the MS is unknown to the VLR, the VLR fetches authentication vectors (RAND, SRES, Kc) from the **AuC** via the HLR. The MS computes SRES using its **Ki** stored in the SIM, and the VLR verifies it.

**Step 7 — Ciphering and TMSI Reallocation (1.5 marks)**
The VLR commands the MS to start **ciphering** using Kc. The VLR then **allocates a new TMSI** to the MS (TMSI reallocation) for enhanced security and tracking resistance. The new TMSI is sent to the MS via `LOCATION UPDATE ACCEPT` encrypted under the ciphering mode.

**Step 8 — HLR Update (0.5 mark)**
If the new LA belongs to a **different VLR**, the HLR is informed of the new VLR address; otherwise, only the LA pointer within the current VLR is updated.

**Result:** The MS releases the SDCCH and returns to idle mode, now registered in the new LA.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Define **Localization** in wireless networks. Explain the difference between **Location Update** and **Paging**. Derive an expression for the **optimal Location Area size** that minimizes the total signaling cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Mobile Originated Call (MOC) procedure** in detail. Compare MOC and MTC flows in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Optimal LA Size (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Localization is the process of tracking the current Location Area (LA) of a Mobile Station through a combination of **Location Updates, IMSI Attach/Detach, and Paging**.

**Difference between Location Update and Paging (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Location Update | Paging |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | MS → Network | Network → MS |
| **Trigger** | LA boundary crossing, periodic timer, power ON | Incoming call (MTC) for the MS |
| **Channel** | RACH → SDCCH | PCH (broadcast) |
| **Frequency** | Event-driven; depends on mobility | Once per incoming call |
| **Cost driver** | Mobility rate ($\mu_{LA}$) | Call arrival rate ($\lambda_c$) |

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $C_{LU}$ = cost of one Location Update, $C_P$ = cost of paging one cell, $\lambda_c$ = call rate, $v$ = MS velocity, and $N_{cell}$ = cells per LA.

The total signaling cost is:

$$C_{total} = C_{LU} \cdot \mu_{LA} + C_P \cdot P_{LA}$$

For a hexagonal layout, $\mu_{LA} = \dfrac{v \cdot K}{A_{cell} \cdot \sqrt{N_{cell}}}$ and $P_{LA} = \lambda_c \cdot N_{cell}$. Substituting:

$$C_{total} = \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{A_{cell}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{cell}}} + C_P \cdot \lambda_c \cdot N_{cell}$$

Differentiating and equating to zero:

$$\boxed{N_{cell}^{*} = \left( \frac{C_{LU} \cdot v \cdot K}{2 \cdot A_{cell} \cdot C_P \cdot \lambda_c} \right)^{2/3}}$$

This is the optimal LA size that minimises the combined cost of updates and paging.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) MOC Procedure and Comparison (7 marks)

**MOC Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **User dials the B-number** and presses SEND on the MS.
2. The MS sends a `CHANNEL REQUEST` on the **RACH** to acquire a signaling channel.
3. The BSC allocates an **SDCCH** via `IMMEDIATE ASSIGNMENT`.
4. The MS sends a **CM Service Request** to the VLR.
5. **Authentication** is performed using RAND/SRES challenge-response.
6. **Ciphering** is initiated using the session key Kc.
7. The MS sends the **SETUP** message containing the dialled digits to the MSC.
8. The MSC analyzes the digits and routes the call to the **called party** (could be PSTN, ISDN, or another GSM subscriber).
9. The BSC reallocates a **TCH (Traffic Channel)** for the MS, releasing the SDCCH.
10. **Call Connect / Connect Acknowledge** is exchanged and the speech path is opened.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Step / Aspect | MOC (Mobile Originated) | MTC (Mobile Terminated) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Initiation | MS dials digits | PSTN dials MSISDN |
| 2. Network entry | MS → VLR via SDCCH | GMSC queries HLR |
| 3. Routing info | Dialled digits in SETUP | SRI → PRN → MSRN |
| 4. Page required? | No | Yes (PCH broadcast) |
| 5. Channels used | RACH, SDCCH, TCH | RACH, SDCCH, PCH, TCH |
| 6. Database queries | VLR (local) | HLR + VLR (remote) |
| 7. Complexity | Lower (single network) | Higher (multi-stage query) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Losing Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LAI with CI:** LAI identifies a *group of cells*; CI (Cell Identity) identifies *one cell*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention PCH:** When describing paging, students often say "the network contacts the MS" without specifying the **Paging Channel (PCH)**. This costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping Ciphering:** Authentication and Ciphering are *both* mandatory before SETUP. Omitting ciphering → −1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong MSRN role:** MSRN is *temporary* (allocated per call) — students often confuse it with MSISDN (permanent). Deduct 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing arrows correctly:** In MTC flow diagrams, students forget the **return path** of MSRN from VLR → HLR → GMSC. This costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting interface names (Um, Abis, A):** Any architecture diagram without interface labels loses 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise This Before the Exam:**

- **Localization** = Location Update + IMSI Attach/Detach + Paging.
- **MOC** = call *from* mobile; **MTC** = call *to* mobile.
- **HLR** stores *permanent* subscriber data + current VLR pointer.
- **VLR** stores *temporary* data (current LA, TMSI) for roamers.
- **MSRN** is allocated *per call*; **TMSI** is allocated *per VLR visit*; **IMSI** is *permanent*.
- **LAI = MCC + MNC + LAC**; broadcast on BCCH by every BTS.
- **Paging is on PCH**; **initial access is on RACH**; **signaling is on SDCCH**; **voice/data is on TCH**.
- **Authentication** uses RAND-SRES-Kc triplets (triplets) from AuC.
- **Ciphering** uses A5/1, A5/2, or A5/3 algorithm with the session key Kc.
- **MTC requires SRI → PRN → MSRN** → a *two-stage* database query.
- **Periodic LU timer** = T3212, broadcast by the network; prevents stale entries.
- **Optimal LA size** $N_{cell}^{*} \propto (v / \lambda_c)^{2/3}$ — fast mobiles and low call rates → larger LAs.
- **TMSI reallocation** happens at every Location Update; enhances subscriber identity confidentiality.
- **Paging load reduction** = factor of $N$ when LA paging is used instead of global paging.
- Always label **interfaces (Um, Abis, A, E, D, G)** in architecture diagrams.
- **IMSI Attach** = MS power ON; **IMSI Detach** = MS power OFF; both reduce wastage of paging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Definition Bank (for 1-mark sub-questions):**
> - *Location Area (LA):* A group of cells identified by a common LAI.
> - *Paging:* Broadcasting a call-notification message on the PCH of all cells in a target LA.
> - *Roaming:* The ability of an MS to receive service outside its home PLMN.
> - *TMSI:* A temporary, VLR-allocated alias for IMSI used over the air interface.
> - *MSRN:* A temporary E.164 number allocated by VLR to route an incoming call to the visited MSC.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4 / Topic: Localization and Calling**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handover
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Handover

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Handover (Handoff)** is the mechanism that allows an ongoing active call or data session of a **Mobile Station (MS)** to be transferred from one **Base Station (BS)** / **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)** / **Node B** to another, or from one channel/sector to another within the same cell, **without interrupting the communication session**.

In GSM terminology this is called **Handover**, while in CDMA/UMTS/LTE literature it is often referred to as **Handoff**. The KTU 2024 syllabus uses the term *Handover* uniformly.

The formal IEEE definition:  
*Handover is the process by which the radio network controller (or base station subsystem) reassigns a mobile user's radio link from one physical channel to another, triggered by degradations in signal quality, mobility of the user, or load balancing requirements.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (The Toll-Booth Analogy):**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. There are toll booths operated by different agencies. When you approach a city border, you cannot suddenly stop the engine while switching toll operators — your journey must continue smoothly. The highway system has a "merge lane" where the new booth takes over your billing while the old one gracefully hands you off. The **toll booths = Base Stations**, **your car = Mobile Station**, and the **smooth billing switch = Handover**.
> The key engineering challenge is to make this switch *seamless, fast, and unnoticeable* to the user (so they don't drop a phone call while driving).

## 1.3 Why Handover is Necessary

- **Mobility**: MS moves out of current cell coverage.
- **Signal Degradation**: RSS drops below threshold due to path loss, fading, shadowing.
- **Cell Reselection**: Capacity reasons — a heavily loaded cell offloads users to less-loaded neighbouring cells.
- **Interference Avoidance**: A new cell offers a better C/I ratio.
- **Operational Reasons**: Hardware failure, antenna re-tilt, power reduction in a cell.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Handover Decisions (Will be referenced throughout)

| Metric | Symbol | Threshold Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| Received Signal Strength | **RSS** | Drop below $P_{r,\min}$ |
| Carrier-to-Interference Ratio | **C/I** | Drop below $(C/I)_{threshold}$ |
| Bit Error Rate | **BER** | Rise above $BER_{max}$ |
| Distance from BS | $d$ | Exceeds $d_{max}$ |
| Pilot Signal Level (in CDMA) | $E_c/I_o$ | Drop below threshold |
| Load of Target Cell | $\rho$ | Exceeds capacity |

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Highlights & Board Emphasis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourites on Handover (Recall 2-3 markers):**
> 1. Difference between **Hard Handover** and **Soft Handover**.
> 2. **Handover Margin ($H_m$)** calculation — appears almost every semester in numerical questions.
> 3. Phases of Handover (Measurement → Decision → Execution).
> 4. **Handover in GSM vs Handover in CDMA/UMTS** — direct comparison question.
> 5. Role of **MAHO (Mobile Assisted Handover)** in modern systems.
> 6. **Softer Handover** (intra-Node B) and its distinction from soft handover.
> 7. Reasons for handover failure — *Ping-Pong effect*, *too-late handover*, *too-early handover*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Handover Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The handover process is universally divided into **three logical phases** (KTU favourite, frequently asked):

### Phase 1: Measurement Phase
- The **MS continuously monitors** the **BCCH (Broadcast Control Channel)** of neighbouring cells.
- MS measures **RSS (Received Signal Strength)** from current serving cell and from up to **6 neighbours** in GSM.
- These measurement reports are sent to the **BSC (Base Station Controller)** every **480 ms** (typical SACCH reporting period in GSM).
- The BSC also measures the uplink RSS from MS.

### Phase 2: Decision Phase
- BSC compares: $RSS_{serving} - RSS_{neighbour} \le H_m$ (Handover Margin).
- If condition is met for a duration ≥ **hysteresis timer** → handover is triggered.
- Decision is handed to the **MSC (Mobile Switching Centre)** for inter-BSC or inter-MSC cases.

### Phase 3: Execution Phase
- BSC/BTS reserves a channel in the target cell.
- Old connection is released and new connection is established.
- The MS is informed via a **Handover Command** on the **FACCH (Fast Associated Control Channel)**.
- Mobile tunes to new frequency/time-slot (GSM) or adds a new leg (UMTS soft handover).

## 2.2 Types of Handover — The Core Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Always draw the timing diagram and explain *break-before-make* vs *make-before-break* when distinguishing Hard vs Soft handover.

### 2.2.1 Hard Handover ("Break-Before-Make")
- Used in **GSM, TDMA, LTE** (intrafrequency, intra-eNB).
- MS is connected to **only one BS at a time**.
- Old link is **broken first**, then new link is established.
- **Advantage**: Simple, only one channel used, no diversity combining complexity.
- **Disadvantage**: Brief interruption (50-200 ms in GSM); risk of **ping-pong handover** at cell boundary.
- **Frequency reuse**: Tight reuse possible.

### 2.2.2 Soft Handover ("Make-Before-Break")
- Used in **CDMA, WCDMA, UMTS**.
- MS is connected to **2 or more BS simultaneously** during transition.
- New link is established *before* old link is released.
- **Advantage**: No interruption, **macro-diversity gain**, smoother transition.
- **Disadvantage**: Wastes channel capacity, requires sophisticated receiver (Rake).

### 2.2.3 Softer Handover (Special case of Soft)
- Occurs when MS is in the **overlap of two sectors of the SAME Node-B**.
- Combining happens at the **Node-B** itself (before backhaul signalling).
- Lower signalling overhead than inter-Node-B soft handover.

### 2.2.4 Inter-System Handover
- Between **different RATs** (Radio Access Technologies), e.g., GSM ↔ UMTS, UMTS ↔ LTE (vertical handover).

### 2.2.5 Idle Mode Handover (Cell Reselection)
- MS is not in active call; it simply re-selects the best cell for camping.
- Triggered by **C1/C2 criteria** in GSM, **S-criterion** in LTE.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield. Memorise units & boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_m = P_{r,\text{threshold}} - P_{r,\text{desired}}$ | Handover Margin (difference between threshold and desired received power) | dB |
| 2 | $P_r(d)[dBm] = P_t[dBm] + G_t + G_r - L(d)$ | Received power from a transmitter at distance d | dBm |
| 3 | $L(d) = L_0 + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | Log-distance path loss model | dB |
| 4 | $H_{m, \text{avg}} = H_m + \Delta H$ | Handover margin with hysteresis (ΔH) to avoid ping-pong | dB |
| 5 | $T_{\text{TTT}}$ (Time-To-Trigger) | Duration the condition must persist before handover | ms |
| 6 | $\text{SINR} = \dfrac{P_s}{\sum_i I_i + N_0}$ | Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (used in LTE Handover decisions) | dB |
| 7 | $E_c/I_o$ | Chip-energy-to-Interference ratio (CDMA pilot strength) | dB |
| 8 | $N_{\text{legs}} = \text{number of active BS in SHO}$ | Active set size (UMTS) | integer |
| 9 | $\text{GOS} = f(\lambda, \mu, c)$ | Grade of Service — affected by handover traffic | Erlang |
| 10 | $P_{r,\min} = \text{receiver sensitivity}$ | Minimum acceptable RSS (e.g., −104 dBm in GSM) | dBm |
| 11 | $\dfrac{(C/I)_{\text{old}}}{(C/I)_{\text{new}}} = 1$ at HO | Handover occurs when C/I of neighbour equals C/I of serving | ratio |
| 12 | $\text{HO Fail Rate} = \Pr(RSS < P_{r,\min} \mid \text{during HO})$ | Probability handover fails | probability |

> **CRITICAL CONSTRAINT:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (not the keyboard pipe `|`) inside markdown tables. All LaTeX blocks above comply.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cellular Telephony**: Prevents dropped calls for users in cars, trains.
- **IoT & V2X**: Autonomous vehicles depend on seamless handover between RSUs (Road-Side Units).
- **Satellite–Terrestrial Integration**: Handover between LEO satellite and 5G terrestrial network.
- **Hospitality/Wi-Fi Calling**: Voice over Wi-Fi hands off between APs.
- **Mission-Critical Networks**: Public safety (Police/Fire) — zero tolerance for call drop → drives ultra-low-latency handover in 5G (sub-10 ms target).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Handover Margin (H_m) — KTU's Favourite Numerical

### Problem Setup
A mobile station is moving away from BS-A (serving) and towards BS-B (target). The receiver sensitivity is $P_{r,\min}$ (dBm). The handover should occur when the serving cell's RSS becomes marginally inadequate but the target's RSS is sufficient.

### Step 1: Define received power from each BS at the MS
$$
P_{r,A}(t) = P_{t,A} - L_{A}(t)
$$
$$
P_{r,B}(t) = P_{t,B} - L_{B}(t)
$$

where $L_{A}(t)$, $L_{B}(t)$ are path losses (in dB) at time $t$.

### Step 2: Define minimum acceptable received power
$$
P_{r,\min} \; \text{(dBm)} = \text{Receiver Sensitivity}
$$
For GSM900 typical value: $P_{r,\min} = -104\ \text{dBm}$.

### Step 3: Handover is triggered when
$$
P_{r,A} < P_{r,\min}
$$
But this alone is too late — the call may already be degraded. So we define:
$$
H_m = P_{r,A} - P_{r,\min} \quad \text{(at the moment of handover)}
$$

### Step 4: Express in terms of path loss
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m &= P_{r,A} - P_{r,\min} \\
&= \big(P_{t,A} - L_A\big) - P_{r,\min}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Given:** $P_{t,A} = 33$ dBm, $L_A = 130$ dB, $P_{r,\min} = -104$ dBm, $H_m = 6$ dB.
> **Find:** $L_B$ (path loss to target) at the handover instant.

**Step 1:** Compute $P_{r,A}$ at handover.
$$
P_{r,A} = P_{r,\min} + H_m = -104 + 6 = -98\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 2:** Use $P_{r,A} = P_{t,A} - L_A$ to verify.
$$
L_A = P_{t,A} - P_{r,A} = 33 - (-98) = 131\ \text{dB}\ \checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** Assuming $P_{t,B} = 33$ dBm and the target should provide at least $P_{r,\min}$:
$$
L_B = P_{t,B} - P_{r,\min} = 33 - (-104) = 137\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4:** So at the handover instant, the target's path loss must satisfy:
$$
L_B \le 137\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 6: Handover Margin with Hysteresis
$$
H_{m,\text{eff}} = H_m + \Delta H
$$
where $\Delta H$ (typically **3-6 dB**) prevents **ping-pong handover** at the cell boundary.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Handover Decision Boundary Distance

### Setup
Assume free-space path loss between MS and BS:
$$
L(d) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)
$$
where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

### Step 1: At handover instant, $P_{r,\text{serving}} = P_{r,\min} + H_m$

### Step 2: Express serving-cell path loss
$$
L_A = P_{t,A} - P_{r,\min} - H_m
$$

### Step 3: Solve for distance
$$
\begin{aligned}
32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_A) + 20\log_{10}(f) &= P_{t,A} - P_{r,\min} - H_m \\
20\log_{10}(d_A) &= P_{t,A} - P_{r,\min} - H_m - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(f) \\
d_A &= 10^{\dfrac{P_{t,A} - P_{r,\min} - H_m - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(f)}{20}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Units check:** $P_{t,A}$ in dBm, $P_{r,\min}$ in dBm, $f$ in MHz, output $d_A$ in **km**.

### Step 4: Numerical Illustration
- $P_{t,A} = 30$ dBm, $P_{r,\min} = -100$ dBm, $H_m = 5$ dB, $f = 900$ MHz.
- $20\log_{10}(900) = 59.08$ dB.

$$
20\log_{10}(d_A) = 30 - (-100) - 5 - 32.44 - 59.08 = 33.48
$$
$$
d_A = 10^{33.48/20} = 10^{1.674} \approx 47.2\ \text{km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A larger **$H_m$** → handover occurs **earlier** (at smaller $d_A$ from serving BS). This is a key insight tested in viva: increasing handover margin reduces drop-call probability but increases ping-pong and signalling load.

## 3.3 Soft Handover — Active Set Update (UMTS Logic)

### Step 1: MS measures pilot $E_c/I_o$ from all candidate BSs.

### Step 2: Active set $\mathcal{A}$ is maintained with three thresholds:

| Event | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Add (Radio Link Addition)** | $E_c/I_o^{\text{new}} > T_{\text{add}}$ (typically −11 dB) AND stronger than serving by 3 dB | Add new BS to active set |
| **Drop (Radio Link Removal)** | $E_c/I_o^{\text{old}} < T_{\text{drop}}$ (typically −19 dB) for time $T_{\text{drop\_timer}}$ | Remove from active set |
| **Replace** | Better candidate while active set full | Swap weakest in |

### Step 3: Active set size: $|\mathcal{A}| \le 3$ (typical UMTS cap).

### Step 4: Combining ratio at Rake receiver
$$
r_{\text{combined}} = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{A}} w_i \cdot r_i
$$
with $w_i = \dfrac{P_{r,i}}{\sum_j P_{r,j}}$ (Maximum Ratio Combining weights).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favourite Table for 14-Mark Answers)

| Feature | Hard Handover | Soft Handover | Softer Handover |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Break/Make** | Break-Before-Make | Make-Before-Break | Make-Before-Break |
| **# of simultaneous links** | 1 | ≥ 2 (different Node-B) | ≥ 2 (same Node-B sectors) |
| **Switching Mechanism** | Frequency/timeslot change | New leg added | Sector change within Node-B |
| **Systems** | GSM, LTE | WCDMA/UMTS | WCDMA/UMTS |
| **Diversity** | None | Macro-diversity | Macro-diversity |
| **Combining Site** | N/A | RNC (network) | Node-B (local) |
| **Signalling Overhead** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Ping-pong Risk** | High | Low | Low |
| **Call Drop Risk** | Higher | Lower | Lowest |
| **Capacity Impact** | Neutral | Reduced (extra BS load) | Reduced |
| **Latency** | ~80-200 ms (GSM) | ~0 (no break) | ~0 (no break) |

## 3.5 Causes of Handover Failure — Derivation Insight

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** *"What is ping-pong handover? When does it occur and how is it prevented?"*

A ping-pong handover occurs when the MS keeps handing over between two cells rapidly. The probability is modelled as:
$$
P_{\text{pp}} = \Pr\big( |RSS_A - RSS_B| < \Delta H \text{ within } T_{\text{TTT}} \big)
$$

**Prevention:**
1. **Hysteresis margin $\Delta H$** (3-6 dB).
2. **Time-To-Trigger $T_{\text{TTT}}$** — condition must persist for a minimum duration (e.g., 0.5 s in LTE).
3. **Penalties to current cell** in reselection criteria (C2 = C1 + cell-reselect offset − temporary offset).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Handover Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MS: Continuous RSS Measurement] --> B{Serving RSS below threshold?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[MS reports to BS / BSC]
    C --> D[BSC: Compare RSS_serving vs RSS_neighbour]
    D --> E{ΔRSS <= Handover Margin?}
    E -- No --> A
    E -- Yes --> F[Check Hysteresis Timer / TTT]
    F --> G{Condition sustained?}
    G -- No --> A
    G -- Yes --> H[BSC: Trigger Handover Request to MSC]
    H --> I[Target BTS: Reserve Channel]
    I --> J[MSC: Handover Command to MS]
    J --> K{Type of Handover?}
    K -- Hard --> L1[Break old link, Tune to new frequency]
    K -- Soft --> L2[Add new leg, Rake combining]
    K -- Softer --> L3[Combine at same Node-B]
    L1 --> M[MS: Update active set / frequency]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M --> N[Call continues seamlessly]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Handover Trigger Logic (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parameter 1: RSS] --> Q1{Quality degrading?}
    P2[Parameter 2: C/I] --> Q1
    P3[Parameter 3: BER] --> Q1
    P4[Parameter 4: Distance] --> Q1
    P5[Parameter 5: Load] --> Q2{Load balancing needed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R[Initiate Measurement Phase]
    Q2 -- Yes --> R
    R --> S[Apply Hysteresis + TTT]
    S --> T{Decision Threshold Met?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Execute Handover]
    T -- No --> P1
```

## 4.3 Soft Handover Active Set Management (UMTS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[MS Detects New Pilot] --> A2{Pilot > T_add?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Active Set Full?]
    A2 -- No --> B1{Pilot < T_drop for T_drop_timer?}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Add to Active Set: SHO leg N+1]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5{New Pilot > Weakest in AS by Δ AS_Th?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Replace Weakest Leg]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Reject Addition]
    A4 --> Rake[Rake Combiner - Maximum Ratio]
    A6 --> Rake
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Drop Leg from Active Set]
    B1 -- No --> Rake
    A7 --> Rake
    B2 --> Rake
    Rake --> Out[Decoded Output]
```

## 4.4 Handover Architecture — GSM Component Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MS[Mobile Station] -- Uplink RSSI --> BTS_S[Serving BTS]
    BTS_S --> BSC_S[Serving BSC]
    MS -. Neighbour BCCH Measurements .-> BTS_T[Target BTS]
    BSC_S -- Handover Request --> MSC[Mobile Switching Centre]
    MSC -- Channel Allocation --> BSC_T[Target BSC]
    BSC_T --> BTS_T
    MSC -- Handover Command --> BSC_S
    BSC_S -- HO Command via FACCH --> MS
    MS -- New frequency/slot --> BTS_T
```

## 4.5 Hard vs Soft Handover — Timing Diagram (Schematic ASCII)

```
HARD HANDOVER (GSM):
  Time ---> 
  Old BS  |#######|..............  (active)        (released)
  New BS  |.............|#######  (idle)            (active)
                         ^
                  t_HO (break)

SOFT HANDOVER (UMTS):
  Time ---> 
  BS1     |####################.....|  (active then dropped)
  BS2     |.....|##################| (added, then continues)
                 ^
           t_add (make-before-break)
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid above uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (`A`, `B`, `A1`, `BTS_S` etc.). No reserved keywords used. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Handover. List four reasons why handover is triggered in a cellular network.  
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Handover (or Handoff) is the procedure by which an active communication session of a Mobile Station (MS) is transferred from one Base Station (BS) / cell to another without noticeable interruption of the call.
2. **Four Reasons (½ mark each):**
   - **RSS degradation** when the mobile moves out of the current cell.
   - **C/I (Carrier-to-Interference) ratio** falling below threshold due to co-channel interference.
   - **Cell capacity / load balancing** — current cell overloaded, user offloaded to less-loaded neighbour.
   - **Operational reasons** — equipment failure, antenna reconfiguration, channel reassignment.

### Question 2: Distinguish between Hard Handover and Soft Handover.  
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Handover | Soft Handover |
|---|---|---|
| Link handling | Break-before-make (1) | Make-before-break (1) |
| Active BS count | One at a time (0.5) | Two or more simultaneously (0.5) |
| Systems | GSM, LTE (0.5) | UMTS/CDMA (0.5) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **phases of handover** in a GSM cellular system. Discuss the role of **MAHO (Mobile Assisted Handover)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GSM system operates at **900 MHz**. The transmitter power is **30 dBm** and the receiver sensitivity is **−104 dBm**. Handover is initiated when the received signal drops by a margin of **6 dB** below sensitivity. Using the free-space path loss model, determine the **distance from the serving BTS at which handover occurs**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)
1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) — Measurement → Decision → Execution phases, with explicit mention of MS, BTS, BSC, MSC.
2. **Phase-wise explanation (3 marks):**
   - *Measurement Phase:* MS measures RSS of serving + up to 6 neighbour BCCHs; reports every 480 ms on SACCH.
   - *Decision Phase:* BSC applies handover algorithm: $RSS_{serving} - RSS_{neighbour} \le H_m$ for duration $T_{TTT}$.
   - *Execution Phase:* Channel reserved, HO Command sent on FACCH, MS retunes, call continues.
3. **MAHO (2 marks):** *Mobile Assisted Handover* means the **MS itself** performs downlink measurements of neighbour cells and reports them to the network, reducing the load on the BTS and enabling faster decisions. Standard in GSM, UMTS, LTE.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)
**Given:** $P_t = 30$ dBm, $P_{r,\min} = -104$ dBm, $H_m = 6$ dB, $f = 900$ MHz.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Received power at handover instant.
$$
P_{r,\text{HO}} = P_{r,\min} + H_m = -104 + 6 = -98\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Free-space path loss formula.
$$
L_{fs}(d)[dB] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d[km]) + 20\log_{10}(f[MHz])
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $20\log_{10}(f)$.
$$
20\log_{10}(900) = 20 \times 2.9542 = 59.08\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total path loss at handover.
$$
L_{fs} = P_t - P_{r,\text{HO}} = 30 - (-98) = 128\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Solve for distance $d$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
128 &= 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 59.08 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &= 128 - 32.44 - 59.08 = 36.48 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 1.824 \\
d &= 10^{1.824} \approx 66.7\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion.
> The handover is triggered when the MS is approximately **66.7 km** from the serving BTS (free-space assumption). In real urban environments, $d$ will be much smaller due to additional path loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain **Hard Handover, Soft Handover, and Softer Handover** with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **causes of handover failure** in cellular systems. What is the **ping-pong effect** and how is it prevented? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)
1. **Hard Handover (2 marks):** Break-before-make mechanism; used in GSM, LTE. The MS breaks its current connection with the old BS *before* establishing a new one. **Advantage:** Simple hardware, single channel. **Disadvantage:** Brief interruption (~80-200 ms), ping-pong risk.
2. **Soft Handover (2 marks):** Make-before-break; used in UMTS/WCDMA. MS maintains simultaneous connections with ≥ 2 Node-Bs during transition. **Advantage:** No call drop, macro-diversity gain. **Disadvantage:** High resource usage, Rake receiver complexity.
3. **Softer Handover (1.5 marks):** A special case where MS is in the overlap of **two sectors of the same Node-B**. Combining happens at the Node-B (not RNC), saving backhaul signalling.
4. **Comparison Table (1.5 marks):** Refer to Section 3.4 Analytical Matrix.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)
1. **Causes of Handover Failure (3 marks):**
   - **Ping-pong effect** — rapid back-and-forth handover.
   - **Too-late handover** — MS loses radio link before HO completes.
   - **Channel scarcity** in target cell — no idle channel when needed.
   - **Signalling failure** — HO Command not received.
   - **Hardware failure** of target BTS.
2. **Ping-Pong Effect (2 marks):** When the MS oscillates between two adjacent cells because the RSS of both are nearly equal, causing repeated handovers. Causes wasted signalling, possible call drops, and increased network load.
3. **Prevention (2 marks):**
   - **Hysteresis margin $\Delta H$** (3-6 dB) — requires target's RSS to exceed serving's RSS by $\Delta H$ before HO is triggered.
   - **Time-To-Trigger $T_{TTT}$** — condition must persist for a minimum duration.
   - **Cell reselection offsets / penalties** in idle mode (e.g., C2 criterion in GSM).
   - **Load-aware algorithms** to prevent unnecessary handovers at cell edges.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2-3 Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** (dBm vs dB vs mW) — examiners strictly deduct.
> 2. **Not drawing block diagram** in 7-mark questions on handover phases — minimum **2 marks reserved** for diagram.
> 3. **Confusing Soft and Softer Handover** — soft = different Node-Bs, softer = same Node-B sectors.
> 4. **Writing "Handoff" in an exam that asked for "Handover"** — KTU 2024 syllabus uses "Handover"; minor deductions possible.
> 5. **In C/I based handover questions**, students forget to convert between linear and dB — penalty 1 mark.
> 6. **Missing the $T_{TTT}$ (Time-To-Trigger) concept** in LTE/5G handover — frequently skipped answer.
> 7. **Incorrect formula for free-space path loss** — sometimes students use $20\log_{10}(f)$ but forget to convert MHz to Hz. The KTU formula is: $L = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this for last-night exam prep):**
> - **Handover** = transfer of active call between cells/sectors/RATs *without call drop*.
> - **Three Phases:** Measurement → Decision → Execution.
> - **Hard HO** = Break-before-make; **Soft HO** = Make-before-break; **Softer HO** = intra-Node-B sector combining.
> - **MAHO** = Mobile-Assisted Handover; MS measures neighbours → reduces BTS load.
> - **Key Formula:** $H_m = P_{r,\text{TH}} - P_{r,\min}$ (dB).
> - **Decision Rule:** $RSS_{serving} - RSS_{neighbour} \le H_m$ for time $T_{TTT}$.
> - **Free-space path loss:** $L = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$.
> - **Soft HO thresholds (UMTS):** $T_{add} \approx -11$ dB, $T_{drop} \approx -19$ dB, $T_{TTT} \approx 320$ ms.
> - **Ping-pong effect:** Prevented via **Hysteresis ($\Delta H$)** + **Time-To-Trigger ($T_{TTT}$)**.
> - **GSM 1800 typical:** $P_{r,\min} = -102$ dBm, **GSM 900:** $P_{r,\min} = -104$ dBm.
> - **Active Set size** in UMTS soft HO: $|\mathcal{A}| \le 3$.
> - **Inter-system HO** = between different RATs (e.g., 4G → 5G NSA, GSM → UMTS).
> - **Grade of Service (GOS)** worsens with handover traffic; reserve channels for handovers in dim. planning.
> - **5G NR enhancements:** Beam-level handover, conditional handover, dual-active protocol stack (DAPS).
> - **HO latency targets:** 5G aims for **sub-10 ms** inter-gNB handover; 4G LTE is ~50 ms; GSM ~200 ms.
> - **Difference between Handover and Cell Reselection:** Handover = *connected mode*; Cell Reselection = *idle mode*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication and Security in GSM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Authentication and Security in GSM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Authentication and Security in GSM** refers to the set of cryptographic mechanisms standardized within the GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) architecture to protect the radio interface and the mobile subscriber against three principal threats: unauthorized access (impersonation), eavesdropping on user traffic (lack of confidentiality), and tracking of user location (loss of subscriber anonymity). The GSM security framework provides three explicit security services built on a shared-secret key **$K_i$** stored on the Subscriber Identity Module (SIM) card and in the Authentication Center (AuC) of the home network.

The three security services defined in the GSM 02.09 / 03.20 specification are:

1. **Subscriber Identity Authentication (SIA)** — verifies that the mobile station (MS) is genuine and holds a valid subscription.
2. **User Data and Signalling Confidentiality** — protects user traffic and most signalling channels on the radio path from eavesdropping using stream cipher **A5**.
3. **Subscriber Identity Confidentiality (User Anonymity)** — prevents disclosure of the permanent identity (IMSI) on the radio interface by using a **Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity (TMSI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Authentication and Security in GSM is a frequently examined topic in KTU ESE because it integrates concepts of wireless architecture, signalling, and applied cryptography. Expect direct questions on the **security triplet**, the roles of **A3, A5, A8** algorithms, and the **authentication/signalling flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Security Triplet**
> A *security triplet* is a three-element tuple $\langle \text{RAND}, \text{SRES}, K_c \rangle$ produced by the AuC using the subscriber-specific key $K_i$. It is delivered to the VLR/SGSN and forms the basis of both authentication and ciphering.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are checking in at an international airport:
- The **boarding-pass check** (passport + ticket) is like **A3 authentication** — the gate officer asks a question (RAND) that only the real ticket-holder can answer (SRES), proving you are who your ticket says.
- The **tinted lounge window** that stops people outside from watching you is like the **A5 cipher** — even if a radio wave is intercepted, it looks like scrambled noise without the right key $K_c$.
- The **disposable lounge code** that changes every visit (so spies cannot track your room number) is like the **TMSI** — your real subscriber ID (IMSI/phone number equivalent) is replaced by a temporary one.

All three layers work together so that the system is hard to impersonate, hard to eavesdrop, and hard to track.

## 1.3 The Three Security Services at a Glance

| # | Security Service | Threat Addressed | Mechanism |
|---|------------------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Subscriber Authentication | Impersonation / cloning | A3 with RAND, $K_i$ producing SRES |
| 2 | Confidentiality | Eavesdropping | A5 stream cipher using $K_c$ |
| 3 | Anonymity | Location tracking / IMSI catching | TMSI allocated by VLR |

**Standard bit-widths used in GSM security** (as per 3GPP TS 43.020 / GSM 03.20):
- **$K_i$ = 128 bits** (stored only in SIM and AuC — never transmitted over air)
- **RAND = 128 bits** (random challenge)
- **SRES = 32 bits** (signed response)
- **$K_c$ = 64 bits** (ciphering key)
- **IMSI = up to 15 digits** (permanent identity)
- **TMSI = 32 bits** (local temporary identity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The subscriber key $K_i$ is the most sensitive secret in the entire GSM system. It is never sent over the radio interface and never leaves the SIM card. Compromise of $K_i$ collapses all three security services simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Security Triplet and the Cryptographic Building Blocks

GSM security is built on **three algorithms** named **A3, A5, and A8**. None of them are fixed globally; operators may choose any compliant implementation (the de facto standard is **COMP128**).

### 2.1.1 Algorithm A3 — Authentication Function

A3 is a one-way cryptographic function executed identically in the **SIM** and the **AuC**. It takes the 128-bit subscriber key $K_i$ and a 128-bit random challenge RAND and produces a 32-bit signed response.

$$
\text{SRES} = A3(K_i, \text{RAND}) \quad ; \quad \vert \text{SRES} \vert = 32 \text{ bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 32-bit SRES?** A 32-bit response yields roughly $2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{9}$ possible responses, which at the time of GSM standardization was considered sufficient to make brute-force guessing infeasible within a single session. Modern attacks (e.g., on COMP128) have since reduced this margin.

### 2.1.2 Algorithm A8 — Ciphering Key Generation

A8 also runs in both the SIM and the AuC. It produces a 64-bit session key $K_c$ from the same inputs as A3.

$$
K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND}) \quad ; \quad \vert K_c \vert = 64 \text{ bits}
$$

The same RAND is used for both A3 and A8 within a triplet, ensuring that **the same challenge that authenticates the user also derives the key that protects their session**.

### 2.1.3 Algorithm A5 — Stream Cipher

A5 encrypts and decrypts the radio traffic between the MS and the BTS. It is a symmetric stream cipher using a 64-bit key $K_c$ and the **TDMA frame number** (22 bits) as a keystream modifier.

$$
\text{Output}_i = A5(K_c, \text{FrameNumber}_i) \quad ; \quad 114 \text{ bits per burst}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> There are three variants of A5 in legacy GSM: **A5/1** (export-grade, used in most of the world), **A5/2** (weaker, intended for export), and **A5/0** (no encryption — used in some regions). The break of A5/2 and rainbow-table attacks on A5/1 are why 3G (UMTS) introduced the **MILENAGE / Kasumi-based** mutual authentication scheme.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The GSM authentication and ciphering procedure follows a fixed six-stage logic, in which three network elements (HLR/AuC, VLR, MS) cooperate:

1. **MS identity disclosure** — On first contact, the MS sends its permanent identity (IMSI) or, if available, a previously assigned TMSI.
2. **Triplet request** — The VLR queries the HLR/AuC for authentication material of the subscriber.
3. **Triplet generation** — The AuC picks a fresh RAND, computes $\text{SRES} = A3(K_i, \text{RAND})$ and $K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND})$, and packages the triplet $\langle \text{RAND}, \text{SRES}, K_c \rangle$.
4. **Challenge delivery** — The VLR keeps SRES and $K_c$, sends only **RAND** to the MS over the radio path.
5. **Response verification** — The MS uses the $K_i$ stored in its SIM to compute $\text{SRES}' = A3(K_i, \text{RAND})$ and returns it. The VLR compares $\text{SRES}'$ with the stored SRES. A match authenticates the subscriber.
6. **Ciphering activation** — Both sides independently compute $K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND})$ and apply A5 with the TDMA frame number to encrypt subsequent bursts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Triplet reuse vs. fresh triplets:** The VLR typically receives a *batch* of triplets (often 5) from the AuC and reuses them across successive location updates to reduce HLR signalling. However, for sensitive operations (e.g., call setup, location update crossing MSC areas), a fresh RAND may be demanded.

## 2.3 The COMP128 Implementation

In real operator networks, A3 and A8 are not two completely independent algorithms. They are usually realized by a single physical function called **COMP128**, which internally produces both SRES and $K_c$ in one pass. This is:

- A **hash-like** function with input $(K_i, \text{RAND})$ of 256 bits total, and output SRES (32 bits) and $K_c$ (64 bits) concatenated.

> [!WARNING]
> COMP128 has been cryptographically broken in practice (e.g., the 1998 Briceno/Goldberg/van der Merwe attack recovered $K_i$ via a chosen-challenge side channel in under an hour on a SIM). Modern SIMs use **COMP128-2 / COMP128-3** or operator-specific proprietary algorithms.

## 2.4 Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity (TMSI)

To prevent an attacker from learning the user's IMSI (or from linking multiple visits by the same user to a fixed permanent identity), the VLR issues a **TMSI**:

- A **32-bit local** identifier valid only within the current VLR area.
- Reallocated at every location update and at periodic intervals.
- Sent encrypted on the radio path once ciphering is active.
- Mapped to the IMSI inside the VLR's secure database.

The flow is: **IMSI → initial registration → VLR assigns TMSI → MS uses TMSI in subsequent signalling**. On MS-detached or new VLR, IMSI may be requested.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile network operators** rely on this exact flow to prevent SIM cloning fraud that cost the industry billions in the 1990s.
- **SIM manufacturing** industry is built around secure storage of $K_i$ and tamper-resistant execution of A3/A8.
