- **Forensic and lawful interception** systems deliberately key into the same triplet pipeline to lawfully obtain $K_c$ for monitoring.
- **Migration to 3G/4G/5G** preserves the *concept* of authentication vectors but replaces A3/A8 with mutual-authentication algorithms (MILENAGE, TUAK, KASUMI, SNOW-3G, AES).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Size (bits) | Lives In | Used By |
|--------|---------|-------------|----------|---------|
| $K_i$ | Subscriber authentication key (secret) | **128** | SIM + AuC only | A3, A8 |
| RAND | Random challenge | **128** | Generated by AuC, sent on air | A3, A8, A5 |
| SRES | Signed response | **32** | AuC → VLR, MS → VLR | A3 |
| $K_c$ | Ciphering session key | **64** | SIM, BTS, MSC/VLR | A5 |
| TMSI | Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity | **32** | MS + VLR database | Anonymity |
| IMSI | International Mobile Subscriber Identity | up to 15 digits (≈ 60) | SIM + HLR | Permanent ID |
| FrameNo | TDMA frame counter | 22 | BTS + MS | A5 keystream |
| Burst payload | Encrypted data per TDMA burst | 114 | MS + BTS | A5 |

**Core equations (verbatim for KTU answers):**

$$
\text{SRES} = A3(K_i, \text{RAND})
$$

$$
K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND})
$$

$$
C_i = P_i \oplus A5(K_c, \text{FrameNumber}_i)
$$

where $P_i$ is plaintext and $C_i$ is ciphertext of the $i$-th TDMA burst; $\oplus$ is bitwise XOR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Authentication Triplet Generation

The AuC receives the subscriber's $K_i$ (already provisioned at subscription time) and the request for a fresh authentication vector. The following derivation shows the triplet construction.

**Step 1 — Random number generation**
The AuC draws a fresh 128-bit random number from a hardware or software CSPRNG.

$$
\text{RAND} = \text{CSPRNG}(128) \quad ; \quad \text{RAND} \in \{0,1\}^{128}
$$

This means RAND is a binary string of exactly 128 bits, with each bit independent and uniformly distributed.

**Step 2 — Signed response computation**
Apply the A3 function (e.g., COMP128-1 internal hash truncated to 32 bits):

$$
\text{SRES} = A3(K_i, \text{RAND}) = \text{TRUNC}_{32}\big(\text{COMP128}(K_i \,\|\, \text{RAND})\big)
$$

The $\|$ symbol denotes concatenation. The 256-bit input becomes the internal hash, and the first 32 bits are output as SRES.

**Step 3 — Ciphering key computation**
Apply the A8 function (or the second half of COMP128):

$$
K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND}) = \text{EXTRACT}_{64 \to 96}\big(\text{COMP128}(K_i \,\|\, \text{RAND})\big)
$$

In COMP128-1, the 128-bit output is split; the first 96 bits are defined, of which 64 bits are kept as $K_c$ and the remaining 10 × 6 = 60 bits are zeroed (a known design weakness exploited by 1998 attacks).

**Step 4 — Triplet assembly**
The AuC packages the three elements and returns the triplet to the VLR:

$$
\text{Triplet} = \langle \text{RAND},\ \text{SRES},\ K_c \rangle
$$

The triplet can be cached by the VLR for reuse in subsequent location-update procedures.

## 3.2 Derivation: Authentication Challenge–Response

We now derive the verification performed by the VLR.

**Step 1 — VLR stores the triplet**
After receiving the triplet from AuC, the VLR stores SRES and $K_c$ in its local VLR database and forwards RAND to the MS over the random-access / dedicated signalling channel.

$$
\text{VLR}_{\text{state}} \;\leftarrow\; \{(\text{RAND}, \text{SRES}, K_c)\}_i
$$

**Step 2 — MS computes the response**
Inside the SIM, the A3 function is recomputed:

$$
\text{SRES}_{\text{MS}} = A3(K_i^{\text{SIM}}, \text{RAND})
$$

The crucial security property is that the SIM's $K_i$ never leaves the chip — only the result SRES is exposed on the wire.

**Step 3 — VLR verification by equality test**
The VLR performs a constant-time comparison:

$$
\text{AuthOK} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \text{SRES}_{\text{MS}} = \text{SRES}_{\text{VLR}} \\[4pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

On AuthOK = 1, the MS is granted service. On AuthOK = 0, the network rejects the attach attempt.

**Step 4 — Probability of false acceptance**
An attacker who guesses SRES uniformly at random has probability

$$
P(\text{false accept}) = \frac{1}{2^{32}} \approx 2.33 \times 10^{-10}
$$

This is the per-guess success probability, which is why SRES is intentionally short — to keep latency on the radio path low.

## 3.3 Derivation: Ciphering Process (A5 Stream Cipher)

The A5 cipher is applied to the 148-bit radio burst; 114 bits carry the user's encrypted data, and 34 bits are training sequence / stealing bits that are not encrypted.

**Step 1 — Keystream generation**

$$
\text{KS}_i = A5(K_c, \text{FrameNumber}_i) \quad ; \quad \vert \text{KS}_i \vert = 114 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Encryption at the MS (uplink)**

$$
C_i = P_i \oplus \text{KS}_i
$$

**Step 3 — Decryption at the BTS (uplink reception)**

$$
\hat{P}_i = C_i \oplus \text{KS}_i = (P_i \oplus \text{KS}_i) \oplus \text{KS}_i = P_i
$$

The same operation recovers the plaintext, since XOR is its own inverse. Note that both BTS and MS must hold the same $K_c$ *and* the same frame number, which is why **frame number synchronization** is essential.

**Step 4 — Cipher mode signalling**
The MS and BTS negotiate the cipher via the **Cipher Mode Command / Complete** signalling messages on the Slow Associated Control Channel (SACCH); the agreed algorithm is one of A5/1, A5/2, A5/3, or A5/0 (no encryption).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Summary

| Property | A3 | A5 | A8 |
|----------|----|----|----|
| Function | Authentication | Encryption | Key generation |
| Inputs | $K_i$, RAND | $K_c$, FrameNumber | $K_i$, RAND |
| Output | SRES (32 bits) | Keystream (114 bits/burst) | $K_c$ (64 bits) |
| Implemented in | SIM + AuC | MS (handset) + BTS | SIM + AuC |
| Type | One-way hash | Synchronous stream cipher | One-way hash |
| Real implementation | COMP128 family | A5/1, A5/2, A5/3, A5/0 | COMP128 family |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Vulnerability vs. Mitigation

| Vulnerability | Threat | Mitigation / Status |
|---------------|--------|--------------------|
| COMP128-1 chosen-challenge attack | $K_i$ recovery | Replaced by COMP128-2/3, proprietary |
| A5/2 ciphertext-only attack | Eavesdropping | A5/2 deprecated in 3GPP |
| A5/1 known-plaintext / rainbow tables | Eavesdropping | Switched to A5/3 in 3G/UMTS |
| IMSI catching (no cipher) | Anonymity loss | TMSI always issued when possible |
| Triplet replay within VLR | Limited impersonation window | Re-authentication on critical events |
| One-way authentication (network not proved) | False base station | Mitigated in UMTS via mutual AKA |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`, `auc`, `vlr`, `ms`) and contain no reserved keywords. All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric strings (no bold, no italics, no inline tables).

## 4.2 GSM Authentication and Security — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[MOBILE STATION MS WITH SIM]:::ms
    nodeB[AUTHENTICATION CENTER AUC IN HLR]:::auc
    nodeC[VISITOR LOCATION REGISTER VLR MSC]:::vlr
    nodeD[BTS BASE TRANSCEIVER STATION]:::bts

    A1[1 SEND IMSI OR TMSI]:::sig
    A2[2 REQUEST AUTHENTICATION TRIPLET]:::sig
    A3[3 GENERATE RAND AND COMPUTE A3 AND A8]:::compute
    A4[4 SEND TRIPLET RAND SRES KC]:::sig
    A5[5 SEND RAND OVER AIR]:::sig
    A6[6 SIM COMPUTES SRES PRIME]:::compute
    A7[7 SEND SRES PRIME TO VLR]:::sig
    A8[8 COMPARE SRES PRIME WITH SRES]:::compute
    A9[9 BOTH SIDES COMPUTE KC VIA A8]:::compute
    A10[10 ACTIVATE A5 STREAM CIPHER]:::cipher
    A11[11 ALLOCATE TMSI FOR ANONYMITY]:::tmsi

    nodeA --> A1 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> A2 --> nodeB
    nodeB --> A3 --> A4 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> A5 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> A6 --> A7 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> A8
    nodeC --> A9
    nodeA --> A9
    nodeC --> A10
    nodeA --> A10
    nodeC --> A11
    nodeA --> A11
    nodeA -.radio interface.-> nodeD

    classDef ms fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef tmsi fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Authentication Triplet Generation — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ki[KI 128 BIT SUBSCRIBER KEY]:::key
    rand[RAND 128 BIT FRESH CHALLENGE]:::rand
    a3[A3 ALGORITHM COMP128]:::algo
    a8[A8 ALGORITHM COMP128]:::algo
    sres[SRES 32 BIT SIGNED RESPONSE]:::out
    kc[KC 64 BIT CIPHERING KEY]:::out
    triplet[SECURITY TRIPLET]:::trip

    ki --> a3
    rand --> a3
    a3 --> sres
    ki --> a8
    rand --> a8
    a8 --> kc
    sres --> triplet
    rand --> triplet
    kc --> triplet
```


# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Note on Mark Distribution

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GSM Security Questions**
> 1. Confusing **A3** (authentication) with **A5** (ciphering) — students often swap SRES and $K_c$ outputs. Remember: A3 → SRES, A8 → $K_c$, A5 → keystream.
> 2. Stating that $K_i$ is transmitted to the VLR for verification — **incorrect**. $K_i$ never leaves the SIM/AuC; only SRES is sent.
> 3. Forgetting the **frame number** as the second input to A5. The cipher is *frame-dependent*; a wrong frame number breaks decryption.
> 4. Saying GSM provides **mutual authentication** — it does not. Only the network authenticates the MS, not vice-versa. This was a flaw fixed in UMTS.
> 5. Confusing **TMSI** with **IMSI**. TMSI is local (32-bit, VLR-scoped); IMSI is the global permanent identity.

## 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

> **Q1.** List the three security services offered by GSM and the corresponding mechanism used for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three GSM security services are:

1. **Subscriber Identity Authentication (SIA):** Ensures the MS is genuine. Achieved using algorithm **A3** together with a random challenge **RAND** and the secret subscriber key **$K_i$**, producing a 32-bit **SRES** for verification by the VLR.

2. **User Data and Signalling Confidentiality:** Protects traffic on the radio path from eavesdropping. Achieved using the **A5** stream cipher with the 64-bit session key **$K_c$** and the TDMA frame number as the keystream generator.

3. **Subscriber Identity Confidentiality (Anonymity):** Prevents disclosure of the permanent IMSI on the radio path. Achieved by allocating a **TMSI (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity)** by the VLR after the initial registration.

*(1 mark per correct service + mechanism. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

> **Q2.** What is a *security triplet* in GSM? State the size in bits of each of its three components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **security triplet** is a tuple of three elements generated by the **Authentication Center (AuC)** using the subscriber-specific secret key $K_i$ and a freshly generated random number RAND. The triplet is delivered to the **VLR** to enable authentication and ciphering for a roaming subscriber.

The three components and their bit sizes are:

- **RAND** — 128 bits (random challenge)
- **SRES** — 32 bits (signed response produced by A3)
- **$K_c$** — 64 bits (ciphering key produced by A8)

The complete triplet is therefore $\langle \text{RAND}, \text{SRES}, K_c \rangle$.

*(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for naming all three elements; 1 mark for correct bit sizes.)*

---

## 5.3 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 choice]`

> **Q A.** (a) Explain in detail the **GSM authentication procedure** with reference to the HLR/AuC, VLR, and MS, clearly showing the role of RAND, SRES, and $K_i$. State the bit-size of each security parameter. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Describe the **GSM confidentiality (ciphering) mechanism** using the A5 algorithm. Show how the ciphering key $K_c$ is generated and applied to the radio bursts. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — GSM Authentication Procedure (7 marks)

The GSM authentication is a **one-way challenge–response** protocol executed when an MS attaches to a VLR. The three entities involved are:

- **MS (Mobile Station)** — contains the SIM card holding the secret key $K_i$.
- **VLR (Visitor Location Register)** — caches authentication triplets and verifies SRES.
- **HLR / AuC (Home Location Register / Authentication Center)** — holds the master copy of $K_i$ and generates triplets.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** On entering a new VLR area, the MS transmits its identity — either the permanent **IMSI** or a previously assigned **TMSI** (decoded by the VLR via local mapping).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The VLR, which does not have $K_i$, sends a **MAP SEND_AUTHENTICATION_INFO** request to the subscriber's HLR/AuC asking for one or more authentication triplets.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Inside the AuC, a fresh **128-bit RAND** is generated by a CSPRNG. The AuC then computes:
$$
\text{SRES} = A3(K_i, \text{RAND}) \quad ; \quad K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND})
$$
and packages $\langle \text{RAND}, \text{SRES}, K_c \rangle$ as the **security triplet**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The AuC returns the triplet to the VLR. The VLR **stores SRES and $K_c$** in its local database but **forwards only RAND** to the MS over the radio path.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Inside the MS, the SIM independently runs $A3(K_i, \text{RAND})$ and produces **SRES'**. The MS returns SRES' to the VLR.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The VLR performs a **constant-time comparison** of SRES' with the stored SRES. A match indicates a genuine subscriber and the VLR proceeds with location registration and call setup. A mismatch causes an attach reject.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Bit-sizes: $K_i$ = 128 bits, RAND = 128 bits, SRES = 32 bits, $K_c$ = 64 bits, IMSI ≤ 15 digits, TMSI = 32 bits. Notably, $K_i$ **never leaves the SIM or the AuC**, which is the fundamental security property of GSM.

#### Part (b) — GSM Confidentiality / A5 Ciphering (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** After authentication, both the MS (in its SIM) and the AuC have already computed the **64-bit session key $K_c$** using $K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND})$ from the same RAND that authenticated the user.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The VLR signals the MS to begin ciphering via the **Cipher Mode Command** message, which includes the chosen A5 algorithm identifier (A5/1, A5/2, A5/3, or A5/0). The MS acknowledges with **Cipher Mode Complete**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The **A5** algorithm is a synchronous stream cipher that takes two inputs:
- the 64-bit $K_c$, and
- the 22-bit **TDMA frame number**.

It outputs a 114-bit keystream block per TDMA burst.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Encryption at the sender:
$$
C_i = P_i \oplus A5(K_c, \text{FrameNumber}_i)
$$
where $P_i$ is the plaintext 114-bit user payload of burst $i$, and $C_i$ is the ciphertext transmitted over the air.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Decryption at the receiver is symmetric:
$$
\hat{P}_i = C_i \oplus A5(K_c, \text{FrameNumber}_i) = P_i
$$
since XOR is self-inverse. Both ends must share $K_c$ **and** be perfectly frame-synchronized.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The 34 non-information bits of each TDMA burst (training sequence + stealing bits) are **not** encrypted, to maintain channel estimation, equalization, and timing recovery at the BTS.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Vulnerabilities:** A5/1 has been broken by rainbow-table and time–memory trade-off attacks; A5/2 is considered broken since 2009. Hence 3G/UMTS replaced A5 with **A5/3 (KASUMI)** and introduced **mutual authentication** to fix the false-base-station flaw of GSM.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 choice alternative]`

> **Q B.** (a) Discuss the **security threats** in wireless communication and explain how GSM addresses them through authentication, confidentiality, and anonymity. (7 marks)
>
> (b) With a neat flow diagram, explain the **role of the Authentication Center (AuC) and HLR** in generating and supplying authentication triplets to the VLR. Discuss the **COMP128** algorithm and its limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Wireless Security Threats & GSM Counter-Measures (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Wireless communication is inherently broadcast — radio waves propagate to any receiver in range, unlike wired links that are physically contained. This creates three categories of threats:

1. **Impersonation / Cloning:** An attacker pretends to be a legitimate subscriber to make free calls or hide their identity.
2. **Eavesdropping:** An attacker passively listens to radio transmissions to recover voice/data/signalling content.
3. **Location tracking / Identity capture:** An attacker captures the IMSI to track a victim's movement.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** GSM counters impersonation via the **A3 authentication** algorithm — a one-way challenge–response using a fresh RAND and the secret $K_i$ shared between the SIM and AuC. The MS proves possession of $K_i$ without revealing it.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** GSM counters eavesdropping via the **A5 stream cipher** which encrypts the 114 user-data bits of every TDMA burst using the 64-bit session key $K_c$ and the burst's frame number. Without $K_c$, the intercepted bits appear pseudo-random.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** GSM counters location tracking via the **TMSI** (Temporary Mobile Subscriber Identity). After registration, the VLR assigns a 32-bit local identifier; the IMSI is only sent over the air when absolutely necessary (e.g., first attach, after VLR failure). The IMSI–TMSI mapping is held securely in the VLR database.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** All three services rely on the **uncompromised secrecy of $K_i$**, which is stored only in the tamper-resistant SIM and in the AuC — never on the radio interface or in the VLR.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Additional safeguards include periodic re-authentication on location update, fresh RAND on call setup, and encrypted TMSI allocation over the radio path.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Residual weaknesses** of GSM security:
- Authentication is one-way (false base-station attack possible),
- COMP128-1 is cryptographically weak,
- A5/1 and A5/2 have been practically broken,
- Subscriber data and signalling integrity are not protected.
These are why UMTS (3G) introduced mutual authentication and stronger ciphers.

#### Part (b) — AuC/HLR Triplet Generation and COMP128 (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Role of HLR/AuC:**
The **HLR** is the home database that holds the subscriber's permanent profile and the current VLR address. The **AuC** is a tightly coupled hardware security module that holds the master copy of every subscriber's $K_i$ and is responsible for computing authentication material. Together, the HLR/AuC form the *trust anchor* of GSM security.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Triplet generation flow:**

1. VLR sends `SEND_AUTHENTICATION_INFO` (MAP message) to HLR/AuC.
2. AuC generates fresh RAND (128 bits).
3. AuC computes $\text{SRES} = A3(K_i, \text{RAND})$ and $K_c = A8(K_i, \text{RAND})$.
4. AuC packages the triplet and returns it to the VLR.
5. VLR stores the triplet, forwards only RAND to the MS.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — COMP128 overview:**
COMP128 is the de facto industry implementation of A3 and A8. It is a hash function with:
- **Input:** $K_i$ (128 bits) concatenated with RAND (128 bits) → 256-bit input.
- **Output:** 128 bits, of which the first 32 bits become **SRES** and the next 64 bits become **$K_c$** (the remaining bits are defined as zero in COMP128-1, which is a known design weakness).

**Step 4 (1 mark) — COMP128 step structure (textual):**
COMP128 internally performs:
1. Permute the 256-bit input by byte-wise XOR with a fixed table,
2. Pass through 8 rounds of substitution and butterfly mixing,
3. Compress to a 128-bit digest,
4. Truncate / split to (SRES, $K_c$, padding zeros).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Limitations of COMP128-1:**
- The 1998 Briceno–Goldberg attack recovered $K_i$ from a SIM in about 8 hours using a chosen-challenge side channel.
- The padding zeros leak 60 bits of internal state, weakening the hash.
- A3 and A8 are not cryptographically separated — compromising one compromises both.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Modern replacements:**
- **COMP128-2** and **COMP128-3** plug the chosen-challenge leak.
- Many operators use **proprietary A3/A8** algorithms on their SIMs.
- **3G/UMTS** replaces A3/A8 with the **MILENAGE** algorithm (AES-based) and adds mutual authentication.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Security implications for the student:**
For KTU, the exam-relevant point is that the **same RAND is used for A3 and A8**, so authentication and key derivation are tightly coupled; an attacker who recovers SRES does **not** recover $K_c$ in one step, but a chosen-challenge oracle on the SIM effectively breaks $K_i$, which then trivially yields $K_c$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Three GSM security services:** *Authentication, Confidentiality, Anonymity*.
- **Three algorithms:** **A3** (auth), **A5** (cipher), **A8** (key gen).
- **Security triplet** = $\langle \text{RAND},\ \text{SRES},\ K_c \rangle$ generated by AuC.
- **Bit-sizes:** $K_i$ = 128, RAND = 128, SRES = 32, $K_c$ = 64, TMSI = 32, IMSI ≤ 15 digits.
- **$K_i$ is sacred** — stored only in SIM and AuC, never transmitted on air.
- **A3 and A8 inputs** = ($K_i$, RAND); **A3 output** = SRES, **A8 output** = $K_c$.
- **A5 inputs** = ($K_c$, FrameNumber); **A5 output** = 114-bit keystream per burst.
- **Ciphering** is XOR of plaintext and keystream; same op decrypts.
- **34 bits per burst (training + stealing) are not encrypted**; only 114 user bits are.
- **TMSI** is local to the VLR (32 bits) — provides anonymity by hiding IMSI.
- **Authentication is one-way** (network → MS), a flaw fixed by UMTS mutual authentication.
- **COMP128** is the common implementation; **COMP128-1 is broken**, replaced by COMP128-2/3 or proprietary.
- **A5/1 and A5/2 have known attacks**; 3G uses A5/3 (KASUMI/SNOW-3G/AES).
- **VLR caches a batch of triplets** (often 5) to reduce HLR signalling; fresh RAND used for call setup.
- **Cipher Mode Command / Complete** signalling on SACCH activates encryption.
- **KTU favorite 7-mark question:** Draw and explain the GSM authentication flow with HLR/AuC, VLR, MS, and label RAND, SRES, $K_i$, $K_c$.
- **KTU favorite 3-mark question:** List the three security services + the algorithm / mechanism for each.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (write on the back of your answer book):**
> **"A3 = who are you? (auth) → SRES"**
> **"A8 = what key do we share? → Kc"**
> **"A5 = shut up and encrypt → keystream"**
> **"Triplet = RAND, SRES, Kc"**
> **"KI never travels."**

topics.title: GSM Speech Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GSM Speech Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Global System for Mobile Communications (GSM)** standard, **Speech Coding** refers to the process of converting the analog voice waveform into a compressed digital bit-stream suitable for transmission over the radio interface, while preserving acceptable perceptual speech quality at the receiver. GSM mandates the **RPE-LTP (Regular Pulse Excitation – Long-Term Prediction)** codec, formally known as the **GSM 06.10 full-rate codec**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Specification (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> The GSM speech codec produces a **net bit-rate of 13 kbps**, derived from **260 bits transmitted every 20 ms** (i.e., $13{,}000 = 260/0.020$ bits per second).

The raw (uncompressed) speech is sampled at **8 kHz** with **13-bit uniform PCM** quantization, yielding a base rate of:

$$R_{raw} = 8000 \times 13 = 104 \text{ kbps}$$

This is compressed by a factor of **8:1** to produce the final 13 kbps stream.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Folding Suitcase":**
> Imagine you have a large **104 kbps voice suitcase** that is too bulky to fit inside a tiny **13 kbps GSM radio channel**. The GSM speech coder is like an expert **packer** who:
> 1. Throws away redundant audio "air" (silence, predictable patterns) using a **vocal-tract model (LPC)**.
> 2. Removes long-term repetition (your voice repeating a syllable rhythm) using **Long-Term Prediction (LTP)**.
> 3. Keeps only **13 pulses per 5 ms block (RPE)** that represent the unpredictable residual "energy bursts."
>
> At the receiver, the unpacking process rebuilds intelligible speech using the same modeling rules.

### 1.3 Why a 13 kbps Rate?

| Factor | Justification |
|---|---|
| Bandwidth efficiency | GSM channel is only **200 kHz**; 8 users must time-share → low bit-rate essential |
| Speech quality | 13 kbps RPE-LTP delivers **MOS ≈ 3.6** (acceptable telephony quality) |
| Algorithmic delay | Must be **< 80 ms** total one-way (codec contributes ~**30 ms**) |
| Robustness | Must survive Rayleigh fading and interference without graceful degradation |

### 1.4 Standardized Bit-Rate Constants

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Sampling Frequency** | **8000 Hz** |
| **Samples per Frame (20 ms)** | **160** |
| **Sub-blocks per Frame** | **4** (each 5 ms = 40 samples) |
| **Quantization (raw PCM)** | **13 bits/sample** |
| **Uncompressed Rate** | **104 kbps** |
| **Compressed (codec output)** | **13 kbps** |
| **Bits per 20 ms frame** | **260 bits** |
| **Compression Ratio** | **8 : 1** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of the RPE-LTP Encoder

The GSM encoder executes **eight sequential stages** every 20 ms:

1. **Pre-processing**
   - DC offset removal (high-pass filter).
   - Pre-emphasis filter: $H_{pre}(z) = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1}$ → flattens spectrum, reduces dynamic range.

2. **Segmentation**
   - 160 samples divided into **4 sub-blocks of 40 samples** each.

3. **Short-Term Prediction (STP) / LPC Analysis**
   - 8th-order Linear Predictive Coder computes **8 reflection coefficients** (PARCOR) using the **Schur recursion**.
   - Converted to **Log-Area Ratios (LAR)** for better quantization.

4. **Quantization of LAR**
   - 8 LARs quantized with **non-uniform bit allocation**: (6, 5, 4, 4, 4, 4, 4, 5) → **36 bits**.

5. **Short-Term Synthesis Filter (Inverse Filter)**
   - The LPC inverse filter $A(z)$ removes short-term redundancy → produces **residual signal $d(n)$**.

6. **Long-Term Prediction (LTP) Analysis**
   - Operates on each sub-block: estimates the **pitch period (lag, $N_0$)** and **pitch gain ($b$)** by maximizing cross-correlation between current sub-block and past excitation.
   - Removes long-term periodicity (voiced-speech harmonic structure).

7. **Regular Pulse Excitation (RPE) Encoding**
   - The LTP residual $e(n)$ is decimated 3:1 → four candidate sub-sequences offset by grid positions $M = 0, 1, 2, 3$.
   - The **sub-sequence with maximum energy** is selected and its grid position $M$ transmitted.

8. **Bit Multiplexing → 260-bit Frame**

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{raw} = f_s \times N_b$ | Raw PCM bit rate | bps |
| 2 | $R_{GSM} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{T_{frame}}$ | GSM encoded bit rate | bps |
| 3 | $R_{GSM} = \dfrac{260}{0.020}$ | Numerical evaluation | **13 kbps** |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{R_{raw}}{R_{GSM}} = \dfrac{104}{13}$ | Compression ratio | **8 : 1** |
| 5 | $T_{frame} = N_{samp}/f_s = 160/8000$ | Frame duration | **20 ms** |
| 6 | $N_{sub} = 4$ | Sub-blocks per frame | integer |
| 7 | $N_{pulse} = 13$ | RPE pulses per sub-block | integer |
| 8 | $H_{pre}(z) = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1}$ | Pre-emphasis transfer function | z-domain |
| 9 | $A(z) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{8} a_k z^{-k}$ | LPC analysis filter (8th order) | z-domain |
| 10 | $d(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{8} a_k s(n-k)$ | LPC residual (time domain) | samples |
| 11 | $r(\tau) = \sum_n e(n) e(n-\tau)$ | LTP cross-correlation lag search | arbitrary |
| 12 | $b = \dfrac{\sum e(n) e(n-N_0)}{\sum e(n-N_0)^2}$ | LTP gain (optimal MMSE) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $T_{delay} \approx 30$ ms | Total codec algorithmic delay | ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The same RPE-LTP concept influenced later vocoders used in **3G/4G AMR codecs** (Adaptive Multi-Rate). Understanding GSM speech coding is a prerequisite for studying modern **EVS (Enhanced Voice Services)** codecs in 5G.

### 2.3 The Why Behind Each Block

- **Pre-emphasis** → boosts high frequencies so LPC coefficients are numerically stable.
- **LAR instead of PARCOR** → log-area ratios have smoother quantization error properties and **unipolar** ranges suited for fixed-length codebooks.
- **LTP after LPC** → once the vocal tract (formants) is removed by $A(z)$, the remaining signal is **quasi-periodic** → ideal for pitch-based long-term prediction.
- **RPE instead of full residual** → the residual after both LPC and LTP is **noise-like**, so keeping only 13 regularly-spaced pulses (3:1 decimation) is perceptually sufficient and **halves the bit budget** versus transmitting all 40 residual samples.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Compression Ratio of GSM Speech Coder

The compression ratio is the ratio of uncompressed to compressed bit-rates.

**Step 1 — Raw PCM rate:**

$$R_{raw} = f_s \times N_b = 8000 \text{ samples/s} \times 13 \text{ bits/sample}$$

$$R_{raw} = 104{,}000 \text{ bps} = 104 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — GSM compressed rate (from 260 bits per 20 ms frame):**

$$R_{GSM} = \frac{260 \text{ bits}}{0.020 \text{ s}} = 13{,}000 \text{ bps} = 13 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compression ratio:**

$$CR = \frac{R_{raw}}{R_{GSM}} = \frac{104 \text{ kbps}}{13 \text{ kbps}} = 8$$

Thus, **every 8 bits of raw PCM are compressed to 1 bit of GSM channel data.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bit Allocation Verification (260 bits per 20 ms)

The GSM frame must contain exactly **260 bits**. The bit allocation is:

| Parameter | Sub-quantity | Bits |
|---|---|---|
| **LAR coefficients** | $6+5+4+4+4+4+4+5$ | **36** |
| **LTP lag** (1 per frame) | $7$ | **7** |
| **LTP gain** (4 sub-blocks × 2 bits) | $4 \times 2$ | **8** |
| **RPE grid position** (4 sub-blocks × 2 bits) | $4 \times 2$ | **8** |
| **Block-max $x_{max}$** (4 sub-blocks × 6 bits) | $4 \times 6$ | **24** |
| **RPE pulse amplitudes** (13 pulses × 4 sub-blocks × 3 bits) | $13 \times 4 \times 3$ | **156** |
| **Unused / parity (per some literature)** | — | **21** |
| **TOTAL** | — | **260** |

The standard canonical allocation that strictly sums to **260** is:

$$36 \;(\text{LAR}) + 7 \;(\text{LTP lag}) + 8 \;(\text{LTP gain}) + 8 \;(\text{RPE grid}) + 24 \;(x_{max}) + 156 \;(\text{RPE pulses}) + 21 \;(\text{reserved/parity}) = 260 \text{ bits} \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Algorithmic Delay of the Codec

The total one-way encoding delay includes three contributors:

**Step 1 — Frame assembly delay:**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{160}{8000} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Sub-block processing (interleaving within frame):**

$$T_{sub} \approx 5 \text{ ms per sub-block} \;\Rightarrow\; 4 \text{ sub-blocks processed sequentially} \Rightarrow T_{proc} \approx 5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Transmission + decoder buffering:**

$$T_{dec} \approx 5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Total codec delay:**

$$T_{delay} = T_{frame} + T_{proc} + T_{dec} \approx 20 + 5 + 5 = 30 \text{ ms}$$

This **30 ms codec delay** is well below the **80 ms ITU-T G.114 budget** for two-way voice conversation, hence acceptable.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Sub-Sequence in RPE

The LTP-residual $e(n)$ of length 40 is split into **four candidate interleaved sub-sequences**, each of length 13, with starting offsets $M \in \{0,1,2,3\}$:

$$e_M(k) = e(M + 3k), \quad k = 0,1,\dots,12$$

The **criterion for selection** is **maximum energy**:

$$M^{*} = \arg\max_{M \in \{0,1,2,3\}} \sum_{k=0}^{12} e_M^{2}(k)$$

Only the chosen $M^{*}$, the block-max $x_{max}$, and the 13 quantized RPE pulses are transmitted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Equivalence Trick:** Selecting the maximum-energy sub-sequence compensates for the energy loss of 3:1 decimation, so the synthesized speech retains its loudness contour.

### 3.5 Bit-Rate Compact Summary

$$13 \text{ kbps} \;\equiv\; 260 \text{ bits} / 20\text{ms} \;\equiv\; 65 \text{ bits} / 5\text{ms sub-block (avg.)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the GSM RPE-LTP Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mic Input Analog Speech] --> B[8 kHz Sampling and 13 bit PCM]
    B --> C[Pre processing DC Removal and Pre emphasis]
    C --> D[Frame Segmentation 160 samples every 20 ms]
    D --> D1[Sub block 1 40 samples]
    D --> D2[Sub block 2 40 samples]
    D --> D3[Sub block 3 40 samples]
    D --> D4[Sub block 4 40 samples]
    D --> E[LPC 8th Order Analysis Schur Recursion]
    E --> F[Log Area Ratio Quantization 36 bits]
    F --> G[Inverse LPC Filter A of z]
    G --> H[Residual Signal d of n]
    H --> I[LTP Lag and Gain Estimation]
    I --> J[LTP Inverse Filter]
    J --> K[Excitation Residual e of n]
    K --> L[RPE Grid Selection M in 0 1 2 3]
    L --> M[RPE Pulse Quantization 13 pulses per sub block]
    M --> N[Bit Multiplexer 260 bits per 20 ms]
    F --> N
    I --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O[Channel Encoder and Interleaver GSM Burst]
```

### 4.2 GSM 260-Bit Frame Bit Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[260 bit Frame] --> B[LAR 36 bits]
    A --> C[LTP Lag 7 bits]
    A --> D[LTP Gain x4 8 bits]
    A --> E[RPE Grid x4 8 bits]
    A --> F[Block Max x4 24 bits]
    A --> G[RPE Pulses 13 x4 x3 156 bits]
    A --> H[Reserved 21 bits]
```

### 4.3 RPE Sub-Sequence Selection Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[40 sample Sub block Residual e n] --> B[3 to 1 Decimation]
    B --> S0[Candidate M = 0 offset 0]
    B --> S1[Candidate M = 1 offset 1]
    B --> S2[Candidate M = 2 offset 2]
    B --> S3[Candidate M = 3 offset 3]
    S0 --> E0[Energy Calculation]
    S1 --> E1[Energy Calculation]
    S2 --> E2[Energy Calculation]
    S3 --> E3[Energy Calculation]
    E0 --> SEL{Max Energy Sub sequence}
    E1 --> SEL
    E2 --> SEL
    E3 --> SEL
    SEL --> OUT[Selected 13 RPE Pulses plus Grid Position M]
```

### 4.4 Pre-emphasis Frequency Response (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[s n Input Speech] --> B[H pre z = 1 minus 0.8 z inv]
    B --> C[Flattened Spectrum Output]
    C --> D[LPC Analysis Stage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The encoder diagram shows the canonical RPE-LTP pipeline. Each functional block directly corresponds to one row of the KTU formula sheet in Section 2.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Set B]**
**Q1. State the bit-rate of the GSM speech codec and the compression ratio achieved relative to standard 13-bit PCM.**

**Model Answer:**
The GSM RPE-LTP codec operates at a net bit-rate of **13 kbps**, corresponding to **260 bits per 20 ms frame**. Standard PCM at 8 kHz sampling with 13-bit quantization produces $8000 \times 13 = 104$ kbps. The compression ratio is therefore:

$$CR = \frac{104}{13} = 8$$

Hence an **8:1 compression** is achieved.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q2. List the four major functional blocks of the GSM RPE-LTP speech encoder.**

**Model Answer:**
The four major functional blocks are:
1. **Pre-processing** (DC removal + pre-emphasis)
2. **Short-Term Prediction (LPC analysis + LAR quantization)**
3. **Long-Term Prediction (LTP lag/gain estimation)**
4. **Regular Pulse Excitation (RPE) encoding and bit multiplexing**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting 4 sub-blocks per frame** — almost every sub-question in KTU valuation carries 2–3 marks for correctly stating frame/sub-block partitioning (160 samples / 4 sub-blocks / 40 samples each).
> 2. **Confusing PARCOR and LAR** — students often write "PARCOR coefficients are quantized" instead of "PARCOR is first converted to LAR, then quantized." This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the 8:1 compression** — when asked about bit-rate, ALWAYS conclude with the compression factor.
> 4. **Saying "GSM uses 13 kbps for voice and 9.8 kbps for error correction"** as if 9.8 kbps is *additional* — the 13 kbps *already excludes* channel coding. The 9.8 kbps is a separate channel-coding rate, not a subtraction from 13.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q4(a)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the GSM RPE-LTP speech encoder. Mention the role of each block. (7 marks)

**(b)** A GSM speech codec operates on 20 ms frames each containing 160 samples. If the compressed output delivers 260 bits per frame, determine: (i) the codec bit-rate, (ii) the compression ratio against 13-bit PCM at 8 kHz, (iii) the number of sub-blocks per frame, and (iv) the average bits per 5 ms sub-block. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The GSM RPE-LTP encoder pipeline (refer Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **Pre-processing:** Removes DC offset and applies pre-emphasis filter $H_{pre}(z) = 1 - 0.8z^{-1}$ to flatten the spectrum.
2. **Frame segmentation:** 160 samples grouped into 4 sub-blocks of 40 samples (5 ms each).
3. **LPC analysis (8th order):** Estimates 8 reflection coefficients via Schur recursion.
4. **LAR conversion & quantization:** Reflection coefficients mapped to 8 Log-Area Ratios, then quantized using non-uniform bit-allocation (6,5,4,4,4,4,4,5) → **36 bits**.
5. **LPC inverse filter:** Generates the prediction residual $d(n) = s(n) - \sum a_k s(n-k)$.
6. **LTP analysis:** Estimates pitch lag (7 bits) and gain (4×2 = 8 bits) per frame to remove long-term periodicity.
7. **RPE encoding:** Residual decimated 3:1; the maximum-energy sub-sequence of 13 pulses is selected, along with its grid position (2 bits/sub-block) and block-max scaling (6 bits/sub-block).
8. **Bit multiplexer:** Assembles all parameters into a 260-bit frame transmitted to the channel encoder.

**(7 marks awarded for: block diagram description — 3 marks, role of each block — 4 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Codec bit-rate:**

$$R_{GSM} = \frac{260 \text{ bits}}{0.020 \text{ s}} = 13{,}000 \text{ bps} = \boxed{13 \text{ kbps}}$$

**(ii) Compression ratio:**

$$R_{PCM} = 8000 \times 13 = 104 \text{ kbps}$$

$$CR = \frac{104}{13} = \boxed{8 : 1}$$

**(iii) Number of sub-blocks per frame:**

$$N_{sub} = \frac{160}{40} = \boxed{4}$$

**(iv) Average bits per 5 ms sub-block:**

$$\text{Bits per sub-block} = \frac{260}{4} = \boxed{65 \text{ bits}}$$

(Each sub-block uses 7 + 2 + 2 + 6 + 39 = 56 bits of payload + 9 bits frame-level, distributed across the 4 sub-blocks.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the Regular Pulse Excitation (RPE) encoding technique used in the GSM speech codec. Discuss why 13 pulses per sub-block are transmitted. (7 marks)

**(b)** A digital cellular system uses 8 kHz sampling and 16-bit linear PCM. (i) Find the raw bit-rate. (ii) If a speech codec compresses this signal into a 260-bit / 20 ms frame, determine the new bit-rate and compression ratio. (iii) Comment on the additional channel-coding rate of 9.8 kbps typically used in GSM traffic channels and explain whether it is *added* to the 13 kbps speech rate. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**RPE Encoding Principle:**
After LPC and LTP filtering, the residual $e(n)$ over a 40-sample sub-block is essentially noise-like and lacks short-term correlation. Transmitting all 40 samples is wasteful; instead, GSM uses **regular pulse excitation**, where:

- The 40-sample block is decimated by 3, yielding 4 candidate interleaved sub-sequences of length 13 each.
- Each candidate starts at offset $M \in \{0,1,2,3\}$: $e_M(k) = e(M + 3k)$, $k = 0,\dots,12$.
- The candidate with **maximum energy** $\sum_k e_M^2(k)$ is selected.
- The chosen 13 pulses are uniformly quantized (3 bits each) along with the grid position $M$ (2 bits) and block-max $x_{max}$ (6 bits).

**Why 13 pulses?**
- 40 samples ÷ 3 (decimation factor) ≈ 13.33 → rounded to **13 pulses**.
- 13 × 3 bits = 39 bits per sub-block for pulse amplitudes → keeps total frame size at 260 bits.
- Pulse density of 1 in 3 preserves sufficient spectral information for the vocal-tract synthesizer to reconstruct intelligible speech while halving the bit-rate versus full residual coding.

**(7 marks: 3 marks for RPE procedure, 4 marks for justification of 13 pulses)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Raw bit-rate (16-bit PCM):**

$$R_{PCM} = 8000 \times 16 = 128 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Compressed rate and CR:**

$$R_{GSM} = \frac{260}{0.020} = 13 \text{ kbps}$$

$$CR = \frac{128}{13} \approx 9.85 : 1 \;\;(\approx 10:1)$$

**(iii) Channel-coding comment:**
The 9.8 kbps is **not added** to the 13 kbps speech rate. In GSM, the **13 kbps speech codec output** is fed into a **channel codec** that adds convolutional coding for error protection. The combined rate (speech + channel coding + signaling) results in a **total gross rate of 22.8 kbps**, divided as:
- 13 kbps (speech)
- 9.8 kbps (channel coding + interleaving overhead)

The two rates are **parallel streams of the same logical channel**, not additive components.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — GSM Speech Coding**

- ✅ GSM uses the **RPE-LTP (Regular Pulse Excitation – Long-Term Prediction)** codec, standard **GSM 06.10**.
- ✅ Sampling: **8 kHz**, raw quantization: **13 bits/sample** → raw rate = **104 kbps**.
- ✅ Compressed output: **13 kbps = 260 bits / 20 ms frame**.
- ✅ Compression ratio: **8 : 1**.
- ✅ Frame = **160 samples** split into **4 sub-blocks of 40 samples (5 ms each)**.
- ✅ LPC is **8th-order**; coefficients stored as **8 Log-Area Ratios (LAR)** quantized to **36 bits** per frame.
- ✅ **LTP** removes pitch periodicity (lag + gain, computed via cross-correlation).
- ✅ **RPE** decimates the residual 3:1 → keeps **13 regularly-spaced pulses per sub-block** from the maximum-energy sub-sequence.
- ✅ RPE bit-budget per sub-block: 39 (pulses) + 2 (grid) + 6 (block-max) = 47 bits payload.
- ✅ Total codec delay ≈ **30 ms** (below 80 ms ITU budget).
- ✅ MOS quality ≈ 3.6 (acceptable telephony).
- ✅ The 9.8 kbps channel-coding rate is **separate from**, not added to, the 13 kbps speech rate.
- ✅ Successor: **AMR (Adaptive Multi-Rate)** codec in 3G/4G; **EVS** in 5G.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Memory Trick:** *"**8 kHz, 13-bit, 104 kbps → 260 bits every 20 ms → 13 kbps** — eight LARs, four LTP lags, thirteen RPE pulses per 5 ms sub-block."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Spread Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Spread Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spread Spectrum (SS)** is a modulation and signaling technique in which the bandwidth of the transmitted signal is deliberately spread (widened) by a factor much greater than the minimum bandwidth necessary to transmit the information, by using a **pseudo-random (pseudonoise, PN) spreading code** that is independent of the data sequence. The same pseudo-random code is used at the receiver to despread and recover the original narrowband information signal.

The transmitted signal occupies a bandwidth **W** that is typically several orders of magnitude greater than the data rate **R** of the underlying message. The pseudo-random code sequence has a much higher rate (called the **chip rate**, $R_c$) than the data bit rate ($R_b$), and it is this rapid switching (chipping) that creates the wideband low-power spectral signature.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cocktail Party Whisper"

Imagine two people, **Alice** and **Bob**, trying to have a private conversation at a noisy cocktail party:

- **Narrowband transmission** = Alice whispers directly to Bob. Anyone standing close by can hear it clearly — there is no privacy and lots of eavesdropping/jamming vulnerability.
- **Spread Spectrum transmission** = Alice and Bob agree *in advance* on a secret language (the **PN code**). Alice then speaks a long, seemingly random sentence of coded words, all describing a short, simple message. To an outsider, the long coded sentence sounds like meaningless noise, and only Bob (who shares the secret codebook) can despread it and instantly reconstruct the short message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works mathematically:** When Bob correlates the incoming spread signal with his *exact* copy of the PN code, the data collapses back to a narrowband signal. But the jammer's signal (or any unauthorized signal), which is uncorrelated with the PN code, is *spread even further* by the correlation process, becoming low-power background noise. This is the essence of spread-spectrum's **anti-jam (AJ)** and **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI)** properties.

### 1.3 The Five Key Properties of Spread Spectrum (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **Scholtz (1982)** and standard KTU wireless texts, a system qualifies as Spread Spectrum only if it satisfies these three classical criteria:
> 1. **Signal Energy Spreading** — The transmitted bandwidth is significantly wider than the information bandwidth.
> 2. **Spreading via Independent Pseudo-Random Code** — The spreading is achieved using a code sequence that is **independent of the data** and pseudo-random in nature.
> 3. **De-spreading at the Receiver** — The receiver uses *the same* synchronized PN code to despread the signal and recover the data.

The **five engineering benefits** (asked frequently in KTU papers):

1. **Anti-Jamming (AJ)** — Resistance to intentional narrowband jamming.
2. **Low Probability of Intercept (LPI)** — The wideband, low-power spectral density is hard to detect by an eavesdropper using a narrowband receiver.
3. **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** — Many users share the same frequency band simultaneously using *different* orthogonal/quasi-orthogonal PN codes.
4. **Mitigation of Multipath Fading** — Wideband signals resolve multipath components and exploit RAKE receivers.
5. **Resolution of Hidden Terminal Problem** in some MAC-layer designs.

### 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Processing Gain ($G_p$)** — The ratio of spread bandwidth to data bandwidth. Units: **dimensionless (or dB)**.
- **Jamming Margin ($M_j$)** — Maximum tolerable jammer-to-signal power ratio, expressed in dB.
- **Chip Duration ($T_c$)** — Time duration of one chip of the PN code.
- **Chip Rate ($R_c$)** — Number of chips per second. Equals **$1/T_c$**.
- **Bit Duration ($T_b$)** — Time duration of one information bit.
- **Number of chips per bit (N)** — Equals **$T_b / T_c$** = **$R_c / R_b$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Logic — How Spread Spectrum Works

The system is best understood as a **two-stage transformation**:

**Stage A — Spreading (Transmitter Side):**

- Step 1: Source data $d(t)$ is generated at bit rate $R_b$ (information bandwidth $\approx R_b$ Hz).
- Step 2: A **PN code sequence** $c(t)$ is generated locally at chip rate $R_c$, where $R_c \gg R_b$.
- Step 3: The data and the PN code are combined (typically via **direct multiplication** in DSSS or **frequency synthesis selection** in FHSS) to produce the wideband spread signal.
- Step 4: The wideband signal is up-converted to RF and transmitted.
- *Why?* The PN code appears noise-like; an observer without the code sees only low spectral density.

**Stage B — De-spreading (Receiver Side):**

- Step 5: A locally generated *synchronized replica* of the PN code is multiplied with the received wideband signal.
- Step 6: Because the receiver's PN code is time-aligned with the incoming code, the multiplication restores the original narrowband data spectrum.
- Step 7: A conventional demodulator (coherent BPSK/QPSK detector) recovers the bitstream.
- *Why does jamming fail?* A narrowband jammer $J(t)$ is multiplied by the receiver's PN code. Since $J(t)$ is uncorrelated with the PN code, the multiplication *spreads* the jammer's energy across the entire wideband bandwidth, so only a tiny fraction of the jammer's power falls inside the narrow post-despreading filter — drastically reducing its effective interference.

### 2.2 Types of Spread Spectrum — The KTU Standard Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module-4 strictly classifies spread spectrum into the following two primary schemes:

**A) Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)**
- The data is multiplied directly (bit-wise XOR for binary, or analog multiplication) with a high-rate PN code.
- Each data bit is replaced by $N$ chips ($N$ = chips per bit).
- The signal occupies a continuous wide bandwidth centered on the carrier.
- *Example:* GPS C/A code, IS-95 CDMA, Wi-Fi (802.11b).

**B) Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)**
- The carrier frequency of the transmitted signal is *rapidly switched* among many discrete frequency channels, following a PN-controlled hopping pattern.
- Hopping rate may be **slow** (multiple bits per hop) or **fast** (multiple hops per bit).
- The signal is a narrowband signal at any instant, but *over time* it sweeps a wide spectrum.
- *Example:* Bluetooth, classic military COMSEC radios.

**C) Time Hopping Spread Spectrum (THSS)** — *Less common, briefly mentioned.*
- A data bit is transmitted in one of $N$ time slots, with the slot chosen pseudo-randomly.

**D) Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** — *Used in LoRa, optional reading.*

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically asks for an **internal choice** between DSSS and FHSS in 14-mark questions. Be prepared to draw both block diagrams and compare.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the formulas in this table — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B question on spread spectrum.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description / Meaning | Units |
|---|----------------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $G_p = \dfrac{W}{R_b} = \dfrac{BW_{spread}}{BW_{info}}$ | **Processing Gain** — bandwidth expansion factor | dimensionless (or dB: $10 \log_{10} G_p$) |
| 2 | $G_p = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b} = \dfrac{T_b}{T_c}$ | Processing gain in terms of chip/bit rates | dimensionless |
| 3 | $M_j = G_p - \left( \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \right)_{req} - L_{sys}$ | **Jamming Margin** (in dB) | dB |
| 4 | $\left( \dfrac{S}{J} \right)_{out} = G_p \cdot \left( \dfrac{S}{J} \right)_{in}$ | SNR improvement after de-spreading | dB / linear |
| 5 | $R_c = N \cdot R_b$ | Chip rate = chips-per-bit $\times$ bit rate | chips/sec |
| 6 | $N = 2^n - 1$ | Length of an m-sequence generated by an $n$-stage LFSR | chips |
| 7 | $\text{Spectral Density} \propto \dfrac{P}{W}$ | Wideband signal has low power per Hz | W/Hz |
| 8 | $\dfrac{E_c}{E_b} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Energy per chip is reduced by the processing gain | Joules |
| 9 | $BER_{DSSS} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot \dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ (AWGN, no jamming) | Standard BPSK BER | dimensionless |
| 10 | $N_{users} \approx G_p$ (ideal synchronous CDMA) | Theoretical capacity of CDMA in single cell | users |

> **Notation convention used in tables:** $N$ = number of chips per bit, $W$ = spread bandwidth, $L_{sys}$ = system implementation loss.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Spread Spectrum |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Military Communications (MILSATCOM, HAVE QUICK)** | Anti-jam (AJ) and LPI for tactical radios in EW environments. |
| **Civilian Cellular (3G IS-95 / CDMA2000)** | Code Division Multiple Access; capacity gain via voice activity factor and sectorization. |
| **GPS (Global Positioning System)** | DSSS C/A code (1.023 Mcps) on L1 (1575.42 MHz) for precise ranging and signal acquisition. |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11b)** | DSSS with Barker-11 code at 11 Mcps for 11 Mbps. |
| **Bluetooth** | FHSS at 1600 hops/sec over 79 channels (classic Bluetooth). |
| **IoT / LoRa (CSS)** | Chirp spread spectrum for long-range, low-power, robust communication. |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Processing Gain $G_p$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Understand every line, do not just memorize.

**Starting Point:** Define the spread and unspread signal energies in the time domain.

The transmitted DSSS signal $s(t)$ can be expressed as:

$$
s(t) = A \cdot d(t) \cdot c(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where:
- $d(t)$ is the data waveform (NRZ, $\pm 1$).
- $c(t)$ is the PN spreading waveform (also $\pm 1$).
- $A$ is the amplitude.
- $f_c$ is the carrier frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the bit energy:**

The information bit duration is $T_b = 1 / R_b$. The energy per information bit is:

$$
E_b = A^2 \cdot T_b
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the chip energy:**

The chip duration is $T_c = 1 / R_c$. The energy per chip is:

$$
E_c = A^2 \cdot T_c
$$

**Step 3 — Relate the two energies using the number of chips per bit ($N$):**

Since each information bit is represented by $N$ chips:

$$
T_b = N \cdot T_c
$$

Substitute into the bit-energy equation:

$$
E_b = A^2 \cdot (N \cdot T_c) = N \cdot (A^2 \cdot T_c) = N \cdot E_c
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for the energy-per-chip ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_c}{E_b} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The same total bit energy $E_b$ is "smeared" across $N$ chips, so each chip carries only $1/N$th of the bit energy. This is why the spectral power density of a DSSS signal is very low.

**Step 5 — Convert the chip count to processing gain using bandwidth proportionality:**

For BPSK-like modulation, the signal's main-lobe bandwidth is approximately equal to the bit rate (before spreading) and the chip rate (after spreading):

$$
BW_{unspread} \approx R_b, \quad BW_{spread} \approx R_c
$$

Therefore:

$$
G_p = \frac{W}{R_b} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{T_b}{T_c} = N
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,G_p = N = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{T_b}{T_c} = \frac{W}{R_b}\,}
$$

**In decibels:**

$$
G_p \text{(dB)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left( \frac{R_c}{R_b} \right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(N)
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Jamming Margin $M_j$

**Scenario Setup:** A DSSS receiver operates in an **AWGN + Jammer** environment.

- $S$ = Received signal power.
- $J$ = Received jammer power (assumed to be in-band but narrowband).
- $N_0$ = Thermal noise PSD (W/Hz).
- $W$ = Spread bandwidth.
- $(E_b/N_0)_{req}$ = Energy-per-bit-to-noise-spectral-density required for a given target BER (e.g., $9.6$ dB for $10^{-5}$ BER in BPSK).

**Step 1 — Total effective interference power after de-spreading:**

The jammer is spread by the receiver's PN code, so its effective in-band interference power becomes:

$$
J_{eff} = \frac{J}{G_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Total noise-plus-jammer spectral density (per Hz) seen by the demodulator:**

$$
(N + J)_{eff} = N_0 + \frac{J / W}{G_p} = N_0 + \frac{J}{W \cdot G_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the link-budget equation:**

For acceptable demodulation, we require:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0 + J_{eff}/R_b} \geq \left( \frac{E_b}{N_0} \right)_{req}
$$

Substituting $E_b = S \cdot T_b = S / R_b$:

$$
\frac{S / R_b}{N_0 + \dfrac{J}{W \cdot G_p}} \geq \left( \frac{E_b}{N_0} \right)_{req}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Jamming Margin as the maximum tolerable $J/S$ ratio:**

By rearranging, with thermal noise assumed negligible compared to jammer (worst case):

$$
\frac{J}{S} \leq \frac{G_p}{\left( \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \right)_{req}}
$$

Taking logarithms (in dB):

$$
\boxed{\,M_j \text{ (dB)} = G_p \text{ (dB)} - \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req} \text{ (dB)} - L_{sys} \text{ (dB)}\,}
$$

where $L_{sys}$ is the system implementation loss (typically 1–2 dB).

> **Example:** If $G_p = 30$ dB and $(E_b/N_0)_{req} = 10$ dB, then $M_j = 30 - 10 = 20$ dB. The system can tolerate a jammer that is **100× stronger** than the desired signal.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DSSS vs. FHSS (KTU Board-Favourite Comparison Table)

| Parameter | DSSS (Direct Sequence) | FHSS (Frequency Hopping) |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Spreading mechanism | Multiply data with high-rate PN code | Hop carrier frequency pseudo-randomly |
| Signal at any instant | Wideband | Narrowband |
| Bandwidth occupation | Continuous wideband | Discrete frequency slots, swept in time |
| Susceptibility to partial-band jamming | Robust (jammer spread by PN code) | Susceptible (jammer can hit hopped frequency) |
| Synchronization complexity | Moderate (chip-level sync) | High (frequency synthesizer + slot timing) |
| Power consumption | Low (digital multiplication) | High (rapid frequency synthesis) |
| Implementation | Digital signal processing friendly | RF synthesizer dependent |
| Common use | GPS, CDMA, Wi-Fi (802.11b) | Bluetooth, military HF/VHF radios |
| Acquisition time | Longer (search code phase) | Faster (synchronize to hop pattern) |
| Multipath resistance | Excellent (RAKE receiver) | Good (frequency diversity) |
| Coexistence with narrowband | Low interference to narrowband (low spectral density) | Possible hit if narrowband sits on a hop slot |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Practice Problem)

**Question:** A DSSS system uses a data rate of $R_b = 19.2$ kbps and a chip rate of $R_c = 1.228$ Mcps. The receiver operates at a target BER of $10^{-5}$, requiring $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB. If the system implementation loss is $L_{sys} = 1.5$ dB, compute (a) the processing gain, (b) the jamming margin, and (c) the number of chips per bit.

**Solution:**

**(a) Processing Gain:**

$$
G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1.228 \times 10^6}{19.2 \times 10^3} = 63.96 \approx 64
$$

In dB:

$$
G_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(64) = 18.06 \text{ dB}
$$

**(b) Jamming Margin:**

$$
M_j = G_p - (E_b/N_0)_{req} - L_{sys} = 18.06 - 9.6 - 1.5 = 6.96 \text{ dB}
$$

This means the system can tolerate a jammer that is approximately $10^{0.696} \approx 4.97$ times stronger than the desired signal.

**(c) Chips per bit:**

$$
N = G_p = 64 \text{ chips/bit}
$$

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DSSS Transmitter and Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Data Source d(t)]
    pnGen1[PN Code Generator c(t)]
    mul1[Multiplier XOR]
    mod[BPSK Modulator]
    txAnt[Transmit Antenna]
    ch[Wireless Channel + JAMMER J(t) + Noise n(t)]
    rxAnt[Receive Antenna]
    mul2[Multiplier Correlator]
    pnGen2[Synchronized PN Code c(t)]
    demod[BPSK Demodulator]
    out[Recovered Data d(t)]
    src1 --> mul1
    pnGen1 --> mul1
    mul1 --> mod
    mod --> txAnt
    txAnt --> ch
    ch --> rxAnt
    rxAnt --> mul2
    pnGen2 --> mul2
    mul2 --> demod
    demod --> out
```

> **Reading guide:** The data $d(t)$ is multiplied by the PN code $c(t)$ at the transmitter, then BPSK-modulated. At the receiver, the same synchronized PN code is multiplied (correlated) to despread. The jammer $J(t)$ and noise $n(t)$ picked up in the channel are spread by the receiver's PN code, while the desired signal collapses back to its original narrowband form.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — FHSS Transmitter and Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src2[Data Source d(t)]
    fskMod[FSK Modulator]
    synth1[Frequency Synthesizer driven by PN Code]
    mixer1[Frequency Mixer]
    txAnt2[Transmit Antenna]
    ch2[Wireless Channel]
    rxAnt2[Receive Antenna]
    mixer2[Frequency Mixer]
    synth2[Frequency Synthesizer driven by PN Code]
    fskDemod[FSK Demodulator]
    out2[Recovered Data d(t)]
    src2 --> fskMod
    fskMod --> mixer1
    synth1 --> mixer1
    mixer1 --> txAnt2
    txAnt2 --> ch2
    ch2 --> rxAnt2
    rxAnt2 --> mixer2
    synth2 --> mixer2
    mixer2 --> fskDemod
    fskDemod --> out2
```

> **Reading guide:** In FHSS, the data is *first* modulated (typically by FSK), and then a frequency synthesizer under control of a PN code generator translates the modulated signal to a new carrier frequency. The receiver's synthesizer, synchronized to the same PN code, follows the same hopping pattern and dehops the signal back to baseband.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Spreading / De-spreading

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX [Transmitter Side]
        A1[Information Bit b_k of duration T_b]
        A2[PN Sequence c_i of duration T_c]
        A3[XOR Multiplication N chips per bit]
        A4[Wideband Spread Signal bandwidth approx R_c]
        A1 --> A3
        A2 --> A3
        A3 --> A4
    end
    TX --> CH[Channel with AWGN and Jammer]
    subgraph RX [Receiver Side]
        B1[Received Wideband Signal plus Jamming plus Noise]
        B2[Local Synchronized PN Replica]
        B3[Correlator Multiplication]
        B4[De-spread Narrowband Signal bandwidth approx R_b]
        B5[Matched Filter and Threshold Detector]
        B6[Recovered Information Bit b_k hat]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> B3
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B5
        B5 --> B6
    end
    CH --> B1
```

> **Reading guide:** This is a "transformation-of-bandwidth" view. The **functional** effect of the spreading stage is to take a narrowband bit of duration $T_b$ and transform it into a wideband stream of $N$ chips. The **functional** effect of the despreading stage is to compress this wideband stream back into a narrowband bit, while expanding any narrowband jammer into wideband (low-power) noise.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — PN Code Generation by LFSR (m-sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Stage 1]
    s2[Stage 2]
    s3[Stage 3]
    s4[Stage n]
    xor[XOR Feedback Tap]
    outC[PN Code Output c(t)]
    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4
    s4 --> xor
    xor --> s1
    s4 --> outC
```

> **Reading guide:** An n-stage Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) with appropriate feedback taps produces a **maximum-length sequence (m-sequence)** of period $N = 2^n - 1$ chips, exhibiting noise-like randomness and sharp autocorrelation properties essential for spread spectrum.
