# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define IEEE 802.11ad (WiGig). List any two key features.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**

**Model Answer:**
IEEE 802.11ad, commonly known as **WiGig**, is an amendment to the IEEE 802.11 standard that operates in the **60 GHz millimeter-wave (mmWave) band** (57–66 GHz) to deliver multi-gigabit wireless connectivity over very short distances.

**Two key features:**
1. **Very wide channel bandwidth** of up to **2160 MHz**, enabling data rates up to **~7 Gbps**.
2. **Mandatory beamforming** is required to overcome the high path loss and oxygen absorption (~15 dB/km) at 60 GHz, restricting typical range to **< 10 m** (line-of-sight).

Applications include wireless docking, VR/AR, and high-definition wireless displays.

---

### Question 2: What is the significance of IEEE 802.11af (White-Fi)?
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Model Answer:**
IEEE 802.11af, also called **White-Fi** or **Super Wi-Fi**, is an amendment that enables WLAN operation in the **VHF/UHF TV white spaces (54–790 MHz)** — the unused portions of the spectrum previously allocated to terrestrial TV broadcasting.

**Significance:**
1. **Long-range coverage** (up to 5 km) due to favorable propagation of low-frequency VHF/UHF signals.
2. **Cognitive radio operation**: STAs query a **Geolocation Database (GDB)** to identify locally available TV channels, avoiding interference with primary users (TV broadcasters and wireless microphones).
3. Provides broadband connectivity to **rural and underserved areas** where cellular/Wi-Fi deployment is uneconomical.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain the major PHY enhancements introduced in IEEE 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) over IEEE 802.11ac. Highlight the role of OFDMA. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the operation of IEEE 802.11p in vehicular environments. What is the role of the Control Channel (CCH)? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The major PHY enhancements in **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6 / HE)** over **802.11ac (VHT)** are:

**1. OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access):**
In 802.11ac, an entire OFDM symbol is allocated to a single user (OFDM = one user per symbol). In 802.11ax, the channel is divided into **Resource Units (RUs)** — subsets of subcarriers — that can be assigned to *different users* within a single OFDM symbol. This dramatically improves multi-user efficiency, especially in dense deployments. (3 marks)

**2. Higher-order Modulation — 1024-QAM:**
802.11ax introduces 1024-QAM (10 bits/symbol) vs. 256-QAM (8 bits/symbol) in 802.11ac, yielding a **25% increase in peak rate**. (1 mark)

**3. Uplink MU-MIMO:**
While 802.11ac supports only **downlink** MU-MIMO, 802.11ax supports both **UL and DL MU-MIMO** with up to 8 spatial streams per user. (1 mark)

**4. BSS Coloring:**
A 6-bit color identifier is added to the preamble so that overlapping BSSs (co-channel interference) can be detected and transmissions deferred, improving spatial reuse. (1 mark)

**5. Target Wake Time (TWT):**
Scheduled wake-up mechanism for IoT/battery devices, reducing power consumption by up to 67%. (1 mark)

**Net effect:** Wi-Fi 6 achieves ~9.6 Gbps peak (vs. 6.93 Gbps for Wi-Fi 5) and **4× improvement in dense-deployment throughput**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**IEEE 802.11p — WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments):**

**Operating Spectrum:** 5.85–5.925 GHz (US FCC allocation, 75 MHz total). 10 MHz channels (vs. 20 MHz in 802.11a) to combat high Doppler spread at vehicular speeds. (1 mark)

**Modified MAC:** 802.11p defines the **WAVE mode**, which **bypasses authentication and association** procedures, allowing vehicles to transmit safety messages within milliseconds of channel entry — critical given the short contact time between fast-moving vehicles. (2 marks)

**Multi-Channel Operation:**
- **CCH (Control Channel) — Channel 178 (5.885 GHz):** Dedicated to high-priority safety messages (beaconing, BSMs).
- **SCH (Service Channels) — Channels 172, 174, 176, 180, 182, 184:** Used for both safety and non-safety applications.

The radio switches between CCH and SCH every **50 ms** (Channel Coordination; default Guard interval). (2 marks)

**Role of the Control Channel (CCH):**
The CCH serves as a common rendezvous channel where every WAVE device listens for broadcast **WAVE Short Messages (WSMs)** and **WAVE Service Advertisements (WSAs)**. It enables vehicles to discover nearby RSUs (Roadside Units) and other OBUs (On-Board Units) without prior association. (2 marks)

**Architecture Diagram (textual):**
```
OBU (Vehicle) ⇄ [CCH: Safety Beacons, WSA] ⇄ RSU (Roadside Unit)
OBU ⇄ [SCH: V2V Data, V2I Non-Safety] ⇄ RSU / Other OBUs
```

**Applications:** Forward collision warning, blind-spot detection, platooning, electronic toll collection. (0 marks — extra)

---

### 📘 Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Compare IEEE 802.11ac and IEEE 802.11ax in terms of frequency band, channel bandwidth, maximum modulation, maximum data rate, and MIMO capability. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain IEEE 802.11ah (HaLow). Why is it suitable for IoT applications? What is the function of RAW and TWT? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | IEEE 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | IEEE 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | **5 GHz only** | **2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz** (6E adds 6 GHz) |
| Maximum Channel BW | **160 MHz** | **160 MHz** |
| Highest Modulation | **256-QAM** (8 bits/sym) | **1024-QAM** (10 bits/sym) |
| Max Data Rate (theoretical) | **6.93 Gbps** | **9.6 Gbps** |
| MIMO Capability | DL MU-MIMO (up to 4 users, 8 SS) | UL + DL MU-MIMO (up to 8 users) |
| Subcarrier Spacing | 312.5 kHz | 78.125 kHz |
| OFDM Symbol Duration | 3.6 µs (SGI) | 13.6 µs (SGI) |
| Multi-user Access Scheme | OFDM (one user per symbol) | OFDMA (multiple users per symbol) |
| BSS Coloring | Not supported | Supported |
| Target Wake Time | Not supported | Supported |

(7 marks — one mark per row × 7 rows, or distribution as needed)

**Key Conceptual Difference:** 802.11ac optimizes for **peak throughput**; 802.11ax optimizes for **spectral efficiency and dense-deployment performance**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**IEEE 802.11ah (HaLow):**
802.11ah is an amendment to the 802.11 standard that operates in the **sub-1 GHz ISM-type bands** (e.g., 902–928 MHz in the US, 863–868 MHz in Europe, 755–787 MHz in China). It is engineered for **long-range, low-power IoT communications**. (2 marks)

**Why 802.11ah is suitable for IoT:**
1. **Long Range:** Sub-1 GHz signals propagate better through walls and over longer distances — typical range up to **1 km** outdoors.
2. **Low Power Consumption:** Designed for battery-powered devices (years of operation on a single cell).
3. **Massive Station Support:** Up to **8191 STAs per AP** (vs. 2007 in 802.11ac), enabling massive sensor networks.
4. **Scalable Data Rates:** From 150 kbps (long range) to 78 Mbps (short range, 16 MHz channel). (2 marks)

**Function of RAW (Restricted Access Window):**
RAW is a MAC mechanism that divides STAs into **groups**, and each group is allocated a specific time window to access the channel. This reduces contention, collision, and energy waste — critical when thousands of sensors compete for channel access. (1.5 marks)

**Function of TWT (Target Wake Time):**
TWT allows an AP to **schedule individual wake-up slots** for each STA. A sensor remains in deep sleep and wakes only at its assigned slot to transmit/receive. This drastically reduces idle listening power, extending battery life to years. (1.5 marks)

**Applications:** Smart agriculture, smart metering, industrial IoT, environmental monitoring, wearables, smart cities.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — KTU Valuation Key Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 802.11ac with 802.11ax modulation:** Students often write "256-QAM" for both. Remember: **ac = 256-QAM, ax = 1024-QAM** (lose 1 mark per error).
> 2. **Forgetting Coding Rate:** When asked for "maximum data rate," students often forget the **5/6 coding rate** factor. Always show the formula clearly: $R = N_{sc} \times N_{ss} \times N_{bps} \times r_c / T_{sym}$.
> 3. **Mixing 802.11p with 802.11a:** 802.11p uses **10 MHz channels** (half of 802.11a's 20 MHz). Writing "20 MHz" costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Frequency for 802.11ad:** Writing "5 GHz" instead of **60 GHz** is a 1-mark deduction but it changes the entire answer.
> 5. **802.11ah ≠ 802.11af:** Many students confuse these. Remember: **ah = sub-1 GHz (IoT)**, **af = TV white spaces (VHF/UHF)**.
> 6. **OFDMA ≠ OFDM:** 802.11ax's biggest innovation is **OFDMA** (multi-user per symbol), not just higher modulation. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 7. **No diagram = partial marks:** For 7-mark questions involving 802.11p, 802.11e, or 802.11ah, **always include a block diagram** (CCH/SCH, RAW groups, AC categories) — KTU examiners allocate 2–3 marks for diagrams.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Other IEEE 802.11 Standards**

- ✅ **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5):** 5 GHz only, 160 MHz, **256-QAM**, 8 SS, **6.93 Gbps**, DL MU-MIMO.
- ✅ **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6):** 2.4/5/6 GHz, 160 MHz, **1024-QAM**, 8 SS, **9.6 Gbps**, **OFDMA**, UL+DL MU-MIMO, BSS Coloring, TWT.
- ✅ **802.11ad (WiGig):** **60 GHz**, 2160 MHz BW, **~7 Gbps**, range < 10 m, mandatory beamforming, oxygen absorption ~15 dB/km.
- ✅ **802.11ah (HaLow):** **Sub-1 GHz**, 1–16 MHz channels, **up to 1 km range**, 150 kbps–78 Mbps, 8191 STAs, **RAW + TWT** for IoT.
- ✅ **802.11af (White-Fi):** **VHF/UHF TVWS (54–790 MHz)**, 6/7/8 MHz channels, up to **5 km range**, **GDB-based cognitive radio**.
- ✅ **802.11p (WAVE):** **5.9 GHz (5.85–5.925 GHz)**, 10 MHz channels, 27 Mbps max, **no association**, **CCH + SCH**, V2V/V2I, DSRC.
- ✅ **802.11e:** MAC QoS, **HCF = EDCA + HCCA**, 4 Access Categories (VO, VI, BE, BK), basis of WMM.
- ✅ **Data Rate Formula:** $R_{max} = N_{sc} \times N_{ss} \times N_{bps} \times r_c / T_{sym}$
- ✅ **Path Loss (60 GHz ≫ 2.4 GHz):** ~28 dB additional loss at 10 m → requires beamforming.
- ✅ **Key Mnemonic — "Very High Speed Air Holds A Vast Packet":** **V**HT(ac), **H**E(ax), **A**D(ad), **A**H(ah), **A**F(af), **P**(p).
- ✅ **Frequency vs Range trade-off:** Lower frequency (sub-1 GHz, TVWS) → longer range; Higher frequency (60 GHz) → shorter range but wider bandwidth.
- ✅ **Channel BW hierarchy (max):** 802.11p (10 MHz) < 802.11af (8 MHz) < 802.11ah (16 MHz) < 802.11ac/ax (160 MHz) < 802.11ad (2160 MHz).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wi-Fi Protected Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT502 - INTRODUCTION TO WIRELESS COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Wi-Fi Protected Access # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA)** is a security protocol and security certification program developed by the **Wi-Fi Alliance** in **2003** to secure wireless computer networks. It was created as an interim, software-upgradable solution to overcome the critical cryptographic weaknesses inherent in the original Wired Equivalent Privacy (WEP) standard while the IEEE 802.11i amendment was still being finalized. WPA implements a subset of the forthcoming IEEE 802.11i standard using the **Temporal Key Integrity Protocol (TKIP)** along with **Michael** (a Message Integrity Check / MIC algorithm) and a stronger user authentication mechanism based on **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** or **Enterprise (802.1X + EAP)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** WPA is studied under Module 4 as a transitional security framework bridging WEP and WPA2/802.11i. Examiners frequently ask comparative questions (WEP vs WPA vs WPA2) and 4-Way Handshake derivations. > [!NOTE] > **Evolution Timeline (Must Memorize):** > - **WEP (1997)** – Original insecure protocol. > - **WPA (2003)** – Stopgap using TKIP + MIC. > - **WPA2 (2004)** – Full IEEE 802.11i compliance using AES-CCMP. > - **WPA3 (2018)** – SAE-based, protected management frames, individualized encryption. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) Imagine a **college hostel with multiple entrances** (a wireless network). - **WEP** is like having a *cheap front door lock* with a key that never changes. Anyone who watches the door long enough can copy the key pattern and sneak in. Worse, every room uses the same key. - **WPA** is like upgrading to a *digital smart-lock* that **changes the key for every visitor** (Temporal Keys), **stamps every visitor's ID photo onto a log** (MIC), and **lets the security desk verify the visitor's identity** (Authentication) before issuing a temporary badge. Even if one temporary key leaks, it expires in seconds and can't open other rooms. - **WPA2** is like replacing the smart-lock with a *military-grade vault door* — the key is mathematically unbreakable (AES). - **WPA3** is the *vault door plus an armed guard* who verifies identity through a *password handshake that resists offline guessing* (Dragonfly/SAE), even if the visitor whispers a weak password. In short: **WPA = "Secure enough to save Wi-Fi from WEP's collapse, fast enough to deploy on existing hardware via firmware updates."** ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Expansion | Significance | |------|-----------|--------------| | **TKIP** | Temporal Key Integrity Protocol | Dynamically generates per-packet keys | | **MIC** | Message Integrity Check (Michael) | 64-bit cryptographic hash for tamper detection | | **PMK** | Pairwise Master Key | Derived from PSK or 802.1X | | **PTK** | Pairwise Transient Key | Session key used for unicast encryption | | **GTK** | Group Temporal Key | Session key used for broadcast/multicast | | **ANonce / SNonce** | Authenticator / Supplicant Nonce | Random numbers used in 4-way handshake | | **EAP** | Extensible Authentication Protocol | Framework for enterprise authentication | | **PSK** | Pre-Shared Key | Shared secret used in home/SOHO mode | > [!NOTE] > **Bolded Constants/Parameters to Memorize:** > - MIC length in WPA = **64 bits** > - TKIP per-packet key length = **128 bits** > - TKIP Sequence Counter (TSC) = **48 bits** > - Re-key interval recommendation = **10,000 packets** or **60 seconds** > - IV size in WEP = **24 bits** → expanded to **48 bits** in TKIP --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why WPA? — The WEP Vulnerabilities That Forced WPA WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) was broken primarily because of: 1. **Short IV (24 bits)** → IV collisions guaranteed within hours of traffic. 2. **Weak RC4 stream cipher key scheduling** → statistical key recovery via Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir (FMS) attack. 3. **No integrity protection** → CRC-32 is linear, enabling bit-flipping attacks. 4. **Single shared key** for all users in a BSS → no per-user isolation. WPA was engineered to *patch* these issues **without requiring new hardware**, because TKIP and Michael were designed to run on legacy WEP silicon. ## 2.2 The Three Pillars of WPA Security ### Pillar 1: Enhanced Data Privacy via **TKIP** TKIP wraps WEP with three enhancements: - **Per-packet key mixing** — each packet gets a fresh 128-bit key derived from the base temporal key, the transmitter MAC, and a 48-bit TKIP Sequence Counter (TSC). - **Extended IV (48 bits)** — eliminates IV reuse. - **Re-keying mechanism** — refreshes keys to bound exposure. ### Pillar 2: Data Integrity via **MIC (Michael)** Michael computes a **64-bit tag** over the packet header + payload using a secret key. It is intentionally *lightweight* (designed for legacy hardware) but adds crucial tamper detection. ### Pillar 3: Frame Authentication & Access Control via **802.1X / EAP (Enterprise)** or **PSK (Personal)** Two flavors exist: - **WPA-Personal (WPA-PSK):** Home/SOHO use, shared password. - **WPA-Enterprise (WPA-802.1X):** Corporate use, RADIUS server + EAP methods (TLS, TTLS, PEAP). ## 2.3 The WPA Key Hierarchy ``` Pass-phrase / 802.1X │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Pairwise Master Key (PMK) │ ← 256 bits, long-lived └─────────────────────────────────┘ │ (4-Way Handshake) ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) │ ← 512 bits, per-session └─────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ KCK (128) KEK (128) TK (128) + MIC Key (confirm) (encrypt) (data) + ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Group Temporal Key (GTK) │ ← 256 bits, multicast └─────────────────────────────────┘ ``` - **KCK** = Key Confirmation Key — used to compute MIC over EAPOL-Key frames. - **KEK** = Key Encryption Key — used to encrypt the GTK during delivery. - **TK** = Temporal Key — the actual encryption key for data traffic. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Units | |---|----------------|---------|-------| | 1 | $TSC_{max} = 2^{48} - 1$ | TKIP Sequence Counter exhaustion limit | packets | | 2 | $TSC_{step} = 1 \text{ per packet}$ | TSC increment rule | packets | | 3 | $PTK = PRF\text{-}384(PMK,\; ANonce,\; SNonce,\; MAC_{AP},\; MAC_{STA})$ | Full PTK derivation | bits | | 4 | $PTK_{length} = 512$ | Total PTK size | bits | | 5 | $PTK = KCK \vert\vert KEK \vert\vert TK$ | Composition rule | bits | | 6 | $KCK_{len} = KEK_{len} = 128$ | Subkey lengths | bits | | 7 | $TK_{len} = 128$ | Data-encryption key length | bits | | 8 | $MIC_{len} = 64$ | Michael tag length | bits | | 9 | $IV_{WEP} = 24$; $IV_{TKIP} = 48$ | IV size comparison | bits | | 10 | $PMK = PBKDF2\text{-}SHA1(passphrase,\; SSID,\; 4096,\; 256)$ | PSK → PMK mapping | bits | | 11 | $MIC_{Michael}(M,\; K) = (L,R) \oplus f(L,R,K)$ | Michael algorithm | 64-bit tag | | 12 | $GTK_{len} = 256$ | Group key length | bits | | 13 | Re-key trigger | $TSC \geq 2^{16}$ *or* time ≥ 60 s | – | | 14 | Countermeasure rule | ≥ 2 MIC failures in ≤ 60 s → tear down | – | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-table-breaking reminder:** In the rows above I deliberately used `\vert\vert` for concatenation rather than the literal `||` character, and `\geq` for comparison — this preserves the markdown table syntax while remaining mathematically valid. ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility WPA matters in practice because: - **IoT & Embedded Networks:** Many low-power Wi-Fi chipsets still rely on WPA2-Personal; WPA3 brings SAE which is friendly to constrained devices. - **Enterprise Mobility:** WPA-Enterprise with EAP-TLS underpins hospital, airport, and defense Wi-Fi. - **Forensics:** Understanding TKIP/MIC is essential when reconstructing attack timelines (e.g., chopchop, Beck-Tews). - **Compliance:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 mandate decommissioning of WEP and adoption of WPA2/WPA3. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Passphrase to PMK (Personal Mode) In WPA/WPA2-Personal, the user enters a pass-phrase (8–63 ASCII characters) plus the SSID. The PMK is derived using PBKDF2 with HMAC-SHA1. $$PMK = PBKDF2\text{-}HMAC\text{-}SHA1(\text{PassPhrase},\; \text{SSID},\; 4096,\; 256)$$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Inputs gathered:** $PassPhrase$ (string), $SSID$ (string), iteration count $= 4096$, output length $= 256$ bits. 2. **Iteration loop:** PBKDF2 repeatedly applies HMAC-SHA1 over the salt ($SSID$) for **4096 rounds** — this *deliberately* slows down brute-force dictionary attacks. 3. **Truncation:** After 4096 iterations, the final 256-bit block is taken as the PMK. 4. **Output:** A 256-bit secret known to both AP and STA because both share the same passphrase and SSID. > [!NOTE] > **Why 4096 iterations?** It is the Wi-Fi Alliance mandated value, raising the cost of offline brute-force so each passphrase guess costs ~4096 SHA-1 operations. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PTK Construction via PRF-384 The PTK is constructed in the 4-Way Handshake using a Pseudo-Random Function (PRF-384 in WPA/WPA2): $$PTK = PRF\text{-}384(PMK,\; \text{"Pairwise key expansion"},\; \min(ANonce, SNonce) \;\vert\vert\; \max(ANonce, SNonce) \;\vert\vert\; \min(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA}) \;\vert\vert\; \max(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA}))$$ **Expanding it into the three sub-keys:** $$\begin{aligned} PTK &= L(PTK, 0, 128) \;\vert\vert\; L(PTK, 128, 128) \;\vert\vert\; L(PTK, 256, 128) \\ &= KCK \;\vert\vert\; KEK \;\vert\vert\; TK \end{aligned}$$ Where $L(X, i, n)$ denotes "extract $n$ bits starting at offset $i$ of pseudo-random output $X$." **Bit-length verification:** $$\begin{aligned} |KCK| &= 128 \text{ bits} \\ |KEK| &= 128 \text{ bits} \\ |TK| &= 128 \text{ bits} \\ |PTK| &= 128 + 128 + 128 = 384 \text{ bits (TKIP) or 512 (CCMP, incl. MIC key)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subtle point examiners test:** The *ordering* of nonces and MACs in the PRF input is **min-then-max**, not concatenation order. This makes the PTK deterministic regardless of which side computes it first. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Four-Way Handshake (Step-by-Step) This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in KTU Module 4. **Assumptions:** - Both AP (Authenticator) and STA (Supplicant) already hold the PMK. - ANonce is a fresh random number from AP; SNonce from STA. ### Message 1: AP → STA - AP sends **ANonce** to STA inside an EAPOL-Key frame. - The PTK cannot yet be computed because STA lacks ANonce. ### Message 2: STA → AP - STA now constructs the PTK: $$\begin{aligned} PTK_{STA} &= PRF\text{-}384(PMK,\; \text{"Pairwise key expansion"}, \\ &\quad \min(ANonce, SNonce) \;\vert\vert\; \max(ANonce, SNonce) \;\vert\vert\; \\ &\quad \min(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA}) \;\vert\vert\; \max(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA})) \end{aligned}$$ - STA sends back its own **SNonce** plus an MIC computed over the entire EAPOL-Key frame using the **KCK**: $$MIC_{STA} = HMAC\text{-}SHA1(KCK,\; EAPOL\text{-}Key_{M2})$$ - AP independently recomputes the PTK (same inputs → same output) and verifies the MIC. ### Message 3: AP → STA - AP includes the **GTK**, encrypted under the **KEK**: $$E[GTK] = RC4(KEK,\; GTK \;\vert\vert\; \text{padding})$$ - Also includes a MIC for integrity (using KCK) and an install-bit instructing STA to install the keys. - After this message, STA installs both PTK and GTK. ### Message 4: STA → AP - Final ACK from STA with its own MIC, confirming key installation. - AP receives it and installs the keys on its side as well. **Outcome:** All four keys (KCK, KEK, TK, GTK) are now active, and encrypted unicast/multicast data traffic can flow. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — TKIP Per-Packet Key Mixing Function For each MAC frame, TKIP computes a unique 128-bit RC4 key as: $$PerPacketKey = Phase1Key \;\vert\vert\; Phase2Key$$ where $$\begin{aligned} Phase1Key &= Mix_1(TA_{low5Bytes},\; TK,\; TSC_{low16}) \\ Phase2Key &= Mix_2(TSC_{high32},\; Phase1Key) \end{aligned}$$ **Mix₁ logic (simplified):** 1. Extract the lower 5 bytes of the Transmitter Address (TA). 2. Combine with TK (128 bits) and the low 16 bits of TSC. 3. Perform an S-box substitution + rotation to produce 80 bits. **Mix₂ logic:** 1. Take the upper 32 bits of TSC and the Phase1 key. 2. Run Feistel-like rounds to produce 64 bits. The full per-packet key is then fed into the standard RC4 stream cipher along with the IV (TSC). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Why:** This mixing binds the per-packet key to the *transmitter's identity* and the *packet sequence number*, which means an attacker cannot replay or relocate packets between sessions. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Michael MIC Algorithm (Lightweight Integrity) Michael is intentionally weak (cryptographically) because legacy hardware cannot afford AES. It works in two 32-bit registers $L$ and $R$: $$(L_0, R_0) = K$$ (the MIC key, derived from PMK) For each 32-bit block $M_i$ of the message: $$\begin{aligned} L_{i+1} &= R_i \oplus (L_i \lll 17) \oplus f(M_i, L_i) \\ R_{i+1} &= L_i \end{aligned}$$ The final tag is $(L_{last}, R_{last})$ truncated/concatenated to **64 bits**. **Countermeasure rule:** $$\text{If MIC failures} \geq 2 \text{ within 60 s} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{tear down all keys + 60 s silence}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Michael is *not collision-resistant*. An attacker who can flip two bits in a specific way can produce the same MIC. This is why the countermeasure rule is mandatory in WPA. ## 3.6 Summary Matrix — Operations & Bit-Lengths | Step | Input(s) | Operation | Output | Length | |------|----------|-----------|--------|--------| | PSK → PMK | Passphrase, SSID | PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 × 4096 | PMK | 256 bits | | 4-Way HS | PMK, ANonce, SNonce, MACs | PRF-384 | PTK | 384/512 bits | | PTK split | PTK | Bit-slicing | KCK ∥ KEK ∥ TK (∥ MIC key) | 128 ea. | | GTK delivery | GTK, KEK | RC4-encrypt | E[GTK] | 256 bits | | MIC over frame | KCK, EAPOL | HMAC-SHA1 | MIC | 128 bits (HS), 64 (data) | | Per-packet | TK, TA, TSC | Mix₁ ∥ Mix₂ | RC4 key | 128 bits | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of WPA Security Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UP["UPPER LAYER (Host/AP CPU)"] A1["Passphrase / 802.1X EAP"] A2["PMK (256 bits)"] A3["4-Way Handshake Controller"] A4["PTK = KCK || KEK || TK"] A5["GTK (256 bits)"] end subgraph MP["MIDDLE LAYER (Firmware)"] B1["TKIP Engine"] B2["Michael MIC Engine"] B3["Per-Packet Key Mixer"] B4["RC4 Stream Cipher"] end subgraph LP["LOWER LAYER (MAC/PHY)"] C1["802.11 MAC Frame Builder"] C2["PHY Modulator (DSSS/OFDM)"] C3["Antenna"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 A3 --> A5 A4 --> B3 A5 --> B4 B3 --> B4 B2 --> B4 B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2 --> C3 ``` ## 4.2 Four-Way Handshake Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant AP as Authenticator (AP) participant STA as Supplicant (Client) Note over AP,STA: Both already hold PMK (256 bits) AP->>STA: Msg1 = EAPOL-Key (ANonce, no MIC) Note right of STA: STA generates SNonce
Computes PTK = PRF-384(PMK, ANonce, SNonce, MACs)
Derives KCK, KEK, TK STA->>AP: Msg2 = EAPOL-Key (SNonce, MIC over frame using KCK) Note left of AP: AP recomputes PTK
Verifies MIC using KCK
Encrypts GTK using KEK AP->>STA: Msg3 = EAPOL-Key (ANonce, encrypted GTK, MIC, install=1) Note right of STA: STA decrypts GTK with KEK
Verifies MIC
Installs PTK and GTK STA->>AP: Msg4 = EAPOL-Key (MIC, ACK) Note left of AP: AP verifies MIC
Installs PTK and GTK Note over AP,STA: Secure channel established ✓ ``` ## 4.3 Key Hierarchy & Re-Keying Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR PP["Pass-Phrase"] -->|PBKDF2 x4096| PMK2["PMK"] A1["802.1X / EAP"] -->|Master Session Key derivation| PMK2 PMK2 -->|4-Way Handshake| PTK2["PTK"] PTK2 --> KCK2["KCK"] PTK2 --> KEK2["KEK"] PTK2 --> TK2["TK"] G1["AP Authenticator"] -->|Group Key Handshake| GTK2["GTK"] TK2 -->|Per-Packet Mix| PPK["Per-Packet RC4 Key"] PPK -->|Encrypts| DF["Data Frames"] REK["Re-key Trigger: TSC >= 2^16 OR Time >= 60 s OR MIC failure"] -.-> PTK2 ``` ## 4.4 WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3 Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR WEP["WEP (1997)
RC4 + 24-bit IV
No Integrity
BROKEN"] -->|2003 Patch| WPA["WPA (2003)
TKIP + Michael
64-bit MIC
INTERIM"] WPA -->|2004 Full| WPA2["WPA2 (2004)
AES-128-CCMP
802.11i Full
STRONG"] WPA2 -->|2018| WPA3["WPA3 (2018)
SAE / Dragonfly
Forward Secrecy
STRONGEST"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Differentiate between WEP and WPA in terms of encryption algorithm, IV length, and integrity protection.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | WEP | WPA | |-----------|-----|-----| | Encryption | RC4 (static key) | RC4 (per-packet TKIP-derived key) | | IV Size | **24 bits** | **48 bits** (TSC) | | Integrity | CRC-32 (linear, weak) | **Michael MIC (64-bit)** | | Authentication | Open / Shared Key | PSK or 802.1X/EAP | | Re-keying | Manual | Automatic via 4-Way Handshake | WEP's short 24-bit IV causes reuse within hours and is vulnerable to FMS attacks, while WPA's TKIP generates a fresh 128-bit key per packet, eliminating IV reuse and adding cryptographic integrity. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **What is the role of the Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) in WPA? Name its components.** **Model Answer:** The PTK is the **per-session, per-STA** key derived during the 4-Way Handshake from the PMK, ANonce, SNonce, and MAC addresses. It is the actual keying material used to protect unicast traffic and to authenticate EAPOL-Key frames. It consists of three sub-keys, each **128 bits** long: 1. **KCK (Key Confirmation Key)** — used to compute MIC over handshake messages. 2. **KEK (Key Encryption Key)** — used to encrypt the GTK during delivery. 3. **TK (Temporal Key)** — used to encrypt/decrypt data frames via TKIP. In some 802.11i variants a 4th 128-bit MIC key is appended, making PTK 512 bits total. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Always state the **PRF-384 formula explicitly**; many students lose 2 marks by writing only "PTK is computed" without showing inputs. > - In 4-Way Handshake diagrams, students forget the **install-bit** in Message 3 — worth 1 mark. > - Confusion between **PMK vs PTK vs TK** is the #1 reason for lost marks. Memorize the hierarchy. > - For MIC questions, students omit the **countermeasure rule** (2 MIC failures in 60 s → tear-down). This costs 2 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Four-Way Handshake** mechanism used in WPA/WPA2. Show how the PTK is derived using the PRF-384 function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **TKIP per-packet key mixing process**. Why is per-packet key generation critical for defeating the attacks that broke WEP? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) The **4-Way Handshake** is performed after the STA has authenticated (PSK or 802.1X) and both AP and STA share the **PMK** (256 bits). Its purpose is to: - Confirm liveness of both peers, - Derive a fresh PTK, - Deliver and install the GTK. **Step-by-step:** 1. **Message 1 (AP → STA):** AP generates a fresh random **ANonce** and sends it in an EAPOL-Key frame. No MIC is included because the STA doesn't yet have KCK. 2. **Message 2 (STA → AP):** STA generates its own **SNonce**, then computes: $$\begin{aligned} PTK &= PRF\text{-}384\Big(PMK,\; \text{"Pairwise key expansion"}, \\ &\quad \min(ANonce, SNonce) \;\vert\vert\; \max(ANonce, SNonce) \;\vert\vert\; \\ &\quad \min(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA}) \;\vert\vert\; \max(MAC_{AP}, MAC_{STA})\Big) \\ &= KCK \;\vert\vert\; KEK \;\vert\vert\; TK \end{aligned}$$ STA replies with **SNonce + MIC** where $MIC = HMAC\text{-}SHA1(KCK, \text{EAPOL-Key frame})$. 3. **Message 3 (AP → STA):** AP independently re-computes PTK and verifies MIC. AP encrypts GTK using KEK: $$E[GTK] = RC4(KEK,\; GTK \;\vert\vert\; padding)$$ and sends $E[GTK]$ along with the install-bit set to 1, instructing STA to install the keys. 4. **Message 4 (STA → AP):** STA sends an ACK with MIC. AP installs PTK and GTK. The handshake is complete. *Diagram:* (refer to SECTION 4.2 above). #### Model Solution — (b) The **TKIP per-packet key mixing** generates a unique 128-bit RC4 key for every MAC frame: $$\begin{aligned} Phase1Key &= Mix_1(TA_{low5Bytes},\; TK,\; TSC_{low16}) \quad \text{80 bits} \\ Phase2Key &= Mix_2(TSC_{high32},\; Phase1Key) \quad \text{48 bits} \\ PerPacketKey &= Phase1Key \;\vert\vert\; Phase2Key \quad \text{128 bits} \end{aligned}$$ **Why this defeats WEP-style attacks:** 1. **Binds key to TSC:** WEP's RC4 IV was reused freely; TKIP's 48-bit TSC, combined with per-packet mixing, makes IV collisions practically impossible (you would need $2^{48}$ packets before reuse). 2. **Binds key to transmitter MAC:** An attacker cannot capture one packet's keystream and replay or transpose it to another STA's session. 3. **Forces key refresh:** Even if an attacker recovers one packet's keystream via weak-RC4 attacks (e.g., Klein), the next packet uses an entirely different key, so cumulative attacks fail. 4. **Limits exposure window:** Re-keying every $\sim 60$ s or $2^{16}$ packets bounds the volume of ciphertext under any single key. Hence TKIP neutralises the **FMS, chopchop, and Beck-Tews attacks** that defeated WEP, while reusing the existing RC4 hardware. **(7 marks complete)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** Explain the **Michael MIC algorithm** used in WPA for data integrity. What are its limitations and the mandatory countermeasure rule? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **WPA, WPA2, and WPA3** in terms of cryptographic primitives, authentication, and key management. Why did WPA3 introduce SAE? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **Michael** is a 64-bit Message Integrity Check designed for legacy WEP hardware that could not afford full AES. It uses two 32-bit registers $(L, R)$ initialized with the MIC key (derived from PMK): $$(L_0, R_0) = K_{MIC}$$ For each 32-bit block $M_i$ of the message: $$\begin{aligned} L_{i+1} &= R_i \oplus (L_i \lll 17) \oplus f(M_i, L_i) \\ R_{i+1} &= L_i \end{aligned}$$ The output tag is $(L_{last}, R_{last})$, 64 bits long, appended to the frame. **Limitations:** 1. **Not collision-resistant:** Cryptographically weaker than HMAC; chosen-prefix forgery possible with $\sim 2^{29}$ operations. 2. **Designed for legacy throughput:** A stronger algorithm would require hardware redesign. 3. **Single-bit invertibility:** Small modifications to the message can be compensated in the tag. **Mandatory Countermeasure Rule:** If **2 or more MIC failures** are detected within **60 seconds**, the STA/AP must: - Tear down all current keys, - Log the event, - Suspend all reception for **60 seconds** before renegotiation. This limits the attacker's brute-force window. #### Model Solution — (b) | Feature | WPA (2003) | WPA2 (2004) | WPA3 (2018) | |---------|------------|-------------|-------------| | Cipher | TKIP + RC4 | AES-128-CCMP | AES-128/256-GCMP, ChaCha20 | | Integrity | Michael (64-bit) | CBC-MAC / CCMP | GCM (128-bit auth tag) | | Authentication | PSK / 802.1X | PSK / 802.1X | SAE / 802.1X | | Key Mgmt | 4-Way HS + TKIP mixing | 4-Way HS unchanged | SAE + 4-Way HS | | Forward Secrecy | No | No | **Yes** (per-session key) | | Offline Dictionary | Vulnerable | Vulnerable (if weak PSK) | **Resistant** | | Mgmt Frame Protection | No | Optional (802.11w) | **Mandatory** | **Why WPA3 introduced SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals):** SAE replaces PSK with a **zero-knowledge proof / Dragonfly Key Exchange**: - Both sides exchange cryptographic commitments and challenges derived from the password. - An **offline dictionary attacker** learns *nothing* useful from observing the handshake — every guess must be tested with a live AP. - Provides **forward secrecy**: compromising one session key does not reveal past sessions. - Resists **active** and **passive** key-recovery attacks, even when the password is weak. Hence SAE makes WPA3 suitable for IoT and public networks (Wi-Fi Enhanced Open / OWE for unauthenticated encryption in open SSIDs). **(7 marks complete)** --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Wi-Fi Protected Access)** - **WPA = TKIP + Michael MIC + 802.1X/PSK**, designed as a firmware patch for WEP. - **PMK (256 bits)** is long-lived; **PTK (384 or 512 bits)** is per-session; **TK (128 bits)** is the data-encryption sub-key. - **PRF-384** is the deterministic function used to expand PMK into PTK using ANonce, SNonce, and both MAC addresses in **min-max** order. - **4-Way Handshake Messages:** 1. AP → STA : ANonce 2. STA → AP : SNonce + MIC(KCK) 3. AP → STA : Encrypted GTK + install-bit + MIC 4. STA → AP : ACK + MIC - **TKIP mixing** = $Mix_1 + Mix_2$ feeding RC4; produces a fresh 128-bit per-packet key. - **MIC length = 64 bits**; **countermeasure = 2 failures / 60 s → tear-down + 60 s silence**. - **WPA2 uses AES-CCMP**; full 802.11i compliance. - **WPA3 uses SAE (Dragonfly)** for password-authenticated key exchange with **forward secrecy** and **offline-attack resistance**. - **IV evolution:** WEP 24-bit → TKIP 48-bit (TSC) → AES-CCMP 48-bit PN. - **Always remember:** PSK ≠ PMK. PSK → PMK needs **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 × 4096 iterations**. - **Common confusions to avoid:** - KCK ≠ TK (KCK verifies handshake; TK encrypts data). - GTK ≠ PTK (GTK is group/multicast; PTK is unicast). - MIC ≠ MAC (MIC = integrity tag from Michael; MAC = Medium Access Control address). - **Exam hot-spots:** 4-Way Handshake sequence + PTK formula + WEP-vs-WPA comparison + Michael countermeasure. ---
